query:
hi, this is melissa grant, dob 04/18/1989, i know this is prob a dumb question but i keep reading different things and im getting myself all confused. i believe the incubation period for covid 19 is 7 days. so if you get sick after 7 days does the 14 say quarantine period starts from the moment you show symptoms or is the 7 days incubation period included in 14 day quarantine period? like i was trying to ask my pcp dr. reena patel thru the portal at keystone family clinic but i dont think i explained it right and now im sitting here overthinking it. my number is 215-555-4078 and email is mel.g89@protonmail.com if they need to reply that way but honestly i just need someone to say it in normal words lol. 

basically im trying to figure out if someone was around a covid person and then felt fine for the first week, then on day 7 started feeling sick, is that when the 14 days starts, or did the first 7 days already count? bc if the 7 days already counts then its like only 7 more days, but if it starts when symptoms show then its a whole 14 more days and that changes work and groceries and not seeing my mom and all that. i dont want to be careless or get anyone else sick, but im also not trying to sit in my room forever if im counting wrong. sorry im rambling, i just keep seeing incubation and quarantine used together and my brain is mixing them up. i also dont know if symptoms showing means mild stuff too or only fever/cough type stuff. any clear answer would help a lot.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a dumb question at all. The terms are confusing, and you’re asking exactly the right thing.

**Incubation period** means the time between being exposed and developing symptoms. For COVID, symptoms can start anywhere from about **2–14 days** after exposure, though many people get symptoms around days 3–5. So day 7 is definitely possible.

**Quarantine** means staying away from others after an exposure in case you become contagious. **Isolation** means staying away once you are sick or test positive.

If someone was exposed and felt fine for 7 days, then developed symptoms on day 7, the first 7 days “count” for the exposure-monitoring period — **but once symptoms start, you should treat that as a new “day 0” for illness/isolation purposes.** That does **not necessarily mean 14 more days**. Current guidance is generally: stay home and away from others until symptoms are clearly improving and you’ve been **fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine**, then use extra precautions like masking, avoiding high-risk people, and improving ventilation for about **5 more days**. Some workplaces or clinics may still use stricter rules, so it’s worth checking their policy.

And yes — **mild symptoms count**. COVID can start as congestion, sore throat, headache, fatigue, body aches, runny nose, stomach upset, or “just allergies.” Fever and cough are not required.

Practical next steps:  
- Take a COVID test now if symptoms have started.  
- If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**, because early tests can be falsely negative.  
- Avoid seeing your mom for now, especially if she is older or has health conditions, until you’re improving and/or testing negative.  
- For groceries, use delivery, curbside pickup, or ask someone to drop items off if possible.

Seek urgent care right away for trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, oxygen levels under 92% if you monitor, or symptoms that rapidly worsen.

Bottom line: **day 7 symptoms mean isolate based on when symptoms started, but it usually is not “14 more days” unless your workplace specifically requires that.**

query: hi, i would like to know where to get tested for covid-19, my name is janelle morris and i work in a call center, brightline customer care, and one agent was confirmed to have tested positive and i still had contact with her close friends and her last wednesday before i sent her to the doctor for a check? i am trying not to panic but its hard bc we sit kind of close and we share the same break room and headset wipes and stuff, and honestly ppl there still come talk right in your face. i dont really know if i should wait for symptoms or just go now, bc i feel ok mostly but every little throat tickle is making me think omg is this it. i live at 428 maple ave apt 3b, newark nj, so if there is somewhere near me that takes walk ins or drive thru i need to know, bc i dont have a car every day and i may need my cousin to take me. my number is 973-555-0148 if they need to call but i am at work weird shifts so texting is better. i called dr. priya shah office but the lady said they are swamped and to look online, but everything online says different things and some places say appointment only. also do i need to tell my boss i cant come in until i get the test, or only if i get fever/cough? i was the one who told that agent to go to the doctor for a check and now i feel like i was already too close to her, plus i talked to her close friends after too, so idk what to do next or where to get tested today if possible.
target_response:
Hi Janelle — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this stress, especially in a close call-center setting. It’s understandable that every throat tickle feels alarming after a confirmed exposure.

**What to do about testing:**  
If your **last close contact was last Wednesday**, the best timing depends on how many days it has been:

- If it has been **5 or more days since the exposure**, it’s reasonable to **test now**. A PCR/NAAT test is most sensitive, but a home rapid antigen test is also useful.
- If it has been **less than 5 days**, testing today can be falsely negative. You can test now for reassurance, but plan to **repeat in 48 hours** and/or test on day 5.
- If you develop symptoms — fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, loss of taste/smell — **test right away** and repeat if negative.

**Where to look near Newark:**  
I can’t see live appointment availability from here, but the most reliable options are:

1. **New Jersey COVID testing locator:** covid19.nj.gov/testing  
2. **Newark Department of Health / Essex County testing resources** online  
3. Local pharmacies such as **CVS, Walgreens, Rite Aid** — many require appointments, but some offer same-day slots.  
4. Nearby urgent care centers — call first and ask if they do walk-in COVID testing and whether they offer PCR or rapid testing.  
5. If transportation is hard, consider **home rapid tests** from a pharmacy; some insurance plans still cover them, depending on your plan.

**Work guidance:**  
You should tell your manager/HR that you had a workplace exposure and ask about their current policy. In the meantime, wear a well-fitting mask, avoid eating in shared spaces if possible, and do not share headsets or supplies. If you develop symptoms or test positive, you should stay home and follow your employer’s sick/COVID policy.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels under 94% if you use a pulse oximeter.

Try not to panic — most exposures do not automatically lead to infection, and testing plus masking is the right next step.

query: my boyfriend michael ray barker was put in the hospital for strep a nemonia 5 days ago and i am just so scared and dont really understand half of what they are telling me. hes at st marys medical center and his wrist band says MRN SM-447219 and i keep staring at it like that makes any of this make sense. yesterday they done another x ray and said that his nemonia was getting worse and had spread to his left lung and was also in his right real bad, like they said both sides but the right was really bad, and they also done a culture on the stuff that hes been coughing up out of his lungs. today they told us he has staph in his lungs too and i just about lost it cause i thought staph was like on skin or in hospitals and now im worried he got something worse while hes been in here. dr. lena patel came in kinda fast and said they were changing meds but i didnt catch all the names cause he was coughing and i was crying and trying to text his mom at the same time. his insurance is anthem bcbs #XQJ8842190 and i keep wondering if that matters with what they can give him or if they should be moving him to icu or doing more tests. he looks so weak and he gets winded just sitting up and the coughing sounds awful. how dangerous is this with strep a nemonia plus staph in his lungs and what should be done, like should i be asking for infectious disease doctor or stronger antibiotics or another x ray or ct scan? i dont wanna be annoying to the nurses but i dont wanna just sit here if its spreading and getting worse.
target_response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. Seeing someone you love that weak and short of breath is terrifying, and it’s completely reasonable to ask questions. Also, you don’t need to share his MRN/insurance numbers in messages like this—his care team will already have that information.

**Strep A pneumonia can be serious**, and having “staph” found in lung sputum can also be important. Staph can live on the skin/nose/throat and can cause pneumonia—especially when someone is already very ill—so it does **not automatically mean he “caught it” in the hospital**. The key is whether it’s **MSSA or MRSA**, and whether the sputum culture truly represents infection versus colonization. Changing antibiotics after culture results is exactly what should happen.

It’s appropriate to ask for a clear update. You are not being annoying. You can say to the nurse:  
**“I’m overwhelmed and need a 5–10 minute explanation from the doctor. Can we go over his diagnosis, antibiotics, oxygen needs, and what would trigger ICU?”**

Good questions to ask Dr. Patel/team:
1. **What staph is it—MSSA or MRSA?**  
2. **What antibiotics is he on now, and do they cover both Strep A and staph/MRSA if needed?**  
3. **Are blood cultures positive or negative?**  
4. **How much oxygen is he requiring, and is that improving or worsening?**  
5. **Is there fluid around the lungs, abscess, or empyema?** Would a **CT scan** help if X-rays are worsening?  
6. **Would an Infectious Disease consult be helpful?** This is a very reasonable request for severe or complicated pneumonia.

Whether he needs ICU depends on things like oxygen level, breathing effort, blood pressure, confusion, kidney function/urine output, and whether he needs high-flow oxygen, BiPAP, or a ventilator. Insurance should **not** determine whether he gets ICU care or necessary antibiotics.

Tell the nurse immediately if he has worsening trouble breathing, blue lips, confusion, extreme sleepiness, chest pain, coughing blood, oxygen levels dropping, very low blood pressure, or decreased urination.

You’re doing the right thing by advocating. Ask them to slow down, write down medication names, and use “teach-back” so you can repeat the plan clearly to his mom.

query: hi, i recently stopped smoking and have inflamed sinuses and since there is a corvid19 pendemic i am worried that these sinuses and runny nose could be linked to coronavirua? my name is melissa ramos, dob 08/14/1987, if that matters for whatever chart thing, and i know i sound paranoid but i have been googling way too much and now every sniffle makes me think its something bad. i stopped smoking pretty recently after doing it for yrs, so part of me is like ok maybe my nose and sinuses are just mad and draining and clearing junk out, but then the runny nose keeps going and my face feels kinda puffy/irritated around my nose and forehead and im like ugh what if this is covid. i dont really have a good sense of what is normal after quitting smoking bc nobody explains that part, like do sinuses get inflamed from that or is it allergies or a cold or coronavirua. i called the office for dr. nina patel but they said to message first unless i have worse stuff, and i dont want to sit in a waiting room with ppl coughing if i dont need to. my phone is 267-555-0198 and email is mel.ramos87@gmail.com if someone needs to tell me what to do, and i usually use cvs on aramingo ave if anything gets called in. i have been trying not to panic, drinking water and using tissues constantly, but the whole corvid19 pendemic has me so nervous. i dont know if just inflamed sinuses and runny nose can be linked to coronavirus or if i should just watch it at home, get tested, or stay away from my family for now. sorry this is rambly, im just anxious and dont wanna ignore something important.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — first, congratulations on stopping smoking. That is a big step, and it’s very common for people to notice more nasal/throat mucus, drainage, coughing, or “irritated” sinuses for a few weeks after quitting. As the tiny clearing hairs in the airways recover, they can start moving old mucus/irritants out more effectively, which can feel like your nose and sinuses are “acting up.”

That said, a runny nose and sinus congestion can also come from allergies, a common cold, or COVID-19. COVID can sometimes present with congestion/runny nose, even without severe symptoms, so it’s reasonable—not paranoid—to consider it.

**What I’d do next:**
1. **Take a home COVID test now** if you have one, especially before being around family. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours or consider a PCR/clinic test depending on availability.
2. **Until you know**, try to mask around others, avoid close contact with high-risk family members, improve ventilation, and wash hands frequently.
3. For symptom relief:  
   - Saline nasal spray or rinse once daily  
   - Humidifier/steam shower  
   - Fluids and rest  
   - If allergies are possible: cetirizine/loratadine or a steroid nasal spray like fluticasone can help, but use as directed  
   - Acetaminophen/ibuprofen if you have sinus discomfort, assuming you can take them safely

**When to contact Dr. Patel’s office or seek care:**  
- Symptoms lasting **more than 10 days**, or improving then suddenly worsening  
- Fever, significant facial/tooth pain, thick foul-smelling nasal drainage  
- Shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, dehydration, or oxygen concerns — these warrant urgent care/emergency evaluation  
- Severe headache, swelling/redness around the eyes, vision changes, or stiff neck — urgent

Based on what you described—runny nose, puffy/irritated sinus area, no mention of fever or breathing trouble—this sounds more like post-smoking airway recovery, allergies, or a mild viral infection than something dangerous, but testing for COVID is the safest way to reduce uncertainty. If symptoms worsen or don’t improve, message/call the office for next steps.

query: hello iam have a very painfull pain in my through since this morning? my name is keisha brown and i dont really know if this is the right place to ask but im kinda nervous and typing fast sorry. my dob 04/18/1992, and if somebody needs to call me my phone is 267-555-0149 but pls text first if u can bc i be at work sometimes and cant pick up. i live at 812 s 9th st apt 3b, philadelphia pa 19147 and im sitting here in my kitchen trying not to panic bc it just started this morning and it is like very painfull pain in my through, like i keep thinking maybe its nothing but then i start worrying. i was gonna wait it out but my pcp dr. maya patel always tells me not to ignore stuff when its really hurting so now im on here asking before i make a whole trip. i dont wanna go sit in urgent care for hours if they just gonna say gargle or whatever, but also i dont wanna be dumb and stay home if i should be seen. i usually pick up meds at cvs on 10th and reed if anything gets sent in, thats close enough for me to walk if i need to. sorry this is messy, i just woke up with it and it has been bothering me all morning and i keep googling and that makes me more scared. can u tell me what i should do for a painfull through pain since this morning and if this sounds like something i need to get checked today?
target_response:
Hi Keisha — I’m glad you reached out, and it’s completely understandable to feel nervous when throat pain comes on suddenly and feels intense. I can’t call or text from here, but I can help you decide what’s safest.

A very painful sore throat starting this morning can be from a virus, COVID/flu, strep throat, irritation/allergies, reflux, or less commonly something more serious like a tonsil abscess. Whether you need to be seen today depends on symptoms and severity.

**Please seek urgent/emergency care now** if you have any of these: trouble breathing, drooling or can’t swallow saliva, severe swelling in the throat/neck, muffled “hot potato” voice, inability to open your mouth fully, severe one-sided throat pain, stiff neck, confusion, rash, chest pain, signs of dehydration, or fever with rapidly worsening symptoms.

If you **can breathe normally and can swallow fluids**, but the pain is “very painful,” I’d recommend **getting checked today or within 24 hours**—especially if you have fever, swollen/tender neck glands, white patches on tonsils, no cough, or known exposure to strep/COVID. Urgent care or your PCP’s office can do a quick throat exam and strep/COVID testing. Antibiotics are only helpful if it’s bacterial, like strep.

While you’re deciding/arranging care, you can try:
- **Acetaminophen 650–1000 mg every 6–8 hours** as needed, max 3000 mg/day unless your doctor says otherwise.  
- Or **ibuprofen 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food** if you’re not pregnant and don’t have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding risk, or blood thinner use.  
- Warm salt-water gargles, warm tea with honey, throat lozenges/sprays, humidifier/steam, and plenty of fluids.  
- Consider a **home COVID test**, and wear a mask around others until you know more.

Also, for your safety, try not to share your full DOB, address, or phone number in online messages unless it’s a secure medical portal.

Bottom line: if this is severe or worsening, I’d lean toward being seen today rather than waiting.

query: i'm a healthcare worker, my name is maria elena rojas, and im seeing walk-in patients and working in emergency department, so im prob overthinking but also i feel like this is exactly how stuff spreads. i work over at temple university hospital, mostly bouncing between triage/fast track and the ed, and i park by 3401 n broad st when im on shift, so im around a ton of ppl all day, random coughs, fevers, families, ppl who dont even know what theyve been exposed to. i have a severe case of flu-like symptoms right now and i feel awful, like the kind where i know i should not be casually standing near patients, but i also dont have any confirmed contact with known covid patients, at least nobody told me they were positive or anything. thats the part making me second guess myself bc employee health keeps sounding like testing is for confirmed exposure or certain criteria, and i dont want to be dramatic or waste a test if scarce, but also i dont want to be the idiot who keeps working and gets a bunch of sick/elderly walk-ins exposed. my supervisor is texting asking if im coming in for my next shift and im just staring at my phone like what am i supposed to do. do my symptoms warrant testing or should i risk spreading my disease? i can be reached at 267-555-0148 if someone needs to call, or email mrojas76@protonmail.com, i just need a clear answer bc i dont want to show up to the emergency department sick just because i cant prove i met a known covid patient. if the right answer is stay home and get tested then ill do it, but i need to know what counts here.
target_response:
Maria, you are **not overreacting**. As a healthcare worker with significant flu-like symptoms who works in triage/fast track/ED around high-risk patients, the safe and appropriate answer is: **do not report to your next shift while symptomatic, and contact Employee Health/Occupational Health now for testing and return-to-work guidance.**

A **known confirmed COVID exposure is not required** for testing or work restriction when you are symptomatic—especially in a healthcare setting. Many respiratory infections spread before a patient is diagnosed, and ED/triage work involves constant exposure to undifferentiated illness. Your symptoms could be COVID, influenza, RSV, or another viral illness, but any of these can be dangerous for elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant, or chronically ill patients.

Practical steps right now:

1. **Text/call your supervisor**: “I have significant flu-like symptoms and should not work around patients. I’m contacting Employee Health for testing and return-to-work instructions.”
2. **Contact Temple Employee Health/Occupational Health** or your institution’s sick-call line and tell them you are a symptomatic ED/triage healthcare worker.
3. **Get tested as directed**—ideally COVID plus influenza testing, and RSV if available/clinically indicated.
4. **Stay home and isolate as much as possible** until you have guidance. Wear a well-fitting mask if you must be around others.
5. **Do not rely on “no known exposure”** as reassurance. In your role, unknown exposure is very plausible.

If testing access is delayed, you still should **not work while acutely symptomatic**, particularly with fever, chills, cough, sore throat, body aches, or significant fatigue.

Seek urgent care or ED evaluation yourself if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, dehydration/inability to keep fluids down, oxygen saturation <94% if you can check it, severe worsening, or persistent high fever**.

Also, since you included personal contact information here, be cautious about sharing phone/email in online medical chats. I can’t call or email you, but the clear clinical guidance is: **stay home, notify your supervisor, contact Employee Health, and get tested.**

query: i think i need to be tested for covid-19? im melissa ramos, dob 04/18/1987, and im sorry if this is dumb but im getting really nervous and i keep going back and forth on whether i should come in or just stay home and wait. i dont wanna clog up the system or waste anyones time but i also dont wanna be that person who ignores it and then finds out later i shouldve been tested. i can be reached at 267-555-0194 or mel.ramos87@gmail.com if someone needs to tell me where to go or if i need an appt first. im at 1816 s 12th st apt 2b right now and i dont have a car so im trying to figure out if i should take a ride share or if thats not ok if im maybe contagious, like i really dont know what the rules are. i was thinking maybe i could go to penn urgent care south philly but idk if they test there or if they just send you somewhere else. also if i do need something called in later my normal place is cvs on passyunk ave, but im not asking for meds i just dont know what info you need. im kinda anxious bc i live with other people and i dont want to freak them out but also dont want to expose them if this is actually covid. do i need a referral or can i just show up? should i call first? and if i should be tested like today, where exactly do i go and how long do results take? sorry this is rambly, i just need clear next step pls.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — this is not dumb at all. You’re doing the right thing by asking before going in, especially since you live with others and don’t have a car.

**Clear next step:** If you have COVID-like symptoms (fever, chills, cough, sore throat, congestion/runny nose, headache, body aches, fatigue, loss of taste/smell, nausea/diarrhea) **or** you had a close exposure, it is reasonable to test. The easiest/safest first option is a **home rapid antigen test today** if you can get one delivered or have a roommate/friend pick one up. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **48 hours** or get a PCR/NAAT test.

If you want in-person testing today, **call first**—don’t just show up. Urgent cares and pharmacies change testing availability frequently, and some require appointments. You can call Penn Urgent Care South Philly or check their website for “COVID testing”/same-day appointments; CVS also often has appointment-based testing. In most cases, **you do not need a referral** for urgent care or pharmacy testing, but they may require scheduling.

**While you’re figuring it out:** act as if you might be contagious. Stay in your room as much as possible, wear a well-fitting mask around roommates, improve ventilation/open windows, don’t share cups/utensils, and wipe high-touch surfaces. Let roommates know calmly: “I may have symptoms/exposure and I’m testing, so I’m masking and staying separate for now.”

**Transportation:** avoid rideshare/public transit if possible. If you must use a rideshare, wear the best mask you have (N95/KN95 if available), sit in the back, keep windows open, avoid conversation, and sanitize hands. If you’re feeling very ill, don’t use rideshare—seek medical advice/911 depending on severity.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips/face, confusion, fainting, severe weakness/dehydration, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse ox, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening.

If you tell us your symptoms, when they started, any exposure, vaccination status, and whether you’re pregnant/immunocompromised or have major medical conditions, we can guide you more specifically—including whether antiviral treatment might be appropriate.

query: i have sle and am currently on 5 day course of dexamethasone 8 mg daily, prescribed by dr. nisha patel, and im trying not to panic but i kinda am. my name is marisa kline dob 03/14/1986, mrn LMC-772904 if that matters, and my blue cross ppo #BCP-4819-22 is usually what they ask me for when i call but i cant get anyone on the phone fast. i still have to work during coronavirus outbreak. how concerned of risk am i? i work in close quarters, like not just the same building but literally desks are a couple of feet apart and people are coughing and touching the printer and fridge and phones and acting like its no big deal. i am not able to work from home, i asked my supervisor twice and they said only managers can for now and everyone else has to come in unless we have a fever, which feels crazy with sle and steroids. i dont want to be dramatic or make trouble at work but i also dont want to end up really sick bc my immune system is already weird and now im on dexamethasone. i picked it up at rite aid on 10th & market and the pharmacist said take it in the morning with food but didnt really say what to do about covid risk besides wash hands. should i be calling my rheum office again, asking for a note, wearing a mask at my desk, staying away from coworkers, or is this not enough steroid to matter? i just need a plain answer bc im sitting here at work feeling dumb and scared and everyone is way too close.
target_response:
Marisa, you are **not being dramatic**. A plain answer: with **SLE** and a **5-day course of dexamethasone 8 mg daily**, your risk from respiratory infections like COVID is **higher than average**, especially in a workplace where people are coughing and sitting only a few feet apart.

Dexamethasone 8 mg is a **meaningful steroid dose**—roughly comparable to a high prednisone dose—though the short 5-day duration is reassuring. It can still temporarily reduce your immune response and may also **blunt fever**, meaning you could be infected without a high temperature early on.

What I would recommend:

1. **Call or message your rheumatology office again today** and specifically ask for a work accommodation note stating that, due to SLE and current systemic steroid use, you should be allowed to work remotely, have strict distancing, or be placed on medical leave if distancing is not possible.
2. **Wear a high-quality mask at your desk**—ideally N95, KN95, or KF94—especially if coworkers are coughing or close by.
3. **Keep as much distance as possible**, avoid shared phones, wipe down printer/fridge handles before touching, wash hands frequently, and avoid touching your face.
4. **Do not stop the dexamethasone suddenly without guidance**, especially since it was prescribed for a reason. Take it in the morning with food as directed.
5. If your workplace refuses accommodation, contact **HR in writing** and explain that you have an autoimmune disease and are temporarily immunosuppressed due to prescribed steroids. You do not need to share every detail, but a doctor’s note helps.

Seek urgent care or call your doctor promptly if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, persistent fever, worsening cough, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below 94%** if you have a pulse oximeter. Also call quickly if you test positive for COVID, because antiviral treatment works best when started early.

Bottom line: your concern is valid. This is enough risk to justify a mask, distancing, and a medical accommodation request—not panic, but definitely proactive protection.

query: how do i know if i have a normal cold or maybe the corona virus. my name is kyle morrison, dob 04/18/1991, and im prob overthinking but i dont want to be dumb about it either. my sinuses are congested and i have a post nasal drip which causes sporadic episodes of dry cough outbreaks. i don’t have a fever, i keep checking like every few hrs bc im anxious, but my body is stiff since i hiked 100km in 4 days.? like my shoulders/back/legs are sore and tight and i keep telling myself thats just from the hike and sleeping weird but then i cough and i start spiraling again. i wasnt around anyone obviously sick that i know of, but we did stop at stores and a gas station and i know that prob counts. i called penn medicine rittenhouse urgent care and they said maybe monitor unless fever/sob but the nurse sounded rushed and i forgot to ask half my questions. if someone needs to look me up my mrn is PMR-772904-B i think, and my phone is 215-555-0946, email k.morrison91@gmail.com, insurance is Aetna PPO #AET-4482017. i dont have chest pain, no fever, just the congestion/post nasal drip and the random dry cough outbreaks when the drip hits the back of my throat. also my throat is kinda scratchy from coughing but not like strep. should i isolate from my roommate or is that too much for just a cold? and does corona usually start with just sinus stuff and no fever or is that more allergy/cold type thing. i just dont know when to actually get tested vs wait it out and drink water/sleep.
target_response:
Hi Kyle — you’re not being dumb; your question is very reasonable. Based on what you described — sinus congestion, post-nasal drip triggering brief dry coughing episodes, scratchy throat, no fever, no chest pain, no shortness of breath — this does sound more like a common cold, allergies/irritant-related congestion, or post-nasal drip than a more concerning lower-respiratory infection. The shoulder/back/leg soreness is very plausibly from hiking 100 km in 4 days plus sleeping differently.

That said, COVID can sometimes start with mild “cold-like” symptoms and no fever, so symptoms alone can’t reliably distinguish it. Since you have a roommate and had some public exposure, the practical approach is:

1. **Take a COVID test now** if you have access to a home rapid test.  
2. If negative but symptoms continue, **repeat a rapid test in 48 hours** — early tests can miss infection. A PCR/NAAT is more sensitive if you need a clearer answer.  
3. Until you know, it’s reasonable to **act cautiously around your roommate**: mask in shared spaces, improve ventilation, avoid sharing drinks/utensils, and sleep separately if feasible. That’s not “too much”; it’s just a temporary precaution.

For symptom care: hydrate, rest, consider saline nasal spray/rinse, warm tea/honey for cough, and an antihistamine like cetirizine/loratadine if allergies are possible. If congestion is significant, a short course of a nasal steroid like fluticasone can help, though it takes a day or two.

You do **not** need to check your temperature every few hours unless you feel worse — once or twice daily is plenty and may reduce the anxiety spiral.

Seek urgent care or medical advice promptly if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen levels <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, persistent fever, worsening cough, dehydration, or symptoms that significantly worsen after initially improving**.

Also, for privacy, avoid sending MRN, insurance, phone, or email in open messages when possible. I can’t look you up here, but your plan to monitor, test, and take precautions is a sensible one.

query:
my 68 yrs old mom linda mae brennan is still waiting for corona virus test results and i keep checking my phone like every 5 mins bc nobody is really telling us anything clear. she’s had 101-102 fever for a week now, like it comes down a little with meds then goes back up and at night it scares me so bad. the ct showed pneumonia and she is at the hospital now at mercy general hospital, they admitted her and i think she is on the regular floor not icu but its all confusing bc we cant all be in there with her and when they call its fast. dr. paola ruiz talked to me once and said they are watching her oxygen and waiting on the corona test, but i dont know if the fever still being there means the pneumonia is getting worse or just that it takes time. her mrn mg-773104 is on the little paper they gave me so i keep saying that when i call but sometimes they still transfer me around forever.

im sorry im rambling im just worried bc she is 68 and she was pretty ok before this and now shes weak and tired and coughing and says she feels cold even with blankets. does the fact that her fever is not getting better means that her pneumonia isn’t getting better? or can pneumonia fever last this long even if antibiotics are starting to work? i dont want to panic but i also dont want to miss something if i should push harder to ask about her lungs or repeat ct or whatever. she is still waiting for corona virus test results and every hour feels like forever.
target_response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. It’s especially hard when you can’t be at the bedside and the updates come quickly. Your worry makes complete sense.

A fever lasting a week **does not automatically mean the pneumonia is getting worse**. With pneumonia—especially if it is viral, including COVID—fevers can persist for several days even after treatment has started. If it is bacterial pneumonia, antibiotics often start helping within **48–72 hours**, but fever and fatigue can still take longer to fully settle. The more important signs the hospital team watches are: her **oxygen level**, whether she needs more oxygen than before, her breathing effort, blood pressure, heart rate, mental status, urine output, and lab trends.

CT showing pneumonia explains the fever, cough, weakness, and chills. Chills can happen when the fever is rising and are not by themselves a sign of ICU-level illness. The fact that she is on a regular floor rather than ICU is somewhat reassuring, but at age 68 she does need close monitoring—which is exactly why admission was appropriate.

You are not “pushing too hard” if you ask for clearer information. When you call, ask to speak with her **bedside nurse** or the **hospitalist/covering doctor**, and consider asking these specific questions:

1. What is her current oxygen saturation, and is she on oxygen? Has it increased?  
2. Are her fever curve and vital signs improving, stable, or worsening?  
3. Is this thought to be viral/COVID, bacterial, or both? Is she on antibiotics?  
4. Do any labs look concerning—white blood count, kidney function, inflammatory markers?  
5. What would make them repeat imaging or escalate her care?  
6. When are COVID results expected, and who will call the family?

It may help to designate **one family contact** and request a scheduled daily update, so you’re not stuck calling repeatedly and getting transferred. If communication remains difficult, ask for the **charge nurse** or patient relations/patient advocate.

Red flags the team would act on urgently include worsening shortness of breath, rising oxygen needs, confusion, low blood pressure, chest pain, bluish lips, inability to stay awake, or very low urine output. Since she’s already admitted, the best thing is to keep asking for clear trend updates rather than focusing only on the fever number.

query: hello sir this is arvind kumar from lucknow ...sir my mom is suffring from lugs pnemonoia.......i am writing again and again because i am very tense and not sleep properly from many days. she was hospitalized 15 days in hospital but ,,,,still she is suffer from pneumonia.....first we admit her in sahara hospital and after discharge also fever and cough and weakness is not going good, she is not able to walk much and taking food very little, so my all family is too much worried. there are many check up has done ...blood test, sputum test, x ray, and during ct scan of thorex a mass of cell is seen in right lungs ......the dr neeraj mishra told us maybe need more test but sir i am confuse because i get test a fnac through usg and ct guided ... but there is no maligant cell are seen in both fnac... her file no they gave is SH-78216 and i keep all reports in one plastic file only, but i dont know which report is most importent. sir i am very worry about this i am not able to undestand what i should do for it......some people saying go for biopsy again, some saying wait and give antibiotic more, some saying cancer center, so my mind is totally disturb. pls tell me if fnac negative then why mass of cell still showing in right lungs, can pneumonia also make like this mass?? sir if you need i can send report photo on whatsapp my no is 98391 44028 but pls guide me what next step i take and which hospital will be best for me ......money also issue but mother is first so i will manage, just dont want wrong treatment and delay more.
target_response:
Dear Arvind ji, I can understand how frightening this is for you and your family. Please don’t feel helpless—this situation needs a clear, step-by-step review by a good pulmonologist, not confusion from many opinions.

A “mass” seen on CT in the right lung does **not always mean cancer**. Pneumonia can sometimes look like a mass due to dense consolidation, lung abscess, TB, fungal infection, or “organizing pneumonia.” However, if fever, cough, poor appetite, and severe weakness are continuing after hospitalization, it should not be ignored.

Also, a **negative FNAC is reassuring but does not 100% rule out malignancy**, because sometimes the needle may sample an inflamed/necrotic area and miss the actual abnormal tissue. In such cases doctors may advise **repeat biopsy (preferably core biopsy), bronchoscopy, or repeat CT after treatment**, depending on the CT appearance and her clinical condition.

What you should do next:

1. **Consult a senior pulmonologist at a tertiary hospital** with all reports and CT films/CD—not only report photos. In Lucknow, options include **KGMU, SGPGI, RMLIMS**, or a reliable chest/pulmonology unit.  
2. Carry these key papers: discharge summary, CT thorax report + CD/films, X-rays, FNAC reports, sputum tests including TB/AFB/CBNAAT if done, blood tests, culture reports, antibiotic details.  
3. Ask the doctor clearly:  
   - Is this non-resolving pneumonia, TB/fungal infection, abscess, or suspected tumor?  
   - Is bronchoscopy needed?  
   - Is core biopsy needed despite negative FNAC?  
   - Are cultures/TB tests complete?  
4. Do not keep changing antibiotics without culture guidance.

At home, ensure small frequent meals, fluids, protein, and gentle walking/physiotherapy as tolerated.

Please seek urgent care immediately if she has worsening breathlessness, oxygen saturation below 92%, chest pain, coughing blood, high persistent fever, confusion/drowsiness, very low urine, or inability to eat/drink.

I cannot access file number SH-78216 or receive WhatsApp here, but if you type the CT impression and FNAC conclusion, I can help you understand the meaning. The most important thing now is prompt review at a tertiary chest center with the CT images.

query: i live in france.and now 7days for home quarantine.i have no fever.but i have parangities in my thoart.last few years it's comes and goes.now i am worried because of covid-19. does only parangities is only symptoms of this??? my name is nabil rahmani, i am staying at 13 rue de la roquette, 75011 paris, and i dont know if i should call anybody or just wait bc everybody here says stay home and dont go out unless emergency. i am in my small flat alone and every little feeling in my thoart makes me panic, like i swallow and it feels scratchy and then i think oh no maybe corona, then i check temp again and still no fever. it is now 7days for home quarantine because i came back from seeing family and also my work told me no office, just stay inside, so i didnt go pharmacie also. i called the office of dr claire moreau before but line was busy and i dont want to bother if this is same old parangities that comes and goes from last few years. i have no chest pain no big fever, only this thoart thing and i cough maybe only when thoart dry, not real cough i think, but still covid-19 on tv all day makes it worse in my head. my email is nabil.r76@orange.fr if they need contact but i am afraid to put info in some websites also. should i isolate more from people even if i already do? can covid start with only parangities and nothing else for 7 days, or if no fever means probably not? pls tell me bc i keep checking mirror and my thoart and i cant sleep good.
target_response:
Dear Nabil,

I’m sorry you’re feeling so anxious—being alone in quarantine with COVID news everywhere can make every throat sensation feel frightening. A scratchy/sore throat (“pharyngitis”) **can** occur with COVID-19, and sometimes it can be an early or mild symptom. However, **a sore throat by itself for 7 days, with no fever, no real cough, no chest pain, and no shortness of breath**, is also very commonly caused by other things—dry air, irritation, allergies/post-nasal drip, reflux, smoking/pollution, or the recurrent pharyngitis you’ve had for years. **No fever makes COVID less likely, but it does not completely rule it out.**

Since you are already staying home, the safest plan is:
1. **Continue isolating and avoiding contact** with others while you have symptoms. Wear a mask if you must open the door or receive deliveries.
2. **Arrange a COVID test** if possible (home antigen test or pharmacy test/delivery/telehealth advice), especially if you will need to return to work or be around others.
3. **Call your GP/Dr Moreau again or use a teleconsultation** rather than going in person. You are not “bothering” them—this is exactly what they are there for.
4. For symptom relief: drink fluids, warm tea/honey, salt-water gargles, throat lozenges, humidify the room, avoid smoke/strong smells, and consider **paracetamol** if painful (follow the package dose; avoid exceeding recommended daily limits).

Please seek urgent medical help in France by calling **15 (SAMU)** or **112** if you develop: difficulty breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion/fainting, severe weakness, persistent high fever, inability to swallow saliva, severe one-sided throat swelling, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

Also, try to limit temperature-checking to 1–2 times daily; repeated checking and mirror-examining can feed panic and worsen sleep. Consider turning off COVID news for set periods and doing slow breathing exercises.

Finally, be careful sharing your address/email on websites—only provide personal details through official medical services.

query: hi, my name is maria lynn torres, dob 04/18/1997, and i've been recently having more nose bleeds lately. 4-5 times per day to be exact. and i have the worst cough ever that comes with it. like it feels so deep and nasty and then my nose just starts going again and i panic bc theres so much blood on tissues and sometimes on my pillow. even when i am asleep my nose starts to bleed and i wake up due to the blood running down my mouth. it sounds gross but thats literally what is happening and its freaking me out, like i wake up coughing and tasting blood and then i cant tell if the cough is making the nose bleed worse or if something else is wrong. is this pneumonia?! or some other illness? i dont have a thermometer right by me so i havent checked a fever right this second, but i feel run down and my chest feels sore probably from coughing so much. i tried sitting up with extra pillows and drinking water and using a little saline spray but it still keeps happening. i was gonna call but i keep talking myself out of it bc i dont wanna be dramatic, but 4-5 times a day seems not normal at all. my call back is 215-555-4089 if someone needs to reach me, i can answer after work usually but if i miss it pls leave a msg. i also usually use CVS on aramingo ave if something needs to be sent in, but i dont even know if this is a meds thing or if i need urgent care or er. i just need to know if pneumonia can cause nose bleeds like this or if coughing blood/nose blood while sleeping is a bad sign bc im honestly scared to go to sleep tonight.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m really sorry you’re dealing with this. You are **not being dramatic**: nosebleeds **4–5 times per day**, especially waking up with blood running into your mouth and a deep cough, deserves **same-day medical evaluation**.

Pneumonia **does not typically cause nosebleeds**. A bad cough can sometimes make a fragile/irritated nasal blood vessel bleed more, and blood from the nose can drain backward while you’re lying down, making you wake up coughing and tasting blood. But we also need to make sure you’re not actually coughing blood from your lungs, and check for infection, low oxygen, anemia, high blood pressure, or a bleeding/clotting issue.

**What I recommend:**  
Please go to **urgent care today** if you are not actively bleeding heavily and you feel stable. If the bleeding is happening again tonight, is heavy, or you feel unsafe going to sleep, it is reasonable to go to the **ER**. You may need a nasal exam/cautery or packing, vitals/oxygen check, lung exam, and possibly a chest X-ray and bloodwork.

**Go to the ER now / call 911 if any of these occur:**
- Bleeding that does not stop after **15–20 minutes of firm pressure**
- Large clots, soaking tissues rapidly, dizziness, fainting, weakness, shortness of breath
- Chest pain, worsening trouble breathing, blue lips, confusion
- Fever with worsening cough, coughing up blood that seems to come from the chest
- You take blood thinners, have a bleeding disorder, or might be pregnant

For a nosebleed: sit upright, lean **forward**, pinch the soft part of your nose continuously for **10–15 minutes**, don’t check repeatedly, and avoid blowing/picking afterward. A small amount of saline is okay, and humidifier/Vaseline just inside the nostrils can help, but with your frequency this needs evaluation. Avoid aspirin/ibuprofen for now unless prescribed, as they can worsen bleeding.

If you contact our office, we can help coordinate next steps, but based on what you described, please seek in-person care **today** rather than waiting.

query: hi this is maria lopez, dob 03/14/1987, sorry if this is a dumb question but im kinda spiraling and googling too much. can the meat i eat give me coronavirus? will cooking kill the coronavirus? like i bought a pack of ground beef and some chicken from kroger on 9th st and now im looking at the packages in my fridge like are they safe?? i know that sounds dramatic but i got home and realized i touched the plastic wrap, the cart, my car keys, then my phone, and then i started making dinner and now im worried i did everything wrong. i dont have fever or anything right now, this is more me worrying bc ive been trying to be careful and then i read something online about viruses being on surfaces and now idk if meat counts as a surface or food or what. if the person at the meat counter coughed or someone handled it before me, can coronavirus be in the meat itself or just on the package? and if i cook the chicken all the way thru like normal is that enough or do i need to do something extra, like wash it, which i also heard not to do?? i feel ridiculous asking but i have kids and i dont wanna feed them something unsafe. i also dont wanna throw away like 40 bucks of food if im just being anxious. my email is mlopez87@gmail.com if someone needs to reply that way but honestly i just need a simple answer like is cooked meat ok and should i just wash my hands and wipe the package or what. sorry again im just nervous.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. A lot of people have had this exact worry, especially after handling groceries and then thinking through every surface they touched.

**Simple answer: yes, cooked meat is OK.** COVID-19 is primarily spread through the air from infected people, not through food. There is **no good evidence that people get COVID from eating meat, chicken, or other foods**. If someone coughed near the package, the more plausible concern would be virus on the **outside packaging**, not “inside” the meat. Even then, surface transmission is considered a much lower-risk route than breathing shared air.

**Cooking is enough.** Normal safe cooking temperatures would inactivate viruses and also protect against common foodborne bacteria:
- Chicken/poultry: **165°F**
- Ground beef: **160°F**
- Steaks/roasts: **145°F with a 3-minute rest**

You do **not** need to do anything extra beyond normal food safety. And you’re right: **don’t wash raw chicken or meat**—that can splash bacteria around the sink/counters and increase food-poisoning risk.

For what to do now:
1. Put the meat in the fridge/freezer as usual.
2. Wash your hands with soap and water before and after handling raw meat or packaging.
3. If you want, wipe the outside of the package or transfer meat to a clean container/freezer bag—optional, not required.
4. Clean any counters/cutting boards/utensils that touched raw meat.
5. Keep raw meat separate from ready-to-eat foods.

You do **not** need to throw away the food unless it smells spoiled, is past safe storage time, or there’s a recall.

Because you mentioned spiraling and Googling: this sounds more like anxiety being triggered than a true food safety emergency. Try to pause the searching, take a few slow breaths, and follow the simple steps above—you’re protecting your kids appropriately.

Seek medical care urgently if you or the kids develop trouble breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, blue lips, severe dehydration, or a high fever that won’t come down. Otherwise, no fever or symptoms right now is reassuring.

query: hi, this is matthew cline, dob 03/14/1982, sorry if this is kinda gross but i dont know who else to ask without making a whole appt. i recently got over either the flu or pnemonia. it knocked me down pretty good for a while, mostly chesty and tired and just felt like junk, but now i feel basically ok. i've noticed my feces are increasingly yellow or whitish. not every single time exactly the same but enough that im looking twice and getting weirded out, like paler than normal and kind of yellowish, and that has me thinking liver/gallbladder stuff even though i really dont feel bad. i had quit smoking thrity days ago and have been on the nicotine gum and now the lozenges. i got the gum first from rite aid on maple ave and then switched to the lozenges cause the gum was bothering my jaw, and now im wondering if that could mess with digestion or bile or whatever. i don't feel bad, but this is unusual. could the nicotine products be contributing to the discoloration? thank you. i did leave a msg for dr. helen park but her nurse said it might be a day or two before i hear back, and im trying not to google myself into panic. no bad belly pain, no fever now, no vomiting, appetite is ok, just this stool color thing and maybe a little more gas than usual but that could be from what im eating after being sick. also i havent smoked at all, so im trying hard not to stop the quit stuff unless i really have to. if somebody needs to reach me or wants pics/notes or whatever my email is mcline82@protonmail.com, but mostly i just want to know if this sounds like normal after being sick/nicotine lozenges or if pale stool is one of those go get seen things.
target_response:
Hi Matthew — first, congrats on 30 days without smoking. That’s a big deal, especially after being sick, and I would not stop the nicotine replacement unless there’s a clear reason to.

Nicotine gum/lozenges can definitely cause GI symptoms — nausea, heartburn, hiccups, extra gas, loose stools, or stomach upset — especially if you swallow a lot of saliva/nicotine or use them frequently. However, they are **not a common cause of truly pale, gray, or white/clay-colored stools**. Yellowish stool can happen from diet changes, faster gut transit after an illness, antibiotics, or recovery from a viral infection. But stool that is repeatedly very pale/whitish can sometimes mean not enough bile is reaching the intestine, which is why people think of liver/gallbladder/bile duct issues.

Since you’re otherwise feeling okay — no abdominal pain, fever, vomiting, appetite is okay — this does **not sound like an immediate panic situation**, but it is worth monitoring closely and discussing with Dr. Park’s office when they call back. If the stool continues to look pale/clay-colored over the next few bowel movements or persists more than a couple days, I’d recommend being seen soon for basic labs: liver enzymes, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, and possibly a gallbladder/liver ultrasound depending on results.

Please seek urgent care sooner if you develop any of these:  
- Yellowing of the eyes/skin  
- Dark tea-colored urine  
- Significant right upper abdominal pain or pain after eating fatty foods  
- Fever/chills returning  
- Persistent vomiting  
- Severe weakness, confusion, or dehydration  
- Black/tarry stool or blood in stool

In the meantime: keep hydrating, eat normally if tolerated, and continue the nicotine lozenges as directed. Try not to chew or swallow them quickly; park them in the cheek and rotate sides, which can reduce stomach upset. It may also help to jot down stool color, diet, any medicines/antibiotics you took during the chest illness, and whether stools are greasy/floating.

Bottom line: nicotine lozenges may explain gas or GI upset, but **recurrent whitish/clay stool should be checked**, especially if it persists or comes with dark urine or jaundice.

query: hi, im mara ellis and i feel dumb asking but this has been going on so long that im kinda losing it. chronic sore throat for 4 months. not strep. no fever. bloody green phlegm for a while, like not pouring blood or anything but streaky gross stuff when i hack it up and it freaks me out every time. sinus congestion and pressure sometimes, esp behind my nose/face, then other days its not that bad and i think ok maybe its going away but nope. bad breath in back of throat sometimes too, like a rotten taste way in the back even after brushing, so maybe post nasal drip? i saw dr. kevin huang at riverbend family care and they swabbed me and said not strep, and i did 2 wks. of amoxicillin and honestly it didn’t help at all, maybe slightly for like one day but then same sore throat again. i picked it up at cvs on elm st and finished it exactly like they said, didnt skip it. im just confused bc no fever and lungs feel basically ok, but the throat and green/bloody phlegm and pressure keep hanging around. is this mild covid-19? i know covid can be weird and mild and i keep reading stuff online which is prob making me more anxious. i dont know if i should ask for another antibiotic, sinus scan, covid test, ent, allergy meds, whatever. i can swallow and breathe fine, its just always sore and gross and annoying and i dont want to keep paying copays if its just post nasal drip but also i dont wanna ignore something serious.
target_response:
Hi Mara — you’re not dumb for asking. Four months of a sore throat plus green/bloody phlegm and facial/nasal pressure is understandably upsetting, and it’s reasonable to want a clearer plan.

A negative strep test and no fever make strep less likely. COVID can cause sore throat and congestion, but **ongoing symptoms for 4 months would be unusual for an active “mild COVID” infection by itself**. If you’ve had a recent exposure or a new flare with new symptoms, a home COVID test or PCR is reasonable, but it may not explain the whole picture.

What you describe most strongly suggests irritation/inflammation coming from the nose/sinuses and draining onto the throat — **post-nasal drip from chronic sinusitis, allergies, or non-allergic rhinitis**. The rotten taste/bad breath can happen with thick drainage, tonsil stones, dental issues, or reflux/LPR. Blood-streaking can occur when the nasal/throat lining is raw from coughing, dryness, or inflammation, but because it’s persistent, it deserves follow-up.

Since you completed 2 weeks of amoxicillin exactly as prescribed and it did not help, I would **not simply repeat the same antibiotic** without a re-check. I’d recommend scheduling with your primary care doctor again or requesting an **ENT referral**. Useful next steps may include:
- Nasal/throat exam, possibly **nasal endoscopy**
- Consideration of **CT scan of the sinuses** if chronic sinusitis is suspected
- Culture-directed treatment if there’s visible drainage
- Assessment for allergies, reflux, tonsil stones, dental sources, and irritants/smoke/vaping exposures

While waiting, you can try:
- **Saline nasal irrigation** daily using distilled/boiled-then-cooled water
- **Fluticasone or similar nasal steroid spray** daily for 2–4 weeks
- Hydration, humidifier, avoiding smoke/vaping
- If reflux symptoms/hoarseness: avoid late meals, alcohol, spicy/acidic foods; consider discussing reflux treatment with your clinician

Seek urgent care sooner if you develop **trouble breathing or swallowing, drooling, severe one-sided throat pain/swelling, neck swelling, high fever, coughing up more than small streaks of blood, chest pain, weight loss, night sweats, or worsening facial pain/swelling/vision changes**.

Bottom line: this is unlikely to be “just strep,” and after 4 months, an ENT workup is appropriate.

query: my 5 year old son mason ellis woke up not feeling well and im trying not to panic but i am honestly scared. im nina ellis, his mom, and if someone needs to call me its 416-555-0198 or email nina.ellis77@gmail.com but i just need some advice first bc i dont know if im over reacting. i measured his temperature and mouth reading is 38.3 and armpit is 38.2, i did it twice because i thought maybe i did it wrong but it was basically the same. he says he’s head is sore and his neck is sore and he keeps kinda laying on the couch not wanting breakfast, which is not like him at all. he is talking to me and drinking little sips of water but he looks tired and glassy eyed and i keep checking his breathing like every 5 min which is probably too much but i cant help it. am scared it may be covid-19? he was at kindy this week and one kid was coughing a lot, no one told us anything official, but now im worried maybe we should all stay home and not see my mum. i have Blue Cross policy BCP-4481029 for him but i dont even know if they cover the covid test or urgent care without a big bill. i can send my husband to Shoppers Drug Mart on Queen st for a test or meds but im not sure if i should give him anything first or wait to speak to someone. his neck being sore is what really scares me, like is that normal with fever or is that something emergency? sorry this is long i just dont want to miss something serious.
pii_units: Shoppers Drug Mart on Queen st
target_response:
Nina, you’re not overreacting. Mason’s temperature (38.2–38.3°C) is a real fever, and fever with headache and neck pain in a 5-year-old deserves careful attention.

COVID-19 is certainly possible, especially with a coughing child at kindergarten, but many viruses can cause fever, headache, tiredness, poor appetite, and body/neck aches. The part that needs caution is the **neck soreness**: it can be from viral muscle aches, but we also watch for more serious infections like meningitis.

**What I’d do now:**
1. **Keep him home and isolate from others**, and do not visit your mum until you know more and he is improving. A rapid COVID test from the pharmacy is reasonable, but don’t let testing delay medical care if he worsens.
2. **Call your child’s doctor/after-hours clinic or Ontario Health811 today** for guidance. Given fever + headache + neck pain, I would want him assessed the same day if the neck pain is significant or persistent.
3. You can give fever/pain medicine now if he can swallow and has no allergy:
   - **Acetaminophen (Tylenol)** or **ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)** dosed by his weight according to the bottle.  
   - Do **not** give aspirin.
4. Encourage small frequent sips of water or oral rehydration fluid. Don’t worry about food today.

**Go to the ER now / call emergency services if any of these are present:**
- Neck is truly stiff: he can’t comfortably bend his chin toward his chest, or moving the neck causes significant pain
- Confusion, extreme sleepiness, hard to wake, unusual behaviour
- Severe or worsening headache, persistent vomiting
- Rash that doesn’t fade when pressed, purple spots
- Trouble breathing, blue lips, chest pulling in
- Seizure
- Signs of dehydration: no urine for 8–10 hours, very dry mouth, no tears

Also, don’t worry about checking his breathing every 5 minutes unless it looks laboured; instead watch for clear signs of distress. Regarding insurance/testing costs, the safest medical decision shouldn’t wait on coverage—urgent care/ER can advise, and Blue Cross can clarify billing separately.

For privacy, avoid sharing policy numbers/contact details in open messages when possible.

query: i'm not sure, my temperature is 36.5, i have some chills but not significant but i yawn a lot and my breath is short, feels like i have to breath more, i'm laying down breathing through the nose is easier. i guess i should say my name is daniel morris bc if this needs to be matched to anything, and the number on my chart should be 267-555-0148 but i dont even know if that matters here. i keep checking myself and then i think im making it worse by thinking about it, like i yawn and then i notice i need to take a bigger breath and then i start wondering if im breathing wrong. i dont have a big fever or anything, its still 36.5 when i check, and the chills are there but not crazy, more like off and on and i cant tell if its from anxiety or being tired or what. when im sitting up i feel like i have to breath more, like not exactly pain, just short, and when i lay down and breath through my nose it is easier which is weird and makes me not know if its serious. i messaged dr. leena patel before but i dont know if she will see it fast and i dont want to go in if this is just me panicking. i have been yawning a lot all evening and then i keep trying to take deep breaths to “test” it, which probably isnt smart. no idea if i should wait, call someone, or just try to sleep, but im nervous about the short breath feeling even tho the temp is normal-ish and the chills arent significant. sorry this is rambling, i just dont know what detail is important.
target_response:
Hi Daniel — I’m sorry you’re feeling this way. The details you gave are helpful, and it’s understandable that the “need to take a bigger breath” feeling would make you nervous.

A temperature of 36.5°C is normal, and mild on/off chills without fever can happen with anxiety, fatigue, early viral illness, or even being tense/cold. Frequent yawning and repeatedly “testing” your breathing can also create a cycle where you notice every breath, take deeper breaths, feel unsatisfied, and then feel more anxious. The fact that nasal breathing while lying down feels easier is somewhat reassuring, but any new shortness-of-breath sensation deserves attention if it persists or worsens.

For the next 20–30 minutes, try not to keep checking your temperature or forcing deep breaths. Sit or lie in the position that feels easiest. Breathe gently through your nose: inhale normally for about 3–4 seconds, exhale slowly for 5–6 seconds, and repeat. Sip water, loosen tight clothing, and avoid caffeine/nicotine if relevant. If you have a pulse oximeter, check once while resting: oxygen ≥95% is generally reassuring; 92–94% should prompt a call for advice; ≤91% or worsening symptoms warrants urgent care.

Please seek urgent/emergency care now if you have any of the following: trouble speaking full sentences, bluish lips/face, chest pain/pressure, fainting, confusion, severe weakness, wheezing/stridor, coughing blood, one-sided leg swelling/pain, oxygen low as above, or shortness of breath that is rapidly worsening or present even at rest.

If none of those are present but the breathing discomfort continues tonight, is new for you, or you can’t settle enough to sleep, I’d recommend calling your clinic’s after-hours line, an on-call nurse/doctor, or urgent care for real-time triage rather than just waiting for Dr. Patel’s message. If it improves with calming breathing and rest, it’s reasonable to monitor, but arrange follow-up if chills, cough, fever, or recurrent shortness of breath develops.

You’re not rambling — you’re paying attention to symptoms. The key is not to repeatedly test your breathing, and to get help promptly if it becomes objectively harder to breathe.

query: will my mask from sherwin williams paint store with filters protect me from corona virus along with paint fumes? im jason miller and i know this is prob a dumb question but im kinda stuck overthinking it bc i do painting and little repair jobs for HomePro Renovations and i got this mask at the sherwin williams paint store by 1420 n broad st, its the kind with the two side filters and it smells like rubber and i usually use it when im rolling primer or sanding or dealing with paint fumes. i dont know if that means it does anything for corona virus tho or if its only for fumes and dust and stuff. i keep hearing masks help but then other ppl say only certain masks help and now im confused. like if im going into a house to paint a room and somebody there coughs, will this mask actually protect me or am i just wearing a paint mask and thinking im safer than i am. i also dont wanna be breathing paint fumes all day either bc that gives me a headache sometimes, so im trying to figure out if one mask can do both things or if i need a different one. i can call back if needed my cell is 610-555-0148, but really i just need a plain answer bc the labels on the filters dont make sense to me and the guys at the store were busy. also if the filters are older or been used for paint already does that make it worse for corona virus? im not sick right now, just trying not to get sick and not breathe fumes while working, sorry for rambling im just worried and dont wanna mess this up.
target_response:
Hi Jason — not a dumb question at all. The plain answer is: **your paint-store respirator may protect you from coronavirus *only if* the cartridges/filters on it include a particulate rating like N95, N100, P95, or P100, and it seals well to your face.** If the cartridges are **only “organic vapor” cartridges** for paint fumes, they help with solvents/fumes but **do not reliably filter virus-containing aerosols**.

For painting/repair work, people often use a half-face respirator with two side cartridges. What it protects against depends on the exact cartridge:

- **Organic vapor (OV)** = paint/solvent fumes, odors  
- **Particulate filter N95/P100/HEPA** = dust, sanding particles, and virus-sized aerosol particles  
- **Combination OV/P100 cartridges** = a good “one mask can do both” option for many painting/sanding situations and COVID-risk reduction

So check the label on the filters/cartridges. If you see **P100** or **N95/N100**, that’s the part that helps with coronavirus. If you only see **OV** or “organic vapor,” it’s for fumes, not infection protection.

If someone coughs in a house you’re working in, the respirator can reduce your risk **if it has the right particulate filters and fits tightly**, but it is not a guarantee. A good seal matters: no facial hair where the mask seals, straps snug, and do a seal check each time. Eye protection also helps because droplets can contact the eyes.

Older/used filters: if they are dirty, damaged, hard to breathe through, expired, or you can smell paint/solvents through them, replace them. Using them for paint does not automatically make them useless for COVID if they are combination filters, but cartridges can become saturated or clogged.

For paint fumes specifically, also use **ventilation**—open windows, fans exhausting outdoors, and take breaks. Headaches with fumes can mean your cartridge is wrong, saturated, or the area is under-ventilated.

Seek urgent care if you develop severe shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, fainting, or severe headache/dizziness after chemical exposure. If your employer provides the respirator, they should help confirm the correct cartridges and fit.

query:
hi, sorry this is prob a dumb question but im getting myself worked up bc everyone keeps saying diff stuff online. my daughter mila rosen turned 1 yr on 20 march, her dob is 20/03/2019, and in the last couple months when she had fever above 38 deg celsius we alternated between 5ml nurofen and 125mg empaped (paracetamol) suppository every 4 hours till temp broke. that was what we had written down from dr lena hoffman at northside family clinic when we asked before, and we bought the nurofen from boots on high street so it wasnt like random internet meds or anything. but now with covid19 im seeing posts saying ibuprofen is bad or makes it worse and then other ppl saying no its fake news, and im just confused and dont wanna mess up with a 1 yr old.

she isnt burning up right this second, just a bit warm on and off and teething maybe, but if she goes above 38 again tonight i dont know if nurofen is safe now regarding ibuprofen and covid19? like should i only use the empaped suppository and avoid nurofen totally, or is it still ok if paracetamol doesnt bring it down? also we already did the every 4 hours thing before when temp broke, but now im second guessing that too bc she is so little and im scared of giving too much even though it worked. she is drinking ok-ish and wet nappies are normal, no big cough just tiny sniffles, but bc covid is everywhere i panic at every temp. i tried calling but lines are mad busy and i dont want to bring her in unless needed. pls just tell me if nurofen is safe now or if we should stop it for covid19 worries.
target_response:
Hi — not a dumb question at all. It’s completely understandable to feel anxious with mixed messages online, especially with a 1-year-old.

**Bottom line:** current medical advice is that **ibuprofen (Nurofen) is not proven to worsen COVID-19**, and it can still be used in children when appropriate. Many clinicians still suggest **paracetamol first** for fever or discomfort, but **ibuprofen is acceptable if she remains uncomfortable or the fever isn’t settling**, provided she is drinking reasonably and has no contraindications.

A few important safety points:

- Fever itself doesn’t always need treating — treat **Mila’s discomfort**, not just the number.
- **Paracetamol suppository 125 mg** is usually given every **4–6 hours**, with a maximum of **4 doses in 24 hours**.
- **Nurofen/ibuprofen** dosing depends on the strength and her weight. Many children’s Nurofen products are **100 mg/5 mL**; 5 mL may be appropriate for some 1-year-olds, but please check the bottle’s age/weight dosing. Ibuprofen is usually given every **6–8 hours**, not every 4 hours, with a maximum daily limit.
- Alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen can be done, but it’s easy to accidentally give too much. If you do alternate, write down the **time, medicine, and dose** each time. Don’t give them automatically “until the temp breaks” if she’s otherwise comfortable.

Avoid ibuprofen if she is **dehydrated**, vomiting a lot, not drinking, has kidney disease, has had a bad reaction to ibuprofen before, or has chickenpox.

Tonight, if she goes above 38°C but is drinking okay, having normal wet nappies, breathing comfortably, and not very distressed, you can monitor, offer fluids, keep her lightly dressed, and use paracetamol if needed. If that doesn’t help and she’s miserable, Nurofen is reasonable if dosed correctly.

Seek urgent medical help if she has breathing difficulty, becomes very drowsy/floppy, has a non-blanching rash, seizure, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, blue lips, severe irritability, or fever lasting more than 3 days or very high fever. Given the sniffles/possible COVID concern, follow local isolation/testing advice and phone ahead before attending.

query: good afternoon, i recently visited a client, melissa grant, for a quick work thing and now im sitting here overthinking every part of it. i was at her house on 11/03 at 2:30pm, not for very long, maybe like an hour or a little more, and i didnt think anything of it at the time bc nobody looked sick or was coughing or anything. then later she told me her husband dr. omar patel works in the same practice as a confirmed covid case at northside family practice, and that is what got me worried. two days after the visit i fell ill, gradually getting a sore throat, fever and feeling tired. it wasnt like all at once, it just sort of crept up on me and now i keep wondering if im being dramatic or if this is exactly the kind of thing people are supposed to call about. i dont want to waste a test if they are scarce but i also dont want to be stupid and go around anyone if this could be covid. i have been staying in my room mostly and trying not to get close to my family, wiping stuff down, washing hands a ton, but its hard to know what is enough. i dont really have anyone else to ask and the news is making it worse bc every symptom list says sore throat, fever, tired etc and then some say thats not enough. should i get tested? or just stay home and monitor unless breathing gets bad? also how long after an exposure would testing even show it, since i got sick two days after?
target_response:
Good afternoon — you are not being dramatic. With fever, sore throat, and fatigue, it is very reasonable to think about COVID and to take precautions, even though the exposure you describe is somewhat indirect.

A few key points:

- Being in Melissa’s home for about an hour could count as a meaningful indoor exposure **if someone there was infectious**, but from what you wrote, the known COVID case was at her husband’s workplace, not necessarily in the home. So your risk is not zero, but it is not the same as being face-to-face with a confirmed positive person.
- COVID symptoms can start anywhere from about **2–14 days after exposure**, most often around days 3–5. Getting sick two days later is possible, but it also could be another viral illness, flu, or even strep depending on your symptoms.

Yes — given that you now have fever and respiratory/throat symptoms, I would recommend testing rather than waiting only to see if breathing worsens. If you have access to a home rapid antigen test, take one now. If it is negative, repeat it in **24–48 hours**, because early tests can miss infection. A PCR/NAAT test is more sensitive and is also reasonable, especially if you need a clearer answer for work or family exposure.

In the meantime, you are doing the right things: stay in your room as much as possible, wear a well-fitting mask if you need to be around family, improve ventilation if you can, avoid sharing cups/utensils, wash hands, and clean high-touch surfaces. Try to use a separate bathroom if available.

You should stay home until you are fever-free for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine and symptoms are improving; if COVID is confirmed, follow current local isolation guidance and wear a mask around others for several additional days.

Seek urgent medical care now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below 92% if you have a pulse oximeter. Also contact your clinician if fever persists more than 3 days, sore throat is severe with swollen glands/no cough, or you are high-risk for complications.

query:
i heard ace inhibitors might exacerbate coronavirus. i also heard nsaids might do the same. does this mean any common meds could have an unexpected effect during coronavirus? im asking bc im not a dr and all the news is making me second guess like every basic thing in the cabinet now, even stuff ppl take forever and dont think about. my name is maria elena cruz, dob 03/14/1981, and i was gonna message dr. nathan bell about it but the portal is being weird and i dont wanna sound dumb if this is just internet panic. like if someone is on an ace inhibitor for bp or takes advil/ibuprofen here and there, is the idea that it actually makes coronavirus worse, or is it more like nobody knows and ppl are just guessing? i saw one post that said stop them and another said absolutely dont stop them, so now im confused and kinda anxious.

my family keeps texting me links and half of them are from facebook so i dont know what to believe. i have kaiser permanente silver #KP-4489201 but i dont want to book a visit if the answer is basically dont change anything unless your own dr says so. also i usually pick up meds at CVS on 19th ave so i was gonna ask the pharmacist but they looked slammed last time and i didnt wanna stand around coughing ppl just to ask a theoretical question. im not trying to start/stop anything on my own, just wondering if coronavirus can make normal meds act differently or make side effects worse, like nsaids, bp meds, allergy meds, stomach meds, all that regular stuff. is there a list of common meds to avoid or is that not really a thing yet?
target_response:
Hi Maria Elena — this is a very reasonable question, not “dumb” at all. Early in the pandemic there were a lot of theoretical concerns and social-media posts about common medicines, and some of them were overstated.

**ACE inhibitors/ARBs for blood pressure:** Current evidence and major medical societies recommend **not stopping ACE inhibitors or ARBs** because of COVID concerns. Examples include lisinopril, benazepril, enalapril, losartan, valsartan. Stopping them suddenly can raise blood pressure or worsen heart/kidney conditions. If someone with COVID becomes very dehydrated, has low blood pressure, or kidney problems, their clinician may temporarily adjust medications—but that is individualized medical care, not a blanket rule.

**NSAIDs like Advil/ibuprofen:** The early concern that ibuprofen “makes COVID worse” has not been proven. For fever/body aches, **acetaminophen/Tylenol is often a good first choice**, but ibuprofen is generally reasonable for many people if taken as directed. Avoid or ask a clinician first if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are on blood thinners, have significant heart failure, are pregnant later-term, or have uncontrolled blood pressure.
