Because he is higher risk due to age, if he tests positive, contact his doctor promptly. Antiviral treatment such as Paxlovid is most useful when started early, typically within 5 days of symptom onset, and a clinician would need to review medications and kidney function.

Please seek urgent care or ER evaluation if he has **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, worsening weakness, or oxygen saturation below 94%**.

One more note: since you included personal details like DOB, insurance, and phone number, it’s best not to share those in general messages unless you’re in a secure clinic portal. For now, using the portal with his symptoms and testing question is very reasonable.

query: hi, its lauren mckinney, dob 09/14/1986, and i was recently diagnosed with pneumonia and i am still kind of in shock about it. i had pneumonia when i was a baby but never since. i was shocked when i heard the diagnosis, like i honestly thought it was maybe allergies or some random chest cold bc i never get anything. the symptoms became apparent on march 28, and i kept telling myself it would pass, but then by the time i saw dr. elena cruz at northside family clinic i was wiped out and scared. i began taking doxycycline monohydrate on monday april 3 and must take them for seven days, i picked it up at cvs on 8th st and have been taking it like it says, with water and trying not to miss any doses. i am mega healthy and have not been sick in years. i can t even remember the last time i was sick. this has really knocked me out. i have zero energy and very little appetite, like i stand up to make toast and then i just want to sit back down. i keep wondering how this even happened to me bc i work out, eat pretty good, dont smoke, and i feel like my body just betrayed me. how does pneumonia manifest in the lung and how does bacteria get in there? is it like i breathed it in and it just settled or was it already in me somehow? how long will it be until i recover? i keep thinking ok tomorrow ill feel normal and then i dont. i am really having a hard time with this. help!
target_response:
Hi Lauren — I’m really sorry you’re going through this. Pneumonia can feel frightening and surprisingly overwhelming, especially when you’re someone who is generally healthy and rarely gets sick. What you’re describing — profound fatigue, low appetite, getting winded or wiped out with small tasks — is very common with pneumonia and does **not** mean your body failed you.

Pneumonia happens when germs reach the small air sacs in the lungs, called alveoli. Those air sacs are where oxygen normally passes into the bloodstream. With pneumonia, bacteria trigger inflammation and fluid/pus can collect in those areas, which makes breathing feel harder and makes your whole body work much harder. Bacteria can get there in a few ways: by breathing in droplets from someone else, from bacteria that normally live in the nose/throat and get “aspirated” down into the lungs, or after a viral illness irritates the airways and makes it easier for bacteria to take hold. Even very healthy people can get it.

Since your symptoms became apparent March 28 and you started doxycycline April 3, it’s reasonable that you still don’t feel normal yet. Many people start to notice some improvement after **48–72 hours on antibiotics**, but energy and appetite often take **1–3 weeks** to meaningfully recover, and cough/fatigue can linger **several weeks**. Try to think in terms of gradual improvement, not waking up suddenly back to normal.

Keep taking the doxycycline exactly as prescribed and finish the 7-day course unless Dr. Cruz tells you otherwise. Take it with a full glass of water and stay upright for at least 30 minutes afterward. Avoid taking it at the same time as calcium, iron, magnesium, antacids, or multivitamins, and be mindful of sun sensitivity.

For now: rest aggressively, hydrate, eat small easy foods, and resume exercise slowly only once you’re clearly improving.

Please contact Dr. Cruz/Northside promptly if you are not improving after 3 days on antibiotics, or if symptoms worsen. Seek urgent care/ER now for trouble breathing at rest, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, coughing blood, persistent high fever, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, or oxygen levels under ~92% if you’re checking.

query: i am melissa gannon and i am recovering from double bilateral pneumonia in hospital 3 days at st marys medical center and i still feel like i got hit by a truck honestly. i m an rt so i knew i had to use incentive spirometer and nebulizers for 2 weeks and i did not mess around with it, i keep it next to the couch and keep doing it even when i dont feel like it. i am 2 weeks post discharge now and i keep thinking i should be better faster bc i work around this stuff but when its you its different and scary. i have to wear 02 at night only .5 to. 1 liter to keep sats above 90 and i keep waking up checking the little pulse ox like a crazy person. during the day i occasionally need o2 if i get up too much or try to shower or make food, then i sit there mad at myself. dr. leah norris said to rest and not push but i am on medical leave for 4 weeks and i keep worrying what if 4 weeks is not enough and what if i cant go back full speed. my blue cross policy #bc-4472091 paperwork is sitting on my kitchen table and even that is stressing me out. is this going to get easier to breath soon i am resting as much as possible. i don t have much choice rather rest than feel pain i. chest. i dont have a big fever now and im trying to eat and drink but the tightness and tired feeling is just so much, like walking to the bathroom makes me feel old. i know lungs take time but i guess i need to hear if this is normal-ish at 2 weeks or if i should be calling again.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — what you’re describing sounds very understandable after **bilateral pneumonia significant enough to require hospitalization**, and I’m sorry you’re in that “I should be better by now” headspace. Even as an RT, it’s different when it’s your own lungs and your own oxygen numbers.

At **2 weeks post-discharge**, it is still very common to feel profoundly wiped out — like walking to the bathroom or showering is a major event. Pneumonia recovery is often measured in **weeks to months**, not days. The infection may be controlled, but the lung inflammation, airway irritation, deconditioning, and pleuritic chest discomfort can lag behind. Fatigue and exertional shortness of breath can be especially intense after **double/bilateral pneumonia**.

That said, since you’re still needing **0.5–1 L oxygen at night** and occasionally during the day with activity, I do think it’s reasonable to **check in with Dr. Norris or the hospital follow-up team**, not because you’re necessarily “doing badly,” but because they may want to reassess your oxygen plan, lung exam, medications, and whether you need follow-up imaging or labs. Many people need longer than 4 weeks before returning full-speed, and medical leave can often be extended if clinically needed. Try not to let the paperwork sit there like a second illness — ask the office if they can help complete disability/leave forms based on your hospitalization and ongoing oxygen requirement.

A few practical points: keep using the incentive spirometer and nebs as prescribed, but also pace yourself. Think “short frequent movement” rather than pushing through. Sit for showers if needed. Hydrate, prioritize protein/calories, and allow naps. For the pulse ox, consider setting specific check times — for example, before bed, if you feel worse, and after activity recovery — rather than checking repeatedly overnight unless instructed.

Please seek urgent care/ER if you develop: worsening shortness of breath at rest, sats persistently **<90% despite your prescribed O2**, new or high fever, coughing blood, new/worsening one-sided chest pain, fainting, confusion, blue lips, racing heart that won’t settle, or calf swelling/pain.

Bottom line: yes, this can be “normal-ish” at 2 weeks after severe bilateral pneumonia — but given ongoing oxygen needs, calling for follow-up is appropriate and not overreacting.

query: hi, i’m maria elena cruz, dob 03/14/1992, and i have a sore throat, slight cough, tiredness. should i get tested fro covid 19? i know that prob sounds like a basic question but im kinda anxious bc i keep reading different stuff and i dont wanna overreact but also dont wanna be dumb about it. the sore throat is the main thing, like scratchy and annoying, not like i cant swallow or anything, and the cough is slight, just here and there, but the tiredness is making me worry bc i dont usually feel this wiped out from just normal allergies. i havent been trying to self diagnose but everyone at home keeps asking if i took a test yet and now im second guessing. i can be reached at 267-555-0198 if someone needs to call me back, but honestly i just need to know if this is enough symptoms to go get tested or if i should wait and see. i was gonna stop by cvs on 9th and market to grab a home test if that makes sense, but if i need the more official test then i dont wanna waste time/money doing the wrong thing. no big fever that i know of, just feeling blah and throat hurts and little cough. im also worried about going around other ppl if it could be covid even tho the symptoms seem mild. should i stay home until i test? and if i test negative once do i still need to test again later? sorry this is rambly, im just tired and my throat is bugging me and i dont know what the correct covid rules even are anymore.
target_response:
Hi Maria Elena — your question is completely reasonable, and you’re not overreacting. A sore throat, cough, and unusual fatigue **are enough symptoms to justify testing for COVID-19**, even if they’re mild and you don’t have a fever.

A **home rapid antigen test from CVS is a good first step** and is usually the most practical option. If it’s positive, you can assume you have COVID and should stay home and avoid exposing others. If it’s negative, it does **not** always rule COVID out early in the illness. If symptoms started recently, I’d recommend **repeating a home test in 48 hours**. If you need a more definitive answer sooner—such as for work, travel, high-risk household members, or you’re getting worse—a **PCR/NAAT test** is more sensitive.

Until you test and while you’re feeling sick, it’s safest to **stay home if you can**, avoid close contact with others, and wear a well-fitting mask if you need to be around people. Current general guidance for respiratory viruses is to stay home until symptoms are improving and you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medication, then take extra precautions like masking and distancing for about 5 more days.

Your symptoms could also be from a non-COVID viral cold, flu, allergies with post-nasal drip, or less likely strep throat. Strep is more likely with fever, swollen/tender neck glands, tonsil spots, and no cough.

For now: hydrate, rest, use warm tea/honey, throat lozenges, saline gargles, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely.

Please seek urgent care now or promptly if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, dehydration, inability to swallow saliva, severe worsening throat pain, persistent high fever, or symptoms that rapidly worsen. Also contact a clinician quickly if you test positive and are at higher risk for severe illness, because antivirals work best early.

Bottom line: yes—grab the home test today, stay home while symptomatic, and repeat in 48 hours if negative.

query: good morning i have all the symptoms for the coronavirus except a high fewer and im prob overthinking but i cant stop checking every little thing. my name is emily grossman and my cell is 267-555-0148 if someone needs to call bc i dont really know if this is the kind of thing i should be seen for or just stay home and not bother anyone. i have the cough, kinda tight chest feeling, sore throat, body aches and i feel wiped out, but my temp is not high like everyone keeps saying it should be, its just been normal-ish and then i feel hot and cold off and on. i have been in contact with someone, jessica moran, who now also are displaying these symptoms, like she texted me saying she is coughing and feels gross too. she are staying with a person, rahul mehta, who visited india in the last few weeks, and i know that doesnt automatically mean anything but now my brain is going there bc of the news and ppl at work talking about it. should i be worried? like do i need to call before going anywhere, should i be wearing a mask inside my house, and should i not go near my mom for now? i havent had a high fewer so im confused if that means its prob not coronavirus or if you can still have it without that. i dont want to panic or be dramatic but also i dont want to be the person who ignores it and gets other ppl sick. i was around them recently enough that it feels relevant and im just sitting here trying to figure out if this is urgent or if i should just watch it, drink fluids, and isolate. sorry this is long, im anxious this morning.
target_response:
Good morning, Emily — I’m sorry you’re feeling so anxious and unwell. You are **not** being dramatic. With cough, sore throat, body aches, fatigue, hot/cold spells, and chest tightness—especially after being around someone who is also now symptomatic—COVID is a real possibility, even **without a high fever**. Many people with COVID have no fever or only mild/normal temperature changes. Other viral infections like flu, RSV, or a common cold can look very similar.

For now, I’d recommend you **act as if you may be contagious**:

- **Stay home and avoid work/school/public places** until you’ve tested and symptoms are improving.  
- **Take a COVID test** if you have one; if negative early on, repeat in 24–48 hours. Consider flu testing too, especially if symptoms started recently.  
- **Call your primary care office or an urgent care before going in**, particularly because you mention chest tightness. They can advise whether you should be seen, tested, or managed at home.  
- **Wear a well-fitting mask inside the house** if you must be around others.  
- **Avoid close contact with your mom for now**, especially if she is older, pregnant, immunocompromised, or has lung/heart conditions. Use a separate room/bathroom if possible, improve ventilation, don’t share cups/utensils, and wash hands often.  

The travel connection you mentioned doesn’t automatically mean anything by itself, but your recent contact with someone symptomatic does make it reasonable to be cautious.

For symptom care: fluids, rest, acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them, warm tea/honey for throat/cough, and monitor your breathing. If you have a pulse oximeter, check oxygen levels.

Please seek **urgent/emergency care now** if you develop: worsening or persistent chest pain/pressure, trouble breathing, blue/gray lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen levels under 92%, severe weakness, dehydration, or symptoms that rapidly worsen.

Also, for privacy, it’s best not to include your cell number or other people’s full names in messages unless you’re in a secure medical portal.

query: hi, i have a friend who is a female, age 16, her name is maddie r. coleman and im asking bc im really worried and she keeps saying shes fine but she does not sound fine to me at all. she has complaints of light-headedness, migraines, blurry vision, can t breathe in deep without coughing, complains of body aches, chills, and a fever. like she will try to take a deep breath and then starts coughing and then says her chest hurts from coughing, and then she gets dizzy again. i dont live with her but we have been texting all day and she called me from 215-555-9048 and sounded really weak and kinda out of it, not like herself. she is going to the er tomorrow because they are unable to go today due to being snowed in, their driveway and road are totally blocked and her mom said the plow has not come by yet. they were talking about going to st. luke's easton er at 801 ostrum st as soon as they can get out, probably early morning if the snow lets up. i know im not her parent or anything, i just dont wanna tell her the wrong thing or scare her more. it sounds like pneumonia but not sure. she said they tested her temp at home and its a fever and shes had chills under like 3 blankets, and body aches too, and the migraine/blurry vision part is what is making me panic more than the cough honestly. her mom said she has highmark bcbs #HMB-4429017 so i guess they can check in ok, but they dont want an ambulance unless it gets super bad bc roads are awful. should they wait til tomorrow or is this like call 911 now if she can t breathe deep without coughing? thank you for your help
target_response:
You’re right to take this seriously. I can’t diagnose Maddie over text, but **fever/chills/body aches plus cough/chest pain, dizziness, blurry vision/severe headache, and sounding weak or “out of it”** can be more than a routine cold. Possibilities include flu/COVID, pneumonia, dehydration, asthma/bronchospasm, or—less commonly but importantly—serious infections or low oxygen.

**If she is currently having trouble breathing, seems confused/out of it, is too weak to sit/stand, has worsening chest pain, bluish lips/face, fainting, or severe headache with blurry vision, they should call 911 now rather than wait until tomorrow.** Being snowed in is exactly the kind of situation where EMS/fire/rescue can help coordinate access, plowing, or safe transport if needed. Insurance concerns should not delay emergency care.

If she is awake, speaking normally, breathing comfortably at rest, drinking fluids, and not worsening, her mom should still call her pediatrician’s after-hours line or a nurse line tonight for guidance and plan to go as soon as roads are passable. But based on what you described—especially “weak and out of it” and dizziness with breathing symptoms—I’d have a **low threshold for 911 tonight**.

While waiting for help or transport:
- Have her **sit upright**, rest, and avoid exertion.
- Encourage **small frequent sips** of water/electrolyte drink.
- Use **acetaminophen or ibuprofen** for fever/aches if she can take them safely; **no aspirin**.
- If they have a pulse oximeter: **oxygen under 94%** is concerning; **90% or lower is an emergency**.
- If possible, monitor temperature, breathing rate, and whether she can speak full sentences.

Other urgent red flags: stiff neck, new rash/purple spots, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, fever around **104°F**, severe worsening headache, new neurological symptoms, or coughing up blood.

Also, you’re being a good friend—but avoid sharing her personal details like phone/insurance info online. The most helpful thing now is to tell her mom clearly: **“She sounded weak/out of it to me, and I’m worried she needs emergency help tonight.”**

query: hi, i am the main member of discovery, my name is melissa van der merwe and i am just trying to figure this out because the app is making me go in circles and im not sure if im clicking the wrong place or if its only showing under my profile. i want to complete a health covid assessment for my husband andrew van der merwe? please let me know how to do so? he is my dependent under Discovery Health #DH-78294413, but when i log in it keeps asking questions like its for me and i dont want to submit it wrong and then we have a problem later or they say the answers dont match him. i tried on my phone and then again on the laptop and same story, maybe im missing the family/dependents tab or maybe it must be done from his own login, but he doesnt really use all these online medical things and asked me to do it. he has been worried about covid exposure and i just want to get the assessment done properly before we decide if he must go for testing or stay home or call someone. if someone can send the steps in simple way that would help alot, like do i go to covid hub, health assessment, then choose dependent, or must i first add consent for him somewhere? you can reply here or call me on 083-417-9026 if its easier, or email mel.vdw82@gmail.com, i just need to know how to do this today because i dont want to wait and then find out it was sitting in the wrong section. sorry if this is a silly question but im the main member and im trying to do it for my husband, not for me.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a silly question at all. You’re right to be careful, because COVID/health assessments are usually linked to the profile of the person being assessed, and submitting Andrew’s answers under your profile could cause confusion later.

I can’t access Discovery’s system from here or complete it for you, but generally the correct approach is:

1. **Log in to the Discovery app or website**
2. Go to **Health / COVID-19 Hub / COVID assessment** — the exact wording may vary.
3. Look for an option such as **“Choose member,” “Select dependent,” “Family members,”** or **“Manage dependants.”**
4. If Andrew does not appear as an option, it may mean that because he is an adult dependent, Discovery requires him to either:
   - log in with his **own Discovery profile**, or  
   - give/confirm **consent** for you to manage certain health actions on his behalf.

If the assessment keeps asking questions as if it is for you and there is no clear way to switch to Andrew, I would **not submit it under your name**. Rather contact Discovery Health support directly through the app’s help/chat function or member services and ask them specifically: “How do I complete a COVID assessment for my adult dependent, Andrew, without it being submitted under my profile?”

While you are sorting that out, Andrew should act based on risk and symptoms. If he has had a significant COVID exposure, he should **limit contact with others, wear a mask around people, avoid vulnerable individuals**, and consider testing especially if he develops symptoms such as fever, cough, sore throat, body aches, headache, loss of smell/taste, diarrhoea, or fatigue. If symptoms are mild, home care with fluids, rest, and paracetamol if needed is reasonable.

He should seek urgent medical care if he has **shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, fainting, worsening weakness, oxygen saturation below 92% if you have a pulse oximeter,** or if he is high-risk and deteriorating.

For today: don’t submit it under your profile unless Discovery confirms that is acceptable; try Andrew’s own login or contact Discovery support for dependent/consent access.

query: i’m so scared that i will die if i get the coronavirus, due to type1 diabetes? like i know that sounds dramatic but i keep seeing stuff online and then i start panicking and checking my sugar every 20 mins and then i get more nervous. my name is maria lopez and my dob is 03/14/1997, i usually see dr. nisha patel for my endo stuff and i have aetna ppo #W48211903 but i dont even know if they cover extra visits or testing if its just me being scared. i dont have symptoms right now besides being anxious and kinda feeling tight in my chest when i think about it, but no fever that i know of. i’ve had type1 diabetes for years and i try to be careful but my numbers arent always perfect, sometimes they run high if im stressed or if i eat late and now this whole virus thing is making me feel like if i even get it then thats it for me. i live with other ppl and they still go out for groceries and work and i keep thinking what if they bring it home and i cant fight it off. i washed my hands so much theyre cracked and i keep wiping the door handles and my phone and then i feel stupid but also i cant stop. should someone with type1 be like totally isolated or is that overreacting? if i get a cough or fever do i go to er right away or call first? and if my sugar goes high from being sick does that mean danger right away? sorry i just need someone to tell me what is actually true bc im spiraling and i dont wanna ignore it if it really is serious for me.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m really sorry you’re feeling this scared. It does **not** sound dramatic; a lot of people with Type 1 diabetes have felt exactly this way after seeing alarming information online.

Here’s what is true: having Type 1 diabetes can increase the risk of complications from infections like COVID, especially if blood sugars are very high or ketones develop. But it absolutely does **not** mean that if you get coronavirus you will die. Many people with Type 1 recover well, especially when they monitor appropriately, stay hydrated, keep taking insulin, and get help early if warning signs appear.

Since you currently have **no fever, cough, or clear COVID symptoms**, you do **not** need the ER right now. The chest tightness that comes on when you think about it can happen with anxiety/panic, though if it becomes severe, persistent, or is associated with shortness of breath, fainting, or pain spreading to the arm/jaw, seek urgent care.

For prevention, you do not need to be “totally isolated” in a panic-driven way, but you should be careful: ask household members to wash hands when they come in, avoid close contact if they’re sick, improve ventilation when possible, disinfect high-touch surfaces once daily or as needed, and avoid sharing cups/utensils. Washing until your hands crack can actually damage your skin—use soap for 20 seconds, then moisturize; consider gloves for cleaning products.

For diabetes: checking every 20 minutes will likely feed the panic. If you are well, follow your usual monitoring schedule. If you become sick, check glucose every 2–4 hours, keep taking basal insulin, hydrate, and check ketones if glucose is persistently above about 240–250, if you’re vomiting, or if you feel very ill.

Call Dr. Nisha Patel’s office or your primary doctor for a sick-day plan and to ask about telehealth. You can also call Aetna directly to confirm coverage for extra visits/testing.

Seek urgent care/ER if you have trouble breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, blue lips, dehydration, repeated vomiting, moderate/large ketones, or glucose staying very high despite correction insulin. You deserve reassurance and a plan—not panic.

query: if my wife (maria ellis, she is a nurse in a surgery dept) comes into contact with a patient with the virus, is she likely to then get it herself, if she is wearing gloves and mask etc? she works over at st anne’s medical center, at 2201 ridge ave, allentown pa, and i know they say they have rules and ppe and all that but im sitting here at home getting myself all worked up bc she came home saying there was a guy in pre op or recovery or whatever who later got flagged and now everyone is acting like its just normal. she says she had gloves and mask on and didnt touch her face and washed hands a million times, but she was still in the room and doing nurse stuff close to him. i called the main line and left my number 610-555-4832 but i dont even know who is supposed to call back, maybe employee health or dr kevin malloy or somebody. i also emailed from mellis77@outlook.com because i didnt want to bug her while she is on shift. i guess im asking like does the mask/gloves make it very unlikely or is it still pretty easy to catch if the patient actually has it? we have kids at home and my mom stops by sometimes so im panicking about whether she should sleep in the spare room or not hug anybody or shower right away when she gets in. she keeps telling me not to freak out and that this is her job but that doesnt really help me at 1am googling stuff. sorry if this is dumb, just trying to understand actual risk and what we should do tonight if she was exposed at work.
target_response:
I understand why you’re anxious—when it’s your spouse and kids at home, “this is routine” doesn’t feel very reassuring at 1 a.m.

In general, **wearing appropriate PPE correctly greatly lowers the risk of infection**, especially if she had a mask on, wore gloves, avoided touching her face, and performed hand hygiene afterward. Healthcare workers do care for patients who later test positive, and most facilities have protocols to determine whether an exposure was “significant.” The risk is **much higher** if there was a PPE breach, no mask/eye protection, prolonged close contact, or an aerosol-generating procedure without the recommended respirator. Being in the room providing nursing care **with proper PPE** is usually considered a **lower-risk exposure**, but Employee Health/Infection Prevention at her hospital should make the official determination.

For tonight, reasonable steps are:

- Have her **follow her hospital’s exposure protocol** and contact Employee Health/occupational health when available.
- When she gets home: **wash hands**, change out of work clothes, and shower if that helps everyone feel more comfortable. Surface/clothing spread is not usually the main route, but this is sensible.
- If Employee Health does **not** consider it a high-risk exposure and she has no symptoms, she likely does **not** need to isolate from the family. If you want to be extra cautious for a few days, she can avoid prolonged close face-to-face contact, improve ventilation, and mask around high-risk people.
- I would have your mom **hold off on visiting** until you know whether this was classified as a true exposure, especially if she is older or has medical problems.
- Watch for symptoms over the next several days: fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, headache, diarrhea, loss of smell/taste, or unusual fatigue. Testing guidance varies, but many protocols test around **day 3–5 after exposure** and sooner if symptoms develop.

Seek urgent care/emergency help if she develops **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels persistently below about 92%** if you have a pulse oximeter.

Bottom line: based on what you described—mask, gloves, no face-touching, lots of handwashing—the risk is **likely relatively low**, but the hospital’s Employee Health team is the right group to confirm next steps.

query: my daughter emma rose miller is 11 years old she has has pneumonia and she has been sick since january 3rd and i feel like i am losing my mind bc symptoms keep changing every few days and nobody can really tell us why. she is up at night itching all over her upper torso, head, and ears, like scratching so much she is crying and then i am crying bc she is exhausted and i dont know what else to put on her. she has major headache and abdominal pain.. she is very upset and crying because this is going on way to long and she has seen many doctors with no relief any suggestions. we saw dr. priya shah and then also went back to st. lukes pediatrics and i feel like i keep repeating the same story and getting told to watch her or try another cream. tried most itching products, the oatmeal bath, allergy meds they said were ok, lotions, changing soap, washing sheets, everything i can think of and she still says her skin is crawling mostly at night. what could be causing the head pain and stomach pain. they check her today for appendicitis and said they were looking into that bc she was doubled over earlier but i still dont understand how the itching, headache and stomach all fit together. could it be from pneumonia or medicine or something missed? her cough was the big thing first and now its like every day a new thing. she barely sleeps, then misses school, then gets scared she is never gonna feel normal again. i dont want to be dramatic but this has been since january 3rd and she is only 11 and i just need ideas what to ask for next or what tests make sense bc we are getting no relief.
target_response:
I’m so sorry Emma — and you — are going through this. You are not being dramatic. An 11-year-old who has been sick since Jan 3, not sleeping, crying from itching, and now having headache and abdominal pain deserves a clear re-check and a plan.

A few things can connect these symptoms:

1. **Medication reaction** – Antibiotics commonly used for pneumonia can cause itching, hives/rash, stomach pain, headache, or diarrhea. Sometimes itching can happen even with a subtle rash. If she has had any new antibiotic, cough medicine, ibuprofen/naproxen, or other medication, this needs to be reviewed carefully.

2. **Post-viral or atypical infection** – Some infections that cause pneumonia, especially “walking pneumonia”/Mycoplasma or certain viruses, can cause shifting symptoms: cough, headache, abdominal pain, rashes/itching, fatigue.

3. **Skin/scalp causes** – Scabies, lice, contact dermatitis, or eczema flare can cause intense nighttime itching, especially scalp/ears/torso. These can be missed if the rash is minimal, so a very detailed skin/scalp exam matters.

4. **Less common but important** – Generalized itching can rarely be related to liver/bile issues, kidney issues, or blood inflammation. That’s why bloodwork may be helpful if not already done.

What I’d ask for next: schedule one focused follow-up with her pediatrician and bring a **one-page timeline**: dates of fever/cough, all medicines with start/stop dates, when itching began, rash/photos, headache pattern, abdominal pain location, bowel movements, appetite, and sleep. Ask whether she needs: **CBC with differential, CMP including liver enzymes/bilirubin, kidney function/electrolytes, ESR/CRP, urinalysis**, and possibly testing for **Mycoplasma/viral causes/mono/strep** depending on exam. Also ask if a **dermatology or allergy referral** is appropriate, and whether a prescription nighttime anti-itch medicine such as **hydroxyzine** is safe for her.

For now: avoid layering many creams, as that can worsen irritation. Use cool compresses, fragrance-free moisturizer, short lukewarm showers, keep nails short, and use only the allergy medicine exactly as directed.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if she has worsening or localized right-lower belly pain, persistent vomiting, stiff neck, confusion, severe headache, trouble breathing, swelling of lips/face, widespread hives, yellow eyes/skin, dark urine, or a fever that won’t come down.

query: my 3 year old son noah benitez, dob 02/14/2023, is complaint of stomach pain he also has fever and 1 week before he was diagnosed with pneumonia. we saw dr. maya kapoor at riverbend pediatrics for that and they said pneumonia and put him on amoxicillin. he was on amoxicillin and we picked it up from cvs on maple ave, i gave it like they told me and he just finished his antibiotics yesterday and this morning he has fever stomach pain increased heart rate what is causing it. im trying not to panic but its hard bc he is only 3 and he keeps saying his belly hurts and then i check him and he feels hot again, and his heart just seems like its going fast when i put my hand on him. i dont know if the amoxicillin can make stomach pain or if the pneumonia is coming back or if fever makes the heart rate go up or if this is something else totally. he was already sick all last week and i thought once the antibiotics were done we would be ok, but now it feels like we are starting over again and i dont know if i should wait for the office to call back or just take him in. i also dont want to over react but the stomach pain is what scares me because he didnt complain like this before, and the fever coming back right after finishing medicine just doesnt seem right to me. could this be from the antibiotic ending yesterday or could he need a different antibiotic or is it a side effect? please tell me what to look for bc im home with him and very worried.
target_response:
I understand why you’re worried. A fever returning the morning after Noah finished amoxicillin for pneumonia, especially with new belly pain and a “racing” heartbeat, is something I would want him assessed for **today** rather than just watching at home.

A few things could be going on:

- **Fever itself commonly makes the heart rate go up** in kids, and anxiety/crying/dehydration can add to that. His heart rate should slow as the fever comes down and he drinks fluids.
- **Amoxicillin can cause stomach upset**, nausea, loose stools, or cramping. However, antibiotic side effects alone usually do **not** cause a new fever right after finishing the medicine.
- The pneumonia may not be fully resolved, he may need a recheck or different treatment, or there could be a complication such as fluid around the lung.
- It could also be a **new infection**—a virus, stomach bug, urinary infection, or, less commonly but importantly, something like appendicitis. Pneumonia in children can also sometimes cause belly pain, especially if it’s lower in the lungs.

Because he is 3, recently had pneumonia, now has recurrent fever plus new abdominal pain and fast heartbeat, I recommend calling Riverbend Pediatrics again and telling them these exact symptoms and that you need **same-day guidance/visit**. If they cannot see him promptly, take him to urgent care or the ER.

Go to the ER now if he has any trouble breathing, ribs pulling in, blue/gray lips, extreme sleepiness or confusion, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, severe or worsening belly pain, pain mainly in the lower right belly, a hard/swollen belly, blood in stool, fever ≥104°F, or if his heart still seems very fast after fever medicine and fluids.

At home while arranging care: check his temperature, offer small frequent fluids, and you can use acetaminophen or ibuprofen if he can take them—dose by his weight and avoid aspirin. Don’t start any leftover antibiotics unless a clinician tells you to.

You are not overreacting—this deserves a same-day evaluation.

query: how soon after being infected can someone transmit covid-19? like im asking bc im kinda spiraling and google is making it worse not better. my name is mike benitez and i was around somebody who later said they might have been exposed, and now im sitting here trying to figure out if i could already be contagious even before i feel anything. i dont have a fever or cough right now, not trying to say i do, i just wanna know the timing part. like if a person got infected monday-ish or whatever, can they pass it the next day, or only after symptoms start, or is it one of those things where you can be spreading it and feel totally normal. i had to be around my dad for a bit and thats what is bothering me, bc hes older and i dont wanna be the reason anything happens. i also was supposed to go into work at northstar packaging tomorrow and i dont know if i should call out or if thats overreacting. i emailed HR already from mikeb_77@gmail.com but they just sent me some generic policy thing that didnt really answer the actual question. i keep replaying everything like did i stand too close, did i touch the same door handle, should i have masked, etc. if exposure was around 11/14, how soon after that could i be contagious to other people? and is there any point testing right away or is that too early and just gonna be negative even if its starting. sorry this is rambly im just trying to make a sane choice and not freak everyone out or ignore it if i should be staying away from people.
target_response:
Hi Mike — you’re not being rambly; this is a very understandable thing to worry about, especially with an older parent involved.

**Timing-wise:** after someone is infected with COVID, they usually are **not contagious immediately the next day**, though there’s some variability. Most people who become infectious do so around **2–3 days after infection**, and the highest contagiousness is often **around the day symptoms start and the 1–2 days before symptoms**. Yes, people can spread COVID while feeling completely normal — either **before symptoms** or if they never develop symptoms.

If your possible exposure was **11/14**, think of that as **Day 0**. The more relevant window for you potentially becoming contagious would generally begin around **11/16–11/17**, with symptoms, if they happen, most often starting around **Day 3–5**.

**Testing right away:** a test immediately after exposure is often too early and can be falsely negative. Practical approach:  
- Test **if symptoms start** at any point.  
- If no symptoms, take an antigen test around **Day 5** after exposure.  
- If negative but you’re still concerned, repeat **48 hours later**.  
PCR can sometimes detect earlier, but even PCR is not very useful on Day 0–1.

**For your dad:** since he’s older, I’d be cautious but not panic. For the next several days, wear a well-fitting mask around him, keep distance when possible, improve ventilation, and avoid eating together indoors. If you develop symptoms or test positive, separate from him as much as possible. If he develops symptoms or tests positive, he should contact his doctor quickly because antivirals work best when started early.

**Work tomorrow:** if you have no symptoms and only a possible exposure, many workplaces allow attendance with masking, but follow HR policy. If you can mask consistently, avoid close unmasked interactions, and skip shared meals/break rooms, that’s a reasonable middle ground. Calling out isn’t “overreacting” if your job allows it, but it may not be medically required.

Seek urgent care for severe trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, or dehydration. If symptoms appear, test and isolate while you clarify.

query: hi .. my name is kelly marino, dob 08/14/1982, and i have been diagnosed with pnemonia and have not slept in 4 days because of the coughing .. i'm hurting everywhere, going sligtly insane and cannot get to sleep no matter how much i try .. as soon as i breath in, it hurts and a cough .. any suggestions ? i know this prob sounds dramatic but i am so tired i keep crying over dumb stuff and then crying makes me cough more so its like this dumb circle. dr. nina patel said it was pnemonia when i was seen at urgent care, and i did try to listen to what they told me but i swear the night time is the worst, like i lay down and it starts all over again and then my ribs and back and even my stomach muscles hurt from coughing so hard. i keep sitting up with pillows, walking around the living room, hot tea, shower steam, all that, and i still cant shut my brain off because every breath feels like its gonna set off another coughing fit. i dont know if im supposed to call back or just tough it out, i dont want to be annoying but 4 days no sleep is making me feel not right in the head. i also need to pick stuff up from cvs on 7th ave but i honestly dont feel safe driving like this since im so foggy. is there anything safe i can do just to sleep a few hrs without making the breathing worse? like position, humidifier, anything. i am not asking for a miracle just need the cough to calm down enough so i can close my eyes because im exhausted and scared and hurting everywhere.
target_response:
Hi Kelly — this does **not** sound dramatic. Pneumonia can cause relentless coughing, rib/back/abdominal muscle pain, and coughing that gets much worse when you lie down. But **4 days without sleep, feeling foggy and “not right,” and pain with every breath** is enough reason to call back — you are not being annoying.

**Please call the urgent care/Dr. Patel’s office today** and tell them you have pneumonia, cannot sleep due to cough, and have pleuritic pain with breathing. They may want to recheck your oxygen level, make sure the antibiotic plan is appropriate, and consider something to help the cough safely, or an inhaler if there is wheezing/bronchospasm.

For tonight/while waiting:

- **Do not drive** if you feel foggy or sleep-deprived. Ask someone to pick up CVS meds, use delivery, or call the pharmacy about options.
- Sleep **upright at 30–45 degrees** in a recliner or with pillows; try side-lying if one side is more comfortable. Avoid lying flat.
- Use a **cool-mist humidifier** or steamy bathroom briefly before bed.
- Sip warm fluids; **honey** can calm cough if you are not diabetic/allergic.
- Consider OTC **dextromethorphan** at night for cough suppression and/or **guaifenesin** during the day to loosen mucus—check labels and avoid duplicating ingredients.
- For rib/muscle pain: **acetaminophen or ibuprofen** if you can take them safely. Avoid ibuprofen if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding risk, are on blood thinners, or were told not to take NSAIDs.
- Avoid alcohol, sleeping pills, benzodiazepines, or leftover narcotics unless specifically prescribed—these can worsen breathing or make you unsafe.

**Go to the ER now** or call emergency services if you have worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking in full sentences, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen saturation <92% if you have a pulse ox, coughing blood, severe or worsening chest pain, persistent fever, dehydration, or you’re getting worse despite treatment.

You deserve help with this. Pneumonia recovery can be rough, but you should not have to “tough out” four sleepless nights.

query: where can i be tested for covid 19? im trying not to be dramatic but i keep reading different stuff and every site sends me somewhere else or says call first and then no one answers. my name is melissa turner, dob 03/14/1987, and the best phone for me is 267-555-0148 if somebody has to call back or text me bc i dont wanna miss it. i live at 118 s 9th st apt 4b and i dont have a car today so im trying to figure out if theres somewhere close or if i need to get a ride, and also if i can just walk in or if they make you schedule online first. i dont know if i need the rapid one or the pcr one or if that even matters, i just need to know where to go and what to bring. do i need id, insurance card, mask, anything else? i work around ppl and im scared to go in if i shouldnt, but also i dont wanna call out for no reason if im not even sick from covid. i dont really know the rules right now, like do they test you if you dont have a referral or symptoms, or only if you were exposed? im confused bc one place says testing is free and another says maybe a copay and another says no appointments available. can someone just tell me where can i be tested for covid 19 and if its same day results or not, bc im kinda anxious and keep refreshing pages and its making me more stressed.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry this has been so confusing. You’re not being dramatic; testing rules and availability can be frustrating and change often.

I can’t schedule or call/text you from here, but here are the most practical options for getting tested today without a car:

1. **At-home rapid antigen test**  
   This is often the fastest option. You can buy one at many pharmacies, grocery stores, or have one delivered if available. Results are usually in **15–30 minutes**. If it’s positive, you should treat it as COVID. If it’s negative but you have symptoms, repeat in **48 hours** or get a PCR/NAAT.

2. **Nearby pharmacy or urgent care testing**  
   CVS, Walgreens, Rite Aid, and local urgent care clinics often offer COVID testing, but many require **online scheduling** even if they accept walk-ins. Search “COVID testing near me” or check the pharmacy/urgent care website directly for same-day appointments. If you’re in Philadelphia, the city health department website or **311** may also list current community testing sites.

3. **PCR/NAAT vs rapid test**  
   - **Rapid antigen:** fastest, good for deciding whether to stay home from work; may miss early infection.  
   - **PCR/NAAT:** more sensitive; results can take same day to 1–3 days depending on site.  
   If you work around people and are symptomatic or recently exposed, a rapid test today is reasonable; PCR/NAAT is helpful if the rapid test is negative but suspicion remains.

What to bring: **photo ID**, **insurance card** if you have one, a **mask**, your phone, appointment confirmation if scheduled, and payment card just in case. Some sites are free, but others may bill insurance or charge a copay—check before booking if cost is a concern.

Until you test, if you have symptoms or a known exposure, wear a well-fitting mask and avoid close contact at work if possible. Call out if you have fever, significant cough, sore throat, body aches, or feel ill.

Seek urgent care now or call 911 for trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening.

query: i am michael ray brennan dob 11/28/1972 and i have had a percocet addiction for 35 years and have had many dr.s in that time span, way too many to even keep straight honestly. i was up to without exaggeration, eating 240 10mg p s, 98 30mg oxycodone pills, as well as an assortment of 5mg pills which were always at my disposal. i have cancer and have severe back injuries but along with the p s i also had monthly access to 240 2mg xannax pills which after awhile ihad no problem eating all of the above each month and would look for some more, that is the ugly truth. i died 4 times in a 6 year span due to double pneumonia which the dr.s told me my medications were playing a large roll in this, Dr. Steven Kline told me more than once i was not gonna keep getting lucky. when i was 16 i became an atheist due to my brother dying, he was my favorite and i became very drug dependent to kill the pain. this lead to my later addictions and no matter what i tried i could not escape their grip. the last time i was in the hospital i said lord i do not know if you are real but if you are i am begging you to open my heart again to you and i need your help or this will probably be it for me when i leave the hospital, i have had enough, and this is only the drug portion of my life. i would not wish the things and amount of death i have had to witness on anyone. i am the last of all my friends left alive and i am 53years old. over 40 friends have passed on, 2 of which i grew up with from the age of 5, others just would not listen. i lost 2 older brothers, 1 my 1st year in highschool the other 8 years later of an overdose. needless to say ptsd, depression and other problems haunted me until i did as many drugs as possible to bury the pain, but demons only stay buried so long which i found out the hard way. if anyone answers i still use tiny2882@comcat,net even tho its old and half the time i type it wrong.
target_response:
Michael, thank you for being so honest. What you described is an enormous amount of loss, trauma, pain, and substance exposure over many years. The fact that you are still reaching out tells me there is a part of you that wants to live and heal—and that matters.

Given your history of very high-dose Percocet/oxycodone use, Xanax use, cancer, severe back injury, pneumonia episodes, and having “died” multiple times, this is **not something to try to handle alone or by abruptly stopping**. Sudden opioid withdrawal is miserable, and sudden benzodiazepine withdrawal—especially after heavy Xanax use—can cause **seizures, delirium, and death**. You need medically supervised help.

The safest next steps would be:

1. **Contact an addiction medicine doctor or go to an ER/hospital-based detox program today if you are still using or at risk of relapse.**  
   Medications like **buprenorphine/Suboxone** or **methadone** can dramatically reduce cravings and overdose risk. Benzodiazepines usually require a **slow supervised taper**.

2. **Make sure you have naloxone/Narcan** at home and that someone around you knows how to use it.

3. Because you have cancer and serious back injuries, ask for a coordinated plan involving **pain management, oncology/palliative care, and addiction medicine**. You deserve pain treatment, but it must be done safely.

4. For PTSD/depression and the grief you described, trauma-focused therapy and possibly non-addictive medications can help. You have been carrying far too much alone.

Please seek urgent care or call 911 immediately if you have: slowed or stopped breathing, blue lips, extreme sleepiness/confusion, chest pain, fever/cough/shortness of breath, seizures, severe withdrawal, or thoughts of harming yourself. If you feel suicidal or unsafe, call/text **988** now.

Also, for your privacy, try not to post your full name, DOB, or email publicly. If this message was meant for a clinic, call them directly and confirm your contact information by phone.

Michael, addiction is a disease—not a moral failure. You are not beyond help, and you do not have to do this alone.

query:
hi, my name is maria santos, dob 03/14/1992, im just trying not to panic but also i dont wanna ignore it if its something. symptoms include irritation in the throat, dry cough, trouble breathing properly, headache. however not really a sign of a fever. temperature is about 35 degrees. should i get tested? i keep checking it cause it seemed weird low-ish but thats what the little home thermometer says, and i tried twice. i dont feel burning hot or anything, just kinda off and tight in my breathing, like i can breathe but not properly if that makes sense. the throat thing is annoying, not super painful, more scratchy/irritated, and the cough is dry like nothing coming up. headache has been on and off and im prob making it worse by googling every 5 minutes.

im asking bc i called cityline urgent care and the phone hold was forever and i didnt know if they even want people walking in with cough stuff right now. my cell is 267-555-0138 if someone needs to call me back but honestly i just need to know if this sounds like a test situation or if i should wait it out at home. i did go to CVS on 19th and market earlier this week to pick up meds and there were alot of ppl around, and i also took the bus, so now im worried maybe i was exposed. no real fever tho, thats why im confused. i dont want to waste a test if im being dramatic but the trouble breathing properly part is scaring me. should i get tested or isolate first and see if it gets worse?
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m glad you reached out. You’re not being dramatic: **a dry cough, scratchy throat, headache, and especially feeling like you can’t breathe properly** are symptoms that should be taken seriously, even without a fever.

Yes — **I would recommend COVID testing** (and ideally flu testing too, depending on what’s circulating locally), given your respiratory symptoms and recent time in crowded places/public transit. Fever is common with COVID/flu, but **many people do not have a fever**, especially early on.

For now, I’d suggest:

1. **Isolate as much as you can** until you test and symptoms are improving. Wear a well-fitting mask if you must be around others.  
2. **Get tested today or tomorrow** if possible — a home rapid test is reasonable, but if negative and symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours or get a PCR/clinic test.  
3. **Do not just walk into urgent care if you can avoid it** — check their website or call ahead if possible, since many clinics have respiratory-symptom instructions. If breathing feels concerning, though, don’t let hold times delay care.

About the temperature: **35°C is low** (95°F), but home thermometers can read falsely low depending on method, battery, placement, or technique. Recheck after sitting indoors for 10–15 minutes, and if possible use an oral thermometer properly. If it truly stays around **35°C or lower**, especially with feeling very weak, confused, shivery, or ill, that needs prompt medical attention.

The breathing description is the key issue. If you have a pulse oximeter, check your oxygen level. **Seek urgent care/ER now** if you have any of the following: worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking full sentences, chest pain/pressure, blue/gray lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen level **below 94%**, severe weakness, or breathing that feels progressively tighter.

If breathing is mild and stable, you can rest, hydrate, use honey/lozenges for throat/cough, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen for headache if you can take them safely. But if the “can’t breathe properly” feeling is new or worsening, it’s better to be evaluated rather than waiting it out.

query: about the ibuprofen and covid 19 should i quit taking it? it’s got me paranoid. the way the media’s been talking about it. i take it everyday for my neck pain and back pain and ive been doing that for awhile now, like its just my normal thing when i get up and when i get home and my neck is all tight again. i’m mariah bentley btw, sorry i always forget to say that first, and i had asked dr lena ortiz about the pain before but not this whole covid thing bc it wasnt all over tv then. i can’t take pain pills because they make me nauseas, like real bad, i throw up and feel weird and then i cant function, so ibuprofen has kinda been the only thing that lets me get thru the day without laying on a heating pad nonstop. any insight please because i keep seeing posts saying not to take it if you might get covid 19 or if you have fever or whatever and now im sitting here thinking am i making myself worse somehow if i keep taking it?? i dont have any new crazy symptoms right now, im just scared and overthinking. i got my last bottle from cvs on 5th ave and it just says ibuprofen 200mg like normal, nothing fancy, and my insurance is Blue Cross PPO #BCP-774291 if that matters for what else i could use instead. i dont wanna run to urgent care over a question but i also dont wanna be dumb and keep taking something everyone says is bad. should i quit cold turkey or is that worse for the neck/back pain? ty
target_response:
Hi Mariah — I’m glad you asked, and you’re not being “dumb” at all. There was a lot of early media concern about ibuprofen and COVID-19, but based on the evidence we have now, ibuprofen has **not** been shown to make COVID worse. If you develop COVID symptoms or a fever, it’s generally okay to use ibuprofen if you normally tolerate it and don’t have reasons to avoid it. Many clinicians suggest **acetaminophen/Tylenol first for fever**, but ibuprofen is still an option.

You do **not** need to quit ibuprofen “cold turkey” because of COVID fears. There’s no withdrawal danger, but stopping suddenly may make your neck/back pain flare. The bigger issue is that taking ibuprofen **every day for a long time** can increase risks such as stomach irritation/ulcers/bleeding, kidney strain, elevated blood pressure, and heart risks—especially at higher doses or if taken without food.

Practical guidance:
- Use the **lowest effective dose** and avoid exceeding the OTC max: typically **1,200 mg/day** unless a clinician specifically told you otherwise.
- Take it **with food** and plenty of water.
- Don’t combine it with other NSAIDs like naproxen/Aleve, aspirin for pain, or prescription anti-inflammatories unless advised.
- Avoid or check with us first if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are on blood thinners, have uncontrolled high blood pressure/heart disease, or are pregnant.

For alternatives that may let you reduce ibuprofen: acetaminophen if you can take it safely, topical diclofenac gel, lidocaine patches, heat, gentle stretching, posture/ergonomic changes, massage, and physical therapy. Since this is chronic neck/back tightness and you’re needing ibuprofen daily, it would be smart to schedule a non-urgent visit with Dr. Ortiz to make a longer-term plan. Your PPO may help cover PT, but you don’t need to send insurance numbers in messages.

Seek urgent care if you develop severe shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, dehydration, persistent high fever, or for back/neck pain with new weakness/numbness, loss of bladder/bowel control, numbness in the groin area, major trauma, or fever with severe spine pain.

For now, if you feel well otherwise, no urgent care is needed just for this question.

query: hi im melissa kramer, dob 04/03/1989, i have mrn 7g-44218 from when i was seen before and i usually msg dr amina patel but i wasnt sure if this is something urgent or just me overthinking. low grade fevers 99.8, constant trigger points that are tender to touch in trapezius muscles and muscles feel warm to touch, pointer finger, and middle finger on right hand are very sore, sore to open and close hand in those fingers, whats wrong? i know 99.8 isnt like a huge fever but for me i can feel it and i keep checking it like every couple hrs bc i feel flushed and weird, then i get anxious and it makes everything feel worse. the trap muscles on both sides are like knots but the right side feels more angry, if i press it its tender and kinda warm and it shoots up toward my neck a little. then the right hand thing is freaking me out bc its specifically the pointer finger and middle finger, like not the whole hand, but those two are sore and stiff and it hurts to make a fist or open my hand all the way. i didnt smash it or anything that i remember, i have been using my laptop and phone a ton and carrying bags but nothing major. i keep thinking is this infection or inflammation or nerve thing or arthritis or something from my neck?? no rash that i can tell, no big swelling that looks crazy, just sore and warm feeling muscles and the fingers hurt. i can still move them but it just feels wrong and im scared to wait if its something serious. do i need urgent care or can i try heat/ice and ibuprofen and watch it?
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this; it’s understandable to feel anxious when symptoms are new and localized like this.

A temperature of **99.8°F is not considered a true fever** medically (we usually use **100.4°F or higher**), but feeling flushed/warm can happen with pain, inflammation, stress/anxiety, dehydration, or early illness. Checking every couple of hours can definitely amplify the anxiety loop—if you can, limit it to **2–3 times/day** unless you feel significantly worse.

Based on what you described, the most likely possibilities are:

- **Muscle strain/myofascial “trigger points”** in the trapezius from posture, laptop/phone use, carrying bags, stress/tension.
- **Overuse tendon irritation** in the right index and middle fingers from typing/phone use/gripping bags.
- **Nerve irritation** from the neck/shoulder area (sometimes C6/C7 or median nerve irritation can refer symptoms into the index/middle fingers).
- Less likely but important to watch for: **infection** or an inflammatory joint issue—usually you’d see more obvious redness, swelling, significant warmth, worsening pain, or true fever.

If you can still move the fingers and there’s no major swelling/redness, it’s reasonable to try conservative care for **24–48 hours**:

- **Ibuprofen 400–600 mg every 6–8 hours with food** if you can take NSAIDs safely (avoid if stomach ulcer/bleeding risk, kidney disease, on blood thinners, pregnant, etc.). Or acetaminophen if NSAIDs aren’t safe.
- **Heat** to trapezius/neck 15–20 min at a time; gentle stretching, posture breaks, avoid heavy bags.
- **Ice** to the sore fingers 10–15 min a few times/day; rest from repetitive typing/phone gripping; consider a neutral wrist position.
- Gentle range-of-motion—don’t force a fist if it sharply hurts.

Please seek **urgent care today** if you develop: temperature **≥100.4°F**, rapidly worsening redness/swelling/warmth, red streaking, severe pain, inability to bend/straighten the fingers, numbness/weakness, hand color change, significant neck stiffness, severe headache, or you feel systemically ill.

If it’s not clearly improving in **2–3 days**, or the finger stiffness persists/worsens, I’d recommend an in-person exam with Dr. Patel or urgent care to check the neck/shoulder, hand tendons/joints, and nerves.

query:
hi, my name is raka santoso. sorry if my english is weird but im really worried and typing fast. my grandfather Pak Hendra Wijaya is suffering from pneumonia right now and refuses to go to the doctor because of the price needed to obtain a patented medicine. in indonesia, it is not hard to find these kinds of doctors that don t want to help people in need, like they just ask for money first and my family gets scared and then we leave with no real help. we went near RSUD Dr. Soetomo once but he got angry in the car and said no, too expensive, just take me home, so now he is back at our house at Jl. Diponegoro 12, Surabaya and coughing and breathing heavy sometimes and i dont know what to do.

he keeps saying it is just old people cough but we were told pneumonia by a small clinic before, and now he refuses to go again because medicine price and doctor fee. my mom is crying, my uncle says try steam and warm water, my aunt says antibiotic but we dont know which one and i know thats dangerous but also doing nothing feels dangerous too. if someone needs to call me my number is +62 812-3456-7789 or email raka.santoso88@gmail.com but mostly i just need advice. he has BPJS Kesehatan #0001567823445 but the office made it confusing and said something not active or needs update so we are stuck.

what medicine can you guys recommend for a home treatment? anything safe we can do at home first, like what to buy, what signs mean hospital now, how to make him agree, because he really refuses and keeps talking about price and patented medicine and bad doctors here.
target_response:
Hi Raka — I’m really sorry your family is going through this. Your English is clear, and your worry is appropriate. An older person with suspected pneumonia who is “breathing heavy sometimes” needs medical assessment urgently, even if he is afraid of cost.

**Most important:** if your grandfather is struggling to breathe, breathing fast, very weak, confused, lips/fingers look bluish, has chest pain, cannot drink, has persistent high fever, or oxygen level is **below 92%** if you have an oximeter, he should go to the **IGD/emergency department now** or you can call local emergency help (**119 / 112 in Indonesia**). Pneumonia in elderly people can deteriorate quickly and may need oxygen, IV fluids, or the correct antibiotics.

At home while arranging help:
- Keep him **sitting upright**, not lying flat.
- Encourage **small frequent sips** of water/ORS if he can swallow safely.
- For fever/body aches: **paracetamol/acetaminophen** as directed on the package, avoiding overdose and avoiding if severe liver disease.
- Use a **fan for airflow** or humidified air if comfortable, but avoid hot steam bowls because burns are common.
- Do **not** start random antibiotics from a pharmacy. The wrong antibiotic or dose can fail, cause side effects, and make resistance worse. Pneumonia treatment depends on age, severity, oxygen level, other diseases, and local patterns.
- Avoid strong cough suppressants, alcohol, sleeping pills, or anything that makes him drowsy.

To help him agree: try saying, “Kakek, we are not going to buy expensive patented medicine first. We only want the emergency doctor to check oxygen and breathing. If oxygen is low, home treatment is not safe.” Emphasize that many effective pneumonia medicines are **generic**, not expensive branded medicines. Ask the hospital/social worker/admin to help check **BPJS status**; in an emergency, the priority should be stabilizing him first, with paperwork clarified afterward.

If he refuses absolutely and seems mentally clear, you can still call an ambulance/health worker to assess at home or speak with him directly. But if he is confused, very breathless, fainting, or blue, treat it as an emergency and seek care immediately.

query: hello, i am a student and dealing with a microbiology assignment and im honestly stuck and prob overthinking it bc i keep going in circles. my name is maria benson and im in the micro class at camden county college, and this is for prof. lauren kim, she gave us this pretend/unknown patient case thing but its written like a real paitent sample and my sheet has the sample id CHOP-77A19 on the top so i dont wanna mess it up. i am given a paitent sample.my paitent is 4 years old -diagnosis pneumonia - summary of peresent illness =recurrant colds, ear infections,and bronchitis.she has been sick for past 3 weeks. developed a fever yesterday.also nausia and vomiting,muscle aches. past medical history= cystic fibrosis diagnosed at age 3. i did all the lab work and found out that the bacteria causes the disease is psudomonas aeruginosa. what is the appropriate treatment? please help. i know this sounds like homework and it is, but i am not trying to cheat exactly, i just dont understand if the answer should be like a regular pneumonia antibiotic or if bc she has cystic fibrosis and its psudomonas aeruginosa then its treated diffrent. i keep reading about resistance and biofilms and nebulized meds and then i get more confused. also she is only 4 so i dont know what meds are even ok in a kid that age. my notes say to think about the patient history not just the gram stain and culture, and i did the oxidase thing and all that and it points to psudomonas. would the appropriate treatment be something like an anti-pseudomonal antibiotic, or combo therapy, or inhaled tobramycin or ciprofloxacin or something else? i have to write the rationale too and i dont wanna put the wrong thing if it should be for cf pneumonia specifically. thanks.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re thinking about this the right way. In a cystic fibrosis (CF) patient, **Pseudomonas aeruginosa pneumonia is treated differently from “typical” community-acquired pneumonia**, because Pseudomonas commonly colonizes CF airways, forms biofilms, and can be resistant to many standard antibiotics.

For your case — **4-year-old with CF, recurrent respiratory infections, now 3 weeks ill with new fever and systemic symptoms, culture consistent with Pseudomonas aeruginosa** — the appropriate treatment would generally be:

**Antipseudomonal antibiotic therapy, guided by susceptibility testing, often with combination therapy.**

A good assignment-style answer might be:

> Treatment would include an antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as **ceftazidime, cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem**, often combined with an aminoglycoside such as **tobramycin**, especially for a CF pulmonary exacerbation or pneumonia. Therapy should be adjusted based on culture and sensitivity results.

The rationale: **Pseudomonas aeruginosa is not reliably covered by routine pneumonia antibiotics** like amoxicillin or many macrolides. CF patients are at increased risk because thick mucus allows chronic infection, biofilm formation, and recurrent exacerbations. Combination therapy is often used in more significant CF pulmonary infections to improve bacterial killing and reduce resistance risk.

About the medications you mentioned:

- **Inhaled tobramycin** is commonly used in CF, especially for chronic Pseudomonas suppression or eradication protocols, but it may not be enough by itself for an acute pneumonia with fever/systemic illness.  
- **IV tobramycin plus an antipseudomonal beta-lactam** is a classic regimen for significant CF pulmonary exacerbations.  
- **Ciprofloxacin** has antipseudomonal activity and may be used in some situations, often orally for milder cases or step-down therapy, but pediatric use depends on severity, risks/benefits, and susceptibilities.

So your instinct is correct: **don’t treat this like routine pneumonia; treat it as CF-associated Pseudomonas pneumonia/pulmonary exacerbation.**

Also include supportive CF care in your rationale: airway clearance, bronchodilators if used, hydration, nutrition, oxygen if needed, and monitoring.

If this were a real child, urgent care would be needed for trouble breathing, low oxygen, persistent vomiting/dehydration, lethargy, worsening fever, or chest pain.

query: flu symptoms, cough and no fever. should i test for coronavirus? i have asthma and im kinda getting myself worked up bc every little cough makes me think im missing something. this is maria lopez, dob 03/14/1988, i put my callback as 510-555-7281 but texting is better if anyone calls bc i keep coughing and dont wanna talk a ton. i know im prob overthinking but i have had like that gross flu-ish run down feeling, some cough, chest feels a little tight off and on but i do have asthma so its hard to tell what is normal asthma crap and what is not. i checked my temp a bunch and no fever, like literally no fever, so thats why im confused. i havent been around anyone that i know for sure has coronavirus but i was at work and the store and ppl are coughing everywhere and now im spiraling. i still have my inhaler and used it like i normally do, not trying to be dramatic, just dont want it to sneak up on me if asthma makes it worse. do i need to go get tested even with no fever or just stay home and watch it? i dont want to sit in a waiting room and catch something worse either. i usually go to kaiser oakland urgent care if i have to, but id rather not unless thats what you think. should i test for coronavirus or wait unless i get fever / worse breathing? also if the cough is from asthma/allergies would the test even matter? sorry this is rambly, just nervous and trying to not panic but also not ignore it.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re not being dramatic. With asthma, it’s very reasonable to be cautious when you develop a new cough, “flu-ish” fatigue, and intermittent chest tightness.

**Yes, I would test for COVID now, even without a fever.** Many people with COVID never develop a fever, and symptoms can overlap with asthma, allergies, colds, and flu. Testing matters because it helps you know whether to isolate, protect others, and whether you might qualify for treatment if positive.

**Best next steps:**
1. **Do a home COVID antigen test today** if you have one.  
2. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, **repeat in 24–48 hours**. Early tests can be falsely negative.  
3. If you want the most accurate answer or symptoms are worsening, consider a **PCR/clinic test**—ideally through a drive-through or scheduled testing site rather than sitting in an urgent care waiting room.  
4. Since you feel flu-ish, if symptoms started within the last 48 hours, it may also be worth asking Kaiser about **flu testing or antiviral treatment**, especially because asthma can raise risk for complications.

For your asthma: keep using your inhaler as prescribed and continue any controller medications. If you have an asthma action plan, follow it. Chest tightness that improves with your rescue inhaler is more reassuring, but needing it more often than usual is a warning sign.

**Go to urgent care or ER now** if you have any of the following: trouble breathing at rest, worsening chest tightness not relieved by inhaler, wheezing that is escalating, lips/face turning blue or gray, confusion, severe weakness, chest pain/pressure, coughing up blood, or oxygen level **below 94%** if you have a pulse oximeter. Also seek care if you need albuterol more often than every 4 hours or it isn’t lasting.

If symptoms stay mild, it’s reasonable to **stay home, mask around others, hydrate, rest, and test/retest** rather than go to urgent care immediately. And yes—COVID can look exactly like “asthma/allergies” at first, so testing is the safest way to avoid guessing.

Hi Jenna — not a dumb question at all. COVID testing rules can be confusing, and you’re doing the right thing by trying not to show up in the wrong place or expose others.

I can’t schedule the test or text you from here, but in general **do not go to Penn Medicine Rittenhouse/1800 Lombard without confirming an appointment first**, because many health-system testing sites are **appointment-only** and may require an order or online scheduling.

query: hi, my name is melissa naidoo and i am just trying to find out what to do because i have been to the emergency room at busamed hospital on thursday and was treated for a tummy bug, since then i have developed a fever and body aches and tiredness, i would like to know where can o go to be tested for covid 19? i am not trying to be dramatic but im a bit scared now because when i went in it really felt like a stomach thing and they said tummy bug and i came home, but now the fever started and my whole body is sore and im so tired i just want to lie down, and i dont know if i should go back to emergency or if there is a testing place i must go to instead. i stay at 28 park lane, sandton and i dont want to drive all over if i might make other people sick or if im not supposed to be out. my cell is 082-555-1934 if someone can maybe tell me where the closest covid 19 test is or if i need a referral or booking first. i also dont know if i should call the hospital again or my normal gp dr sean mthembu because i dont want to waste anyones time but the fever and body aches are making me worry. since thursday i have just been resting, drinking water and trying to eat but i feel worse now, not better, and im worried because i was in the emergency room around other sick people too. please can someone tell me where can o go to be tested for covid 19 and if i should isolate now already or wait for a test.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you are **not being dramatic** and you are not wasting anyone’s time. A “tummy bug” can sometimes be the first part of a viral illness, including **COVID-19 or influenza**, and developing **fever, body aches and marked tiredness** after the ER visit is a good reason to get advice and test.

**Please start isolating now — don’t wait for the test.** Stay in a separate room if possible, wear a mask around others, avoid visitors, and don’t go to work/shops until you know what this is and your fever has settled.

For testing near Sandton, the safest next step is to **phone before going anywhere**:
1. **Call your GP, Dr Sean Mthembu**, and explain that you were treated for gastroenteritis but now have fever/body aches/fatigue. They can advise whether you need review and may direct you to the closest testing site.  
2. If your GP is unavailable, call **Busamed Hospital** and ask if they offer COVID testing or where they are referring symptomatic patients.  
3. You can also contact private pathology providers such as **Lancet, Ampath or PathCare** via their websites/call centres for the nearest branch/drive-through testing option. Many pharmacies/clinics also offer rapid antigen tests, but phone first.

A **rapid antigen test** is useful if positive; if it’s negative but you still feel flu-like, repeat in 24–48 hours or ask for a **PCR**, which is more sensitive.

While resting: drink fluids frequently, consider oral rehydration solution if diarrhoea/vomiting, eat small bland meals, and use **paracetamol** for fever/body aches as directed on the packet. Avoid taking anti-inflammatories if you are dehydrated, vomiting, have kidney disease, ulcers, or are unsure.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, fainting, blue lips, oxygen saturation <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, severe/worsening abdominal pain, blood in stool/vomit, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration** such as very little urine/dizziness, or a fever that is very high or not improving.

If you do go for testing, drive yourself if safe, wear a mask, keep windows open, and avoid waiting indoors where possible.
