Possible causes her doctor may be considering include:  
- **Heart strain or heart failure** after illness, which can cause cough, leg swelling, fast heart rate, and exertional fatigue  
- **Myocarditis/pericarditis** after infection  
- **Pulmonary embolism**—a blood clot in the lung, especially concerning with fast heart rate, shortness of breath, chest discomfort, or one-sided leg swelling  
- **Persistent/recurrent pneumonia** or complications such as fluid around the lung  
- **Arrhythmia**, anemia, kidney issues, medication side effects, etc.

Given that her symptoms are **new and progressing**, I would not wait passively if the swelling and rapid heartbeat are continuing. She should call Dr. Patel’s office **today** and report: persistent cough after two pneumonias, new bilateral leg swelling, rapid heartbeat, and getting worn out walking around the house. Ask whether she should be seen same day or go to the ER.

She should go to the **ER now**—not urgent care—if she has any chest pain/pressure, worsening shortness of breath, fainting, oxygen saturation <92% if she can check it, coughing blood, blue lips, confusion, heart rate persistently >120 at rest, one leg much more swollen/painful than the other, or rapid worsening swelling/breathlessness.

Regarding insurance: for urgent symptoms, she should not delay care over prior authorization. The PCP office and Aetna can sort out approvals for scheduled tests, but the ER evaluates potentially serious symptoms immediately. Also, avoid sharing insurance ID numbers online for privacy.

query: hi my name is samir hassan and i just want ask bc i am getting worried and i dont know if i need go clinic or just wait. i have tonsillitis long time ago and every year i feel allergic problem in my thort, like scratchy and tight and i keep swallowing and checking mirror too much. about 7 days i feel same allergic problem in my thort and tongue i have 1 canker sore. it is not big but i feel it all day and then i start thinking maybe my tongue is swelling but maybe it is my anxiety also. i take just anti histamine, the one i got from walgreens on 86th st, and it help little maybe but not fully. but ago i treat this with prednisone from dr lena morris and it was better fast that time, thats why im confused now if i should ask again or no. i dont have high fever or anything like that im mostly just scared bc now every throat thing make me think covid. does it covid 19? i mean i know covid can be throat and cough and all differnt symptoms, and i keep reading online and it makes me more nervous. i also had tonsillitis before so maybe it is old problem coming back or allergy from dust or weather, idk. i dont want take prednisone if it is not safe or if covid maybe bad to take it, but antihistamine only is not making me normal. the thort feeling comes and goes but has been 7 days and tongue feel weird and that canker sore make me notice everything. should i test covid or see doctor first or just continue anti histamine and gargle salt water? sorry for long message i am just nervous.
target_response:
Hi Samir — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this; throat/tongue sensations can feel very alarming, especially when you’ve had tonsillitis before and are reading about COVID online.

From what you describe — scratchy/tight throat that comes and goes, no high fever, one small canker sore, and partial improvement with an antihistamine — common possibilities include allergies/post-nasal drip, irritation/dryness, a viral sore throat including COVID, recurrence of tonsil inflammation, and anxiety making normal sensations feel much more intense. A canker sore can also make your tongue feel “weird” because you keep noticing and checking the area.

**What I’d do next:**
1. **Take a COVID test** — a home rapid test is a reasonable first step since COVID can present with throat symptoms. If negative but symptoms continue or you had an exposure, repeat in 24–48 hours or consider a PCR test.
2. **Continue supportive care for now:** fluids, warm salt-water gargles, honey/tea, avoid spicy/acidic foods, and use acetaminophen/ibuprofen if safe for you.
3. If allergies are likely, you can continue the antihistamine as directed. Many people get better relief by adding a **saline nasal rinse/spray** and/or an OTC nasal steroid spray like fluticasone, but follow the label and avoid if you’ve been told not to use it.
4. **Avoid prednisone unless a clinician prescribes it after evaluating you.** It can help inflammation/allergy in some cases, but it can also mask or worsen certain infections and isn’t something to restart just because it helped before.

Because this has lasted **7 days** and you’re feeling tongue/throat tightness, I’d recommend scheduling a clinic/urgent care visit in the next day or two, especially if the COVID test is negative and you’re not improving.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have trouble breathing, trouble swallowing saliva, drooling, rapidly worsening tongue/lip/face swelling, hives, severe one-sided throat pain, muffled “hot potato” voice, neck swelling, inability to open your mouth, or fever >101–102°F.

In the meantime, try to limit mirror-checking and online searching — they often amplify the sensation and anxiety.

query: i am a little concerned that i may be showing some symptoms for coronavirus, and i feel kinda dumb even typing it bc it sounds maybe small but its just been sitting in my head and i keep checking stuff online which prob isnt helping. my name is morgan ellis and if someone has to reach me later my cell is 267-555-0148 or email morgan.ellis77@gmail.com, but honestly i just want to know if this sounds like something i should be calling about or if im overreacting. i have had a bit of a dry cough about 2 weeks ago for 3 days, it wasnt like horrible or anything but it was dry and weird enough that i noticed it. then a week later i had a headache and got nauseaus, and the headache part is what worried me bc i never get headaches, like almost never ever, so it stood out. my chest has felt unusual for 5 days now, not exactly pain that i can explain right, just unusual and enough that i keep thinking about my breathing and then i get more anxious so idk whats real and whats me stressing. i havent wanted to bug anyone or go sit somewhere if its not needed, but also i dont wanna ignore it if its coronavirus bc i dont want to put other ppl at risk. i tried to leave a message for dr priya nair but i know everyone is swamped and i may not hear back quick. should i be isolating like for real right now, and is there a test i should be asking for even though the dry cough was like 2 weeks ago and only lasted 3 days? sorry this is rambling, im just nervous.
target_response:
Hi Morgan — you’re not overreacting, and you’re definitely not “dumb” for asking. The combination you describe — a dry cough a couple of weeks ago, then headache/nausea, and now an unusual chest sensation — *could* fit with COVID-19 or another viral illness, but it could also be anxiety-related breathing awareness, reflux, muscle strain, allergies/asthma, or another non-COVID issue. The fact that you’re noticing your chest for 5 days is worth taking seriously, even if it’s not severe pain.

**What I’d do now:**

1. **Act as if it could be COVID for the moment.** Stay home as much as possible, avoid close contact with others, and wear a well-fitting mask if you need to be around anyone. This is especially important until you’ve tested and/or symptoms are clearly improving.

2. **Get tested.** Even though the cough was 2 weeks ago, testing can still be reasonable because you’re having ongoing symptoms. A rapid home antigen test is a good first step; if it’s negative but you’re still concerned or symptoms continue, consider repeating it in 24–48 hours or getting a PCR/NAAT test if available. A negative test now doesn’t fully prove you didn’t have COVID earlier, but it helps guide what to do today.

3. **Contact Dr. Nair’s office or your clinic today/tomorrow**, especially because of the ongoing chest sensation. They can decide whether you need an exam, oxygen level check, ECG, or other evaluation.

**Seek urgent care / ER now** if you have any of the following: trouble breathing, worsening shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure that is persistent or severe, pain spreading to arm/jaw/back, fainting, bluish lips, confusion, coughing blood, oxygen level under 94% if you have a pulse oximeter, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening.

In the meantime: rest, hydrate, avoid strenuous exercise, and monitor temperature and breathing. Anxiety can absolutely amplify chest sensations, but we shouldn’t assume that without checking the medical side. You’re doing the right thing by asking and by thinking about protecting others.

query: i have covid 19 symptoms? like im not trying to be dramatic but im sitting here googling and it just makes me more nervous, my name is maria lopez and dob is 03/08/1991, i know u prob need that if u look me up, i think my chart thing says mrn hhc-772910 but im not totally sure bc i can never find the right paper when i need it. i dont know if i should go get tested or just stay home and wait it out, bc everything online says different stuff and im scared of going somewhere and getting other ppl sick if it is covid, but also scared of ignoring it if its not fine. i keep asking myself do i have covid 19 symptoms? and then i start panicking again, sorry. i live with family and im trying not to touch anything and staying mostly in my room but thats hard too, and i dont wanna freak everybody out if its just something else. if someone can call me at 267-555-0148 that would be easier than me trying to keep refreshing messages. i usually pick up meds at cvs on 9th & reed if that matters, but i havent gone yet bc idk what im supposed to take or not take. i dont have a regular dr appt set up or anything, im just confused about whether i need a test, urgent care, or just quarantine. i keep thinking maybe im overreacting but then with covid 19 stuff i dont wanna be dumb about it either. please just tell me what i should do first, like step 1, bc my brain is stuck on this and im worried.
target_response:
Maria, you’re not being dramatic—COVID worries can be stressful, and Googling often makes that worse. The good news is that most respiratory infections can be handled safely at home, but it’s smart to take precautions and test.

**Step 1: Test today if you can.** Use an at-home COVID rapid test, or schedule a test at a pharmacy/clinic. If you go out, wear a well-fitting mask and avoid close contact. If the first home test is negative but you still have symptoms, repeat it in **24–48 hours**, because early tests can miss COVID.

**Step 2: Stay home for now and reduce exposure to family.** Stay in your room as much as possible, wear a mask if you’re around others, wash hands, improve ventilation/open a window if safe, and avoid sharing cups/utensils. You don’t need to panic everyone—just say you may have a respiratory virus and are being careful.

**Step 3: Treat symptoms supportively.** For fever/aches/sore throat, you can use **acetaminophen or ibuprofen** as directed on the label, unless you’ve been told not to take them. Drink fluids, rest, use saline spray/steam for congestion, and honey/tea for cough. Avoid taking multiple cold/flu products that all contain acetaminophen.

COVID symptoms can overlap with **flu, RSV, common cold, strep throat, or allergies**, so testing helps. If you test positive and symptoms started within the last **5 days**, contact a clinician promptly to see if antiviral treatment is appropriate—especially if you are pregnant, immunocompromised, or have asthma, diabetes, heart/lung disease, kidney disease, or other higher-risk conditions.

**Seek urgent care now / call 911** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, oxygen level below 94% if you have a pulse ox, or a high fever that won’t come down.

If you’re using a patient portal, I can’t guarantee a phone call from here, so if you feel worse or need same-day guidance, call your clinic/urgent care directly rather than waiting on messages.

query: hi, im nadia van der merwe and sorry if this is a bit rambly but im really not sure what to do. should someone with a compromised immune system due to rheumatoid arthritis get the flu vaccine, especially during the covid-19 crisis? im asking bc my ra has been up and down and my immune system is not great from the meds, and every time i read something online it makes me more confused and then i panic. my rheumy is dr. thabo mthembu at rosebank rheumatology clinic and i did leave a msg there but they take ages to call back, so im trying to understand the basics before i go stand in a queue somewhere with everyone coughing around me. also, does the flu vaccine in south africa use live or dead viruses? like is it the actual live flu in the shot or is it killed/inactive, bc that part is whats scaring me the most with being immune compromised. i dont want to skip it if that makes me more at risk, but i also dont want to take something dumb during covid and end up worse. i normally get scripts filled at dis-chem at 50 bath ave and they said they have flu shots but the lady at the counter couldnt really answer about live vs dead and just said ask your doctor. my email is nadia.vdm74@gmail.com if needed but mostly i just need to know if generally ppl with rheumatoid arthritis and weak immune systems are meant to get the flu vaccine now or avoid it. im not asking for a whole consult, just worried and trying to be careful bc covid-19 is everywhere and its hard to know whats safe.
target_response:
Hi Nadia — it’s completely understandable to feel anxious with RA, immune-suppressing medicines, and all the mixed information online. In general, **people with rheumatoid arthritis, including those on immune-suppressing treatment, are recommended to get the annual flu vaccine** unless there is a specific contraindication.

The key point: **the standard flu injection used in South Africa is an inactivated vaccine** — meaning it contains **killed/inactive virus components**, not live flu virus. **It cannot give you influenza.** The main side effects are usually a sore arm, mild fever, tiredness or body aches for a day or two.

For people who are immunocompromised, we generally **avoid live vaccines** unless a specialist specifically advises otherwise. The flu vaccine you get as an injection at pharmacies/clinics is the inactivated one. Some countries have a live attenuated nasal-spray flu vaccine, but that is not the usual flu shot and would generally be avoided in immunosuppressed patients.

During the COVID-19 era, flu vaccination is still encouraged because it reduces your risk of severe flu, complications, and needing medical care. It also helps avoid the difficult situation of having flu and COVID circulating together. It won’t protect against COVID, so staying up to date with COVID boosters, masking in crowded indoor places, and hand hygiene still matter.

A few RA-specific points to discuss with Dr Thabo Mthembu’s rooms when they call back: if you are on **methotrexate**, some rheumatologists advise briefly holding it after the flu vaccine if your RA is stable, to improve vaccine response. If you are on **rituximab or high-dose steroids**, timing can matter. But for most RA meds, the flu shot is still advised.

To reduce exposure at Dis-Chem/clinic: phone ahead, ask for a quiet time, wear a well-fitting mask, avoid busy queues, and leave promptly after the observation period.

Seek urgent care if you develop severe shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, persistent high fever, dehydration, or a severe allergic reaction after any vaccine.

Bottom line: **don’t skip the flu vaccine because of RA—just make sure it’s the injectable inactivated flu shot and coordinate timing with your rheumatologist if possible.**

query: good day my 2 year old toddler maya johnson has been coughing and now she started to vomit when coughing, is it something i should be consent about or it's just flue? i dont know if im over thinking it but its been stressing me bad because she will cough hard like she cant stop and then she throws up, not alot every time but enough that i have to change her shirt and clean the bed and then she crys because shes tired. she still wants me to hold her and she is drinking some water but not like normal, and i keep thinking maybe its just the mucus making her gag but then i get scared bc she is so little. i called bright start pediatrics before but the nurse line was busy and i wasnt sure if i should wait or just take her in. my number is 267-555-0148 if somebody need to call me back because i may miss the app msg while watching her. i didnt give her much besides the little toddler cough stuff i had and some warm water, and i was going to ask at cvs on 22nd and south st what is ok for her age but i know they always say ask the doctor. she doesnt really say her chest hurts or anything bc she only 2, she just coughs and then vomit when coughing and then wants to sleep on me. no one in the house is really sick except maybe a runny nose last week so idk where this came from. should i be consent and go urgent care today or can this be flue and just watch her, i dont want to panic but i also dont want to wait if vomiting with cough is a bad sign.
target_response:
Good day — I’m sorry you and Maya are going through this. Coughing so hard that a toddler vomits can happen with viral colds/flu/RSV because mucus drains into the throat and triggers gagging, but repeated “can’t stop” coughing fits with vomiting can also be seen with things like whooping cough, asthma/reactive airway, croup, or pneumonia. Because Maya is only 2, is drinking less than normal, and is having exhausting coughing spells with vomiting, I would **try to have her seen today** by Bright Start Pediatrics or an urgent care, rather than just watching for several days.

Please seek **ER care now** if you notice any of these: fast or hard breathing, ribs/neck pulling in, nostrils flaring, blue/gray lips, wheezing/stridor, pauses in breathing, extreme sleepiness or hard to wake, signs of dehydration (no urine for 8+ hours, very dry mouth, no tears), persistent vomiting/not keeping fluids down, fever in the 104°F range or fever with worsening appearance, or if you feel she is getting worse quickly.

For now:  
- Offer **small frequent sips** of water or oral rehydration solution/Pedialyte; popsicles can help.  
- Use **saline drops/spray and gentle suction**, especially before sleep.  
- Run a **cool-mist humidifier** or sit with her in steamy bathroom air for 10–15 minutes.  
- Keep her slightly upright while supervised.  
- For a 2-year-old, **avoid over-the-counter cough/cold medicines** unless her doctor specifically says to use them; they often don’t help and can cause side effects. Honey may help cough **only if she is over 1 year old**—½ to 1 teaspoon as needed, especially before sleep.

If she has fever or discomfort, you can use acetaminophen/ibuprofen based on her weight and the package directions.

Also, please don’t post or rely on app messages for urgent callbacks—call the pediatric office again and choose any urgent option, or go to urgent care today. If her breathing looks abnormal at any point, go straight to the ER/call 911.

query: yes i had pneumonia and now have costochondritis. this is maria keller, i saw dr nina patel about it and my chart thing says mrn KC-33817, but i still feel like im just stuck in this loop and dont really know what to do next. i am just wondering if it will ever go away. i am active and miss my workouts so much, like i know that prob sounds dramatic but working out was my stress thing and now every time i try to start working out it comes back. even if i start slow, like just light weights or a short jog or some easy bike, i get that chest wall pain again and then i panic bc i remember the pneumonia and i worry something is wrong with my lungs even though they keep saying costochondritis. will i ever be able to work out again? it has been since april when i had pneumonia and i still am dealing with this. some days are better and then i think ok finally im past it, then i do one normal workout or even pushups or planks and boom the soreness/tight pain is back and i feel like im starting over. i dont want to be lazy or lose all my fitness, but i also dont want to make it worse forever. is there a point where rest doesnt help anymore? should i be stretching or not stretching? heat, ice, advil, nothing seems like a real fix. i keep googling and that just makes me more anxious bc some ppl say months and some say years. please tell me if this is normal after pneumonia or if i need more testing, bc im tired and honestly discouraged. email is maria.keller72@gmail.com if needed, sorry this is long i just miss feeling like myself.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re stuck in this cycle. It does **not** sound dramatic at all. When exercise is your stress outlet, being repeatedly limited by chest pain can feel really discouraging and anxiety-provoking, especially after pneumonia.

Costochondritis can absolutely linger after a respiratory infection like pneumonia, especially if there was a lot of coughing or inflammation around the ribs/chest wall. It often improves gradually, but it can flare with activities that load the rib cage: pushups, planks, heavy/light weights depending on movement, jogging impact, rowing, and even deep breathing with cardio. A flare after activity does **not automatically mean your lungs are worsening**, but I understand why it feels scary.

The key is usually not “total rest forever,” but a **more structured reset and return**:

- For 1–2 weeks, avoid the biggest triggers: pushups, planks, chest presses, dips, burpees, heavy upper-body lifting, and high-impact running.  
- Keep moving with low-irritation activity: walking, gentle cycling, lower-body strength that doesn’t strain the chest, mobility work.  
- Use heat before activity and ice after if sore.  
- Anti-inflammatories like ibuprofen/Advil can help if you’re able to take them safely, but they are not a cure by themselves.  
- Gentle stretching may help, but avoid aggressive chest opening stretches if they reproduce sharp pain.  
- Consider asking Dr. Patel about **physical therapy**. A PT can work on rib/thoracic mobility, posture, breathing mechanics, and a graded return-to-exercise plan.

A good rule: activity is acceptable if pain stays mild, doesn’t worsen during the session, and settles back to baseline within 24 hours. If it flares for days, that was too much too soon.

You should seek urgent care if you develop chest pressure not clearly reproducible with touch/movement, shortness of breath at rest, fainting, coughing blood, fever returning, oxygen levels low, new leg swelling, or pain radiating to jaw/arm/back.

Given this has lasted since April and is affecting your life, I do think it’s reasonable to follow up—not because this sounds dangerous, but because you deserve a clearer plan and reassurance. You likely *will* work out again, but it may need a slower, chest-wall-specific progression rather than repeated stop/start cycles.

query: hi, my name is maria bennett, dob 04/18/1989, and sorry if this is a dumb question but im getting myself worked up over it. is covering mouth and nose by mask sufficient to avoid corona exposure? can it not be transmitted through eyes and ears? i keep seeing ppl say wear a mask and dont touch your face, but then i start thinking ok well my eyes are still out and my ears too and im confused if thats like an open door for it or if im being ridiculous. i had called penn medicine urgent care earlier and they told me to ask my dr if i had more questions, but dr. kevin shah isnt answering til later and i dont wanna sit here spiraling all day. i live at 3400 spruce st and have to go downstairs for mail and laundry and ppl in my building are not being careful at all, like coughing in the hallway and no space in the elevator, so i put on a mask but then i keep wondering if that even does enough. my phone is 215-555-7349 if someone needs to reach me and my email is maria.bennett89@gmail.com but really im just trying to know basic risk. like if someone coughs near me and i have my mouth/nose covered, should i also have glasses or some kind of face shield? do regular glasses help any? and ears, can droplets go in there and infect you or no? i dont have symptoms right now, just nervous and trying not to do something stupid. also i dont wanna waste ppe or overreact, but i also dont wanna bring anything home. pls explain like normal person terms bc everything online is either scary or too technical.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. It makes sense that you’re trying to understand the “open doors” for infection, especially if people in your building aren’t being careful.

In plain terms: **COVID spreads mainly through the air you breathe in through your nose and mouth**—especially in indoor, poorly ventilated spaces and when people are close together. A **well-fitting mask covering both nose and mouth** is one of the most effective ways to reduce that risk.

**Eyes:** It *is possible* for droplets to land on the eye surface and contribute to infection, but this is generally a **less common route** than breathing in virus through the nose/mouth. In higher-risk settings—healthcare, crowded indoor spaces, someone coughing very close to you—eye protection can add another layer. **Regular glasses help a little** by blocking some droplets from the front, but they don’t seal around the eyes. **Goggles or a face shield** offer more protection, but a face shield should be **in addition to a mask**, not instead of one.

**Ears:** Your ears are **not considered a meaningful route of COVID infection**. The ear canal ends at the eardrum; droplets going into the ear are not like entering the nose or mouth. You do not need ear protection for COVID prevention.

For mail/laundry/elevators, reasonable steps are:
- Wear a **well-fitting mask**—ideally KN95/N95/KF94 if you have one.
- Avoid crowded elevators if possible; wait for an empty one or take stairs if safe.
- Keep trips brief and avoid standing close to someone coughing.
- Wash or sanitize hands after touching shared surfaces.
- Try not to touch your eyes/nose/mouth until hands are clean.

If someone coughs near you while you’re masked, it doesn’t automatically mean you’ll get infected. Move away, keep your mask on, wash hands, and monitor. Testing is reasonable if you develop symptoms or have a close/prolonged exposure.

Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or persistent high fever**.

Bottom line: **masking your nose and mouth is the big protection. Eye protection is optional extra for higher-risk close indoor encounters. Ear protection isn’t needed.**

query: hi this is tina morales, i know this is prob a dumb msg but i am experiencing headache (severity: moderate) (time frame: gradual) (occurred: 1 - 3 hours) (side: front) (worsened by: psychological stress, being tired, menstruation, skipping meals) (unaffected by: bending forward, changes in sleep pattern, sneeze or like moving around really). i have my info under dob 04/18/1991 and i think my mrn is HMC-77291 if that helps, i always forget if u need that first. it started kinda slowly while i was doing emails and then got annoying in the front of my head, not like worst pain ever but enough that i keep touching my forehead and getting worried. i did skip lunch bc work was nuts and im on my period too, plus i have been stressed and tired so i know those are probably not helping. i dont think it changed when i bent forward to pick up laundry, and i didnt notice it from sneezing either. i slept normal for me last night so not sure if that matters. i see dr. reena patel usually but i didnt wanna bug the office if its just a normal headache thing. i took water and tried to eat a granola bar but im still anxious bc headaches make me spiral, like what if its something bad even tho its just moderate. if i need meds i usually use cvs on 9th ave, but i havent taken anything yet bc i wasnt sure what is ok with cramps and headache together. should i just rest/eat and watch it or do i need to be seen today?
target_response:
Hi Tina — not a dumb message at all. Headaches can be really anxiety-provoking, and the pattern you describe is reassuring in several ways: gradual onset, moderate severity, front-of-head location, and clear common triggers present today — skipped lunch, stress, fatigue, and menstruation. The fact that it is **not worse with bending forward, sneezing, or movement** also makes sinus pressure or certain pressure-related headaches less likely.

Most likely possibilities include a **tension-type headache**, a **menstrual/hormonal headache**, or a headache triggered by **low blood sugar/dehydration/stress**. A migraine is also possible even without severe pain, nausea, or light sensitivity, especially around your period.

For now, it would be reasonable to treat this at home and monitor, assuming you do not have any red flags below:

1. **Eat a real meal or substantial snack** with protein/carbs if you can, and keep sipping water or an electrolyte drink.  
2. Rest your eyes/screens for 20–30 minutes if possible; try a dim room, cool compress on the forehead, or gentle neck/shoulder stretching.  
3. For medication:  
   - **Ibuprofen 400 mg with food** can help both headache and menstrual cramps, as long as you are not pregnant, do not have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are not on blood thinners, and have been told to avoid NSAIDs.  
   - Alternatively, **acetaminophen 650–1000 mg** is okay for headache/cramps if you avoid exceeding the daily maximum and do not have significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use.  
   - Do not combine ibuprofen with naproxen, but acetaminophen can generally be taken with either if needed.

You likely do **not** need to be seen today if it improves over the next few hours with food, fluids, rest, and/or medication.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop: “worst headache of life” or sudden thunderclap onset, weakness/numbness, facial droop, confusion, fainting, trouble speaking, vision loss, fever with stiff neck, repeated vomiting, severe eye pain, headache after head injury, or a rapidly worsening headache. Also contact us today if it persists despite treatment, becomes severe, or feels very different from your usual headaches.

query: my friend linda carver suffers from diagnosed bronchiectias and gets pneumonia constantly. she is 72, dob 11/18/1953 if that matters, and i am asking bc i feel like we are just watching the same thing happen over and over and no one really tells her what to do between flare ups. each time they give her antibotics for a week and then the xray will show the pneumonia gone, however, it contantly returns. she has been to mayo, iowa city had many test, even has a chart number i wrote down as MC-77842-Q bc she forgets it and i end up helping with forms. it's beginning to take a toll on her, like she acts brave but she is tired and scared and i can tell. i would say she had pneumonia about 6 times last year and so far 2 or 3 this year. her regular lung dr is dr alan pritchard and he is nice but it feels like we leave with the same plan every single time. she uses medicare aarp plan #9XK-441-208 and gets most meds at walgreens on 1st ave, so she is able to pick them up, but then a few weeks later she is coughing again and back for another xray. no one has really said if this is normal with bronchiectias or if there should be some daily prevention thing, breathing treatment, culture, vest, different antibotic, anything. she has had so many test and trips and it is wearing her down bad, plus she is embarassed to keep calling. any suggestions on what i should tell her to ask for or what kind of specialist maybe, bc i dont want to push but this cycle cant be good.
target_response:
I’m sorry Linda is going through this. With diagnosed bronchiectasis, repeated “pneumonias” or flare-ups can happen, but **6 in a year plus 2–3 already this year is not something to simply accept as inevitable**. Bronchiectasis usually needs a **between-flare prevention plan**, not just a week of antibiotics each time.

A few concrete things she can ask Dr. Pritchard or a bronchiectasis-focused pulmonologist about:

1. **Sputum cultures before antibiotics**  
   Ideally, when she starts coughing more or producing darker/thicker mucus, she should submit a sputum sample *before* starting antibiotics. Ask for routine bacterial culture and also **AFB/NTM testing** — nontuberculous mycobacteria can mimic recurrent pneumonia and affects treatment choices.

2. **Airway clearance regimen**  
   Many patients need daily mucus-clearance therapy, such as an **Aerobika/Acapella device**, chest physiotherapy, or a **high-frequency chest wall oscillation vest**. Nebulized **saline**, sometimes hypertonic saline, and/or a bronchodilator before clearance may help. A respiratory therapist can teach this.

3. **Longer or targeted antibiotics**  
   In bronchiectasis, flares often require **10–14 days** of antibiotics, guided by prior cultures, not always a standard 7-day course. If she grows bacteria like *Pseudomonas*, management may change.

4. **Prevention medicines**  
   If she has frequent exacerbations despite airway clearance, some patients benefit from long-term prevention such as **azithromycin 3 times weekly** or inhaled antibiotics — but only after checking for NTM infection, heart rhythm/QT risk, hearing issues, and drug interactions.

5. **Look for underlying contributors**  
   Ask whether she has been evaluated for immune deficiency, aspiration/swallowing problems, reflux, asthma/COPD overlap, ABPA/allergic fungal disease, and whether her CT scan pattern explains repeated infections.

6. **Consider a dedicated bronchiectasis clinic**  
   A pulmonologist with bronchiectasis/NTM expertise, and sometimes infectious disease, can be very helpful.

Urgent care is warranted for worsening shortness of breath, oxygen levels below her usual, chest pain, confusion, coughing blood, high fever, dehydration, or rapid decline.

Most importantly: she should not feel embarrassed to call. Recurrent flares are exactly what her lung team needs to know about, and she deserves a written action/prevention plan.

query: do you think this corona virus stuff will ever go away? i'm tired of being a prisoner in my own home cause of everything getting cancelled, im not working and i cant volunteer cause of a bad knee and cant lift heavy stuff. my name is donna marie kelso and i swear i was not always like this, i used to be busy all day and now i just sit here watching the news and getting more scared and mad at the same time. i keep waiting for somebody to say ok its done now, go back to normal, but every day its another thing closed, another appt moved, another person saying dont go out unless you have to. i know ppl have it worse but i feel like im losing my mind being stuck here. my knee is bad so even the volunteer food boxes thing said i cant really help cause i cant lift the crates, and i feel useless. i tried calling the nurse line from my aetna silver hmo #AET-4420197 but i got put on hold forever and then they just said wash hands and stay home, which i am doing already. if anyone calls me back my number is 267-555-0198 but honestly idk what they can even tell me. i also gotta pick up my regular meds at rite aid on 22nd and oregon and im scared to even go in there but delivery takes too long and my neighbor is also scared. so when will this be over? like weeks, months, next year? are we just supposed to keep cancelling birthdays and church and work and everything forever? i dont want to be dramatic but this feels like jail except nobody tells you the release date, and im just tired and cranky and worried about money too.
target_response:
Donna Marie, I’m really sorry you’re feeling this trapped. What you’re describing—fear, anger, boredom, feeling useless, worrying about money, and getting more upset the more news you watch—is a very human reaction to a situation that has gone on longer than anyone wanted.

I don’t think we will be “stuck like this forever,” but I also can’t honestly give you a single release date. With viruses like coronavirus, things usually improve in phases: fewer severe cases, more predictable waves, better treatments/vaccines, and more things reopening with precautions. It may not disappear completely, but it can become something we manage rather than something that shuts life down.

A few practical steps for right now:

1. **Limit news exposure**: Pick 1–2 times daily from a reliable source, then turn it off. Constant updates can keep your body in “alarm mode.”
2. **Create a daily schedule**: Wake time, meals, a phone call, gentle movement, one chore, one enjoyable thing. Structure helps the brain feel less imprisoned.
3. **Stay useful without lifting**: Many volunteer roles can be phone-based—calling isolated seniors, church check-ins, organizing donation lists, writing cards, helping schedule deliveries. Your bad knee does not make you useless.
4. **For your medications**: Call the Rite Aid directly and ask about curbside pickup, drive-up options, mail delivery, or whether they can transfer prescriptions to a pharmacy with faster delivery. Also ask your prescriber/insurance about a 90-day supply so you don’t have to go as often. If you must go in, wear a well-fitting mask, go at a quiet time, use hand sanitizer, keep distance, and leave promptly.
5. **Money stress**: If you’re not working, consider calling 211 for local help with food, rent, utilities, and benefits.

Please seek urgent help now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, dehydration, or if you feel you might hurt yourself or can’t stay safe. For emotional crisis, call/text **988**.

Also, for your privacy, avoid sharing insurance numbers or phone numbers in messages. You deserve support through this—not just “wash your hands and stay home.”

query: hi my 3yrold mason ellis having rash and im getting worried bc its not just one little spot anymore. specially under armpits and his thighs too, like the folds where he sweats and runs around all day. he can’t sleep at night due to that and he keeps scratching and crying and then i get no sleep either, last night he was up so much i almost just packed him in the car but i don’t want to take him to hospital due to virus! his dob is 09/14/2022 if that matters for what creams are ok for his age. i’ve tried lotion to hydrate, just the regular baby lotion we had at home, and i tried not to put too much bc idk if that makes it worse or traps heat. he had minor eczema when born but not too serious, like dr. priya nanda told us back then just keep him moisturized and it calmed down, but this looks more angry and red especially under both armpits and inner thighs. no new soap that i can think of, same laundry stuff, but he has been sweating alot and wearing pull ups at night and sometimes tight shorts so idk if its rubbing or heat rash or eczema coming back or maybe allergy. i keep checking if it looks infected and i dont know what im even looking for tbh. can i use hydrocortisone or aquaphor or oatmeal bath or should i avoid all that? i can go to cvs on 9th st if theres something over the counter thats safe but i really dont wanna sit in urgent care with everyone coughing. what can i do tonight so he can sleep and when is it serious enough that i have no choice but hospital?
target_response:
Hi — I’m sorry Mason is so uncomfortable. A red, itchy rash in the armpit and inner-thigh folds in a sweaty, active 3-year-old is commonly from **heat/friction with an eczema flare**, and sometimes **yeast/intertrigo** can develop in warm moist folds. Less commonly it can become **bacterial infection**, so it’s good you’re watching.

For **tonight**, you can try:

1. **Cool/soothe:** Lukewarm bath (not hot). An **oatmeal bath** is okay. Pat dry gently, especially in folds.
2. **Keep dry + reduce rubbing:** Loose cotton pajamas, avoid tight shorts, keep him cool. Change sweaty clothes quickly. If pull-ups are contributing, use a generous **zinc oxide diaper barrier** where the pull-up rubs.
3. **Moisturize/barrier:** Use **Aquaphor or plain petroleum jelly** thinly over irritated dry areas after the bath. Avoid scented baby lotion for now, as fragrance can sting/irritate.
4. **Hydrocortisone:** For very itchy, red eczema-looking areas, **OTC hydrocortisone 1%** is generally safe at his age. Apply a **very thin layer** 1–2 times daily for up to **3–5 days**. In skin folds, don’t overuse it and don’t apply to open, oozing, or clearly infected skin.
5. **If it looks yeast-like:** Bright red, moist rash with little “satellite” red bumps around the edges in folds may be yeast. In that case, **clotrimazole 1%** cream twice daily can help; avoid using steroid alone on that type because it can worsen yeast.

For itching, you can ask your pharmacist about **cetirizine** dosing for his age/weight; it can reduce itch and help sleep. I’d avoid sedating medicines unless your pediatrician advises.

Please contact his pediatrician/portal tomorrow if it’s spreading, disrupting sleep, or not improving in 2–3 days—photos can be very helpful.

Go to urgent care/ER despite virus concerns if he has **fever**, rapidly worsening redness, warmth/swelling, significant pain, pus, honey-colored crusting, red streaks, blisters/peeling skin, rash near eyes/mouth, lethargy, trouble breathing, facial/lip swelling, or he seems very ill.

query: my 22 yo son matthew keller travelled by plane between baltimore (bwi) & rhode island on Southwest WN-3842 on 03/14 and i know im prob being anxious but realistically, what are the risks of covid 19? he has no symptoms at all right now, no fever no cough no sore throat no sob nothing, and there have not been any reported cases on his flight that i can find, i keep checking the airline emails and the state pages and all that. i am quarantining him as best i can at home at 2217 maple ave, lancaster pa, basically he is in the back bedroom with the window cracked and im leaving food outside the door like hes some kind of raccoon lol but im nervous bc we have my husband here too and i dont want to overreact but also dont want to be stupid. he says the plane was not full, he wore a mask some of the time but not the whole time bc he said it was annoying, and he did use the bathroom at the airport so of course now im thinking about every handle and seat and tray table. he got back and i told him no friends, no store, no gym, just stay put. if he stays totally fine, how many days should i really keep him away from us? i have been wiping stuff down and making him use a separate towel and cup. if anyone needs to call me back my cell is 443-555-0196, but mostly i just need a straight answer bc all the info online is either scary or vague and im trying to be reasonable while still protecting everyone.
target_response:
I understand why you’re anxious—you’re trying to protect your family and sort through a lot of confusing information. The straight answer: **if Matthew has no symptoms and there is no known COVID case linked to his flight, his risk is probably low, but not zero.**

The biggest COVID risk is **close indoor exposure to an infected person**, especially unmasked and for a longer period. Airplanes are not risk-free, but the air filtration/ventilation is generally good, and a not-full plane lowers risk. The airport bathroom, tray table, handles, etc. are **much lower risk** than breathing shared air near an infected person—handwashing matters more than obsessively wiping every surface.

A reasonable plan if he remains well:

- **Have him stay mostly separate for 5 days after travel**, especially if your husband is older or has medical risks.
- **Test around day 3–5 after the flight** with a rapid antigen test or PCR. If using rapid tests and you want more reassurance, repeat 24–48 hours later.
- If he is negative and still symptom-free, he can be around the household more normally, but I’d still have him **wear a mask around you through day 10** and avoid close face-to-face time.
- The most conservative approach is separation for **10 days**; **14 days** is the older/maximal cautious window because incubation can rarely be that long, but most cases show up within 5 days.

What you’re already doing—no friends, no gym, separate towel/cup, food outside the door—is cautious and reasonable, though you don’t need to treat him like a raccoon forever! If possible, have him use a separate bathroom; if not, have him mask when leaving the room, wash hands well, and wipe high-touch surfaces after use.

Watch for: fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, loss of taste/smell, fatigue, headache, GI symptoms. Seek urgent care if he develops **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips, dehydration, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse ox, or persistent high fever**.

I can’t call from here, but based on what you described, you’re being appropriately cautious—not irrational.

query: hi, my name is melissa grady and i use athraway chronic for rheumatoid arthritis, will this complicate things relating to the corona virus? im trying not to panic but its hard bc every thing online says immune system this and immune system that and i dont really know if my med counts like that or if its a big deal. i been on it for awhile for the ra and most days it keeps me moving, hands still ache but not as bad, so i dont want to just stop it and then flare up and be useless, but i also dont wanna be dumb if this corona virus thing is worse for ppl like me. dr. nina patel is the one who usually handles my ra stuff but i havent gotten a clear answer yet and i keep missing calls bc im at home dealing with regular life stuff and then i start googling and that makes it worse lol. i have aetna hmo #AET-44219-77 if that matters for where i should call or if i need to be seen, but mostly im just asking like should i keep taking athraway chronic like normal or is this something that could make getting corona virus more serious? i dont have big symptoms right now besides the normal aches from the rheumatoid arthritis and a little worry cough that is prob just anxiety, but i keep thinking what if i get exposed and then the meds make it worse. sorry this is rambling, i just need plain english bc i dont understand all the warnings and i dont know if i should avoid people extra or call my doctor again or just chill.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you are not rambling. This is a very understandable worry, and I’m glad you asked before stopping anything.

Plain English version: **many rheumatoid arthritis medicines do “turn down” parts of the immune system**, which can slightly increase the risk of infections or make some infections harder to fight. That said, **stopping RA medication on your own can cause a flare**, and a bad flare may require steroids like prednisone, which can also raise infection risk. So in most cases, if you are **well and not clearly infected**, we usually advise patients to **keep taking their RA medicine as prescribed until their rheumatologist tells them otherwise**.

I’m not 100% sure what “Athraway Chronic” is from the name alone, so please check the bottle for the **generic/active ingredient** and dose. Different RA meds have different advice — methotrexate, biologic injections/infusions, JAK inhibitors, hydroxychloroquine, prednisone, etc. are handled differently. If you can, message or call Dr. Nina Patel’s office with: medication name, dose, how often you take it, and whether you’ve had any COVID exposure or symptoms.

For now, since you only have your usual RA aches and a mild “worry cough,” I would **not stop the medication today** just because of fear. Do monitor yourself. If the cough becomes persistent, or you develop fever, sore throat, congestion, body aches beyond your usual RA pattern, loss of taste/smell, or known exposure, take a COVID test and call your doctor promptly. Because of RA and possible immune-suppressing medication, you may qualify for early treatment such as antivirals if you test positive.

Practical steps: stay up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters, avoid crowded poorly ventilated indoor spaces when possible, wear a good mask in higher-risk settings, wash hands, and ask sick visitors to stay away. Don’t share your insurance/member number in general messages unless it’s a secure medical portal; Aetna member services can help identify in-network urgent care if needed.

**Seek urgent care now** for trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels under 92% if you use a pulse oximeter.

query: my 19 year old son matthew kline was diagnosed with pneumonia 5 months ago and was given antibiotics but with minimal results. unfortunately he still suffers from the same symptoms but since he is in the military he refuses to go back to the doctor for fear of reprisal. his cough is still deep and his energy is low. are there any long term repercussions for his lungs if he continues without treatment. i keep trying to tell him this isnt about being weak or getting in trouble, its just his health, but he says if he goes back to naval branch health clinic miramar they will put something in his record or his chain will think hes trying to get out of work or training or whatever. he is so young and stubborn and acts like im over reacting, but i hear him on the phone and that cough is still that heavy chesty cough, not just a little tickle, and he sounds worn out all the time. the dr before was dr. anika patel and they did give him antibiotics but it never really seemed to knock it out all the way, like maybe a little better for a short time but then basically same symptoms hanging around. his insurance is tricare west #TW-8841-2097 but he wont even use it bc he is scared of reprisal and keeps saying mom leave it alone. i even told him to just call me from my cell 619-555-0148 after sick call or whatever and i would talk him thru it, but he just brushes me off. i dont want to panic him or make him mad, but i also dont want him ending up with lung damage just bc he is afraid to be seen again. can pneumonia linger like this for 5 months, and if his xray was never rechecked could there be scarring or something permanent happening while he keeps ignoring it?
target_response:
I understand why you’re worried. A deep, “chesty” cough and low energy **5 months after pneumonia is not something I’d consider normal recovery**, especially if antibiotics only helped briefly. Many people feel tired for a few weeks after pneumonia, and a cough can linger up to 6–8 weeks, but **persistent symptoms for months should be re-evaluated**.

There are several possibilities besides “the pneumonia never went away”: incomplete treatment or resistant infection, asthma/reactive airway after infection, pertussis, vaping/smoke-related bronchitis, tuberculosis exposure, or—in Southern California—fungal infections such as coccidioidomycosis (“Valley fever”). Less commonly, complications like scarring, bronchiectasis, fluid around the lung, or an abscess can occur. A repeat chest X-ray is often important to confirm the pneumonia cleared.

Yes, there can be long-term repercussions if an ongoing lung problem is ignored—chronic inflammation can lead to reduced exercise tolerance, airway damage/bronchiectasis, or scarring in some cases. The goal is to catch and treat the cause before it becomes harder to reverse.

What Matthew needs is not “toughing it out,” but a straightforward medical reassessment: vital signs and oxygen level, lung exam, repeat chest X-ray, and possibly bloodwork, sputum testing, TB/fungal testing, and/or spirometry depending on findings. He should be seen **within the next few days**, sooner if worsening.

Urgent care/ER now if he has shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, coughing blood, blue lips, fainting, high fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, confusion, or severe weakness.

Regarding his military concern: seeking care for persistent respiratory symptoms is not weakness—it’s medical readiness. Untreated illness is more likely to impair training/work than getting evaluated. He can also ask about speaking with a patient advocate or his corpsman/PCM about confidentiality and how documentation works.

Since he’s 19, you can’t force him unless there’s immediate danger. A non-confrontational approach may help: “I’m not trying to get in your business—I’m worried this hasn’t cleared in 5 months. Please get one repeat check so you know your lungs are okay.”

Also, for privacy, avoid sharing insurance or phone numbers in online messages.

query: hi, this is nisha patel, dob 03/14/1992, i dont usually msg like this but i am kinda worried and also at work trying to not panic. body aching all of a sudden, like my whole back and legs and arms just started hurting out of no where, head heavy and paining too, sneezing a lot, somewhat fever since yesterday. i didnt check exact temp yet bc my thermometer battery is dead but i feel warm and then cold, and my head feels like pressure sitting on it. i was fine before yesterday morning, just tired, then by evening it hit me all at once. i dont know if its flu or cold or allergy or what, bc sneezing is there but the body aching is what is making me nervous. i called Penn Medicine South Philly Urgent Care but was on hold and then had to hang up. if someone can call me back at 215-604-7718 that would help bc i dont know if i should come in or just rest and take something. i live near 1840 South St, Philadelphia PA and can get a ride if needed but i really dont want to sit in waiting room if this is nothing. i also have a old patient number i think MRN SP-447190 from last time i went there, not sure if that helps. i usually pick meds at CVS on 10th and Reed so if u say take something or send anything there thats easiest. sorry for long msg, my head is paining and heavy so im typing messy, just need to know what to do since the aching came all of a sudden and fever feeling started yesterday.
pii_units: Penn Medicine South Philly Urgent Care
target_response:
Hi Nisha — I’m sorry you’re feeling so awful, especially while you’re at work. Sudden onset of **body aches (back/legs/arms), headache/heavy pressure, chills/feverish feeling, and sneezing** most often fits a **viral infection**, especially **flu or COVID**. Allergies can cause sneezing and head pressure, but they usually **do not cause fever/chills or significant whole-body aches**, so this sounds more infectious than allergy.

**What to do now:**
1. **Go home if you can**, rest, hydrate, and avoid close contact with others. Wear a mask if you need to be around people.
2. **Check your temperature** when you can replace the thermometer battery.
3. If available, take a **home COVID test today** and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative. If flu testing is available through urgent care/clinic, it may be useful since symptoms started yesterday.
4. For aches/headache/fever:
   - **Acetaminophen (Tylenol) 650–1000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed**; max **3000 mg/day** unless your clinician has told you otherwise.
   - OR **ibuprofen 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food** if you are not pregnant, do not have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are not on blood thinners, and can take NSAIDs safely.
   - You can also use saline spray, warm fluids, and rest.

Because this started within ~48 hours, if it is flu, an antiviral like **oseltamivir (Tamiflu)** may help most if started early—especially if you are pregnant, have asthma/heart/lung disease, diabetes, immune suppression, or other higher-risk conditions. That would require testing/evaluation or a clinician prescribing it.

**Please seek urgent care today or ER care immediately** if you develop: trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion/fainting, severe/worst headache, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, dehydration, bluish lips, fever ≥103°F, fever lasting >3 days, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

If you’re stable and breathing comfortably, it’s reasonable to rest at home today and test, but if you want confirmation or treatment options, urgent care is appropriate. I can’t place a callback from here, so please contact the urgent care/your primary care office directly again if possible.

query: hi, i am maria bell, dob 03/14/1979, and i am recovering from pnuemonia. i was seen at harborview family clinic by dr. kevin patel and my papers say MRN HMC-77219, i keep staring at them like they are gonna explain this but they dont really. i was thinking that that i tore a muscle coughing but now i am beginning to wonder. on the right side of my chest, under my breast it hurts when i cough. it hurts when i press on it, but when i pinchthe muscles with my fingers i have no tenderness, which is why im confused and prob overthinking it. it hurts as i go to touch my toes, like that bending forward motion makes it grab under there and then i get scared to move too much. could i be having a problem with an organ under my ribs ? i have no pain on my left side. i dont know if this is just from coughing so hard for days with the pneumonia or if its something else like gallbladder or liver or whatever is even on that side, i feel dumb asking but its freaking me out. i already picked up the antibiotics and inhaler stuff from cvs on 18th ave and ive been trying to rest, drink water, sleep propped up, all that. the cough is still there some and every time it hits this spot hurts again so then i start poking it and making myself more nervous. i dont have sharp pain just sitting still, mostly when coughing/pressing/bending, but im scared because it feels like under the ribs and not just on top. do i need to go back in or is this normal after pnuemonia coughing?
target_response:
Hi Maria — you are not “dumb” for asking. Chest/rib pain during recovery from pneumonia can be scary, especially when coughing keeps re-triggering it.

From what you describe — **right-sided pain under the breast/ribs that hurts with coughing, pressing on the area, and bending forward, but is not sharp at rest** — the most likely causes are **chest wall irritation** from days of coughing: an intercostal muscle strain, rib joint inflammation/costochondritis, or irritation of the lining around the lung from the pneumonia. These can feel “deep” or “under the ribs” even when they are coming from the rib muscles/joints. Pinching the surface muscle not being tender does not rule this out, because the irritated area can be between the ribs or where the ribs attach.

A gallbladder or liver problem is **less likely** based on your description, because those pains usually are not mainly triggered by coughing/pressing/bending. Gallbladder pain is often deeper in the right upper abdomen, may come after fatty meals, and can be associated with nausea/vomiting.

What you can do now:
- **Continue the antibiotics and inhaler exactly as prescribed.**
- Try not to keep poking the area — it can make soreness and anxiety worse.
- When you cough, **hold a pillow firmly against that spot** to “splint” it.
- Use **acetaminophen or ibuprofen** for pain if you have been told these are safe for you. Avoid ibuprofen/NSAIDs if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are on blood thinners, or were told not to take them.
- Heat or ice to the area may help.
- Gentle movement is okay, but avoid heavy lifting or deep bending if it “grabs.”

You should **call Harborview Family Clinic/Dr. Patel’s office** if the pain is worsening, not improving over the next few days, or if your pneumonia symptoms are not clearly improving after 48–72 hours on antibiotics.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop **worsening shortness of breath, chest pain at rest, blue lips, fainting, coughing blood, persistent high fever, oxygen levels low if you check them, severe right upper belly pain, repeated vomiting, yellowing of the skin/eyes, or new calf swelling/pain.**

query: hi im tiana brooks and i feel a bit embarassed writing this but i dont really know what else to do. hpv virus has affected my sex life so much, like its honestly making me scared of my own body. i bleed a lot during sex and on my period. i did a cervical cauterisation at st marys womens clinic after dr. melissa kwan said it might help with the bleeding/spotting, but my period is still the same and i’m scared to have sex due to bleeding as i was once hospitalised for it. it was so scary bc it wasnt just a little spotting, it was like a lot and i panicked and now every time my partner even tries to be intimate i get tense and then i just say no or cry or make an excuse. i know hpv is common and everyone says dont worry too much but it feels like my whole sex life is ruined and i cant relax at all. my period is still heavy and messy and i get scared when i see blood after sex even if its not as bad as before. i keep thinking what if the cauterisation didnt work or what if something else is wrong with my cervix and im ignoring it. i dont want to keep going back over and over if they just tell me its hpv again, but also i dont want to end up hospitalised again. should i avoid sex completely for now? is there something i can use or ask for so i dont bleed so much, or is this just how its going to be? any tips on what i can do?
target_response:
Hi Tiana — please don’t feel embarrassed. What you’re describing sounds genuinely frightening, especially after being hospitalised for bleeding. It makes complete sense that your body now “braces” for intimacy and that sex feels scary rather than enjoyable.

A few important points: HPV is very common, but **heavy bleeding during sex and heavy periods should not be automatically written off as “just HPV.”** Cervical cauterisation can help if bleeding is coming from a friable area on the cervix, cervical ectropion, or inflammation — but it **won’t usually fix heavy menstrual bleeding**, and it may not solve bleeding if there’s another cause such as cervical polyps, infection/cervicitis, fibroids, hormonal issues, endometriosis/adenomyosis, or less commonly abnormal cervical cells.

I’d recommend booking a follow-up with Dr. Kwan or another gynaecologist and being very direct: “I was hospitalised for post-sex bleeding, I’m still having heavy periods, and I’m scared to have sex.” Ask whether you need:  
- a speculum exam to check the cervix has healed after cauterisation  
- confirmation your Pap/HPV results are up to date  
- colposcopy/biopsy if indicated  
- swabs for infections/STIs  
- pregnancy test if relevant  
- pelvic ultrasound for fibroids/polyps  
- blood tests for anaemia/iron levels, thyroid, and clotting if bleeding is very heavy

For now, it’s reasonable to **avoid penetrative sex until you’re reassessed**, especially if bleeding is more than light spotting, painful, or triggering panic. You don’t have to avoid intimacy entirely — focus on non-penetrative sex, closeness, and rebuilding safety. If/when you try penetration again: go slowly, use plenty of lubricant, consider condoms, stop immediately with pain or bleeding, and don’t push through fear.

There are treatments for heavy bleeding — this is not necessarily “how it will always be.” Options include tranexamic acid during periods, NSAIDs if safe for you, hormonal pills/patch/ring, progesterone options, or a hormonal IUD, depending on the cause and your preferences.

Seek urgent care if you soak a pad/tampon every hour, pass large clots, feel faint/dizzy, have severe pelvic pain, fever, foul discharge, or heavy bleeding after sex.

query: i'm finding difficult to maintain precisely 6 ft in grocery stores and i know that prob sounds dumb but its making me spiral. my name is maria elena rojas, dob 04/18/1989, and i live in philadelphia, pa and i usually shop at trader joe's on 2121 market st bc its close and usually not too bad, but lately every aisle feels way too narrow. today, as i was leaving, someone entering the store that was (possibly) 3 ft away was coughing lightly and i took a shower when i got home. like i literally came in, put my keys down, wiped my phone, threw clothes in laundry, showered, and still keep thinking what if i walked thru their cough cloud or whatever. i'm a hypochondriac. possible covid-19? i wasnt standing there talking to them, it was just passing by near the doors for maybe 2 seconds, but my brain keeps replaying it like i did something reckless. i dont have fever or cough right now, just anxious and kind of tight chest from panic maybe, and im trying not to google bc that makes it worse. i put my number 215-555-2846 on my chart before but idk if someone actually calls back for questions like this, so im asking here. i also emailed the portal from maria.rojas89@mailhaven.com but it said 24-48 hrs and thats not helping me tonight. do i need to isolate from my roommate over this one quick exposure or just monitor? should i get tested now or is it too soon / not enough exposure? sorry i know this is probably annoying, i just cant tell what is normal caution anymore vs me being me.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not annoying, and it doesn’t sound dumb. What you’re describing is a very common anxiety spiral: a brief, uncertain exposure gets replayed as if it were a definite danger.

Based on what you described — passing someone near the door for about **2 seconds**, not stopping to talk, and not being in a prolonged close interaction — this would generally be considered a **low-risk exposure**, even if they coughed lightly. COVID transmission is more likely with longer time indoors near someone infected, especially repeated or sustained contact. A quick pass-by is not zero risk, but it is **not the kind of exposure that usually requires isolation from a roommate**.

What I’d recommend tonight:

- **You do not need to isolate from your roommate** solely because of this brief encounter.  
- Monitor yourself for symptoms over the next few days: fever, new cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, loss of smell/taste, or worsening fatigue.  
- Testing **right now is likely too soon** to be useful if this just happened today. If you develop symptoms, test then. If you remain well but want reassurance, testing around **day 3–5 after exposure** is more informative.  
- Showering, laundering clothes, and wiping your phone are more than enough; COVID is mainly spread through the air, not from clothing after a brief store trip.

For future grocery trips, it may help to wear a well-fitting mask, go at off-hours, use a list to minimize time inside, or consider pickup/delivery when your anxiety is running high. Those are reasonable precautions — not failures.

The chest tightness you’re noticing can absolutely happen with panic/anxiety, especially when you’re scanning your body. Try slow breathing: inhale 4 seconds, exhale 6–8 seconds, repeat for a few minutes.

Please seek urgent care or emergency help if you develop **significant trouble breathing, persistent chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen levels low if you monitor them, or symptoms rapidly worsening**.

I can’t guarantee a phone callback through this chat, so if you need direct clinic follow-up, calling the office line is best. But from what you described, this sounds like **monitoring, not isolation or immediate testing**.

query: hi, its maria lopez, dob 06/14/1989, i know this is prob a dumb question but im kinda spiraling and reading too much online. what symptoms would make you think i do not have covid-19. i have a bad cold, but got diarrhea twice, so does diarrhea mean it is not covid? like i keep seeing ppl say stomach stuff can happen and then other ppl say no its just a cold or food or nerves, so now im confused and i dont wanna overreact but also dont wanna be the person who ignores it. i messaged dr. kevin patel earlier but i havent heard back yet and i keep refreshing the portal like a crazy person. i can be reached at 267-555-0198 if someone needs to call bc typing it all out makes it sound worse maybe. i just feel like with a bad cold i would expect cold stuff, but the diarrhea twice threw me off and now im wondering if that makes covid less likely or if it doesnt mean anything at all. i havent been able to think straight bc every symptom list is different and then my family keeps asking me if i should isolate and im like i dont even know. i dont want to waste a test if this is just a regular cold, but i also dont want to go around anyone if it could be covid. so basically im asking if there are certain symptoms that point away from covid, or if diarrhea can still fit with it. sorry this is rambling, im anxious and just trying to figure out what to watch for and what would make this more or less concerning.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. Symptom lists online can be confusing because COVID can look very different from person to person.

**Diarrhea does *not* mean it is “not COVID.”** COVID can cause GI symptoms like diarrhea, nausea, or stomach upset, sometimes along with typical “cold” symptoms. Having diarrhea twice could also be from food, stress/anxiety, another virus, or something unrelated — but it does **not** rule COVID in or out.

There also really aren’t symptoms that *reliably* prove you do **not** have COVID. Some things may point more toward other causes — for example, itchy/watery eyes and sneezing with known allergies, or sudden diarrhea after a specific food — but with a bad cold plus GI symptoms, the safest assumption is that a contagious virus is possible.

**What I’d recommend:**
1. **Take a home COVID test now** if you have one. That is not wasting a test — symptoms are exactly what the tests are for.  
2. If it’s **negative**, repeat another home test in **48 hours**, especially if symptoms continue or worsen. Early tests can be falsely negative.  
3. Until you know more, **stay home as much as possible, avoid close contact, and wear a mask around family**, especially anyone older, pregnant, immunocompromised, or with lung/heart conditions.  
4. Hydrate well; with diarrhea, use water plus electrolytes/soup, and eat bland foods if your stomach is off.

If you test positive and symptoms started within the last 5 days, let us know promptly — depending on risk factors, some people benefit from antiviral medication.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, signs of dehydration, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, bloody stool, or diarrhea that becomes frequent or lasts more than a few days.

Bottom line: diarrhea twice doesn’t make COVID less likely enough to ignore it. Testing and masking/isolation for now is the practical middle ground — not overreacting, just being careful.

query:
hi, this is mark ellison, sorry if this is a dumb question but i keep going in circles on it. i take irbesartan (angiotensin receptor blocker) for uncomplicated hypertension. it increases cell expression of angiotensin receptors (ar’s). covid enters cells via ar’s. should i change my blood pressure meds? thanks. i know i prob should just wait to hear back from dr. priya shah but the office said msgs are backed up and i am sitting here reading too much online and making myself nuts. i have been on the same irbesartan for a while and my bp has been pretty boring/controlled, no big drama, no heart failure or kidney stuff that i know of, just uncomplicated hypertension like i said. but now i keep seeing posts saying ace inhibitors and arbs might make more receptors and then covid can get in easier, then other posts say dont stop anything, and honestly i cant tell what is real info and what is people yelling on facebook.

im not trying to self adjust meds, i just dont want to accidentally make myself higher risk if there is a safer bp med to switch to for now. also if i stop it suddenly i assume my pressure could go up and that sounds bad too, so im kind of stuck between two worries. my insurance is aetna ppo #AXP-771204 if that matters for what meds are usually covered, tho i know you prob cant check that here. should i keep taking the irbesartan until my regular dr answers, or ask for a different class like a calcium channel blocker or something? again no emergency right now, just anxious and trying to not do the wrong thing.
target_response:
Hi Mark — not a dumb question at all. This has been a confusing topic online, and you’re thinking about the right issues rather than changing things on your own.

The key point: COVID-19 enters cells mainly through **ACE2 receptors**, not the usual angiotensin II receptors that irbesartan blocks. Irbesartan is an **ARB**; it blocks the angiotensin II type 1 receptor to lower blood pressure. Early in the pandemic there was theoretical concern that ACE inhibitors/ARBs might affect ACE2 levels, but large clinical studies have **not shown that people taking ARBs are more likely to get COVID or have worse outcomes**. Major cardiology and hypertension groups recommend **continuing ACE inhibitors/ARBs** unless your own clinician has a specific reason to stop.

Given what you said — uncomplicated hypertension, stable on irbesartan, controlled “boring” blood pressures, no known heart failure or kidney disease — I would **keep taking the irbesartan as prescribed until Dr. Shah gets back to you**. Switching to a calcium channel blocker “just in case” is not generally recommended, because it may destabilize your blood pressure, cause side effects like ankle swelling or headaches, and may not provide any COVID-related benefit. Stopping irbesartan suddenly usually does not cause a classic rebound effect like some medications can, but your blood pressure can rise back up, which is a real and avoidable risk.

Practical next steps:  
- Continue irbesartan at your usual dose.  
- If you have a home cuff, check BP calmly once daily or a few times per week, seated/rested, and write it down.  
- Send Dr. Shah a concise message asking whether she recommends any change; most likely she will say to continue.  
- Try to avoid Facebook threads on this — they tend to amplify old theories without current evidence.

Seek urgent care if you develop chest pain, severe shortness of breath, fainting, new weakness/numbness, confusion, severe headache with very high BP, or BP persistently above about **180/120**, especially with symptoms. If you get COVID symptoms and are high-risk or worsening, contact your doctor promptly about testing and treatment options.

Bottom line: **don’t stop or switch irbesartan just because of COVID concerns.**

query: hi this is mike donnelly, i know this is probably a dumb question but im trying not to spiral and also trying not to ignore it. if you feel not quite well 3 or 4 evenings in a row,no fever,minor dry cough,little stuffy,maybe scratchy throat very minor,just crummy feeling in general and evenings only (feel fine during the day) could that be a mild case of coronavirus? thanks. like i wake up and im basically normal, make coffee, do emails, walk around the house, no fever at all when i check, then around dinner-ish i start feeling kinda off and chilled-ish but not real chills, just blah. the cough is barely there and dry, not like im hacking, and the stuffy nose is little, more annoying than anything. i called dr. priya shah office but the voicemail said use the portal unless urgent and i dont wanna clog things up if this is just allergies or stress or whatever. my callback is 617-555-2048 if anyone from triage actually sees this, but honestly i mostly just want to know if mild coronavirus can be like only at night and then fine during the day. i havent gone anywhere big besides pickup at walgreens on beacon st and grocery curbside, and ive been trying to wash hands etc. my wife keeps saying im over checking and maybe i am, but with all the news its hard not to think every scratchy throat is covid. no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no fever, eating ok, sleeping ok except i wake up thinking about it. should i isolate from the house or just watch it?
target_response:
Hi Mike — not a dumb question at all. What you’re describing *could* be a mild COVID infection, but it could also very reasonably be a regular mild viral cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry indoor air, reflux, fatigue at the end of the day, or stress/anxiety amplifying normal sensations. COVID can be mild, can occur without fever, and symptoms can fluctuate during the day — so “fine in the morning, crummy around dinner” doesn’t rule it out.

Given the minor dry cough, scratchy throat, stuffiness, and “blah/chilled-ish” feeling for 3–4 evenings, I’d treat it as a possible contagious respiratory illness for now, without panicking.

**What I’d do next:**
1. **Take a home COVID antigen test now** if you have one. If it’s negative, **repeat in 48 hours**, since early tests can miss mild cases.  
2. **Limit close contact at home until you know more** — you don’t necessarily need extreme isolation if symptoms are very mild, but it’s smart to mask around your wife, avoid sharing cups/utensils, improve ventilation, and sleep separately if practical, especially if she’s higher risk.  
3. If you test positive, follow current respiratory-virus guidance: stay home/away from others until symptoms are improving and you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers, then take extra precautions like masking for several more days.  
4. Supportive care: fluids, rest, saline spray, honey/tea for cough, and consider an antihistamine if allergies are likely. Check your temperature once or twice daily — try not to repeatedly check, since that can feed the spiral.

**Please seek urgent care or call promptly** if you develop shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, worsening or persistent high fever, true shaking chills, confusion, blue lips, inability to keep fluids down, oxygen levels below ~94% if you use a pulse ox, or if symptoms noticeably worsen rather than slowly improve.

For now, since you have no fever, no shortness of breath, no chest pain, and you’re eating/sleeping okay, this sounds appropriate to monitor at home while testing and taking reasonable precautions. If it lasts more than a week, worsens, or your wife becomes ill, send Dr. Shah’s office another portal message.

query:
good afternoon, my mom mary ellen barker is 68 and went to the hospital, she is at st luke's hospital right now and i am sitting here in the waiting area just shaking and not knowing what to do or even what questions to ask. her left long is completely covered with namonia spelling sorry and is going into the right. dr. nina patel came out and tried to explain but honestly my head is spinning and i cant tell if im hearing it right. she is full code and so off the oxygen is 23 pulse is 18 so they put her on a vintalator, they said they had to because she couldnt keep up on her own. she has had a stroke, heart atack in the previous she has smoked for the longest i can remember, like since we were little kids, and she always said she would quit but never really did. they have her chart under MRN 77-204881 and everybody keeps saying they are doing everything but i dont know what everything means anymore.
