Levofloxacin can cause significant stomach irritation, but also more serious side effects such as tendon pain/swelling, severe diarrhea, palpitations, dizziness, numbness/tingling, or mood/insomnia changes.

The steroid injection/Medrol and albuterol can absolutely cause **insomnia, feeling overheated, jitteriness, and a racing heart**, but they shouldn’t make worsening chest pain something to “ride out.”

Please seek urgent/emergency care now if you have any of these: shortness of breath at rest, oxygen saturation <94% if you can check, chest pain that is severe or worsening, fainting, confusion, coughing blood, persistent fever, heart rate staying >120, blue lips, new one-sided leg swelling/pain, or inability to keep fluids down.

I understand the worry about another bill, but worsening chest pain with pneumonia treatment failure is exactly the kind of situation where being checked promptly is safer than waiting. Bring your inhalers, antibiotics, steroid pack, and any visit paperwork so they can reconcile everything.

query: my 2 year old ava marie collins has a small fever and is vomiting. i'm worried about her. are these symptoms of the coronavirus? i know every little thing prob makes me panic now but she is only 2 and i cant tell if im over reacting or if i should take her in right now. her dob is 03/14/2024 and the last time we were at the ped they had her listed as MRN KID-77429, if that matters. she was acting mostly normal earlier, kinda cranky and clingy, then after dinner she threw up and then again a little later, not a huge amount but enough that i got scared. temp is just small fever, not crazy high, but she feels warm and keeps laying on me and saying her tummy hurts. no big cough really, maybe a tiny sniffle but toddlers always have something, so idk. i called the nurse line but was on hold forever and my phone is 267-555-0198 if someone needs to call me back. dr. nina patel is her regular dr but the office is closed now and i dont know if they want us to go to urgent care or just keep fluids and watch her. we did pick up kids tylenol from cvs on 1901 oregon ave last week but i havent given anything yet bc she vomited and im scared it will just come back up. she has had some water sips, no food really. no one in our house has tested positive but my older kid was around a bunch of people at daycare and now im thinking what if it is covid. should i be looking for breathing stuff or just fever getting worse? sorry im rambling im just really worried about her.
target_response:
I understand why you’re worried—vomiting plus fever in a 2-year-old can be scary, especially when she can’t explain much. Based on what you described (low-grade fever, two episodes of vomiting, tummy discomfort, clingy but earlier mostly normal, minimal sniffle/no significant cough), this **could be COVID**, but it’s also very commonly caused by a routine stomach virus, another viral illness, or something she ate. COVID in toddlers can include fever, vomiting, diarrhea, runny nose, cough, or sometimes very mild symptoms, so testing is reasonable if you have one.

**What to do tonight if she is otherwise alert and breathing normally:**
- After vomiting, wait **15–20 minutes**, then offer very small amounts of fluid: **1–2 teaspoons (5–10 mL) every 2–3 minutes**. Pedialyte or an oral rehydration solution is ideal; water is okay in small sips, but don’t let her chug.
- Avoid food for a bit. Once she keeps fluids down for several hours, try bland foods if she wants them.
- For fever/discomfort, you can give children’s acetaminophen **based on her weight** (follow the bottle or pediatrician’s dosing chart). If she vomits immediately, hold off and retry later after fluids are staying down. I’d avoid ibuprofen until she’s drinking well because it can upset the stomach.
- Consider a home COVID test tonight or tomorrow; if negative but symptoms continue/exposure is possible, repeat in 24–48 hours.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if any of these happen: trouble breathing, fast/labored breathing, bluish lips, she is very sleepy/hard to wake or inconsolable, signs of dehydration (no urine for **8–10 hours**, very dry mouth, no tears, sunken eyes), repeated vomiting and can’t keep even small sips down, green or bloody vomit, blood in stool, severe/worsening belly pain or pain mostly on the lower right, stiff neck, purple rash, or fever around **104°F** or higher.

If none of those are present, it’s reasonable to **monitor closely and focus on fluids tonight**, then call Dr. Patel’s office/after-hours line in the morning. Trust your instincts—if she looks “not right” to you, it’s okay to take her in.

query: hi. this covid-19 outbreak is scary. my name is maria lopez, dob 07/14/1988, and i feel dumb even typing all this but im getting anxious and dont really know what im supposed to do next. i got screened this week at Penn urgent care on 3737 Market St and it was negative, they gave me MRN P-448219 on the paper, but prior to screening i had a week of continuous dry coughs and also throat was sore. not like super painful but annoying and scratchy and i kept coughing in little bursts, mostly dry, and then reading stuff online made me panic more. dr kevin shah was the person i think who checked me real quick, or at least thats the name on my discharge sheet, but it was so rushed and everyone looked stressed so i didnt wanna ask a million questions. i've put myself in a quarantine in my room since before the test even came back, using my own cup, wiping the bathroom, not sitting with my roommate, and im trying not to touch anything. my phone is 267-555-0148 and email is mlopez88@gmail.com if someone from the office needs to message me bc i honestly might miss calls while working from home. what next? do i still need to screen again? im confused bc the screen was negative but i had symptoms before it and i still dont feel 100% normal, and i dont wanna be that person who spreads something by accident. also how long do i stay inside if it was negative, like do i count from when cough started or from the screen date? sorry im rambling, just nervous and trying to do the right thing.
pii_units: Penn urgent care on 3737 Market St
target_response:
Hi Maria — you are not dumb for asking. You’re being thoughtful and responsible, and it’s very understandable to feel anxious when you have symptoms and are reading a lot online.

A negative COVID screen/test is reassuring, but it doesn’t make COVID impossible. Tests can be falsely negative depending on timing, test type, and sample quality. That said, a week of dry cough and scratchy sore throat can also come from many non-COVID causes: other respiratory viruses, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry air, reflux, or irritation from frequent coughing.

**What to do next:**  
- If you are improving, have no fever, and no known close exposure to someone with COVID, you may not need repeat testing.  
- If your symptoms are continuing, worsening, or you need extra reassurance before being around others, repeat testing is reasonable—especially if the first test was a rapid/antigen test. A repeat antigen test 24–48 hours later or a PCR/NAAT test would be more informative.  
- If you develop new fever, worsening cough, or shortness of breath, contact your primary care office or urgent care for guidance.

**How long to stay isolated:** Count from the day symptoms started, not the test date. Day 0 is the first day of cough/sore throat. In general, stay home and avoid close contact until you’ve been fever-free for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine and your symptoms are clearly improving. Because COVID is still possible despite a negative test, it’s prudent to continue masking around your roommate and avoiding shared meals/close indoor contact through about day 10 from symptom onset if you can. Keep doing what you’re doing: separate cup/towel, handwashing, ventilation, and wiping high-touch bathroom surfaces.

For the cough/sore throat: fluids, honey in tea, throat lozenges, saline spray, humidifier/steam, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if needed can help.

**Seek urgent care now** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, fainting, dehydration, oxygen saturation under 94% if you have a pulse oximeter, coughing blood, or a high/persistent fever or worsening after initially improving.

And for the anxiety: limit doom-scrolling, check one reliable source once daily, and focus on the steps you’re already taking—you’re doing the right things.

query: hi, im lisa maren, sorry this is prob a dumb long msg but im getting anxious and not sure what to do next. my chest x-ray reveals i have a small lingular infiltrate.wbc,lymphocytes,hct etc elevated due to infection.i had a course of augmentin duo and steroid.currently on azithromycin 250 mgs.symptoms of pneumonia still persisting.do i need to change my treatment? i was seen thru harborview family clinic and dr nisha patel looked at the report, but i feel like im going in circles bc the xray says one thing, bloodwork says infection, and im still sitting here feeling crappy and worrying it is not clearing. my chart has me as dob 08/14/1981 and mrn HMC-77291 if that matters, and i picked up the azithromycin from cvs on 5th ave so i know im taking the right med they gave me, 250 mgs like it says. i finished the augmentin duo and steroid already, didnt skip it, and now im just wondering if azithro is enough or if i should be calling back today instead of waiting. i dont want to overreact but also dont want pneumonia to get worse while im trying to be patient. the cough/ chest stuff is still there and im tired of guessing, like should a small lingular infiltrate still be showing symptoms after these meds or does that mean the bacteria is resistant or maybe its viral or something. i keep re-reading the labs and seeing wbc, lymphocytes, hct etc elevated and it freaks me out more. should i ask for repeat x-ray, sputum test, different antibiotic, or just finish the azithromycin first? i have work and cant keep taking off but i also dont want to mess around with pneumonia.
target_response:
Hi Lisa — this is not a dumb message at all. Pneumonia can feel scary, especially when the X-ray and labs confirm infection but you still feel unwell.

A **small lingular infiltrate** means there is a small area in the left lung that looks inflamed/infected, commonly from pneumonia. It is very common for the **cough, chest congestion, fatigue, and “crummy” feeling to persist for days to a few weeks**, even after starting antibiotics. The **X-ray can also lag behind symptoms** and may remain abnormal for several weeks, so a repeat X-ray immediately often doesn’t show much unless symptoms are worsening.

That said, if you have already completed **Augmentin** and steroids and are now on **azithromycin**, it’s reasonable to **call Harborview Family Clinic today**, especially if you are not improving after **48–72 hours on the current antibiotic**, or if symptoms are worsening. Augmentin plus azithromycin generally covers many typical and atypical causes of community-acquired pneumonia, but treatment decisions depend on your exam, oxygen level, fever pattern, lung sounds, and risk factors.

A few points about the labs: elevated **WBC** can fit with infection, but steroids can also raise WBC temporarily. Elevated **lymphocytes** can sometimes suggest a viral component. Elevated **hematocrit** can be from dehydration or other factors and is not by itself proof the pneumonia is worsening.

What I’d suggest asking your clinician today:
- “Given I still feel unwell, do I need to be rechecked in person?”
- “Should my oxygen level/vitals be checked?”
- “Do I need a different antibiotic, or should I finish azithromycin first?”
- “Is sputum testing indicated?”
- “When should we repeat the chest X-ray if symptoms persist?”

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you have **worsening shortness of breath, oxygen level below ~92%, chest pain/pressure, coughing blood, blue lips, confusion, fainting, persistent high fever, inability to keep fluids down, or rapidly worsening weakness**.

If you are stable but just not improving, calling today is not overreacting — it’s appropriate follow-up. Continue the azithromycin as prescribed unless your clinician changes it or you develop a concerning reaction.

query: i am marilyn kosinski, dob 03/14/1950, i am a 76 year old female non smoker and non drinker i drink 1 cup of decaf a day and that is it, no wine no beer nothing, and i am sorry this is long but i keep thinking about it at night and getting myself upset. in the past year i have had 3 or 4 bouts of pneumonia one was infiltrated just got bronchitis and it feels like as soon as i get over one thing i am back coughing or tight in the chest again. i have have been treated with z pack and amoxicillin and i am also taking blood thinners due to atrial fibulation so i worry about every pill they give me and what mixes with what. i have a pacemaker also and sometimes i dont know if the tired feeling is from my heart, my lungs, age, or just all these infections wearing me down. i had a pneumonia shot in 2011 .one month after taking the shot i got pneumonia. my brother who is 2 years older had the very same reaction, same kind of thing and it scares me because it makes me wonder if something runs in our family or if we both just reacted bad to it. i usually go to mercy pulmonology on 301 s 8th st and they have my chart i think, but i dont always understand the portal stuff so i told them just call me at 215-555-7428 if they need me. our parents were very heavy smoker, the house and car always smelled like smoke when we were kids, can this be the cause of all my problems now. i never smoked myself so i keep asking how i ended up with this many lung problems, and should i be asking for a different kind of test or lung dr opinion
target_response:
Marilyn, I’m sorry you’ve been going through this repeatedly — it’s very understandable that 3–4 episodes of pneumonia/bronchitis in a year, plus atrial fibrillation, a pacemaker, and blood thinners, would make you anxious and worn down.

A few important points:

**1) Recurrent “pneumonia” deserves a closer look.**  
When someone has repeated chest infections or an infiltrate that keeps returning, we usually want to confirm whether these are true pneumonias versus bronchitis, asthma/COPD-type inflammation, fluid from heart issues, aspiration/reflux, or bronchiectasis. It would be reasonable to ask your pulmonologist about:
- **Chest CT scan** if not recently done  
- **Pulmonary function testing** to check for asthma/COPD or restriction  
- Review of prior X-rays to see if infiltrates occur in the **same area**  
- **Sputum culture** if you produce phlegm  
- Basic immune labs if infections are truly recurrent  
- Evaluation for **aspiration/GERD or swallowing issues**, especially if coughing after eating or lying down

**2) Secondhand smoke exposure can matter.**  
Growing up in a smoky home/car can increase risk for chronic airway irritation and lung disease later, even if you never smoked. It may contribute, but it does not automatically explain everything — which is why the above testing is worthwhile.

**3) Pneumonia shot concern.**  
The pneumococcal vaccines do **not contain live bacteria** and cannot cause pneumonia. Getting pneumonia a month later is usually coincidence, though feeling achy or “off” after a vaccine can happen. Since your last pneumonia shot was in 2011, ask your doctor whether you should receive an updated pneumococcal vaccine such as **PCV20** or the current recommended schedule for your age and history.

**4) Medication interactions.**  
Because you’re on a blood thinner, every antibiotic should be checked. Azithromycin (“Z-pack”) and some antibiotics can interact with certain blood thinners, especially warfarin, and may require INR monitoring or closer observation. Amoxicillin is often safer, but still worth confirming with your pharmacist/doctor.

Please seek urgent care if you have worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, coughing blood, oxygen saturation <92%, high fever, or rapid worsening weakness. Otherwise, I’d call Mercy Pulmonology and request a focused “recurrent pneumonia” workup and medication review.

query: i was diagnosed with pneumonia 5 days ago and went to urgent care, it was fairview urgent care and i saw dr. alan meyer there, and i am still trying not to panic bc i dont know what is normal after finishing meds. my name is maria thompson if that matters for the chart or whatever. been on prescribed antibiotics and i completed the 5 day treatment today, (levofloxacin) and i picked it up at cvs on main st the same day they gave it to me. i took it like they told me and did not skip any, just finished the last pill today, but now i keep thinking maybe 5 days is too short even tho thats what was prescribed and i dont want to mess anything up or end up worse. i have flight reservations to michigan on friday morning 4/28/2017, it is delta flight dl1832, and i already paid for the ticket so im trying to decide soon if i should cancel or still go. will it be safe to fly. please advise. i know you cant see me over this message but i am just nervous about sitting on a plane with pneumonia so recently, and the air pressure and being around ppl and all that. i also dont want to be that person coughing or getting other ppl worried, and i dont know if i am still contagious after finishing the levofloxacin today. if someone can call me back my cell is 734-555-0149, or just answer here, either is fine. your anticipated help will be appreciated. i just need a plain answer like should i fly or should i delay, bc friday morning is coming fast and im overthinking every little thing.
target_response:
Maria, it’s very understandable to feel nervous right after pneumonia—especially with a flight coming up. A **5-day course of levofloxacin can be an appropriate, standard treatment duration for community-acquired pneumonia** when the patient is improving. You should **not restart or extend antibiotics on your own** unless a clinician who examines you recommends it.

The key question is how you are doing now. In general, flying is usually reasonable after pneumonia **if you are clearly improving**, meaning:

- No fever for at least 24–48 hours without fever reducers  
- Breathing is comfortable at rest and with light walking  
- Chest pain is gone or improving  
- Cough is improving, even if not fully gone  
- You are not dizzy, severely weak, or dehydrated  
- Your oxygen level, if checked, is normal—typically **94% or higher**, or your usual baseline

Cough and fatigue can linger for **2–4 weeks or longer** after pneumonia, even when the infection is adequately treated. That does not necessarily mean the antibiotic failed.

Regarding being contagious: after several days of appropriate antibiotics and if you are fever-free and improving, the risk of contagiousness is generally much lower. Still, because you may cough, I’d recommend wearing a mask on the plane, using hand sanitizer, covering coughs, staying hydrated, and walking/stretching your legs periodically.

I would **delay the flight and seek urgent care today/tomorrow** if you have any of these: worsening shortness of breath, trouble walking across a room, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, coughing blood, persistent fever, oxygen below 94%, rapid worsening, or inability to keep fluids down.

If you are still unsure, the most practical next step is to call Fairview Urgent Care or your primary care office and ask whether they can do a quick recheck before Friday—especially oxygen level and lung exam.  

Plain answer: **If you are improving, fever-free, and breathing comfortably, flying Friday is likely okay. If symptoms are worsening or you’re short of breath, delay and get re-evaluated.**

query: hi, its maria bell here, dob 03/14/1987, i have mrn KGH-20491 if that helps, and im just trying to ask bc im going round in circles and getting more panicky tbh. with a bad immunity deficiency, how can i keep as safe as possible from contracting covid19 in a family household. work is not an issue but shopping and accepting deliveries is. i already asked dr. nisha patel before but i forgot half of what she said bc i was stressed, and now every time someone knocks at the door i feel like im doing it wrong. we live at 46 maple ave, bristol, pa and its a small place, home is small, no garden, so also need to take child outsides or we both go mad in here. i dont know if im meant to wipe every single tin, box, packet, bag, milk bottle etc or if thats too much and im just making myself anxious. if i order groceries and they leave them outside, how long do i leave them, do i wear gloves, mask, change clothes after, wash hands between each item? same with parcels, like if the delivery driver touches the doorbell then i touch the box then my child touches me, is that enough to pass it on? sorry i know this sounds a bit much but bc my immune system is rubbish i feel like one small mistake could be bad. we arent having visitors and work is not a issue, but family household means we still share bathroom/kitchen and the child needs fresh air. is it safer to go out very early, or just walk round block, no playground, no shops? also what should the other adult do when coming back from shopping, shoes off, shower, clothes straight in wash? just need clear practical steps really, not a scary answer if possible.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry this has been feeling so frightening. It’s very understandable to want clear rules, especially with an immune deficiency. The reassuring part is that COVID spreads mainly through breathing in virus from infected people in shared air; catching it from tins, bags, parcels, or doorbells is possible but **much less common**. So the goal is to reduce close indoor exposure, not to disinfect every object perfectly.

**For groceries/deliveries:**
- Choose **no-contact delivery** when possible; have items left at the door.
- Put on a well-fitting mask if you must open the door near someone; otherwise wait until they’ve gone.
- Bring items in, put them away normally, then **wash hands well for 20 seconds**.
- You do **not** need to wipe every tin/box/milk bottle or wash hands between each item. Wiping high-touch things is optional, not required.
- For parcels: open, discard packaging if convenient, wash hands. No need to quarantine boxes for hours/days.

**Gloves/clothes/showering:** Gloves often make people touch more surfaces and aren’t necessary. Handwashing is better. The adult who goes shopping should wear a high-quality mask, avoid crowded aisles, use hand sanitizer after leaving, and wash hands on return. Shoes off is fine for general cleanliness, but showering and putting clothes straight in the wash is usually not needed unless they were in a high-risk setting or had close contact with someone ill.

**Inside the household:**
- Keep vaccinations/boosters up to date for all eligible household members.
- Improve ventilation: crack windows when possible, use exhaust fans, or consider a HEPA purifier if affordable.
- If anyone develops symptoms, have them mask, distance as much as the small space allows, sleep separately if possible, and test.

**Fresh air for your child:** Yes — going outside is a good idea. Early/less busy times, walks around the block, avoiding crowds and indoor shops, and skipping busy playgrounds are sensible. Outdoor transmission is much lower.

Because you’re immunocompromised, ask Dr. Patel/immunology for a written plan: when to test, whether you qualify for early antivirals if positive, and what number to call.

Seek urgent care for trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, dehydration, or oxygen levels persistently below your advised range/under 92%. You’re not failing — you’re trying to keep your family safe.

query: i went to the doctor yesterday, this is melissa grant btw, and shesaid i had a sinus infection,bronchitis, and i was starting to getpneumonia. it was dr. nisha patel at riverbend family clinic and i was kind of out of it so im not even sure i asked the right questions. she gave me a shot ofantibiotics and steroids while i was there and said it should start helping but i swear i feel like im going backwards. she also wroteme a prescription for bactrim ds andcough syrup and i picked it up at cvs on maple ave on the way home, took it like they said, and the cough syrup isn'tworking at all, i'm up all night coughing, like hard coughing where my chest and ribs hurt and my head feels full. ihaven't slept for more than 30 min sincesaturday and im getting really worn down and anxious bc every time i lay down it starts again and i end up sitting on the couch with water and tissues and still hacking. i feel worse than i didyesterday and thats whats scaring me bc if she said starting to getpneumonia then does that mean it can get worse even with the shot and meds? i dont know if i should wait another day for the antibiotics to kick in or call back or go somewhere tonight. i dont want to be dramatic but i also dont want to ignore it if its pneumonia getting worse. no sleep is making me feel crazy too. what should i do? any advice?thanks!
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry you’re feeling so awful. You’re not being dramatic. With a clinician telling you there were signs you were “starting to get pneumonia,” and now you feel worse despite the injection and starting Bactrim DS, it’s reasonable to be re-checked rather than just trying to tough it out.

Antibiotics often take **24–48 hours** to noticeably help, and cough can lag behind for days. Also, bronchitis/sinus drainage can cause relentless coughing when you lie down. That said, pneumonia **can worsen early on**, or you may need a chest X-ray, oxygen level check, different treatment, an inhaler/nebulizer, or stronger cough-control plan—especially since you’re barely sleeping and having chest/rib pain from coughing.

**What I’d do tonight:**  
- If Riverbend Family Clinic has an after-hours line, call now and tell them: diagnosed with sinus infection/bronchitis/early pneumonia yesterday, received antibiotic/steroid shots, started Bactrim DS and cough syrup, but worsening cough and unable to sleep. Ask if they want you seen tonight.  
- If you can’t reach them, or you feel significantly worse, go to **urgent care tonight** for vitals, lung exam, oxygen saturation, and likely chest X-ray.  

**Go to the ER now** if you have any of these: trouble breathing, shortness of breath at rest, lips/face bluish, chest pain not just from coughing, coughing blood, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, fever over 103°F or fever not coming down, oxygen saturation <92% if you have a pulse ox, dehydration/can’t keep fluids down, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

For symptom relief while you’re arranging care: sit upright or sleep propped up, use a humidifier/steamy shower, sip warm fluids, consider honey before bed if you’re not diabetic/allergic, and use acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely for rib/head pain. Take the cough syrup exactly as prescribed—don’t combine with alcohol, sleep meds, or extra sedating medications unless a doctor says it’s okay.

Also, if Bactrim causes a widespread rash, mouth sores, facial swelling, or trouble breathing, seek urgent care immediately. Given your worsening and exhaustion, I’d err on the side of being checked tonight or at least speaking with the on-call clinician.

query: hi this is molly keane, sorry if this is a dumb question but do i just go to a dr to determine if it's a sinus infection or do i go get tested for covid first? to not risk anyone in practice? i keep going back and forth bc i dont wanna be that person walking into main line family care and then find out i shouldve stayed in my car or gone thru some testing line first. i tried calling but got the menu thing and then i hung up bc i wasnt even sure what option to pick, and i also dont wanna waste dr. eric han's time if the answer is like obviously test first. but also if its not covid and its just a sinus infection or whatever then i dont wanna sit around waiting and then end up needing meds later, you know? i have people at home and im trying to be careful, like i can mask and all that but i still worry about the front desk people and the nurse and other patients sitting there. can someone just tell me the order im supposed to do this in, like test first and then call, or call and they decide, or make an appt but say covid concern when i check in? i can be reached at 267-555-0194 if someone needs to tell me what door to use or if there is a separate entrance or if i should not come in at all. sorry im rambling, im just confused bc every place seems to do it different and i dont want to risk anyone in practice but i also dont want to ignore it if a dr actually needs to look at me.
target_response:
Hi Molly — this is absolutely not a dumb question. You’re being thoughtful and considerate, and it’s very reasonable to ask what the safest “order of operations” is.

In general, **don’t just walk into the office for sinus/respiratory symptoms without checking in first**, because offices often have specific triage procedures. The best next step is:

1. **If you have a home COVID test available, take one now** — especially if you have congestion, sore throat, cough, fever, body aches, headache, or new loss of taste/smell.  
2. **Call the office or send a portal message** and say you have “possible sinus infection vs COVID/respiratory symptoms” and need guidance on whether you should do a video visit, in-person visit, car-side testing, or use a separate entrance. If the phone menu is confusing, choose the option closest to “appointments,” “nurse/clinical question,” or “sick visit.”  
3. **If you do come in**, wear a well-fitting mask, avoid sitting close to others, and tell the front desk right away that you have respiratory symptoms and/or COVID concern.

A negative home COVID test is helpful, but if symptoms just started, it can be falsely negative. If you test negative and still feel sick, it’s reasonable to repeat it in 24–48 hours or follow the office’s testing advice.

Also, many “sinus infections” are viral at first and don’t need antibiotics. We start thinking more about a bacterial sinus infection if symptoms last **more than 10 days without improvement**, are **severe** with high fever and significant facial pain/pus-like nasal drainage for several days, or if you get better and then suddenly worsen again.

In the meantime: rest, fluids, saline nasal spray/rinse, steam/humidifier, acetaminophen or ibuprofen if safe for you, and possibly an intranasal steroid like fluticasone can help congestion.

Please seek urgent care sooner if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, dehydration, persistent high fever, severe headache, swelling/redness around the eye, stiff neck, or worsening facial swelling.

Bottom line: **test if you can, then call/message the office and let them direct the safest visit type.**

query: i have a sore throat and mild cough, do i need to be tested for corona? i know this prob sounds like everyone else asking but im getting myself worked up bc i keep reading stuff online and then i cant tell what is normal cold stuff and what is like, ok go get checked now. im mike hanley and i live at 48 willow bend rd so im trying to figure out if i should just stay home and not be around ppl or call first or what. the sore throat is annoying more than terrible and the cough is mild, not like im hacking all day, but its there enough that i keep noticing it and then i worry. i dont wanna overreact and take up a spot if ppl are really sick, but also i dont wanna be dumb and ignore it if corona testing is something i should do early. i havent really been sure who to ask bc every place says something diff and the phone lines are busy. i did leave a msg for dr. nina patel but havent heard back yet, so thats why im asking here too. should i be tested just for sore throat and mild cough or only if it gets worse? like do i need to wait for fever or shortness of breath or anything before trying to get a test? i also dont know if i should be staying in a separate room from my family until i know, wiping everything down, wearing a mask in the house, all that stuff. sorry im rambling, just anxious and trying not to scare everyone. if someone could just tell me if testing makes sense with these symptoms or if i should monitor at home that would help a lot.
target_response:
Hi Mike — you’re not overreacting. A sore throat with a mild cough can absolutely be a routine cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, flu/RSV, or COVID-19, and the symptoms often overlap. You do **not** need to wait for fever or shortness of breath before testing.

Given that you have new respiratory symptoms, it’s reasonable to **test for COVID now**, especially if you’ve been around others, have family at home, or might be near anyone higher-risk. If you have access to a home rapid antigen test, take one today. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat it in **48 hours** (rapid tests can be falsely negative early). If you need a more definitive answer, a PCR/NAAT test through a clinic/pharmacy is an option. While waiting to hear back from Dr. Patel, testing at home is a practical first step.

Until you know more, I’d recommend acting cautiously:
- **Stay home** and avoid work/school/social contact if possible.
- **Wear a mask around family**, especially in shared spaces.
- If feasible, **sleep/use a separate room** and use a separate bathroom if available.
- Improve ventilation, wash hands, don’t share cups/utensils, and wipe high-touch surfaces.
- Once symptoms are improving and you’re fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers, you can generally ease back, but keep masking for a few more days if around others.

For symptom relief: fluids, rest, honey or warm tea for cough, salt-water gargles/lozenges for throat, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if needed and safe for you.

Please seek urgent care or emergency help if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, coughing up blood, or oxygen levels under ~92% if you have a pulse oximeter**. Also call promptly if you’re higher-risk for severe COVID—age over 65, significant lung/heart disease, diabetes, immune suppression, pregnancy—because antivirals work best early.

Bottom line: with your sore throat and mild cough, **testing makes sense now**, and you should take sensible precautions at home while monitoring.

target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry this has been such a maze. You’re not wasting anyone’s time; COVID testing logistics can be frustrating.

I can’t directly place you on a testing list or schedule from here, and I wouldn’t recommend showing up to Penn Medicine Rittenhouse without confirming first, since many sites require an appointment or specific registration even for “walk-in” testing. The best next step is to use the Penn Medicine patient portal/website if you have access, or call the specific testing location before going. If their system is sending you in circles, pharmacies like CVS, Walgreens, Rite Aid, and some urgent care centers often have clearer online scheduling for COVID tests and can provide documentation.

For “proof,” ask the person/organization requesting it what they accept:
- **PCR/NAAT test:** Usually the most accepted for official proof; results often take 24–72 hours.
- **Rapid antigen test:** Faster, often same day, but may not be accepted everywhere and is less sensitive.
- **Home test:** Sometimes accepted only if proctored/verified; many places do **not** accept an unobserved home test.

Bring your photo ID, insurance card if you have one, and a mask. Testing may no longer be universally free; cost depends on the site, your insurance, whether you have symptoms/exposure, and the type of test. It’s reasonable to ask up front: “Will I be billed, and what is the self-pay cost?”

If you have symptoms or a known exposure, try to test as soon as possible, but timing matters: antigen tests are more reliable after symptoms start; if exposed without symptoms, testing around day 5 is often recommended. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat testing in 24–48 hours or get a PCR.

Please seek urgent care or ER evaluation if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below 92% if you monitor at home.

Since your voicemail is full and you prefer texting, I’d suggest using an online scheduler that sends text confirmations, or a patient portal message if you are already established with a health system.

query: hi im marisol vega and sorry if im asking this weird but where can i test on for free on a essential smart plan for covid-19? i keep calling around and every place says something diff and im getting stressed bc i dont wanna show up and then they charge me or say i need a referral or whatever. my card says healthfirst essential smart plan id HF-77290413 and i honestly dont understand if that means free covid testing or only if a dr orders it. i live at 82-14 roosevelt ave apt 3b, jackson heights ny 11372 and im trying to find somewhere close bc i dont have a car and i dont wanna sit on a bus forever if im maybe sick or around ppl. i can be reached at 917-555-2846 if someone has to call me back but i really just need a straight answer, like do i go urgent care, city site, pharmacy, clinic, or what. i dont have big symptoms right now but i was around someone at work who said they tested positive and now im nervous and my boss keeps asking if i got tested before i come back. also when i search online it says free, then another page asks for insurance, then another says appointment only, so im confused. if i have this essential smart plan do i pay nothing at the place or do they bill me later? i cant afford a surprise bill right now. pls just tell me where can i test for covid-19 for free with this plan and if i need to bring anything besides my card and id.
pii_units: healthfirst essential smart plan id HF-77290413
82-14 roosevelt ave apt 3b, jackson heights ny 11372
target_response:
Hi Marisol — I understand why this is stressful, especially when every place gives a different answer and you’re trying to avoid a surprise bill.

With a **Healthfirst Essential Smart Plan**, COVID testing is generally covered when it’s a **diagnostic test** because of symptoms or a known exposure (like your coworker). You usually **do not need a doctor’s referral**, but the key is going somewhere **in-network** or a public testing site. The biggest risk for a bill is an **urgent care** that charges an “office visit” fee in addition to the test, or a pharmacy/site that is no longer offering no-cost testing.

Since you’re in Jackson Heights and don’t have a car, the safest options to avoid surprise charges are:

1. **NYC Health + Hospitals / public testing location**  
   Call **311** or check NYC Health + Hospitals testing options and ask for the closest site to Jackson Heights/Elmhurst. **NYC H+H/Elmhurst** is likely one of the closer public hospital options. Ask: “Is COVID testing available today, and is there any out-of-pocket cost with Healthfirst Essential Plan?”

2. **Call Healthfirst Member Services**  
   Use the number on the back of your card and ask:  
   “Where is the nearest in-network COVID testing site near Jackson Heights, Queens, and will I owe $0 for a diagnostic COVID test after exposure?”  
   Ask them to document the call or give you a reference number.

3. **Avoid urgent care unless they confirm in writing/clearly by phone**  
   Ask: “Will you bill this as only a COVID test, or will there be an office visit charge? Will I owe anything today or later?”

Bring: **photo ID, Healthfirst card, mask**, and the date of your exposure. If you have no symptoms, test about **5 days after exposure** if possible; test sooner if symptoms start. Wear a well-fitting mask around others for 10 days after exposure.

Seek urgent care/ER if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or worsening high fever**.

Also, for privacy, try not to post your insurance ID/address publicly online.

query: my boyfriend matthew kline suddenly lost his sense of smell and im trying not to spiral but its freaking me out. he had a sleep apnea test done 3 days ago, on a machine, at sleepwell center in allentown, the place over by 1820 w tilghman st, and it was with dr. nina patel i think, or at least thats the name on the papers they gave him. the appt was 4/12 at 8:30 pm and they had him hooked up to all that stuff overnight, and he said it was annoying but nothing painful or weird happened. plus one if his nostrils is stuffed up now, due to a slight cold, like he sounds kinda blocked on one side and keeps sniffing, but when the test was done, his nostrils were clear. thats why im confused bc this smell thing came on suddenly and he swears he could smell fine before, like coffee, food, shampoo, all that, and now he says he cant smell hardly anything. he isnt acting super sick or anything, no big drama, but its just odd and i keep thinking did the machine dry him out or irritate something or did he catch something at the clinic or is it just the cold making one side blocked. i called the number on the sheet 610-555-2749 but got put on hold forever so im asking here while he says im overreacting. what caused this? could losing smell happen from a slight cold even if only one nostril is stuffed, or from that sleep apnea machine thing? also is it something that should be checked fast or just wait a few days and see if smell comes back?
target_response:
I can understand why this feels alarming, especially when it came on suddenly. The reassuring part is that sudden loss of smell is **most commonly due to a viral upper respiratory infection**—including COVID—or nasal/sinus inflammation, and it can happen even when someone only feels “slightly” sick.

A few likely possibilities for Matthew:

1. **Cold/viral infection:** Even if only one nostril feels blocked, inflammation can extend higher in the nose where the smell nerves are. Smell can drop dramatically before congestion seems severe. COVID is still a common cause of sudden smell loss, sometimes with minimal symptoms.

2. **Nasal congestion/allergies/sinus irritation:** Stuffiness, postnasal drip, or swelling can prevent odor molecules from reaching the smell receptors.

3. **Sleep apnea testing equipment:** A sleep study typically uses sensors/nasal airflow monitors and sometimes CPAP. These can cause dryness or irritation, but they **would be an uncommon cause of near-complete sudden smell loss** by themselves. If CPAP was used, dryness could worsen congestion, but the cold/virus explanation is more likely.

What I’d do next:
- Have him take a **COVID test today**, and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative and symptoms continue.
- Treat it like a cold: fluids, rest, humidifier/steam, saline nasal spray or rinse.
- If he can take them safely, an OTC nasal steroid like fluticasone can help swelling, but it may take a couple days.
- Avoid overusing decongestant sprays like Afrin—no more than 3 days.
- Make sure smoke detectors work and be cautious with gas stoves/expired food since his smell is impaired.

He does **not necessarily need urgent care tonight** if he otherwise feels okay. I’d contact his primary care doctor or the sleep center if the smell loss is severe, worsening, or not improving over the next several days. If it persists beyond **2–3 weeks**, he should be evaluated; an ENT visit may be useful.

Seek urgent care now if he has: severe headache, confusion, weakness/numbness, facial droop, vision changes, head injury, high fever, severe facial/sinus pain, bloody or foul nasal drainage, trouble breathing, chest pain, or a persistent one-sided blockage.

query:
is my wife is at huge risk of getting the virus, as she is a nurse in a gp surgery? i’m so scared for her. i’m scared she will get it, and we will both end up with it. her name is laura benson and she works at riverside gp surgery, the one at 22 king street, bristol bs1 4qa, and she keeps saying its just part of the job and i know it is but i cant stop thinking about it. she is still going in every day, seeing pts, answering phones, helping with bloods and dressings and all that, and even tho they say they have masks and aprons and stuff i just dont trust that its enough. dr priya nanda is the gp she mostly works with and she says they are trying to keep anyone coughing separate but how can they really know before someone walks in??

like if someone comes in with no fever yet or just says its allergies, does that mean she could bring it home on her clothes or hair or shoes? we live together obviously and our flat is small so i dont see how we could keep apart if she got it. ive been wiping door handles and the kettle and her phone like a mad person. she laughs but im honestly not sleeping much. i keep asking if she should change at work or shower right when she gets in and she says im over reacting but am i? i emailed the surgery from tom.benson77@outlook.com but nobody has replied, probly bc theyre swamped. do nurses in gp surgeries get proper testing if they start coughing? should i be sleeping in the spare room now or only if she has symptoms? i feel stupid asking but im scared she will get it, and then we will both end up with it, and i just dont know what is sensible vs panic.
target_response:
I can hear how frightened you are, and it’s completely understandable to worry when Laura is working face-to-face in healthcare. A nurse in a GP surgery does have a higher exposure risk than someone working from home, but it does **not** mean she is at “huge” or inevitable risk—especially if the practice is using triage, separating respiratory patients, hand hygiene, masks/aprons/gloves/eye protection when appropriate, cleaning procedures, and staff sickness policies.

You’re right that people can be infectious before they have obvious symptoms, or may mislabel symptoms as allergies. That is exactly why healthcare settings use “standard precautions” for everyone, not just people who look unwell. The biggest risk is usually close, prolonged contact with an infectious person, particularly without appropriate PPE. Bringing the virus home on hair, shoes, or clothing is possible in theory, but it’s generally a much lower risk than direct respiratory exposure.

Sensible steps—without letting it take over—would be:

- Laura should follow her workplace infection-control guidance and raise any PPE concerns with her manager/occupational health.
- When she gets home: wash hands thoroughly, change out of work clothes, and launder them normally. Showering immediately is reasonable if it reassures you, but it isn’t always essential.
- Keep cleaning high-touch surfaces, but once daily or after obvious contamination is enough—try not to let it become constant.
- Don’t sleep separately unless Laura develops symptoms or has a known high-risk exposure and you’re trying to reduce spread at home. If she becomes symptomatic, she should isolate as much as your flat allows: separate room if possible, good ventilation, separate towels, mask when close, and frequent hand hygiene.

Testing rules for healthcare staff can vary by local policy and current guidance, but nurses generally should have clear routes for reporting symptoms and accessing testing/occupational health advice. Laura’s workplace is the right place for that information.

Seek urgent medical help if either of you develops severe breathlessness, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening. Use NHS 111 for advice if unsure; call 999 for emergencies.

Also—your lack of sleep and constant checking matter. This anxiety is very real. Consider speaking to your GP or a mental health support service; you deserve support too.

query: state officials put temp ban on all group activities & shut down nonessential businesses to curb covid-19 spread. in light of that, would you say hosting a group yoga class in school parking lot is unwise? neighbor is doing that & i'm concerned. its at penn wynne elementary, like literally in the side lot by 250 haverford rd, and my neighbor marcy dell keeps posting in the little neighborhood chat that its “outside so its fine” and telling ppl to bring their own mats and stay apart. i dont wanna be the crazy person or the tattletale neighbor but also i feel like the whole point of the temp ban is so ppl stop finding loopholes? i walked past with the dog and saw a bunch of cars and ppl standing around before class, not just 2 or 3 ppl, and honestly once ppl start talking and laughing and moving around its not like everyone stays perfectly spaced. plus its a school parking lot, even if the school is closed, and kids in the area still ride bikes thru there and parents cut thru it walking. i have kids at home and one of them has asthma so im prob extra nervous, but this just seems like a bad idea right now. she says they are “not a business” and “not indoors” so the shut down doesnt apply, but it still feels like a group activity to me? i dont know if i should call someone, message her, or just mind my business, but im worried if one person has covid-19 and doesnt know it then the whole thing is just dumb. am i overreacting or is this actually unwise like i think it is?
target_response:
You are not overreacting. If state/local officials have temporarily banned group activities and closed nonessential activity to reduce COVID-19 spread, then organizing an in-person yoga class—even outdoors—is unwise and likely against the spirit, and possibly the letter, of the restrictions.

Outdoor settings are generally safer than indoor ones because air circulation dilutes respiratory droplets/aerosols. But “outside” does not mean “no risk.” A group exercise class brings together multiple households, often with people talking, laughing, breathing more heavily, arriving/leaving at the same time, and mingling before or after. Even if everyone brings their own mat and intends to stay apart, spacing tends to break down. COVID can also be spread by people who feel completely well. Your concern is especially understandable given that you have a child with asthma at home.

I would handle it in a calm, non-confrontational way:

1. **Do not attend and avoid walking through that area during class times** if possible.  
2. **Consider sending one polite message** to the organizer, such as: “I know you’re trying to offer something positive, but with the current ban on group activities and families/kids using that lot, I’m concerned this could put people at risk. Could you switch to Zoom or postpone until restrictions are lifted?”  
3. If it continues or the group is sizable, it is reasonable to **contact the school district/property owner or local health department/non-emergency municipal line** and ask what the current rules are and whether this is permitted. That is not being a “tattletale”; it’s addressing a public health concern. You don’t need to make it personal—focus on the gathering, location, and safety issue.

For your family, continue the basics: avoid crowded areas, wash hands after walks, keep asthma medicines available, and follow your child’s asthma action plan.

Seek urgent care for your child or anyone in the household if there is **trouble breathing, persistent chest pain/pressure, blue lips, severe weakness/confusion, dehydration, worsening wheezing not relieved by rescue inhaler, or oxygen levels low if you monitor them**.

Bottom line: your instincts are sound. Outdoor is better than indoor, but a group class during a temporary ban is not a good idea.

query: i have had a sore throat for a few days, tuesday night it felt like tonsils, like that swollen gross feeling when u keep swallowing to check it and it hurts, yesterday it started becoming scratchy and today i have started coughing and spitting mucus. i do not have a fever, i keep checking bc im paranoid, but i get light headed here and there, not like passing out just kinda woozy for a min then ok again, could it be covid? my name is jessica ramirez and my dob is 09/14/1998, i only say that bc if i end up needing an appt they always ask me the same stuff over and over and i get flustered. i called northside family clinic earlier but i didnt really know what to say besides sore throat/cough and they were like if breathing gets bad go in, but im not at that point. im mostly worried bc it came on kinda weird, first just throat, then the scratchy part, now the coughing mucus thing today and i keep thinking is that normal cold stuff or covid stuff. i have been trying tea and cough drops and drinking water but then i start googling and make it worse in my head. i dont have a fever tho and that is the part that makes me confused, bc everyone says covid can be no fever too. i also have to be around people for work and i dont wanna be dramatic and miss if its just allergies or something, but also dont wanna be that person coughing everywhere. should i test now or wait another day since today is when the coughing really started? and is the light headed thing from not eating much maybe or is that more concerning with covid? sorry this is rambly im just nervous and tired of guessing.
target_response:
Hi Jessica — you’re not being dramatic or rambly. What you’re describing (sore/swollen-feeling throat for a couple days, then scratchiness, then cough with mucus) is a very common pattern for a viral upper respiratory infection, and COVID can look exactly like this. Fever is **not required** for COVID; many people have no fever. It could also be a non-COVID cold, flu/RSV, post-nasal drip/allergies, or irritation. Strep throat is a bit less likely once cough and mucus are present, but still possible if you develop fever, swollen/tender neck glands, tonsil spots, or no cough.

**Testing:** Since you’re now symptomatic and have to be around people for work, I’d test **today** with a home COVID test if you have one. If it’s negative, repeat in **24–48 hours**, because early tests can miss it. If you have access to a PCR/clinic test, that’s more sensitive. Until you know, it’s reasonable to wear a well-fitting mask around others and avoid close contact if you can—especially while actively coughing.

**Lightheadedness:** Brief woozy spells can happen with viral illness, poor sleep, anxiety, dehydration, or not eating much. Try regular fluids plus something with salt/electrolytes, and eat small easy foods even if your throat hurts (soup, yogurt, toast, bananas). If it improves after eating/drinking, that’s reassuring.

For comfort: warm tea/honey, cough drops, saline gargles, humidifier/steam, acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them, and consider guaifenesin for thick mucus. Avoid smoking/vaping if applicable.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, severe weakness/confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, dehydration signs (very little urine, very dizzy standing), fever >103°F, or worsening one-sided throat swelling/drooling/trouble swallowing saliva. Call your clinic for an appointment if symptoms worsen after day 5–7, last >10 days, you develop high fever, or you need a work note/testing.

Bottom line: yes, it *could* be COVID, but it also fits a regular viral cold. Testing today + repeating if negative is the best way to stop guessing.

query: hi this is maria lopez, sorry this is prob dumb but im really confused and kinda anxious bc i keep reading different stuff online and everyone in my house is arguing abt it. to kill virus on hand which soap better? antibacterial or normal handsoap? is antibacterial better since it specialized to get rid germs better? im confused since i read on internet, any kind of soap doesnt matter. like i bought the blue antibacterial one at cvs on 22nd & market but my sister keeps saying regular handsoap is fine if you scrub long enough, and then my coworker says only antibacterial counts which made me panic again. i work around boxes and people touching the same counter all day and i wash a lot already, my hands are dry and cracked now, so i dont wanna be doing the wrong thing 20 times a day and still not killing virus. also if i touch groceries or my phone or door handle at my apt on 814 n 6th st, do i need antibacterial every time or is normal soap really ok? i feel silly asking but the internet says soap breaks up the virus or whatever, but then why do stores sell antibacterial like its stronger? is antibacterial more for bacteria and not virus, or does it still help more? i just dont get if “germs” means all the same stuff or not. i got kids here too and they do a bad job washing, like 5 sec and done, so im trying to tell them the right thing and not scare them. please just tell me plain, normal handsoap vs antibacterial, any real difference for virus on hands?
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. The wording on soaps and “germs” can be genuinely confusing.

**Plain answer: for viruses on your hands, regular hand soap is enough. Antibacterial soap is not meaningfully better.** Your sister is right on this one: what matters most is **washing well and long enough**, not whether the label says “antibacterial.”

Here’s why: many viruses are surrounded by a fatty outer layer. **Soap helps break that layer apart and, just as importantly, lifts germs off your skin so they rinse away.** “Antibacterial” means it has ingredients aimed at **bacteria**, not necessarily viruses. It may still clean your hands because it is soap, but it does not give you a special advantage against common respiratory viruses compared with regular soap.

What to do:
- Use **any regular hand soap** — liquid or bar is fine.
- Wash for **at least 20 seconds**, getting palms, backs of hands, between fingers, thumbs, fingertips, and under nails.
- Rinse and dry well.
- If soap/water isn’t available, use hand sanitizer with **at least 60% alcohol**.

You do **not** need antibacterial soap every time you touch groceries, your phone, or a door handle. Wash before eating/cooking, after the bathroom, after coughing/sneezing/blowing your nose, when coming home from work or public places, and when hands are visibly dirty. For your phone, wiping it regularly with an appropriate disinfecting wipe is often more practical than washing your hands repeatedly every few minutes.

Since your hands are already dry and cracked, over-washing can damage the skin barrier and make irritation worse. Use a gentle/fragrance-free soap if you can, and apply a thick moisturizer or ointment after washing and before bed. Wearing gloves for cleaning can help.

For your kids, keep it simple: “Soap + water + 20 seconds.” A song or timer helps. Don’t scare them — make it routine.

Seek care promptly if cracks become very painful, swollen, warm, oozing pus, spreading redness, or you develop fever — those can be signs of skin infection. But for virus prevention: **regular soap, used properly, is the right tool.**

query: i am marissa jean cole and i am experiencing nasal congestion, sneezing (unaffected by: recent exposure to allergens, exposure to secondhand smoke), sore throat, itchy eyes, ear pressure, nasal drainage, post nasal drip, eye irritation, runny nose, and watery eyes. i am not experie, like idk how to finish that thing in the portal bc it cut me off or i hit the wrong button, but thats the stuff going on and it is driving me nuts. my dob is 03/14/1989 if that helps find me, and my mrn pvh-662914 should be in there from my last visit. i keep thinking maybe allergies but i havent been around anything new that i know of and no smoke around me, so thats why im confused. its mostly my face/nose/throat/ears and eyes, like constant drip and then the ear pressure makes me feel weird when i swallow. i have been trying to work and just keep wiping my nose and my eyes water like im crying which is super annoying on calls. if someone needs to call me my cell is 267-555-0198, but texting is better cause i might be in meetings. email is mjcole89@proton.me too if the portal message doesnt go thru again. i was gonna run to cvs on 19th ave after work but i dont wanna buy random meds if this is something else or if i should be seen. i just need to know if this sounds like a cold thing, sinus thing, or just take something otc and wait. sorry this is rambly, im just tired of feeling stuffed up and itchy and gross all day and then worse when i lay down.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — I’m sorry you’re feeling so miserable. The combination of **sneezing, runny nose, post-nasal drip, itchy/watery eyes, eye irritation, sore throat, and ear pressure** most often fits **allergic rhinitis** or a **viral upper respiratory infection**. The **itchy/watery eyes and sneezing** lean more toward allergies, even if you haven’t been around anything “new” — pollen, dust, mold, pets, and indoor allergens can flare without an obvious exposure. The ear pressure is commonly from nasal inflammation causing Eustachian tube dysfunction, which can feel odd when swallowing.

This sounds **less like a bacterial sinus infection** unless you’ve had symptoms **>10 days**, severe one-sided facial/tooth pain, fever, or you were improving and then suddenly worsened.

For CVS, a reasonable OTC plan would be:

1. **Steroid nasal spray**: fluticasone/Flonase or triamcinolone/Nasacort — 2 sprays each nostril daily for a few days, then 1 spray daily. Aim slightly outward, not toward the septum.  
2. **Non-drowsy antihistamine**: cetirizine/Zyrtec, loratadine/Claritin, or fexofenadine/Allegra daily. Cetirizine can be a bit sedating for some.  
3. **Saline spray or rinse** once or twice daily to clear drainage. Use distilled/boiled-then-cooled water for rinses.  
4. For itchy/watery eyes: **ketotifen** or **olopatadine** allergy eye drops.  
5. If very congested: **pseudoephedrine** can help, but avoid if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure, heart rhythm issues, significant anxiety, or are pregnant. Avoid Afrin/oxymetazoline longer than 3 days.

Given the sore throat/runny nose, consider a **COVID test**, especially if you’ve had exposures or will be around others.

Please seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, wheezing, swelling of lips/tongue/face, high fever, severe headache/stiff neck, vision changes, severe eye pain, severe ear pain or drainage, or significant facial swelling**.

If symptoms persist beyond **7–10 days**, worsen, or you’re not getting relief with the above, schedule a visit so we can check your ears/sinuses and tailor treatment. Also, for privacy, it’s best not to include full identifiers/contact details in portal messages unless required.

query: my father, gerald m. hanley, recently passed over the weekend and im still kinda in shock so sorry if this sounds scattered. his dob was 03/14/1949 and his chart had MRN 8K-21477 on the bracelet and papers they gave us. he was at st. marys hospital and we were dealing mostly with dr. rebecca nolan, who was nice but everything was so rushed and i keep replaying it in my head. his body was fighting an infection along with pneumonia for 1 week. during that time, he would urinate bloody urine into the foley. like not just a little pink or tea colored, it was very dark red, and it was going into the foley bag where we could see it every time we were sitting there. the doctors said that was completely normal. they kept saying with the foley and everything going on it can happen, but now that he passed i feel like i didnt ask enough and i dont know if i shouldve pushed harder or asked for urology or something. i had his aetna medicare #AET-903-4412 card in my purse the whole time and kept signing whatever they put in front of me, but honestly i barely understood half of it. my sister is saying maybe the blood was from the catheter and my brother is saying maybe it meant kidneys or infection getting worse, and now im just stuck on it. it was very dark red. is that normal? i know it wont change anything now, but i need to know if this really can be normal in someone with pneumonia and infection for that week, or if we were just told not to worry bc he was already so sick.
target_response:
I’m very sorry about your father’s passing. It’s completely understandable that you’re replaying details and wondering whether you should have asked more—especially when things moved quickly and you were signing forms under stress.

I can’t review his actual chart here, but I can explain the possibilities. **Very dark red urine in a Foley bag is not “normal” in the sense of healthy urine**, but it is something that can happen in very ill hospitalized patients and is often monitored rather than treated by a urologist unless certain problems arise.

Common reasons include:

- **Foley catheter irritation/trauma**, especially during placement or if the tube is tugged or the prostate is enlarged.
- **Urinary tract infection**, which can occur with or before catheter use.
- **Severe infection/sepsis effects on the body**, including inflammation, low platelets, or clotting problems.
- **Blood thinners** often used in the hospital to prevent clots, or medications he may have already been taking.
- **Kidney stress or injury** from severe infection, low blood pressure, dehydration, or sepsis.
- Less commonly, an underlying bladder/kidney issue that was unmasked during the illness.

With pneumonia plus a systemic infection, doctors would typically be watching kidney function, blood counts, clotting labs, urine output, blood pressure, and whether the Foley was draining. A urology consult is usually more urgent if there are **large clots, the catheter stops draining, the bladder becomes distended/painful, bleeding causes a significant drop in hemoglobin, or there is suspected injury or obstruction**.

So the honest answer is: **dark red urine is concerning and should be assessed, but it can occur as part of severe illness and catheterization. Its presence alone does not necessarily mean something was missed or that pushing harder would have changed the outcome.** Families are not expected to know what questions to ask in a crisis.

If it would help you find peace, request a copy of his records and ask for a follow-up call with the attending physician or hospital patient relations. Specifically ask about: urine tests/cultures, kidney function, blood counts, clotting labs, blood thinners, and whether the Foley ever clotted or obstructed.

And please be gentle with yourself. Shock and second-guessing are very common after a sudden hospital death.

query: hi, i’m sarah kline, dob 03/14/1989, and i have mild asthma which is well controlled if i were to contract covid 19 would i experience any difficulties as can’t seem to find much information on asthma patients who have had the coronavirus many thanks ? sorry if this is a silly question but ive been going round in circles googling and it just makes me more anxious. my asthma is usually fine, i dont really get attacks unless im around loads of dust or i get a bad cold, and i just use my inhaler as needed, so i dont know if im classed as “high risk” or not. i’m on file at riverbend family practice, MRN HN-774182, and i normally see dr leila patel there but i couldnt get a straight answer from the receptionist bc they said to just check the website and honestly theres not much there about mild asthma people, only severe asthma or copd and stuff. i have aetna silver ppo #AET-5920-118 but im trying not to book a visit if this is just reassurance i need. i dont currently have covid 19 symptoms, no fever or anything, but i work around people and im scared that if i catch it i might suddenly not be able to breathe even though my asthma has been well controlled for ages. i keep seeing mixed things saying asthma is a lung condition so be careful, but then others say mild asthma isnt the same, so im confused. should i be doing anything extra now besides normal precautions, and if i did get coronavirus would i need to start my preventer or call someone earlier than other people? many thanks again, just worried and trying not to panic.
target_response:
Hi Sarah — not a silly question at all. It’s very understandable to feel anxious when the information online is mixed.

For someone your age with **mild, well-controlled asthma**, the overall risk of severe COVID-19 is generally **much lower** than for people with severe asthma, COPD, older age, significant heart/lung disease, or those needing frequent oral steroids/hospital care. That said, **any viral infection, including COVID-19, can trigger asthma symptoms** in some people, especially if colds are a usual trigger for you. So it’s reasonable to be cautious, but you should not assume you would “suddenly not be able to breathe” just because you have mild asthma.

What I’d suggest now:

- Stay up to date with **COVID vaccination/boosters** and your **flu vaccine**.
- Keep your **reliever inhaler** available and check it isn’t expired.
- If you have a spacer, use it; if not, it’s worth asking your practice/pharmacy about one.
- Avoid known triggers where possible, especially heavy dust exposure.
- Consider asking Dr. Patel’s team for an updated **asthma action plan**. This is especially helpful so you know exactly when to step up treatment or call.

You do **not necessarily need to start a preventer inhaler just in case** if your asthma is usually very mild and infrequent, but this is worth reviewing. Current asthma guidance often favors having some inhaled steroid protection for many patients, depending on symptom frequency and triggers.

If you develop COVID symptoms or test positive, you should:

- Continue your asthma inhalers as prescribed.
- Monitor symptoms, especially wheeze, chest tightness, or needing your reliever more often.
- Contact your GP/practice early if your asthma starts flaring, if you need your reliever repeatedly, or if you’re unsure whether you need antivirals.

Seek urgent care/emergency help if you have **severe shortness of breath, trouble speaking full sentences, blue lips, chest pain, confusion, oxygen levels persistently below about 92–94% if you monitor them, or your reliever inhaler isn’t helping**.

Bottom line: mild well-controlled asthma does not automatically mean you’re in the highest-risk group, but having a plan and acting early if symptoms worsen is sensible.

query: hi this is marissa j bell, sorry if this is a dumb question but im kind of spiraling over here and googling too much. can a cool mist vaporizer help with the corona virus since it doesn’t like humidity? like i keep reading stuff saying the virus maybe doesnt do as good when air is humid, and then other places say thats not really true, so now im confused and my brain wont shut up. i have a little cool mist one i got last winter from CVS on 19th and oregon and i was thinking of running it in the bedroom tonight but idk if that actually helps or if its just making me feel like im doing something. my dob is 03/14/1986 if that matters for the chart, and my cell is 215-555-0198 but pls dont call super early bc im barely sleeping rn. i also sent a msg to dr. nisha patel thru the portal but i dont know when she’ll answer and im impatient/anxious. my email is marissajbell86@gmail.com if you need that too. i dont have any big answer here, just worried bc the news makes it sound like everything is airborne and sitting around in the house and i keep thinking maybe more humidity helps my throat or cough if one starts, but i dont want to do something pointless or make mold or make the room gross. also do i need distilled water only or is tap ok for one night? if someone has corona virus does the vaporizer make droplets worse or spread it around more? sorry this is rambly, im just trying to figure out if running the cool mist is smart, harmless, or a bad idea.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — not a dumb question at all. This is exactly the kind of thing that gets confusing when you’re reading different things online, especially when you’re anxious and not sleeping.

A cool mist humidifier/vaporizer is **not a treatment or prevention for COVID**, and it should not be relied on to “kill” or stop the virus. While some viruses may survive or travel differently depending on humidity, real-life COVID spread is much more about **close contact, shared indoor air, ventilation, masking when appropriate, and vaccination/boosters** than about the room humidity.

That said, running a cool mist humidifier can be **reasonable and harmless if used correctly**, especially if the air is dry and you have throat irritation, nasal congestion, or a cough. It may make breathing feel more comfortable, but it won’t disinfect the air.

A few practical tips for tonight:

- Aim for indoor humidity around **30–50%**. If windows fog up or the room feels damp, turn it down/off.
- **Clean it well** and empty it daily. Dirty humidifiers can grow mold/bacteria and irritate lungs.
- **Distilled water is best**, especially for ultrasonic cool mist units, because tap water can leave “white dust” mineral particles. For **one night**, tap water is usually not a crisis, but distilled is preferable if you have it.
- Don’t run it nonstop in a closed room for days, and don’t let carpets/walls/bedding get damp.

If someone in the home has COVID, a humidifier is **not the main concern**, but it also doesn’t replace isolation precautions. The bigger steps are: have the sick person stay in a separate room if possible, improve ventilation/open a window if safe, use masks if sharing air, and clean hands/surfaces. A humidifier is unlikely to “make COVID droplets worse” in a major way, but fans/strong airflow blowing from one person toward another can spread respiratory particles, so avoid that setup.

Seek urgent care now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration**, or oxygen levels below **94%** if you’re monitoring.

For tonight: if it helps you feel calmer and the unit is clean, running it at a modest setting is okay. Then try to stop Googling, dim screens, and give Dr. Patel’s office time to reply.

query: hi its maria lopez and sorry if this sounds dumb but im getting really anxious about this and i keep googling which prob makes it worse. can i catch coronavirus on public transportation from someone who has it? how long do germs on public transportation last? i had to take septa route 21 yesterday and it was kinda packed, like people standing close and one guy behind me was coughing into his hand and then holding the pole and i was trying not to touch anything but obv you have to hold on or you fall over. i live at 1234 spruce st and i dont have a car so i cant really just avoid buses/trains totally, and i still have to get to my job and get groceries and stuff. i washed my hands when i got home but i keep thinking about my coat sleeve, my phone, my bag, the seat, the rail, like do i need to wipe everything down every time i get home or am i over doing it?? i dont have symptoms right now, no fever or anything, just nervous bc i know people can have it and not know yet or be coughing and still riding around. if someone sick was on the bus before me, is the air still bad or is it mainly surfaces? like if germs are on the metal pole or the plastic seat, is it minutes or hours or days? i feel silly asking but i live with my aunt and dont wanna bring anything home to her. if you need to call me its 267-555-0148 or email mlopez82@gmail.com but mostly i just wanna know whats realistic and what i should actually do after riding public transportation.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not a dumb question at all. Public transportation can feel especially stressful because you’re close to others and touching shared surfaces. The realistic answer is: **yes, COVID can spread on buses/trains**, but the **main risk is breathing in respiratory particles from an infected person nearby**, especially in a crowded, poorly ventilated space. **Surface spread is possible but much less common** than airborne spread.

If someone coughs into their hand and then touches a pole, virus could be left behind. On metal/plastic, coronavirus can sometimes be detected for hours to a few days in lab conditions, but the amount that can actually infect you usually drops quickly. The key is whether you then touch your eyes/nose/mouth before cleaning your hands. So washing your hands when you got home was exactly the right move.

The air question: if someone sick was on the bus before you, risk from “leftover air” is generally lower once doors open and ventilation/air exchange occurs. The bigger risk is being on the bus at the same time, close to someone coughing or breathing heavily.

What I’d recommend after SEPTA rides:
- Wear a well-fitting mask, ideally **KN95/N95**, especially when packed.
- Use hand sanitizer after getting off and wash hands when home.
- Avoid touching your face during/after the ride.
- Wipe your phone periodically, especially if you used it on the bus.
- You do **not** need to disinfect your coat, bag, seat area, or everything you own every time. If something is visibly dirty or you touched it a lot with unclean hands, wiping it is reasonable.
- When home with your aunt, wash hands before shared activities/food prep. If she is older or high-risk, staying up to date on vaccines/boosters and using good ventilation at home helps.

Monitor for symptoms over the next few days: fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, loss of smell/taste, or fatigue. If symptoms develop, test and avoid close contact with your aunt until you know more.

Seek urgent care for trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, or high/persistent fever.

You’re not overreacting for wanting to protect your aunt — just try to focus on the steps that actually reduce risk rather than wiping everything out of fear.

query: hi, this is marina jacobs, sorry if this sounds dumb but i got an sms stating the following: taking ibuprofin/ advil? mypaid/ myprodol to control fewer should stop. they also say that ibuprofen is increasing the rate of multiplication of the virus. and that we should not take these meds. how true is this? the sms came thru on my phone 267-555-0148 and now everyone in my family group chat is arguing about it and sending screenshots and voice notes and its making me more anxious than helpful tbh. i usually keep advil in the kitchen drawer and my husband sometimes uses myprodol if he feels fluey or has body aches, and now im scared like did we do something wrong if we took it before knowing this?? i dont have a fever right now but i had a headache yesterday and almost took advil and then remembered the msg and stopped. i tried googling and it just made it worse bc one page says dont take it and another says its fine and then some random person says the virus multiplies faster and im like ok who do i trust. i normally ask dr. ethan koh but the office line was busy and i dont want to bug them if this is just fake whatsapp type stuff. i also asked the pharmacist at cvs on 19th & market last time i picked up cough drops but they were slammed and i didnt get a straight answer. should we switch to something else for fewer only, or is this whole warning not proven? i just need plain answer bc im scared to give anything to my mom if she gets sick, but also dont want her sitting with chills and pain for no reason.
target_response:
