- Changing the cabin filter on schedule is reasonable. A higher-quality cabin filter, if compatible with your Camry, may help with general air quality, but it will not reliably “prevent COVID.” No need to change it excessively unless it’s dirty or overdue.

For your **office**:  
“Filtered” and “up to code” can mean many things. You can ask facilities what **MERV rating** the building uses and whether outside-air ventilation has been increased. MERV-13 or higher is generally better for reducing airborne particles, if the system can handle it. Vents blowing over your desk are not necessarily dangerous by themselves, but crowded elevators, break rooms, and poorly ventilated meeting rooms are higher-risk areas.

The biggest practical risk reducers are still:  
- staying home when sick  
- avoiding close contact with coughing people when possible  
- wearing a well-fitting mask, ideally KF94/KN95/N95, in crowded indoor spaces  
- improving ventilation/opening windows when feasible  
- vaccination/boosters if you’re eligible  
- hand hygiene, though COVID is more airborne than surface-spread

Seek urgent care if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, or oxygen levels below about 92% if you monitor at home. Otherwise, your concerns are reasonable — filters help somewhat, but masks and ventilation matter more.

query: hi is the flu injection available? im asking for myself, melissa ann parker, dob 04/18/1989, bc i keep meaning to get it and then i forget and now everybody at work is coughing and im getting nervous lol. i dont need anything fancy i just wanna know if u guys actually have the flu shot in stock before i come all the way over and sit around, bc last yr i went somewhere and they were like nope we ran out after i already parked and filled out papers. also idk if it matters but i have aetna choice pos #AET-4491027 and i can bring the card, i just dont wanna get there and find out its not covered or they need some other thing from me. my phone is 267-555-0148 if someone needs to call, or email melissa.parker89@gmail.com but honestly phone is better bc i dont always check email till late. i havent had the flu injection yet this season, no fever or anything right now, just trying to get ahead of it because my nephew is tiny and my dad gets sick easy so im trying not to be the one who brings germs to everybody. do i need an appt or can i just walk in? and do u have the regular one or is there different kinds, like do i need to say which one i want? sorry if this is a dumb question, i just dont wanna waste a trip and im trying to squeeze it in between errands, so if its available can u let me know what i need to bring and if theres a best time to come when its not packed.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a dumb question at all, and I’m glad you’re trying to get this done before you’re around your nephew and your dad. The flu shot is a good way to reduce your chance of getting influenza and, importantly, spreading it to people who may get sicker from it. It takes about **2 weeks** after the shot for protection to build.

I don’t want you to make a wasted trip, so the best next step is to **call the office before coming in** so the front desk can confirm **same-day flu vaccine stock** and whether walk-ins are being accepted at that time. If this message is being handled through the office, I’ll ask staff to contact you by phone if possible.

For someone your age, the usual option is the **standard inactivated flu shot**. You typically do **not** need to request anything special. The “high-dose” or adjuvanted versions are generally for adults **65+**, and the nasal spray vaccine has more restrictions. If you have a history of a severe allergic reaction to a flu vaccine or any vaccine component, let us know first; otherwise most people can receive it safely.

Please bring:
- Your **insurance card** and photo ID  
- Any updated medication/allergy list  
- Your vaccine record if you happen to have it, though it’s not required  

Flu vaccination is commonly covered as preventive care, but coverage can vary by plan/location, so the front desk can verify with your Aetna information or let you know if there may be a cost.

You can still get the shot if you feel well or have only mild sniffles, but if you develop a **fever or feel significantly ill**, it’s better to wait until you’re improving. If you develop flu-like symptoms—fever, body aches, cough, sore throat—especially with **shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, dehydration, persistent high fever, or worsening symptoms**, seek urgent care. Also call promptly if you become ill and are at higher risk or have close contact with high-risk family members, since antivirals work best early.

For avoiding crowds, mid-morning or early afternoon is often lighter, but calling first is the safest bet.

query: hi, this is melissa kay norton, dob 03/14/1987, i know this is prob a dumb msg but i’ve been sick for the last two weeks with severe sinus pain, hot flashes and a very tight chest along with coughing up some mucus. like my face hurts behind my eyes and cheeks and then the chest tightness makes me panic bc i keep thinking it should be getting better by now. i went to bayside family care and saw dr. emily patel, they put me in as MRN BMC-772904 i think, and i’ve been treated with antibiotics however it doesn’t feel like it has worked. i picked it up at cvs on 1420 atlantic ave and i’ve taken it the way they told me, not skipping it, with food and water and all that, but i still feel gross and wiped out and the cough is still bringing up mucus. sometimes i feel hot like a flash and then chilly-ish later and i keep checking myself even tho i dont know if im doing it right. i’m trying not to overreact but i have work and family around me and i dont want to be the person who ignores something if its contagious. is it possible that this can be covid-1? i know everyone says different stuff and online just makes me more nervous. the flu/cold stuff i usually get doesnt feel this tight in my chest and i’m just confused bc if it was sinus infection wouldnt the antibiotics be helping by now? should i go back in or ask for a covid test or chest check or just wait it out a few more days? sorry this is rambling, i just feel awful and kinda scared.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — this is not a dumb message at all. Two weeks of severe sinus/face pain plus chest tightness and a productive cough is absolutely worth re-checking, especially since you’ve taken the antibiotic as directed and don’t feel you’re improving.

A few possibilities: this may have started as a viral infection (COVID-19, flu, RSV, or another respiratory virus), in which case antibiotics often don’t help. It’s also possible the sinus infection is resistant to that antibiotic, the diagnosis is more bronchitis/pneumonia than sinus-only, or there’s lingering airway inflammation causing the tight chest and cough. Chest tightness can trigger panic, but we should not assume it’s “just anxiety” without checking your lungs and oxygen level.

I’d recommend you contact Bayside Family Care today for a same-day/next-day re-evaluation. Ask whether they can do a COVID-19 test and assess your chest—vitals, oxygen saturation, lung exam, and possibly a chest X-ray depending on what they find. Also let them know exactly which antibiotic you took and when you started it.

In the meantime: drink fluids, rest, use saline nasal spray or a sinus rinse with distilled/boiled-cooled water, consider a humidifier/steam, and use acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely. Guaifenesin may help loosen mucus. Avoid sharing close space, mask around family/work, and stay home if you have fever or feel acutely ill until you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducers and symptoms are improving.

For temperature, use an oral thermometer if possible, wait 15 minutes after eating/drinking, and write down readings.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you have worsening shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen under 94% if you have a pulse ox, coughing blood, severe/worst headache, stiff neck, swelling/redness around an eye, vision changes, or a high/persistent fever.

Bottom line: yes, COVID-19 testing is reasonable, and given the chest tightness and lack of improvement, I would not just wait several more days.

query:
hi i am 39 years old and returned from germany 19 days ago, my name is matthew klein and im prob overthinking this but im getting nervous bc the timing feels weird. i was on lufthansa LH403 coming back and i felt fine the whole flight and the first couple wks after, no fever or anything, just tired from travel and work and all that. then yesterday i started getting a sore throat, runny nose. today i have sinus pressure and a headache with a blocked nose, throat seems to be improving, like it doesnt hurt as much to swallow now, but my nose is totally stuffed and i feel that heavy face/forehead pressure. i dont really have chest pain or sob and im not coughing much, mostly just drainage i think, but with everything ppl say about travel i dont know if this is just a normal cold/sinus thing or if i should be more worried.

my regular dr is dr. priya nolan but i havent called yet bc i dont want to waste anyones time or go sit in a waiting room if im contagious. i usually go to northside family clinic and they have my insurance aetna ppo #AET-772814 on file, but idk if they even do testing there or if i need some special place. should i get tested. if so how? do i call first, do i need an order, or can i just go somewhere? also should i stay home from work right now even tho i only feel like i have sinus pressure and a blocked nose? sorry this is rambly, just trying to figure out the right thing to do and not expose ppl if i shouldnt.
target_response:
Hi Matthew — you’re not wasting anyone’s time. Given the timing and symptoms, this sounds most consistent with a common viral upper respiratory infection (“cold”) or early viral sinus congestion. COVID is also still possible, but getting sick **19 days after the flight** makes something caught specifically on the plane much less likely; most respiratory viruses show up within a few days, and COVID is usually within 2–5 days.

Your current pattern — sore throat improving, then runny/stuffy nose, sinus/forehead pressure, headache, post-nasal drainage, little cough, no shortness of breath or chest pain — fits a typical viral cold. Early sinus pressure does **not** usually mean bacterial sinus infection; antibiotics generally aren’t helpful unless symptoms persist or worsen in certain ways.

**What to do now**
- Take a **home COVID rapid test today** if you have one. If negative, repeat in **24–48 hours** because early tests can miss infection.
- If you need documentation for work, are high-risk, or symptoms worsen, call **Dr. Nolan/Northside Family Clinic first** rather than walking in. They can tell you whether they offer testing, prefer telehealth, or can place an order. Many pharmacies/urgent cares also do COVID/flu testing without a doctor’s order, depending on location.
- For symptoms: fluids, rest, saline nasal spray/rinse, steam/humidifier, acetaminophen or ibuprofen for headache/pressure. A nasal steroid like fluticasone can help congestion. A decongestant may help, but avoid oral pseudoephedrine if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure, heart rhythm issues, or certain medications.

**Work/contagiousness**
Yes, I’d recommend staying home or working remotely while symptoms are new and active, especially until you’ve tested and are improving. If you must be around others, wear a well-fitting mask. In general, return when symptoms are clearly improving and you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers; consider masking for several more days.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, severe/worst headache, stiff neck, swelling/redness around an eye, vision changes, persistent high fever, dehydration, or rapidly worsening symptoms. Call your doctor if symptoms last >10 days, improve then suddenly worsen, or you develop significant fever or one-sided facial/tooth pain.

query: hi, i'm linda martin and i'm on plasmaquine and methotrexate 7.5mg x 1 per week for arthritis. i'm concerned that the methotrexate may weaken my immune system and make me more vulnerable to covid-19. would it not be safer to discontinue use untill the threat has passed? i know i prob sound like im panicking but i have been reading too much online and then i start thinking every little cough in the grocery store is gonna be the thing that gets me. dr nisha patel at penn rheumatology put me on this plan and it has helped my hands some, not perfect but i can open jars better and my morning stiffness isnt as awful, so i also dont want to mess myself up by stopping it wrong. i get the methotrexate filled at cvs on 19th st and i still have a few weeks left, and my bottle says take once weekly, so i keep staring at it like do i skip this week or is that dumb. my insurance is aetna hmo #W23988417 if that matters for maybe asking about a phone visit, but i wasnt sure if this is something i should call about or if its common sense to hold it for now. i dont have a fever or anything right now, this is more me worrying ahead of time, bc covid-19 seems everywhere and i keep hearing immune suppressed ppl are higher risk. my husband says dont stop any meds without asking, but then i think what if asking takes days and meanwhile i take another dose and made myself weaker. sorry for rambling, just trying to know if continuing methotrexate is ok or if stopping untill this whole threat has passed is safer.
target_response:
Hi Linda — you do **not** sound like you’re panicking; this is a very understandable concern, and your husband is right that it’s best not to stop methotrexate without checking with Dr. Patel’s team.

In general, **low-dose methotrexate (7.5 mg once weekly)** can modestly suppress parts of the immune system, but for many arthritis patients the safer plan is to **continue it if you are well and have no COVID symptoms or known exposure**. Stopping suddenly can cause an arthritis flare, and flares sometimes lead to needing prednisone/steroids, which can carry more infection risk than your current low dose of methotrexate. Since you’ve noticed real improvement in your hands and stiffness, maintaining disease control matters.

Your “plasmaquine” is likely **Plaquenil/hydroxychloroquine**, which is generally **not considered strongly immunosuppressive** and is usually continued.

What I’d suggest now:  
1. **Do not skip this week solely out of fear** if you are feeling well, unless Dr. Patel advises otherwise.  
2. **Call Penn Rheumatology and ask for a phone/telehealth message visit**—this is exactly the kind of question they handle frequently. You can tell them: no fever, no COVID symptoms, on methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly and hydroxychloroquine, asking whether to continue.  
3. If you develop **fever, significant cough, sore throat, body aches, loss of taste/smell, or test positive for COVID**, contact them promptly. Many rheumatologists advise **temporarily holding methotrexate during an active infection**, but that should be individualized. Hydroxychloroquine is often continued.

In the meantime, focus on the things that truly reduce risk: stay up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters if eligible, mask in crowded indoor spaces, wash hands, avoid poorly ventilated crowded areas, and have a plan for testing if symptoms occur.

Seek urgent care now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or persistent high fever**.

Bottom line: continuing methotrexate right now is commonly appropriate when you’re well, but please message/call Dr. Patel’s office so you have a personalized plan and peace of mind.

query:
hi, im maddie collins, dob 03/14/1997, and this is prob dumb but i keep thinking about it and then i get nervous bc im a type one diabetic and i know i can get sick weird sometimes. the bottom of my lungs hurt when i yawn. its been going on for about 3 months right after my boyfriends grandmother got sick with pneumonia and sepsis. we were at mercy west hospital with her a lot, like sitting in the room and hallways and cafeteria and stuff, and she was coughing so bad and she cough on me quite a bit and i didnt think much of it then bc everybody was just stressed and worried about her. but now every time i yawn or take that bigger breath it feels like sore or sharp-ish at the bottom, not like i cant breathe exactly, just like something is there and it freaks me out.

i live at 2218 maple ave, dayton, oh and i was gonna call my family doctor dr. lena patel but i hate bothering them if its nothing, plus the last appt took forever and my insurance is anthem bcbs #XQH4472901 and i always get confused what they cover. my number is 937-555-2846 if they need to call me back but i figured id ask first if this sounds like something from being around pneumonia/sepsis or if thats not how it works. i havent really had a big fever or anything that i know of, just this lung bottom pain when i yawn and sometimes i notice it when im laying there thinking about it too much. should i go talk to my family doctor about it? like could something be wrong after all this time or would it have shown up sooner? i dont wanna ignore it bc diabetes but also dont wanna be dramatic.
target_response:
Hi Maddie — it’s not dumb to ask, especially with type 1 diabetes. Pain with a big breath/yawn is something worth discussing, even if it turns out to be benign.

A few reassuring points: **sepsis itself isn’t contagious**, and pneumonia exposure would usually cause symptoms within days to a couple of weeks—not a single deep-breath pain starting/continuing for 3 months without fever, worsening cough, or feeling acutely ill. That said, diabetes can make infections more serious, so it’s reasonable not to ignore persistent chest/lung-area pain.

Pain that happens mainly with yawning or a deep breath is often **pleuritic** or chest-wall related. Common possibilities include:
- **Muscle strain or rib/chest wall inflammation** from coughing, posture, stress, or tension  
- **Costochondritis** or irritation around the ribs  
- Mild **pleural irritation** after a viral illness  
- Less commonly: pneumonia, asthma/reactive airways, reflux, blood clot, or other lung/heart issues

Because it has lasted **3 months**, I do think you should schedule a visit with your family doctor, Dr. Patel, rather than continuing to worry. This doesn’t sound like an ER situation based on what you described, but a routine appointment is appropriate. They may listen to your lungs, check oxygen level/vitals, and consider a **chest X-ray** or other testing if needed.

Seek urgent care/ER now if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pressure, coughing blood, fever/chills, worsening cough, fast heartbeat, fainting, one-sided leg swelling/pain, oxygen levels low if you check them, or moderate/large ketones/high sugars that aren’t coming down**.

In the meantime, note what triggers it—position, exercise, meals, anxiety, pressing on ribs. If you can take them safely, an anti-inflammatory like ibuprofen may help chest wall pain, but avoid it if you have kidney disease, ulcers/bleeding risk, are on blood thinners, or were told not to use NSAIDs.

Also, for privacy, try not to include your address, insurance ID, or phone number in general messages unless you’re in a secure patient portal. But yes—please call your doctor’s office and ask for an appointment. You’re being appropriately cautious, not dramatic.

target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re feeling this way, and your message does **not** sound dumb. Headache with neck soreness can feel scary, especially when you’re already achy and “off.”

What you’re describing could fit with a few common things: an early viral illness such as COVID/flu, dehydration, a tension-type headache/neck muscle spasm, or dizziness/vertigo triggered by head movement. The “sloshing/loose” feeling when you turn your head quickly sounds more like dizziness/vertigo than your brain actually moving, but it is still worth monitoring closely.

**You should be seen urgently today / go to the ER now** if you have any of the following:

If none of those are present, it’s reasonable to do supportive care while arranging a **same-day call or visit with our office/triage nurse**, especially since this is unusual enough that you missed work and feel scared. Please call the office directly or the after-hours line if it’s after hours; email is not ideal for urgent symptoms.

For now: rest, drink fluids/electrolytes, avoid driving if the head-turning sensation makes you dizzy, and move your head slowly. You can use **ibuprofen** if you normally tolerate it: take with food and avoid it if you have stomach ulcers/bleeding, kidney disease, are on blood thinners, or might be pregnant. query: hi , can i use oralcon birth control pills even though we have the coronavirus epademic ? im asking for myself, maria nkosi, dob 02/14/1997, sorry if this is dumb but i keep seeing scary stuff online and now im overthinking every little thing. i usually just take my oralcon every night like normal and i dont wanna mess up my cycle or risk getting pregnant just because everyone is saying stay home and dont go to clinics unless its urgent. i tried calling dr priya menon but the phone line was busy forever and then i got put on hold, and i feel bad calling again bc i know everyone is dealing with corona stuff. i still have like a bit left in my pack but im not sure if i should start the next one like usual or stop until this coronavirus epademic is over, like does the pill make your immune system weaker or make corona worse?? i dont have symptoms or anything, im just nervous. also i normally pick it up at cvs on 5th ave and im trying not to go out if i dont have to, but if i skip it then im scared my body will go weird and my period will be all over the place. my email is maria.nkosi27@gmail.com if someone needs to msg me but really i just need a clear yes or no bc i keep reading mixed things and my friends are saying different things too. so can i still use oralcon birth control pills now during this whole coronavirus thing or should i pause it for safety ?
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is **not a dumb question at all**, and I’m glad you asked rather than stopping based on scary posts online.

**Clear answer: yes, you can continue taking your Oralcon birth control pills during the coronavirus epidemic.** If you are well and have no COVID symptoms, you should **start your next pack as usual** and keep taking it every night as you normally do. The pill **does not weaken your immune system** and there is no good evidence that it makes you more likely to catch coronavirus or makes COVID worse in otherwise healthy people.

Stopping suddenly can lead to **irregular bleeding**, cycle disruption, and—most importantly—**risk of pregnancy**, so continuing is usually the safest plan unless a clinician has told you to stop for another reason.

A few important exceptions: Oralcon is a combined pill, which very slightly increases blood clot risk. COVID—especially if severe or requiring hospital care—can also increase clot risk. So if you develop **serious COVID illness**, are **bedbound**, or are **hospitalized**, tell the treating doctor you are on a combined birth control pill; they may temporarily switch you to another method. For mild cold-like symptoms, most people can continue, but call your clinician if unsure.

For refills, try to avoid unnecessary clinic visits:  
- Call CVS on 5th Ave and ask about **refill status, curbside pickup, delivery, or mail order**.  
- Ask whether they can dispense a **90-day supply**.  
- If your prescription has expired, many doctors’ offices can renew it by phone/telehealth.

If you cannot get your next pack on time, use **condoms** until you restart and for **7 days after restarting**. If you miss pills and have unprotected sex, ask a pharmacist/clinician about emergency contraception.

Seek urgent care if you have: **trouble breathing, chest pain, fainting, coughing blood, one-sided leg swelling/pain, severe headache with vision changes, weakness/numbness, or severe abdominal pain**.

Also, since you included personal details like your DOB/email, be cautious sharing those online. But medically: **continue Oralcon as usual unless you become seriously ill or are told otherwise by your doctor.**

query: i got back from israel last monday and i am in quarantine and honestly im getting more nervous the longer i sit here googling stuff. my name is marta levin and i came home on el al ly027, i dont know if that matters but i keep thinking maybe someone on the plane was sick or maybe it was just anxiety from travel. i have been staying in my room at 4412 pine hollow rd, trying not to touch stuff in the kitchen and wiping the bathroom after i use it, but its hard bc my husband keeps asking if im ok and i dont really know what to say. my symptoms are tight chest and shortness of breath, not like im gasping every second but its there and i notice it more when im laying down or walking to the stairs, and that makes me panic more. i am not trying to be dramatic but i keep thinking what if this is the start of something and i wait too long. i dont have a fever that i know of, i have been checking with the old thermometer and it seems normal, but the chest tightness is freaking me out. do i need to have a test? should i call somewhere first or just stay in quarantine unless it gets worse? should i be taking any medication like inhaler or cold meds or tylenol or anything, or is that bad to take if its maybe coronavirus? i also dont want to show up anywhere and expose people if im not supposed to. if someone has to call me my cell is 267-555-0198 but texting is better bc im anxious and keep checking it. sorry this is rambling i just need to know what the next step is.
target_response:
Hi Marta — I’m sorry you’re feeling so scared. Travel, quarantine, and Googling symptoms can absolutely amplify anxiety, but **chest tightness and shortness of breath are symptoms we should take seriously**, especially after recent travel.

Because you have new breathing symptoms, **yes: you should call for medical guidance/testing rather than simply waiting it out.** The safest next step is to contact your primary care clinician or local health department/COVID testing line **by phone first** and tell them: recent international travel, quarantine status, no known fever, but chest tightness and shortness of breath. They can direct you to the right testing location or evaluation site without exposing others.

That said, please seek **urgent care / ER now or call emergency services** if any of the following occur: shortness of breath at rest or worsening quickly, trouble speaking full sentences, blue/gray lips, severe or crushing chest pain, fainting, confusion, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, coughing blood, or one-sided leg swelling/pain after the flight. A long flight can rarely increase risk of a blood clot, which is another reason not to ignore worsening chest symptoms.

For now, while arranging care: stay isolated in your room, wear a mask if you must be around your husband, use a separate bathroom if possible, wash hands, and continue wiping high-touch surfaces. Ask your husband to keep distance and monitor himself for symptoms.

Medication-wise: **acetaminophen/Tylenol is okay** for fever or aches if needed, following the label and avoiding excess dose. Don’t start an inhaler unless it was prescribed for you. Cold medicines are only for symptoms like congestion/cough and won’t treat breathing tightness. Hydrate, rest, sit upright if lying flat worsens the sensation, and try slow breathing exercises to reduce panic while you make the call.

Bottom line: **don’t walk into a clinic unannounced**, but do call today for guidance/testing. If breathing worsens or chest pain becomes significant, treat it as urgent and call emergency services, letting them know about your travel/quarantine before they arrive.

query: hi, this is melissa grant and im kinda embarrassed but also really anxious bc i got this xray report in my chart and i keep rereading it and googling every line which is prob making it worse. my dob is 03/14/1982 and the paper they gave me has MRN RMC-742918 on top, from riverside imaging center at 8840 pinecrest ave, tampa fl 33614, ordered by dr. nina patel. what is a right central pneumonia? my diagnostic x-ray report states there are hazy densities in the right perihilar and paracardiac areas; trachea is midline; cardiac sillhouette is not enlarged; hemidiaphragms, costophrenic, sulci and bony thoracic cage are unremarkable. i don t understand it. like i know some of those words sound ok maybe but then “hazy densities” sounds scary and “pneumonia” sounds scary too and i dont know if right central means in the middle of my lung or around my heart or what. i was sitting in the parking lot reading it on my phone and now i cant tell if this is something i should be panicking about tonight or just wait for the dr office to call. they said the report would go to my dr but nobody explained it to me and the portal just drops all this medical language like im supposed to know. is right perihilar/paracardiac basically the same as right central? and if it says heart not enlarged and trachea midline and everything else unremarkable is that good news? im sorry this is so long, i just need someone to translate it into normal words, bc i keep thinking hazy densities means something worse than pneumonia even tho maybe it doesnt.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — please don’t be embarrassed. X-ray reports often sound alarming because they’re written for clinicians, not patients, and “hazy densities” is a very common phrase.

In plain English: **“right central pneumonia”** means the radiologist sees an area in the **central part of the right lung** that looks most consistent with pneumonia/infection. The terms **right perihilar** and **paracardiac** describe location:

- **Perihilar** = near the “hilum,” the central area of the lung where the main airways and blood vessels enter.
- **Paracardiac** = near the heart border.
- Together, these can reasonably be summarized as **right central**.

So yes — **right perihilar/paracardiac is basically describing the same general area**: central/right lung near the heart area, not “inside the heart.”

The reassuring parts are important:
- **Trachea is midline** = no shift suggesting lung collapse or major pressure problem.
- **Cardiac silhouette not enlarged** = heart size looks normal.
- **Hemidiaphragms/costophrenic sulci unremarkable** = no obvious fluid around the lungs and no concerning diaphragm findings.
- **Bony thoracic cage unremarkable** = no obvious bone abnormality seen.

“Hazy densities” does **not automatically mean cancer**. It often means the lung markings look cloudy/patchy in a way that can happen with **pneumonia, inflammation, or sometimes atelectasis**—small areas of partial under-inflation. The radiologist is interpreting that pattern as pneumonia.

What to do next: if you have symptoms like cough, fever, chills, chest discomfort with breathing, fatigue, or shortness of breath, you should contact Dr. Patel’s office promptly to discuss treatment—often antibiotics are prescribed depending on the clinical picture. If you were already started on antibiotics, take them as directed and ask when follow-up is needed. Many clinicians repeat a chest X-ray in several weeks to confirm it clears, especially if symptoms persist or if there are risk factors like smoking.

Please seek urgent care tonight if you have **significant trouble breathing, blue lips, confusion, fainting, chest pain/pressure, oxygen level under 92%, coughing blood, or high fever that won’t come down**.

But based on the wording you shared, this sounds like a **localized right-sided pneumonia with several reassuring normal findings**, not something you need to panic over.

query: just found out i was pregnant and i am honestly a mess right now. my name is melissa ann riley and i dont even know if im asking this right bc my brain is going everywhere. yesterday diagnosed with pneumonia. i am a high risk pregnancy. fertility issues, pcos, weak cervix. delivered first daughter at 29 weeks, miscarried, and gave birth at 38 weeks to second daughter, but was on bedrest for weak cervix beginning at 5 months. i m a wreck. when i miscarried they said my progesterone level is low which caused me to miscarry, and gave me progesterone shots every week. can t see doctor for two days, dr. kavita menon is booked and the front desk at st luke's women's clinic just keeps saying if i get worse go in, but like what does worse mean when im pregnant and already scared?? i just found out and now pneumonia on top of it and i keep thinking about my cervix and the pcos and the low progesterone and im panicking that waiting two days is too long. i dont know if pneumonia can hurt the baby this early or if the coughing can do anything to my cervix or if fever or meds are the problem. i asked about progesterone bc last time they gave me shots every week after the miscarriage thing but they said i have to talk to the doctor first. im trying to rest and drink water but im coughing and anxious and crying on and off, and i keep checking every little cramp like is this normal or is this bad. should i push harder to be seen sooner or go to er, or is it ok to wait the two days if im not worse?
target_response:
Melissa, I’m so sorry you’re going through this all at once. Given your history—PCOS/fertility issues, prior 29-week delivery, miscarriage with low progesterone, and cervical weakness—it makes complete sense that you feel panicked. The good news is: **if your pneumonia is being treated, you can breathe comfortably, you do not have a persistent high fever, and you’re not bleeding or having significant pain, waiting 2 days to see Dr. Menon is usually reasonable.**

A few key points:

- **Pneumonia itself usually does not directly harm an early pregnancy**, but **high fever, low oxygen levels, dehydration, or worsening infection** are what we worry about. Take the antibiotics exactly as prescribed and confirm with the clinic/pharmacist that each medication is pregnancy-safe.
- **Coughing does not typically cause miscarriage or “open” the cervix**, especially this early. Cervical insufficiency is monitored later with cervical length ultrasounds, and sometimes progesterone and/or cerclage are considered depending on your history.
- **Progesterone is important to discuss promptly**, but it generally does not have to be started within hours. That said, with your history, I would call back and specifically ask for: “Can the on-call OB review my chart today and advise whether I should start progesterone before my appointment?”

For now: rest, hydrate, eat small amounts if you can, and use **acetaminophen/Tylenol for fever** as directed. Avoid ibuprofen/naproxen unless your OB specifically approves. If you have a pulse oximeter, oxygen should generally stay **95% or higher**.

Please go to the ER/urgent care now if you have **shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen below 95%, fever 100.4°F or higher that won’t come down with Tylenol, inability to keep fluids down, worsening pneumonia symptoms, coughing blood**, or pregnancy symptoms such as **heavy bleeding, severe one-sided pelvic pain, shoulder pain, or severe cramping**.

If none of those are happening, keep the appointment in 2 days—but it is absolutely reasonable to push for an on-call OB/nurse callback today because you are high-risk.

query: nervous about coronavirus. i am 26 years old and have high blood pressure. my name is mike delgado and i know im prob overthinking but i cant stop googling stuff and it just makes me worse. i am curious chances of me dieing if i contract the virus. like i keep seeing ppl say young ppl are fine but then other posts say if u have blood pressure problems ur high risk and now im just sitting here checking my temp every hour even tho i dont even feel that sick right now, just anxious. i see dr priya nolan for my bp and she told me before to take it serious and keep taking my meds, but i dont know if high blood pressure at my age counts the same as older ppl with it or if its not as bad bc im only 26. i use cvs on 5th ave for my refills and i got my bp pills there last week, but now im scared to even go inside anywhere. my insurance is keystone hmo #KST-4480192 and i dont even know if they cover testing unless u already have bad symptoms, and i dont want to waste a dr visit if im just panicking. i work around ppl and i wash my hands a lot but im still thinking what if i catch it from some random person and then thats it. like realistically if i got coronavirus with high blood pressure, what are the odds it gets severe or i die? should i be staying home completely, or is that being dramatic? sorry if this sounds dumb i just need a straight answer bc the internet is all over the place and im freaking myself out bad.
target_response:
Hi Mike — this does **not** sound dumb. It sounds like anxiety getting amplified by a lot of scary, inconsistent information online.

Straight answer: at **26**, your chance of dying from COVID is generally **very low**, especially if you are otherwise healthy and your blood pressure is controlled. High blood pressure is listed as a risk factor for more severe COVID, but most of the higher-risk data comes from people who are **older**, have **poorly controlled hypertension**, or have other conditions like diabetes, kidney disease, obesity, heart disease, or lung disease. Having hypertension at your age does not automatically put you in the same risk category as an older adult with multiple health problems. It does mean you should take prevention seriously and keep your BP well managed — which you are already doing.

Please **keep taking your blood pressure medication** unless Dr. Nolan specifically tells you to stop. This includes common BP meds like ACE inhibitors or ARBs; stopping them can be more dangerous than continuing them.

You do not need to check your temperature every hour if you feel well — that will likely feed the anxiety. If it helps, limit yourself to checking **once daily** or only if you develop symptoms. Also consider taking a break from Googling and using one reliable source, such as the CDC or your local health department.

As for staying home completely: you do not necessarily need to isolate if you are well and have no known exposure, but you should be sensible — stay up to date on vaccination/boosters, wear a well-fitting mask in crowded indoor settings, wash hands, avoid close contact with sick people, and stay home/test if symptoms develop.

Testing coverage varies, so for insurance specifics, call Keystone or Dr. Nolan’s office; many offices can advise whether testing is appropriate without a full visit.

Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, fainting, oxygen levels under 92% if you have a pulse oximeter, or persistent high fever/worsening symptoms**.

Given how distressed you feel, I’d also message or call Dr. Nolan’s office — not because you’re in danger right now, but because your anxiety deserves care too.

query: how do i know if i have corona virus? this is tanya mills btw and im sorry if this sounds dumb but im kinda spiraling and reading stuff online is making it worse not better. i keep seeing ppl say its like a cold and then other ppl say u can be fine then suddenly not fine, so idk what im even supposed to watch for. like do i need a fever for it to count or can u have it without one, and if i just feel weird or tired is that enough to worry? i dont wanna overreact and run somewhere if its nothing but i also dont wanna be the person who waits too long or gives it to somebody else. i was gonna call but the hold music makes me anxious lol, my number is 410-555-0197 if someone actually needs to reach me. i also have my chart thing at Mercy QuickCare but i dont know if im supposed to message them first or just go to urgent care or stay home, and every website says something different. do i need a test only if i was around someone confirmed, or if i just think maybe i was? and how long after being around ppl would it show up? i dont have a ton of money for random visits either so im trying to figure out the smart way to do this. should i wear a mask in the house, stay in my room, not share cups, all that stuff even before knowing? sorry this is rambling, i just want like plain english signs that mean yes go get checked now vs just monitor it and dont panic.
target_response:
Hi Tanya — this is not dumb at all. COVID information can be confusing, and anxiety can make every symptom feel scary. Let’s put it in plain English.

You **do not need a fever** to have COVID. Some people have fever, but many have only mild symptoms or no fever at all. Possible COVID symptoms include: sore throat, runny/stuffy nose, cough, headache, body aches, chills, fatigue, “weird/tired” feeling, loss of taste or smell, nausea/diarrhea, or shortness of breath. Feeling tired by itself is not an emergency, but it can be a reason to **monitor and consider testing**, especially if you had a possible exposure.

**When to test:**  
- If you have symptoms: take a home rapid test now if you have one. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**.  
- If you were exposed but feel okay: test around **day 5 after exposure**, or sooner if symptoms start.  
- COVID symptoms usually show up **2–14 days** after exposure, commonly around days 3–5.

**What to do right now while unsure:**  
Yes — be cautious, but don’t panic. Stay home if you can, wear a mask around others in the house, avoid sharing cups/utensils, wash hands, improve ventilation/open windows if possible, and sleep in a separate room if practical. This is especially important if anyone at home is older, pregnant, immunocompromised, or has lung/heart disease.

**When to get checked urgently / now:**  
Go to urgent care or ER if you have trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, dehydration, oxygen level under 92% if you have a pulse ox, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening. Also seek medical advice early if you are high-risk because antiviral treatment works best within the first 5 days.

For the lowest-cost next step: use a **home test** or a pharmacy/community testing site if available, and message **Mercy QuickCare through MyChart** describing symptoms/exposure and asking if a visit is needed. Avoid walking into urgent care unless you have red flags or need evaluation.

Also, for privacy, it’s best not to post phone numbers in messages unless you’re in a secure patient portal.

query: hi, may i answer your health queries right now ? please type your query here... this is melissa ann keller, dob 03/14/1979, and i know this msg is probably messy but i am tired and kinda scared. i have been to the doctor 2 times. first time, it was a severe sinus infection, week later had to go back, turned into pneumonia, had to go back third time. all 3 times i was given a shot, antibotics & c-pack first time, steroids, antibotics & shot second and antibotics & shot third. i saw dr priya nair at baptist health urgent care on shelbyville rd and then one of the other people there the last time, i cant remember his name bc i felt so bad and was coughing and trying not to cry in the room. i picked up the meds at cvs on bardstown rd and i have taken them like they said, or at least i think i did, but i am still hurting. i have been hurting under my ribs and has gone to my back and shoulder. it is not like a tiny ache either, its sharp sometimes when i move or cough and then sometimes it just sits there and makes me worry. i keep thinking maybe i slept weird but then i remember the pneumonia thing and get scared it has moved or gotten worse or something. i dont want to keep running back and being dramatic, and money is tight, but i also dont want to ignore it if its bad. my husband says call again but i feel dumb because i was just there. do you think the pneumonia has gotten worse? do i need to go back to the doctor?
pii_units: baptist health urgent care on shelbyville rd
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m really sorry you’re going through this. You are **not being dramatic or “dumb”**. With pneumonia, new or persistent pain under the ribs that goes into the back/shoulder—especially if it’s sharp with coughing or movement—deserves to be taken seriously.

There are a few possibilities. Sometimes after pneumonia or a lot of coughing, people develop **muscle strain/costochondritis** (rib/chest wall inflammation), which can cause sharp pain with movement, deep breaths, or coughing. Pneumonia can also irritate the lining around the lung (**pleurisy**), which causes sharp “catching” pain and can radiate to the back or shoulder. Less commonly, pneumonia can worsen or cause complications like **fluid around the lung**, an abscess/empyema, or another issue such as a blood clot—so it’s important to be checked if the pain is significant or not improving.

Given you’ve already had multiple visits, multiple antibiotics/steroid shots, and you’re still having notable rib/back/shoulder pain, I do think you should **call the urgent care/your doctor today** and tell them exactly this: “I was treated for pneumonia, but I still have sharp pain under my ribs going to my back/shoulder, worse with coughing/movement.” They may want to recheck your oxygen level, lungs, temperature, and possibly repeat a **chest X-ray** or adjust treatment.

Please go to the **ER now** if you have any of these: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure not just with coughing, coughing up blood, blue lips, confusion/fainting, fever that won’t come down or returns after improving, heart racing, oxygen level <92% if you can check it, severe one-sided chest pain, or pain with swelling in one leg.

While waiting to be seen/called back: rest, hydrate, use a pillow to “splint” your ribs when coughing, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely. Continue medications as prescribed unless a clinician tells you otherwise.

Your husband is right to encourage you to call. This is a reasonable reason to be re-evaluated.

query: dear doctor my littel son adam rahman 11 months, i am his mom and i am very worry and tired because this story from when he was so small and i dont know what is normal now or not. when he was 2 months and 15 days he got chest infection pneumonia in the right lung and after 2 weeks of treatment in the hospital with antibiotic he discharged next day he was admitted again with new infected by pneumonia in the right lung at al noor children hospital and then doctor leila haddad did some test a barium swallow and ph. the resulte ge reflux grade 4. he was with ngt and ar formula by ngt for 3 moths with zantac and motinorm also with sitting position ,but all of those dosn t change any thing. i was sitting all night holding him up and every feeding i was scared it will go to his chest again, and my family keep telling me maybe i am over thinking but i see him every day and i know this is not easy. after that when he was 5 months and 15 days he had an a laparoscopic fundoplication 5 ports, 360m degree wrap over 10fr ryles tube. he trying to vomit but he can t. i observed every time when he trying to vomit like for 5 to 7 minutes and when he sneeze every time after trying vomit then he got back to normal after sneezing.. it look like he is fighting to bring something out but nothing come, only after sneeze he relax and breathe normal and play again. and he drinking now neocate formula based on free amino acid. also he is allergy with lots of food ,. i am asking if this sneezing after trying vomiting is because of the wrap or reflux still there or allergy, and do i need repeat ph or swallow test again because i am really scared pneumonia come back again.
target_response:
Dear Adam’s mom, I’m so sorry you and Adam have been through so much. Your worry is very understandable—recurrent pneumonia in a small baby, severe reflux, tube feeds, and then fundoplication is exhausting and frightening.

After a 360° fundoplication, many babies **cannot vomit normally**. Instead, they may have episodes of **retching/gagging** where they look like they are trying to bring something up but nothing comes. This can happen from the wrap being tight/effective, trapped gas (“gas-bloat”), ongoing reflux above the wrap, delayed stomach emptying, or irritation from mucus/allergies. The **sneeze afterward** may be a reflex from irritation in the nose/throat or vagus nerve stimulation during gagging/retching. Sneezing by itself is not usually dangerous, but the pattern you describe—5–7 minutes of retching then relief—deserves review.

It is also important to know that a fundoplication reduces reflux, but it does not always eliminate it, and babies with food allergy can still have discomfort, mucus, gagging, or feeding intolerance. Since Adam has multiple food allergies and is on Neocate, he should ideally be followed by a **pediatric gastroenterologist and pediatric allergist/dietitian**.

I would recommend arranging a follow-up with his pediatric surgeon/GI doctor soon and bringing **videos of these episodes**. Ask whether he needs:
- **pH-impedance study**: better than pH alone because it can detect acid and non-acid reflux.
- **Contrast/barium swallow**: to check the wrap position/tightness and anatomy.
- **Swallow/aspiration assessment** if there is coughing/choking during feeds or recurrent chest infections.

Seek urgent care immediately if he has breathing difficulty, blue lips, persistent cough after feeds, fever, fast breathing, wheezing, poor feeding, dehydration, repeated long retching episodes, severe abdominal swelling, green vomiting, blood in vomit/stool, or unusual sleepiness.

Until reviewed, keep feeds as advised, avoid overfeeding, burp frequently, keep him upright after feeds, and follow his allergy plan strictly. You are not overthinking—your observations are important, and Adam should be reassessed to make sure the wrap is working properly and he is not aspirating.

query: hey, this is maddie ramos, dob 03/14/1997, i know this is prob not a huge emergency but im kinda spiraling bc my tonsils are swollen, cough, and feeling slightly feverish. its not like a super high fever or anything i dont think, i just feel warm and gross and my throat feels puffy when i swallow, like i keep checking in the mirror w my phone light and then freaking myself out more. my cough is annoying too, not constant every second but enough that ppl keep looking at me and i feel bad. i have to work tomorrow and i dont wanna be that person coughing around everyone if its something contagious, but also i dont wanna go sit in urgent care forever if its just a random cold thing. i had a message in the portal under MRN PR-44819 but i didnt see anyone answer yet, so im trying here too. if someone can call me at 267-555-0196 thats easiest bc my app notifications are weird, or email maddie.ramos27@gmail.com if its after hours. i saw dr lena patel once before for strep stuff and she said i get big tonsils easy, so idk if this is just that again or if i should get swabbed. i havent taken much besides water and cough drops, was gonna maybe grab something at CVS on 10th and Pine but not sure what actually helps swollen tonsils. do i need antibiotics or a strep/covid test or just wait it out? im trying not to overreact but i feel slightly feverish and the swollen tonsils are making me nervous esp with the cough, so just tell me what next step makes sense.
target_response:
Hi Maddie — I’m sorry you’re feeling this way. Swollen tonsils can feel really alarming, especially when you keep checking them, but the combination of swollen tonsils + cough + feeling warm is most often a viral upper respiratory infection. That said, because you’ve had strep before and tend to get enlarged tonsils, it’s reasonable to get tested rather than guess.

**Best next step:**  
If you can, do a **home COVID test today**. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours. For **strep**, you’d need a rapid strep test/throat culture through our office, urgent care, or a pharmacy clinic. Strep is more likely when there is fever, no cough, swollen/tender neck glands, and white patches on tonsils—but cough makes a viral cause more likely. **Antibiotics are only recommended if strep is confirmed** or strongly suspected after evaluation.

**For work tomorrow:**  
If you feel feverish, are coughing, or may be contagious, it’s best to **stay home if possible**, or at minimum wear a well-fitting mask, avoid close contact, and wash hands frequently. If you have a true fever, stay home until fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers.

**What to pick up at CVS:**  
- **Ibuprofen or acetaminophen** for throat pain/feverish feeling, if you can take them safely  
- **Warm salt-water gargles**, tea with honey, throat lozenges  
- **Guaifenesin** for mucus-y cough or **dextromethorphan** for dry irritating cough  
- Plenty of fluids; humidifier/steam can help

Try not to keep repeatedly checking with the phone light—it can make the anxiety spike and doesn’t usually change what we do unless breathing/swallowing is worsening.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have trouble breathing, drooling, inability to swallow liquids, one tonsil much larger than the other with uvula deviation, severe neck swelling/stiffness, persistent fever >103°F, dehydration, chest pain, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

Otherwise, I’d recommend arranging a **same-day or next-day strep/COVID/flu evaluation** if symptoms persist or you develop a clearer fever.

query:
is it true (or a myth) that coronavirus will not survive hotter climates. as summer approaches, is it expected to reduce in tropical summers at least. im asking bc im marisol vega, dob 03/14/1986, and i keep hearing totally opposite things from ppl at work and family and online, like one person says heat kills it and another says no it spreads anywhere and honestly its making me nuts. i left a msg for dr hina patel too but havent heard back yet and i know everyone is slammed so im not trying to be annoying, just confused. my phone is 305-555-0148 if somebody needs to call but email is easier sometimes, marisol.vega86@gmail.com, bc i keep missing calls while im running around doing errands and checking on my mom.

like if the weather is hot and humid and its basically summer, does that mean the virus is weaker or less likely to spread, or is that just one of those internet myths ppl keep repeating to feel better? im not asking bc i want to ignore precautions, im still washing hands and trying not to get too close to ppl, but i keep wondering if tropical summers at least reduce it some, or if it can still be just as bad in hot places. also does sun/heat outside matter but ac inside makes it different? bc stores and buses and offices are all freezing cold inside even when outside is burning hot, so im not sure how that works. sorry this is rambly, i just want a straight answer if there even is one. like should we expect cases to go down as summer approaches, or is it not safe to assume that at all?
target_response:
Hi Marisol — you are **not being annoying at all**. This has been a confusing topic because people often mix together a few different ideas: heat, humidity, sunlight, indoor air, and “seasonality.”

**Straight answer:** It is a myth that coronavirus “won’t survive” or “can’t spread” in hot climates. COVID-19 can and has spread in tropical, hot, and humid places. Warmer weather may have *some* effect on transmission, but it is not strong enough to rely on, and it does **not** make precautions unnecessary.

A few key points:

- **Heat/humidity may reduce survival of the virus on some surfaces or in droplets somewhat**, but most COVID spread is through the air when people breathe, talk, cough, or sneeze near others—especially indoors.
- **Sunlight/UV outdoors can help break down virus particles**, and outdoor air disperses them much better. That’s why outdoor settings are generally lower risk than crowded indoor ones.
- **Air-conditioned indoor spaces can still be high risk**, even in a hot summer climate. Stores, buses, offices, and waiting rooms may have poor ventilation, recirculated air, crowding, and close contact—those factors matter more than the outdoor temperature.
- Cases may sometimes go down in warmer months, but that depends on many things: immunity levels, variants, vaccination rates, people gathering indoors, travel, testing, and public health measures. So it’s **not safe to assume summer will make cases drop**.

What I’d recommend: keep doing the basics—stay home when sick, test if you have symptoms or an exposure, wash hands, improve ventilation when possible, and consider a well-fitting mask in crowded indoor places, especially while checking on your mom if she is older or has health conditions. Stay up to date on COVID vaccination/boosters as recommended for your age and risk factors.

Seek urgent care if you or your mom develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels persistently below about 92%** if you use a pulse oximeter.

Bottom line: **hot weather may help a little, but it does not “kill off” COVID or prevent spread. Indoor precautions still matter.**

query: my 80 year old wife margaret ellis is home recovering from pneumonia (double) twice in august and i am sitting here checking on her every few min cause im scared and dont know if im over reacting or not. her blood pressure has dropped to 82/44. how dangerous and how can i increase the level. she says shes just tired and wants to sleep but that number looks way too low to me, and i keep taking it again on the same cuff thinking maybe i did it wrong but its staying low. she was at st agnes hospital both times, first trip they sent her home with oral antibiotics the first time, no antibiotics the second time, and i still dont really understand why it changed because she seemed just as weak to me. dr. nina patel told us to watch breathing and fever and fluids, but nobody really said what to do if the pressure drops like this after we are already home. she has been treated for this disease 4 times in past 12 months. im worried maybe the pneumonia never really cleared up or maybe the medicine is too strong or maybe she is dehydrated, i dont know. she is drinking some water but not much, eating almost nothing, and walking to bathroom makes her shaky. i have her prescriptions filled at CVS on market st and i can call them if its a med question but i dont know if this is more like call 911 stuff. her card is Aetna medicare #AE-4491027 if that matters for home nurse or something. i just need to know if 82/44 is dangerous for a woman her age recovering from double pneumonia and what i can safely do right now to get it up.
target_response:
I’m very glad you checked and asked. **A blood pressure of 82/44 in an 80-year-old recovering from bilateral pneumonia, especially with very poor eating/drinking and shakiness walking to the bathroom, is potentially dangerous and should be treated as urgent. Please call 911 now.** This is not something to try to manage at home or wait out overnight.

Low blood pressure like this can happen from **dehydration**, but in her situation it can also signal **sepsis or worsening infection**, low oxygen, a heart rhythm problem, medication effects, or another serious complication after pneumonia. Her saying she is “just tired and wants to sleep” may be simple exhaustion, but with that pressure it could also mean her brain and organs are not getting enough blood flow.

While waiting for EMS:

1. **Have her lie flat** on her back if she can breathe comfortably.  
2. **Elevate her legs** on pillows 8–12 inches.  
3. **Do not let her walk to the bathroom**—use a bedside commode/bedpan if needed.  
4. If she is fully awake and not coughing/choking, she can take **small sips of water or an electrolyte drink**, but don’t force fluids.  
5. Keep her warm, and gather her medication bottles, discharge papers from St. Agnes, allergy list, and recent temperature/oxygen readings if you have them.  
6. If she takes blood pressure medicines, diuretics/water pills, or sedating medicines, **do not give another dose until EMS/doctor advises**, unless specifically instructed otherwise.

Also call 911 immediately/mention if she has any of these: confusion, fainting, blue lips, chest pain, severe shortness of breath, oxygen saturation low if you measure it, fever or very low temperature, new weakness, little/no urination, or inability to stay awake.

You are **not overreacting**. With repeated pneumonia and four episodes in 12 months, she also needs a follow-up plan after she is stabilized—sometimes recurrent pneumonia is related to aspiration/swallowing issues, COPD/asthma, heart failure, immune problems, or an unresolved lung finding. But right now, the priority is emergency evaluation for the low blood pressure. Insurance details are not needed to decide this—**call 911 now.**

query: hi, i am a 20/f, my name is mia van der merwe and im sorry if this is dumb but im kinda panicking and i dont know if i should just sleep or go back in. i went to the dentist & had a root canal at BrightSmile Dental in hatfield with Dr. Priya Naidoo, it was honestly awful and i was already sore and scared before they even started. i also had a abscess so they said i needed meds and was put on purmycin & adco-metrondaz. i got them from Clicks Pharmacy on Burnett st and the lady there said take them like they wrote it but they made me nauseous basically the whole time, like not throwing up every second but that gross sick feeling in my stomach and no appetite and just feeling weak. i tried eating with them and drinking more water but still felt bad. today was the last day of the medications and i didn't take it as i havea fever of 38.3 and now im worried i messed it up or maybe the meds are doing something to me. is it the medication? or is it the tooth infection still? my gum is still tender but not like before, and my face isnt huge or anything, but i keep checking my temp and then checking again and making myself worse probably. my mom keeps saying phone the dentist in the morning but i dont know if fever means emergency or if it can happen after antibiotics. i just feel hot then cold and nauseous and anxious and im scared bc the root canal was supposed to fix it. i dont know if missing the last dose is bad either, like do i take it later or no since i feel feverish. pls help im really stressed
target_response:
Hi Mia — this is not dumb at all. A fever after a dental abscess/root canal is understandably scary, especially when the whole experience was already awful.

A few things can be true here:

1. **The nausea and poor appetite are very likely from the antibiotics**, especially **metronidazole** (Adco-metronidazole), which commonly causes nausea, metallic taste, stomach upset and weakness. Purmycin can also upset the stomach. Avoid alcohol completely while on metronidazole and for 48 hours after.

2. **A temperature of 38.3°C can mean your body is still reacting to the infection/procedure**, or sometimes a separate viral illness. Antibiotics do not always make fever disappear immediately, and a root canal/abscess can remain tender for a few days. However, fever near the end of antibiotics means you should be **reviewed by the dentist or a doctor within 24 hours**, because occasionally the abscess needs further drainage, a different antibiotic, or reassessment.

3. **Missing the very last dose is unlikely to ruin everything**, especially if you completed almost the full course. Don’t double-dose. Because you feel feverish/nauseous, I’d suggest you **phone the dentist/pharmacy in the morning before taking more**, unless your prescription instructions clearly say otherwise and you can tolerate it.

For tonight, if you have **no facial swelling, no trouble swallowing/breathing, and the pain is not rapidly worsening**, it is reasonable to rest, sip fluids, eat bland food if possible, and take **paracetamol** for fever/pain if you can take it safely. You can also use ibuprofen if you normally tolerate it and have no stomach ulcer, kidney disease, blood thinners, or allergy.

**Go to urgent care/emergency tonight** if you develop: increasing facial/jaw/neck swelling, difficulty opening your mouth, swallowing or breathing, drooling, severe worsening pain, confusion, fainting, persistent vomiting, a rash/hives or swelling of lips/tongue, fever ≥39°C, or fever with feeling very unwell/rigors.

Try not to keep rechecking your temperature every few minutes — check every 4–6 hours. But please do contact Dr Naidoo/BrightSmile in the morning and tell them you still have fever after treatment.

query: true or false? anyone infected with covid-19 start have diarrhea and increases the chances of death compared to person who just start respiratory symptoms, why? im asking because i keep seeing random posts and then other posts say the opposite and now im confused and kinda scared. this is maria lorenzo, dob 08/14/1987, and my chart thing says mrn HUP-774291 if that matters. i called the nurse line earlier but i prob explained it bad bc i was talking fast, my call back is 267-555-0198 and email is maria.lo1987@gmail.com, but i didnt know if i should bother dr. nisha patel again with just a general question. like if somebody has covid and the first thing is diarrhea, does that mean the virus is worse in their body or like already spread more, or is it just one of the normal symptoms? i dont mean a little upset stomach after meds, i mean it starts that way before cough or breathing stuff, then maybe fever later. i read people with only respiratory symptoms can still get bad too so i dont understand why stomach symptoms would mean higher death or not. also does dehydration make it worse and that is why ppl say death chance goes up? sorry im rambling, i just want a straight true or false if diarrhea at the start is a bad sign compared with someone who just start respiratory symptoms. i know nobody can diagnose from a message, im just trying to understand before i panic and tell everybody in my house the wrong thing. pls explain it simple if possible bc all the articles are all medical words and i get lost.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m glad you asked. The straight answer is: **False, as a general rule.** If COVID-19 starts with diarrhea, that **does not automatically mean the virus is “worse,” more spread through the body, or that the person is more likely to die** compared with someone who starts with cough or breathing symptoms.

Diarrhea can be one of the **normal early symptoms** of COVID. Some people get fever/cough first, some get sore throat or fatigue first, and some get stomach symptoms like diarrhea, nausea, or cramps first. The order symptoms appear in does **not reliably predict** how severe the illness will become.

Why do some posts say diarrhea increases death risk? A few studies found that people with gastrointestinal symptoms sometimes had worse outcomes, but that does **not prove diarrhea itself causes severe COVID**. Those findings may be influenced by other factors, such as older age, other medical conditions, dehydration, delayed testing/diagnosis, or more overall inflammation. Other studies have not found the same risk. So it’s not a dependable “bad sign” by itself.

**Dehydration can make anyone feel much worse** and can be dangerous, especially with fever, vomiting, or frequent watery stools. But dehydration is usually something we can reduce risk from by treating early: sip fluids often, use oral rehydration solution/electrolyte drinks, eat bland foods if tolerated, and avoid alcohol. If diarrhea is severe or persistent, it’s reasonable to check in with your doctor.

For someone with possible COVID and diarrhea: test for COVID if available, stay home/avoid exposing others, wear a mask around household members, and monitor symptoms.

**Seek urgent care now** if there is trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, fainting, signs of dehydration such as very little urine/dizziness/dry mouth, blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, fever that won’t come down, or diarrhea lasting more than 2–3 days or occurring many times per day.

And no, this is not “bothering” Dr. Patel—this is a reasonable question. Also, for privacy, try not to include DOB/MRN/phone/email in general messages unless you’re in a secure patient portal.

query:
hi, so my colleague michael tran is covid 19 positive and im trying not to panic but also dont wanna be dumb about it. i had meetings with him, like actual sit down conference room meetings at Comcast Center at 1701 John F Kennedy Blvd in Philadelphia, PA, not just passing in the hallway, and now im replaying every cough and every time someone touched the coffee pot ugh. work sent around the same basic instructions are to self isolate and get tested if show symptoms. i get that, and im doing the self isolate thing as much as i can, staying in the spare room and wiping handles and not hugging the kids, but i have no symptoms. no fever, no cough, no sore throat, nothing really except anxiety and checking my temp way too much.

my question is, is it possible to opt to get tested anyway even if i have no symptoms? like i dont want to waste tests if theyre scarce, but i also dont want to go back to work or be near my family if im carrying it and dont know. i called my pcp dr. nina patel and left a msg but havent heard back yet, and my insurance is Independence Blue Cross #IBX-44721903 so idk if they cover testing without symptoms or if i need a referral. i can be reached at 267-555-0148 if someone actually needs to call me, but mostly just wondering what the rule is. if exposed in meetings, no symptoms, self isolating, can i choose to test or do i have to wait until symptoms show up? also how long after exposure would a test even mean anything, bc testing too early seems like it could be pointless maybe.
target_response:
Hi — I understand why this feels unsettling. A sit-down meeting in a conference room is a meaningful exposure if you were near him for a prolonged period, especially if masks/ventilation were limited. The good news is that having no symptoms right now is reassuring, and you’re already doing the right things to reduce risk to your family.

**Yes, you can choose to get tested even without symptoms.** You do not necessarily have to wait for symptoms. The key is timing:

- **If symptoms develop at any point**: test right away and stay away from others.
- **If you remain asymptomatic**: testing is most useful **about 5 full days after your last exposure**. Testing earlier can be falsely negative because the viral level may not be detectable yet.
- A **PCR/NAAT** is more sensitive, especially if you have no symptoms.
- If using **home antigen tests**, consider testing on day 5 and again **48 hours later**, because one negative antigen test does not fully rule it out.

Until then, I’d recommend you continue precautions for **10 days after the last exposure**: mask around others, avoid close contact with household members when feasible, improve ventilation, wash hands, and wipe high-touch surfaces. You don’t need to obsessively disinfect everything, and checking your temperature repeatedly may just feed the anxiety — once or twice daily is enough unless you feel ill.

Coverage rules vary. Many insurers cover medically indicated COVID testing after a known exposure, but asymptomatic “screening” may be handled differently depending on the plan and testing site. Since you already left a message for Dr. Patel, that’s a good next step; you can also check with Independence Blue Cross or a pharmacy/testing site about whether a referral is needed and any cost.

Seek urgent care promptly if you develop **trouble breathing, persistent chest pain/pressure, new confusion, fainting, blue/gray lips, severe weakness, dehydration**, or a high fever that won’t come down. Also contact your doctor quickly if you test positive and are higher risk, because antiviral treatment works best early.

Bottom line: don’t panic, but testing around day 5 is reasonable and responsible.

query: i've been in contact with a possible covid-19 case who is waiting on results and im trying not to panic but its hard bc it feels like every little cough now is a big deal. my name is tied to the chart under tanya.ellis82@gmail.com if that matters, and the best call back for me is 267-555-0198, but mostly im writing about my brother marcus ellis. he seems to be i'll with corona symptoms and i'd like to get advice on how to help him, as his safety will ensure my safety and prevent the spread of the virus. we live in the same place right now and share the kitchen/bathroom so i dont even know how realistic “stay away from him” is, like do i bring food to his door, do i wipe every handle every time, does he need to wear a mask in his room too or just if he comes out. he is acting like its prob just a cold but he has been laying around and looking rough and im scared bc the person i was near is still waiting on results, so we dont know anything for sure. i called penn medicine urgent care on south st earlier but got confused on what they were saying about testing and if he should wait, and i dont want to clog up the er if its not needed but i also dont want to miss something. we have keystone first #KF-7842091 if insurance info is needed, but im mainly asking what is the safest thing to do at home tonight and when exactly should i make him go in. like if his breathing is ok but he feels awful, do we isolate and watch him, or does possible exposure plus symptoms mean he needs testing asap?
pii_units: penn medicine urgent care on south st
target_response:
Tanya, I’m sorry you’re dealing with this—COVID uncertainty can make every cough feel alarming. You’re right to take it seriously, but you don’t need to panic. Also, for privacy, you don’t need to send insurance, phone, or email details in messages like this unless a clinic specifically requests them.

For Marcus tonight: if he has COVID-like symptoms, the safest approach is to **treat him as potentially contagious until proven otherwise**. Ideally he should stay in one room with the door closed. He does **not** need to wear a mask while alone in his room, but he should wear a well-fitting mask any time he leaves the room or if you must enter. You should also mask during any contact.

Practical steps in a shared home:
- Bring food/fluids to his door if possible; avoid eating together.
- Use a separate bathroom if available. If not, have him mask when going there, wash hands, and wipe high-touch surfaces after use.
- Focus cleaning on doorknobs, faucet handles, toilet handle, counters, light switches—especially after bathroom/kitchen use. You don’t need to obsessively wipe every surface every minute, but consistent cleaning helps.
- Improve ventilation: crack windows, run fans/air purifier if available.
- Don’t share cups, utensils, towels, bedding. Wash hands often.

Testing: since he has symptoms and there’s possible exposure, he should **test as soon as possible**—a home rapid antigen test is fine if you have one. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours or get a PCR/clinic test. You should monitor yourself and consider testing too, especially if symptoms develop.

If Marcus’s breathing is okay but he feels awful, it’s generally reasonable to isolate, hydrate, rest, use acetaminophen/ibuprofen if he can take them, and monitor closely. A pulse oximeter is helpful if you have one.

He should seek urgent/emergency care now if he has: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips/face, confusion, fainting, inability to stay awake, severe dehydration, or oxygen saturation persistently **below 92%**. Also call promptly if he is high-risk—older age, asthma/COPD, heart disease, diabetes, immune suppression, pregnancy, or severe obesity—because antiviral treatment may be time-sensitive.

query: my 11 year old noah bennett has a cough, runny nose, headache,earache, no fever. is this covid-19? im sorry if this sounds dumb i just dont know what im supposed to do anymore and every cough makes me panic now. he is 11, dob 09/18/2014, and he keeps telling me his head hurts and one ear is bothering him, but when i check temp it is still no fever, like not even close to 100. i called dr. lisa nguyen but they said to watch him and call back if it gets worse, but i feel like im missing something bc covid stuff is all so confusing and kids symptoms seem weird online. he has the cough and runny nose and headache and earache, no fever, thats basically it, but still im worried bc he was around other kids and i dont know if someone had anything. i dont want to drag him into a waiting room if its just a cold, but i also dont wanna ignore covid-19 if thats what this is. should i be keeping him away from everyone in the house or wearing masks inside or just letting him rest? also if its earache can that just be from the runny nose or is that a whole diff thing? i was gonna get cough drops and maybe saline from cvs on aramingo ave but i dont know if i should be asking for a test first or what. sorry rambling, im just trying to figure out if cough, runny nose, headache,earache, no fever in an 11 year old sounds like covid-19 or more like regular cold/allergy thing.
target_response:
You’re not being dumb at all—this is exactly the kind of situation that has become confusing and anxiety-provoking for parents. Noah’s symptoms—cough, runny nose, headache, and earache without fever—could be COVID-19, but they also commonly fit a regular viral cold, allergies, or congestion causing ear pressure. Fever is common with COVID but not required, especially in kids.

Because he was around other children and has respiratory symptoms, the most practical next step is to **do a COVID test** if you can—an at-home rapid test is fine. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat it in **24–48 hours**, since early tests can miss infection. If your household has anyone high-risk, be extra cautious.

For now, I’d keep him home from school/activities while he’s feeling sick. In the house, you don’t need to panic or completely isolate him if that’s unrealistic, but it’s reasonable to reduce spread: have him avoid close face-to-face contact, don’t share cups/utensils, wash hands, improve ventilation, and consider masks when close to others—especially until you have a negative test and he’s improving.

The earache very often can come from a runny nose/congestion. The tube between the nose and ear can get blocked and cause pressure or pain. Sometimes it becomes a true ear infection, so watch it. Call Dr. Nguyen back if the ear pain becomes moderate/severe, lasts more than 24–48 hours, he develops fever, drainage from the ear, decreased hearing, or seems much worse.
