Bottom line: **don’t just wait it out if breathing is scaring you — call ahead and get evaluated today; ER if any red flags above.**

query: i have a dizzy head and some phlegm. need to check against covid symptoms please? sorry if this is a dumb question but im kinda overthinking it, this is mike arroyo and i dont really know if im supposed to be calling urgent care or just sitting tight at home and drinking water or what. i woke up feeling like my head is a little swimmy, not like the room is flying around but just dizzy head, and then i keep having to clear some phlegm. thats really it, but bc everybody keeps saying covid can start weird i wanted to check against covid symptoms please before i go around ppl or go into work and make anybody nervous. i put a msg in to Penn Family Care earlier but i havent heard back yet and i dont want to clog the phone line if its nothing. my phone is 267-555-0198 if somebody needs to tell me if i should test or not, or email mike.a77@protonmail.com bc sometimes my cell reception in the apt is trash. i have a home test somewhere but i dont know if its old or if i should go buy a newer one at CVS on 19th and market. im also trying not to be dramatic bc i dont have anything else major going on, just the dizzy head and some phlegm, but i keep reading stuff online and then i get more worried. should i compare this to covid symptoms or is this more like allergies/normal junk? if i do test, when is the best time, like now or wait til it gets worse? just trying to do the right thing and not be that person walking around if this could be covid.
target_response:
Hi Mike — not a dumb question at all. You’re doing the right thing by thinking about testing before going around other people.

A “swimmy”/lightheaded feeling plus some phlegm **can** fit with many common things: early viral cold/COVID, post-nasal drip/allergies, mild dehydration, poor sleep, not eating yet, or sinus/ear congestion. Dizziness by itself is not one of the most classic COVID symptoms, but COVID can start with mild or vague symptoms, so it’s reasonable to check.

**What I’d do today:**
1. **Take a home COVID test now** since you have new respiratory-type symptoms and are deciding about work/contacts.  
2. If the test is **positive**, stay home, mask if around others, and contact your clinician—especially if you’re older, immunocompromised, or have conditions that might qualify you for antiviral treatment, which works best within 5 days.  
3. If the test is **negative**, it does not fully rule COVID out early. **Repeat a home test in 48 hours** if symptoms continue, worsen, or before being around higher-risk people.  
4. Check the test’s **expiration date**—many were extended by the FDA, but if it’s clearly expired and you can’t confirm an extension, buying a newer one is reasonable.

Until you know more, I’d avoid close contact if possible, wear a well-fitting mask if you must go out, and don’t go to work if your workplace policy says to stay home with new respiratory symptoms.

For the dizziness: drink fluids, eat something light if you haven’t, get up slowly, and avoid driving or ladders while you feel off. A saline spray, steam, or an antihistamine may help if this feels like post-nasal drip/allergies.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, fainting, severe or spinning vertigo, new weakness/numbness, facial droop, confusion, severe headache, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, high fever, or signs of dehydration.

If symptoms persist beyond 1–2 days, worsen, or you test positive and need treatment advice, follow up with Penn Family Care or urgent care.

query: hi, this is for me, maria elena cruz, dob 09/14/1984, and sorry if im rambling but im kinda scared and dont know if im supposed to just wait this out or go somewhere. if you are tested positive for covid-19, and your fever keeps going up daily (100 to 101 to 102 to 103), what should you do if all other symptoms remain the same? i got the positive result thru riverside family care and it is under mrn rvc-30291, and i already left a msg for dr nina patel but havent heard back yet so im trying not to panic. my number is 215-555-0196 and email is mcruz84@proton.me, i keep checking both bc i dont wanna miss a call. i have been writing the temp down because it keeps creeping up each day and that part is whats making me nervous, like 100 then 101 then 102 and now 103, but besides that everything else feels pretty much the same as it has been since the test, not really better but not suddenly different either. i dont wanna overreact and go to urgent care if theyll just send me home, but also i dont wanna be dumb and sit here while the fever keeps climbing. i live alone so its not like someone is watching me all night, and i keep wondering if 103 means er now or if i should take fever meds and recheck. also should i call the covid line or my regular dr again, and at what point is it too high if it goes up again tomorrow? i know everyone says rest and fluids but when the number keeps going up daily it feels not okay.
target_response:
Hi Maria Elena — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this, especially living alone. A fever that is steadily rising with COVID can feel scary. A temperature of **103°F is not automatically an ER emergency if you are breathing comfortably, thinking clearly, able to drink fluids, and the fever comes down with medication** — but the upward trend means you should get **same-day medical guidance** rather than simply waiting another day.

**What to do now:**
1. **Take a fever reducer** if you can safely use it:  
   - **Acetaminophen/Tylenol 650–1,000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed**; do not exceed **3,000 mg/day** if possible, and avoid if you have significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use.  
   - **Ibuprofen 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food** can also help if you do not have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are not on blood thinners, and are not pregnant.
2. **Recheck your temperature 60–90 minutes after medication** and keep logging it.
3. **Hydrate aggressively**: water, oral rehydration solution, broth, electrolyte drinks. Watch that you are urinating at least every 6–8 hours.
4. If you have or can get a **pulse oximeter**, check oxygen levels at rest and after walking around the room.

**Call Dr. Patel/Riverside Family Care again or their COVID line today** and tell them your fever has climbed to 103°F. Also ask whether you qualify for an antiviral such as **Paxlovid**, especially if you are within the first 5 days of symptoms or have risk factors.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have any of the following: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, oxygen saturation **below 94%** or dropping, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, fever **104°F or higher**, or a 103°F fever that **does not improve after fever medicine**.

Because you live alone, consider texting/calling a trusted person to check in on you tonight. If your fever keeps climbing tomorrow or you feel worse in any way, it is appropriate to be seen.

query:
hi, my name is nina patel, DOB 09/14/1988, sorry if this is a bit messy but im kinda anxious and overthinking this. on the night of 23rd march i started experiencing soar throat and little bit nose block, it was not like full blocked nose but enough that i kept waking up and checking if i could breath normal. i visited gp yesterday at greenwood family practice and saw dr emily rosen, she checked me and was given flue medication, i picked it up from cvs on 5th ave after the appointment. gp advised against corona testing since my temp was normal, she said it looked more like regular cold/flu type thing and to rest, drink warm water, take the meds, all that. but i keep reading online and now every little cough or throat feeling is making me panic. i dont have high fever, my temp was normal at the clinic, but the soar throat is still there and nose block comes and goes, mostly at night. i havent been out much except groceries and the gp visit, but i was around a few people in the waiting area and now im thinking what if i was already having it and didnt know.

should i be concern about the corona virus? like is normal temp enough reason to not test, or can corona still start like this with just throat and nose? im trying not to be dramatic but i live with family and dont want to put anyone at risk. should i isolate in my room for a few days just in case, or only if fever starts? also do i keep taking the flue medication even if this might be viral/corona or should i call back the gp. just need some guidance pls.
target_response:
Hi Nina — I’m sorry you’re feeling so anxious. What you’re describing (sore throat starting March 23, mild/intermittent nasal blockage worse at night, no fever at the GP visit) is very commonly seen with routine viral upper respiratory infections, allergies/post-nasal drip, or irritation from congestion. That said, COVID *can* start with mild symptoms such as sore throat, congestion, runny nose, or cough, and a normal temperature does **not** completely rule it out.

If COVID testing is available to you, it would be reasonable to do a home rapid test now, especially because you live with family and are worried about exposing them. If it’s negative but symptoms continue or worsen, repeat in 24–48 hours (rapid tests can be falsely negative early). If you can’t test, it’s sensible to take precautions as if it could be contagious for a few days.

Practical steps:
- Stay home as much as possible while symptomatic.
- If feasible, sleep separately and use a separate bathroom if available.
- Wear a well-fitting mask around family, improve ventilation, and wash hands often.
- Avoid sharing cups/utensils and clean high-touch surfaces.
- Continue supportive care: fluids, warm tea/honey, salt-water gargles, saline spray, humidifier/steam, and rest.

Regarding the “flu medication” from your GP/CVS: most cold/flu medicines are for symptom relief and can be used for viral illnesses, including COVID, unless you were told otherwise or have conditions like high blood pressure, heart disease, pregnancy, liver disease, or medication interactions. Take it exactly as directed on the label/GP instructions. If you’re unsure what ingredients are in it, call the pharmacy or your GP before combining it with other medicines—especially acetaminophen/paracetamol, which is often included in combination products.

Please seek urgent medical care if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids down, worsening dehydration, or a high/persistent fever. Also call your GP if symptoms are worsening after 3–5 days, sore throat becomes severe with swollen glands/white patches, or you have known exposure to COVID.

You’re not being dramatic—being cautious around family is reasonable. Testing plus short-term masking/isolation is a balanced approach.

query: hi, my name is marissa kline and im sorry if this is not the right place but i dont really know what else to do. i have had a sore throat (glands enlarged in throat) for over a week and i have constant headache. it has been like every day, not just when i wake up, and the throat glands feel swollen and weird and im trying not to panic but its hard because every little thing now makes me think covid. do i need covid-19 test. if yes, how do a get someone to come to my house to test. i really would rather not sit in a waiting room if i might have it or if i dont, and i dont have anyone who can drive me around right now. my dob is 04/18/1987 if that matters for the form stuff, and i live at 312 maple ave, harrisburg pa 17102, its the tan duplex with the blue door so ppl get confused with the house next door all the time. i can be reached at 717-555-0139 but please text first if possible bc my throat hurts and talking makes it worse and my head is pounding. i have upmc health plan #UPM-88310422 but i honestly dont know if they pay for home testing or if i need some kind of referral first. i dont have a fever that i know of, but i also only have the old thermometer and im not sure i trust it. i just need to know if a home covid test person can come out or if there is a number i call, because im nervous about exposing anybody and also nervous about waiting too long.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — I’m sorry you’re feeling so miserable and anxious. A sore throat with swollen “glands” (lymph nodes) and a daily headache for over a week can happen with COVID, but also with other common infections like a non-COVID virus, strep throat, flu, or mono. Given your symptoms, **yes, it is reasonable to test for COVID**, especially before being around others.

A few practical options that avoid a waiting room:

1. **Home rapid antigen test:** If you can, order one for delivery from a pharmacy/grocery delivery service, or ask someone to drop it at your door. If negative and symptoms continue, repeat in 48 hours.
2. **UPMC/PCP telehealth:** Call your primary care office or UPMC member services and ask about **telehealth and testing options**, including any covered home testing or mail-in PCR. They can also decide if you need a strep test.
3. **Pennsylvania/County health resources:** Check the PA Department of Health or Dauphin County resources for local testing sites; some may have drive-through or appointment-based testing, though true in-home testing is often limited.
4. If you must go in, **call ahead**, wear a well-fitting mask, and ask if they can room you quickly or test outside.

Because your sore throat and swollen nodes have lasted **more than a week**, you should also arrange a medical evaluation soon—especially if the COVID test is negative—since **strep throat** or mono may need specific testing and treatment.

Until then: rest, drink fluids, use warm salt-water gargles, throat lozenges, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely. Try to isolate, mask around others, and avoid sharing utensils.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, severe or “worst” headache, stiff neck, trouble swallowing saliva/drooling, dehydration, one-sided throat/neck swelling, rash, or a persistent high fever.

Also, for privacy, avoid sharing your full address, DOB, phone, and insurance number in unsecured messages. I can’t text or arrange a home visit directly here, but the steps above should help you get tested safely.

query: i travelled to mauritius and do not have symptoms. should i get tested for covid19? sorry if this is a dumb question but im getting myself worked up reading stuff online and then my family keeps asking me the same thing over and over. my name is jason miller and the best number for me if someone needs to call is 215-555-0148, but really im just trying to know if i should go get swabbed or if thats just wasting a test when i feel totally fine. i got back from mauritius on emirates ek703 and i was careful as much as i could be, wore a mask in the airport, used sanitizer like crazy, tried not to sit near ppl coughing, all that stuff. i dont have fever, cough, sore throat, breathing problems, body aches, nothing, like literally i feel normal, just anxious. i called penn medicine travel clinic but got stuck on hold and then started second guessing if i even need to call since i do not have symptoms. do i need to quarantine anyway just bc of travel, and for how long, or only if i start getting sick? i live with my dad and he is older so thats the part making me nervous, i dont want to be careless and then find out i should have tested right away. also if i test too early will it be negative and not mean anything? im not trying to panic but everybody keeps saying different things and i cant tell what the actual rule is right now for someone who just travelled to mauritius and feels ok. should i get tested for covid19 or just stay home and watch for symptoms?
target_response:
Hi Jason — this is not a dumb question at all. It’s very understandable to feel anxious, especially since you live with your older dad and you’re getting mixed messages.

If you **feel completely well and have no known close exposure to someone with COVID**, you generally **do not need an immediate COVID swab just because you traveled**. Testing right away after travel can be falsely reassuring: if you were exposed during the airport/flight, the virus may not be detectable for a few days.

A practical plan would be:

- **Monitor for symptoms for 10 days** after travel: fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, fatigue, headache, loss of taste/smell, diarrhea, or shortness of breath.
- Because your dad is older, take extra precautions for the next several days: **wear a well-fitting mask around him, improve ventilation, avoid close prolonged contact, don’t share utensils/cups, and wash hands often**.
- If you want reassurance, it is reasonable to test **around day 3–5 after your return or last possible exposure**. A home antigen test is okay; if negative but you develop symptoms later, test again. If you use antigen tests while asymptomatic, repeating 24–48 hours later can improve confidence.
- If you develop any symptoms, **stay away from your dad, test promptly, and act as potentially contagious** until you know more.

As for quarantine: current recommendations vary by location and change over time, but for someone who is **asymptomatic with no known exposure**, strict quarantine is usually not required solely due to travel. That said, with a higher-risk household member, a few days of “extra cautious” behavior is smart and not overreacting.

Seek urgent care right away if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or persistent high fever**. If your dad develops symptoms, he should test and contact his doctor early, because older adults may benefit from treatment if COVID is confirmed.

Bottom line: you likely don’t need to rush out for a swab today, but testing 3–5 days after travel plus masking/distancing from your dad is a reasonable, careful approach.

query: my son aarav shrestha is 4 years old and i am really very worried now because this coughing problem is not stopping and every time i feel may be this time it will be ok but again it comes back. at the age of six months he suffered from pneumonia.since then he has problem of coughing at the interval of 1 to 2 month. we had shown him first in civil service hospital and his file there is mrn KDH-77419, i keep carrying the same old papers in my bag because every new visit they ask everything again and again. the doctor dr. rohit basnet provided bactoclav and beta2 cough syrup till he became 2 years old. but there is no any improvement so the doctor refered budecort 200mcg/dose 2 puff at a time twice a day n also a nasal spray beclometason dipropionate bp 50 micro gram. but still the case is not improve. the frequency of coughing starts almost every months and it is very hard for us because he is small and when cough starts at night he cannot sleep well and i also sit beside him whole night thinking if it is again pneumonia or asthma or allergy or some weak lungs problem from when he was baby. sometime it is ok for few weeks and we feel happy then suddenly again cough cough for days and i get scared to send him out or even let him play too much. i am not understanding if inhaler is safe for long time or dose is wrong or we need some other test. please suggest me what could i do now? should i go to chest specialist or child lung doctor or allergy test, because i am really confused and tired now.
target_response:
I’m sorry you and Aarav are going through this. Recurrent night cough every 1–2 months since pneumonia can be very stressful, and it’s reasonable to ask for a more complete review rather than repeating the same medicines.

In a 4-year-old, the common causes include **preschool asthma/viral-triggered wheeze**, **allergic rhinitis/post-nasal drip**, recurrent viral infections from school/exposure, and sometimes reflux. Less commonly, if cough is wet/productive, persistent, associated with poor growth, repeated pneumonia, or abnormal chest X-rays, doctors consider conditions like **bronchiectasis, TB, aspiration, or immune problems**.

**Budecort is an inhaled steroid** and is generally safe when used correctly, but Aarav’s dose—200 mcg, 2 puffs twice daily—should be reviewed by a pediatric lung specialist, especially if it has not clearly helped. Many “failures” happen because of wrong inhaler technique or no spacer/mask. He should use a **spacer with mask/mouthpiece**, breathe normally for several breaths after each puff, and rinse/clean mouth afterward. Long-term use should include monitoring height/weight and using the **lowest effective dose**.

What I suggest now:
1. **Book an appointment with a pediatric pulmonologist/child chest specialist** and carry all previous papers, prescriptions, X-rays, and a cough diary.
2. Ask for a structured review: inhaler technique check, growth check, oxygen saturation, chest examination, and whether he needs a **chest X-ray**, blood count/eosinophils, allergy testing, TB evaluation if relevant, and spirometry if he can perform it.
3. Clarify an **asthma action plan**: what controller to use daily, what rescue medicine to use during attacks, and when to seek help.
4. Avoid repeated antibiotics or cough syrups unless there is clear evidence of bacterial infection.

Please seek urgent care if he has fast or difficult breathing, chest indrawing, bluish lips, persistent high fever, extreme sleepiness, dehydration, oxygen low if measured, coughing blood, or cough with weight loss/night sweats.

You’re not overreacting—because this is recurring and affecting sleep, a pediatric lung specialist review is the right next step.

query:
hi, my name is jenny morales and i know this is prob a dumb question but im spiraling a little. i know hand washing (20+ seconds) is best to kill coronavirus, and that if not available use sanitizer of 60%+ alcohol. i thought my sanitizer was but realized it's only 57.6%. been using lots for weeks. like literally in my purse, in the car, after touching carts, after pumping gas, after mail, after being at work at BrightPath Daycare, everything. i bought it at CVS on 5th st and just assumed it was the right kind bc it says hand sanitizer big on the front and smelled strong, but then last night i actually read the tiny label and saw 57.6% and now im mad at myself and nervous.

ive stopped using it now and im back to soap/water whenever i can, but im worried bc for weeks i used this stuff like it was protecting me. i wasnt using it instead of washing every single time, but def when i was out and couldnt wash. i dont have symptoms right now besides normal anxiety stuff lol, no fever or cough, but i keep thinking did i basically do nothing all these times? should i be worried that i exposed myself more bc i trusted the bottle? does 57.6% do anything at all or is it basically useless for coronavirus?

also i have kids and im trying not to overreact and toss every bag and coat in the house. if someone can reply or call/text me at 267-555-0198 that would help, or email is jenny.morales82@gmail.com. sorry this is long, just feeling dumb and nervous.
target_response:
Hi Jenny — this is absolutely not a dumb question, and you’re not alone. A lot of sanitizer labels are confusing, and it’s understandable that seeing “57.6%” after weeks of use would make you feel unsettled.

The reassuring part: **57.6% alcohol is not “basically doing nothing.”** It’s just **below the CDC/FDA recommended cutoff** for hand sanitizer effectiveness against coronavirus, which is **at least 60% ethanol/ethyl alcohol or 70% isopropyl alcohol**. That recommendation gives a safety margin because real-life use varies — people may not use enough, may wipe it off too soon, or hands may be dirty/greasy. A 57.6% product may still reduce germs somewhat, but it’s **less reliable**, so switching away from it is the right move.

Also, try not to replay every cart, gas pump, or mail interaction in your head. COVID spreads **mainly through respiratory droplets/aerosols from being near infected people**, especially indoors, rather than from objects. Hand hygiene matters, but using a slightly under-strength sanitizer for a few weeks does **not** mean you were highly exposed or that you “failed.” Since you have **no fever, cough, or other symptoms**, there’s no specific action needed beyond your usual precautions.

What to do now:
- Use **soap and water for 20+ seconds** whenever available.
- When out, use sanitizer labeled **≥60% alcohol** and rub until fully dry.
- Avoid touching your face before cleaning hands.
- At BrightPath Daycare, focus on masking/ventilation if applicable, staying home when sick, and cleaning shared high-touch areas.
- No need to throw away bags, coats, or mail. Routine laundering/cleaning is enough.

Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, dehydration, or severe/worsening symptoms**. If you develop typical COVID symptoms, consider testing and follow your workplace/daycare policy.

And please don’t beat yourself up — you noticed the issue, corrected it, and you’ve been trying hard to protect yourself and your kids. That’s responsible, not dumb.

target_response:
Hi Maya — I’m sorry you’ve been stuck waiting, especially when your workplace is asking for documentation and you’re trying to do the responsible thing for your family and coworkers. I can understand why this feels frustrating.

I’m not able to directly access your chart or confirm whether Dr. Nair has seen the original message from here, and I also can’t issue a referral letter through this chat. **What I’d suggest you do now:**

query:
hi, my name is melissa carter and im sorry this is prob gonna be long but im kinda scared and trying not to panic. about 6 weeks ago i was diagnosed with pe in right lung and phnemoia in the left lung. i was in teh hospital for 9 days at hartford hospital and it was a mess and honestly im still not over it, like every weird pain now makes me think the clot is back or moving or something. they have me under dr. nina patel, and my paperwork has MRN HMC-771904 on it if that matters, but i wasnt sure who to call after hours without sounding dumb. i am on 20mg of warfin per day! i pick it up at cvs on maple ave and i take it exactly like they told me, even tho it makes me nervous taking that much. my blood levels are holding steady at 2.67, thats what they said last time, so i thought i was doing okay and trying to just rest and get back to normal stuff slowly.

yesterday i had a bit of lower back pain and today it is between my shoulder blades, mostly on my right side. it hurts my back to breathe, swallow, and move. like if i take a deeper breath it catches and if i turn wrong i feel it, so then i start thinking is this just sore muscles from sleeping weird or coughing or being tense, or could it be something serious with the blood clot? i dont have a good sense anymore of what is normal healing and what is danger. i hate to rush into the doctor if it is nothing....i really really do, esp after being stuck in the hospital so long and getting all those bills, but i also dont want to ignore something stupidly. i hope you can help! thanks
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m really sorry you’re going through this. After a PE and pneumonia hospitalization, it is completely understandable that new chest/back pain would feel scary. You are not “sounding dumb” by calling; this is exactly the kind of symptom your care team wants to know about.

Pain between the shoulder blades that **hurts with deep breathing, movement, or swallowing** can be from several things: muscle strain from coughing/tension/sleeping position, inflammation around the lung after pneumonia/PE (pleurisy), irritation from the esophagus, or less commonly a new/worsening clot or another lung issue. Because you had a PE only 6 weeks ago, this needs to be treated more cautiously than ordinary back pain.

Your INR of **2.67** is in the usual therapeutic range for PE treatment, which is reassuring and makes a new clot less likely — but it does **not** make it impossible. Also, warfarin can increase bleeding risk, and new significant pain sometimes needs evaluation from that standpoint too. A daily dose of **20 mg warfarin is on the higher side**, but some people do require higher doses; the key is that your INR is monitored and stable.

**What I recommend:** call Dr. Nina Patel’s office/after-hours line today and tell them: “Recent PE 6 weeks ago, on warfarin with INR 2.67, now having right-sided pain between shoulder blades that hurts with breathing/swallowing/movement.” They can decide whether you need the ER, urgent imaging, INR recheck, or close follow-up.

Please go to the **ER now / call 911** if you have any of the following: shortness of breath that is new or worse, chest pressure, coughing blood, fainting, rapid heart rate, new dizziness/weakness, blue lips, fever/chills, severe or worsening pain, one-sided leg swelling/pain, black/bloody stools, vomiting blood, severe headache, or any fall/head injury while on warfarin.

Until you speak with someone: avoid NSAIDs like ibuprofen/naproxen unless your doctor okays them; acetaminophen is usually safer but should be limited and consistent with warfarin guidance.

Also, for privacy, avoid sharing your MRN in general online messages unless it’s through your hospital’s secure portal.

query: i have a aunt linda sue parker that is in the hospital at st vincents blount in alabama and im just trying to understand what all this means bc nobody in my family explains stuff good and i get scared reading things online. she may or not have pneumonia, they said something bout checking her lungs and watching her, but then another nurse said they wasnt sure yet so idk. she hasnt never eat right all she lives on is coffee and junk food and toaster strudel, like for years thats just how she is, she will drink coffee all day and maybe eat a little sweet thing and then say shes full. i keep telling her u cant live on that but she dont listen to nobody, not me not my mom not anybody. i called up there from my phone 205-555-0198 and they said dr. rachel kline was on the floor or seeing her or whatever but i didnt get to talk much cause they said privacy stuff, and my cousin is supposed to call me back but she never answers neither. what causes naited however u spell that, like is it from pneumonia maybe or from not eating right for so long or the meds they giving her? she tried to eat something but she got nasated and said the smell made her feel sick too. im worried bc she already dont eat much and now if shes in the hospital and cant even try to eat then how is she gonna get strength back. she is hard headed and says she just wants coffee but i dont think that helps. i just need to know if being nasated is normal when u maybe got pneumonia or if its a bad sign and we need to push them to check something else.
target_response:
I’m sorry you’re dealing with this uncertainty — it’s scary when you hear “maybe pneumonia” and can’t get clear answers. “Naited” sounds like **nauseated**, meaning she feels sick to her stomach or like she may vomit.

Yes, **nausea can happen with pneumonia or other infections**, especially in older or medically frail people. It can also come from several other common hospital-related issues: **antibiotics**, pain medicines, dehydration, low sodium/potassium, constipation, acid reflux, not eating for long periods, or even the smell/taste of hospital food. So nausea by itself does **not automatically mean something terrible**, but it’s important because your aunt already eats very little and needs nutrition to recover.

Her long-term pattern of mostly coffee, sweets, and junk food could absolutely make recovery harder. She may be low on protein, vitamins, minerals, and overall calories. Coffee can also worsen nausea, suppress appetite, irritate the stomach, and contribute to dehydration if she’s not drinking enough water.

What I’d suggest: ask the family member who is allowed to get information — or ask your aunt to give permission for you to speak with the team — to request a simple update from the nurse or doctor. Good questions are:

- “Does she have pneumonia, or are you still ruling it out?”
- “What did the chest X-ray/CT and oxygen levels show?”
- “Is she getting antibiotics?”
- “What do you think is causing the nausea?”
- “Can she have nausea medicine before meals?”
- “Can a dietitian see her?”
- “Can she get protein shakes or small frequent meals?”

Hospitals can help with this: **IV fluids, electrolyte correction, anti-nausea medicine, nutrition consults, supplements like Ensure/Boost, and monitoring intake**.

Urgent concerns to alert staff about right away include: trouble breathing, blue lips, confusion or extreme sleepiness, chest pain, oxygen dropping, high fever, repeated vomiting, severe belly pain, vomiting blood, black stools, or not peeing much.

You’re right to be concerned, but nausea is common and treatable. The key is making sure the team knows she’s barely eating and that nausea is blocking her recovery.

query:
hello. i’d like some help please, i’ve started getting these symptoms and am wondering if i should see a doctor or stay at home and try to get better. i’m emily parker, dob 09/14/1990 if that matters, and i usually see dr. lena morris but i cant get anyone on the phone there right now and the portal is being annoying so im trying here first before i panic and go sit in urgent care forever.

- headache
- weird yellowish growth on the side of my throat
- tight chest
- mild cough ?

like the headache isnt the worst headache ever but its been nagging and making me nervous, and the throat thing is what is really freaking me out bc i looked with my phone flashlight and there is this weird yellowish growth on the side of my throat, not just normal red sore throat looking stuff. i dont know if its tonsil related or an infection or something gross stuck there, sorry. my chest feels tight too, not like crushing pain or anything, just tight and weird, and i have a mild cough that keeps making me think maybe i shouldnt ignore it. i dont have a thermometer that works right now because mine died and i was gonna grab one at walgreens on n 7th st if i need to, but im trying not to run around if this is something contagious.

should i be seen today or is this a drink fluids/rest/keep an eye on it thing? im not trying to be dramatic but throat growth + tight chest together sounds bad in my head. also if i do go in should it be urgent care or regular doctor? i just dont wanna waste time if this is one of those things that needs meds or someone to actually look at my throat.
target_response:
Hi Emily — you’re not being dramatic. A new yellow/whitish area on one side of the throat plus chest tightness and cough is worth having someone look at in person, especially since you can’t check your temperature right now.

The throat finding could be something relatively minor like a tonsil stone or trapped debris, but it could also be tonsillar exudate from strep/tonsillitis, a viral infection, mono, an ulcer, or — less commonly but more urgently — an early abscess near the tonsil. The chest tightness with cough could be from a viral respiratory infection, bronchitis, asthma/reactive airways, anxiety from feeling unwell, COVID/flu, etc. An exam and possibly a strep/COVID/flu test would help decide whether you need antibiotics or other treatment.

**My recommendation:** if your regular doctor can see you today, that’s ideal. If not, **urgent care today is reasonable**, particularly because of the one-sided throat “growth” and chest tightness. Wear a mask, and if you can, pick up a working thermometer and/or do a home COVID test before going, but don’t delay care if symptoms are concerning.

**Go to the ER or call emergency services now** if you have any of the following: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure that is severe or spreading to arm/jaw/back, blue lips, fainting, confusion, inability to swallow saliva/drooling, rapidly worsening throat swelling, muffled “hot potato” voice, severe one-sided throat pain with jaw stiffness, neck swelling, high fever with feeling very ill, or the “worst headache of your life,” new neurologic symptoms, stiff neck, or rash.

While waiting to be seen: drink fluids, rest, use acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely, gargle warm salt water, avoid poking/scraping the area, and avoid close contact with others until you know if it’s contagious.

Bottom line: this may still be a routine infection or tonsil stone, but because it’s new, visible, and paired with chest tightness, I’d choose **same-day evaluation** rather than just watching it at home.

query: hi, im matthew kline, dob 03/14/1989, and im prob overthinking but i wanted to ask bc i keep googling and then getting myself worked up. i had a fever from friday to yesterday (sunday), along with a cough. the temperature is now gone, but my cough is still here. i have been clearing my throat for a couple of weeks, like kind of that annoying little throat clearing thing where it feels like something is sitting there, but the coughing part started i believe on saturday. today, the cough is breaking, and other then the cough i feel 100% fine. like i got up, showered, ate normal, not laying around sick anymore, but then i cough and immediately think ok is this pneumonia or something worse. do i have pneumonia? i was gonna maybe go to Penn Medicine urgent care on 3737 Market St but i dont wanna be dramatic if its just the tail end of something, plus the waiting rooms make me nervous now. my phone is 215-555-7420 if they need it and my email is mkline89@gmail.com, and insurance is Blue Cross PPO #BCP-774219, but honestly i would rather not use it unless i should. i keep checking myself every hour which is probably dumb, and the fever being gone makes me think im ok, but the cough hanging around is what is messing with my head. i dont have that sick feeling anymore, just coughing and some throat clearing, and it is more loose/breaking up today. should i just watch it a couple days, drink stuff, rest, or does pneumonia still happen even when the temp is gone and i otherwise feel fine? sorry this is rambling, just trying to figure out if i need to be seen today or not.
pii_units: Penn Medicine urgent care on 3737 Market St
target_response:
Hi Matthew — you’re not being dramatic. It’s very common for a cough to linger after a viral respiratory infection, and the pattern you describe is reassuring: fever for a couple of days, now resolved, energy/appetite back to normal, and the cough becoming looser/“breaking up.” That often means your airways are clearing mucus and calming down.

Pneumonia is **less likely** when the fever is gone and you otherwise feel well, especially if you’re not short of breath and don’t have chest pain. That said, pneumonia can occasionally present without a fever, so the decision to be seen depends more on how you’re breathing and how you’re trending over the next 24–48 hours.

For now, if you feel 100% aside from cough, it would be reasonable to **watch it for a couple of days** and focus on supportive care:

- Drink fluids; warm tea/honey can help calm coughing  
- Use a humidifier or steamy shower  
- Consider saline nasal spray/rinse if postnasal drip is contributing  
- If you have allergies, an antihistamine or nasal steroid may help the throat-clearing sensation  
- Avoid smoke/vaping and intense exercise until the cough settles  
- Consider a COVID test if you haven’t tested, especially before being around others

The throat-clearing for a couple weeks could be from **postnasal drip, allergies, reflux, or irritation**—not necessarily something dangerous.

Please seek urgent care today or sooner if you develop any red flags: **shortness of breath, chest pain, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, fever returning or persisting, coughing up blood, severe weakness/confusion, blue lips, dehydration, or worsening instead of improving.** Also get checked if the cough is not clearly improving over 1–2 weeks, or lasts more than 3–4 weeks.

If your anxiety is driving hourly checking, try limiting check-ins to twice daily unless symptoms change. Based on what you wrote, it sounds reasonable not to rush to urgent care today unless any red flags appear or you start feeling worse again.

query: hello, i have had a chest infection about once a quarter for the last year and a half . i was diagnosed with asthma not long ago as well as having developed pneumonia for the first time in the past week. what could be causing such frequent illness? i have generally been a healthy person for all of my life. sick maybe once every four years. my name is megan riley and i feel weird even asking bc i never used to be the person who was always at the dr or always needing meds, and now it feels like every few months i get knocked down again with the same chest junk and coughing and tightness and then i get anxious waiting for it to turn into something worse. dr. nora patel at elm street family clinic said asthma can make infections feel worse or hang on longer, but it still seems like a lot to me and now with pneumonia this week i keep thinking ok what am i missing. i pick up my inhaler and antibiotics at CVS on maple ave and i take them like im told, but then a few months later it feels like it starts all over again. i dont smoke, i try to keep things pretty normal, and like i said i was barely ever sick before all this, maybe once every four years, so this change is freaking me out. could allergies or mold or some immune issue cause chest infection about once a quarter? should i be asking for blood work or a lung specialist or something, or is this just how asthma can be when its newly diagnosed? i just dont want to keep being told its another infection and then find out later there was a bigger reason.
target_response:
Hi Megan — I’m really glad you asked. A sudden shift from “rarely sick” to chest illnesses every few months is understandably unsettling, and it’s reasonable to look for a pattern rather than just treating each episode in isolation.

Asthma *can* make viral infections feel much worse and last longer: the airways become inflamed and twitchy, causing cough, tightness, wheeze, and mucus even after the infection is gone. If asthma is not fully controlled, it can also mimic “recurrent bronchitis.” That said, having pneumonia this week plus repeated chest episodes is enough reason to reassess.

Common possibilities to discuss with Dr. Patel include:

- **Asthma control issues** — needing a daily controller inhaler, adjusting dose, checking inhaler technique, or creating an asthma action plan.  
- **Allergies/post-nasal drip or chronic sinus irritation** — can trigger cough and asthma flares.  
- **Environmental exposure** — mold, dampness, dust mites, pets, workplace irritants, smoke exposure, or recent home/work changes.  
- **Reflux/GERD** — can irritate airways and worsen nighttime cough.  
- **Incomplete recovery or resistant bacteria** — especially with pneumonia; sometimes follow-up imaging or sputum testing is needed.  
- Less commonly, **immune system issues** or structural lung problems such as bronchiectasis, especially if infections are truly bacterial and recurrent.

I’d suggest asking for a follow-up appointment after this pneumonia treatment to review the bigger picture. Reasonable next steps may include: **spirometry/pulmonary function testing**, review of your inhalers and technique, possibly **allergy testing**, and basic labs such as **CBC with differential** and **immunoglobulin levels**. If pneumonia recurs, occurs in the same area, or your chest X-ray doesn’t clear, a **pulmonology referral** and possibly a **CT scan** may be appropriate.

Seek urgent care now if you have worsening shortness of breath, blue lips, chest pain, confusion, coughing blood, oxygen levels under 92% if you monitor them, persistent high fever, or you can’t keep fluids/meds down.

Bottom line: this may be newly recognized asthma plus triggers, but your concern is valid. You’re not overreacting by asking for a more complete evaluation.

query: hi this is maria lopez and i know this is prob a dumb question but im kinda spiraling today, will lysol wipes kill coronavirus? how long can coronavirus live on household surfaces? i keep seeing diff things online and now im wiping the same counter like 10 times and still feel like its dirty. we are at 312 w elm st apt 4b and its a small place so if one person coughs i swear it feels like its on every doorknob, light switch, fridge handle, remote, bathroom sink, all of it. i bought lysol wipes from CVS on broad st but the tub says kills viruses or whatever and i cant tell if that means this coronavirus too or only older stuff, and do i have to leave it wet for a certain number of minutes or just wipe and go? bc honestly i wipe it and it dries fast and then i worry i didnt do it right. my husband thinks im over doing it but he also drops keys and mail on the table right after being out, and the kids touch literally everything then touch their faces, so im like ok great. if someone brings in groceries or a package do i need to wipe every box? what about the couch or clothes or shoes by the door? i dont want to poison us with too many chemicals either, like i have the window cracked but its cold and im getting a headache from the smell. if someone can explain in plain words how long coronavirus can live on household surfaces and whether lysol wipes really kill it that would help. if you need to call me its 267-555-0198, but im mostly just trying to know what is actually necessary and what is me panicking.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. There has been a lot of confusing information online, and it makes sense that you’d feel anxious in a small apartment with kids touching everything.

**Yes: Lysol disinfecting wipes can kill the coronavirus that causes COVID-19** *if* the product is used according to the label. Look for wording like “kills SARS-CoV-2” or an **EPA registration number** / EPA “List N” disinfectant. Most Lysol disinfecting wipes are approved, but the key is **contact time**: the surface usually needs to stay visibly wet for several minutes, often around **4 minutes** depending on the exact product. If it dries immediately, use another wipe so the surface stays wet for the full label time. After that, let it air dry.

That said, you do **not** need to wipe the same counter 10 times. One proper disinfection is enough.

In lab studies, coronavirus can survive for **hours to a few days** on surfaces — roughly up to 24 hours on cardboard and 2–3 days on plastic/stainless steel — but the amount of live virus drops a lot over time. In real life, COVID spreads mainly through **airborne/respiratory droplets**, not from touching every object.

A reasonable plan:
- Clean/disinfect **high-touch surfaces once daily**: doorknobs, light switches, fridge handle, faucet handles, remote, counters.
- Do it more often if someone is sick or coughing a lot.
- For groceries/packages: you do **not** need to wipe every box. Put items away, discard outer packaging if you want, then **wash hands**.
- Clothes, couch, shoes: routine washing/cleaning is fine. Shoes by the door is reasonable; no need to disinfect them.
- Encourage everyone to wash hands when coming home and before eating; keep keys/mail in one spot.

Also important: don’t overuse chemicals. If you’re getting a headache from the smell, that’s a sign to back off, ventilate, and never mix cleaners.

Seek urgent care for COVID symptoms like trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, severe dehydration, or worsening high fever. And if the cleaning worry feels uncontrollable, please tell us — anxiety spirals are treatable, and you don’t have to manage that alone.

query: hi im michael van der merwe, dob 08/09/1986, sorry this is prob messy but im a bit stressed and dont know if im over reacting or not. was in cape town 2020/03/12 - 2020/03/15 close proximity to sevral overseas tourists, like proper close at the hotel breakfast and in the lift and on a day tour, and i didnt think much then but now im replaying every single cough i heard. i flew back on klm flight kl598 and was around ppl from germany/uk/netherlands i think, nobody was really distancing then. 2020/03/20 throat sore and voice gone back (upper middle area) and shoulders hurt shivering, dry cough, headache and lethargic should i go for covid19 testing? i phoned the clinic before but the lady just said stay home unless breathing gets bad, but i dont wanna be the idiot who spreads it if this is actually it. i also have to tell my work and my flatmate is now panicking bc we share a tiny kitchen and bathroom and i touched everything before thinking. my usual gp is dr priya naidoo and my old file there is dis-27491-a but i havent been in ages and dont know if they even have tests or if i need a referral. my throat isnt like normal flu sore exactly, more scratchy and voice just gone, and the shivering at night is freaking me out. no idea if the back upper middle area pain and shoulders hurt is from coughing or from sleeping weird. i dont want to clog up emergency if they scarce on tests, but also dont want to wait too long. can someone pls say if with this travel and tourists contact plus dry cough/headache/lethargic i should go in for covid19 testing or just isolate and watch it?
target_response:
Hi Michael — you’re not overreacting. Given your recent travel/close proximity to international tourists and then developing symptoms on 20/03 — sore/scratchy throat with loss of voice, shivering, dry cough, headache and lethargy — COVID-19 is definitely one of the possibilities. Other viral infections can cause the same picture, and the shoulder/upper back pain could be muscle aches from a viral illness, coughing, or sleeping tensely, but your history is enough that you should **treat this as possible COVID until proven otherwise**.

**What to do now:**
1. **Do not go to the clinic or ER without phoning first.** Call Dr Priya Naidoo’s rooms or the local COVID/Department of Health/NICD hotline and explain: recent Cape Town travel, close contact with overseas tourists, flight exposure, and current dry cough/shivers/headache/lethargy. Ask specifically whether you meet testing criteria and where to be tested safely.
2. **Self-isolate immediately** while awaiting advice/testing. Stay in your room if possible, use a mask when leaving the room, keep distance from your flatmate, don’t share towels/cups/utensils, clean bathroom/kitchen surfaces after use, and wash hands often.
3. **Tell work now** that you have compatible symptoms after travel and are self-isolating pending medical advice/testing. That’s the responsible thing to do.
4. Your flatmate should monitor for symptoms and minimise outside contact. If they develop cough, fever/chills, sore throat, loss of smell/taste, or shortness of breath, they should also call for guidance.

For mild symptoms, home care is usually appropriate: rest, fluids, paracetamol for fever/aches, and avoid smoking. Track your temperature if you can.

**Seek urgent medical help immediately** if you develop shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness, or a fever that is very high or not improving. Also seek earlier help if you have asthma, chronic lung/heart disease, diabetes, immune suppression, or if symptoms rapidly worsen.

So: **yes, you should call for COVID testing/assessment**, but **don’t present in person without being directed**. In the meantime, isolate as if positive.

query: my son mason elliott parker has been diagnosed with pneumonia, and started a five day treatment today. he is 11 years old and very lean almost under weight, always been a skinny kid even when he eats good, so this is making me more worried than maybe i should be. he hasn t been eating well this week and coughing is pristant as of today, like every time he gets up or talks too much he starts again and then says hes ok bc he doesnt want to miss hockey. we saw dr. anika shah at northside pediatrics and they have him in their system as MRN NP-39284, they sent the script over to CVS on 5th ave and i picked it up after work, using Blue Cross PPO #BCP-7742190, so he did start it today like they said. i know he wants to go to practice and be on the ice because tryouts and lines are all a big deal to him and he keeps saying he can just take it easy, but honestly he does not take it easy, he does play hard, he skates hard and gets all red faced and acts like he is fine until he is not. i believe he should not go on the ice, at least not right now, but his dad thinks maybe if no fever and he feels up to it then let him sit on bench or skate light. i just dont want to make the pneumonia worse or have him coughing all over the place either. he is already tired, not eating much, and he is so lean almost under weight that a few days of bad eating shows on him fast. can i get your opinion? like would you keep an 11 yr old off the ice during the five day treatment even if he begs to go?
target_response:
I can understand why you’re worried — pneumonia plus poor appetite in a very lean 11-year-old can be stressful, and hockey is a very high-intensity sport even when a kid promises to “take it easy.”

**My opinion: I would keep Mason off the ice right now, including during the first several days of treatment, and likely for the full 5-day course unless his pediatrician specifically clears him sooner.** Pneumonia means the lungs are inflamed/infected and need rest. Skating hard raises breathing demand significantly, and if he’s already coughing with talking or getting up, that’s a sign his lungs are not ready for exertion. Kids often compensate well until they suddenly don’t, especially competitive kids who don’t want to be pulled.

A reasonable return-to-play plan would be:

- **No practice/games while he has fever, significant fatigue, poor intake, chest pain, shortness of breath, or coughing fits with mild activity.**
- He should be **fever-free for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine**, breathing comfortably, drinking well, and have improving energy.
- After **48–72 hours on antibiotics**, many kids start improving, but that does not automatically mean they’re ready for hockey.
- When he does return, it should be **gradual**: light activity first, stop if coughing worsens, he gets winded, dizzy, chest tightness, or unusually tired.

The cough can persist for **1–3 weeks** after pneumonia, but it should trend better. If he is coughing every time he talks or moves today, I would not put him in a cold rink environment where deep breathing and cold air can trigger more coughing/bronchospasm.

For food: focus less on full meals and more on **fluids + calories** — smoothies, chocolate milk, yogurt, soup, nut butter, eggs, pasta, Pedialyte/fluids if needed. Hydration matters most early on.

**Seek urgent care today/tonight** if he has trouble breathing, ribs pulling in, blue lips, persistent chest pain, confusion, dehydration/very little urine, fever not improving after 48–72 hours of antibiotics, or he seems significantly worse.

I’d support you setting a firm boundary: “Your lungs need recovery now so you can get back stronger.” A quick call to his pediatrician for a written sports restriction note may also help with coaches/dad.

query: hi i’m jessica marin and i’m prob overthinking but i can’t stop googling and now i’m scared. i have a dry burning sensation in my throat and no fever (temp is 35.6), i’m 24 weeks pregnant and worried that it may be covid19, do i need to have a fever with covid19? will my baby be okay? it started more like scratchy/dry then kinda burning when i swallow but not like strep pain, just annoying and scary bc every little thing feels huge right now. i already left a msg for dr. priya natarajan at main line women’s health but the nurse line is busy and they said they might not call back till later, and my next regular appt is 12/08 at 9:30am so idk if i should wait or not. i don’t feel hot, no fever at all, and i keep checking bc i’m nervous, but then i read some ppl don’t get fever and that made me panic again. i’m trying to drink water and tea and not freak out but i keep thinking what if i have covid and i’m hurting the baby somehow or what if low temp means something too. i have not taken anything except my prenatal, and i was thinking of calling cvs on 19th and chestnut to ask what’s safe but i don’t even know what to ask for since it’s just dry burning throat. my insurance is aetna ob plan #AET-774201-Q if that matters for testing, but i mostly just need to know if fever is required with covid19 and if baby is usually ok if mom has mild symptoms. sorry this is long i’m just really anxious and pregnant brain is making it worse.
target_response:
Hi Jessica — I’m glad you reached out. It’s very understandable to feel anxious at 24 weeks pregnant, especially after Googling symptoms.

A fever is **not required** to have COVID-19. Some people have COVID with only a scratchy throat, congestion, fatigue, or even no symptoms. That said, a dry/burning throat can also come from many non-COVID causes: dry air, post-nasal drip/allergies, reflux/heartburn, mild viral irritation, dehydration, or talking a lot. The way you describe it — scratchy/dry and burning when swallowing, without “strep-like” severe pain or fever — sounds more like irritation or a mild viral/reflux/allergy-type symptom than something dangerous.

Most importantly: if this is COVID and your symptoms stay mild, **babies are usually okay**. The bigger concerns in pregnancy are significant maternal illness — high fever, dehydration, low oxygen, or trouble breathing — not a mild sore throat by itself.

Your temperature of **35.6°C** is a bit low, but if you feel otherwise okay, it’s often due to thermometer variation, checking after drinking something cool, being in a cool room, or technique. Recheck once after resting 10–15 minutes, using the same method consistently. A low reading alone is not usually concerning if you’re alert, warm, and feeling stable.

What I’d do next:
- Take a **home COVID test** now or within the next day; if negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours.
- Until you know, mask around others and avoid close contact if possible.
- Continue fluids, warm tea with honey, humidifier/steam, throat lozenges, and salt-water gargles.
- If you need medicine, **acetaminophen/Tylenol** is generally considered safe in pregnancy for pain/fever. Avoid ibuprofen/NSAIDs unless Dr. Natarajan specifically says otherwise.
- If reflux is possible, ask your OB/nurse about pregnancy-safe options like antacids.

Please seek urgent care or labor & delivery guidance now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, persistent fever, inability to keep fluids down, confusion/fainting, blue lips, severe throat swelling, or decreased fetal movement when you’re at a stage where you reliably track it.

Waiting for Dr. Natarajan’s nurse call later is reasonable if symptoms remain mild, but testing today may help ease your mind.

query: my mom helen marie dawson has inclusion body myositis and is 81 years old - she has had the disease for approximately 20 years+ . . .she has been experiencing bouts of pneumonia and congestive heart failure - i am wondering if she will deal with this to the end and if you can tell me what the end will look like? i know thats a terrible thing to ask and i feel awful even typing it but i feel like nobody is really saying it straight to me. she has been so strong for so long and we just keep going from one scare to another, she gets better a little, then another pneumonia thing, then the breathing, then the swelling and the heart failure stuff, and then we are all back to waiting and watching her struggle. dr. priya nandan has been kind but i still feel like i dont understand what is actually happening vs what we all hope is happening. she was recently at mercy general hospital again and they talk about treating the infection and getting fluid off and all that, but i keep thinking ok but is this the pattern now, like will it just keep cycling until her body cant anymore? she cant do much for herself anymore and eating is hard sometimes and coughing takes everything out of her. i am not trying to give up on her, i swear, i just want to be prepared and not miss what she needs, like hospice or comfort care or whatever. my family gets upset when i say end stage but i need someone to explain if inclusion body myositis plus pneumonia plus congestive heart failure usually ends slowly, or sudden, or with not being able to breathe. i just want to know what signs to watch for and what questions to ask before we are in panic mode again.
target_response:
I’m really sorry your family is going through this with Helen. And it is **not** a terrible thing to ask. Wanting honest information is not “giving up”—it is how you protect her from panic-driven decisions and make sure her care matches what she would want.

With **inclusion body myositis for 20+ years**, severe weakness, trouble eating/swallowing, recurrent pneumonia, and congestive heart failure, what you are describing can absolutely become a pattern: infection or aspiration → hospitalization/antibiotics → fluid overload or heart strain → diuretics/oxygen → partial recovery, but often not back to the prior baseline. Over time, the body has less reserve, so each episode can take more out of her.

The “end” in this situation is often a **slow decline punctuated by sudden crises**. It may look like worsening shortness of breath, more frequent pneumonias, increasing swelling or fluid retention, less ability to cough or clear secretions, more fatigue, sleeping more, eating/drinking less, confusion or delirium during infections, and longer recovery times. Sometimes death occurs during a severe pneumonia or heart failure episode; sometimes it is a quieter decline where she becomes weaker, less awake, and less able to take in food or fluids.

I would strongly ask Dr. Priya Nandan and the hospital team for a **palliative care consult now**. Palliative care is not the same as hospice—it helps with symptom control, planning, and honest communication. Hospice may be appropriate if the overall goal is comfort and if she has recurrent hospitalizations, worsening CHF, recurrent aspiration/pneumonia, weight loss or poor intake, severe functional dependence, or declining despite treatment.

Questions to ask directly:
- “Are we at the point where these episodes are likely to keep recurring?”
- “Would you be surprised if she died in the next 6–12 months?”
- “Is she aspirating when she eats or drinks?”
- “What would CPR, intubation, or a feeding tube realistically do for her?”
- “Can we have a hospice informational visit?”

Urgent red flags include severe breathing distress, blue lips, new confusion, chest pain, high fever, oxygen levels dropping, inability to swallow, choking/aspiration, very low urine output, or rapidly worsening swelling.

You are asking the right questions. Helen’s strength is clear—but so is your love in wanting clarity before the next crisis.

query: is gargling with listerine effective against corona virus induced sore throat? will it kill the virus? how about with mixture of warm water and salt, will this also kill virus! im asking for myself, maria lopez, bc i woke up with this scratchy sore throat and now im sitting here overthinking every tiny thing. i dont have a bad cough or anything crazy right now, just the sore throat feeling and like that dry weird burn when i swallow, but with all the corona stuff i keep worrying if its starting. i called dr benjamin reed before but i didnt get a real answer yet, they just said watch symptoms and call back if fever or breathing trouble, but that doesnt really tell me if rinsing my throat helps or not. i have listerine at home and also salt and warm water, and i know ppl say gargle salt water for sore throat, but im asking specifically does it actually kill the virus in the throat or just make it feel better for a few min? i dont wanna think im cleaning it away if thats not true. i was gonna go buy more mouthwash at CVS on 10th and Market but idk if thats pointless and i dont wanna go out if im maybe contagious. my number is 215-555-7842 if someone needs to call but really i just need simple advice. like should i gargle every few hours, or is too much listerine bad and dries it out worse? and if warm water and salt doesnt kill virus, is there anything safe at home that does, or do i just rest and drink fluids and not panic. sorry i know this sounds dumb, im just nervous and keep reading different things online and they all say something else.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not a dumb question at all. A scratchy, burning sore throat can definitely make you worry, especially with COVID still around.

**Short answer:** gargling with Listerine or warm salt water may help your throat *feel better*, but you should **not rely on either one to “kill” coronavirus in your throat or prevent COVID from starting.**

Some mouthwashes can inactivate viruses in a lab dish, but that does **not** mean they eliminate virus in your nose/throat or stop infection in the body. The virus can be in nasal passages and cells lining the airway, where a gargle won’t reach. **Salt water is mainly soothing**—it can reduce irritation, loosen mucus, and decrease swelling a bit, but it is not a proven virus-killer.

What I’d suggest:
- **Warm salt-water gargles:** ¼–½ teaspoon salt in 8 oz warm water, gargle and spit. You can do this a few times daily.
- **Listerine:** okay if you tolerate it, but follow the label and don’t overdo it. Alcohol-containing mouthwash can dry or burn the throat and sometimes makes irritation worse. I wouldn’t use it every few hours.
- **Fluids, warm tea/honey, humidifier/steam, throat lozenges**, and rest can help.
- Avoid unsafe “disinfecting” ideas—no bleach, peroxide gargles, essential oils, or anything not meant for mucous membranes.

Because COVID can start with just a sore throat, consider taking a **home COVID test today**, and if negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**. Since you might be contagious, it’s reasonable **not to go to CVS** unless necessary—use delivery or ask someone else if you need supplies. Mask around others for now.

Call Dr. Reed’s office again or seek care urgently if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, fever that persists, inability to swallow saliva, severe one-sided throat pain/swelling, or symptoms rapidly worsening**.

For now, your plan can be: don’t panic, treat the discomfort, test, rest, hydrate, and monitor. A scratchy throat alone is very often from a minor virus, allergies, dry air, or reflux—not necessarily COVID.

query: what must i do if i have been in contact with a covid19 positive person? im asking bc im kinda panicking and dont wanna do the wrong thing, like i was around my cousin who just told me she tested positive and now im sitting here replaying the whole thing in my head. i’m maria lopez and i feel fine right this second but i dont know if that even matters, bc we were in the same room for awhile and i was prob too close, and we did talk a lot, and i dont think either of us had masks on the whole time. i didnt hug her i dont think, but we were eating and drinking coffee and now im worried that was dumb. i work at Target on 14th st and i dont know if i should call out or still go in if i dont have symptoms, bc i dont wanna get people sick, but i also dont wanna get in trouble for missing shifts if im not actually sick. my manager just says to follow whatever the dr says but i dont have a regular dr and im not sure if urgent care is needed or if i just stay home. do i need to quarantine right away and for how long, and when should i test, like today or wait a few days? if i test negative once does that mean im ok or can it show up later? i live with my kid and my mom so im also scared abt bringing it home, should i wear a mask inside and sleep in another room or is that too much. if someone can email me at mlopez82@gmail.com or just tell me what steps to take i’d appreciate it, im just confused and anxious.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this; it’s very understandable to feel anxious. Being indoors, close together, talking, and eating/drinking without masks **does count as a meaningful COVID exposure**, even if you feel fine right now.

Here’s what I’d do:

1. **Start precautions now.** For the next **5 days**, wear a well-fitting mask around others, especially at home and at work if you go in. Improve ventilation if possible.

2. **Testing timing:**  
   - If you develop symptoms, **test right away** and stay home.  
   - If you remain symptom-free, test around **day 5 after the exposure**.  
   - A negative test today may be too early and does **not** fully rule it out. If using home antigen tests, repeat testing 48 hours later is helpful.

3. **Work:** Since you’re asymptomatic, many workplaces allow you to work while masked, but policies vary. Call your manager/HR and say you had a close COVID exposure and are following medical guidance: mask, monitor symptoms, and test on day 5. If you develop symptoms, you should not go in.

4. **At home with your child and mom:** It’s not “too much” to be cautious. If possible, wear a mask in shared spaces for several days, avoid sharing cups/utensils, wash hands, keep windows open or use a fan/air purifier, and sleep separately if practical—especially if your mom is older or has health conditions.

5. **If you test positive:** Stay home and away from others. Current guidance generally says you can return to normal activities when symptoms are improving and you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever medicine, but continue extra precautions/masking for several more days.

You do **not** need urgent care just because you were exposed and feel well. Seek urgent care/emergency help if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or a fever that won’t come down.

Also, for privacy, I can’t email you directly here—but you’re doing the right thing by asking before acting.

query: i’m maria elena cruz, dob 08/14/1987, and im sorry this is long but im kinda panicking and also trying to remember everything in order. i went to the dr for a cough and fever on march 5, it was with dr nisha patel, bc i had been coughing and felt hot and just gross and tired and my chest felt irritated. they diagnosed me with bronchitis and said to rest and do the meds and fluids and all that, and i really did try to not overthink it. but i was not doing any better, like not even a little really, still coughing and still feverish and i kept waking up sweaty and anxious, so i returned to the dr on march 15. i told them i felt dumb coming back but it just wasnt clearing up and with everything on the news i was scared. they sent me to the er to get tested for covid. at the er, mercy hospital er, they ran a number of tests including a covid t, blood stuff, checked my oxygen, and i think maybe another swab too, honestly it was all a blur. my wrist band had mrn MHC-771492 on it and they also took my blue cross ppo #BCP8842019 info which makes me nervous bc i dont know what this is all gonna cost if they say its not covered or whatever. i keep wondering if bronchitis can look like covid or if i could have had both or if i went too early for the first visit. i dont want to expose anyone, ive been staying in my room and wiping everything down but im scared i already got my family sick. should i assume covid until the test comes back? and if the covid t is negative do i just treat it like bronchitis still or go back again if the fever keeps hanging around?
target_response:
Hi Maria Elena — I’m sorry you’re going through this. It makes sense that you feel anxious: a cough plus fever that isn’t improving after 10 days can be scary, especially with COVID concerns.

Yes, **bronchitis and COVID can look very similar**, especially early on: cough, fever, fatigue, chest irritation/tightness, sweats, and feeling “run down” can happen with either. Some “bronchitis” is caused by viruses, and COVID is one possible viral cause. It’s also possible to have another respiratory virus, flu, pneumonia, or less commonly a bacterial infection. Going in on March 5 was not “too early” or wrong — many illnesses start out looking like uncomplicated bronchitis and only declare themselves over time.

Until your COVID test result is back, I would **act as if you may have COVID**: stay in your room as much as possible, wear a mask if you need to be near family, use a separate bathroom if available, don’t share cups/utensils, improve ventilation, and keep wiping high-touch surfaces. Your family should monitor for symptoms and follow local guidance about exposure.

If the COVID test is **negative**, that lowers the chance but does not always eliminate it, depending on timing and the type of test. If you still have fever or worsening symptoms, you should contact your doctor/ER follow-up line rather than assuming it’s “just bronchitis.” Persistent fever after this long may need reassessment, sometimes including a chest X-ray, repeat oxygen check, flu/other testing, or treatment changes.

For now: rest, fluids, acetaminophen/ibuprofen if you can take them safely, honey or cough medicine as directed, and avoid smoking/vaping. Track your temperature and symptoms.

Please seek urgent care or return to the ER now if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen saturation under 92–94% if you have a pulse ox, coughing blood, dehydration, or fever that is very high or worsening**.

Regarding cost/insurance: the hospital billing office or your Blue Cross plan can tell you coverage details; keep your ER paperwork and ask about COVID-related billing policies or payment plans if needed.

query: hi, what are my chances of becoming severely unwell with the coronavirus, as i have type 1 diabetes? im maddie collins, dob 03/14/1997, and i know u prob cant say for sure but im really anxious and keep reading different things online and its making me spiral a bit. i have type 1 diabetes and thats the bit that keeps getting mentioned under “higher risk” and i dont know if that means like definitely hospital level sick or just be more careful than other ppl. i dont feel ill right now, i dont think, but every little cough or warm feeling makes me check again and then i worry im missing something. i sent a message to dr priya naren but havent heard back yet and my portal has me listed under mrn kf-20488 if that matters, sorry i know this is not very organized. i still have to go out for food sometimes and i live with other ppl so its hard to know what counts as safe enough. like if i caught it would my diabetes automatically make it severe, or is it more about if sugars get messed up during fever and not eating right? i can be sensible and stay home but i cant stop thinking about worst case stuff, especially seeing stories about young ppl too. i guess im asking in normal words, how worried should i actually be, and what signs would mean dont wait around? should i be calling early just bc im type 1, or only if breathing gets bad? sorry this is long i just want a real answer and not another scary internet article.
target_response:
Hi Maddie — I’m really glad you asked, and I’m sorry this has been making you spiral. The short version is: **having type 1 diabetes does not mean you would automatically become severely unwell if you caught COVID**, especially as a young adult. It does mean you should be **more cautious and have a lower threshold to get advice**, because infections can make blood sugars harder to manage and can increase the risk of dehydration and ketones/DKA.

For people with type 1 diabetes, the risk is usually related to a few things: how well sugars are running overall, whether you develop fever/vomiting or can’t eat/drink normally, whether ketones appear, and any other health conditions. Many people with type 1 recover at home, but it’s wise to plan ahead.

**What to do now if you feel well:**
- Keep up your usual diabetes routine and monitoring.
- Make sure you have insulin, glucose strips/CGM supplies, ketone strips, hypo treatments, and easy fluids/carbs at home.
- If you’re not already up to date with COVID vaccination/boosters, that’s one of the best ways to reduce severe illness risk.
- When going out for food: use well-fitting masks in crowded indoor places, go at quieter times, wash/sanitize hands, and improve ventilation at home where possible.

**If you develop symptoms or test positive:** I would contact your diabetes/primary care team early — not because you’re definitely in danger, but because early advice can prevent problems, and some people at higher risk may be eligible for antivirals if started promptly. Follow your sick-day plan: check glucose more often, check ketones if sugars are high or you feel unwell, keep fluids going, and never stop basal insulin unless specifically instructed.

**Don’t wait — seek urgent care/ER help** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, persistent high blood sugars despite correction insulin, moderate/large ketones, vomiting, or symptoms of DKA such as abdominal pain, deep/rapid breathing, or fruity breath.

Your worry is understandable, but “higher risk” means **be prepared and act early**, not “severe illness is inevitable.” If the checking/reassurance loop keeps taking over, it may also help to limit COVID searching and speak with your doctor about the anxiety side too.

query: hi, its me mariah benson, dob 03/14/1991, and sorry if this sounds stupid but im really anxious and i keep seeing stuff online and ppl in my building keep coughing and now im like spiraling. if i inhale enough lysol, will it keep me from getting sick from novel coronavirus? like i dont mean drink it or anything, i mean i sprayed some in the bathroom and hallway and then i kinda stood there breathing it in bc i thought maybe it kills the germs before they get into me?? i know that probably sounds crazy but im scared and i dont have anyone calm to ask right now. i tried calling the nurse line but was on hold forever and then my phone dropped, my number is 267-555-0149 if someone is supposed to call back but idk if they even got my message. i also emailed from mbenson91@proton.me earlier and havent heard anything. i bought the lysol at CVS on 19th and market yesterday when everyone was buying wipes and soap and there was barely anything left, so now im like maybe i should be spraying it more often, but then i started coughing a little just from the smell and that made me panic more bc i cant tell if its from the spray or if im getting sick. i washed my hands a bunch and wiped the doorknobs and my keys and my phone and now my chest just feels tight bc im nervous. i dont wanna go to urgent care if im just being dramatic and i dont wanna make it worse by sitting in a waiting room. pls just tell me if inhaling lysol actually helps prevent novel coronavirus or if im being dumb bc im honestly scared.
target_response:
