A chest X-ray usually cannot “prove” recent smoking, and tests generally won’t automatically expose it. But it’s important to tell the doctor honestly so they can assess your cough properly.

Seek urgent care if you develop **fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, coughing blood, weight loss, night sweats, persistent worsening cough, or wheezing that doesn’t settle**.

You did the right thing by stopping smoking—keep it up. Please don’t delay the consult; this is likely treatable, but it’s worth checking properly.

query: does a person get all the covid 19 symptoms all at once eg temperature, cold , tiredness , or we can get any of them eg cold but no temperature? im asking bc im getting myself all wound up reading stuff online and every site says it different and then i panic more. my name is marisa kelly, dob 03/14/1987, i usually go to dr nisha patel at riverbend family clinic but i dont wanna keep calling them like a crazy person if this is a dumb question. i left a msg already from 555-013-8821 and gave them my email marisa.kelly87@gmail.com but nobody called back yet and its making me more anxious bc i dont know if i should be worried only if all of it comes together or if one thing can count too. like if someone just has a cold feeling and a bit run down but no temperature does that still maybe be covid 19 or does the temperature usually come first? and if tiredness is there but no fever then what? i am not trying to over react but i have my mum around and i dont wanna pass something if im missing it. also i dont know if cold means runny nose/sneezy kind of cold or chills cold, bc ppl say cold and mean different stuff. i keep checking my temp and then thinking maybe im checking too much and making myself nervous. sorry this is rambling, i just need to know if covid symptoms come like a package all at once or if it can be random one by one, like cold but no temperature, tired but no cough, that kind of thing.
target_response:
Hi Marisa — this is **not a dumb question at all**, and you’re not “crazy” for asking. COVID symptoms can be confusing because they **do not have to come as a full package**.

A person can have COVID with **only one or two symptoms**, such as:
- runny/stuffy nose, sneezing, sore throat (“a cold” feeling)
- tiredness or body aches
- chills or feeling cold
- cough
- headache
- loss of taste/smell
- fever — but **fever is not required**

So yes: someone can have a “cold” feeling and feel run down **without a temperature** and still possibly have COVID — but it could also be a regular cold, allergies, flu, or another virus. The order varies too: fever does **not** always come first, and some people never get one.

Also, you’re right that “cold” can mean different things. Some people mean **runny nose/sneezing**, while others mean **chills/shivery feeling**. Both can happen with viral illnesses, including COVID.

Because your mum is around, the safest practical approach is: **act as if it could be contagious until you know more.** If you have new symptoms, consider doing a COVID test now, and if it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours. In the meantime: mask around her, keep distance where possible, improve ventilation, wash hands, and avoid sharing cups/utensils.

For your temperature, try checking only **2–3 times a day** unless you feel significantly worse. Rechecking constantly can feed anxiety and usually doesn’t add useful information.

You should seek urgent care now if you develop: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below 92% if you use a pulse oximeter. Also contact Dr. Patel’s clinic promptly if you test positive and your mum is elderly/high-risk, or if you have high-risk conditions, because treatment works best early.

For now, your symptoms alone don’t mean panic — they mean **test, take precautions, monitor, and call if worsening**.

query: hi, my name is becky lynn parker and i already typed half this once and it disappeared so im gonna try again, i exasberated back in sept with heavy pneumonia in my right lung...... i was so sick and tired and coughing then and i did what i was told, went to mercy health urgent care on 1325 n limestone st, got the meds, picked up whatever they sent to cvs on east main st, and tried to just get thru it. my dob is 03/14/1979 if that even matters but nobody ever listens anyway unless you have a card ready. the last few months i have experienced tremendous hair loss and breakage--it is so thin i only have about 1/3 of my hair left but no bald spots. like it comes out in my hands and in the shower and on my pillow and i keep thinking ok maybe stress from being sick but then it just keeps going and going and i am scared to brush it now. i also have a big lump (or bulge) about 1 inch above the base of my hairline on the back of my head. it goes all across the back of my skull. it isnt like one little pimple or something, its more like a ridge or swelling and i keep touching it which prob makes me more crazy. any advice? could pneumonia or the meds do this to hair months later, or is the lump something seperate?? i dont have money to keep paying people to shrug at me and send me home. i thought you were here to help....... but hell no....... everybody wants my money....... which i don t have. thanks alot
pii_units: mercy health urgent care on 1325 n limestone st
target_response:
Hi Becky — I’m really sorry you’ve been through this, and I hear how frustrating and scary it is to feel like you’re paying to be dismissed. What you’re describing deserves a real exam, but I can give you a likely framework.

**Yes: severe pneumonia can absolutely trigger major hair shedding months later.** A serious illness with fever, inflammation, poor sleep/appetite, weight change, emotional stress, or certain medications can push many hairs into a “resting” phase at once. This is called **telogen effluvium**. It typically starts **2–3 months after the illness**, causes **diffuse shedding without bald spots**, and can feel dramatic—hair in hands, shower, pillow. It often improves gradually over **3–6 months**, but it’s worth checking for contributors that keep it going.

Common things to test for include:  
- **CBC** for anemia/infection recovery  
- **Ferritin/iron** levels  
- **TSH/free T4** for thyroid disease  
- **Vitamin D, B12**, sometimes zinc  
- Review of any meds taken during/after pneumonia  
- Nutrition/weight loss and menstrual changes

The **bulge/ridge at the back of your skull** may be separate. Some people have a prominent normal bony ridge called the **external occipital protuberance/nuchal ridge**, and muscle tension can make the area feel more noticeable. But swelling across the back of the head could also be scalp inflammation, cyst/lipoma, enlarged nodes, or something else—so it really needs someone to **feel it directly**.

Please seek urgent care sooner if the lump is **rapidly growing**, very painful, red/warm, draining, associated with **fever**, severe headache, neck stiffness, vomiting, vision changes, weakness/numbness, confusion, recent head injury, night sweats, or unexplained weight loss.

Given cost concerns, I’d suggest calling a **community health clinic/sliding-scale primary care office** and asking for a focused visit for “diffuse hair loss after pneumonia plus posterior scalp/skull swelling.” Bring any urgent care paperwork or pharmacy med list if you can. Until then: avoid tight hairstyles, heat/bleach, aggressive brushing, and prioritize protein/iron-rich foods. If this is telogen effluvium, it is usually reversible—but the lump should be examined.

query: should patients with covid-19 avoid ibuprofen or raas antagonists? im asking bc i keep seeing random posts and then other ppl saying the opposite and its making me more confused not less. im michael denner, dob 03/14/1979, and my chart has me as MRN KJ-77319 at bayview family med, but i dont wanna bug the office with a dumb question if this is already settled. dr. priya nair had told me before not to just stop meds without asking, so im not trying to do anything weird, i just wanna know what the actual advice is for covid-19. like if someone has fever/body aches and they have ibuprofen at home, is that bad or was that just an internet rumor from early covid days? and same with raas antagonists, are ppl supposed to avoid those during covid-19 or keep taking them unless their own dr says stop? i get my regular stuff filled at cvs on maple ave and i was gonna call the pharmacist too but they are always slammed and i feel like they just rush me off the phone. insurance is aetna ppo #552-90-1187 if that matters for who i can message, but honestly im just trying not to make a stupid choice at 11pm when everyone in the house is anxious and googling too much. i dont need a huge lecture, just like general guidance, should patients with covid-19 avoid ibuprofen or raas antagonists, or is the safest answer still ask your doctor and dont stop normal prescriptions on your own? sorry if this is basic, the wording online is all over the place and its freaking me out.
target_response:
Hi Michael — not a dumb question at all. The early COVID-era messaging around this was confusing, and a lot of outdated posts still circulate.

**Short answer:** For most patients, **COVID-19 is not a reason by itself to avoid ibuprofen**, and it is **not a reason to stop RAAS medications** such as ACE inhibitors or ARBs unless your own clinician tells you to.

**Ibuprofen:** The early concern that ibuprofen worsened COVID outcomes has not held up well. Current general guidance is that ibuprofen can be used for fever/body aches if you normally tolerate it. Avoid or check first if you have a history of stomach ulcers/bleeding, significant kidney disease, are on blood thinners, have uncontrolled high blood pressure/heart failure, severe dehydration, or have been told not to take NSAIDs. Acetaminophen/Tylenol is also a good option for fever/aches, as long as you stay within dosing limits and avoid excess if you have liver disease or heavy alcohol use.

**RAAS antagonists:** This includes common blood pressure/heart medications like **lisinopril, losartan, valsartan, etc.** The standard advice is **do not stop them just because of COVID-19.** Stopping suddenly can worsen blood pressure, heart failure, kidney protection, or other conditions they are treating. That matches what Dr. Nair told you: don’t stop regular prescriptions without checking.

There are exceptions during any acute illness, COVID included. A clinician may temporarily hold certain medications if someone has **very low blood pressure, significant dehydration, severe vomiting/diarrhea, acute kidney injury, dangerously high potassium, or is hospitalized/seriously ill.** But that is individualized.

For tonight: if someone has COVID symptoms and needs fever/body ache relief, using **acetaminophen or ibuprofen according to the label** is generally reasonable if they have no contraindications. Keep fluids up, rest, and monitor symptoms.

Seek urgent care now for **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen saturation persistently below ~92% if you have a pulse ox, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness, or worsening despite initial improvement.**

You are thinking about this the right way: use reliable guidance, don’t stop chronic meds on your own, and message/call your doctor if your specific health conditions or medication list raise concerns.

query: i m linda mae harlow, 63 female, dob 02/18/1954, and i started a cough in 2009 that was diagnosed as severe bronchitis and then a couple years i had pneumonia twice and now i ve got combination of bronchitis and pneumonia bad cough. i feel like i have been coughing forever and nobody ever really says the same thing twice. i just had a second ct scan one was august 22nd and the other one was october 23rd and i ve never been told a diagnosis before but i happened to see it on my chart what: hospital outpatient visit with lks ct 1 when: monday october 23, 2017 7:30 am (30 minutes) where: lakeside hospital cat scan (lakeside) diagnosis: interstitial lung disease. my chart also has MRN LH-663902 on it and that is how i found it, i was just clicking around and then there it was and now im scared sick. i am going november 27th to discuss this diagnosis i hope with dr peter kline, because i was just told i don t have copd and then i get this lung disease which i ve never heard of and it doesn t have a very good survival rate. i keep thinking did they know before and not tell me, or is it just from the second scan, idk. my insurance is blue cross blue shield #LHX4492081 and i already called them once and they said they dont explain results. my meds usually go to Walgreens on 5th Ave but i dont even know if there is medicine for this. my daughter said to message because i keep googling and crying, she can reach me at lindaharlow54@aol.com if someone needs the chart screenshot. i just want to know if this is always fatal or if they could be wrong from a ct scan.
target_response:
Dear Linda,

I’m very sorry you found that wording on your chart without anyone explaining it to you. It is completely understandable that seeing “interstitial lung disease” and then reading about it online would make you scared.

The most important thing to know is: **“Interstitial lung disease” is not one single diagnosis, and it is not always fatal.** It is a broad category that means there may be inflammation, scarring, or other changes in the lung tissue seen on imaging. Some forms are mild, stable, or treatable; others need closer monitoring and specific medication. A CT scan can strongly suggest ILD, but the exact type usually requires putting together the CT pattern, symptoms, lung function tests, oxygen levels, blood tests, medication/exposure history, and sometimes specialist review.

It is also possible that the term appeared as a **working diagnosis, billing diagnosis, or radiology impression** rather than a final confirmed diagnosis. The fact that you were told you do **not** have COPD is separate—COPD affects the airways, while ILD affects the lung tissue itself. Chronic cough after bronchitis/pneumonia can also be due to asthma, reflux, postnasal drip, bronchiectasis, medication side effects, or recurrent infection, so it’s important not to assume the worst yet.

Between now and your November 27 appointment, I’d suggest you call Dr. Kline’s office and ask:  
1. “Did my CT report show interstitial lung disease?”  
2. “Can I get a copy of the actual radiology report?”  
3. “Do I need pulmonary function tests or a high-resolution CT?”  
4. “Should I be seen sooner or referred to a pulmonologist/ILD specialist?”

Treatment depends on the cause—sometimes inhalers help symptoms, sometimes steroids or other anti-inflammatory medicines are used, and for certain scarring conditions there are antifibrotic medicines. But the right treatment depends on the exact diagnosis.

Please seek urgent care now if you have worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, coughing blood, high fever, confusion, fainting, or oxygen levels below about 90% if you monitor them.

Try to stop Googling for now—online survival statistics often reflect the most severe forms and may not apply to you. The next step is getting the CT report explained clearly.

query: hi- i’m melissa grant and i was diagnosed a month ago with community pneumonia and i’m still kinda worried i guess. i saw dr. nora patel at mercy family care, they have me in their system as MRN MFC-884201, and at the time i felt awful but it made sense bc the dr said pneumonia and gave me antibiotics. after two rounds of antibiotics i was feeling better, like not 100% but at least able to get around the house and do normal stuff without thinking about every breath. now my cough is back and i have a strange heavy feeling especially in my legs. i have been exhausted too for the last few days. could this be from pneumonia? it’s not like the exact same as before, which is why i’m confused, bc the cough is annoying and coming back but the leg heaviness is whats freaking me out more. it feels like my legs are full of sand or something and i just want to lay down, and i keep thinking maybe i pushed myself too soon after being sick or maybe the infection never really fully went away. i dont know if i should call the clinic again or if this is just normal recovery after community pneumonia. i finished what they gave me and didnt skip doses, so i thought i was done with it. i’m not trying to be dramatic but i’m tired in a weird way, like showering and walking around makes me want to sit. no new diagnosis or anything, just this cough back plus heavy legs and being so wiped out the last few days, and i keep googling which is prob making me worse. should i be seen again or can pneumonia make you feel like this even after antibiotics?
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m glad you reached out. It’s understandable to feel worried, especially since you had started improving after two antibiotic courses and now the cough and exhaustion are coming back.

Yes, **pneumonia can leave people feeling wiped out for several weeks**, even after the infection is treated. A lingering cough and reduced stamina are common during recovery. That said, the pattern you describe — **cough returning plus a new “heavy legs/full of sand” feeling and being unusually exhausted with basic activity** — is worth being checked rather than assuming it’s normal recovery.

A few possibilities include:
- **Post-pneumonia inflammation/post-infectious cough**, which can last weeks.
- **The infection not fully resolving** or a new viral infection on top of recovery.
- **Low oxygen levels, anemia, dehydration, electrolyte issues**, or general deconditioning after being ill.
- Less commonly but importantly: a **blood clot**, especially if you were less active while sick. This is more concerning if one leg is more swollen/painful/warm than the other, or if you have chest pain or shortness of breath.

Because your symptoms have changed and you’re feeling markedly limited by fatigue, I’d recommend you **call Mercy Family Care today** and let them know you recently had community-acquired pneumonia, completed antibiotics, improved, and now have recurrent cough with new leg heaviness and exertional fatigue. They may want to check your vitals and oxygen level, listen to your lungs, and possibly repeat a chest X-ray or do labs.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you have any of the following: **shortness of breath at rest, worsening trouble breathing, chest pain, coughing blood, fainting, confusion, blue lips, rapid heart rate, fever returning, oxygen level below 92–94% if you can measure it, or one-sided leg swelling/pain/redness/warmth.**

In the meantime, rest, hydrate, avoid pushing yourself hard, and don’t restart or take leftover antibiotics unless a clinician tells you to. You’re not being dramatic — this is exactly the kind of change after pneumonia that deserves a reassessment.

query: well quick question i had pneumonia back in november2013 and theres a concern i have now, my name is jason reed and im probly over thinking this but its been bugging me enough that i keep googling stuff and then i get more nervous. i took antibiotics s and got better back then but im simply not all there in the breathing department, like i dont feel sick-sick all the time but i can tell my chest just isnt like it used to be. lately ive been waking up with a raspy chest but goes away as day progresses, usually after im up moving around and drinking coffee or water or whatever, also feel as tho if i had dust in my throat and its annoying cause it makes me keep clearing it. when i manage to get the plegm out its not all colorful its just clear, so that part makes me think maybe its not infection but idk. i get a tight chest feelings sometimes albuterol does help before exerting myself/ excersise, like if i use it before i go walk or try to workout it seems to make things easier. i saw dr. lena morales a while back for regular stuff and meant to bring it up but then didnt cause of course it was one of those days i felt ok. i can be reached at 267-555-0148 if needed but really im just wondering if this sounds like leftover airway irritation from old pneumonia or asthma type thing or if i should be worried its something more serious. i dont wanna run in for every little thing but also dont wanna ignore my lungs again.
target_response:
Hi Jason — I’m glad you brought this up, and you’re not “overthinking” it. Breathing symptoms that keep recurring are worth checking out, especially since albuterol helps.

A pneumonia from 2013 would be unlikely to still be causing simple “leftover irritation” all these years later. That said, pneumonia can sometimes make people more aware of their lungs, and occasionally it can unmask an underlying tendency toward airway reactivity.

What you describe—morning raspy chest, clear phlegm, throat-clearing/dusty sensation, chest tightness with exertion that improves with albuterol—could fit several common possibilities:

- **Asthma or exercise-induced bronchospasm/reactive airways**, especially since albuterol helps.
- **Post-nasal drip/allergies**, which often causes morning mucus, throat clearing, and clear phlegm.
- **Reflux/GERD**, which can irritate the throat and airways overnight and be worse in the morning.
- Irritation from **smoke, vaping, dust, pets, mold, workplace exposures**, or dry air.
- Less commonly, chronic lung changes/scarring or bronchiectasis after past infection—usually associated with more frequent infections, larger amounts of mucus, or abnormal imaging.

I would recommend scheduling a non-urgent visit with Dr. Morales or your primary care clinician to specifically discuss this. It would be reasonable to do a lung exam and consider **spirometry/pulmonary function testing**, possibly before and after albuterol, to look for asthma. Depending on your history and exam, a **chest X-ray** may also be reasonable for reassurance and to rule out anything structural.

In the meantime: stay well-hydrated, avoid smoke/vaping/dust when possible, consider a humidifier if your air is dry, and try nasal saline or an over-the-counter allergy spray/antihistamine if you have congestion or drainage. Keep track of how often you use albuterol. If you need it more than about **2 days per week**, wake up coughing/wheezing, or avoid activity because of breathing, you may need a controller inhaler rather than just rescue albuterol.

Seek urgent care now if you develop shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, blue lips, coughing blood, high fever, worsening wheezing not relieved by albuterol, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

query: coronavirus how do we test? im sorry if this is dumb but im getting like 5 diff answers online and i dont wanna drag everybody all over town if there is a normal way to do this. my name is maria lopez, dob 03/14/1989, and i was gonna call but the hold music at the nurse line had me sitting forever and i had to get back on a work zoom. if someone can just tell me if we need an order or if we can just pull up somewhere that would help a lot. my cell is 267-555-0198 but text is better bc i may not answer if im with the kids, email is maria.lpz89@gmail.com too if thats easier. i keep seeing stuff about kits and swabs and rapid tests but then other ppl say you have to go thru your regular dr first and i honestly dont know what is real anymore. i was thinking maybe penn urgent care south street but idk if they even want people walking in asking about coronavirus or if that makes it worse. we have aetna hmo #AET-4471902 if that matters, but i dont want a giant bill just for someone to say go home and wait. do we need to call ahead, stay in the car, wear masks, what do we bring, how long does the result take, and can the whole house test or just one person? sorry im rambling, im just nervous and trying not to panic but also dont wanna ignore it if testing is what we should do.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not dumb at all. The testing options have changed a lot, so it’s completely understandable to feel like you’re getting mixed answers.

**The simplest “normal” way to test for COVID now:**

1. **Home rapid antigen test**  
   - You can buy these at pharmacies or use any kits you already have.  
   - Results take about **15–30 minutes**.  
   - Best if someone has symptoms. If the first test is negative but symptoms continue, repeat **24–48 hours later**.

2. **PCR/NAAT test**  
   - Usually done at urgent care, pharmacies, some doctor offices, or testing sites.  
   - More sensitive than home tests.  
   - Results often take **same day to 1–2 days**, depending on the site.

3. **Do you need a doctor’s order?**  
   - Often **no** for pharmacy/urgent care testing, but some sites require an appointment or online registration.  
   - With an **HMO**, coverage can depend on whether the site is in-network or whether a referral is required, so checking Aetna’s site/app or calling the number on the card can help avoid a surprise bill.

**For Penn Urgent Care or any urgent care:** I would **call ahead or use online check-in** rather than walking in. Ask: “Do you offer COVID testing, do I need an appointment/order, do you take Aetna HMO, and what is the expected cost?” Many places prefer symptomatic patients to **mask and wait outside/in the car** until called.

**What to bring:** photo ID, insurance card, a mask for each person, list of symptoms and when they started, and any medications/health conditions.

**Can the whole household test?**  
Yes, but practically: test anyone with symptoms first. If someone was exposed but feels well, test around **day 5 after exposure**, or sooner if symptoms start. If one person tests positive, assume close household contacts may have been exposed and mask/avoid high-risk people.

**Go to urgent/emergency care now** if anyone has trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness/dehydration, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse ox, or a child with persistent high fever, breathing difficulty, or lethargy.

If someone tests positive and is high-risk, treatment may be time-sensitive, so contact your clinician promptly.

query: can sinusitis cause white spots on tonsils? im asking bc im melissa tran and i swear i get myself worked up googling stuff and then i dont know whats normal anymore. i already left a msg for dr. nisha patel but the portal is slow and the front desk said maybe tomorrow, and im sitting here staring at my throat with my phone flashlight like a crazy person. i have the sinusitis thing going on or at least thats what it feels like, all up in my face and head, but then i noticed these white spots on tonsils and now im like ok is that even from sinus stuff or is that a totally different thing. i know nobody can diagnose me over text, i just want to know if thats even a thing that happens. i was seen before at penn urgent care at 3737 market st for sinus stuff and they always ask my dob which is 04/17/1992 and my chart never pulls up right, mrn PT-884210, so im trying not to go back unless i should. my phone is 267-555-0148 if they call but i miss calls when im at work so im trying to figure it out here first. also if i do need meds i usually use cvs on 200 s broad st cause its close, but i dont wanna ask for anything if this is just normal drainage or irritation or whatever. sorry this is long, i just keep thinking sinusitis should be nose/face stuff and tonsil spots seems weird and now im anxious about it.
pii_units: penn urgent care at 3737 market st
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you’re not “crazy”; throat-checking with a phone flashlight is very common when you’re anxious and not getting quick answers. And yes: sinus/post‑nasal drainage can irritate the throat and tonsils, and sometimes mucus can collect around tonsil crypts and look like whitish spots. So it *can* be related.

That said, true white spots on the tonsils can also happen with things separate from sinusitis, including:

- **Viral sore throat** — very common, often with congestion, cough, drainage.
- **Strep throat** — more likely if you have fever, significant throat pain, swollen/tender neck glands, and little/no cough.
- **Tonsil stones** — small white/yellow bits, sometimes with bad breath or a “something stuck” feeling.
- **Mono** — fatigue, swollen glands, sometimes enlarged tonsils.
- **Thrush** — white patches that may wipe off and leave redness, more common after antibiotics/inhaled steroids or with immune issues.

While waiting for Dr. Patel’s office, I’d suggest: don’t scrape or poke at the spots; hydrate; use saline nasal spray/rinse if you tolerate it; warm salt-water gargles; acetaminophen/ibuprofen if you can take them safely; and consider a humidifier/steam. If you have significant sore throat or fever, it’s reasonable to get a **rapid strep test** at urgent care or a clinic rather than guessing about antibiotics.

For sinus symptoms, antibiotics are usually considered when symptoms last **10+ days without improvement**, are severe with high fever/pus-like nasal discharge for several days, or worsen after initially improving.

Please seek urgent care sooner if you develop trouble breathing or swallowing, drooling, severe one-sided throat pain/swelling, muffled “hot potato” voice, inability to open your mouth well, neck swelling/stiffness, dehydration, fever over 103°F, or you feel rapidly worse.

Also, for your privacy, avoid posting DOB/MRN/phone details in messages unless you’re in a secure medical portal. For now, a strep test would be the main “should I go in?” factor if the throat symptoms are prominent.

query: i am experiencing cough (worsened by: lying flat, exposure to secondhand smoke, night time) (associated with: phlegm or mucus) (not associated with: barky, hoarse, or hacking sound, whooping sound upon breathing in, chronic), swelling around eyes, fatigue. my name is maria elena cruz and i know this is prob too much info but im tired and kinda anxious bc it feels worse when i lay down flat like to sleep, and then i get all panicky bc the mucus/phlegm is there and i keep clearing my throat. my dob is 03/18/1987 if that matters for the chart or whatever. i dont have that barking cough or whooping thing and it doesnt sound hoarse or hacky to me, its just this annoying cough that gets worse at night and also when im around smoke. my neighbor smokes outside but it still comes thru the hall and my eyes get puffy/swollen around them and then im just exhausted the next day. i was going to call dr. nisha patel but i wasnt sure if this is more like allergies, sinus stuff, or something chest related, and i dont wanna waste an appt if i should just do otc stuff first. i can be reached at 267-555-0149 but sometimes my phone is on silent bc im trying to sleep during the day after being up coughing all night. i usually pick up meds at cvs on 12th and walnut if anything needs to be sent in. i also emailed once from maria.cruz87@gmail.com but idk if that went thru. mainly asking bc the swelling around eyes plus fatigue is freaking me out a bit, even tho the cough doesnt seem chronic, its just really dragging me down and worse lying flat.
target_response:
Hi Maria Elena — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this. A cough that worsens **lying flat**, at **night**, and with **secondhand smoke**, plus mucus/throat-clearing and puffy eyes, commonly fits with **allergies/irritant exposure and post-nasal drip**. Smoke can inflame the nose/sinuses and airways, causing mucus to drain when you lie down, which triggers coughing and that “need to clear my throat” feeling. Other possibilities include a viral upper respiratory infection, reflux, or smoke-triggered asthma/reactive airways.

The swelling around the eyes can happen with allergies, especially if both eyes are puffy/itchy/watery, but it’s worth monitoring closely.

**What you can try now for the next few days:**
- Sleep with your head/upper chest elevated; avoid lying completely flat.
- Use **saline nasal spray or rinse** before bed.
- Consider an OTC **non-drowsy antihistamine** such as cetirizine, loratadine, or fexofenadine.
- Consider a nasal steroid spray like **fluticasone** once daily for several days; it works best with consistent use.
- Drink fluids; warm tea/honey can calm cough. Guaifenesin may help loosen mucus.
- Minimize smoke exposure as much as possible: door draft blocker, air purifier/HEPA filter, keep hallway air out if possible.

I do think it would be reasonable to contact Dr. Nisha Patel, especially because the cough is disrupting sleep and you’re having eye swelling and fatigue. This is not “wasting an appointment” — they can check your lungs, oxygen level, sinuses, and decide if you need an inhaler, allergy treatment, testing, or further evaluation.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop shortness of breath, wheezing that won’t settle, chest pain, blue lips, coughing blood, high/persistent fever, severe weakness, confusion, or if the eye swelling becomes severe, one-sided, painful, associated with vision changes, redness/warmth, hives, lip/tongue/throat swelling, or trouble swallowing/breathing.

Also get checked promptly if you cannot lie flat due to breathing difficulty, or if you notice leg swelling or sudden weight gain. Otherwise, starting allergy/post-nasal drip measures while arranging follow-up is a sensible next step.

query:
i was swimming in a pool on 2/15 at LA Fitness on 2560 Grant Ave and i slipped and went under the water unexpectedly and i wound up choking quite badly on some water. my name is marissa kendall btw if that matters for this thing, i can be reached at 215-555-7394, but im mostly just trying to figure out if i should be worried or if im being dramatic. i couldn't catch my breath and i felt like i had water in my lung that i couldn't expel with coughing, like i kept coughing and coughing right after it happened and it still felt like something was stuck down there. i got out and sat there for awhile trying not to make a whole scene bc ppl were staring and i was embarrassed, but it scared me honestly.

yesterday i began having a hacking cough and a sore throat (fells like my throats on fire). at first i thought maybe it was just from all the coughing after i swallowed/choked on the pool water, or maybe the chlorine irritated it, but then today i feel even worse, my cough is worse and sounds more "cruddy" and my throat still hurts, my head is pounding and my body is starting to ache. i keep thinking about the water going down wrong and now im nervous something is brewing in my chest even tho i know its only been a couple days. i dont know if this is a thing that can happen after a pool accident or if i should wait it out, drink tea, whatever, but im anxious and i keep googling which is obviously making it worse. could i be developing aspiration pneumonia?
target_response:
Hi Marissa — you are **not being dramatic**. Choking/aspirating water unexpectedly can be scary, and it’s reasonable to ask whether your worsening cough could be related.

A few possibilities fit what you’re describing:

1. **Throat/airway irritation** from coughing hard and from chlorinated pool water can cause a burning sore throat and cough for 24–48 hours.
2. **A viral infection** could coincidentally be starting — sore throat, headache, body aches, and a “cruddy” cough often fit COVID/flu/other respiratory viruses.
3. **Aspiration pneumonitis or pneumonia** is possible after water “goes down the wrong pipe.” Pneumonitis is irritation/inflammation and often starts within hours; aspiration pneumonia can develop over the next 1–3 days, especially if symptoms worsen, fever develops, or breathing becomes harder. Pool water is chlorinated, which lowers bacterial risk, but it doesn’t make risk zero.

Because your cough is **worsening** and you now have systemic symptoms like headache/body aches, I’d recommend you get checked **today or within the next 24 hours** at urgent care or with your primary care clinician. They can listen to your lungs, check your oxygen level, and decide whether you need a chest X-ray, viral testing, or treatment. Antibiotics are not always needed, but a clinician should assess.

Go to urgent care/ER sooner if you have any of these red flags:  
- Shortness of breath, wheezing, or trouble speaking in full sentences  
- Chest pain or tightness  
- Fever ≥100.4°F/38°C, shaking chills, or feeling rapidly worse  
- Coughing up blood or lots of thick green/brown sputum  
- Blue lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness  
- Home pulse ox below ~94% if you have one

For now: hydrate, use honey/tea or lozenges for throat, consider acetaminophen/ibuprofen if safe for you, avoid smoke/vaping, and consider a COVID/flu test.

Also, try not to let “dry drowning” searches spiral you — delayed severe problems days later are uncommon, but **worsening cough after aspiration is worth a medical check**.

query: hi where in cape town can you get tested for covid19? im asking for myself, nomsa petersen, and sorry if this is a dumb question but im getting so confused with all the different info online and every place says something else. i tried googling and then i get like private labs and then some say you need a dr letter and some say walk in and some say book first and i honestly dont know what is real anymore. i dont have bad symptoms or anything im not saying its emergency, but i was around someone who is now waiting on results and now im sitting here overthinking every cough and throat tickle and i dont want to just pitch up somewhere and get turned away. im staying at 12 kloof nek rd for now and dont have a car so if there is somewhere easy with uber or taxi thats better, like does clicks sea point do it or is that only the pharmacy stuff? also do they still test if you dont have medical aid, and what does it cost more or less, bc i dont wanna get there and then its like crazy expensive. if someone knows can u pls tell me the actual place and if i must call first. my number is 072-555-1934 but i might miss calls bc im working from home and my email is nomsa.petersen83@gmail.com if they send bookings that way. just need straight answer like where in cape town can you get tested for covid19 and how long results take, because im trying not to stress but also dont want to be careless around my auntie and the kids in the house.
target_response:
Hi Nomsa — not a dumb question at all. COVID testing arrangements have changed a lot, so it’s understandable that the online information is confusing.

**Near Kloof Nek/CBD/Sea Point, your most practical options are:**

1. **Private labs: PathCare, Lancet or Ampath**  
   These usually have collection points around **Gardens/Cape Town CBD/Sea Point/Green Point**. Most allow testing **without medical aid** if you pay yourself, but some sites require an online booking or request form. Call or check their websites before going so you don’t get turned away.

2. **Clicks / Dis-Chem clinic testing**  
   Some branches offer **rapid COVID antigen tests**, but it varies by branch and staffing. **Clicks Sea Point may offer it only if their clinic service is running**, so phone the branch or use the Clicks clinic booking system first.

3. **Public sector clinics**  
   If cost is a concern, you can contact your nearest public clinic or the **Western Cape COVID hotline: 021 928 4102** to ask where testing is currently being done and whether you meet criteria. Public testing availability may depend on symptoms/risk.

**Costs and results:**  
- **Rapid antigen test:** usually about **R150–R350**, results in **15–30 minutes**. Best if you have symptoms.  
- **PCR test:** often around **R500–R900**, results usually **24–48 hours**. More sensitive, especially after exposure.

Because you were exposed to someone awaiting results and you have only mild throat/cough symptoms, try to **mask indoors, avoid close contact with your auntie and the kids, improve ventilation, and don’t share cups/utensils** until you know more. If you test too early after exposure, it can be falsely negative — generally test **when symptoms start** or **3–5 days after exposure**.

Please seek urgent care if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, fainting, severe dehydration, or worsening high fever**, or if you have significant underlying health issues.

One extra note: for privacy, avoid posting your phone number/email publicly online. For now, I’d suggest phoning **Clicks Sea Point** and one nearby **PathCare/Lancet/Ampath depot** first, then take the closest Uber/taxi option that confirms they can test you today.

query: my mother margaret ellis was diagnosed with left basal pneumonia she is 74 yeas old and was discharged from st marys hospital yesterday and im just a bit panicky now cos i dont really understand it all and nobody explained much when we left, it was all rushed and she was tired and i was trying to listen but also sort her bag and taxi and everything. she was given amoxicillin and clarithromycin by dr nisha patel and we picked them up from boots pharmacy on high st on the way home, her label has nhs no 485 927 3162 on it if that matters but i dont know who im even supposed to ring first. she has only had like a day of them but now she is being sick and has diarohhea, like running to the toilet and then feeling all weak after, and im worried cos she only just got home from hospital yesterday and i dont want her going backwards. she has had amoxicillin before which hasnt effected her this way at all, she took it for a tooth thing ages ago and was fine, so will it be the other antibiotics, the clarithromycin, or can the two together do this? she is trying to keep water down but then says her stomach is turning and she doesnt want food, and im scared if she misses doses the pneumonia will get worse but also scared if she keeps taking them she will get more sick. no one said much about side effects apart from maybe upset stomach but this seems more than that to me. also what is left basal pneumonia actually, is it one lung at the bottom on the left or something different, and is it more serious because its basal? sorry this is long im just sitting here watching her cough and look pale and dont know if this is normal after discharge or not.
target_response:
I’m sorry this was all so rushed — it’s completely understandable to feel panicky when your mum has just come home with pneumonia and is now being sick and having diarrhoea.

**“Left basal pneumonia”** means an infection in the lower part (“base”) of the **left lung**. “Basal” describes the location, not necessarily that it is more serious. However, at **74**, and just discharged, she does need close watching.

Yes — **amoxicillin and clarithromycin can both cause nausea/diarrhoea**, and clarithromycin is particularly well known for stomach upset, metallic taste, nausea, and diarrhoea. Taking the two together can make this more likely. That said, **repeated vomiting/diarrhoea with weakness** can lead to dehydration and can also mean she isn’t absorbing the antibiotics properly. Antibiotics can also rarely trigger a more serious bowel infection, especially after hospital care, so this shouldn’t be ignored.

**What I’d do now:**  
1. **Contact someone today** — don’t wait. Use any number on her discharge paperwork for the ward/hospital team. If there isn’t one, call **St Mary’s switchboard** and ask for the ward/team that discharged Margaret yesterday.  
2. If you can’t get through promptly, call her **GP/GP out-of-hours**, or **NHS 111** for same-day advice.  
3. You can also ask the Boots pharmacist about side effects/interactions, but because she’s weak and just post-discharge, she may need clinical review.

Until you get advice, encourage **small frequent sips** of water or oral rehydration solution. Food matters less for the moment. If she can tolerate it, taking antibiotics **with a little food** may reduce nausea — but don’t force food.

**Seek urgent help now / 999 / A&E** if she has: worsening breathlessness, blue lips, chest pain, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, can’t keep fluids down, very little urine, high fever/rigors, blood in diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, or oxygen levels low if you have a monitor.

Don’t stop or change the antibiotics without speaking to a clinician, but given how unwell she sounds, she needs **same-day advice**. Also, avoid sharing her NHS number online or in messages unless it’s directly with NHS staff.

query:
hi, my name is mark delaney, dob 04/18/1981, phone 215-555-7394, and i know this maybe sounds paranoid but i had a severe bout of pneumonia is nov. and it really knocked me down hard for a while. i am feeling pain in the same area of my right lung again, kind of the same spot that scared me last time, and im trying not to spiral but its def making me nervous. am coughing a bit but do not have a cold, like no runny nose or sore throat or anything that feels like a normal cold. did notice a little mucos over the past couple of days, not a ton, just enough that i keep checking it and thinking ok is this starting again or am i overthinking it. could the pneumonia be back? typcially i ma in great shape, i work out, dont usually get sick, so this is why its bugging me.

last time i was seen at Penn Medicine Rittenhouse and i think Dr. Elena Ruiz was the one who followed up, my chart has MRN PM-772914 if that matters. i have Independence Blue Cross #XQ9821145 but honestly even with that i dont want to run in for another xray if this is just leftover irritation or a pulled muscle from coughing. but the pain being in the same area of my right lung is what keeps getting in my head. i dont have a fever that ive noticed and im still moving around ok, but i remember in nov. it started kinda small too. should i call and ask for a chest xray again or wait a day or two and watch the cough/mucos? and what signs would mean dont wait, like go in right away?
target_response:
Hi Mark — I can understand why this would make you anxious, especially after a severe pneumonia in November that started subtly and affected the same area.

Yes, pneumonia *can* recur, but pain in the same right-sided area with a mild cough and small amount of mucus could also be from several less serious causes: lingering airway/pleural irritation after the prior infection, a mild bronchitis, inflammation around the lung lining, or even a chest wall/intercostal muscle strain—especially if the pain changes with movement, deep breaths, or pressing on the ribs.

That said, because you had a significant pneumonia recently and this feels localized and familiar, I would **not ignore it**. If you’re otherwise stable—no fever, breathing normally, able to move around—I think it’s reasonable to **call Penn Medicine/Dr. Ruiz’s office today or tomorrow for triage**. They may recommend an exam and possibly a repeat chest X-ray, especially if the pain is pleuritic, the cough is increasing, or mucus persists/worsens. A chest X-ray is often the quickest way to distinguish “watch and wait” from something that needs treatment.

For the next 24 hours, you can monitor:
- Temperature 2–3 times daily  
- Cough frequency and mucus amount/color  
- Shortness of breath with normal activity  
- If you have a pulse oximeter: oxygen level at rest and walking  

Hydrate, avoid intense workouts for now, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely.

**Do not wait—seek urgent care/ER** if you develop any of the following: fever ≥100.4°F, shaking chills, worsening or sharp chest pain, shortness of breath, oxygen saturation <94%, coughing blood, rapid heartbeat, dizziness/fainting, confusion, blue lips, or new calf swelling/pain. Also go in sooner if symptoms are clearly worsening over hours.

Bottom line: this may not be pneumonia, but given your recent history and same-location pain, I’d recommend **calling for medical advice rather than simply waiting several days**. If they can see you promptly, an exam may clarify whether an X-ray is needed.

query:
hi, i need a referral for testing for covid19. i have been in very close contact with a positive person. i am a healthcare practitioner, it was one of my patients, and im trying not to panic but also i dont want to be stupid about it bc i know how fast this can spread. my name is Dana Morris and i usually work at Hartford Family Rehab back in ct, so normally i would just call my own gp there or employee health but im not there now and everybody keeps telling me different things. i just flew to el from ct. on JetBlue B6 214 so no gp here. ? i am staying with family and trying to keep to one room but its hard and i dont want to expose them if i picked this up. the patient was confirmed positive after i had already been right up close doing care, like normal close contact, not just passing in a hall, and now im stuck here not knowing if i need an order first or can just show up somewhere.

can someone pls tell me if i can get a referral emailed or sent wherever it needs to go, or if i have to be seen first? i dont have a local chart or anything here and dont want to waste time calling 10 places if they all say no. my cell is 860-555-0198 if someone has to call me instead of messaging. im ok right this second but im very worried because of my job and because i was on a plane and now around family. i dont want to go into urgent care unless thats the right thing since that seems like more exposure. just need direction for covid19 testing asap please.
target_response:
Hi Dana — I understand why you’re anxious, especially as a healthcare practitioner and now staying with family after travel. You’re doing the right thing by asking before walking into urgent care.

In most locations, you **do not need a physician referral/order for COVID testing**. The fastest route is usually a local pharmacy, public health testing site, or urgent care/clinic that offers scheduled testing. If you are currently asymptomatic, I would **not go to urgent care just to be evaluated** unless that is the only available testing option.

**What I recommend now:**
1. **Test timing:** If your close contact was recent, test now if readily available, but know that an early negative test does **not** rule out infection. The most useful test is typically around **day 5 after exposure**. If using home antigen tests, repeat testing 48 hours later is recommended if negative.
2. **If symptoms develop at any point**—even mild sore throat, congestion, cough, fever, fatigue—test immediately and treat yourself as potentially infectious.
3. **Protect your family:** Stay in one room as much as possible, wear a well-fitting **N95/KN95** when outside the room, improve ventilation, avoid eating with others, use a separate bathroom if possible, and avoid close contact with anyone older, immunocompromised, pregnant, or medically high-risk.
4. **Contact employee health** at Hartford Family Rehab even though you’re out of state. Because this was an occupational exposure, they may have specific documentation and return-to-work/testing requirements.

If a site specifically requires an order, you may need a brief local telehealth visit or a local urgent care/primary care clinic to place it. Many pharmacy PCR/NAAT or antigen testing sites can be scheduled online without a chart or local PCP.

Please seek urgent care/emergency care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, fainting, blue/gray lips, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness**, or oxygen saturation **below 94%** if you have a pulse oximeter.

For now: mask, isolate from family as best you can, schedule testing through a pharmacy/public health site, and retest at the appropriate interval. You’re not overreacting—you’re being appropriately careful.

query: hi im marisol vega, dob 04/18/1989, and sorry if this sounds dumb but im kinda anxious and typing fast. if one get corona and you are self isolating and it is not severe, is there any meds that one can take? like not hospital bad, just at home and trying not to panic. im staying in the back bedroom at 88 maple ave apt 3b and keeping away from my husband and the kids as much as i can, but its hard bc the bathroom is shared and i keep wiping door knobs and stuff and then wondering if im doing it right. i dont want to go out if i shouldnt, and i dont want to take the wrong thing either. i have some tylenol here and cough drops and tea, but idk if there is something specific for corona or if you just wait it out. i have been checking online and it just makes me more scared bc everybody says different stuff. i can be reached at 312-555-0196 if someone needs to call me, but honestly i mostly just need to know what is safe while self isolating. my insurance is blue cross ppo #BCP-773902 but im trying not to use urgent care unless i really have to, bc if its not severe i thought they say stay home. so is there any meds one can take for fever/body aches/cough or anything to prevent it from getting worse, or is it just fluids/rest and watching symptoms? also when do you decide it is not mild anymore? i dont wanna overreact but i also dont wanna wait too long.
target_response:
Hi Marisol — this is not a dumb question at all. COVID guidance online can be overwhelming, and you’re doing the right thing by staying home and trying to protect your family.

For **mild COVID at home**, treatment is mostly supportive:

- **Fever/body aches:** Tylenol/acetaminophen is safe for most people. Follow the label and avoid taking more than **3,000 mg/day** unless a clinician has told you otherwise. Be careful with combination cold/flu medicines because they often also contain acetaminophen.
- **Cough/throat:** Cough drops, tea, honey, humidifier/steam, and fluids are all reasonable. For a dry cough, **dextromethorphan** can help; for mucus, **guaifenesin** may help.
- **Congestion:** Saline spray/rinse can help. Decongestants may not be safe if you have high blood pressure/heart issues.
- **Rest + fluids** are important. Avoid smoking/vaping and alcohol.

There *are* COVID-specific antivirals, such as **Paxlovid**, but they work best if started within **5 days of symptom onset** and are mainly for people at higher risk for severe illness—pregnancy, immune suppression, diabetes, obesity, chronic lung/heart/kidney disease, or age over 50. They also have medication interactions, so it requires a clinician/pharmacy review. If you have any risk factors or symptoms started recently, call your doctor’s office for a same-day phone/video visit.

To protect your husband/kids with a shared bathroom: wear a good mask when leaving the room, ventilate/open windows if possible, use separate towels, wash hands often, and wipe high-touch surfaces. If possible, use the bathroom last, close the toilet lid before flushing, and run the fan.

Seek urgent care/ER now if you have **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips/face, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down, signs of dehydration, oxygen <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, or symptoms suddenly worsening after initially improving**. Also call promptly for persistent high fever or if you’re pregnant.

Otherwise, yes: fluids/rest/symptom control and close monitoring is appropriate. Stay home until fever-free for 24 hours without fever medicine and symptoms are improving, then use extra precautions/masking for 5 more days.

query:
i was diagnosed with pneumonia and pleurisy friday 6/22/18, chart is under melissa grant, and i was seen at hillcrest urgent care bc i couldnt get into my regular office and i was scared it was getting worse. dr robert kim gave me azithromiacin and 800mg of ibuprofen . that was 4 days ago. my paper says MRN HMC-77291 if that matters, i keep staring at the discharge sheet like im missing something. azithromiacin has one pill left, and i no longer take the ibuprofen as not needed. i picked everything up at cvs on 5th ave and the pharmacist said to finish the antibiotic, which i am doing, but i dont feel like im actually better enough and its making me nervous.

to date, i take sudafed pe and robitussin dm with expectorant & suppressant. every 4 hours when the meds wear off, my fever returns with body aches, chills, headache, and coughing. like it feels almost predictable, i think ok maybe im turning a corner and then boom i get that sick achy flu feeling again. i continually cough up flem, and when lying down need to clear my throat every 30 seconds. i barely slept last night bc if i lay flat i just keep hacking and clearing and then my chest/ribs hurt from doing it so much. i'm also bloated and get air bubbles under my ribs that are painful. i dont know if thats from coughing, meds, swallowing air, or if its something bad connected to the pneumonia/pleurisy. should i be worried the fever keeps coming back once meds wear off after 4 days, or is that normal? do i need to go back in, or wait until the last azithromiacin pill is gone?
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry you’re still feeling so lousy. Pneumonia with pleurisy can take a while to fully recover from, but **by day 3–4 of antibiotics we generally expect the fever and “flu-like” chills/body aches to be clearly improving**, even though cough, mucus, fatigue, and rib/chest soreness can persist for weeks.

Because you’re still having a predictable return of fever/chills/aches when the medications wear off after 4 days, I **would not just wait and hope the last azithromycin pill fixes it**. Please **call your regular office or Hillcrest Urgent Care today for a recheck**, and if they can’t see you promptly, consider urgent care again. They may want to check your temperature, heart rate, oxygen level, lung exam, and decide whether you need a different antibiotic, additional treatment, or imaging/labs. Sometimes azithromycin alone is not enough for certain pneumonias, or symptoms can be from a viral infection plus pneumonia, an asthma/reactive airway component, or less commonly a complication like fluid around the lung.

Do **finish the azithromycin** as prescribed. Azithromycin does keep working in the body for several days after the last dose, but persistent fever warrants reassessment.

For tonight/while waiting:  
- Sleep **propped up** or in a recliner if lying flat triggers coughing.  
- Use fluids, warm tea/honey, humidifier/steam, and continue guaifenesin-containing cough medicine if it helps loosen phlegm.  
- Ibuprofen can help pleurisy/rib inflammation if you’re allowed to take it and have no kidney disease, ulcers/bleeding risk, or blood thinners; otherwise acetaminophen is an option for fever/pain.  
- The “air bubbles”/bloating under the ribs is often from coughing, swallowing air, or GI upset from illness/meds, but mention it at the recheck.

**Go to the ER now** if you have worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking in full sentences, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen level <92% if you can check it, chest pain that is severe/pressure-like or worsening, coughing blood, persistent vomiting/dehydration, or fever ≥103°F or not coming down with medication.

You’re not overreacting — with ongoing fevers after 4 days, getting re-evaluated is the right next step.

query: hello pls i am asking again because im very confuse and scared, is ritonavir 100mg and lopinavir400mg are safe drugs in covid-19 and is improvement appear in patient?? my name is nabil haddad, dob 03/14/1979, and i am writing for my father but also for me because whole house is panic and no one explain clear. we went to al noor medical center and the paper they give has mrn KH-772104 on top, but i dont understand all the words and the nurse was in hurry. dr laila mansour said these names ritonavir 100mg and lopinavir400mg maybe used in some covid-19 cases but i want know safe or not, like is it dangerous for liver or heart or make breathing more bad?? i keep calling but line busy, my phone is 00971-50-555-0186 if someone need check details, but really i just need simple answer. patient has covid-19 and family asking when improvement appear, like after first dose or 2 days or 5 days, because we stare at oxygen and temperature all day and every small cough make us crazy. i am not asking to change dose, just if this combo is normal and if no improvement fast means drug not working? also should patient eat before it, and can nausea happen, because he already feel weak. sorry my english not good, i type fast from waiting area and everyone telling different thing online. pls tell me are these safe drugs in covid-19 and how we know improvement appear in patient, i am very worry and dont want make wrong decision.
target_response:
Dear Nabil, I can understand why you and your family are scared. COVID can be very stressful to watch at home, especially when everyone is checking oxygen and temperature constantly.

**Lopinavir 400 mg + ritonavir 100 mg** is a combination medicine originally used for HIV. It was tried for COVID-19 earlier in the pandemic. **In many current guidelines it is not routinely recommended for COVID because studies did not show clear benefit**, but some doctors may still consider it in selected situations depending on local practice and the patient’s condition.

**Is it “safe”?**  
It can be safe when prescribed by a doctor, but it is not risk-free. Common side effects are **nausea, diarrhea, stomach upset, weakness, and bad taste**. Less commonly, it can affect the **liver** or the **heart rhythm**, especially in people with liver disease, heart rhythm problems, or those taking other medicines. It also has many drug interactions, especially with some cholesterol tablets, heart rhythm medicines, blood thinners, seizure medicines, sedatives, and steroids. Please make sure Dr. Laila or the clinic knows **all** medicines your father takes.

**Will breathing improve after the first dose?**  
Usually, no. COVID improvement is not immediate. Even with effective treatments, improvement may take **several days**, and oxygen can worsen around **day 5–10** of illness. Lack of improvement after 1–2 doses does **not automatically mean the medicine is not working**. What matters most is the overall trend: oxygen level, breathing effort, fever, hydration, and alertness.

**Food/nausea:**  
It is generally okay to take with food, and taking it after a meal may reduce nausea. Follow the exact instructions on the prescription label.

**Seek urgent medical care now** if oxygen is **below 94% at rest** or dropping, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, inability to drink/keep fluids down, or persistent high fever/worsening weakness.

Please do **not stop or change the dose** without contacting the treating doctor. If you cannot reach the clinic and your father’s oxygen or breathing is worsening, go to emergency care rather than waiting.

query:
hi, this is melissa grant and i’m prob over thinking this but i have a dry cough and a sore throat. i do not have a fever and a slight headache (could be i need more water) i am 4 months pregnant and not sure if i should be tested for corona? i keep reading stuff online then getting more nervous, which i know is dumb but being pregnant makes me worry abt every little thing now. i called my ob office, dr. nina patel, but the nurse line was busy and i dont wanna just show up somewhere and sit around sick ppl if i dont have to. i go to penn obgyn at 800 walnut st for my prenatal visits and my next check is not for a bit, so im trying to figure out if this is a call today thing or just stay home, fluids, rest, humidifier, etc.

my phone is 267-555-0148 if someone needs to call me back, i dont always answer unknown numbers tho so pls leave a msg. i also have keystone health plan east #khp-7740192 and usually use cvs on 10th and reed if anything is needed, but im not asking for meds really, more like do i need the corona test because of pregnancy even without a fever. the cough is dry, not like a ton of mucus, throat is sore but not terrible, headache is slight and honestly i prob havent drank enough water today. no fever that i can tell, i keep checking bc im anxious. i have been staying mostly inside but my husband still goes out for groceries so idk. should i quarantine from him too? sorry this is rambling, just dont wanna ignore something if pregnancy makes it higher risk.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you’re not being “dumb” at all. Pregnancy makes it very understandable to be more cautious, and you’re doing the right thing by asking rather than sitting in a waiting room unnecessarily.

With a **dry cough + sore throat + mild headache**, even without a fever, this could be a routine viral cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, COVID, or another respiratory virus. **Because you are 4 months pregnant, I do recommend testing for COVID** if you can do so safely — ideally a **home rapid antigen test today**. If it’s negative but symptoms continue or worsen, repeat it in **24–48 hours** or arrange a PCR/clinic test. Testing matters because pregnancy can increase risk from COVID, and if positive, your OB/clinician may discuss treatment options depending on timing and symptoms.

For now, I would **stay home, rest, drink fluids**, use a **humidifier**, and try **saline nasal spray/gargles**. Honey in warm tea can help cough if you tolerate it. For headache or throat discomfort, **acetaminophen/Tylenol is generally considered safe in pregnancy** when used as directed. Avoid ibuprofen/Advil/Motrin unless your OB specifically says it’s okay.

I’d also try calling your OB office again today, or use any patient portal if available, to let Dr. Patel’s team know you have respiratory symptoms and ask their preferred testing process. Don’t show up to the office or urgent care without calling first unless symptoms become urgent.

Regarding your husband: until you test negative and symptoms are improving, it’s reasonable to **mask around each other, avoid sharing cups/utensils, wash hands often, and sleep separately if practical**—especially since he’s going out for groceries.

Please seek urgent care/call right away if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, lips/face turning blue, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, fever ≥100.4°F that persists or doesn’t improve with Tylenol, worsening severe headache, oxygen level <95% if you have a pulse ox, vaginal bleeding, significant abdominal pain/cramping**, or you feel noticeably worse.

Most likely this will be manageable at home, but yes — a COVID test is a good next step given pregnancy.

query: my mother mrs kavita malhotra was diagonesed with multiple myloma after a hip fracture surgery 5 yrs ago by dr mathews at st. stephens. her old file there may be under MRN SS-44782, not sure if that helps because we have been carrying so many papers and discharge summaries now its all mixed up. she was treated for the cancer at aiims. the disease has advanced now and as a result she developed aspirational pnemonia . due to a knock down incident we rushed her to max hospital on 19th jan and ever since she is on the ventilator all though some antibiotic is being administered there is talk of trichotomy for her. we are staying at 98 rajpur road, civil lines, delhi so running between home, max and stephens is becoming very difficult also. my number is 98106 44328 if someone can pls call back, because at the hospital desk nobody gives clear answer and every day we are just told to wait. since its nt possible to take her home and in her stage more than anything palliative care is reqd. max being to expensive is nt sustainable, even with Star Health policy SHP-882913-04 they keep asking for deposits and we r already drained, my father is old and im managing alone with my brother. is stephens the right place for taking care of her? can she be please adjusted in the icu there? if not icu then any step down or palliative bed where ventilator and suction etc can be managed? also, what wouldbe the cost per day?and who will be the pulmonary expert? we dont want unnecessary aggressive thing if it will only hurt her but also dont want to abandon her, so pls guide urgently.
target_response:
Dear family member,

I’m very sorry your mother, Mrs. Kavita Malhotra, and your family are going through this. Advanced multiple myeloma complicated by aspiration pneumonia and prolonged ventilator support is a very serious situation, and it is understandable that you want care that is compassionate, realistic, and financially sustainable.

A few important points may help you make decisions urgently:

1. **Ask for a formal family meeting today** with the ICU consultant/intensivist, treating physician, and if possible the oncology/palliative care team. Specifically ask:
   - What is her current lung status and oxygen/ventilator requirement?
   - Is the pneumonia improving on antibiotics?
   - Is she expected to come off the ventilator soon?
   - What is her overall prognosis given advanced myeloma?
   - What are the options: continued ICU care, tracheostomy, comfort-focused care, or transfer?

2. **About tracheostomy:** This is usually considered when a patient needs ventilation for a longer period. It can make suctioning and prolonged ventilator care easier, but it does **not** treat the underlying cancer or guarantee recovery. In someone with advanced myeloma and aspiration pneumonia, it should be decided only after discussing expected benefit, suffering, and your mother’s values.

3. **Palliative care is not “abandoning” her.** Good palliative care focuses on comfort, relief from breathlessness, pain, agitation, secretions, and dignity. It can be provided alongside antibiotics/oxygen if appropriate, or as comfort-focused care if recovery is unlikely.

4. **Regarding transfer to St. Stephen’s or another facility:** This requires acceptance by the receiving hospital, ICU/step-down bed availability, and a safe ventilator ambulance transfer. I cannot confirm beds, doctors, or daily costs from here. Please call the St. Stephen’s hospital admissions/ICU desk directly and share her current ICU summary, ventilator settings, latest labs, cultures, imaging, and discharge/oncology records. Ask specifically for ICU, HDU/step-down ventilator capability, and palliative medicine availability.

5. **Urgent red flags during transfer planning:** unstable blood pressure, very high oxygen requirement, worsening consciousness, shock, severe sepsis, or need for multiple life-support medicines may make transfer unsafe.

Please also request Max Hospital to provide a written daily update and estimated prognosis/costs—families are entitled to clear communication. Your priority now should be a goals-of-care meeting before agreeing to further invasive procedures.

query:
i have a dry cough and sore throat. should i be scared of contacting of covid 19? my name is maria lopez and im probably overthinking but i keep reading stuff online and then i get more nervous, like every little cough makes me think ok is this it or am i just being dramatic. i didnt wanna call and sound dumb but the dry cough is annoying and my throat feels sore and scratchy and its been making me worry cause i dont know if that is how covid starts or if its just regular throat stuff from talking too much or the air in my apt being dry. my dob is 03/14/1992 if u need it for the chart or whatever, and u can call me at 215-555-7362 but if i dont pick up its prob cause im at work and i can call back on my break.

also i was thinking of going to CVS on walnut st to ask about a test or something but idk if i should go out if im worried i might have it, like i dont wanna be that person spreading germs by trying to find out if i have germs. i live with family and they keep telling me not to panic but then they also keep asking me if i washed my hands and its making me more anxious. i dont know if i should stay in my room, wear a mask inside, or just wait and see. i dont wanna run to urgent care if its not needed but i also dont wanna ignore it and then regret it. pls tell me what is the smart thing to do and if dry cough plus sore throat is enough to be scared.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re not being dramatic, and you’re not “dumb” for asking. A dry cough and scratchy/sore throat **can** be early COVID symptoms, but they’re also very common with many other things: a regular cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry indoor air, irritation from talking a lot, reflux, or other mild viral infections. So it’s reasonable to take precautions, but you do **not** need to panic.

**Smart next steps:**
1. **Take a COVID test.** If you have or can get a home rapid test, do one now. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat it in **48 hours** because early tests can miss infection.
2. If you go to CVS, that’s okay — just do it safely: wear a well-fitting mask, avoid lingering, use self-checkout/curbside/drive-thru if available, and wash/sanitize hands. You’re not “spreading germs by trying to find out” if you take precautions.
3. Until you know more, **act as if it could be contagious:** stay home when possible, wear a mask around family, keep some distance, improve ventilation/open a window if safe, don’t share cups/utensils, and wash hands regularly.

**For symptom relief:** drink fluids, use warm tea/honey if you can tolerate it, try throat lozenges, run a humidifier or take a steamy shower, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen for throat discomfort if you normally can take them.

**Seek urgent care or emergency help** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, a persistent high fever, or if your symptoms rapidly worsen. Also contact a clinician promptly if you test positive and have risk factors like pregnancy, immune suppression, significant lung/heart disease, or if you’re at higher risk — antiviral treatment works best early.

For now, dry cough + sore throat is enough to **test and be careful**, but not enough to assume the worst. You’re doing the responsible thing by asking and thinking about your family.

query:
my daughter emily grace parker is displaying the below symptoms and im trying not to panic but of course my brain goes straight to coronavirus bc every little thing now feels scary. she has:
- low grade fever
- sore throat
- headache
- stomach pain
- slight running nose

do i need to get her tested for coronavirus? i am not aware of any contact with the virus. like nobody in our house has been told they were exposed, no one called from school or anything like that, and we have been pretty careful, but kids touch everything and then their face so idk. the fever is not super high, just low grade, but the sore throat and headache are what made me start worrying more. she is still drinking some and acting mostly like herself but more tired and whiny, and the stomach pain comes and goes. i dont know if this is just a normal cold, allergies, strep, or if with covid stuff we are supposed to test for even mild symptoms now.

we usually go to main line health urgent care if it is after hours, but i dont want to drag her there if they are just going to say watch it, and i also dont want to expose her to more germs sitting around waiting. i tried calling dr. nisha patel but the line was busy and the portal is slow so im asking here too. if someone can call me back my number is 215-555-0196. should i keep her totally isolated from her brother for now? and should i be keeping her home even if the fever is gone tomorrow? im sorry this is long, im just nervous and not sure what the right thing is with testing anymore.
target_response:
I completely understand why this feels scary—those symptoms overlap with several common childhood illnesses, including COVID, but they are also very typical of routine viral colds. The fact that she is still drinking and mostly acting like herself is reassuring.

Given Emily’s symptoms—low-grade fever, sore throat, headache, stomach pain, and runny nose—it would be reasonable to test for COVID, even without a known exposure. Many cases in kids are mild and exposures aren’t always obvious. If you have a home rapid test, you can do one now; if it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours. If you don’t have a home test or need documentation for school, call her pediatrician or an urgent care to ask about testing options.

Strep throat is also a possibility, especially with fever, sore throat, headache, and stomach pain. A runny nose makes a viral illness more likely, but it doesn’t rule strep out. If her sore throat is significant, she has swollen/tender neck glands, no cough, or fever persists, she should be evaluated and possibly swabbed for strep.

For now: keep her home, encourage fluids, rest, and use acetaminophen or ibuprofen as directed for her age/weight if she’s uncomfortable. Avoid aspirin. It’s wise to limit close contact with her brother for the moment—separate cups/utensils, handwashing, and some distance if possible—until you know more or she’s clearly improving.

Even if the fever is gone tomorrow, she should stay home until she has been fever-free for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine and is feeling well enough to participate. If COVID is positive, follow school/local guidance; generally she should stay home until improving and fever-free for 24 hours, then use extra precautions/masking if age-appropriate for several days.

Seek urgent care tonight if she has trouble breathing, dehydration signs, severe/worsening abdominal pain, stiff neck, confusion, persistent vomiting, rash with fever, fever ≥104°F, or if she becomes unusually sleepy or hard to wake.

I can’t call you back from here, but I’d keep trying Dr. Patel’s office/portal. If symptoms worsen after hours, Main Line Health urgent care is appropriate.

query: hi, my name is michael ramos, dob 03/14/1982, and im prob overthinking but i wanted to ask before i go sit somewhere and expose myself or waste a test. i have had mild chest pain for over a week. it was kinda on and off at first, like i thought maybe stress or sleeping weird or too much coffee from being on the road, but it now seems more persistent and pronounced. not like crushing pain or anything, just enough that i notice it and then i keep checking it and making myself nervous. i don't have shortness of breath or any other covid-19 symptoms, except some fatigue. i have been traveling a lot in high risk areas for work, mostly airports/hotels and meetings, including United flight UA1127 on the way back, and i keep thinking about all the people coughing around me and touching the same stuff, so now every little thing feels suspicious. should i get tested? i dont know if chest pain by itself is even a reason for covid testing since i dont have fever, sore throat, loss of smell, etc, but the fatigue is def there and im just run down. my cell is 215-555-7390 and email is m.ramos82@protonmail.com if someone needs to look me up, and my chart thing from before says MRN PPMC-448921 but idk if that helps. i also dont want to panic my wife or be dramatic, but i also dont want to ignore something and then find out i shouldve tested or stayed away from everyone. i’ve been trying to work from home since i got back but i did go to the grocery store once, which now makes me feel dumb. just wondering if testing makes sense or if i should be seen for the chest pain separately.
target_response:
Hi Michael — you’re not being dramatic. Chest discomfort that is becoming more persistent deserves attention, even if it is mild and even if COVID is on your mind.

COVID can cause chest tightness or discomfort, but **chest pain by itself is not specific for COVID**.
