Fatigue after heavy travel, stress, poor sleep, dehydration, excess caffeine, reflux, muscle strain, or anxiety can all contribute. That said, because you’ve had **over a week of chest pain that now feels more pronounced**, I would treat the chest pain as its own issue and not assume it’s only viral or stress-related.

**What I’d recommend:**
1. **Arrange a same-day medical evaluation** through your primary care office, an urgent care, or a clinician who can assess you. They may want to check vitals, oxygen level, listen to your lungs/heart, and consider an **EKG** and possibly blood work depending on your risk factors and symptoms.
2. **COVID testing is reasonable** given your recent travel through airports/hotels/meetings and fatigue. If available, take a home rapid test now and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative, or get a PCR/NAAT test if you need a more reliable result.
3. Until you have more clarity, it’s sensible to **limit close contact**, wear a well-fitting mask around others, and avoid unnecessary errands—especially around higher-risk people. One grocery trip does not mean you “messed up”; just be cautious going forward.

**Go to the ER now / call emergency services** if the chest pain becomes severe, crushing, or pressure-like; spreads to your arm, jaw, back, or neck; occurs with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, dizziness, fainting, palpitations, coughing blood, new leg swelling, or if you feel suddenly worse. Also seek urgent care if your oxygen level is low if you have a pulse oximeter.

Bottom line: **yes, testing makes sense**, but don’t let the COVID question distract from getting the chest pain evaluated—especially since it’s persistent and changing. If you currently have chest discomfort while reading this, I’d lean toward being seen today rather than waiting.

query: hi this is melissa grant and sorry if this is a dumb question but im confused and kinda spiraling after googling too much. is the coronavirus 229e the same as covid-19? like i know they both say coronavirus so my brain is going straight to covid and then i start thinking i have to cancel everything and warn everyone and all that. i was looking at some old paperwork from a visit and it had coronavirus 229e on it and then when i search online every page starts talking about covid-19 and sars and pandemics and im like ok wait is this the exact same thing or just same family or what. dr. nina patel had mentioned before not to trust random google stuff but of course i did anyway at 1am lol. i dont want to call and sound ridiculous if its obvious, but i also dont want to ignore something if it means covid-19. i picked up meds at CVS on 5th ave earlier and even the pharmacist was busy so i didnt wanna hold up the line asking a million questions. my email is melgrant82@gmail.com if anyone needs to send info but honestly i just need plain english please. like if a test says coronavirus 229e does that automatically mean covid-19 positive or no? and if it isnt the same, why do they name it so similar?? i swear this stuff is so confusing for normal ppl. im not trying to make a big deal, im just anxious bc the word coronavirus still freaks me out and i dont know if i should be treating it like covid-19 rules or more like a regular cold thing. sorry this is rambling, i just need someone to explain it without all the fancy terms.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a dumb question at all. The naming is genuinely confusing, and I can see why seeing “coronavirus 229E” on paperwork would make your brain jump to COVID.

Plain English answer: **Coronavirus 229E is *not* the same as COVID-19.** A test that says **coronavirus 229E** does **not automatically mean you are COVID-19 positive**.

“Coronavirus” is the name of a **large family of viruses**. Some coronaviruses cause common colds, and others cause more serious illnesses. **229E** is one of the older, common “seasonal” coronaviruses that has circulated for many years and usually causes cold-like symptoms. **COVID-19 is caused by a different coronavirus called SARS-CoV-2.** They are in the same general family — more like “cousins” — but they are not the same virus.

They’re named similarly because under a microscope coronaviruses have a crown-like appearance, and “corona” means crown. Unfortunately, after the pandemic, the word “coronavirus” became strongly associated with COVID, even though it includes several non-COVID cold viruses.

If this was on **old paperwork**, and you are not currently sick, you do not need to cancel plans, warn everyone, or treat it like a new COVID diagnosis. If you *are* currently having cold symptoms, it is reasonable to act like you have a contagious respiratory virus: rest, fluids, wash hands, consider masking around others, and stay home if you have a fever or feel significantly ill. If you have symptoms or a known COVID exposure, taking a home COVID test can help clarify things.

Seek urgent care if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, severe dehydration, persistent high fever, or if you are immunocompromised/pregnant and symptoms are worsening.

Also, for your privacy, be cautious about sharing your email in messages like this. But medically: **229E ≠ COVID-19**, and Dr. Patel’s advice about not trusting late-night Google spirals was very reasonable. You’re not being ridiculous — the terminology really is confusing.

query: hi, this is melissa grant, dob 03/14/1992, i know this is prob a dumb question but im confused and kinda anxious and i keep googling and making it worse. how do you get hpv on the cervix? is it from skin to skin contact during intercourse from a partner’s genital touching the cervix? thank you. like i get that hpv is sexually transmitted but i dont understand the actual way it gets up there or onto the cervix, bc the cervix is inside and not like outer skin, so im sitting here wondering if it has to be direct rubbing/touching during sex or if it can just be from fluids or hands or oral or whatever. i had a pap recently and they said hpv showed up and now im spiraling bc ive only been with one partner for a while and i dont know if this means someone cheated or if it can be from years ago and just sitting there. dr. priya nanda told me not to panic and that its common but of course i am panicking anyway. i also feel embarrassed asking bc i should prob understand this by now but i really dont. i dont have pain or anything new that i can tell, im just scared by the word cervix and hpv together. i picked up the paperwork after work and then just sat in my car reading it over and over. my pharmacy is cvs on maple ave if that matters for anything later but right now im mostly just trying to understand the basic contact part, like partner’s genital touching the cervix during intercourse = how hpv gets there? or is that not how to think about it? sorry this is long, im just really nervous and trying not to freak out before my follow up, my chart also has MRN GYN-78214 on the top if needed.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — this is absolutely **not** a dumb question. HPV is confusing, and it’s very common to feel anxious when you see “HPV” and “cervix” on a report.

HPV gets to the cervix mainly through **genital skin/mucosal contact during sexual activity**, especially vaginal intercourse. It’s not usually helpful to picture it as only “a partner’s genitals directly rubbing the cervix,” although that can be part of exposure during sex. The cervix is inside the vagina, but during intercourse it is exposed to genital skin cells/secretions and friction. HPV infects the surface cells of the cervix—especially an area called the transformation zone—often through tiny microscopic breaks in the tissue that you would never feel.

HPV is **not primarily a “fluid” infection** like some STIs, but virus can be present on skin/mucosal cells and genital secretions. Hands, fingers, or sex toys could theoretically transfer HPV if they contact infected genital skin and then the vagina/cervix, but the most common route is genital-to-genital sexual contact. Oral sex is more associated with oral/throat HPV exposure than cervical HPV, though indirect transfer is possible.

Importantly: a positive HPV test **does not mean your partner cheated**. HPV can be acquired years earlier and remain quiet, undetectable, or intermittently detectable. It can show up later due to normal immune fluctuations. Many people never know they have it, and there is no routine HPV test for men.

Dr. Nanda is right: HPV is extremely common, and in most people your age it clears or becomes inactive on its own. The next step depends on the details of the Pap: whether the cells were normal or abnormal, and whether HPV 16/18 was detected. Your follow-up may simply be repeat testing, or sometimes a colposcopy to look more closely.

For now: keep the follow-up appointment, avoid smoking if applicable, consider condoms to reduce spread/re-exposure, and ask about the HPV vaccine if you haven’t had it.

Seek urgent care if you develop heavy vaginal bleeding, severe pelvic pain, fever, foul-smelling discharge, or bleeding after sex that is persistent. Otherwise, this is usually not an emergency—just something to follow carefully.

query:
my husband robert allen miller passed away in march, on 03/14/2024, after a short illness his doctor said was bronchitis then pneumonia. i keep going over it in my head and i know that probly dont help but i cant stop. dr. kendra patel had told us at first it sounded like bronchitis, then pneumonia, and we were trying to do what we were told, meds and rest and all that, but he just kept getting weaker and more tired. then he was diagnosed wit small cell lung cancer after an ex-ray revealed a mass in his lung, and that was at st. marys hospital in scranton pa, his paper work had MRN smh-772940 on it if that matters. it all just felt so fast, like one min we were talking about cough meds and the next its cancer and hospice talk and i still dont really understand how that can happen so quick.

he was a 22 year veteran in the army. he was proud of that, even when he didnt talk much about it. he also was a smoker, yes, and i know everybody says smoking causes lung cancer and maybe thats the answer, but i keep wondering if it was only that or if army stuff could have had anything to do with it too, like dust, fumes, burn pits, chemicals, i dont know. can you tell me hat causes small cell lung cancer? he was 66. i guess im asking bc i feel guilty too, like should i have pushed harder when they said bronchitis, should i have demanded more tests sooner, could an ex-ray earlier have found it before it got bad. sorry this is long i just need someone to explain it plain bc all the words from the hospital are still running thru my head.
pii_units: st. marys hospital in scranton pa
target_response:
I’m very sorry for the loss of your husband, Robert. What you’re describing—going back over each step and wondering “what if”—is very common after a sudden, shocking illness, but it can be incredibly painful.

Small cell lung cancer is a fast-growing type of lung cancer. It often starts near the larger airways and can cause cough, shortness of breath, fatigue, or pneumonia-like symptoms. Sometimes a tumor blocks part of the airway, leading to “post-obstructive pneumonia,” so it can genuinely look like bronchitis or pneumonia at first. Small cell can also spread early, so it may feel like things went from routine infection treatment to hospice discussions very quickly.

The strongest known risk factor is cigarette smoking. Most cases of small cell lung cancer occur in people with a significant smoking history. That said, smoking is not the only possible contributor. Other exposures can increase lung cancer risk, including radon, asbestos, diesel exhaust, silica dust, certain metals/chemicals, radiation, and heavy air pollution. Some military-related exposures—dust, fumes, burn pits, vehicle exhaust, industrial chemicals—may also contribute to respiratory disease and cancer risk. In one individual person, it’s often impossible to say with certainty, “this one thing caused it,” but his Army service is relevant history.

About whether you “should have pushed harder”: based on what you wrote, you were following medical advice for symptoms that commonly are bronchitis or pneumonia. Even when an X-ray is done earlier, small cell lung cancer can be hard to catch early, and it can progress very quickly. Please try not to place the blame on yourself.

Practical next steps: you can request his hospital records, imaging reports, pathology report, oncology notes, and discharge/hospice summaries from St. Mary’s. Ask his primary doctor or oncologist to review the timeline with you in plain language. Because he was a 22-year Army veteran, consider contacting the VA or an accredited Veterans Service Officer about possible survivor benefits or service-connection review, especially under exposure-related programs.

If your grief is causing panic, inability to sleep/eat, or any thoughts of harming yourself, please seek urgent help—call 988 or go to an ER. You deserve support while you process this.

query: hi doctor,pls help me as my 67 year old mother Meera Khanna is critical due to tuberclosis and pneumonia .her right lung is almost destroyed due to tb..this is a 3rd attack of the disease in last 14yrs.she is having difficulty breathing without oxygen and her oxygen levels are pretty low without support..they dropped to 50% at one time when she was off it and i just cannot get that number out of my head, i was standing there and she was gasping and everyone was running. she is admitted at Apollo Hospital Delhi under Dr. Rakesh Malhotra and the file says MRN APD-774219, they keep saying wait and watch and oxygen and antibiotics but i feel like we r losing time. pls tell me if she is a candidate of lung transplant and what are her chances.urgently help..i live in dubai but right now i am in delhi to take care of it..i came on Emirates EK516 and have not gone back because i am scared if i leave she will get worse. i am told lung transplant isnt an option for her also it is not done in india..we r ready to come any where...usa uk singapore anywhere if there is a real chance, we can arrange papers somehow and my brother is trying also but nobody is giving clear answer. she is on oxygen almost all the time and without it she becomes breathless very fast, and i dont know if tb damage means no transplant or if pneumonia makes it impossible right now. pls if any doctor can just guide me what reports to send, ct scan, xray, culture, whatever, i can send from my email nisha.khanna78@gmail.com or you can call/whatsapp me on +971-50-438-2196 anytime even night. pls help distressed daughter
target_response:
Dear Nisha, I’m very sorry you and your mother are going through this. Seeing an oxygen saturation fall to 50% and watching her gasp is frightening; that number means she was in **severe respiratory distress** at that moment. The most important thing right now is that she remains in a setting where oxygen, ICU support, and rapid escalation are available.

A lung transplant is sometimes considered for end-stage lung disease, including severe post-TB lung destruction, **but several factors matter**: whether TB is active or fully treated, whether pneumonia/sepsis is present, the condition of the other lung, heart/kidney/liver function, nutrition/strength, drug-resistant TB status, and overall ability to survive major surgery. **Active infection—especially active TB or uncontrolled pneumonia—is usually a temporary contraindication** to transplant until controlled. Age 67 is not an absolute “no,” but it is a higher-risk age group and centers assess very carefully.

It is not correct that lung transplant is never done in India; several Indian centers do lung transplants, though candidacy and timing are strict. However, transferring her abroad while oxygen-dependent and unstable may be dangerous unless arranged as a formal medical evacuation after a receiving transplant/ICU team accepts her.

What I would ask her current team urgently today:
1. Is she in ICU or does she need ICU/HFNC/NIV support?  
2. What is her ABG result—oxygen and CO₂ levels?  
3. Is current TB active? Are sputum/CBNAAT/GeneXpert, AFB smear/culture, and drug-resistance tests done?  
4. What organism is causing pneumonia? Blood/sputum cultures?  
5. CT chest findings—how much disease in left lung?  
6. Has a transplant pulmonologist/thoracic transplant center reviewed her case?

Reports useful for a second opinion: CT chest images/report, latest X-rays, ABG, oxygen requirement details, sputum AFB/CBNAAT/cultures, antibiotic/anti-TB medicines, CBC, kidney/liver tests, ECG/echo, and discharge summaries from prior TB episodes.

Please do not share personal phone/email/MRN publicly. Red flags requiring immediate escalation: worsening drowsiness/confusion, bluish lips, severe chest pain, very fast breathing, low BP, SpO₂ not improving despite oxygen, or reduced urine.

Stay with the treating ICU/pulmonary team and request a formal transplant-center opinion now.

query: hello dr pls reply, my son aarav mehta is 3.3 yrs old , dob is 03/05/2023 , he is allergic to dust and weather conditions from small age only and whenever season change he starts coughing and nose block, so i am very scared now. we went to rainbow children hospital yesterday evening and dr neha kulkarni saw him, his file no / mrn is RCH-77291 and on insurance they wrote star health policy SHP-4482071 but still i am not sure what to do because so many medicines are written. in the recent x ray report it has been detected that he is having a little congestion in heart which is a symptom of pneumonia and his wbc count is 39000 ( normal range is 4k-11k) doctors have suggested to give ibugesic paracetamol 7.5 ml in case if fever ( he got fits during fever tht lasted few seconds) , frizium for 3 days ,s- mucolite 5ml , duolin nebuliser and refzil 5ml for infection .is all these medicines are safe ? i asked there also but in tension i could not understand properly and now at home i keep checking him again and again like his breathing, fever, lips color everything. he is playing little bit then again getting dull and not eating proper, only taking few sips water and milk, so i am worrying if refzil and nebuliser all together is too much for his age. the fever fit was only few seconds but i got very scared, he became stiff and eyes went up little then came back, after that he was sleepy. pls tell me these medicines can be given together or should i wait between them, and wbc 39000 means very serious or infection can cause this much also?
target_response:
Hello, I can understand how frightening this is—especially seeing a fever-related fit. I’ll explain clearly.

A WBC count of **39,000 is quite high**. It can happen with a significant infection like pneumonia, severe chest infection, or sometimes due to stress after a seizure, but it **needs close monitoring and follow-up**, especially since Aarav is dull at times and not drinking well. Please **call Rainbow/Dr. Neha’s team today** and ask whether they want a recheck, admission, or repeat blood tests depending on his condition.

About the medicines: the combination prescribed is commonly used in children when there is fever/seizure risk plus chest infection/wheezing.

- **Refzil** is an antibiotic—give exactly as prescribed and complete the course unless the doctor changes it.  
- **Duolin nebuliser** helps open the airways; it is generally safe but may cause temporary fast heartbeat, shakiness, or restlessness.  
- **S-Mucolite** helps loosen mucus.  
- **Frizium** is often given for a few days after a febrile seizure to reduce recurrence risk; it may make him sleepy.  
- For fever medicine, please be careful: **Ibugesic and paracetamol are different medicines** depending on the formulation. Check the bottle label. Do not overdose. If it is paracetamol, give only as prescribed. If it is ibuprofen, avoid it if he is dehydrated, vomiting repeatedly, or not passing urine well. Since you were told 7.5 ml, confirm the exact bottle name/concentration with the hospital/pharmacy.

These medicines do **not require long gaps** between them. You can give them around the prescribed times; if he vomits easily, space oral medicines by 10–15 minutes.

For the fever fit: a few seconds with stiffness/eyes rolling followed by sleepiness sounds like a **febrile seizure**, which is scary but often not dangerous. If it happens again, place him on his side, don’t put anything in his mouth, and time it.

Go to emergency urgently if: breathing fast, chest pulling in, blue lips, persistent drowsiness/unresponsiveness, seizure >5 minutes or repeated seizures, fever not settling, no urine for 6–8 hours, refusing fluids, or worsening dullness. Given his poor intake and high WBC, I would keep a low threshold for reassessment today.

query:
hi, i am lena rodriguez, sorry if this sounds confused but i am getting nervous and dont know what is the right thing to do. i have scratchy throat, phlegm, loss of appetite, fatigue. not severe severe but enough that i keep thinking about it and checking myself every hour. i am pharmacist in research facility, morris-klein research center, mostly in the lab/med room side and not seeing patients but i am still around people and shared spaces, counters, fridges, door handles etc. i have been trying to wipe everything and wear mask but now that i feel off i am worried i should not even be there. my son had 3rd contact. he has flu symptoms. he was quarantined. we are waiting for the results for his contact. that is the part making me panic bc its like not direct contact maybe, but still too close for comfort and now i have symptoms too, even if they could be normal cold or stress or allergies or whatever.

my appetite is just gone and i feel tired like heavy tired, and throat feels scratchy with some phlegm, no idea if that counts as enough to test. i dont want to overreact and take a test away from someone who needs it more, but also i dont want to be the person walking around a research facility sick and then find out later i should have stayed home. i can be reached at lena.r.rx77@gmail.com if needed but really just trying to know what the practical answer is. do i need to test myself? should i quarantine also while waiting for his contact result or only if his contact is positive? and should i tell work now or wait until something is confirmed?
target_response:
Hi Lena — you’re not overreacting. With **scratchy throat, phlegm, loss of appetite, and significant fatigue**, plus a possible exposure chain through your son, the practical and safest answer is: **treat this as a potentially contagious respiratory illness until proven otherwise.**

Because you work around shared lab/med-room spaces, counters, fridges, and door handles, I would recommend:

1. **Do not go into work right now if you can avoid it.**  
   Even if this turns out to be a cold, flu, COVID, allergies, or stress-related, you are symptomatic and around shared work areas. Staying home is the responsible choice.

2. **Test yourself.**  
   You are not “taking a test away” from someone by testing with symptoms and a possible exposure. If available, take a **COVID rapid antigen test now**. If negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**, or get a **PCR/NAAT** if your workplace requires higher certainty. Depending on what’s circulating locally, consider **flu testing** too, especially if fever, body aches, or worsening fatigue develop.

3. **Tell work now — don’t wait for confirmation.**  
   You can simply say: “I have respiratory symptoms and a possible exposure chain at home; I’m staying home and testing. I’ll update Occupational Health/supervisor with results.” That protects others and shows good judgment.

4. **Regarding quarantine:**  
   Since *you* have symptoms, focus less on the “third contact” label and more on illness precautions. Stay home until you are clearly improving and fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers. After returning, wear a well-fitting mask and be extra careful with hand hygiene and shared surfaces for several days, per your workplace policy.

For now: rest, hydrate, use saline gargles/sprays, honey if you tolerate it, and acetaminophen/ibuprofen as appropriate. Monitor temperature and symptoms.

Please seek urgent care promptly if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, fainting, dehydration, persistent high fever, severe weakness, or symptoms rapidly worsening**.

Bottom line: **stay home, test, notify work, and repeat testing if initially negative.** Your caution is appropriate, especially given your workplace setting.

query: hi this is maria lopez, i know this prob sounds dumb but i have mild irritation in my chest but i am not coughing, i just feel a tingling sensation on my thought. i have no fever, no aches should i be worried about covid 19? its not like real chest pain or like i cant breath, its more like a weird scratchy/tingly thing and then i start thinking about it too much and it makes me nervous. i called dr kevin patel before for stuff like allergies but i dont wanna be that person calling for every little thing, esp right now with covid and everyone being busy. i havent been around anyone that i know is positive, but i did go to the store and i touched carts and stuff and now im replaying every little thing in my head. i also get nervous because sometimes when the air is dry or i drink cold stuff my throat feels weird, but this feels like its kinda in my upper chest too, mild irritation only, no coughing still. no fever when i checked it twice, no body aches, no chills, no runny nose really. i picked up cough drops at cvs on walnut st but i havent needed them bc im not coughing, i just thought maybe it would help the tingling. should i isolate just in case or wait and see if fever/cough shows up? like do i need a test if the only thing is this tiny chest irritation and tingling in my thought? i dont wanna panic but also dont wanna ignore it and make someone else sick. sorry for rambling im just anxious and google makes everything sound scary.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this does **not** sound dumb at all. It’s very reasonable to ask, especially when COVID is on your mind and you’re trying to be careful around others.

Based on what you described — mild scratchy/tingly throat sensation with a little upper-chest irritation, **no fever**, **no cough**, **no shortness of breath**, no body aches/chills, and no known exposure — COVID is **possible but not the most likely explanation**. Dry air, allergies/post-nasal drip, throat irritation from cold drinks, mild reflux, or even anxiety making you focus on normal sensations can all cause this kind of feeling.

That said, because COVID and other respiratory viruses can start mildly, the safest practical approach is:

1. **Monitor symptoms for the next 24–48 hours.**  
   Check your temperature once or twice daily, but try not to repeatedly check if it increases anxiety.

2. **Consider taking a home COVID test**, especially if you’ll be around others, work with vulnerable people, or want peace of mind.  
   If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 48 hours.

3. **Limit close contact until you know what’s happening.**  
   You don’t necessarily need strict isolation if symptoms are very mild and no fever, but it’s wise to stay home if possible, avoid visiting high-risk people, and wear a mask around others until symptoms improve and/or testing is negative.

4. **Supportive care:** fluids, warm tea/honey, humidifier/steam, saline nasal spray, avoiding very cold drinks, and cough drops/lozenges are fine even if you’re not coughing. If you have allergy history, an over-the-counter antihistamine may help if you’ve used one safely before.

Also, try to reassure yourself: touching carts is a much less common way to get COVID than being near someone contagious indoors. Handwashing is enough after stores.

Please seek urgent care right away if you develop **true chest pain/pressure**, trouble breathing, wheezing, blue lips, fainting, confusion, coughing blood, or a high/persistent fever. Call Dr. Patel’s office if symptoms worsen, last more than a few days, or you develop cough/fever.

You’re not “being that person” — you’re being thoughtful and responsible.

query: hi, this is melissa grant, dob 03/14/1982, i’m the one with MRN RCH-77291 if that helps anyone look me up. hi,got pneumonia,1month ago.had hemoptasis,positive blood culture x1.have been treated with erythromycin x5days,clindamycin x10 xays,then levaquin,x10days,and prednisone.i have been thru with medications for 5dags,but have had low grade temp(99) a couple of evenings since.i still feel horrible,but my np hasn t done anything since and i feel like im just sitting here waiting for something bad to happen. like i know 99 isnt super high but it keeps coming back at night and i get all shaky and sweaty and then i start thinking about the blood culture and the coughing blood thing and it scares me. i did contact my pulmanologist today, dr. evan patel, and waiting for response, but you know how it is, messages sit there and i dont know if they even saw it. all my meds were filled at rite aid on 5th ave in pittsburgh and ive taken them exactly how they told me, even wrote the times down bc i didnt want to mess up after being in the hospital. i still have that wiped out feeling, like walking to the kitchen feels like too much, chest just feels weird/tight sometimes but not like severe pain, more like heavy and worn out. is there anymore i can do?really don t want to go back to hospital.thank you. i just dont know if i should push for another xray, more blood cultures, sputum test, or if this is just normal after pneumonia. im nervous bc i already did 3 antibiotics plus prednisone and still feel awful 5dags later, and my np kinda made me feel like i should just wait it out, but my gut says something still isnt right.
pii_units: rite aid on 5th ave in pittsburgh
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry you’re feeling so scared and still so wiped out. Pneumonia can take several weeks to recover from, and fatigue/chest “heaviness” can linger even after the infection is treated. A temperature of 99°F by itself is not technically a fever. **But** given your history of hemoptysis, a positive blood culture, hospitalization, three antibiotics, prednisone, and now shakiness/sweats with persistent severe fatigue, I agree this deserves a prompt reassessment rather than just “waiting it out.”

What I’d recommend next:

1. **Call Dr. Patel’s office directly** rather than waiting only on the message. Ask to speak with the nurse/triage line and tell them: “recent pneumonia with hemoptysis and a positive blood culture; completed antibiotics 5 days ago; ongoing sweats/shaking, low-grade temps, severe fatigue, chest tightness.”  
2. Ask whether they want you seen **today/tomorrow** and whether to repeat:
   - Chest X-ray, or possibly CT chest if symptoms/hemoptysis persist  
   - CBC and inflammatory markers  
   - Repeat blood cultures, especially depending on what organism grew previously  
   - Sputum culture if you’re still producing phlegm  
   - Pulse oximetry/oxygen check  
3. If you have a home pulse oximeter, check oxygen at rest and after walking. Write down temps, heart rate, oxygen numbers, and symptoms.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if any of these occur: temperature **100.4°F or higher**, worsening shortness of breath, oxygen consistently **<92–94%**, chest pain that is sharp/severe or pressure-like, coughing up more than streaks of blood or clots, fainting, confusion, blue lips, severe weakness where you can’t walk around safely, rapid heart rate, shaking chills/rigors, or one-sided leg swelling/pain.

It’s possible this is still normal post-pneumonia recovery, but it’s also reasonable to make sure there isn’t persistent infection, fluid around the lung, a resistant organism, a complication, or a non-pneumonia issue like a clot. Your gut feeling is valid here. You don’t necessarily need automatic hospital readmission, but you do need timely clinical reassessment.

query: i m dana mills and i live at 425 maple ridge rd and i know this is prob too much info but i am so confused and kinda scared. my chart at st agnes medical center is MRN SA-77419 and i see dr. nina patel in pulmonary there, she is nice but i still feel like i dont understand what happened. i ve had pneumonia numerous times always in my left lung, like over and over, and the last time was a year ago, it never went away. my pulmonary explained that the disease is between all the scar tissue, which i sort of get but also dont, bc how can it just sit there and not clear up after all the meds and all the waiting. i also have crohns disease and am treated with remicade, so i know my immune system is not normal and i keep thinking maybe that made everything weird or hid something. after several c-scans which showed a mass and biopsies and cultures which came back negative, i had the left lung completely removed last month and was diagnosed with pulmonary eosinothioia. my insurance is Blue Cross PPO #BCP-381-9927 and they keep sending papers that make no sense either, so that adds to the panic. i keep replaying it bc first it was pneumonia, then scar tissue, then a mass, then negative cultures, then surgery, then this rare sounding name. does this sound correct? like can pulmonary eosinothioia be missed like that and only found after the lung is out? i dont want to accuse anyone, i just want to understand if this all adds up or if i should be asking for another pathology review or sending the slides somewhere else.
target_response:
Dana, I’m really sorry you’re having to process all of this after such a major surgery. It’s completely understandable to feel scared and confused when the story seemed to change from “pneumonia” to “scar tissue” to “mass” to a rare pathology diagnosis.

A few points that may help this make sense:

- Recurrent infection or inflammation in the same part of one lung often means there is something local preventing normal clearing — scarring, airway narrowing/obstruction, bronchiectasis, old infection damage, or an inflammatory mass-like process.
- When tissue is heavily scarred, abnormal areas can be “walled off” and difficult for antibiotics or immune cells to clear. CT scans may show a persistent mass or consolidation, but imaging cannot always tell infection, scar, cancer, and inflammatory disease apart.
- Biopsies can be falsely negative if they sample nearby scar or inflammation but miss the key area. Cultures can also be negative if the process is not an active infection, or if organisms are hard to grow.
- A diagnosis involving eosinophils — I wonder if the term was “pulmonary eosinophilia” or “eosinophilic pneumonia/granulomatous disease” — can sometimes only be confidently diagnosed when a large surgical specimen is examined.

Given your Crohn’s disease and Remicade/infliximab, it is also very reasonable to ask whether opportunistic infections like TB or fungal disease were fully excluded, and whether medication-related or immune-related lung inflammation was considered.

I do think it is reasonable — and not accusatory — to request a second pathology review, especially after removal of an entire lung and a rare-sounding diagnosis. Ask Dr. Patel for: the final pathology report, operative report, CT summaries, bronchoscopy/biopsy results, and culture/stain results including bacterial, fungal, and AFB/TB testing. You can request the slides be reviewed by an academic pulmonary pathologist.

Seek urgent care now if you develop worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, coughing blood, fainting, new leg swelling, rapid heartbeat, low oxygen levels, or redness/drainage from the incision.

Also, please avoid sending addresses, MRNs, or insurance numbers in messages unless you’re in a secure portal. For the insurance paperwork, ask the hospital for a case manager or billing advocate.

query: what risk am i from coronavirus as i have vte? my name is matthew kline and im prob overthinking but i keep seeing stuff online and then i go down a rabbit hole and none of it says it in a normal person way. i already messaged dr. nina shah once but i dont wanna be annoying and keep sending panicky portal msgs, my mrn is MRN VTE-60418 if that matters. like i know vte is a clot thing and coronavirus seems to mess with lungs and blood and all that from what ppl keep saying, so im sitting here wondering am i in the higher risk group or is it only ppl with heart stuff and diabetes and older age and whatever. i dont know if having vte means my body would handle coronavirus worse, or if it just means i need to be extra careful not to catch it. im trying to be sensible but also i feel like every cough i hear in a grocery store makes me want to leave. not asking for a whole diagnosis, just like should i act as if im high risk and stay home more, avoid family things, masks, work meetings, all that, or is that too much. i dont want to waste anyone time but i also dont wanna be stupid about it. if i did get coronavirus would i need to call sooner than other ppl or only if breathing gets bad or fever gets high. sorry this is rambling, im just anxious bc vte already freaked me out once and now all this coronavirus talk makes me feel like im waiting for something bad to happen.
target_response:
Hi Matthew — you’re not being annoying, and you’re not “stupid” for asking. VTE is scary to go through, and it makes sense that COVID news about lungs and clotting would hit a nerve.

In “normal person” terms: having had a VTE means you’ve had a blood clot problem, but it does **not automatically mean** your body will handle coronavirus badly in the way that older age, major heart/lung disease, diabetes, cancer treatment, or immune suppression can. COVID can increase clot risk, especially in people who are very ill or hospitalized, so your history is relevant — but it doesn’t necessarily put you in the highest-risk category by itself.

Your actual risk depends on details like: how recent the VTE was, whether you’re still on a blood thinner, whether you’ve had recurrent clots, have an underlying clotting disorder, cancer, significant obesity, smoking, or chronic heart/lung disease. If you’re on anticoagulation, **keep taking it exactly as prescribed** unless your doctor tells you otherwise.

For day-to-day precautions, I’d recommend being “careful, not panicked”: stay up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters, use a well-fitting mask in crowded indoor places, prefer ventilation/outdoor gatherings, avoid close contact with sick people, and wash hands. You probably do **not** need to completely isolate from family or avoid all work meetings unless community spread is high, people are ill, or your clinician has told you you’re especially high risk.

If you test positive, I’d suggest contacting Dr. Shah’s office **early**, even if symptoms are mild, because antivirals may be time-sensitive and they’ll need to check medication interactions, especially if you take a blood thinner.

Seek urgent care now/quickly for: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, coughing blood, fainting, new confusion, bluish lips, oxygen levels under ~94% if you monitor, severe dehydration, or signs of a new clot such as one-sided leg swelling, pain, redness, or warmth.

And please don’t hesitate to send a portal message if you’re unsure — that’s exactly what it’s for. You’re trying to be responsible, not wasting anyone’s time.

query: hi, this is maria lynn bautista, dob 05/14/1989, sorry if this is a dumb question but my directors have asked me to take a covid-19 test. i work at brightsteps learning center and they are getting nervous bc one parent was coughing at drop off last wk and now every sniffle is like a whole thing. but i am not sure if i just have a common cold or if i really need the test. i am monitoring my temperature which has been stable at around 37.3 or so. it hasnt really gone higher than that, i keep checking it too much probably, like every couple hrs, bc i get anxious and then i feel warm just from being stressed lol. i have a stuffy nose and that tired gross cold feeling, but nothing dramatic, and i keep thinking maybe its just from the weather changing and being around little kids all day. my directors keep saying better safe than sorry and they dont want me back in the classroom unless i know, but i also dont want to waste a test or sit in a waiting room if this is just a normal cold. i looked at CVS minuteclinic on river rd but wasnt sure if i should book there or call first, and i can be reached at maria.bautista88@mailhaven.com if someone needs to reply that way. i need some advice please? like do i test just because work asked, even with a stable temp around 37.3, or wait and see if symptoms get worse? also should i stay home now while deciding, bc i dont want to freak everyone out but i also dont wanna be irresponsible.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. Given that you work in a childcare setting and currently have symptoms (stuffy nose, fatigue/“cold” feeling), I do think it’s reasonable to get a COVID-19 test, even though your temperature around 37.3°C is not a fever. A fever is typically 38.0°C/100.4°F or higher.

COVID can look exactly like a mild cold, especially early on, and many people never develop a fever. Since your directors are asking for confirmation before you return to the classroom, testing is the most practical way to protect the kids/staff and also give you a clear answer for work.

**What I’d recommend:**
1. **Stay home for now** while you have symptoms and while you’re arranging testing, especially since you work closely with young children.
2. **Book a COVID test** through CVS MinuteClinic or another pharmacy/testing site. It’s fine to book online; if you’re unsure about appointment type or insurance/cost, call first.
3. If you do a **rapid antigen test** and it’s negative but symptoms continue, consider repeating it in **24–48 hours**, since early tests can miss infection. A PCR/NAAT test is more sensitive if available.
4. While waiting, rest, hydrate, use saline spray/steam for congestion, and consider acetaminophen/ibuprofen if achy—assuming you can take those safely.

If your test is negative and you’re improving, follow your center’s return-to-work policy. In general, it’s best to return when symptoms are improving and you’ve had no fever for 24 hours without fever-reducing medication. If you test positive, follow current isolation guidance and your workplace policy.

Also, try not to check your temperature every couple of hours—stress can make you feel flushed and can worsen anxiety. Checking 1–2 times daily is plenty unless you feel significantly worse.

**Seek urgent care** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, persistent high fever, confusion, dehydration, bluish lips, or symptoms that rapidly worsen.

Bottom line: because you’re symptomatic and work with kids, **test now and stay home until you have results/clearance**. That’s not wasting a test—that’s being responsible.

query: hi, this is maria benson, i just had a quick question but now im overthinking it like crazy. my throat a bit sore and want to get a good imune booster, especially in light of the virus. please advise. have not been in contact with nyone with the virus that i know of, like no one at work said they had it and i havent been around anybody who was tested positive or anything. i dont have a big cough or chest thing going on, just that scratchy sore throat feeling and i keep swallowing to check if its worse, which prob makes me notice it more. i was gonna ask dr. rina patel but the office line was busy and i dont wanna be dramatic if its just dry air or allergies or me sleeping with the fan on. i also dont wanna take a bunch of random stuff if it doesnt help, bc everyone online is saying vitamin c, zinc, elderberry, teas, all kinds of things and i dont know whats actually safe or worth it. i can run to cvs on walnut st later if you think there is something simple i should get, but im trying not to go out too much unless needed. i dont have fever that i can tell and i feel mostly ok, just nervous bc of the virus talk everywhere and i live with family so i dont wanna bring anything home or miss early signs. if you reply can you just tell me what imune booster is ok, and if sore throat by itself means i should isolate or test or just watch it? sorry this is long, i get anxious about this stuff.
target_response:
Hi Maria — please don’t apologize. It’s very understandable to feel anxious with all the “virus” talk, especially when you live with family and want to be careful.

A scratchy/sore throat by itself is very common and can come from dry air, allergies/post-nasal drip, sleeping with a fan on, mild irritation, reflux, or the start of a regular cold. It *can* also be an early symptom of COVID/other respiratory viruses, even without known exposure, so it’s reasonable to be cautious without panicking.

**About “immune boosters”:** unfortunately, there isn’t a supplement that reliably “boosts” immunity or prevents viruses. The best-supported basics are: good sleep, fluids, regular meals, handwashing, and avoiding close contact if symptoms are developing. If you want something simple and safe from CVS, I’d focus on symptom relief rather than lots of supplements:

- **Warm tea with honey** or honey by itself for throat irritation  
- **Throat lozenges** or hard candy  
- **Salt-water gargles**  
- **Acetaminophen or ibuprofen** if you develop pain/aches, assuming you can take them safely  
- **Saline nasal spray** or an antihistamine like cetirizine/loratadine if allergies/post-nasal drip seem likely  

Vitamin C is generally safe in normal doses, but high doses can upset the stomach. Zinc may slightly shorten colds if started early, but can cause nausea and should not be overdone; avoid zinc nasal sprays. Elderberry has mixed evidence and can interact with some conditions/medications, so I wouldn’t consider it necessary.

**Should you isolate or test?** Since you have a new sore throat and live with family, I’d recommend acting cautiously: wear a mask around family, avoid sharing drinks/utensils, wash hands, and consider taking a home COVID test today or tomorrow. If negative but symptoms persist or worsen, repeat in 24–48 hours.

**Seek urgent care** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, high or persistent fever, severe one-sided throat pain/swelling, drooling, inability to swallow fluids, dehydration, confusion, or symptoms that worsen significantly.

For now, this sounds mild. Rest, hydrate, monitor your temperature, and test if you can. If it lasts more than 3–5 days or worsens, check in with Dr. Patel’s office.

query: my name is michael r. bennett, dob 03/14/1979, and i have been on methadone for approx. 5 years now at a dose of 30mg taken 4 times daily. my file at kingston metro methadone clinic is KMC-77291 if that matters. this has not only stopped any unpleasant withdrawl symptoms but has also offered considerable relief in easing chronic pain from a severe back injury 11 years ago. for the first 3 years of taking the medication i found that while it was effective in these areas their was an exceptional side effect in that it absolutely obliterated any interest in sex or even the ability to physically have sex. also i found that i had next to no energy, making many things i used to enjoy fall by the wayside which in turn led to severe depression. after extensive talks with my doctor i was sent for blood analysis and it was found that my level of testosterone was so low it barely registered. i was then put on a 3 week cycle of injections of 2mls of delatestryl, filled at shoppers drug mart on princess st, and for the past several years i have been the person that i used to be (it gave me my life back). know i have been given a new doctor at the clinic, dr. alan greer, and he has informed me that he is only there to prescribe methadone and will not be responsible for any other prescriptions. i do not have a family doctor and our emerg has said that this falls outside their domain. the walk in clinic treats every individual that i know so badly that their is no doubt in my mind that they will refuse to continue this presciption. (to give you some perspective on our walk in clinic - i went their several mnths back with sever pneumonia and the doctor looked at my chart once and told me that i would get know %$# narcotics from him and to leave immediately) i can be reached at 613-555-0198 but honestly i just need suggestions on how to get across to my knew methadone doctor (who has only been in prescribing methadone for 2 months) just how much this would severely negatively impact my life?
pii_units: shoppers drug mart on princess st
target_response:
Michael, I’m sorry you’re being put in this position. What you describe is very consistent with **opioid-induced androgen deficiency**: long-term methadone can suppress the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, causing very low testosterone, loss of libido/erections, fatigue, low mood, and loss of motivation. It’s also very understandable that testosterone replacement felt like it “gave your life back.”

That said, testosterone therapy is not just a simple refill—it needs ongoing monitoring for safety, including testosterone levels, CBC/hematocrit, PSA/prostate risk assessment, blood pressure, sleep apnea symptoms, mood, and sometimes lipids/liver tests. So Dr. Greer may be uncomfortable managing it if he sees his clinic role as methadone-only, but he still can help by **bridging and referring**.

Practical steps for your next visit:

1. **Bring documentation**: prior low testosterone results, dose/frequency of Delatestryl, pharmacy history, and any monitoring labs.  
2. **Use clear, non-confrontational language**:  
   “I understand your role may be methadone prescribing. I’m not asking you to take on long-term endocrine care if that’s outside your scope. I’m asking for help preventing a medically predictable relapse of severe symptoms while I get connected to appropriate care.”  
3. **Ask for one of three things**:  
   - a short-term bridge prescription while referrals are arranged;  
   - referral to an endocrinologist/urologist or internist;  
   - referral to a primary-care provider, nurse practitioner clinic, community health centre, or addiction medicine physician comfortable managing opioid-related hypogonadism.  
4. **Register with Ontario Health Care Connect** if you haven’t already, and ask the methadone clinic social worker/case manager to help find a primary-care option.

If testosterone is suddenly stopped, symptoms may gradually return rather than an emergency, but given your history of severe depression, please seek urgent help if you develop suicidal thoughts, inability to function, chest pain, severe shortness of breath, new leg swelling, fainting, severe headache/vision changes, or signs of infection at injection sites.

You have a legitimate medical issue—not “drug-seeking.” The best approach is to frame this as continuity of care and safe monitoring, not just a prescription request.

query: i have pure pressures head aches and coughing persistent ? like its just annoying and making me nervous bc i keep thinking maybe im missing something or maybe im over reacting idk. this is marissa coleman btw, dob 03/14/1989, i tried to make sense of it on the portal but it keeps sending me in circles and i dont wanna sit in urgent care all day if its something basic. my head feels like that full tight pressure thing and then the head aches come and go and the cough is just staying there, not like one cough then done, just persistent and making me feel gross and tired from dealing with it. i keep drinking water and trying to rest but i have stuff to do and i cant tell if i should go be seen or just wait it out. i dont wanna sound dramatic but when it is in my head and chest area i get anxious fast, especially when im trying to sleep and then i start noticing every little cough more. if someone can call me at 215-555-7438 that would be easier than messages bc i might not explain it right typing. i can also pick up anything if needed at CVS on 9th and market, thats the one i usually use, but i dont know what would even help or if i need a test or just cough meds or what. i dont have a fancy way to describe it, just pressure in head, head aches, and coughing that wont quit. should i make an appt or is there anything i should watch for that means dont wait? sorry this is messy im just worried and trying not to panic over a cough and head pressure.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this. Head pressure/headaches with a persistent cough is really common with viral upper respiratory infections, COVID/flu, post-nasal drip/sinus congestion, or bronchitis-type irritation. It can feel scary because it’s in the head/chest area, especially at night, but many cases are not dangerous and improve with supportive care.

**What I’d do next:**
- If you haven’t already, take a **home COVID test**. If symptoms are early and you have fever/body aches, flu testing may also be useful.
- If this has been going on more than a few days, is disrupting sleep, or you’re feeling unsure, it’s reasonable to schedule a **same/next-day office or telehealth visit** rather than sitting in urgent care.
- For symptom relief:  
  - **Saline nasal spray/rinse** and a humidifier/steam can help head pressure/post-nasal drip.  
  - **Acetaminophen or ibuprofen** can help headache/pressure if you can take them safely.  
  - For cough: **honey** at night, **guaifenesin** for mucus, or **dextromethorphan** for a dry nagging cough may help.  
  - If allergies/post-nasal drip are contributing, an OTC antihistamine or nasal steroid like fluticasone may help.

**Don’t wait / seek urgent care today** if you have any of these: shortness of breath, wheezing that’s new or worsening, chest pain/pressure, coughing up blood, oxygen level <94% if you can check, fever ≥103 or fever lasting more than 3 days, severe “worst headache,” stiff neck, confusion, fainting, one-sided weakness/numbness, severe sinus/face pain with swelling, dehydration, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

Also, if symptoms last **>10 days**, improve then suddenly worsen, or you develop high fever with thick/pus-like nasal drainage and significant facial pain, that can suggest bacterial sinusitis and should be evaluated.

Since you’d prefer a call, please contact the office scheduling line/portal callback request and ask for a nurse/clinician call. We can also confirm the CVS on 9th and Market if medication is needed. You’re not being dramatic—persistent cough plus head pressure is uncomfortable and worth checking if it’s not settling.

query: hi, i am chaitanya rao, 27 years old. i use to swim daily in morning, mostly before work because if i dont go early then i never go later and my whole routine gets messed up. i stay at GreenLeaf Apartments near 221B Maple Ave in Austin, TX and the pool i use is not my apartment pool, its the one at Life Time Fitness on Braker Ln, so there are always lot of people coming in and out, kids classes sometimes, older people, and everyone is touching lockers, shower taps, benches etc. does covid-19 spreads through water ? i mean like if someone infected was swimming before me or near me, can the virus be in the pool water or on the wet floor, or is it only from breathing close to them? i am getting little worried because i swim daily in morning and sometimes i share the lane when its crowded, and people cough or spit water out and i dont know if chlorine kills everything or not. what precautions should i take to avoid infection? should i stop swimming for few days or is it ok if i keep distance and shower after? i usually carry my towel, goggles, water bottle and slippers but sometimes i put my phone on the bench and then touch my face, so now i am thinking maybe that is dumb. also the changing room is small and people stand close, no one really waits. pls tell me in simple way like should i wear mask till pool side, avoid steam room, not use common shower, wash hands, or anything else. i dont want to panic but i also dont want to get covid-19 just because of swimming daily.
pii_units: Life Time Fitness on Braker Ln
target_response:
Hi Chaitanya — your concern is very reasonable, especially since you swim daily and the gym pool/locker area can get crowded.

**Simple answer:** COVID-19 is **not thought to spread through properly chlorinated pool water**. Chlorine and normal pool disinfection make transmission through the water very unlikely. The bigger risk is **breathing shared air near infected people**, especially in **indoor, crowded, poorly ventilated spaces** like locker rooms, showers, steam rooms, and when sharing a lane closely with someone who is coughing or talking near you.

**What I’d recommend for your routine:**

1. **Do not wear a mask in the water** — it is unsafe when wet.  
2. **Wear a well-fitting mask from entry to poolside and in the locker room** if it’s crowded, then remove it just before swimming. Put it back on after drying off.  
3. **Avoid the steam room/sauna** for now if it’s crowded or poorly ventilated — these are higher-risk because people are close together and unmasked.  
4. **Try to avoid lane sharing** when possible. If you must share, choose circle swimming with distance and avoid stopping face-to-face at the wall.  
5. **Use your own towel, goggles, bottle, slippers** — good habits. Keep them in your bag rather than on benches when possible.  
6. **Phone on bench → touch face** is a real, common issue. Put your phone in a zip bag or pocket, clean it later, and avoid touching your eyes/nose/mouth until hands are clean.  
7. **Wash or sanitize hands** after touching lockers, taps, benches, door handles, and before eating or touching your face.  
8. **Showering after swimming** is good hygiene, but it does not “wash away” COVID risk from breathing exposure. If common showers are crowded, consider showering at home.  
9. Stay up to date with **COVID vaccination/boosters**, and don’t swim if you have fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, or recent known exposure.

You do **not necessarily need to stop swimming** if you can keep distance, avoid crowded locker rooms, and mask before/after. But if the locker room is packed daily, consider going at a less busy time or temporarily shortening your routine.

Seek urgent care if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, or severe/worsening symptoms.

query: my son ethan morris who is 19, suffered from mono last spring with a tonsillar abscess requiring hospitalization at st. luke's allentown for steroid and antibiotic infusion. due to how ill he was, they did not want to remove the tonsils but recommended he have them removed when he improved. i remember dr. rebecca han saying lets not do anything while hes that sick, just get him thru it first and then plan tonsils later. he went off to college at penn state altoona, became ill, was diagnosed with pneumonia and still had a positive mono spot. so we were unable to schedule it for the christmas break as we planned but now he has a piece of his tonsil that is actually hanging off=it looks like the strands between his cryptic tonsil areas broke off and left this piece dangling. it is umcomfortable but i am not sure if it is emergent. no fever or c/o sore throat right now, but overall still not up to par since this continued bouts of illness. i keep looking with a flashlight and i know i shouldnt obsess but it honestly looks so gross and weird, like tissue hanging there, and he says it feels like something stuck in his throat more than real pain. he can swallow and talk, no drooling, no trouble breathing, but hes tired and just not himself since all this mono/pneumonia stuff. we still have his meds history at cvs on 5th ave if that matters, and im trying to decide if this is urgent care/er tonight or can wait for an ent call monday. i dont want to be the mom who ignores something after the abscess last time, but also dont want to drag him in if its just a cryptic tonsil thing and not an emergency.
target_response:
I understand why this is worrying—given Ethan’s prior mono with a tonsillar abscess and hospitalization, it’s very reasonable to be cautious. From what you describe, the fact that he can swallow and talk normally, has no drooling, no breathing trouble, no fever, and no significant sore throat makes this sound **less like an emergency tonight**.

A “piece hanging off” of a cryptic tonsil can sometimes be inflamed tonsil tissue, a strand/tag of tissue from chronically enlarged tonsils, sloughed debris, or tissue irritated after repeated infections. It can feel like something is stuck in the throat without being very painful. That said, because of his history of a tonsillar/peritonsillar abscess and ongoing illness, he **does need ENT follow-up**, and I would call first thing Monday to describe the dangling tissue and his history. If you can safely take a clear photo without touching it, that may help the ENT triage him.

For tonight/tomorrow, I would suggest:
- **Do not poke, pull, or try to remove it**—tonsil tissue can bleed.
- Encourage fluids, soft foods if uncomfortable.
- Warm salt-water gargles if he tolerates them.
- Acetaminophen or ibuprofen if needed, assuming he can take them safely.
- Monitor temperature and symptoms.

He should be seen **urgently tonight/ER** if any of the following develop: fever, worsening or severe throat pain, one-sided tonsil swelling, uvula pushed to one side, muffled “hot potato” voice, trouble opening his mouth, drooling, inability to swallow fluids, neck swelling/stiffness, trouble breathing, bleeding from the tonsil, or he appears acutely worse.

The persistent positive Monospot can remain positive for months after mono, so that alone does not necessarily mean active new mono. However, his ongoing fatigue and “not himself” after mono plus pneumonia deserves follow-up with his primary doctor/student health as well—sometimes labs, anemia/inflammation checks, and recovery guidance are helpful.

Based on what you’ve written, this sounds reasonable to **wait for an ENT call Monday**, provided none of the red flags appear. Your concern is completely appropriate—you’re not overreacting, just watching closely after a serious prior episode.

query: hi i’m marissa lane and i have been feeling sick for a couple of days now and tried self medication but does not seem to help. i suffer from polycystic kidney disease, low blood pressure, anemic, endometriosis, chronic sinus. i know thats a lot and i always feel dumb listing it all out but every time i get sick i get nervous bc i dont know what is just a normal little bug and what is gonna turn into something bigger with my kidneys or blood pressure or whatever. i tried to just handle it at home first bc i hate running to the dr for every little thing and i dont wanna waste anyones time, but now im sitting here thinking maybe i waited too long or took the wrong over the counter stuff. i usually use CVS on 9th ave for my meds and i was gonna ask the pharmacist but it was busy and i kinda got embarrassed and left, so now im asking here. dr. rachel kim knows my history but i cant get a quick answer from the office and the nurse line just keeps saying call back or go in if worse which doesnt really help me figure out what to do right now. i have to work and i dont have a ride till later so im trying to know if i should keep resting and fluids or if with my polycystic kidney disease and low bp and anemia this is something i should be seen for sooner. also with the chronic sinus i never know if feeling sick is sinus related or not, and the endometriosis stuff already makes my body feel off sometimes, so its all confusing and im kinda worried.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — please don’t feel dumb listing your conditions. With polycystic kidney disease, low blood pressure, anemia, endometriosis, and chronic sinus issues, it’s completely reasonable to be more cautious when you get sick.

Since you’ve been sick for a couple of days and home treatment isn’t helping, the main question is whether this still sounds like a routine viral/sinus illness or whether there are signs your kidneys, hydration, blood pressure, or blood count could be affected.

**If your symptoms are mild** — congestion, sore throat, cough, sinus pressure, body aches, low-grade fever, and you’re drinking fluids, urinating normally, not dizzy, and breathing okay — it is reasonable to continue rest, fluids, saline nasal spray/rinse, humidifier/steam, and monitor closely. For pain/fever, **acetaminophen/Tylenol is usually preferred** with kidney disease, as long as you stay within the label dose and don’t have liver disease.

**Be careful with OTC meds:** with kidney disease, it’s generally best to **avoid ibuprofen, naproxen, or other NSAIDs** unless Dr. Kim has specifically told you they’re safe for you. Also be cautious with multi-symptom cold/flu products because they often contain several medications and can accidentally double-dose acetaminophen or include decongestants that may not be ideal for everyone.

Given your **low blood pressure**, please check your BP if you can. If you’re feeling lightheaded, faint, unusually weak, or can’t keep fluids down, that’s more concerning.

You should be seen **sooner today/urgent care** if you have any of the following: fever over 101–102°F or fever lasting more than 3 days, chills/feeling very ill, flank or back pain near the kidneys, burning with urination, blood in urine, much less urine than usual, severe dizziness/fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, confusion, severe headache/neck stiffness, worsening anemia symptoms like racing heart or extreme fatigue, or severe abdominal/pelvic pain different from your usual endometriosis pain.

If none of those are present but you’re still not improving, I’d call Dr. Kim’s office again and say: “I have PKD, low BP, anemia, and symptoms for several days not improving with OTC meds — I need same-day advice.” If you tell me your exact symptoms, temperature, BP, and what you took, I can help you sort the urgency more specifically.

query:
i went to the doctor today and found out that i have pneumonia. my name is kayla moreno, im 23, and i went to Northside Family Clinic earlier because i was coughing so much and felt so tight in my chest and just worn out. Dr. Amy Cheng listened to my lungs and said it sounded like pneumonia and they put it in my chart under MRN NFA-23891, if that even matters. i got a steroid injection and breathing treatment in the office and was sent home with levaquin, albuterol, and cough medicine. i took the first levaquin when i got home and did the inhaler like they told me but now my forearms and legs are very tingly and almost numb and heavy feeling. like not totally dead numb but weird pins and needles and heavy, and it is making me nervous because i dont know if this is normal after the steroid shot or breathing treatment or if its one of the meds mixing bad or something.

is this from the steroid injection? i am 23 years old and i have juvenile rheumatoid arthritis so i am also taking humira, methotrexate, plaquenil, and folic acid for the ra. ive been on those for a while and usually dont get this feeling, so thats why im worried. i know i probably sound dramatic but pneumonia plus ra meds already freaks me out cause my immune system is weird and i dont want to ignore something serious. my hands still work and i can walk but my legs feel heavy and my forearms feel buzzy and tingly and i keep checking if its getting worse. is this a side effect or a drug reaction or nothing to be concerned about? should i call them back tonight or just watch it?
target_response:
Hi Kayla — you do **not** sound dramatic. With pneumonia, immune-suppressing RA medications, and new neurologic symptoms after starting treatment, it’s reasonable to take this seriously.

Tingling/buzzy sensations can happen after a **breathing treatment/albuterol**, especially if it causes shakiness, fast heartbeat, anxiety, or changes in breathing pattern; it can also sometimes relate to temporary electrolyte shifts. A **steroid injection** can make people feel wired or jittery, but it is less likely to cause new numb/heavy sensations in the arms and legs.

However, **Levaquin (levofloxacin)** is important here: fluoroquinolone antibiotics can rarely cause **nerve symptoms (peripheral neuropathy)** such as tingling, burning, numbness, or weakness, and it can start early. Because your symptoms began after the first dose, I would **call Northside Family Clinic’s after-hours line tonight** or the prescribing clinician’s on-call service. Ask specifically whether you should **hold further Levaquin and switch antibiotics**. Don’t just go without pneumonia treatment, but I would not “just watch it” without speaking to someone.

Also tell them you take **Humira, methotrexate, Plaquenil, and folic acid**. During active pneumonia, clinicians often temporarily hold biologics like Humira and sometimes methotrexate, but that should be confirmed by your doctor/rheumatologist.

Please seek **urgent care/ER tonight** if any of the following occur: worsening or true weakness, trouble walking, one-sided numbness/weakness, facial droop, confusion, severe headache, loss of bladder/bowel control, chest pain, fainting, severe palpitations, swelling of lips/tongue/throat, hives, worsening shortness of breath, blue lips, oxygen level <92% if you have a pulse ox, or fever/worsening pneumonia symptoms despite treatment.

For now: sit/lie down, hydrate, avoid extra albuterol beyond the prescribed schedule, and note timing of symptoms relative to Levaquin and inhaler use. But given the Levaquin possibility and your immune-suppressed state, **call tonight rather than waiting until tomorrow**.

query: should i really be worried about my elderly parents catching covid-19? what steps should they take to stay healthy? im asking bc my parents ronald and linda baker are both older and they keep saying they are “fine” but they still go out for little dumb stuff like bread or the bank and it makes me nuts. my dad is the one with dob 04/18/1946 and he acts like washing hands once is enough, and my mom is a worrier but also somehow still wants to stand close to neighbors and chat at the mailbox. they live at 912 cedar hollow rd and i dont live right there so i cant just pop in every day and make sure theyre doing things right. i called and tried to explain masks, staying away from people, not touching face, wiping down carts, all that, but then my dad says he doesnt want to “live scared” and i dont know how to say it without sounding bossy. they dont have symptoms right now, no fever or anything, this is more me trying to prevent it before it starts. should they be staying totally inside, like no grocery store at all, or is quick trips ok if they wear masks? should i make them cancel church and cards with friends even if its just a few people? i was thinking of calling dr. meena patel but i dont want to waste her time if its just common sense stuff. also what about me bringing food to them, do i leave it outside and not go in? i have kids too so im scared of being the one who carries something to them. sorry this is rambly, im just really worried bc elderly parents and covid-19 sounds like a bad combo and i dont know what steps are actually enough.
target_response:
Yes—you’re right to be concerned, but not panicked. Older adults, especially in their late 70s and beyond, are at higher risk of severe COVID even if they feel “fine” now. The goal is not for your parents to “live scared,” but to lower the odds of infection while still allowing some normal life.

The most important steps for them:

1. **Stay up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters** and flu/RSV vaccines if recommended by their doctor. This is the biggest protection against hospitalization.
2. **Avoid crowded indoor places when possible.** Grocery stores, banks, church, and card games are higher risk if indoors, close together, or poorly ventilated.
3. **Use safer alternatives:** grocery delivery, curbside pickup, pharmacy delivery, online/phone banking, drive-through services.
4. **Wear a high-quality mask** such as an N95/KN95 in indoor public places or around people outside their household.
5. **Keep distance during chats.** Your mom can still talk to neighbors, ideally outdoors and several feet apart.
6. **Hand hygiene matters**, but COVID spreads mainly through the air. Your dad washing once is not enough if he goes out—he should sanitize after stores, before eating, and avoid touching his face.

I would strongly encourage canceling or modifying **indoor church and card gatherings**, especially if people are unmasked or vaccination status is unclear. Virtual church, outdoor visits, or small masked gatherings are safer.

For you bringing food: if you or your kids have any symptoms or known exposure, do not go inside. Ideally, drop groceries at the door, text/call, and step back. If you visit, wear a good mask, keep it brief, improve ventilation, and consider a rapid test beforehand.

Calling Dr. Patel is not wasting her time—this is exactly the kind of prevention planning that matters. Also ask what they should do if they test positive, because antivirals like Paxlovid work best when started early.

Seek urgent care if either develops trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below about 92% if they use a pulse oximeter.

A helpful way to frame it to them: “I’m not asking you to be scared—I’m asking you to be smart so we get many more years together.”

query: how do i book a virtual consultation with my doctor for covid-19? i feel dumb asking but i keep clicking around and cant tell if im supposed to call first or do it thru the portal or wait for someone to send me a link. my regular doctor is dr. maya patel at northside family medicine and i have seen her before for normal stuff, but not like this, and i dont wanna just show up in person if im maybe contagious or if they dont even want people coming in right now. i tried logging in with my email jess.rivera82@gmail.com but then it asks me what kind of visit and theres like urgent, follow up, video, message the care team, and idk which one is right for covid-19 questions. i have been around a couple ppl who were sick and now im getting anxious and checking my temp too much, so i just want to talk to my doctor on video if possible and ask what i should do next, if i need a test, and if i should stay away from my family. do i need to download an app or can i use my laptop camera? also do they usually call me or do i call them at the appt time? im not trying to clog up the phone lines but the website is kinda confusing and i dont want to mess it up and then miss the consult. if i request a virtual consultation is that the same thing as telehealth? and will the doctor be able to see my chart and meds and stuff or do i have to type all that in again? just trying to figure out the right steps bc im nervous and dont wanna do the wrong thing.
target_response:
You’re not dumb at all—these portals can be confusing, especially when you’re anxious and trying not to expose anyone. You’re doing the right thing by *not* just showing up in person if COVID is a possibility.

I can’t access Northside Family Medicine’s scheduling system directly, but in most practices the right path would be:

1. **Log into the patient portal.**
2. Choose **“Video”** or **“Telehealth”** visit if available. Yes—**virtual consultation and telehealth usually mean the same thing**.
3. For the visit reason, write something like:  
   *“COVID exposure, developing symptoms/anxiety, would like advice about testing and isolation.”*
4. If there is no COVID-specific option, choose **“Urgent”** if you want to be seen soon, or **“Video”** if it lets you pick Dr. Maya Patel.
5. If you’re still unsure, use **“Message the care team”** and say you’re trying to schedule a COVID-related video visit and ask them to book the correct type—or call the office. Calling for help with scheduling is completely appropriate and not “clogging the lines.”

Many offices send a **link by text/email or portal message** before the appointment. Some use Zoom, Doximity, MyChart, or another app. Often you can use a **laptop camera** through a browser, but some systems require an app—check the appointment confirmation instructions. At the appointment time, usually you either **click the link and wait in the virtual room**, or a medical assistant/doctor calls you first. The confirmation should say which; if not, join the link 5–10 minutes early and keep your phone nearby.

Dr. Patel’s office should be able to see your chart, medication list, allergies, and past visits, but it’s still helpful to have your medication bottles/list nearby and be ready to share symptoms, exposure dates, temperature, and any home test results.

For now, until you’re advised otherwise: consider wearing a mask around family, improve ventilation, avoid close contact, and consider a home COVID test—especially if symptoms start.  

Seek urgent care/ER now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels <92%** if you have a pulse oximeter.

query:
good day, my son mason reed, who is 4 yrs old, has been diagnosed with lobar pneumonia and is now hospitalised at childrens hospital of san antonio. this is about his 6th or 7th infection and i am just tired and scared now, like every time we start to think he is ok again he gets another cough/fever and we end up back with drs and machines and antibiotics. he had a double aortic arch that led to the entrance of his right lung and his trachea and esophagus being squeezed, and they told us that explained alot of the early breathing/noisy eating stuff, but even after all that history i still dont know what is “normal” for him anymore. he is now diagnosed with asthma too and dr priya nambiar said we need to be careful with triggers, but i keep wondering if we are missing something else. his chart has mrn CHSA-774291 and we are under blue cross blue shield #TXP4482091 if that matters for referrals or lung testing, i dont even know who is supposed to coordinate all this.

can the pneumonia deplete his lung function over time? like if this keeps happening, does each infection leave scars or make his right lung weaker, or can kids recover fully between them? what, if anything, can i do to reduce the number of infections? we already try hand washing, keeping him away from sick cousins, changing sheets, avoiding smoke and dust, and i panic clean his toys but still it happens. should we ask for immune testing, a swallow study, repeat scan, pulmonology follow up, vaccines check, anything like that? im not trying to second guess the hospital, im just his mom and im worried he is only 4 and already been through so much. i feel dumb asking but i need to know what questions to push for before discharge.
target_response:
Good day — I’m so sorry Mason and your family are going through this. You are not “dumb” for asking; with a 4-year-old who has had 6–7 significant respiratory infections plus a history of double aortic arch compressing the airway/esophagus and asthma, it is very reasonable to ask for a coordinated plan before discharge.
