Please seek urgent care/ER now if you have worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, coughing blood, confusion, fainting, persistent fever, oxygen levels under ~92%, rapid worsening weakness, or inability to keep fluids down.

When you call, you might say: “Given persistent large left upper-lobe opacity after three antibiotic courses, can we order a chest CT and expedite pulmonology before starting a fourth antibiotic?” That is a very reasonable request.

query: i was diagnosed with pneumonia on june 30. this is linda morales and i know i already called once but im still worried and prob overthinking it. i went to riverside family clinic and saw dr. priya shah and she said it was pneumonia and i finished taking my antibiotics and cough pills like i was told, didnt skip them or anything. i still continue to cough sometimes gaging, like its not every second but when it hits it hits hard and i get scared bc i feel like i cant catch it. shortness of breath and wheezing, i have an inhaler, i am not coughing up anymore phlem which i guess is good but the cough is still there and its annoying and makes me nervous at night. my doctor says to take mucinex dm. i have finished a bottle. i also have taken robitussin cough medicine and got both from cvs on walnut st bc thats where i always go. she also sd cough cld last up to 4-6 weeks but im just wondering if this much coughing and wheezing is still normal after the meds are done or if i should be seen again. i dont have a fever now that i can tell, but im tired from not sleeping and my chest feels tight when i start coughing. i keep trying water, tea, sitting up, the inhaler when i need it, all that. i dont want to be dramatic and run back in if its just healing, but i also dont want to miss something if the pneumonia didnt clear all the way. please let me know if i should keep doing the mucinex dm or stop since i already finished a bottle, and if the gaging cough is expected.
target_response:
Hi Linda — I’m glad you reached out. You are **not being dramatic**. It’s very common to feel anxious when coughing fits make you gag or feel like you can’t catch your breath.

After pneumonia, the infection may be treated, but the airways can stay **inflamed and “twitchy” for several weeks**. A cough lasting **4–6 weeks** can be normal, and hard coughing/gagging spells can happen from airway irritation, post-nasal drip, or bronchospasm. The fact that you’re no longer coughing up phlegm and don’t have a fever is reassuring.

That said, because you’re still having **wheezing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness with coughing**, I do think it would be reasonable to be **seen again**, especially since you’ve completed antibiotics and are still needing the inhaler. A follow-up visit can check your oxygen level, listen to your lungs, and decide if you need anything else—sometimes patients need an inhaler adjustment, breathing treatment, steroid, or repeat chest X-ray depending on the exam.

For the **Mucinex DM**: it’s okay to use it as directed for a short time if it helps, but if you’ve finished a bottle and it’s not clearly helping, you can stop. Also be careful not to take **Mucinex DM and Robitussin** together unless you confirm the ingredients, because many contain the same cough suppressant, dextromethorphan, and you don’t want to double-dose. Since you’re not bringing up mucus anymore, plain supportive measures may help just as much: warm fluids, honey if you can take it, humidifier/steam, avoiding smoke/fragrances, and sleeping propped up.

Please seek **urgent care or ER today** if you have worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking full sentences, lips/face turning blue, chest pain not just with coughing, coughing blood, fever returning, confusion, severe weakness, or if you have a pulse ox reading persistently below about **92%**.

Otherwise, I’d recommend calling Riverside Family Clinic and asking for a **recheck within the next 24–48 hours**, especially because of the wheezing and tightness. You’re doing the right things—this may still be healing, but it deserves another listen.

query:
hi, my name is thabo mthembu and i just need to ask because im a bit confused and honestly stressing now. on saturday evening i arrived from a vacation in canada and i traveled via the european union(amsterdam), i came back on KLM flight KL592 and only afterwards when i was already home and unpacking i saw all the notices and people sending me screenshots about the rules. according to the nicd, i am required to be in self quarantine for 14 days. i understand that and i am not trying to avoid it, im staying in my room as much as possible, not going to shops, not seeing friends, washing hands etc, but the problem is work is asking for something in writing and i dont know what the correct way is to do this.

can one request a medical certificate for this from a doctor? like do i have to actually go in to see dr naledi van wyk or can it be done over the phone/email because it feels silly and also wrong to go sit in a waiting room if the whole point is that i must self quarantine. i dont have bad symptoms right now, just tired from travel and anxious, no major thing, but the 14 days is going to be an issue with my manager if i dont have some proof. also do they write it as sick leave or quarantine leave or just a note saying i must remain at home because of the travel route? i am worried they will say its not a real medical certificate since im not technically sick. sorry for the long msg, i just need to know what is normally done and if a dr is allowed to give a certificate based on travel history and nicd requirement, because i dont want to break the rules but also dont want trouble at work.
target_response:
Hi Thabo — I understand why you’re stressed, and you’re doing the right thing by taking the self‑quarantine requirement seriously.

You **should not go and sit in a doctor’s waiting room** just to get paperwork if you are meant to be quarantining. The correct approach is to **phone or email Dr Naledi van Wyk’s rooms** and explain exactly what you wrote here: you returned from Canada via Amsterdam on KLM KL592, have been advised by NICD guidance to self‑quarantine for 14 days, and your employer requires written confirmation.

A doctor can generally provide documentation after a **telephonic/virtual consultation**, especially when the issue is public-health quarantine rather than an in-person examination. The doctor may issue one of the following:

1. **A medical certificate** if you have symptoms and are unfit for work, or  
2. **A quarantine/isolation letter** stating that, based on your travel history and public health guidance, you have been advised to remain at home for 14 days from your arrival date.

Because you are not currently significantly ill, it may be more accurate for the doctor to write a **quarantine advisory letter** rather than “sick leave.” Whether your employer records it as sick leave, quarantine leave, special leave, or remote work is usually an HR/employer policy issue, not something the doctor decides. The letter can simply confirm the medical/public-health recommendation without sharing unnecessary personal details.

For now, continue what you’re doing: stay in a separate room as much as possible, avoid visitors/shops/public transport, wear a mask if you must be near others at home, wash hands regularly, clean shared surfaces, and monitor symptoms daily. If possible, arrange groceries/medication by delivery or through someone who can leave them at the door.

Please contact your doctor or the relevant health hotline promptly if you develop **fever, cough, sore throat, loss of smell/taste, body aches, diarrhoea, or worsening fatigue**. Seek urgent care immediately if you have **shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, fainting, or you cannot keep fluids down**.

So yes: request the letter remotely. That is the safest and most appropriate way to handle this.

query: i have tested positive for covid 19 what medication must i take? im marisol vega and i did the home test this morning and then another one cuz i thought maybe i messed it up but it was positive too, so now im just sitting here kinda panicking and googling too much which i know is prob making me worse. my dob is 08/14/1987 if that matters for whatever meds ppl can take, i dont know if there is like a certain pill or antibiotic or cough stuff or if im supposed to just ride it out. i dont want to go to er unless i have to because i feel bad taking up space and also money is tight, but i also dont wanna ignore it and then get worse. i tried calling the nurse line but was on hold forever and my phone number is 555-294-7718 if someone needs to call me back or tell me what to do. i can pick stuff up from cvs on maple ave if there is something over the counter or a prescription, but i dont know what is safe to mix and i dont wanna take random meds and mess myself up. right now im mainly scared bc the test says covid and everyone says different things, some ppl say vitamins, some say fever reducer, some say you need antivirals, some say dont take anything unless breathing gets bad. im drinking water and trying to rest but im anxious and checking my temp every 10 min. can u please tell me plain, like what medication must i take, and do i need to isolate and for how long, and when is it serious enough to go in?
target_response:
Hi Marisol — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this. Two positive home tests are very likely a true COVID infection, and it’s understandable to feel anxious. The good news is that many otherwise healthy adults recover with supportive care at home.

**What medication “must” you take?**  
There is no antibiotic for COVID, and antibiotics don’t help unless there’s a separate bacterial infection. For most people, treatment is symptom relief:

- **Fever/body aches:** acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin) as directed on the bottle. Avoid doubling up on acetaminophen if it’s also in cold/flu combo products.  
- **Cough:** honey, warm fluids, or OTC dextromethorphan cough medicine if needed.  
- **Congestion/sore throat:** saline spray, humidifier/steam, lozenges.  
- **Hydration/rest:** keep doing what you’re doing.

**Antivirals:** Paxlovid can reduce risk of severe illness, but it’s mainly recommended for people at higher risk and must be started within **5 days of symptom onset**. Higher-risk factors include pregnancy, immune suppression, significant heart/lung/kidney/liver disease, diabetes, obesity, or being unvaccinated/older age. Paxlovid also has important medication interactions and kidney/liver considerations, so it needs a clinician/pharmacist review. If any of those apply to you, contact your primary care office, urgent care telehealth, or CVS MinuteClinic today/tomorrow.

**Isolation:** Stay home and away from others until your symptoms are improving and you’ve been **fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine**. After that, use extra precautions for **5 more days**: mask around others, improve ventilation, avoid high-risk people, and wash hands often.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue/gray lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, dehydration/can’t keep fluids down, oxygen level <92% if you have a pulse ox, or a fever that won’t come down or lasts >3 days.

Try not to check your temperature every 10 minutes—every 4–6 hours is enough unless you feel worse. I can’t call you directly from here, but I can help you decide if Paxlovid may be appropriate if you tell me your symptoms, when they started, vaccination status, medical conditions, pregnancy status, and current medications.

query: i have a slight cough over the last few days but haven't been exposed to people or traveled to covid 19 areas. i have recently quit smoking so could that be it. what should i do? my name is mike tanner and im prob overthinking this but its making me nervous bc every little cough now makes me think covid even tho like i said i really have not been around anyone sick that i know of and i havent been traveling anywhere weird or to covid 19 areas. my dob is 07/18/1989 if that matters for the chart, and i think my old clinic had me under MRN-4472-kt but idk if that helps here. the cough is not huge or anything, just this annoying little tickle cough that has been hanging around the last few days and i keep wondering if its from quitting smoking recently, like maybe my lungs are clearing out or irritated or whatever. i quit pretty recently and im trying not to run back to cigarettes just bc im anxious. i dont have a fever that i noticed and im not saying i feel super sick, its more like im hyper aware of it. i left a msg for dr elena ruiz but havent heard back yet and i dont wanna clog up urgent care if this is just normal after stopping smoking. you can call me at 215-555-7392 or email mike.tanner89@gmail.com if thats easier, im checking both like crazy. should i stay home and watch it, do steam or cough drops, or should i get tested anyway even with no exposure/travel? also like how many days of a slight cough is normal before i should worry? sorry for rambling, just trying to do the right thing and not freak my family out.
target_response:
Hi Mike — I’m glad you reached out, and it makes sense that a new cough would feel anxiety-provoking right now. Based on what you described — a mild “tickle” cough for a few days, no fever, not feeling very sick, no known sick contacts or travel — this does **not sound immediately alarming**.

Yes, **recently quitting smoking can absolutely cause a temporary cough**. As the airways recover, the tiny clearing hairs in the lungs start working better again, mucus and irritants can move out, and the throat/airways can feel sensitive. This can cause a tickly or mild cough for days to weeks. The important thing is: **don’t let this push you back to cigarettes** — quitting is one of the best things you can do for your lungs.

That said, COVID and other common viruses can still happen even without a known exposure. If you have access to a home COVID test, it’s reasonable to take one for reassurance, especially if you’ll be around family or higher-risk people. If negative but symptoms continue or worsen, repeat in 24–48 hours. While coughing, it’s considerate to stay home as much as practical, wash hands, and consider masking around others.

For now, supportive care is appropriate:  
- Fluids and warm tea  
- Honey if you tolerate it  
- Cough drops/lozenges  
- Humidifier or steamy shower  
- Avoid smoke, vaping, strong odors, and cold dry air  
- Consider saline nasal spray if you have postnasal drip  

You should seek urgent care or medical evaluation sooner if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain, wheezing that’s new or severe, coughing up blood, persistent fever, blue lips, confusion, dehydration, or oxygen levels below ~94% if you check them**. Also get checked if the cough is worsening, lasts more than **2–3 weeks**, or you develop significant fatigue, weight loss, or night sweats.

If you left a message for Dr. Ruiz, it’s reasonable to wait for a callback unless red flags appear. You’re not overreacting — you’re being cautious. For now, monitor symptoms, consider testing, and keep going with the smoking quit plan.

query: can animal byproducts spread coronavirus? do i have to worry about milk, eggs, or fruits? im maria lopez and im asking bc im getting myself all worked up over groceries and i feel dumb but also not dumb bc nobody seems to say the same thing twice. i live at 1847 s 9th st and i usually get groceries from giant on columbus blvd, and yesterday i bought milk, eggs, bananas, apples, and some cut fruit cups and then when i got home i started thinking like wait if this virus is on stuff or from animals or whatever can it be in the milk or on eggs shells or on fruit skin?? i wiped the cartons and washed the fruit but then i was like am i supposed to be washing eggs too or is that worse?? i got kids touching everything and my mom is always telling me not to be paranoid but then she also says dont bring anything in the house, so im just confused. i dont mean meat really, i mean animal byproducts like milk and eggs and then fruits that ppl touch in the store. if a worker coughs near the apples or if the eggs came from chickens or whatever, can coronavirus spread that way or only from people breathing/coughing? i tried calling but got put on hold so if anyone needs to call me back its 267-555-0198, but mainly i just need plain answer like do i throw this food out or is it ok if cooked/washed. i already opened the milk for cereal this morning and now im thinking about it nonstop. sorry this is probably silly but i cant stop worrying and i dont wanna make everybody sick over breakfast.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not silly at all. A lot of mixed messaging has made groceries feel scarier than they need to be.

**Plain answer: you do not need to throw out the milk, eggs, bananas, apples, or cut fruit cups.** COVID-19 is spread mainly through **respiratory droplets/aerosols from infected people breathing, talking, coughing, or sneezing**, especially in close indoor contact. It is **not considered a foodborne illness**, and there’s no good evidence that people get COVID from eating milk, eggs, or fruit.

For your specific groceries:

- **Milk:** If it is pasteurized and has been refrigerated, it is safe. Opening it for cereal this morning is not a COVID risk.
- **Eggs:** Chickens/eggs are not a known route of COVID spread. **Do not wash eggs** before storing them; washing can sometimes make contamination issues worse. Keep them refrigerated, cook them as usual, and wash your hands after handling eggs/shells.
- **Bananas/apples:** Wash whole fruits under running water before eating or cutting. No soap, bleach, or disinfectants on food.
- **Cut fruit cups:** These are meant to be ready-to-eat. Keep them refrigerated and use by the date. Throw away only if they were left out more than about 2 hours, smell/look off, or are expired.

Could a worker coughing near apples leave virus on the surface? In theory, tiny amounts could land on surfaces, but **catching COVID from groceries is considered very unlikely**. The best protection is simple: wash hands after putting groceries away and before eating; avoid touching your face while shopping; and have the kids wash hands when they come in and before snacks/meals. Wiping cartons is optional, not required.

Seek urgent care or emergency help if anyone develops **trouble breathing, persistent chest pain, bluish lips/face, severe weakness/confusion, dehydration, or a high fever that won’t come down**. For possible food poisoning, get care for **bloody diarrhea, severe belly pain, repeated vomiting, or signs of dehydration**.

Bottom line: your breakfast did not put your family in danger from COVID. You’re being careful—not dumb—and the routine steps you already took are enough.

query: hi, i got my tdap shot? shots? when i was little, now that i am 18, should i get them again this year or when should i? (after this epidemic of course) thank you and stay safe. sorry if this is a dumb question lol i just keep seeing random posts about vaccines and i dont wanna be behind on anything before college stuff starts. for the chart im mya bennett, dob 09/18/2007, and i think my MRN KMC-774219 if that helps, but im not 100% sure bc my mom usually keeps all that stuff in her folder and she is at work right now. i dont remember if it was one tdap or like a bunch of tdap shots when i was little, i just remember the nurse saying i cried a lot and got stickers after, so not super useful info. dr. lena ortiz was the last person i saw for a checkup and i meant to ask then but i forgot bc i was more worried about school forms and my anxiety was bad that day. i can also call the office later but phones make me weirdly nervous so i figured id ask here first. i dont have any current symptoms or anything, just trying to figure out if 18 means i need another one now, or if its every so many years, or only if i get cut by rusty metal or something. i also use CVS on 9th ave for most stuff if it matters, like if vaccines are done there or if i need an actual appointment. i know with the epidemic i shouldnt run around getting random appointments unless needed, so im trying to plan and not waste anyones time. thank you again and stay safe please.
target_response:
Hi Mya — not a dumb question at all. This is exactly the kind of thing that’s smart to sort out before college.

Most kids get a **series of DTaP shots** when they’re little, then a **Tdap booster around age 11–12**, then a **tetanus-containing booster every 10 years** after that. So:

- If you received your routine **Tdap at 11–12**, you likely **do not need another one at 18**; your next booster would usually be around **age 21–22**.  
- If you **missed** the 11–12 Tdap, then you should get **one Tdap now**.  
- After that, boosters are typically **Td or Tdap every 10 years**.  
- If you were ever pregnant in the future, Tdap is recommended **during each pregnancy**, regardless of timing.

For cuts/wounds: it’s not only “rusty metal” — the concern is tetanus bacteria from dirt/soil/contaminated wounds. If you have a dirty puncture wound, animal bite, deep cut, or wound with debris and it has been **more than 5 years** since your last tetanus shot, you may need a booster sooner. For clean minor cuts, the cutoff is usually **10 years**.

Since you’re not having symptoms and this is planning, the best next step is to confirm your vaccine record. You can do that by sending a portal message or asking the office to check your immunization history from your last checkup with Dr. Ortiz. If phones make you anxious, a portal message like: “Can you confirm the date of my last Tdap and whether I need any vaccines before college?” is totally reasonable. CVS on 9th Ave may also be able to give Tdap, but it’s ideal to confirm what you actually need first and keep documentation for school forms.

Also worth checking before college: **meningococcal ACWY**, **MenB depending on school/risk**, **HPV**, **MMR**, **varicella**, flu/COVID, and hepatitis vaccines.

Seek urgent care if you get a deep/dirty wound and don’t know your tetanus status, or if a wound develops spreading redness, pus, fever, severe pain, or swelling. Stay safe too!

query: i am positive for alpha 1 but at this point am not being treated, its me melissa grant and i keep feeling like im being annoying calling but honestly i am getting kinda scared. i developed bilateral pneumonia 4 weeks ago, have been on two rounds of levaquin, and other than my fever leaving (which was 102 for three days) don t feel any better. like yes the fever is gone so i guess thats something but my ribs feel like someone punched me, both sides, and whenever i cough, i taste copper which i am assuming is blood. it s probably from how hard i cough, or at least thats what i keep telling myself so i dont panic, but then i start thinking about the alpha 1 thing and pneumonia and i go down a rabbit hole. been to the doc twice, both times with dr. kevin barker, this time added mucinex to the mix to try to loosen things up and i picked it up at cvs on 620 market st, but i swear it just makes me cough more and then the rib pain gets worse. i am trying to drink water, sleep propped up, steam shower, all the stuff, but im wiped out and my chest is so sore i dread coughing. not sure how much longer i can do this! do i need to go back in again or ask for another xray or different med or is this just how pneumonia feels after 4 weeks?? my email is melissa.grant72@gmail.com if someone is messaging me back, i just dont want to miss it bc im honestly losing my mind a little over this.
target_response:
Melissa, you are **not being annoying**—with alpha‑1 and pneumonia symptoms persisting this long, it is very reasonable to call and be rechecked.

While pneumonia can leave people coughing and exhausted for several weeks, the combination of **4 weeks of symptoms**, **two courses of Levaquin**, **ongoing severe cough/rib pain**, and a **copper/blood taste** means you should be reassessed rather than just waiting it out. The rib pain may be from strained chest wall muscles, pleurisy, or even a small rib injury from coughing, but we also need to be sure there isn’t a complication such as persistent pneumonia, fluid around the lung, resistant infection, asthma/COPD-type flare, or—less commonly—a blood clot.

The Mucinex can make you cough more because it loosens secretions; that can be helpful, but if coughing is making pain severe or you are not bringing much up, the plan may need adjusting. Do not start another antibiotic without being seen, because after two rounds it’s important to confirm what we’re treating.

**What I’d recommend next:** call Dr. Barker’s office today and ask for a same-day/next-day reassessment. It would be reasonable to discuss:
- Repeat chest X-ray, and possibly CT chest if the X-ray is not improving or symptoms are out of proportion  
- Pulse ox/oxygen level check  
- Lung exam, possible labs, and sputum culture if you’re producing mucus  
- Pain control options so you can cough/breathe deeply without suffering  
- Whether you need inhalers, steroids, or a different approach given your alpha‑1 status  

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if you have shortness of breath at rest, oxygen level under 92% if you can check it, chest pain/pressure not just with coughing, coughing up more than streaks of blood, fainting, blue lips, confusion, fever returning, worsening weakness/dehydration, or one-sided leg swelling/pain.

Also, I wouldn’t rely on email for something like this—please call or use the patient portal so it’s documented and triaged promptly. You’re right to take this seriously, and you deserve to be seen again.

query: my daughter emily rose was thought to have pneomonia back in sept. and i keep going back over it in my head bc none of it makes sense to me and im scared. after several courses of antibiotics and ct scans they found a spot on both lungs that was growing and then the pet scan showed a spot on the brain. lung biopsy was inconclusive. dr.s beginning thought before biopsy was bacterial infection, that is what dr. nina patel had said was kinda the first guess before we got the biopsy done at st. marys medical center, but now with the brain thing i dont know what to think. is it likely something like this would go to the brain if it was bacterial or does that sound more like cancer spreading? i know you cant diagnose over a message but im sitting here trying not to google every single thing bc then i just panic worse. she did the antibiotics like they told her, more than one round, and we thought okay maybe it will shrink or clear up but instead they said the spots were growing, both lungs, and then pet scan brain spot too. the biopsy not giving answers is what is making me nuts, like how can it be inconclusive when all this is happening. we have another appt coming up but i feel like every day waiting is forever and i dont know what questions to even ask. if the original dr.s thought was bacterial infection before biopsy, can that still be true with a pet scan showing a spot on the brain, or would infection normally show up different? im trying to stay calm for her but honestly im terrified.
target_response:
I’m so sorry you and Emily Rose are going through this. Waiting with incomplete information is incredibly hard, and it makes sense that your mind keeps trying to “solve” it.

To answer your main question: **yes, infection can sometimes involve both the lungs and the brain**, but **cancer is also one of the possibilities doctors have to take seriously** when lung spots are growing and a PET scan shows another spot. A PET scan shows areas of increased activity/metabolism—it **does not by itself prove cancer**. Infection, inflammation, fungal infections, TB-like infections, autoimmune/inflammatory diseases, and cancer can all sometimes “light up.” A brain finding often needs a **dedicated brain MRI with contrast** to better characterize it.

Bacterial infection can spread to the brain in certain ways, such as a **brain abscess** or septic emboli, though that usually comes with particular clinical features and bloodwork/imaging clues. Some infections, especially **fungal or atypical infections**, can be slow, may not respond to standard antibiotics, and can involve lungs and brain. On the other hand, **growth despite several antibiotic courses** is understandably concerning and is why further tissue diagnosis is so important.

An “inconclusive” lung biopsy doesn’t mean nothing is there—it often means the sample didn’t capture the diagnostic part, or tissue was necrotic/inflamed, or there wasn’t enough material for stains/cultures/genetic testing. Sometimes the next step is a **repeat biopsy**, biopsy of a different/larger or more accessible lesion, bronchoscopy, or consultation with pulmonology, oncology, infectious disease, and neurosurgery/neurology depending on the brain imaging.

For the upcoming appointment, I’d ask:
1. What are the top 3 possibilities right now—cancer, bacterial infection, fungal/TB, inflammatory?
2. Has she had a **brain MRI with contrast**?
3. What exactly did the biopsy show, and were special stains/cultures done?
4. Is a repeat biopsy recommended, and from which site?
5. Should infectious disease or oncology be involved now?
6. What symptoms should prompt ER care?

Please seek urgent care now if she has worsening headache, seizure, confusion, weakness/numbness, vision changes, trouble speaking, high fever, stiff neck, severe shortness of breath, coughing blood, or rapid decline.

You’re doing the right thing by not relying on Google and by pushing for clear answers.

query:
hi im maria lopez, dob 03/14/1989, and im just trying to figure out if this is something i should be worried about or if im being dramatic bc i keep going back and forth in my head. ive got a scratching sore thoat and slight chest irritation. with body aches and tired with slight fever? like not a big scary fever but enough where i feel warm and just kinda off, and my throat feels scratchy every time i swallow or talk too much. the chest thing is not like major pain or anything, more like irritation or tight-ish feeling and then i start paying attention to every breath and getting anxious which prob makes it worse. i dont really know if this is just a cold starting or allergies or something else, but the body aches and being tired is what made me stop and ask bc i dont usually feel this wiped out from just a scratchy throat.

ive been trying to drink water and rest but i also have stuff i cant really miss, so im not sure if i should stay home or get checked. i havent done anything fancy for it besides tea and just laying around, and i keep checking if the fever is going up. if somebody can call me back at 646-555-0198 that would help bc i dont wanna sit in a waiting room if this is normal, but i also dont wanna ignore it and then it turns into something. i usually use riverside family clinic if i need to be seen, but wanted to ask first. also sorry if this msg is rambly im just tired and my chest feeling weird is making me nervous.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re not being dramatic. A scratchy/sore throat with body aches, fatigue, and feeling feverish is very commonly the start of a viral infection such as a cold, COVID, or flu. Allergies can cause throat irritation, but they usually don’t cause body aches or fever, so it makes sense that this feels different to you. The mild chest “tight-ish/irritated” feeling can happen with viral inflammation, post-nasal drip, coughing/throat irritation, or anxiety focusing on breathing — but it’s still worth monitoring closely.

**What I’d recommend today:**
- If you can, **take a COVID test** now and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative and symptoms continue. If flu is circulating and symptoms started within the last 48 hours, a clinic may consider flu testing/treatment depending on your risk factors.
- **Stay home if you have a fever or feel significantly unwell**, and mask around others until you’re improving and fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine.
- For comfort: fluids, rest, warm tea/honey, throat lozenges, salt-water gargles, humidifier/steam, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen as directed if you can take them safely.
- Track your temperature, but try not to check constantly — every few hours is enough unless you feel worse.

**You should be seen urgently today or go to urgent care/ER if you develop:**
- Chest pain/pressure, worsening tightness, shortness of breath, wheezing, or trouble speaking in full sentences  
- Blue lips, fainting, confusion, severe weakness  
- Fever ≥103°F, fever lasting more than 3 days, or rapidly worsening symptoms  
- Trouble swallowing saliva, drooling, one-sided throat swelling, stiff neck  
- Signs of dehydration or oxygen saturation <94% if you have a pulse ox  

If the chest irritation stays mild and you’re breathing normally, it’s reasonable to call Riverside Family Clinic for nurse triage rather than sitting in a waiting room. Since I can’t place a call from here, please call the clinic directly and mention the chest tightness plus fever/body aches so they can advise whether testing or an appointment is needed.

query: my son mason ellis has a fever, i am concerned due to the coronavirus ? i know everybody is prob asking the same thing right now but im sitting here just staring at the thermometer and getting more worried by the minute. he is usually running around and being loud and now hes just laying on the couch and i keep checking him like every 20 mins even tho i know thats prob too much. i called and left a msg for dr nina patel but havent heard back yet and the nurse line just keeps putting me on hold so i figured id ask here while im waiting. i dont want to overreact and drag him somewhere if it is not needed, but also i dont want to miss something serious bc of all this coronavirus stuff going around. i can take him to the walk in place if i have to but i really dont know if fever alone means we should go in or stay home and watch it. i also dont know if i should keep him in his room away from the rest of us or if thats being too much. my number is 267-555-0194 if anyone from the office needs to call back, i had it on the voicemail too but im nervous i said it too fast. i have kids meds at home from cvs on walnut st but i dont want to give the wrong thing or cover up something important. sorry this is rambly, im just scared and tired and every news thing says something different. do we need a coronavirus test just because of the fever or only if other stuff starts too?
target_response:
I’m sorry you’re going through this — it’s very understandable to feel anxious when Mason is suddenly quiet and feverish, especially with COVID concerns. Fever alone does **not automatically mean he needs to be taken to urgent care**, but how he looks and acts matters a lot.

A few key things to check while you’re waiting for Dr. Patel/nurse line:

1. **What is his temperature and how was it taken?**  
   If it’s under about 102–103°F and he’s drinking some fluids, breathing comfortably, and can be awakened/comforted, it’s usually reasonable to monitor at home.

2. **Treat for comfort, not just the number.**  
   You can give **acetaminophen/Tylenol** based on his weight. If he’s over 6 months old, **ibuprofen/Motrin/Advil** is also an option. Avoid aspirin. Fever medicine won’t “hide” something dangerous — it helps him feel better and drink. If you tell the nurse his age/weight, they can confirm dosing.

3. **Hydration is important.**  
   Encourage small frequent sips of water, diluted juice, Pedialyte, popsicles, soup, etc. Peeing at least every 6–8 hours is reassuring.

Regarding COVID: a fever **can** be COVID, but it can also be many other common viruses. If you have a home COVID test, it’s reasonable to test now and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative and symptoms continue. Testing is especially helpful if he’s had an exposure, attends school/daycare, or there are vulnerable people at home.

For now, keep him home and limit close contact as much as practical. He doesn’t necessarily need to be isolated alone in his room if that scares him, but have him rest in one area, wash hands, avoid sharing cups/utensils, and consider masks around others if tolerated.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if he has trouble breathing, blue lips, severe sleepiness/confusion, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, a seizure, a rash that doesn’t blanch, fever in a baby under 3 months, or if your parent instinct says he looks seriously ill. Otherwise, it’s okay to monitor and recheck temperature every few hours rather than every 20 minutes.

query: last tuesday i had a flu shot and a neumonia shot at northside family medicine, my name there is robert mclane and the lady at the desk said my MRN HN-77391 if that matters. i am 68 and male. i had a very sore arm like really sore, not just a little tender, and felt bad the next day in which i had blood drawn for a test that dr kelly tran had ordered for my regular check up. i was already nervous cause i dont do great with needles and then the next morning after the shots i felt kind of run down and achy and my arm was hot and hurt to lift, so when the lab results popped up i started googling and that was probly a mistake. the results showed a high white cell blood count as well as high anc and amc. could these high readings be because of the shots the day before? i am not trying to bother anyone but i keep thinking maybe the vaccines made my body react or maybe it means an infection or something worse. my wife keeps saying call the office but i feel dumb calling if its just normal after shots. i also gave them blue cross medicare #BCM-4820-117 when i checked in and i usually use CVS on maple ave for anything they send in, if that matters. if somebody needs to call me back my cell is 412-555-0196 but mostly i just want to know if a flu shot and a neumonia shot can make the white count, anc and amc look high the very next day or if i should be seen again.
target_response:
Hi Robert — you are absolutely not “bothering” anyone. It’s very reasonable to ask about this, especially when results pop up before you’ve had a chance to discuss them.

Yes: a flu shot and a pneumonia shot **can** cause a short-term immune response that may make the **white blood cell count** look higher the next day, including the **ANC** (absolute neutrophil count) and sometimes **AMC** (absolute monocyte count). Feeling run down, achy, and having a very sore/hot arm within 24–48 hours can fit with a normal vaccine reaction. Anxiety/stress around needles and the blood draw can also transiently raise certain white cells.

That said, the meaning depends on **how high** the numbers were and whether there were any other abnormal findings on the CBC. A mild-to-moderate bump right after vaccines is often watched or rechecked. A very high count, persistent elevation, or abnormal cells would need a closer look. The best next step is to **call Dr. Tran’s office** and ask them to review the exact CBC values in context. They may simply recommend repeating the CBC in 1–2 weeks once you’re feeling back to normal.

For the arm: try gentle movement, a cool compress if it feels hot/swollen, and acetaminophen if you can take it safely. Ibuprofen/naproxen can help too, but avoid them if you’ve been told not to take NSAIDs due to kidney disease, ulcers/bleeding risk, blood thinners, etc.

Please seek urgent care sooner if you develop: fever over 101–102°F that persists, rapidly worsening redness/swelling, red streaks, pus/drainage, severe or worsening pain, inability to move the arm, shortness of breath, chest pain, facial/lip/tongue swelling, fainting, confusion, or you feel seriously ill.

Also, for privacy, you don’t need to include insurance numbers or phone numbers in messages like this unless your clinic specifically asks. But overall, given the timing—shots Tuesday, symptoms and blood work the next day—the vaccines are a very plausible explanation. Calling the office is still the right move so they can verify the degree of elevation and decide whether a repeat test is needed.

query: hi i have been have a sore throat for almost a week now, im marisol vega dob 03/14/1989, sorry if im putting too much but i dont know what info you need and im getting nervous. i tried gaghling with warm and apple cider vinegar, like a few times bc my aunt said it helps, and i drank warm water with apple, warm with lemon too, pretty much all the home stuff ppl keep telling me. now i still have a sore throat and burning on my chest? its not like im trying to be dramatic but the burning is making me think is it from the vinegar or acid or something else idk. i keep checking the mirror at my throat and then i get more worried bc it feels scratchy and raw and i keep swallowing to see if it changed which prob makes me notice it more. i havent gone in yet bc i thought it would pass after a couple days and i been busy and also dont wanna sit in urgent care for hrs if they just tell me tea and rest. i can be called at 215-555-0148 if someone needs to ask me more, and i usually use CVS on walnut st if anything has to be sent in. i dont know if i should keep doing the warm lemon stuff or stop the apple cider vinegar since maybe that made the chest burning worse? i also dont know when sore throat is like go get checked vs just wait. i just want to know if this sounds like something i should come in for today or if there is something else safe i can try at home, bc almost a week feels long to me and its making me anxious.
target_response:
Hi Marisol — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this, and it’s understandable to feel anxious when it’s been almost a week and now there’s chest burning too.

A sore throat lasting this long can still be from a viral infection, post-nasal drip/allergies, COVID, irritation from coughing/dry air, reflux, or less commonly strep throat. The apple cider vinegar and lemon are both acidic and can definitely make the throat feel more raw and can trigger reflux/heartburn-type burning in the chest. I would stop the vinegar and lemon for now.

For home care, try:
- Warm water/tea with honey  
- Salt-water gargles instead of vinegar  
- Throat lozenges or hard candy  
- Humidifier/steam and plenty of fluids  
- Acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely  
- Avoid acidic/spicy foods, alcohol, smoking/vaping, and lying down right after eating  
- For chest burning, an antacid like Tums or famotidine may help if you’ve used these safely before

Because it has been nearly a week, I do think it’s reasonable to get checked in the next 24–48 hours, especially if symptoms are not clearly improving. You may need a throat exam and possibly testing for strep, COVID, or flu depending on your symptoms. Antibiotics only help if it’s bacterial, so testing matters.

Please seek urgent care today or the ER sooner if you have trouble breathing, trouble swallowing saliva, drooling, severe one-sided throat pain or swelling, muffled “hot potato” voice, neck swelling/stiffness, fever over 102°F, rash, signs of dehydration, or chest pain that feels like pressure/tightness, spreads to your arm/jaw/back, or comes with shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea.

Also, try not to keep checking your throat repeatedly—looking and swallowing over and over can make the sensation feel worse and increase anxiety.

Bottom line: stop the acidic remedies, treat it gently, and arrange an appointment soon. If the chest burning is mild and started after vinegar/lemon, reflux/irritation is quite possible, but any concerning chest symptoms should be evaluated urgently.

query: hi i have been exposed to someone with covid do i qualify for a test at a laboratory? my name is marissa kendall and im just trying to figure this out before i show up somewhere and they tell me no or make me pay for nothing. i was around the person for a while, not like just passing them in a store, and then they told me after that they tested positive, so now im sitting here overthinking every little cough and sneeze even tho im mostly just worried about bringing it home to people here. i tried looking online but every site says something diff and some say you need symptoms and some say exposure is enough and some say call first, so im confused. my dob is 08/14/1989 if that matters for the form or whatever, and my call back number is 267-555-0196 but texting is easier bc im at work on and off and cant always answer. i was thinking of going to quest diagnostics on 15th st because its close and i can get there without taking off the whole day, but do they actually do covid testing if you were exposed or do i need a dr order first? i dont wanna go to the er for this because that seems dramatic and i also dont want to wait until i feel worse if the whole point is to know early. also how many days after exposure is the test even worth doing? like if i go too soon will it just be negative and not mean anything? sorry im rambling, im just anxious and trying to do the right thing and not expose anybody else if im supposed to quarantine or get tested first.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — you’re not rambling; this is a very reasonable thing to clarify, especially if you’re worried about bringing COVID home to others.

Because you had a **true close exposure** (being around the person for a while, not just briefly passing by), you generally **do qualify for COVID testing**, even if you do not have symptoms. The practical details depend on the testing site, insurance, and whether they require a provider order.

For **Quest Diagnostics on 15th St**, I would **not just walk in**. Quest locations vary in what they collect on-site and whether they require an appointment, online order, or clinician order. Check Quest’s website for that specific location or call ahead. Some testing can be ordered through a provider, and some may be available direct-to-consumer, but cost/coverage can vary—so ask specifically: “Do you do COVID PCR/NAAT testing for exposure, do I need a doctor’s order, and what will it cost with my insurance/self-pay?”

Timing matters:
- If you develop symptoms: **test right away**.
- If you have no symptoms: test around **day 5 after exposure**. Count the exposure day as **day 0**.
- Testing too early can be falsely negative. If you test negative early but symptoms develop or exposure was significant, repeat testing is reasonable.
- Home antigen tests are useful, but if negative after exposure, repeat 48 hours later. A lab PCR/NAAT is more sensitive.

In the meantime, you do **not need the ER** unless you’re very ill. Wear a well-fitting mask around others, avoid close contact with higher-risk people if possible, improve ventilation, and monitor for symptoms for about 10 days after exposure.

If you test positive, stay home and away from others until symptoms are improving and you’re fever-free for 24 hours without fever reducers, then continue masking/precautions for several more days. If anyone at home is older, immunocompromised, pregnant, or has major medical conditions, be extra cautious.

Seek urgent care now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or worsening high fever.

query:
i’m absolutely worried sick about getting coronavirus and what it would mean for me if i got it, because of my diabetes. like i know everyone is anxious but i feel like my brain will not stop going in circles and then i read one thing online and it makes it worse. my name is melissa ryan and i prob sound ridiculous but i’ve had diabetes long enough that any infection talk makes me panic, and now with coronavirus i keep thinking what if i get it and what if my sugars go crazy and what if i end up in the hospital, and then i can’t calm down at all. i tried calling my regular office and left my info, MRN DMC-774921, but i haven’t heard back yet and i don’t want to keep bothering them if everybody is slammed. i usually see dr priya nair for my diabetes stuff and she’s always calm with me but i dont have an appt right now and i’m just sitting here refreshing news pages like an idiot. i have BlueCross PPO #BCP-3901182 but even thinking about what my insurance would cover if i got really sick is making me spiral too, which i know is not helping anything.

is there anything you can say that can help me in any way? like real practical stuff, not just “dont worry” because my anxiety is so high and i feel like i need someone to tell me what matters most for a diabetic person right now. should i be doing anything different day to day, or just the same hand washing and staying away from sick ppl and trying not to panic? thank you. i’m sorry this is long, i’m just scared and i keep imagining the worst.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you do not sound ridiculous. Diabetes can make infections more complicated, and it makes sense that COVID news would hit a very sensitive “what if” button for you. The goal is not to pretend there’s no risk; it’s to focus on what actually reduces risk and gives you a plan.

For people with diabetes, the most important things right now are:

1. **Keep your blood sugars as steady as you can.** Continue your usual diabetes medications/insulin unless your clinician tells you otherwise. Try to eat regularly, hydrate, sleep, and avoid “panic skipping” meals or doses.

2. **Have a sick-day plan ready.** If you develop fever, cough, sore throat, vomiting/diarrhea, or feel ill:
   - Check glucose more often than usual.
   - Stay well hydrated.
   - If you use insulin, do **not** stop basal/long-acting insulin.
   - If you have type 1 diabetes, or are prone to ketosis, check ketones when sugars are high or you feel unwell.
   - Call your diabetes team early for dose guidance, especially if readings are persistently high.

3. **Stock practical supplies.** Aim for at least 1–2 weeks of diabetes meds/insulin, test strips/CGM supplies, ketone strips if relevant, simple carbs for lows, fever medication you can take, and fluids/electrolytes.

4. **Prevention still matters most.** Stay up to date on COVID vaccination/boosters if you’re eligible, wash hands, avoid close contact with sick people, improve ventilation, and consider masking in crowded indoor spaces—especially during surges.

5. **Limit the news loop.** Refreshing news pages is feeding the alarm system in your brain. Try setting two brief “check-in” times per day from reliable sources only, then stop. When panic spikes: breathe in 4 seconds, out 6–8 seconds, repeat for 3–5 minutes; then do one concrete task from the list above.

Please seek urgent care or emergency help if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, severe dehydration, persistent vomiting, moderate/large ketones, or glucose staying very high despite your correction plan.

You’re doing the right thing by reaching out. It’s reasonable to call Dr. Nair’s office again and say you need a diabetes sick-day/COVID plan; that is not “bothering” them.

query:
dear sir, good evening, i am writing again because i am very much tense and not understanding what to do now. my mother is aged about 58 years and is patient of acute diabetic since last 30 years, she is now in ventillation due to pneumonia in lungs and spreading of infection speticemia all over the body and she is having becoly too. it is almost 14 days on ventilation but there is no progressive result of her ailment. she is surviving on artificial lungs. e.c.g. - sr respiratory - 28 oxygen - 84 b. p. - 118/70 heartbeat - 96 pulse - 96 sugar - 130 mg/dl lungs pressure - 39 other organs of the body are working properly and are good in condition till now, doctors are saying wait and watch but we are family people and every hour feels like very heavy. i am sitting outside micu and seeing machines sound and all pipes and i am not able to decide anything, sometimes they say infection is not coming down and sometimes they say kidney and other organs are good, so please guide me sir.

what should i do?. what specific treatment should be given to her?. please help me in this regard. i am in very much need of your help?. i am raashid mustafa, ranchi, jharkhand, and i am son only arranging all things and talking to nurse again and again. mother name - shahin mustafa - 58 years admitted - abdur razzaque ansari weavers hospital fernchizee of apollo group of hospitals. micu - bed no - 07. if any senior chest doctor or infection doctor should be called then tell me, or if any other test for septicemia or lungs pressure is needed then also tell me because here all relatives are asking me only and i am totally helpless and afraid.
target_response:
Dear Raashid, I can understand how frightening it is to sit outside the MICU and see your mother on a ventilator for 14 days. Your anxiety is completely understandable.

In severe pneumonia with septicemia, especially in a person with long-standing diabetes, recovery can be slow and uneven. The fact that her BP is 118/70, pulse 96, sugar 130, and kidneys/other organs are still working is a positive sign. However, an oxygen saturation of 84% is concerning **if that is her SpO₂ despite ventilator support**—please ask the ICU team what her oxygen level is on ABG, what FiO₂ and PEEP she is requiring, and whether this is improving or worsening.

What you should do now is request a **formal family meeting** with the treating ICU consultant, preferably with a **pulmonologist/chest physician and infectious disease specialist** involved. Ask them directly:

1. What organism is causing infection? Are blood/sputum/urine cultures positive? Is it E. coli/“becoly”?  
2. Are antibiotics being given according to culture sensitivity? Is there concern for resistant bacteria or ventilator-associated pneumonia?  
3. What are her latest CBC, CRP/procalcitonin, lactate, kidney/liver tests, ABG?  
4. What does chest X-ray/CT show—pneumonia, ARDS, fluid, collapse, pus collection?  
5. Is bronchoscopy needed to clear secretions or obtain samples?  
6. What is the plan for weaning from ventilator, and if prolonged, whether tracheostomy is being considered?

Specific treatment depends on these results, but generally includes appropriate IV antibiotics, ventilator lung-protective strategy, control of sugar, nutrition, prevention of clots/ulcers, physiotherapy/suctioning, and careful fluid/BP support.

Urgent concerns to ask about include: falling BP needing high medicines, urine decreasing, rising creatinine, worsening oxygen despite high ventilator settings, high lactate/acidosis, new heart rhythm problems, or spreading infection despite antibiotics.

You are not helpless—your role is to ask these structured questions and ensure senior ICU/pulmonary/infection specialists are reviewing her daily. Please try to rest in shifts; this is a long ICU battle.

query: how can one be sure of a diagnosis of pneumonia or pertusses in adults. how do you compare? my name is maria elena rojas and i am 65 years old, dob 08/14/1960, and i know i sound anxious but i am just so confused and tired. my doc here in l.a., dr. steven kim, thinks i have pertusses. but the doctor in mexico where i was originally treated said i had pb pneumonia. i was on intravenous antibiotics for three days in mexico before coming home to my dr. here in l.a. and then i was seen at cedars-sinai urgent care on 8501 wilshire blvd, they put me in under MRN CS-3097742 i think, and the xrays showed lots of mucus in my lungs. i am confused. is there a definitive test for petusses in adults? like a swab or blood test or anything that says yes this is it and not pneumonia? dr said i will be fine. gave me advair and cough medicine and says i will be fine but will cough for a pretty long while until the lungs are clear. he said i do not need antibiotics because i have already had zpack and 10 days of ampicillin, and then 3 days fo intravenous antibiotics. who do i believe? i keep replaying the whole thing because i was so sick in mexico and then flying home feeling weak scared me, and now being told its pertusses makes me worry i was around people and didnt know. never was feeling this sick or tired before or have had any kind of lung involvement in my life. i am a strong healthy woman. no immune deficiency or anything like that. i just want to understand if the xray mucus means pneumonia for sure or can pertusses look like that too.
pii_units: cedars-sinai urgent care on 8501 wilshire blvd
target_response:
Dear Maria Elena, I’m sorry you’ve been through such a frightening and confusing illness. It’s very understandable to feel anxious after being very sick abroad, flying home weak, and then hearing different labels for what’s going on.

**Pneumonia and pertussis can overlap in symptoms**, but they are diagnosed a bit differently:

- **Pneumonia** is usually diagnosed by a combination of symptoms/exam plus a chest X-ray showing an **infiltrate/opacity** consistent with infection in the lung tissue. Blood tests, oxygen level, sputum culture, or CT scan can help in unclear cases, but there is not always one “single” definitive test.
- **Pertussis/whooping cough** in adults can be hard to recognize. The best test is a **nasopharyngeal swab PCR** for *Bordetella pertussis*—a deep swab through the nose, not a regular throat swab. It is most useful in the first **2–3 weeks of cough** and can become falsely negative after antibiotics. A **blood antibody test/serology** may help later, often after 2–8 weeks, but results can be less straightforward.

An X-ray described as “lots of mucus” does **not automatically mean bacterial pneumonia**. X-rays don’t literally show mucus well; they show patterns such as airway thickening, patchy opacities, atelectasis, or infiltrates. Pertussis/bronchitis can cause prominent airway changes and mucus plugging, and pneumonia can also occur during or after pertussis—so both doctors may be describing parts of the same illness.

Given you already had **azithromycin/Z-pack**, **10 days of ampicillin**, and **3 days of IV antibiotics**, it is reasonable that Dr. Kim may not want to add more antibiotics, especially if you are improving, fever is gone, and oxygen levels are okay. Azithromycin is also the standard treatment for pertussis; after completing it, you are generally considered much less contagious after 5 days of therapy.

What I’d do next: ask for the exact X-ray report, consider whether a pertussis PCR/serology is still useful based on cough timing, and schedule follow-up. At age 65, after pneumonia-like findings, many clinicians repeat a chest X-ray in about **6–8 weeks** to confirm clearing.

Seek urgent care for worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen saturation <92%, coughing blood, persistent high fever, or worsening weakness/dehydration.

query: hi, my name is kelly marston and i have a bad cough, sinus congestion and body aches but no fever. should i consult with a doctor regarding the current covid19 pandemic? i keep going back and forth bc i dont wanna be dramatic or take up an appt if its just a normal cold or allergies, but also im sitting here coughing and thinking ok what if this is that covid thing and im being dumb by waiting. the cough is annoying and kinda deep sometimes, then other times its just dry, my nose/sinuses feel so stuffed up and my whole body feels sore like when you know youre coming down with something, but again no fever when i check. i havent really been out much except grabbing stuff quick and picking up medicine at cvs on 22nd and spruce, and now im second guessing every little trip i made. i tried calling around but the hold times are crazy and i dont know if they want people like me to come in or stay home and just monitor it. my regular dr is dr. maya patel but i havent seen her in awhile so idk if i should call there first or do urgent care or some telehealth thing. if someone needs to call me back my cell is 267-555-0148, but honestly im mostly just trying to know if cough + sinus congestion + body aches with no fever is enough to be seen right now. i dont feel like im dying or anything, no fever, but im anxious and i dont wanna expose anyone if im contagious. should i isolate like it could be covid19, and at what point is it not ok to just wait it out?
target_response:
Hi Kelly — you’re not being dramatic. A cough with body aches and sinus congestion can certainly be a “regular” viral cold, allergies, flu, or COVID, and **not having a fever does not rule out COVID**. Given the cough and body aches, it’s reasonable to treat this as potentially contagious for now.

**What I’d recommend next:**

1. **Stay home and limit contact** as much as possible until you’re clearly improving. If you must be around others, wear a well-fitting mask and avoid close indoor contact.
2. **Take a COVID test** if you have access to one. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours, since early tests can miss infection.
3. **Call Dr. Maya Patel’s office first** or use their patient portal if available. This is exactly the kind of situation primary care/telehealth is meant for. You likely do **not** need to go to urgent care in person unless symptoms worsen or you have red flags.
4. **Supportive care:** fluids, rest, saline nasal spray/rinse, humidifier/steam, honey for cough if you can take it, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen for body aches if safe for you. Avoid combining multiple cold medicines with the same ingredients.

**Seek urgent care or emergency help now** if you develop any of the following: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, oxygen levels below 92–94% if you have a pulse oximeter, coughing up blood, or symptoms that are rapidly worsening. Also get medical advice promptly if you’re pregnant, immunocompromised, have significant asthma/COPD/heart disease, or are at higher risk, because treatments such as antivirals work best early.

If symptoms remain mild, it’s okay to monitor at home, but I would still **reach out to Dr. Patel or a telehealth service** for guidance and testing recommendations. And yes — for now, isolate/mask like it could be COVID until you know more and are improving.

query: hi, my name is jason miller and i am experiencing neck strain, and i have had a weird/burning sensation in my chest. sometimes it leads muscle tension. been going on since friday last week. i keep trying to tell myself its prob just posture or stress or sleeping dumb on my pillow but then the chest burning happens and i get worried and then i tense up more, which prob makes the neck strain worse, so its like a weird loop. i sit a lot during the day and i know i hunch over my laptop, and i did carry some heavy grocery bags last week too, but i cant really tell if that matters or if its something totally separate. its not like crushing pain or anything, more like odd heat/burning and tightness, and then my shoulders/neck feel pulled and stiff. i was going to just wait it out but since its been since friday last week i figured i should ask before i ignore something i shouldnt. i havent been sure if i should call dr. nina patel or go to urgent care or if thats overreacting. i can be reached at 215-555-0198 if someone needs to call me back, tho texting is easier bc i keep checking my phone every 5 mins anyway. also i get anxious about chest stuff bc everyone always says dont mess around with it, but i also dont want to make a big deal if its just muscle tension or reflux or whatever. i didnt change anything major besides drinking more coffee lately and sleeping kinda bad. just want to know if this sounds like neck strain causing weird chest burning/tension or if i should be seen today.
target_response:
Hi Jason — I’m glad you reached out. What you’re describing *could* fit with a posture/strain pattern: hunching over a laptop, poor sleep, carrying heavy bags, and anxiety-related muscle tension can all cause neck/shoulder tightness and even chest wall tightness. Increased coffee and poor sleep can also trigger reflux, which often feels like burning/heat in the chest and can worsen when you’re stressed.

That said, because this is a **new chest burning/tightness sensation that has persisted or recurred since Friday**, it’s reasonable to be checked rather than assuming it’s only muscular. I would recommend you **call Dr. Nina Patel’s office today** for guidance and a same-day/next-day visit. If they can’t see you today and the chest symptoms are ongoing or recurring, **urgent care today** is appropriate—they can check vitals, examine your chest/neck, and usually do an EKG if needed. That is not overreacting.

Please go to the **ER now / call 911** if you have any of the following: chest pressure/heaviness, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea/vomiting, dizziness/fainting, pain spreading to the arm/jaw/back, symptoms brought on by exertion, irregular heartbeat, new weakness/numbness, severe headache, fever, or worsening/severe pain.

While arranging care, you can try supportive measures if symptoms are mild: gentle neck/shoulder stretches, heat to the neck/upper back, frequent laptop breaks, keeping the screen at eye level, avoiding heavy lifting, reducing coffee, avoiding late/spicy meals, and trying an antacid if the burning feels reflux-like. Acetaminophen or ibuprofen can help muscle strain if you can take them safely, but don’t use them to “push through” worsening chest symptoms.

The “loop” you described—chest sensation → worry → more tension—is very real, but the safest approach is to rule out anything concerning first. If you’re currently having chest tightness/burning that isn’t clearly improving, I’d lean toward being seen today.

query: re: covid-19 -- is it safe to go out in public without a face mask on right now as long as the recommended 6ft distance is maintained? i’m lisa marie kendall and i keep going back and forth on this bc every article says something a little diff and i dont wanna be dramatic but also dont wanna be dumb. i’ve been trying to stay home at 4829 spruce ave, apt 3b, lancaster pa and only do curbside for food, like literally havent gone inside anywhere if i can help it. am wondering if picking up groceries curbside (have to get out of car and open trunk for store employee) is safe? like they bring the bags out and i stay by the back of the car, but i still have to pop the trunk and sometimes they are right there near me and i feel weird bc its not always exactly 6 ft for a second, and if im not wearing a face mask is that a bad idea right now? i dont have symptoms or anything, no fever/cough that i know of, but im worried about touching the trunk, bags, keypad on my phone, steering wheel, all of it. i was gonna do a pickup tomorrow morning but now im anxious and thinking maybe i should cancel or ask them to just leave it beside the car?? my callback number is 717-555-2096 if needed but really i just need a straight answer, like is distance enough for this quick curbside thing or should i wear a mask every time even outside? also if the store employee isnt wearing one, does that change it? sorry this is rambling, just trying to not mess this up.
target_response:
Hi Lisa — you’re not being dramatic or dumb; you’re trying to make a reasonable decision in a situation where advice has often changed.

**Straight answer:** for a quick curbside grocery pickup outdoors, the risk is generally **low**, especially if you are only near the employee briefly. But **6 feet is not a magic shield**, and if there’s any chance you’ll be within a few feet of someone — especially if they are not masked — I’d recommend wearing a **well-fitting mask**. A KN95/N95 or good surgical mask is more protective than a loose cloth mask.

For your pickup tomorrow, I would **not cancel** solely because of this. A practical plan:

1. **Wear a mask before the employee approaches.**
2. **Pop the trunk from inside the car if you can**, then step back or stay in the car.
3. If you need to get out, stand several feet away while they load the bags.
4. It’s reasonable to ask: “Would you mind setting the bags in the trunk/next to the car while I step back?”
5. Afterward, use **hand sanitizer** before touching your phone/steering wheel much, or wash hands when you get home.

The risk from **touching bags, the trunk, phone, steering wheel, etc.** is much lower than the risk from breathing shared air at close range. You don’t need to obsessively disinfect every item, but basic hand hygiene is smart: sanitize after pickup, avoid touching your face, and wash hands after putting groceries away.

If the store employee **isn’t wearing a mask**, that does increase your reason to wear one and keep distance, but a brief outdoor interaction is still usually much safer than going inside a crowded store.

Also, staying up to date on COVID vaccination/boosters, if you’re eligible, lowers your risk of severe illness.

Seek medical care urgently if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, or oxygen levels below about 92% if you monitor them**. Otherwise, if you develop fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, or known exposure, consider testing and masking around others.

Bottom line: **curbside pickup is a good, low-risk choice — wear a mask during the interaction and use hand hygiene.**

query: why are so many people with diabetes dying from the coronavirus? it’s really frightening me. my name is tanya morales and i know this probably sounds like im spiraling but i keep seeing posts and news clips and then ppl on facebook saying “underlying conditions” like its just this casual thing, and diabetes keeps being listed right there and i dont understand if its bc of sugar levels, immune system, kidneys, weight, meds, or what. i asked dr kevin patel once on a phone visit but i got nervous and forgot half my questions, and now im sitting here at night googling which i know is dumb but i cant stop. every article makes it sound like if someone has diabetes and catches covid then thats it, like theyre automatically gonna die, and i need somebody to explain it in plain english not scare words. is it mostly older ppl? is it uncontrolled diabetes? is it people already in the hospital? or is any diabetic person just at huge risk even if they seem ok? i also dont get why the reports never say if they had other stuff going on too, they just say diabetes and then i panic. i have my insurance card sitting here too, blue cross ppo #BCP-3928841, because i was thinking maybe i should book another telehealth thing but i dont even know what to ask besides basically “how scared should i be.” sorry this is long, im not trying to be dramatic, its just really frightening me and i feel like every answer online is either too technical or too vague and then i start imagining the worst.
target_response:
Hi Tanya — I’m really glad you asked, and you’re not being dramatic. The way “underlying conditions” gets said in the news can feel cold and terrifying, and it often leaves out the details that matter.

In plain English: **diabetes does not mean that catching COVID is automatically fatal.** Many people with diabetes recover. But diabetes can **increase the risk of severe illness**, especially when combined with other factors.

The risk tends to be higher in people who have one or more of these:

- **Older age**, especially over 65  
- **Poorly controlled blood sugars** or very high A1c  
- **Kidney disease, heart disease, high blood pressure, lung disease**  
- **Obesity**  
- **Weakened immune system** or major medical problems  
- Being **already hospitalized or medically fragile**

Why diabetes matters: high blood sugar can make it harder for the immune system to fight infections, and infections can also make blood sugar rise, creating a difficult cycle. COVID can also stress the heart, kidneys, and blood vessels — areas that may already be vulnerable in some people with diabetes.

So the key distinction is: **diabetes is a risk factor, not a sentence.** A younger person with diabetes that is well-managed and no major complications is generally in a very different situation than an older person with long-standing uncontrolled diabetes plus kidney or heart disease.

What you can do now:

1. **Book the telehealth visit with Dr. Patel** if it will help you feel grounded. Ask:  
   - “What is my personal COVID risk?”  
   - “What was my last A1c?”  
   - “Do I have kidney/heart complications?”  
   - “What should I do if I test positive?”  
   - “Would I qualify for antiviral treatment like Paxlovid?”  
2. Stay up to date on **COVID vaccines/boosters** and flu vaccine.  
3. Keep a **sick-day diabetes plan**: fluids, glucose checks, ketone checks if advised, and when to call.  
4. Avoid doom-scrolling at night — it genuinely fuels panic.

Seek urgent care if you have **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, severe weakness, dehydration, persistent very high sugars, vomiting, or ketones**.

Also, for privacy: avoid sharing insurance numbers in messages or public forums. You deserve clear answers, not fear.

query:
can the coronavirus leave lung scarring? i thought i heard something about it and now i cant get it out of my head. im mike hanley and i know this prob sounds like me spiraling on google but i was reading stuff in the penn medicine portal after messaging dr. priya nolan and i swear there was something about lungs not looking normal after, or like scarring, or maybe i misunderstood it. my chart thing has MRN PM-774091 on it if that matters but im not trying to make this a whole official thing, i just need someone to explain it in normal words bc every article says something different and then the comments make it worse.

like does it mean actual permanent scars in the lungs or just inflammation that shows up weird for awhile? and is that only if somebody was super sick and in the hospital, or can it happen from a more mild case too? i keep thinking about ppl saying their xray was clear and then later they had problems and idk if thats real or just internet panic. i dont wanna be dramatic but lung stuff freaks me out more than anything, esp when you hear cough and shortness of breath and all that and then ppl say it can leave damage. if someone had coronavirus and felt better, would they need another xray or ct scan to check, or is that overkill unless symptoms stay bad? also if there is scarring can it heal or is that like forever forever? sorry this is rambling, i just keep rereading the same 3 pages and making myself more nervous. if you need to reply to my email its mike.hanley82@mail.com but really i just want the basic answer, like yes/no/maybe and when to worry.
target_response:
Hi Mike — this is a very understandable worry, and you’re not being dramatic. COVID/lung articles can be confusing because they use words like “scarring,” “fibrosis,” “changes,” and “inflammation” interchangeably when they don’t always mean the same thing.

**Basic answer: yes, COVID can leave lung changes in some people, but permanent scarring is much less common and is mainly seen after severe illness.** People who were hospitalized, needed oxygen/ICU care, had severe pneumonia/ARDS, or had abnormal imaging during the infection are at the highest risk.

For many people, especially after a mild infection, any “abnormal” findings on imaging are more like **temporary inflammation or healing tissue**—often described as “ground-glass changes” or residual opacities. These can take **weeks to months** to clear and do not necessarily mean permanent damage.

A **clear chest X-ray** is generally reassuring, but it’s true that a CT scan can sometimes see subtle changes that an X-ray misses. That does *not* mean everyone needs a CT. CT scans involve more radiation and can find small incidental things that create more anxiety without changing treatment.

In general, if someone had COVID and is now feeling well—breathing normally, no ongoing chest pain, no worsening cough, normal activity level—**routine repeat X-ray or CT is usually overkill**. Follow-up imaging is more commonly considered if symptoms persist or worsen, especially shortness of breath, ongoing low oxygen levels, persistent chest tightness, or a cough that isn’t improving after several weeks.

If true scarring/fibrosis occurs, some changes may be long-lasting, but even then symptoms and lung function can improve over time with healing, rehab, inhalers if appropriate, and conditioning. “Scarring” does not automatically mean progressive or “forever forever” lung failure.

**Get urgent care now** if you have severe or worsening shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, coughing blood, fainting, or oxygen readings persistently below ~92% if you use a pulse oximeter.

If you’re currently having lingering breathing symptoms, it would be reasonable to message Dr. Nolan with specifics: when COVID was, current symptoms, activity tolerance, cough, fever, and any oxygen readings. If you’re feeling okay, reassurance and monitoring is usually enough.

query: my name is cathy tate and im sorry this is prob dumb but im scared and my brain is going in circles. my dr. dr. benjamin kline did a routine cnbc last week at westbrook family medicine, i think it was just my yearly blood work or whatever, nothing crazy. his nurse called and my blood showed signs of anemia. ok today his nurse called again and the blood they deeper searched on showed: the disease of chronic pneumonia? i wrote it down exactly how she said it but maybe i heard wrong bc i was at work and shaking. my chart thing has MRN WFM-774219 if that matters. ok i do not have hiv/aids. i already said that to them bc i know ppl ask and it makes me feel weird. my question is she said no cure. like just said it fast and then told me they would call back, so now im sitting here googling and crying. my mom linda marie tate died from a chronic infection in her longs acronym boop. i contacted cdc.gov they put me in touch with center for rare diseases. no cure for mom. question: the told me boop was not genetically transferred. this is exactly how my moms lung diseases started, blood weird, then cough stuff, then they kept saying chronic something and then boom boop and oxygen and all that. is thimy lung disease genetic? is it curable? help please. im not trying to be dramatic but i have kids and i keep thinking what if this is the same thing starting. nurse said they sent something to cvs on baltimore ave but i dont even know if its for iron or infection or what. if anyone calls my number is 215-555-9038 and my email is cathy.tate77@gmail.com bc i keep missing calls at work and then panic more.
target_response:
Hi Cathy — this is **not dumb at all**. It’s completely understandable to be scared, especially with what happened to your mom and with a rushed phone call that used frightening words.

A few important clarifications:

1. **A CBC** is the routine blood count test. If it showed anemia, the “deeper search” was likely things like iron levels, ferritin, B12/folate, kidney/liver tests, or inflammation markers.

2. What you heard as “the disease of chronic pneumonia” may very well have been **“anemia of chronic disease”** or **“anemia of chronic inflammation.”** That is a common phrase and can be misheard. It does **not** mean you have pneumonia, and it does **not** diagnose a lung disease by itself.

3. **BOOP** is now usually called **organizing pneumonia** or **cryptogenic organizing pneumonia**.
