query: i’m so scared that i will die if i get the coronavirus, due to type1 diabetes? like i know maybe i sound dramatic but i can’t stop thinking about it and i keep googling stuff and then i panic worse. my name is maria elena rios and i have had type1 since i was a kid and every time they say people with diabetes are high risk i feel like they mean me and that im just waiting for something bad to happen. my dob is 05/14/1998 if that matters for risk stuff but i dont even know what counts as young enough to be ok bc type1 makes everything feel different. i havent been around anyone confirmed with it but people cough at work and in stores and i swear i feel my chest get tight just from being scared. my sugars go up when im stressed too so then i get more scared bc im like is it stress or am i getting sick or is my body already messed up. i asked dr. nisha patel before about sick day rules but i wasnt really thinking coronavirus then, i was just thinking regular flu/cold, and now im scared if i get a fever or cough i wont know when to go in or when to stay home. i dont want to overreact and clog up the hospital but i also dont want to wait too long and die in my sleep or something. is type1 diabetes by itself enough that coronavirus could kill me even if im otherwise ok? should i be doing anything different now besides washing hands and trying not to touch my face? sorry im rambling i just need someone to tell me what is actually true and not just the scary stuff online.
target_response:
Maria Elena, I’m really glad you reached out. You are not being dramatic—living with type 1 diabetes can make health warnings feel very personal and frightening, and “high risk” language online often sounds scarier than it is.

Here is the practical truth: **type 1 diabetes does increase risk of complications from infections like COVID**, especially if blood sugars are running high, if someone develops dehydration or ketones, or has other medical problems. But **type 1 diabetes by itself does not mean you are likely to die if you get COVID**, especially as a young adult who is otherwise well and has a plan. Many people with T1D recover from COVID at home.

What matters most is being prepared:

- Stay up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters if you’re able.
- Keep rapid tests at home if possible.
- Have insulin, pump/CGM supplies if applicable, ketone strips, glucose tabs, and fever medicine available.
- If you develop symptoms, test and **call Dr. Nisha Patel’s office early**, especially within the first few days—people with diabetes may qualify for antiviral treatment like Paxlovid depending on timing/medications.
- During illness: **never stop basal insulin**, check glucose more often, drink fluids, and check ketones if sugars are high or you feel sick.

For sick-day guidance, a common rule is: if glucose is persistently above ~250–300 mg/dL, check ketones. **Moderate/large ketones, vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, or rising sugars despite correction insulin need urgent medical advice.**

Go to urgent care/ER or call emergency services if you have: trouble breathing, chest pain that doesn’t go away, confusion, bluish lips/face, severe weakness, oxygen saturation below about 94% if you have a pulse oximeter, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, or moderate/large ketones.

The chest tightness you feel when you see people coughing may very well be anxiety—stress can absolutely raise glucose. But if tightness is persistent, occurs with shortness of breath, fainting, or pain spreading to arm/jaw, get urgent care.

For now, besides handwashing: mask in crowded indoor places, improve ventilation, avoid close contact with sick people, and make a written sick-day plan with Dr. Patel. Also, please consider limiting Googling and using reliable sources only—your fear deserves care too.

query: if my wife melissa grant (she is a nurse in a surgery at northside surgical center) comes into contact with a patient with the virus, is she likely to then get it herself, if she is wearing gloves and mask etc? im asking bc she came home kinda quiet after work and said there was a possible positive patient or maybe confirmed, idk she didnt wanna tell me too much bc of privacy stuff, and now im sitting here over thinking every cough in the house. she says she had gloves and a mask and did what they tell them to do, washed hands like crazy, changed before coming home, all that, but i still dont get how much that really protects her when she is right there near the patient in surgery area and people are breathing and coughing and all. she is trying to be calm and says this is just part of her job but im not calm lol. we have kids at home and her mom sometimes comes over and im wondering do we need to keep everyone away from her now or sleep in diff rooms or wipe down the car and door knobs every 5 mins. she told me to ask dr. nina patel but i dont wanna bug them after hours if this is a dumb question. if someone can call me at 412-555-0198 or email dan.grant82@gmail.com that would help, bc i keep reading different things online and its making it worse. im not saying she has symptoms, she doesnt, im just scared that contact one time is enough even with ppe. does mask and gloves make it unlikely or is it still pretty likely?
target_response:
Dan, this is not a dumb question at all. It’s very understandable to feel anxious when Melissa works in a clinical/surgical setting and there may have been a patient with a contagious respiratory virus.

In general, **proper PPE does significantly reduce the risk of infection**. If she was wearing the recommended mask, gloves, eye protection/face shield if indicated, practiced hand hygiene, changed before leaving, and followed her workplace protocols, then a single contact does **not** automatically mean she is likely to get sick. The risk depends on details such as how close/long the exposure was, whether the patient was coughing, whether any aerosol-generating procedures occurred, and whether PPE was used correctly the whole time. In surgical centers, staff are trained for exactly these situations.

Because she is **asymptomatic**, I would not recommend panic measures like wiping doorknobs every 5 minutes or isolating her from the whole family unless her occupational health team specifically tells her she had a high-risk exposure. What I would suggest:

- Have Melissa follow up with **Northside Surgical Center’s employee/occupational health protocol** for possible exposure guidance.
- Monitor for symptoms for the recommended window for that virus — often fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, fatigue, shortness of breath, loss of taste/smell, vomiting/diarrhea depending on the illness.
- Use good home habits: handwashing, avoid sharing cups/utensils, routine cleaning of high-touch surfaces, and good ventilation.
- If her mom is older or high-risk, consider limiting close contact for a few days until Melissa knows whether the exposure was considered significant.
- If Melissa develops symptoms, she should mask, avoid close contact at home, test if appropriate, and contact employee health/primary care.

Seek urgent care right away if she develops **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels low if you use a pulse oximeter**.

Also, for privacy and safety, I can’t call or email from here, and you may want to avoid posting phone/email publicly. But medically: **PPE plus proper hand hygiene usually makes transmission much less likely, not “pretty likely.”** Your concern is normal, but Melissa’s precautions matter a lot.

query: my daughter lily ann parker is 11 years old she has has pneumonia she has been sick since january 3rd symptoms keep changing and i feel like im losing my mind bc every day its something different. she is up at night itching all over her upper torso, head, and ears, like scratching til she cries and then i cry too bc i dont know what else to put on her. she has major headache and abdominal pain.. she is very upset and crying because this is going on way to long and she has seen many doctors with no relief any suggestions. we saw dr. megan rios last week and then went to st. marys children's urgent care at 1800 maple ave today and i keep repeating the same story and still nobody really tells me why this is happening. tried most itching products, benadryl cream, oatmeal bath, lotion, changed laundry soap, washed sheets, nothing is really helping. what could be causing the head pain and stomach pain. they check her today for appendicitis and said they were worried enough to look but now we are waiting and im scared to take her home if she is still hurting like this. she had the pneumonia and then it just keeps shifting from cough and tired to itching and head and belly and i dont know if meds can do that or if its allergy or something missed. we use cvs on 9th street and her insurance is blue cross hmo #BC-4478201 but im worried they are just gonna send us everywhere and nobody fixes it. if anyone calls me back my phone is 717-555-0298, i just need to know what to ask next or if i should push for more testing tonight.
target_response:
I’m so sorry Lily is going through this — and I understand why you feel scared and exhausted. When a child has been sick for weeks and the symptoms keep changing, it is very reasonable to ask for a more complete explanation before going home.

Since she is **currently being evaluated for abdominal pain/possible appendicitis**, I would not leave until you have spoken directly with the clinician about: what appendicitis testing showed, what they think is causing the pain, and what would make you return tonight.

The **severe itching**, especially if it started after pneumonia treatment, could be from several things:  
- **Medication reaction** to an antibiotic, steroid, cough medicine, or ibuprofen/acetaminophen product  
- **Hives/allergic reaction**, even without an obvious trigger  
- **Post-viral or post-infection rash/itching**  
- Less commonly, issues involving the **liver/bile flow** can cause intense itching, sometimes with belly pain  
- Skin causes like eczema, scabies, or lice can also involve the scalp/ears, though the abdominal pain/headache makes me want broader evaluation

The **headache and stomach pain** could be from dehydration, constipation, medication side effects, ongoing infection/inflammation, migraine, sinus involvement, or abdominal causes like appendicitis. Some “walking pneumonia” infections can also cause headache, belly pain, and rashes/itching.

Tonight, I would ask specifically:  
1. “Could this be a medication reaction?” and review every medication she has taken since Jan 3.  
2. “Can we check bloodwork including CBC, liver tests/bilirubin, kidney function/electrolytes, and inflammatory markers?”  
3. “Does she need urinalysis, repeat chest exam/x-ray, or imaging for the belly pain?”  
4. “What is the plan for controlling itching tonight?”

For itching, avoid more **Benadryl cream** if it is irritating her skin. Ask if she can take an oral antihistamine such as cetirizine/loratadine during the day and diphenhydramine at night, based on her weight and other meds.

Go to ER/urgent care immediately if she has trouble breathing, facial/lip/tongue swelling, stiff neck, confusion, worst headache, persistent vomiting, worsening right-lower belly pain, dehydration, blistering/peeling rash, purple spots, yellow eyes/skin, or dark urine.

query: my 3 year old son mason reed is complaint of stomach pain he also has fever and 1 week before he was diagnosed with pneumonia. he was on amoxicillin from dr nina patel and we picked it up at cvs on maple ave, he just finished his antibiotics yesterday and this morning he has fever stomach pain increased heart rate what is causing it. i am so worried bc last week when they said pneumonia i thought ok at least we know what it is and the medicine will fix it, and he was acting a little better for couple days like playing with cars and asking for snacks, but now today he is just laying around and saying tummy hurt and crying off and on. he keeps touching his belly but he cant really explain if its sharp or cramp or just hurts all over bc he is only 3. the fever is back and his heart feels like beating so fast when i put my hand on his chest, and that is scaring me more than anything. he did finish all the amoxicillin like we were told, i did not skip doses, only thing is he hated it and sometimes spit a little but mostly took it. he had some loose poop with the antibiotics too so idk if stomach pain is from that or from fever or something worse. he drank a little water this morning but not much food, maybe a few bites. no one told me what to watch after antibiotics are done, just said come back if worse, but i dont know if this is normal after pneumonia or a reaction or infection coming back. should i take him er now or call the dr first, im scared to wait if increased heart rate with fever means something bad.
target_response:
I’m sorry you’re going through this — it’s very scary when a 3-year-old seems to improve and then suddenly has fever, belly pain, and a fast heartbeat again.

Given Mason just finished antibiotics for pneumonia and is now **more tired/lying around, crying with abdominal pain, drinking less, fever is back, and his heart feels very fast**, I would **not wait this out at home**. He should be evaluated **today**, and if you cannot reach his pediatrician immediately for a same-day visit, I’d take him to a **pediatric urgent care or ER now**.

A few things could be going on:

- **Fever itself can make the heart beat fast**, and dehydration can make it faster.
- The pneumonia may not be fully resolved, or there could be a complication or a new infection.
- Antibiotics can cause **loose stools and stomach cramping**, but significant pain plus fever and lethargy needs evaluation.
- Other causes of belly pain/fever in kids include **appendicitis, urinary infection, viral illness, constipation**, or less commonly antibiotic-associated bowel infection.

While you’re getting ready to go:  
- Offer small frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution.  
- You may give **acetaminophen or ibuprofen according to his weight and the package/pediatrician instructions** to help the fever, unless he has been told not to take them.  
- Note his temperature, last dose of fever medicine, last urine, and bring the antibiotic bottle/details.

Please go to the **ER immediately** if he has any of these: trouble breathing, blue lips, grunting or ribs pulling in, very sleepy/hard to wake, stiff neck, severe or worsening belly pain, belly swelling, repeated vomiting, blood in stool, no urine for 8+ hours, signs of dehydration, or if his heart rate stays very fast even after fever medicine and fluids.

Your worry is appropriate. This may still turn out to be something treatable like dehydration/fever or antibiotic stomach upset, but with his recent pneumonia and current change in behavior, he needs an in-person exam and possibly oxygen check, lung exam, hydration assessment, and sometimes labs or imaging.

query: hi im jessica morales and im sorry if this is a dumb question but how soon after being infected can someone transmit covid-19? like i dont even know if i was actually infected yet, thats the whole thing making me spiral. i was around my cousin saturday night and then monday she texted saying her test was positive, and now im sitting here replaying every stupid minute like did i hug her too long, did we share chips, was i already around ppl after that. i work at riverbend dental billing and i was in the office yesterday doing the front desk phones bc maria called out, and now im worried i could have passed it to coworkers before even feeling sick. i dont have a fever right now, just tired but that could be me stressing and not sleeping. i called lakeside family clinic this morning but they said the nurse line is backed up and to message, and i hate waiting bc my dad is older and i usually bring him groceries. my number is 312-555-6048 if they need to call me back but honestly i just need to know the timing. can someone spread it like the next day after exposure or only once symptoms start? i keep seeing different stuff online and its making me nuts. i have a test at home but dont wanna waste it too early if it wont show yet, and also dont wanna go to work and be the person who gets everyone sick. i was supposed to have dinner with my sister friday too and now im thinking cancel?? pls just tell me the earliest someone can be contagious after getting covid-19 bc im trying to do the right thing and not overreact but also not be careless.
target_response:
Hi Jessica — not a dumb question at all. This is exactly the kind of situation that makes people anxious, especially when you’re trying to protect coworkers and your dad.

**In general, people with COVID are most contagious starting about 1–2 days before symptoms begin and during the first few days of illness.** After an exposure, it usually takes a little time for the virus to multiply enough for someone to become contagious. With current variants, symptoms often start around **2–4 days after exposure**, but it can be longer. So, if you were exposed Saturday night, it would be **uncommon to be contagious the very next day**, but by days 2–4 after exposure it becomes more possible — especially if symptoms start.

Because your cousin tested positive Monday, it’s very possible she was already contagious Saturday, so it’s reasonable to take precautions.

Here’s what I’d suggest:

1. **Test timing:** If you have no symptoms, an at-home antigen test is most useful around **day 3–5 after exposure**. If you test early and it’s negative, repeat it **48 hours later**. If you develop symptoms, test right away and repeat in 48 hours if negative.

2. **Work:** Let your supervisor know you had a close COVID exposure. Follow Riverbend’s policy, but at minimum wear a well-fitting mask at work for the next several days, avoid eating near coworkers, and stay home if symptoms develop.

3. **Your dad:** Since he’s older, I’d avoid close indoor contact for now. If he needs groceries, consider dropping them at the door, wearing a mask, and keeping distance until you’re past the higher-risk window and/or have repeat negative tests.

4. **Dinner Friday:** I’d consider postponing or only going if you remain symptom-free and have a negative test that day — and ideally still mask around higher-risk people.

Watch for symptoms like fever, sore throat, cough, congestion, body aches, headache, loss of taste/smell, vomiting/diarrhea, or unusual fatigue. Seek urgent care now if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, or oxygen levels below 92% if you monitor them.

You’re not overreacting — you’re being considerate. The key is masking, smart testing, and avoiding high-risk contacts for a few days.

query: hi .. i have been diagnosed with pnemonia and have not slept in 4 days because of the coughing .. i'm melissa grant and i swear i am not usually this dramatic but i feel like im coming unglued. i'm hurting everywhere, like ribs, back, chest, head from coughing so much, and going sligtly insane and cannot get to sleep no matter how much i try .. as soon as i breath in, it hurts and a cough .. any suggestions ? i was seen at northside urgent care and dr. kevin malloy said pnemonia, and i know they said it can be awful but i didnt think it would be like this, like literally every time i start to drift off my body does that deep inhale thing and then boom coughing fit all over again. i keep sitting up on pillows, laying on my side, laying flat, hot tea, steamy shower, nothing is letting me actually sleep and im so tired i feel shaky and weird. i keep worrying im making it worse bc i cant rest, and then i worry the coughing is doing damage because my whole chest feels bruised. i dont know if this is normal pnemonia misery or if i should call back or go in again, i dont wanna be annoying but 4 days with basically no sleep feels nuts. i use cvs on maple ave if anything otc is okay to ask about but i dont want to mix things wrong or take something that makes breathing worse. i just need like practical ideas for getting even 2 hours of sleep without coughing myself awake. no matter how slow i breath in it catches and hurts and then i cough again. im honestly exhausted and scared and just need some advice please.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m really sorry you’re going through this. Pneumonia coughing can be absolutely miserable, and 4 days with essentially no sleep would make anyone feel shaky, scared, and “unglued.” The chest/rib/back/head pain you describe is often from repeated coughing and irritated airways/muscles, but the severity and inability to rest is a good reason to **call Northside/Dr. Malloy’s office today** for advice or reassessment.

A few practical options that may help tonight, assuming you’re not allergic and don’t have conditions that prevent them:

- **Pain control before bed:** acetaminophen or ibuprofen can reduce the rib/chest soreness that triggers coughing with deep breaths. Avoid ibuprofen if you have kidney disease, ulcers/bleeding risk, are on blood thinners, or were told not to take NSAIDs. Don’t double up acetaminophen if it’s in any combo cold medicine.
- **Cough suppression at night:** ask the CVS pharmacist about a product with **dextromethorphan** for nighttime cough. Avoid combining multiple cough/cold products, and avoid alcohol or extra sedatives.
- **Honey**: 1–2 teaspoons before bed can calm cough for some adults.
- **Humidity + upright sleep:** humidifier or steamy bathroom before bed, then sleep propped up fairly high or in a recliner if possible.
- **Slow “splinted” breathing:** hold a pillow firmly against your ribs/chest when you cough; it can reduce pain and help you take gentler breaths.
- If there is wheezing/tightness or you can’t take a breath without coughing, your clinician may consider an **inhaler** or a prescription cough medicine—this is exactly the kind of thing to call back about.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you have **shortness of breath at rest**, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen level **<92%** if you have a pulse ox, chest pain/pressure not just with coughing, coughing blood, severe worsening weakness, dehydration, or fever/worsening symptoms despite 48–72 hours of antibiotics.

You are not being annoying. Pneumonia can be awful, but not sleeping for 4 days and feeling shaky/weird deserves help. Call them today and tell them exactly: “I have pneumonia and cannot sleep due to coughing fits with painful breaths.”

query: where can i be tested for covid 19? im marissa kline and im sorry if this is dumb but ive been clicking around and everything says something diff and i dont know if i need an appt or if i can just show up somewhere. i live at 1148 s 9th st apt 3b and i dont have a car right now so im trying to figure out somewhere i can get to without making a whole huge thing of it. i can call if thats easier, my cell is 267-555-0149, but i keep getting those robot menus and then it hangs up or says check online and the online thing just sends me in circles. i dont even know if they still do drive thru testing or if that was only before. also i have independence blue cross #ibx-4409127 if that matters, but i honestly dont know if a covid 19 test is free or if im gonna get stuck with some random bill later and i cant really do that right now. i just need to know where can i be tested for covid 19, like actual place or what kind of place, pharmacy, urgent care, health dept, idk. i dont wanna walk into the wrong spot and be around ppl if im not supposed to be there. do i need symptoms to get tested or can i get one bc i was around someone who might have it? and do they do the fast test or the pcr thing, and how long for results? sorry im rambling i just need a straight answer bc im anxious and dont wanna mess this up.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — not a dumb question at all. The testing rules and websites have changed a lot, so it’s understandable to feel stuck.

Since you’re in South Philadelphia and don’t have a car, the most practical options are:

1. **Pharmacy testing** — CVS, Walgreens, and Rite Aid often offer COVID rapid antigen and/or PCR testing. Most require you to **schedule online**, but some stores allow walk-ins for home test kits or limited testing.
2. **Urgent care centers** — many do walk-in COVID testing, but I’d call first to confirm cost and availability.
3. **Philadelphia Department of Public Health resources** — check the City’s COVID testing page or call **311** and ask for nearby COVID testing options you can reach without a car.

I would **not assume drive-through testing is still available** unless a site specifically says so. If you don’t have a car, look for “walk-up testing,” pharmacy testing, or urgent care.

You **do not necessarily need symptoms** to be tested. Testing is reasonable if you were exposed to someone who may have COVID. If you were exposed but feel fine, the best time to test is usually about **5 days after exposure**, or sooner if symptoms start. If you use a home rapid test and it’s negative, repeat it in **48 hours**, especially if symptoms continue.

For test types:  
- **Rapid antigen**: results in ~15–30 minutes; good if positive, but can miss early infection.  
- **PCR/NAAT**: more sensitive; results often same day to 2 days, depending on site.

For cost, ask before testing: “Do you bill Independence Blue Cross, and will I owe anything?” Coverage varies now, especially for testing without symptoms. Home tests may be cheaper if you just need a quick answer, but PCR is better if you need documentation.

Until you know, wear a well-fitting mask, avoid crowded indoor places, and don’t walk into a clinic unmasked if you might be infectious.

Please seek urgent care now if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, or worsening high fever.

tiny2882@comcat,net cause i check that more than anything.
target_response:
Michael, I’m really glad you wrote this. What you described is not “insane” — it’s the story of someone who has lived through enormous grief, trauma, severe pain, cancer/back injuries, and a very powerful opioid/benzodiazepine dependence. The fact that you’re being honest about it is a major protective step.

A few important medical points:

1. **Do not stop suddenly on your own**, especially if you are still taking Xanax or other benzodiazepines. Abrupt withdrawal can cause **seizures, delirium, dangerous blood pressure/heart changes, and can be life-threatening**. Opioid withdrawal is usually not fatal by itself, but combined with your medical history it can still be dangerous.

2. Given your history of very high-dose opioids plus Xanax and past episodes where you “died”/had severe pneumonia, you need a **medically supervised plan**. The combination of opioids and benzodiazepines can suppress breathing, increase aspiration risk, worsen pneumonia risk, and dramatically raise overdose risk.

3. The safest next step is to contact an **addiction medicine physician**, your primary doctor, oncologist/pain specialist, or go to an ER if you feel at risk of using heavily again. Treatments like **buprenorphine/Suboxone or methadone** can reduce cravings and overdose risk while still addressing pain in many patients. Benzodiazepines usually require a **slow, supervised taper**.

4. Please keep **naloxone/Narcan** with you and make sure someone around you knows how to use it.

Because you mentioned fear of sliding back, PTSD, depression, and so much loss: if you have any thoughts of harming yourself, feel like you might overdose, or feel unable to stay safe, call **988** in the U.S. or go to the nearest ER immediately. For treatment resources, you can also call **SAMHSA: 1-800-662-HELP (4357)**.

I can’t email you directly, and I wouldn’t want you to post personal contact info publicly. But you can keep using this platform to write things out. For today: tell one trusted person, secure/dispose of extra pills if possible, avoid alcohol or sedatives, and make one medical call for addiction treatment. You do not have to fight this alone.

query: hi, my name is maria lopez, dob 04/18/1991, and im trying not to panic but i keep going back and forth on if this is something i should get checked for or if im overthinking it. symptoms include irritation in the throat, dry cough, trouble breathing properly, headache. however not really a sign of a fever. temperature is about 35 degrees. should i get tested? i tried calling my regular doctor dr. nisha patel but the line was busy and i didnt wanna sit on hold forever while i feel like this, my phone is 555-713-4082 if someone actually needs to call me back or whatever. the throat thing started more like scratchy and annoying, not super painful, then the dry cough keeps coming and its not like a wet cough or anything. the breathing part is what makes me nervous bc i can still walk around and talk but it feels like im not getting a full breath properly, esp when im laying down or thinking about it too much, which prob makes it worse. headache is just there in the background, not the worst headache of my life but enough that im irritated and tired. i dont have a real fever as far as i can tell, like i said temp is around 35 degrees, so thats why im confused bc everyone keeps saying fever is the big thing. i havent been out much besides groceries and work stuff and i keep washing my hands but idk, people cough everywhere. i also dont wanna waste a test if they are scarce, but i dont wanna be stupid either and go around other ppl if i shouldnt. pls just tell me if with these symptoms and no fever i should still get tested or wait it out, im really unsure.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re feeling this way, and you’re not overthinking it. A scratchy throat, dry cough, headache, and any sense of breathing difficulty can happen with common viral infections, COVID, flu, RSV, allergies/post-nasal drip, asthma/reactive airways, reflux, or anxiety/panic amplifying the sensation. Importantly, **you can have COVID or another contagious respiratory virus without a fever**, so lack of fever does not rule it out.

**Yes — I would recommend testing**, especially since you have a dry cough and throat irritation and you’re around others for groceries/work. If you have access to a home COVID test, take one now; if negative but symptoms continue, repeat in 24–48 hours. If flu/COVID testing is available through a pharmacy/urgent care, that’s reasonable too. Until you know, try to **stay home, avoid close contact, and wear a well-fitting mask** if you must be around others.

About the temperature: if you mean **35°C (95°F)**, that is lower than expected. Sometimes thermometers read low due to technique, cold drinks, or device issues. Recheck after sitting indoors for 10–15 minutes, using the thermometer as directed. If it repeatedly stays around **35°C or lower**, especially with confusion, severe weakness, shivering, or feeling very cold, seek medical care.

For now: rest, drink fluids, use honey/lozenges for cough/throat irritation, and consider acetaminophen or ibuprofen for headache if you can take them safely. Sleeping slightly propped up may help the “can’t get a full breath” feeling. Try slow breathing: inhale through the nose 4 seconds, exhale 6–8 seconds.

**Please seek urgent care now / ER or call emergency services** if you have: worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking full sentences, chest pain/pressure, blue lips/face, fainting, confusion, oxygen saturation <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, severe dehydration, or a sudden “worst headache of life.”

Since you couldn’t reach Dr. Patel, consider calling again, using their patient portal, or contacting an urgent care/nurse line today—particularly because of the breathing symptom. Also, avoid sharing personal phone/DOB in unsecured messages when possible.

query: about the ibuprofen and covid 19 should i quit taking it? it’s got me paranoid. the way the media’s been talking about it. i take it everyday for my neck pain and back pain. i can’t take pain pills because they make me nauseas. any insight please. this is melissa ann carver, dob 04/18/1979, and i know i prob sound ridiculous but i keep seeing posts and tv people saying ibuprofen might make covid worse or whatever and now every time i take it i get nervous and then my neck starts hurting more bc im all tense. i usually just take it in the morning and again later if my back is acting up, been doing that for a long time bc nothing else really helps and the stronger pain pills always make me so sick to my stomach i cant function. dr. nina patel had told me before it was ok to use it as long as i didnt overdo it, but that was before all this covid 19 stuff got so scary. i got mine from CVS on 5th ave last week and now the bottle is just sitting on the counter staring at me lol. i dont have a fever or cough or anything right now, im just worried if i get exposed or have it and dont know yet then am i making it worse by taking ibuprofen everyday? should i switch to tylenol or just stop cold turkey? i dont wanna be dramatic but the pain gets bad enough that i cant sleep and then i get more paranoid from being tired. if someone can call me back at 412-555-0196 or just message here thats fine too, i just need plain advice bc google is making me spiral.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — you do **not** sound ridiculous. A lot of people got worried because early in COVID there were media reports suggesting ibuprofen might make COVID worse. Based on the evidence we have now, **ibuprofen has not been shown to worsen COVID-19 or increase complications**. Major health organizations have said there is no clear reason to avoid it solely because of COVID.

Since you **do not currently have fever, cough, or other COVID symptoms**, you do **not need to stop ibuprofen cold turkey** just out of fear. There’s no withdrawal issue with stopping it, but your neck/back pain may flare, and poor sleep can make pain and anxiety worse.

That said, because you take it most days, the bigger issue is the usual long-term ibuprofen risks: stomach irritation/ulcers/bleeding, kidney strain, raising blood pressure, and interactions with blood thinners or certain heart/kidney conditions. If Dr. Patel previously okayed it, it’s reasonable to continue **at the lowest effective dose**, taken **with food**, and avoid taking it with other NSAIDs like naproxen/Aleve, aspirin for pain, or more ibuprofen-containing cold meds.

If you do get COVID symptoms, you can use **Tylenol/acetaminophen first** for fever/aches if you prefer. Keep total acetaminophen under **3,000 mg/day** unless your clinician says otherwise, and avoid or limit it if you have significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use. If Tylenol doesn’t control your neck/back pain, ibuprofen is still generally acceptable as long as you’re eating/drinking okay and not dehydrated.

Please seek urgent care if you develop: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or a high/persistent fever. Also stop ibuprofen and get medical help for black/tarry stools, vomiting blood, severe stomach pain, very little urination, new leg swelling, or allergic-type symptoms.

For the chronic neck/back pain, it would be worth checking in with Dr. Patel about safer long-term options—physical therapy, stretching/strengthening, heat, topical anti-inflammatory gel, lidocaine patches, or other non-narcotic options that may not upset your stomach.

query: hi im tanya brooks, dob 04/17/1989, and im sorry this is prob long but im getting nervous and dont know if this is like something dumb or something i should go get seen for. low grade fevers 99.8, constant trigger points that are tender to touch in trapezius muscles and muscles feel warm to touch, pointer finger, and middle finger on right hand are very sore, sore to open and close hand in those fingers, whats wrong? like the trapezius spots are the ones up by my neck/shoulders and if i press there it feels bruised and angry and warm, and then my right hand is freaking me out bc its mostly the pointer finger and middle finger, they hurt when i try to grip stuff or open and close hand, like even grabbing my keys or my phone is annoying. i keep checking my temp and it keeps being around 99.8 so i know thats not huge but it feels like low grade fevers to me and i get anxious bc my body feels off. i work at target on city ave and i do a lot of register/scanner and lifting bags and random boxes so idk if its from that or if a fever plus sore hand means infection or inflammation or what. i was thinking about going to main line health urgent care but im trying to not waste money if they just tell me rest/ice. if someone needs to call me my cell is 267-555-0148, but really im just trying to figure out if this sounds like nerve/muscle thing or something more serious. i dont have a fancy way to explain it, its just constant tender trigger points, warm muscles, and my right pointer and middle finger hurt bad to use.
target_response:
Hi Tanya — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this; it makes sense you’re nervous when your body feels “off” and your hand is starting to interfere with normal tasks.

A temperature around **99.8°F is usually not considered a true fever** (we typically use **100.4°F or higher**), though it can still feel uncomfortable and can happen with stress, pain, dehydration, being warm, or inflammation.

Based on what you described — **tender “trigger points” in the upper trapezius/neck-shoulder area** plus **right index and middle finger pain with gripping/opening/closing** — a few likely possibilities include:

- **Overuse/repetitive strain** from scanning, register work, lifting bags/boxes  
- **Tendon irritation/tenosynovitis** in the hand/fingers  
- **Median nerve irritation/carpal tunnel–type symptoms**, which can affect the thumb/index/middle fingers  
- **Neck/shoulder muscle spasm or nerve irritation** referring pain down into the hand  

Infection is less likely if there’s no clear cut/wound, spreading redness, significant swelling, or true fever — but warmth and tenderness should be watched.

For the next 24–48 hours, you can try:  
- **Rest/modify use** of that hand if possible; avoid heavy gripping/lifting  
- **Ice the sore fingers/hand** 10–15 minutes a few times daily; heat may help the trapezius tightness  
- Consider a **wrist brace**, especially during work or sleep  
- **Ibuprofen or naproxen** if you can take NSAIDs safely, or acetaminophen if not  
- Gentle neck/shoulder stretching, avoid deep aggressive massage if it feels inflamed

I do think it would be reasonable to get seen at urgent care or primary care **within the next couple of days**, especially since the hand pain is affecting daily function. They can check strength/sensation, look for tendon/nerve signs, and decide if you need a splint, anti-inflammatory plan, work note, or further testing.

Please seek urgent care sooner/today if you develop: **temp ≥100.4°F**, rapidly worsening pain, significant swelling, redness spreading, red streaks, pus/wound/bite, inability to move the fingers, numbness/weakness, severe neck stiffness, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

This does not sound “dumb” — your symptoms are real, and it’s appropriate to ask.

query: hi, my name is nina ayu permata. sorry if my english is messy, im typing this fast and really worried. my grandfather pak harun permata is suffering from pneumonia right now and refuses to go to the doctor because of the price needed to obtain a patented medicine. he keeps saying we already asked once at rs pondok indah bintaro jaya and the cost was too much, and now he is angry anytime i mention going back. in indonesia, it is not hard to find these kinds of doctors that don t want to help people in need, like they talk nicely but then everything is about money first and medicine later. i dont know if that is normal but it makes me feel helpless. he is coughing a lot and weak and my grandma keeps trying steam and warm tea and he says he is fine but he is not fine, i can hear it. i am not trying to be dramatic but i am scared to sleep because maybe his breathing gets worse at night and nobody notices. i tried calling around but they keep asking for payment and registration first, so if anyone can contact me my number is +62 813-7712-4409 or email nina.permata21@gmail.com, but mostly i just need to know what can be done at home while he refuses. what medicine can you guys recommend for a home treatment? like anything safe or common that families use, or should i keep forcing him to go even if he gets mad? i know pneumonia is serious but he is very stubborn and says hospital only wants to sell expensive patented medicine, so i am stuck between respecting him and being scared he will get worse.
target_response:
Hi Nina — I’m very sorry you and your family are going through this. Your English is completely clear, and your worry is very understandable. Pneumonia in an older person can worsen quickly, especially at night, and weakness plus a lot of coughing means he really should be assessed.

I can’t contact you by phone/email from here, but I can guide you. **Home treatment alone is not enough if this is true pneumonia**, because he may need **antibiotics, oxygen, IV fluids, or testing**. Steam and warm tea may soothe symptoms, but they do not treat lung infection. Also, antibiotics should not be chosen blindly because the wrong one can fail or cause harm.

What you can do at home while trying to get him seen:

- Keep him **sitting upright**, not lying flat.  
- Encourage **small frequent fluids** if he can swallow safely.  
- Use **paracetamol/acetaminophen** for fever or body aches, following the package dose and avoiding overdose.  
- Avoid sedating cough medicines, alcohol, or anything that makes him sleepy.  
- If available, use a **pulse oximeter**. Oxygen saturation **below 92%**, or any blue lips/fingertips, is urgent.  
- Monitor breathing rate: if he is breathing very fast, struggling, or cannot speak full sentences, seek emergency help.

**Red flags — do not wait if any occur:** worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, extreme sleepiness, fainting, coughing blood, persistent high fever, severe weakness/cannot walk, dehydration/not urinating, or oxygen saturation <92%. In those cases, call local emergency help such as **119/112** or go to the nearest **IGD/emergency department**, even if he is angry.

About cost: it is reasonable to ask specifically for **generic medicines**, BPJS options if he has it, or a lower-cost clinic/ **puskesmas**. Pneumonia treatment does not always require “patented” expensive medicine; many effective antibiotics are generic. You can say to him: “I’m not asking you to agree to expensive treatment. I’m asking for one check of your oxygen and lungs, and we will request generic options.”

If he becomes confused or cannot make safe decisions, the family should treat it as an emergency. Your fear is not dramatic — it is appropriate.

query: hello, i am a student, my name is lena patel and im dealing with a microbiology assignment for Hudson Valley Community College and im kinda stuck and stressed bc its due soon and i feel like im mixing up the meds and the organism stuff. i am given a paitent sample, like its one of those case study sample things in lab, and my paitent is 4 years old - diagnosis pneumonia - summary of peresent illness = recurrant colds, ear infections,and bronchitis. she has been sick for past 3 weeks. developed a fever yesterday. also nausia and vomiting,muscle aches. past medical history= cystic fibrosis diagnosed at age 3. i did all the lab work and found out that the bacteria causes the disease is psudomonas aeruginosa. i already checked my gram stain notes and culture notes like 5 times and i keep second guessing if im supposed to write about empiric therapy first or the targeted one after the lab result. prof. maribel santos said to explain why the drug fits the organism and also the patient history, and thats where im confused bc cystic fibrosis makes me think of chronic infections and resistant stuff and i dont wanna put the wrong antibiotic in the answer. im not asking anyone to do the whole homework, i just need help understanding the appropriate treatment direction for this kind of case. is it like an antipseudomonal antibiotic and would it be different bc the paitent is only 4 years old? also do you mention supportive care for pneumonia too or only the bacteria treatment? please help, i have been reading the slides all night and my brain is fried.
target_response:
Hi Lena — take a breath. You’re thinking about this the right way, and cystic fibrosis is exactly the detail that changes the treatment discussion.

For a **4-year-old with cystic fibrosis and pneumonia due to *Pseudomonas aeruginosa***, the key point is that treatment usually needs **antipseudomonal coverage**. *Pseudomonas* is a **Gram-negative, oxidase-positive rod** that is common in CF because thick airway mucus allows chronic colonization, biofilm formation, and recurrent infections. It can also become resistant, so culture and susceptibility results matter a lot.

For your assignment, you can explain it like this:

1. **Empiric therapy** is what clinicians start before the organism is confirmed.  
   In a child with CF and pneumonia, empiric therapy often includes coverage for organisms common in CF, especially ***Pseudomonas aeruginosa*** and sometimes *Staphylococcus aureus*, depending on history and local resistance patterns.

2. **Targeted therapy** comes after the lab identifies *Pseudomonas* and susceptibility testing is available.  
   Then treatment is narrowed or adjusted to an antibiotic active against that specific isolate.

Common antipseudomonal options include **cefepime, ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem**. In CF pulmonary exacerbations, clinicians often use an IV antipseudomonal beta-lactam **plus an aminoglycoside such as tobramycin**, especially for more severe infections or resistant risk. Tobramycin levels and kidney function need monitoring. Inhaled antibiotics like **inhaled tobramycin or aztreonam** may be used in CF for chronic suppression, but acute pneumonia often requires systemic therapy.

Because the patient is only **4 years old**, dosing would be **weight-based**, and medication safety matters. For example, ciprofloxacin can be used in children when needed for *Pseudomonas*, but it is generally reserved for specific situations because of pediatric safety considerations.

Yes — you should absolutely mention **supportive care** too. Pneumonia care in CF includes airway clearance, hydration, fever control, oxygen if needed, nutrition support, bronchodilators/mucolytics if prescribed, and monitoring vomiting/dehydration.

Red flags in a real child would include trouble breathing, blue lips, severe lethargy, persistent vomiting, dehydration, chest pain, coughing blood, or low oxygen — those need urgent care.

You’re not mixing it up: **empiric first, then targeted once *Pseudomonas* is confirmed.**

query: flu symptoms, cough and no fever. should i test for coronavirus? i have asthma and im trying not to be dramatic but also not trying to be stupid about it either. im jessica marlowe, dob 03/14/1987, and ive had like the achy tired gross flu feeling, kinda scratchy throat and cough, but every time i check temp its normal, no fever at all. my chest feels a little tight but thats also how my asthma acts when i get any cold so i cant tell if im just anxious or if its something else. i used my inhaler like usual and it helps some, but the cough is still there and then i start googling and of course everything says coronavirus and now im spiraling. i called dr. nina patel before but i dont wanna keep bugging the office if this is just regular flu/cold stuff, plus i know everyone is probably swamped. i was thinking about going to cvs on maple ave to ask about a test or meds but i also dont wanna walk in somewhere coughing and freak people out or expose myself to more germs. no fever though, thats the part making me unsure. like do people with covid always get fever or can it be cough only? and with asthma should i be testing sooner even if its mild? i havent been gasping or anything, just cough and that weird heavy feeling that comes with being sick. im drinking water, resting, avoiding people in the house as much as i can, but i need to know if testing makes sense or if i should wait unless fever shows up or breathing gets worse. sorry this is rambly, im just worried bc asthma makes every cough feel like a big deal.
target_response:
Hi Jessica — you’re not being dramatic. With asthma, it’s very reasonable to take a new cough/chest tightness seriously, even if you don’t have a fever.

COVID **can absolutely present without fever**. Some people have only cough, fatigue/aches, sore throat, congestion, or chest tightness. Flu and other viruses can also do this, and asthma often flares with any respiratory infection, so testing is the best way to sort out risk rather than guessing.

Given your symptoms and asthma, I’d recommend you **test for COVID now** rather than waiting for a fever. A home rapid test is fine if you have one. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**, or consider a PCR/NAAT test for more accuracy. If you go through CVS, I’d avoid walking in unannounced while coughing—check online/call first for drive-through or scheduled testing, and wear a well-fitting mask.

In the meantime, keep doing what you’re doing: rest, fluids, minimize close contact at home, mask around others, and ventilate shared spaces. Use your rescue inhaler **as prescribed**; if you have an asthma action plan or peak flow meter, follow that. Because your chest feels tight and the inhaler only helps “some,” it would be appropriate to message or call Dr. Patel’s office—this is exactly the kind of situation they’d want to know about, and it’s not “bugging” them.

Also, if your symptoms started within the last 48 hours, ask about whether flu testing or antiviral medication makes sense, since asthma can increase risk from flu as well.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop: worsening shortness of breath, trouble speaking in full sentences, wheezing or chest tightness not improving with inhaler, needing your rescue inhaler much more often than usual, blue lips/face, confusion, fainting, persistent chest pain/pressure, oxygen level <92–94% if you monitor it, or rapid worsening.

Bottom line: **yes, testing makes sense even without fever**, and with asthma it’s smart—not stupid—to be cautious.

query: can i get a covid 19 test? im maria lopez and i know this is prob a dumb simple question but i keep going in circles trying to figure out where im supposed to ask and if i need an appt or if i can just walk in somewhere. i dont wanna clog up the phone line if this is the wrong place but also i dont wanna wait too long if theres a way to just get tested and know, bc everything online says something diff and its making me more anxious honestly. i can be reached at 267-555-0149 if somebody is actually calling ppl back, but texting is easier bc i dont always hear my phone when im dealing with the kids and work stuff. my email is maria.lopez84@northmail.com too if thats better for sending whatever link or form i need. i just need to know like do i need a dr order, do i have to be a current patient, is there a fee, do i bring id, do i stay in the car, all that basic stuff. i tried clicking around and it kept asking me questions then sending me to another page and i got confused. also if there is a certain time i should come or if mornings are better pls say that bc i dont wanna show up and be told i did it wrong. sorry im rambling, i just want the covid 19 test and clear directions, like where to go and what to do when i get there, and if results come by portal or phone or email. thanks.
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not a dumb question at all. Testing access has changed many times, so it’s completely understandable that the online information feels confusing.

Yes, you can get a COVID-19 test. The exact process depends on the testing site, but here is the simplest way to proceed:

1. **If you have symptoms or a known exposure:** it’s reasonable to test now. A home rapid antigen test is acceptable; if it’s negative and symptoms continue, repeat it in **48 hours**. A PCR test is more sensitive and may be preferred if you need a definitive result for work/school/travel or if you’re high risk.

2. **Appointment vs walk-in:** many pharmacies and urgent care/testing sites allow online scheduling, and some allow walk-ins. Walk-ins can involve long waits or being turned away if they’re full, so **making an appointment is usually safest**. Mornings are often better because sites are less likely to be backed up.

3. **Doctor’s order/current patient:** most pharmacy/community testing sites **do not require a doctor’s order** and usually do not require you to be an established patient. Medical offices may have their own rules.

4. **What to bring:** bring a photo ID and insurance card if you have one. There may be a fee depending on the site, insurance, and type of test, so check before booking.

5. **When you arrive:** follow the site’s instructions. Some are drive-through and ask you to stay in the car; others require you to come inside wearing a mask.

6. **Results:** rapid tests may return results within minutes to hours. PCR results often come in 1–3 days, usually by portal, text, phone, or email depending on the site.

While you’re waiting, please wear a mask around others, avoid close contact, and stay home if you’re sick. If you have trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips/face, severe weakness, confusion, dehydration, or oxygen levels under 92% if you’re checking, seek urgent care or call 911.

If you’re in our system, send a portal message or appointment request titled “COVID test,” and include your preferred text contact.

query: hi, my name is lindiwe mthembu and i have been to the emergency room at busamed hospital on thursday 06/06 and was treated for a tummy bug, since then i have developed a fever and body aches and tiredness, i would like to know where can o go to be tested for covid 19? im sorry if im asking the wrong place but im a bit stressed now bc at first i really thought it was just the tummy thing and i was told to rest and drink fluids and go home, but now the fever is there and my whole body is sore and im so tired i just want to sleep, and its making me nervous bc i was sitting in the er with other people too. i dont know if i must go back to er or if there is like a drive thru test place or clinic i can phone first so i dont make other ppl sick. they gave me a paper but i dont really understand if my file number MRN BH-293884 is needed when i call or not. i am also worried because i stay with my aunt and she is older so i have been trying to stay in my room but its hard in a small place. my medical aid is Discovery Health #DH-77429103 if that matters for the test, i dont know if they pay or if i must pay cash. if someone can please tell me the closest or safest place to go and if i need a booking, i can be contacted on 082-555-4109, i just dont want to arrive somewhere and be turned away or told i should have called first.
target_response:
Hi Lindiwe — I’m sorry you’re feeling so unwell and stressed. Fever, body aches and marked tiredness after an initial “tummy bug” could still be a viral infection such as COVID-19 or flu, but it can also happen with gastroenteritis, dehydration, or another infection. It’s sensible that you’re thinking about testing and protecting your aunt.

**What to do about COVID testing**
- **Phone before going anywhere.** Call Busamed Hospital’s switchboard/ER or your GP and explain you were seen on 06/06 and now have fever/body aches/fatigue. Your **hospital file/MRN may help them find your record**, but don’t worry if you don’t have it immediately.
- You can also contact **Discovery Health** to ask which nearby testing sites/pharmacies/pathology labs are covered and whether you need pre-authorisation. Testing availability and payment rules vary.
- Many areas have testing through **pharmacies or pathology labs** such as Dis-Chem/Clicks, Lancet, Ampath, PathCare, or a GP clinic. Some require booking; some offer rapid antigen tests. A **home rapid antigen test** is also an option if available, especially to avoid exposing others.

**Should you go back to ER?**
If your symptoms are manageable, it’s usually best to **call first** rather than sit in a waiting room. However, please seek urgent care now if you have any of these: shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, fainting, blue lips, severe weakness, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration (very little urine, dizziness), severe abdominal pain, blood in stool/vomit, fever above 39°C or fever lasting more than 3 days, or if you feel you are getting rapidly worse.

**Protecting your aunt**
Until you know more, act as if it could be contagious: stay in your room if possible, wear a mask when near her, keep windows open, don’t share cups/utensils/towels, clean bathroom handles/taps, and wash hands often. If she is elderly or has medical conditions, she should avoid close contact with you and consider testing if she develops symptoms.

For now: rest, drink small frequent fluids/ORS, and use paracetamol for fever/body aches if you can take it safely.

query: my father is in the hospital on ventilator currently and im just so lost right now and scared and dont really understand what all this means. his name is robert malloy and he is at st. luke's memorial hospital, his mrn is MRN MGH-772184 if that even matters for anything, and the main doctor i spoke with was dr. nisha patel. the doctor says he now has what is considered whiteout in his lungs. he has said that the outline of the heart can no longer be seen. i keep replaying that in my head bc that sounds so bad and nobody is really telling us plainly if this is like something people come back from or if we should be preparing for the worst. my father aspirated and then was diagnosed with pneumonia. three days later he ended up in ccu on a ventilator and has not been able to be weened off/ he is currently taking 3 antibiotics. they keep saying they are waiting to see if the antibiotics help and that his oxygen numbers and the xray are the big things but every time we call the nurse says he is still critical and sedated and on the vent. i dont know if whiteout means both lungs are full of infection or fluid or if the ventilator can cause more damage, and we are all arguing in the family about whether to ask for transfer or a lung specialist or just trust the ccu team. he was eating ok before all this and then the aspiration happened and it feels like it went downhill so fast. what is your advise. like what questions should i be asking, is there anything else they should be checking, and is there any chance of getting off the ventilator after this long or is that unlikely?
target_response:
I’m so sorry you and your family are going through this. Hearing words like “whiteout” and “can’t see the heart outline” is terrifying, especially when the updates feel vague. I can’t access your father’s chart, but I can explain what this often means and what to ask.

A “whiteout” on chest X-ray usually means the lungs are filled with something that blocks air from showing normally—this can be pneumonia/infection, inflammation from aspiration, fluid, collapse of part of the lung, or a combination. When aspiration pneumonia becomes severe, it can trigger **ARDS**—a widespread lung inflammation where oxygen can’t pass well into the blood. This can worsen quickly over days, even if someone was eating and functioning before.

People **can** recover from this and come off a ventilator, even after many days, but it depends on several things: how severe his oxygen needs are, whether his infection is responding, whether other organs are affected, and whether his lungs are becoming easier or harder to ventilate. Being “critical, sedated, on the vent” does not automatically mean hopeless—but it does mean he is very sick.

Questions I’d ask Dr. Patel or the ICU/CCU team directly:

1. **Is this aspiration pneumonia alone, or has he developed ARDS?**  
2. **What are his ventilator settings?** Especially FiO₂ oxygen percentage and PEEP. Are they improving, stable, or worsening?  
3. **What are his oxygen numbers/ABGs?** Is his blood oxygen improving?  
4. **Have cultures identified a germ?** Blood/sputum cultures? Are the 3 antibiotics targeted or broad coverage?  
5. **Any fluid overload or heart problem contributing?** Has an echocardiogram or fluid balance been reviewed?  
6. **Are kidneys, blood pressure, and other organs stable?** Is he on pressors?  
7. **Has a pulmonologist/critical care specialist seen him?** Most ICUs have intensivists; asking for a pulmonary/critical care review is reasonable.  
8. **What would improvement look like over the next 24–72 hours, and what signs would worry you?**

A transfer may help if he needs services not available there—advanced ICU care, bronchoscopy, proning, ECMO evaluation—but transferring a critically ill ventilated patient also has risks. Ask plainly: “Do you think he is getting all appropriate ICU-level treatment here, or should we seek transfer to a tertiary center?”

Also ask for a scheduled family meeting with the attending physician, not just nurse updates. You deserve a clear, honest explanation of best case, worst case, and most likely course.

query: hi this is maggie rosen, dob 03/14/1992, sorry if this is dumb but if i have had a sore throat for 3 days but with no fever should i be tested for covid-19? its not like crazy horrible but its there every time i swallow and i keep overthinking it bc everyone at work acts like any throat thing is automatically covid now. i keep checking my temp like way too much and still no fever, which is why im confused if im supposed to go get tested or just wait it out. i tried calling the office earlier and left my number 215-555-0198 but i prob rambled on the voicemail too, and my email is maggie.rosen92@gmail.com if thats easier. i dont wanna waste a test or sit around urgent care if its not needed, but also i dont wanna be the person who ignores it and then gets someone else sick. dr. nina patel told me before to watch symptoms and not panic, but that was for a dif thing so idk if it applies. i was gonna stop at cvs on walnut st after work if you think i should do a rapid test or ask the pharmacist, but i also dont know if the rapid ones are worth it for just a sore throat and no fever. should i stay home from work while i wait, or wear a mask and just keep distance? its only been 3 days but i feel like covid rules keep changing and google is not helping. pls just tell me if a sore throat alone for 3 days is enough reason to be tested for covid-19 or if i should wait another day or two unless fever starts.
target_response:
Hi Maggie — not a dumb question at all. A sore throat by itself *can* be COVID, even without fever, so it is reasonable to test rather than just guess—especially because you’re going to work and are worried about exposing others.

**What I’d recommend:**  
- Do a **rapid COVID test today** (CVS/home test is fine). Rapid tests are useful, but a single negative early on isn’t perfect.  
- If it’s **negative** and your sore throat continues, **repeat a rapid test in 48 hours**. If you need the most certainty sooner, a **PCR/NAAT** test is more sensitive.  
- If it’s **positive**, stay home and follow current workplace/public health guidance—generally stay home while symptoms are worsening or you feel unwell, and mask carefully around others as you recover.

**Work:** If you can stay home until you have a negative test, that’s ideal. If you must go in, wear a well-fitting mask, avoid close contact, don’t share food/drinks, and wash hands frequently. Fever is not required to be contagious with COVID or other viruses.

Other common causes include a routine viral sore throat, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry air, reflux, or strep. Strep is more likely if you have **fever, swollen/tender neck glands, tonsil spots/exudate, and no cough**—if those develop, you should be evaluated for a strep test.

For now: fluids, warm tea/honey, salt-water gargles, throat lozenges, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely. Try not to check your temperature repeatedly—once or twice a day is plenty unless you feel significantly worse.

**Seek urgent care now** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, drooling or inability to swallow saliva, severe one-sided throat pain/swelling, muffled “hot potato” voice, neck swelling/stiffness, dehydration, or a high/persistent fever. Also call us if symptoms last more than about a week or worsen.

Bottom line: **yes, a sore throat for 3 days is enough reason to take a COVID test**, but it doesn’t mean you should panic. Testing is a responsible next step.

query: hi, this is maria lopez, i know this might sound like a dumb question but i keep thinking about it and cant really find a straight answer that makes sense to a normal person. can vaccines for coronavirus in domestic pets be modified for humans as a way to have a vaccine for covid-19? like i read that some dogs/cats or maybe other domestic pets have coronavirus shots or at least vets talk about coronavirus in animals, and i was wondering if its the same kind of thing at all or if it is totally different and useless for people. i asked at riverside animal hospital when i was there getting my dogs records and the front desk girl was nice but said to ask a human dr, not them, which i get. i also sent a note to dr eric nadel but i prob wont hear back quick, and im sitting here googling and making myself more nervous. im not trying to do anything crazy or take pet medicine or anything like that, promise, i just dont understand if scientists can take an existing vaccine and tweak it faster instead of starting from nothing. my insurance is aetna choice pos #w74291306 so i dont know if they even cover a telehealth question like this, and my email is mlopez77@proton.me if someone from the office needs to send me info. i have older family at home and im just scared, bc everything online says scarce or delayed or trials take forever, and then i see stuff about animal coronavirus vaccines and my brain is like why cant they just modify those for humans?? sorry this is rambly, just trying to understand if that is a real possibility or if pet coronavirus is not related enough to covid-19.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. It’s a very reasonable thing to wonder about, especially when you’re worried about older family members and seeing mixed information online.

The short answer is: **pet coronavirus vaccines cannot simply be “tweaked” and used as COVID-19 vaccines for humans.** “Coronavirus” is the name of a large family of viruses. Some infect dogs, cats, cattle, pigs, or birds, and some infect humans. They are related in a broad family-tree sense, but many are quite different from SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19.

For example, canine coronavirus usually causes intestinal illness in dogs and is not the same virus as SARS-CoV-2. Feline coronavirus is also different and can be associated with FIP in cats. Vaccines used or discussed in veterinary medicine are designed for those specific animal viruses, animal immune systems, and animal dosing. They are **not tested for safety, effectiveness, purity, or correct dosing in humans**, so they should never be used by people.

That said, your instinct about “not starting from nothing” is partly right. Scientists often use existing vaccine **platforms**—such as mRNA, viral vector, or protein-based technology—and adapt them to a new virus once the genetic sequence is known. That is different from taking a dog or cat vaccine and modifying it for people. Human vaccines still need careful clinical trials to confirm safety and effectiveness.

The practical guidance is: protect yourself and your older family members with the **currently recommended human COVID vaccines/boosters**, good ventilation, staying home when sick, testing when symptomatic or exposed, and masking in higher-risk indoor settings. If anyone in your home is high-risk and develops COVID symptoms or tests positive, contact their clinician promptly because antiviral treatment works best early.

Please seek urgent care for trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips/face, dehydration, or oxygen levels persistently below about 92% if you use a pulse oximeter.

Also, avoid taking or experimenting with any veterinary medication or vaccine—I'm glad you said you weren’t planning to. For insurance/telehealth coverage, Aetna or your doctor’s office can confirm benefits. And for privacy, try not to include insurance numbers or personal email in general messages unless it’s through a secure portal.

query: i have a problem with with my chest. pain and a bit short of breath. i cough every now and then, no fever and no headaches. but i am concerned it might be this new coronavirus. ? my name is marcus bell and im trying not to panic but its been on my mind all day bc every little tight feeling in my chest makes me think ok is this the thing or am i just making myself nervous. the pain isnt like i got hit or anything, its just there and annoying and then when i walk around the house or go up the stairs i feel a bit short of breath and then i start checking if i have fever again. still no fever, no headaches, just the cough every now and then and the chest problem. i called 267-555-0148 earlier trying to ask what i should do but i was on hold and then i hung up bc i had to get back to the kids and now im here typing this. i havent gone to er because i dont wanna sit there if this is nothing and also dont wanna expose myself if it is packed, but i also dont want to ignore chest pain and then be stupid about it. i picked up cough drops at walgreens on 5th ave but that didnt really answer anything obviously. i keep reading stuff online and it says some ppl dont have fever, some do, some cough, some chest tightness, so its making me more confused. should i get tested for coronavirus or call a dr first, or is chest pain plus a bit short of breath enough that i need urgent care even with no fever and no headaches?
target_response:
Hi Marcus — I’m glad you reached out. I understand why you’re worried: chest tightness plus shortness of breath can feel scary, and COVID information online can be confusing.

COVID-19 **can** sometimes cause cough, chest tightness, and shortness of breath **without fever**, so testing may be appropriate. However, chest pain and shortness of breath can also come from other causes — including asthma/bronchitis, pneumonia, anxiety/panic, reflux, muscle strain, or more serious heart/lung problems. Because you’re noticing shortness of breath when walking around the house or going upstairs, I would not dismiss this as “just nerves” without being assessed.

**What I recommend now:**
1. **Call your primary care doctor or a local urgent care/telehealth line today** and tell them clearly: “I have chest discomfort and shortness of breath with activity, plus an occasional cough.” Ask whether they want you seen in person and where to go safely.
2. If you cannot get through promptly, or if symptoms are continuing/worsening, it is reasonable to go to an **urgent care or ER**—especially because chest pain + shortness of breath needs evaluation beyond COVID testing.
3. Ask about **COVID testing**, and in the meantime act as if it could be contagious: wear a mask around others, avoid close contact, wash hands, and stay home if possible.

**Go to the ER or call 911 now** if you have any of the following: chest pain/pressure that is severe, crushing, spreading to your arm/jaw/back, shortness of breath at rest, trouble speaking full sentences, fainting, blue lips, confusion, coughing blood, rapid worsening, or oxygen level below 92% if you have a pulse oximeter.

If you have a pulse ox, check it while resting and after walking a bit; write down the numbers. Also note when the chest discomfort started, what makes it better/worse, and whether you have risk factors like asthma, heart disease, blood clots, smoking, or recent travel/exposure.

You’re not being “stupid” or overreacting — you’re being appropriately cautious. Given your symptoms, please seek same-day medical guidance rather than just watching it overnight.

query: is the cough of a patient with covid-19 any different sounding than a cough of a patient with a cold? im asking bc im melissa grant and i know this prob sounds like a dumb question but i keep listening to myself cough and then listening to my kid cough and now every little noise is making me panic. like is covid cough more dry or barky or deep in the chest or is that just internet stuff?? mine is kinda off and on, not constant, and i keep thinking maybe its just a cold bc i dont feel that awful, but then i read that some ppl barely feel sick and still have it so now im stuck in my head. i tried calling Dr. Priya Nair but the office line was busy forever and i dont wanna be that person taking up an appt if its just sniffles, but i also dont wanna ignore it if the sound means something. i have no idea if a regular cold cough can sound exactly the same as covid-19 or if nurses/drs can tell right away just from hearing it. my throat feels scratchy from coughing and i keep clearing it which probably makes it sound worse too. i picked up cough drops at CVS on maple ave and have been drinking tea, but im still sitting here like ok does this sound dry enough to be covid or wet enough to be a cold?? sorry im rambling, im just nervous and trying to figure out if there is actually a difference in the sound or if testing is the only real way to know. also should i isolate just based on cough even if it could be a cold?
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a dumb question at all. It’s very common to start “monitoring” every cough sound when COVID is on your mind, especially when your child is coughing too.

The short answer: **you generally cannot tell COVID-19 from a cold by the sound of the cough.** COVID coughs are often described as “dry,” but they can also be mild, intermittent, phlegmy, throat-clearing, or chesty. Regular colds, allergies/post-nasal drip, flu, RSV, and even throat irritation from repeated coughing can sound exactly the same. Clinicians may notice certain patterns, but **we cannot reliably diagnose COVID just by hearing a cough.** Testing and the overall symptom picture matter much more.

Your description — off-and-on cough, scratchy throat, clearing your throat, not feeling very awful — could fit a cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, early COVID, or another virus. And you’re right: some people with COVID have mild symptoms.

What I’d do next:
1. **Take a home COVID test today** if you have one.  
2. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, **repeat in 48 hours** because early tests can be negative.  
3. If you or your child had a known exposure, fever, worsening symptoms, or you need a definitive answer, consider a PCR/NAAT test or call Dr. Nair’s office again when you can.
4. Until you know more, it’s reasonable to **act cautiously**: stay home if possible, avoid close contact with high-risk people, improve ventilation, and wear a mask around others. Your child should also avoid school/daycare if feverish or feeling unwell, and follow local policies.

For comfort: tea, honey if age-appropriate for your child over 1 year, cough drops for you, fluids, humidifier/steam, and avoiding excessive throat-clearing can help. Saline spray or allergy treatment may help if post-nasal drip is contributing.

Seek urgent care now if you or your child develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, bluish lips, confusion, severe weakness, dehydration, persistent high fever, wheezing/stridor, or oxygen levels under 92–94% if you have a pulse oximeter.**

You’re not overreacting — you’re trying to protect your family. But the cough “sound” alone isn’t the deciding factor; testing is the best next step.

query: i have type 1 diabetes and to put it simply, i am living in an anxious state right now, worrying constantly about the coronavirus. my wife is a practice nurse in a gp surgery, riverbank gp surgery, and i worry constantly about the risk for us both. i am so scared. my name is adam keane, dob 14/09/1985, and i know that prob doesnt matter but i keep thinking if i ring someone theyll ask all that anyway and ill forget it bc im in such a state. i keep checking my sugars more than normal and then panicking when they move even a little, and then i panic more because stress makes everything worse, its like a stupid loop. my wife is trying to be calm but shes seeing people all day, coughing people, worried people, and then she comes home and i know she changes and washes and all that but i still feel like the virus is just walking in with her on her clothes or hair or bag. i keep asking her questions and i can tell im doing her head in, but im honestly terrified for both of us. i emailed dr meera patel before at adam.keane82@gmail.com but i dont know if i should call again or if im wasting their time when everyone is busy. my number is 07843 555 019 if anyone actually needs to ring, but really i just need to know if type 1 means im basically in huge danger if she brings it home. i havent slept right for days, i lie there listening to her breathe and then checking my own chest, and every tiny tickle in my throat makes me think thats it. sorry this is rambling, i just cant switch it off and im so scared.
target_response:
Hi Adam — I’m really sorry you’re feeling this frightened. What you’re describing sounds like a very understandable anxiety spiral: worry → checking sugars/body sensations → seeing small changes → more worry. That doesn’t mean you’re “wasting anyone’s time”; it means you need support.

Having type 1 diabetes does **not** mean you are “basically in huge danger” if you catch COVID. It can increase the risk of more serious illness, especially if glucose levels run very high, ketones develop, or there are diabetes complications. But many people with type 1 recover well, particularly when they have a sick-day plan and seek help early if things deteriorate.

Your wife working in a GP surgery understandably feels scary, but the measures she’s already taking—PPE at work, handwashing, changing clothes—make a real difference. Practical steps at home: she can wash hands as soon as she enters, avoid touching her face before washing, keep work shoes/bag in one place, clean phone/ID/badge regularly, shower/change if she’s had high-risk exposure, and launder uniforms/clothes normally. If either of you develops symptoms, follow current local testing/isolation guidance.

For your diabetes: try not to check continuously unless you’re unwell or your diabetes team has advised it. Stress can raise glucose, and small fluctuations are normal. Use a structured plan—e.g., check at usual times, plus if symptomatic, and check ketones if glucose is persistently high or you feel ill. Keep hypo treatment, insulin, ketone strips, and sick-day guidance available.
