People with advanced MS can be at higher risk of aspiration — tiny amounts of saliva, food, or stomach contents going down the wrong way — even if it is not obvious.

If Robert is breathing comfortably, has no fever, is acting like himself, is not more weak or confused than usual, and his oxygen level is normal for him if you have a pulse oximeter, it is reasonable to avoid dragging him out tonight — but I would call his nurse/doctor first thing in the morning and ask for same-day guidance. He may need repeat lung exam, oxygen check, and possibly another chest X-ray or different treatment plan.

That said, please seek urgent care/ER tonight or call emergency services if any of the following are present: trouble breathing, fast or labored breathing, blue/gray lips or fingertips, chest pain, coughing blood, fever or shaking chills, oxygen level below about 92% or below his usual baseline, marked sleepiness/confusion, choking episode, inability to clear secretions, or he just seems significantly worse to you.

For tonight: keep him upright as much as possible, especially after eating/drinking; encourage fluids only if he swallows safely; note his temperature, breathing rate, and any oxygen readings; and observe whether the odor continues or the sputum changes. Good mouth care can also help reduce bacterial odor.

Given his MS and recent pneumonia, if you feel uneasy or cannot confidently assess his breathing, it is absolutely appropriate to call the on-call clinician tonight. Your instincts as his caregiver are important.

query: what is the treatment for covid-19? im asking for myself, maria lopez, dob 03/14/1989, bc i keep reading totally different stuff online and its making me more nervous instead of less. i dont know if im supposed to just stay home and ride it out or if there is a real medicine they give now, like pills or inhaler or antibiotics or what. i called dr. neil patel office but the nurse said they are backed up and someone will call me later, and i dont wanna wait all day if there is something i should be doing now. i feel like everyone says drink water and rest but then other people say you need treatment quick or it can get worse, so im confused. i dont want to go to the er if thats not needed, but also dont want to be dumb and ignore it. if its covid-19 do you treat fever and cough the same way as a cold or flu, or is there specific covid meds? and do i need a test first before anything can be prescribed? i can get someone to pick stuff up at cvs on 5th ave if its just otc stuff, but if its prescription idk how that works if im trying not to go in anywhere. also what are the signs that mean ok enough waiting go get checked, like breathing, chest pain, fever number, how many days, all that. sorry this is rambling i just want simple steps like what do i take, what do i avoid, when do i call back, and when is it urgent.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I understand why the mixed information online is making you anxious. The good news is that many healthy adults with COVID-19 can recover at home, but there **are** prescription treatments for certain people, and timing matters.

**1) First step: test.**  
If you have symptoms, take a home COVID test now if you can. If it’s negative but symptoms are new, repeat in 24–48 hours. Most clinicians will want a positive test before prescribing COVID antivirals.

**2) Treatment at home if symptoms are mild:**  
Treat fever/cough similarly to a cold/flu:
- **Fever/aches:** acetaminophen or ibuprofen as directed on the label.  
- **Cough/congestion:** honey (if not allergic), fluids, humidifier/steam, saline spray; guaifenesin or dextromethorphan can help depending on cough type.  
- **Rest + fluids.**  
- Avoid taking leftover **antibiotics**, steroids, or unproven meds—antibiotics do not treat COVID unless there’s a separate bacterial infection.

**3) Prescription COVID medicine:**  
The main pill is **Paxlovid**, which works best if started within **5 days of symptom onset**. It’s usually recommended for people at higher risk of severe illness—examples: pregnancy, immune suppression, significant heart/lung/kidney disease, diabetes, obesity, age over 50, or certain other conditions. It also has important drug interactions and kidney-dose considerations, so a clinician/pharmacist needs to review your meds/health history. If you have risk factors, don’t wait all day—call Dr. Patel’s office again and say you may be within the antiviral window, or use an urgent telehealth/urgent care option.

**4) When to seek urgent/emergency care now:**  
Go to the ER/call emergency services for: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips/face, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, oxygen level **<92%** if you have a pulse oximeter, or inability to keep fluids down/signs of dehydration.

**Call your doctor/urgent care today** if fever is **≥103°F**, fever lasts more than 3 days, symptoms are worsening after initially improving, you have wheezing/asthma/COPD, pregnancy, or any high-risk condition.

For CVS: someone can pick up OTC meds for you; prescriptions can often be sent electronically after a phone/video visit.

query: hi, this is melissa ryan, dob 03/14/1987, sorry if this is a dumb question but ive been going in circles reading stuff online and now im more nervous than before. i read that people taking steroids for asthma are at higher risk for poor outcomes from covid19. does this include inhalers containing steroids? like the daily type inhaler, not pills, bc i keep seeing “steroids” and i cant tell if they mean prednisone or if they also mean inhaled meds. also, is mild intermittent asthma a risk for poor outcomes or only more severe forms of asthma? thanks. i have mild intermittent asthma, usually only flares if i get a bad cold or if im around dust/cats, and i barely use my rescue inhaler most months, but dr. nina patel had me keep an inhaled steroid around after i had bronchitis last year and now im scared to use it if it somehow makes covid worse. at the same time im also scared not to use it and then have my breathing get dumb for no reason. i called the office but i know everyone is swamped and the portal reply might take awhile, so im asking here too. i picked up the inhaler at CVS on 2nd ave and the label says steroid on the info sheet, which is what started this whole panic spiral. no fever or anything right now, just anxious and trying to figure out what category i fall into, bc all the articles say asthma but then dont say if they mean mild asthma like mine or people who have severe asthma and are on oral steroids a lot. should i be changing anything or just keep doing what i was told?
target_response:
Hi Melissa — not a dumb question at all. The wording online is confusing, and “steroids” can mean very different things.

In general, the concern about steroids and infections is mostly about **systemic steroids** — pills or injections such as **prednisone**, especially when used frequently or for longer courses. These can suppress the immune system more broadly. **Inhaled corticosteroids** used for asthma are different: the medicine goes mainly to the lungs, with much less absorption into the rest of the body. For people with asthma, the benefit of keeping airway inflammation controlled generally outweighs any theoretical concern.

For COVID-19 risk: the highest concern is usually in people with **moderate to severe asthma**, poorly controlled asthma, frequent exacerbations, recent ER visits/hospitalizations, or those needing repeated oral prednisone. From what you describe — **mild intermittent asthma**, rare rescue inhaler use, triggered mostly by colds/dust/cats — you would not typically be in the same risk category as someone with severe or uncontrolled asthma.

The most important point: **do not avoid an inhaled steroid if it was part of your asthma plan or if Dr. Patel instructed you to use it during flares.** Letting asthma worsen can create more risk than using the inhaler. If the inhaled steroid was prescribed “to keep around” after bronchitis, it would be reasonable to confirm the exact instructions with your office when they can reply — for example, whether you should use it daily during colds/flares, or only if symptoms start. But you do not need to panic about having it or using it as directed.

For now, keep doing the basics: avoid known triggers when possible, keep your rescue inhaler available, stay current on COVID/flu vaccines if you are eligible, and test if you develop respiratory symptoms.

Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing at rest, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, severe wheezing not improving with rescue inhaler, needing the rescue inhaler repeatedly more often than your plan allows, or oxygen levels persistently below ~92% if you monitor at home**.

Bottom line: your mild intermittent asthma plus an inhaled steroid does **not** mean you should stop your asthma plan. Using it appropriately is usually protective, not harmful.

query: hi, my name is nadia botha and sorry this is prob messy but im stressing and just trying to explain it right. i travelled to the uk, went there for a bit and came back in rsa on the 1st or march on British Airways BA55, but the symptoms only appeared thursday 19 march with mild ear ache to severe in the evening. like during the day i thought maybe it was just the plane or weather or my ears from flying, but by evening it was proper sore and i was getting anxious. then it was followed by soar throat, cougth and body ache on friday, and fever and weekness on saturday? i dont know if im over reacting or if this is how corona starts, because the travel was 2 wks plus before and i was fine in between, doing normal things at home and trying not to panic, then suddenly thursday it all started. i did not feel this bad when i landed, which is why im confused. my family keeps asking if i should call someone or isolate more serious, and i dont want to waste anyones time but also dont want to ignore it if its dangerous. i spoke quickly to dr lerato mokoena before about my ear stuff once, not this time yet, and i dont know if i must phone her or rather go for a test or just stay inside and take something for fever. the cough is there but not crazy all the time, throat is sore, body ache is making me tired and the weakness saturday scared me. please can you tell me if the timing after uk travel still fits or if its more likely flu/ear infection, and what i should do now?
target_response:
Hi Nadia — I’m sorry you’re feeling so worried. You’re not overreacting; with fever, cough, sore throat, body aches and weakness, it’s sensible to take this seriously and get advice.

The timing is a bit mixed: COVID symptoms most often start about 2–14 days after exposure, so becoming ill around 18 days after your flight makes infection *from the flight/UK travel itself* less likely, though not impossible. It also could be that you were exposed after returning to RSA. Your symptoms could fit COVID, but they can also fit influenza or another viral infection. The severe ear pain at the start could be from pressure/eustachian tube blockage after a cold, or a middle-ear infection, especially if the ear pain persists, worsens, or there is fever.

What I’d recommend now:

1. **Self-isolate immediately** as if it could be COVID: stay in one room if possible, avoid close contact with family, use a separate bathroom if available, wear a mask around others, don’t share cups/towels, and clean high-touch surfaces.
2. **Phone Dr Lerato Mokoena or your local COVID/health hotline before going anywhere.** Don’t walk into a clinic unless told to, because they may direct you to a specific testing site.
3. **Ask about testing**, given your fever/cough plus recent international travel history. In South Africa you can also contact the COVID hotline **0800 029 999** for guidance.
4. For symptoms: **paracetamol** for fever/body aches as directed on the packet, fluids, rest, warm salt-water gargles, and monitor your temperature. Avoid exceeding the daily paracetamol limit.

Please seek urgent medical help now if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, confusion, fainting, blue lips, severe dehydration, a fever that won’t come down, worsening weakness**, or if the ear pain becomes severe with **swelling behind the ear, discharge, dizziness, severe headache, stiff neck**, or hearing loss.

So: yes, isolate and call for advice/testing today, and also mention the severe ear pain specifically, as that may need assessment or treatment.

query: hi im asking about my little girl riley ann cohen, she is 18 month old, dob 07/18/2013, and i feel like we have been going in circles since august 2014 and im just tired and scared. she has had chronic running nose and cough since august 2014, like it never fully stops, some days less but still there, and she was diagnosed with bacterial infections and took antibiotics and did not get better. her chart number they keep asking me for is mrn pch-448291 if that matters. on january 26th dr. melissa grant sent us for chest x-ray and they found pneumonia. she took antibiotics did not get better with runny nose and coughing, still coughing all night and nose just pouring, and im wiping her face constantly. then 10 weeks later another chest x ray still found pneumonia, so now im just thinking how can that still be there?? has been taking clindamyacin for 11 days now does not seem to be getting better, she is still cranky and coughing and sounds junky to me. and just now been prescribed proventil and qvar, which i picked up at cvs on 9th and market, but i dont understand how inhalers help if this is bacteria. my number is 215-555-7310 if nurse needs to call bc i dont always explain it right typing. im not trying to second guess but i am worried bc she is so little and every time they say antibiotic and then still pneumonia. but if she is suffering from bacterial infection with those inhalers make it worse or can they hide symptoms while infection keeps going? i just need to know if this sounds normal or if i should push for more tests.
target_response:
Hi — I’m really sorry you and Riley have been dealing with this for so long. Your concern is very understandable. An 18‑month‑old with months of runny nose/cough plus pneumonia that is still being seen on X‑ray 10 weeks later deserves a careful re-check and likely further evaluation.

A few important points:

**1) Inhalers do not treat bacteria, but they can still help.**  
Proventil/albuterol opens tight airways and can reduce coughing/wheezing. Qvar is an inhaled steroid that decreases airway inflammation, often used for asthma/reactive airway disease. At prescribed doses, Qvar generally does **not** “make pneumonia worse,” though it also will not cure a bacterial infection. It may improve cough from airway irritation even while antibiotics address infection. Make sure she uses a spacer with mask, and wipe/rinse her mouth after Qvar.

**2) Persistent “pneumonia” can mean different things.**  
Sometimes chest X-ray changes lag behind symptoms, but after 10 weeks — especially if she is still coughing all night and sounding “junky” after 11 days of clindamycin — she should be reassessed. Possibilities include resistant/atypical infection, recurrent viral infections, asthma/reactive airways, chronic sinus/adenoid drainage, aspiration/reflux/swallowing issues, an airway blockage/foreign body, immune system issues, or less commonly cystic fibrosis or an anatomic lung problem.

**3) What I would push for next:**  
Please contact her pediatrician today and ask for an in-person follow-up soon, ideally within 24–48 hours, with oxygen level, lung exam, and review of both X-rays. Ask whether she needs referral to a **pediatric pulmonologist**. Reasonable next tests may include repeat/targeted imaging, CBC, immune globulins/vaccine antibody response, sweat chloride test, pertussis testing if not done, and consideration of swallow study/ENT evaluation depending on the exam and X-ray pattern.

**Go to urgent care/ER now** if she has fast or labored breathing, ribs pulling in, blue lips, oxygen concerns, dehydration, persistent high fever, unusual sleepiness, worsening crankiness, or cannot drink.

Also, for privacy, avoid sharing her MRN/phone in online messages if possible. But medically: no, this does not sound like something to just “wait out” without re-evaluation.

query: hi, its mark ellis here, sorry if im rambling but i cant stop thinking about this. my wife sarah ellis is a nurse in a gp practice, riverside gp practice, and although she has a mask and gloves etc, is that enough to protect her, if an infected patient with the virus was to come in to her? she keeps saying its fine and they have procedures and she washes her hands all day, but im sat at home picturing some patient coughing right in front of her at reception or in the little treatment room and it makes me feel sick. she works under dr priya nair most days and theyre still seeing people who ring up saying they have fever/cough but then some just walk in anyway which is what scares me. she has a mask and gloves etc like i said, sometimes apron too, but i dont know if that actually stops it or if its just sort of better than nothing. im not trying to be dramatic but we have kids at home and i keep thinking what if she brings it back on her clothes or hair or phone. her nhs staff id gp-44721 is on her badge and shes proud of the job, i get that, but im worried. they told staff to call 01632 960184 if they get symptoms and she gave them sarah.ellis77@gmail.com for updates, but none of that tells me if shes really safe. should she be asking for a better mask or not seeing anyone with symptoms at all? i dont want to nag her but i need to know if mask and gloves is enough or if im right to be worried.
target_response:
Hi Mark — I can hear how frightening this feels, especially with children at home and Sarah working face-to-face with patients. It’s understandable to worry, but PPE and good procedures do reduce risk a lot when used properly.

For respiratory viruses like COVID/flu, the main risks are close-range droplets/aerosols and contaminated hands. In a GP practice, protection usually relies on **several layers**, not just gloves: telephone triage, keeping symptomatic patients separate or masked, distancing/screens at reception, ventilation, cleaning surfaces, and staff using appropriate PPE.

For routine close patient contact, a **fluid-resistant surgical mask**, hand hygiene, and appropriate gloves/apron are generally effective. **Eye protection** is important if someone is coughing close to her or there’s risk of splashes. A higher-grade mask such as **FFP2/FFP3** is usually reserved for higher-risk situations, especially aerosol-generating procedures, and it must be fit-tested to work properly. Gloves help only if changed appropriately; handwashing/alcohol gel is still the key protection.

It would be reasonable—not “nagging”—for Sarah to check with her practice manager/clinical lead that they have: clear triage for fever/cough walk-ins, patient masking, a separate area for symptomatic patients, adequate PPE including eye protection when needed, and a process for staff to raise concerns. If she feels PPE is inadequate for the task, she should escalate this at work.

At home, practical steps are: hand hygiene as soon as she gets in, wipe her phone, change out of uniform/work clothes and wash them normally, and avoid sharing towels/cups. Showering can help her feel reassured, but clothes/hair are a much smaller risk than close indoor exposure.

Seek urgent medical help if Sarah or anyone at home develops severe breathlessness, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, severe dehydration, or rapidly worsening symptoms. Use NHS 111 for advice, and 999 for emergencies.

One extra point: try not to share Sarah’s work ID, email, or workplace details online—it’s safer for her privacy. Your concern comes from love, but if these thoughts are becoming constant or overwhelming, it may also help to talk it through with your GP.

query: hi,my sinuses usually act up during seasonal change (like now). my worry is my symptoms resemble that of covid-19 and im trying not to be dramatic but also i dont wanna be the person who ignores it and gets everybody sick. my name is jessica miller and i can be reached at 267-555-0146 or jess.miller89@gmail.com if someone needs to tell me where to go or how this works. throat has been a bit irritated, lately nose has been runny. its not like im gasping or anything, and i keep telling myself this happens to me every time the weather switches, but then i read stuff online and start panicking bc its like ok sore throat, runny nose, maybe allergies, maybe covid, who knows anymore. wanted to know how i could get myself tested as i don’t live alone, thanks? i share the place with my bf and his mom and she gets nervous about everything, plus we have one tiny bathroom so if i need to isolate i dont even know how that would work. i was thinking maybe Temple ReadyCare on 2301 E Allegheny Ave if they do swabs, but i dont know if i need an appt or referral or if i just show up and wait outside? i have Independence Blue Cross #XQ3829041 if that matters for testing cost, bc i really cant afford some surprise bill right now. also do i need to have a fever to qualify? i havent been checking nonstop, just feel kinda blah and congested. i keep washing my hands and wiping handles but im still anxious. pls just let me know the fastest way to get tested and if i should stay in my room til then.
pii_units: Temple ReadyCare on 2301 E Allegheny Ave
target_response:
Hi Jessica — you’re not being dramatic. Sore throat/runny nose/congestion can absolutely be seasonal allergies or a sinus flare, but they can also be COVID or another respiratory virus, so it’s reasonable to test—especially since you live with others and your boyfriend’s mom is understandably anxious.

**Fastest way to test:**  
1. **Use an at-home rapid COVID test today** if you can get one from a pharmacy. You do **not** need a fever to test.  
2. If it’s **negative**, repeat a rapid test in **48 hours** if symptoms continue, because early tests can miss infection.  
3. If you want the most reliable single test, schedule a **PCR/NAAT test** through an urgent care, pharmacy, or your primary care office. For Temple ReadyCare or any urgent care, **call or check online first** to confirm whether they do COVID swabs, whether an appointment is required, and what your out-of-pocket cost may be. Insurance coverage varies, so also consider calling Independence Blue Cross member services to ask about testing costs at that location. Don’t just walk in unmasked—wear a well-fitting mask and follow their instructions.

**Until you know:**  
Yes, I’d recommend staying mostly in your room if possible, especially around your boyfriend’s mom. Wear a mask when leaving the room, improve ventilation/open windows if feasible, avoid sharing cups/towels, wash hands, and wipe high-touch surfaces. With one bathroom: use it after others if possible, wear a mask, wash hands well, wipe faucet/handles, and keep your own towel separate.

**Symptom care:**  
For likely allergies/sinus irritation: saline nasal spray/rinse, fluids, humidifier/steam, and an antihistamine such as cetirizine or loratadine may help. Acetaminophen or ibuprofen can help throat discomfort if you can take them safely.

**Seek urgent care now** if you develop shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, dehydration, severe worsening headache or facial swelling, fever that is high/persistent, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

For now: mask, limit contact, test today if possible, and repeat or get PCR if negative but symptoms persist.

query: hi sorry if this is a dumb question but can we use disprin or compral pils when we have corona. they dont contain ibuprophen? im asking for me and my husband, im noluthando mthembu and we are stuck at home and i keep reading diff stuff on fb and whatsapp and now im more confused than before. i have the box here and it says disprin and another old pack of compral pils in the cupboard but i dont wanna take something wrong if corona makes it worse or if it thins blood or whatever ppl keep saying. i tried calling dr anika steyn earlier but the line was busy and the receptionist just said message the clinic if its not urgent. our normal place is khayelitsha family clinic and they know us there, i think my file is MRN KF-204889, but i dont wanna go sit there with other sick ppl if the answer is just yes/no about these pills. we use clicks pharmacy at 18 main rd usually and the lady there once told me no ibuprophen if covid but now im seeing some ppl say thats old news so idk. my number is 082-555-9146 if someone needs to phone me back, but otherwise just answer here is fine. also my medical aid is bonitas #BN4471092 but it doesnt help much after hours lol. we both have corona symptoms and im just trying to keep the fever/head pain under control without doing something stupid. can disprin or compral be used or should we rather stick to paracetamol only?
target_response:
Hi Noluthando — not a dumb question at all. There is a lot of confusing information going around.

**Short answer:** for COVID symptoms like fever and headache, **paracetamol is usually the safest first choice**. If it controls the fever/headache, I’d stick with that.

**Disprin contains aspirin.** Many **Compral** products also contain **aspirin** and often **paracetamol/caffeine** too, so check the ingredients carefully. Aspirin does **not** “make COVID worse” in general, but it **can thin the blood** and can irritate the stomach, increase bleeding risk, and worsen asthma in some people. You should avoid aspirin/Disprin/Compral if either of you has a stomach ulcer/bleeding, aspirin allergy, severe asthma triggered by aspirin, kidney disease, is on blood thinners, has a bleeding disorder, is pregnant unless prescribed, or drinks heavy alcohol. Also don’t use it “to prevent clots” unless a doctor specifically told you to.

**Important:** If Compral contains paracetamol, don’t take it together with other paracetamol without counting the total dose. For most adults, paracetamol is **500–1000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed**, maximum **4000 mg in 24 hours**; use a lower maximum if liver disease, heavy alcohol use, low body weight, or older/frail.

About **ibuprofen**: the early COVID warnings were not strongly supported by evidence. Many people can use it safely, but avoid it with ulcers, kidney disease, blood thinners, uncontrolled high blood pressure/heart failure, or pregnancy unless advised.

At home: rest, fluids, light meals, isolate, and monitor symptoms. If you can get an oximeter, check oxygen levels.

**Seek urgent care now** if either of you has shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, confusion, blue lips, oxygen saturation **below 92–93%**, severe weakness, signs of dehydration, fever not settling after a few days, or if symptoms improve then suddenly worsen.

If you’re unsure about the exact Compral ingredients, phone your Clicks pharmacist or message Khayelitsha Family Clinic with a photo of the box before taking it. Also, avoid sharing medical aid/file/phone details in open messages when possible for privacy.

query: hello doctor, i am ramesh kumar das, sorry for long msg again, my online file no is RM-774218-B and i am suffering from coughing, throat infection from last week. at that time fever did not persist and also did not felt any chest pain. two days later, i consulted with dr. arindam sen at apollo clinic salt lake because nearby govt hospital was too crowded and i was scared. he prescribed cavidur 625, montek lc, ambrolite syrup and betaline gargle solution. since then throat infection improved and frequent cough also coming out. coughing also improved remarkably though not completely. from yesterday onwards fever is occuring (maximum 100-degree celcius). i have not come in touch with any foreign returned person nor went outside. in our state, there is no incidence of covid-19, still family members are worried and my mother is telling to check again and again. please suggest what to do? i am keeping separate glass and towel at home but not sure if needed. thank you doctor, after doing all these i can upload all for further query, if required i can mail from ramesh.das82@rediffmail.com also because app is sometimes not uploading properly. thank you doctor, as per your guidelines, i have made one test. due to city shutdown, i could not able to make an x-ray of chest, all x ray places nearby are closed or asking for police pass. fever is coming every 12 hours. but cough does not persist currently. the only problem is a fever. i am uploading the blood examination report. please advice. thank you doctor, how many tablets of azithromycin to be taken? is it in the morning? i want to make you know that i have already taken azithromycin five days before. should she start it? sorry my wife typed some line so it is written she. and also can i start an iron tablet today itself?
target_response:
Hello Mr. Ramesh Kumar Das, thank you for explaining everything clearly. I understand your family’s worry, especially with fever appearing after the cough/throat symptoms had started improving.

First, I want to clarify: I assume you mean fever around **100°F** (not 100°C). A temperature of 100°F is a **low-grade fever**. Since your cough is now much better and there is no chest pain or breathlessness, this is somewhat reassuring. Fever can continue for a few days in viral throat/respiratory infections, even as cough improves. However, fever coming every 12 hours should be monitored.

Regarding medicines:  
- **Do not restart azithromycin on your own**, especially since you already took it 5 days ago. Repeating antibiotics without clear indication can cause side effects and resistance. If a doctor has specifically prescribed it again, please confirm the dose with that doctor.  
- If you are currently on **Cavidur 625**, take it only for the duration prescribed by Dr. Sen; do not extend it without review.  
- **Iron tablet** is not urgent during an acute fever unless you have confirmed iron deficiency/anemia and were advised to take it. It can also cause stomach upset, so it is better to start after fever settles or after your doctor reviews the blood report.

For now:  
1. Take **paracetamol 500–650 mg** for fever if temperature is ≥100.4°F or if you feel uncomfortable, keeping within safe daily limits.  
2. Drink plenty of fluids, rest, continue gargles, and monitor temperature 3–4 times/day.  
3. Maintain precautions at home: separate towel/glass, mask if close to family, handwashing, and good ventilation. This is sensible even if COVID risk seems low. If testing is available locally, a COVID/flu test is reasonable for fever with respiratory symptoms.

Please seek urgent care if you develop **breathlessness, chest pain, oxygen saturation <94% if you have an oximeter, persistent fever >101–102°F for more than 3 days, confusion, severe weakness, blood in sputum, or worsening cough**.

If you can upload the blood report here, I can comment better. If X-ray is not possible now, it may be needed only if fever persists/worsens or breathing symptoms develop.

query: i travelled to austria on 25 jan 2020 and returned on 31jan 2020. my trip included a stopover in munich. im dana morris and i know this is maybe me being paranoid but i keep seeing all the coronavirus stuff online and on tv and now im second guessing everything even tho i currently do not show any symptoms listed for coronavirus. no fever, no cough, no breathing thing, nothing like that, i feel normal, but i was in airports and on the plane and then the stopover in munich so now im like was i around someone and just dont know it. i came back and went to work like usual and saw my parents too so im anxious about that part more than myself tbh. i have kaiser medi-cal #KM27491833 and i dont want to get stuck with some huge bill if its just a precaution test and they say it wasnt needed. i also left a msg for dr. maya seghal but havent heard back yet and the nurse line wait was forever, like i hung up after sitting there stressed for a while. should i get lab tested as a precaution and will this test be covered by the medical? i was thinking of going to sutter urgent care if thats the right place but i dont want to walk in and freak people out or expose anyone if the advice is just stay home and watch for symptoms. also if there is a certain number of days after return that matters, i returned on 31jan 2020 so im trying to count it right and not overreact but also not be dumb about it.
target_response:
Hi Dana — I understand why this is making you anxious, especially after being in airports/planes and then seeing your parents. Based on what you wrote, you are currently **asymptomatic** — no fever, cough, or breathing symptoms — which is reassuring.

For coronavirus/COVID-19, the usual incubation period is **2–14 days after exposure**. Since you returned on **Jan 31**, the key monitoring window would generally run through about **Feb 14**. During that time, watch for fever, cough, shortness of breath, sore throat, or feeling significantly ill. A stopover in Munich and travel to Austria alone would not usually mean you need testing unless you had **close contact with a confirmed case** or had traveled to a higher-risk outbreak area as defined by public health guidance at that time.

I would **not recommend walking into urgent care just for precautionary testing while you feel well**. In many settings, testing is reserved for people with symptoms and/or specific exposure criteria, and urgent care may not be able to test you without public health approval. Also, if you have Kaiser Medi-Cal, going to a non-Kaiser/Sutter urgent care could create coverage issues unless it is an emergency or authorized. I can’t confirm your benefits here, so the safest financial step is to contact **Kaiser Member Services** or the Kaiser advice line and ask specifically about COVID-related evaluation/testing coverage and where they want you seen if needed.

For now:  
- Continue normal hygiene: frequent handwashing, avoid touching your face, disinfect high-touch surfaces.  
- If possible, avoid very close contact with older/high-risk family members until your 14-day window passes, mainly for peace of mind.  
- If symptoms develop, **stay home**, wear a mask if around others, and call Kaiser or your local public health department before going in.

Seek urgent care/emergency help right away if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, severe weakness/confusion, bluish lips, dehydration, or a high/persistent fever**.

You’re not being “dumb” or paranoid — you’re trying to be responsible. Given no symptoms and no known confirmed exposure, monitoring at home and calling Kaiser for guidance is the right next step.

query: hi, my name is maria gomez and im trying not to panic but i am a bit honestly. my daughter lily gomez of 10 has a high fever 39-40 degrees., sore throat and body aches. she was fine earlier and then it just went up and down and now she is laying on the couch crying that everything hurts and her throat burns. her dob is 11/14/2015 if that matters for dosing or whatever. i gave fluids and tried to keep her in light clothes but when it hits 40 i get scared, then it drops a little and comes back. i have elderly parents living with us and i am concerned about covid 19. they are both in the same house and my dad keeps walking into the kitchen even tho i told him please stay away from her room, so im worried about exposing them if this is covid or flu or strep or idk. we do have insurance blue cross ppo #BG7429110 but i dont know if i should go straight to urgent care or wait and call dr. kevin patel in the morning because the offices always say only come if breathing is bad. she is breathing ok right now, no blue lips or anything, but the fever is high and the body aches are a lot. please advise. should i isolate her completely from my parents like mask in the hall and separate bathroom if possible, should we all test for covid 19 now or wait, and when is 39-40 degrees too high for a 10 yr old? i dont want to overreact but i also dont want to miss something and then regret it. please tell me what to do tonight.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry, I know a 39–40°C fever is scary, especially with elderly parents at home. Lily’s sudden high fever, sore throat, and body aches could be **flu, COVID-19, strep throat, or another viral infection**. Breathing normally is reassuring, but she should be evaluated soon.

**What to do tonight:**

1. **Treat the fever/pain**
   - Use **acetaminophen or ibuprofen based on her weight and the bottle directions**.
   - General dosing:  
     - **Acetaminophen:** 10–15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours  
     - **Ibuprofen:** 10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours, only if she’s drinking reasonably well and not vomiting/dehydrated  
   - **Do not give aspirin.**
   - Light clothing, fluids, and a comfortably cool room are good. Avoid cold baths or alcohol rubs.

2. **Hydration and monitoring**
   - Encourage small frequent sips: water, electrolyte drink, popsicles, broth.
   - Watch her alertness and urine output. She should be peeing at least every 8–10 hours.

3. **Testing/isolation**
   - Yes, **isolate her from your parents as much as possible** starting now: separate room, separate bathroom if possible, mask in shared spaces, good ventilation, handwashing, disinfect high-touch surfaces.
   - Do a **home COVID test now** if you have one. If negative, repeat in 24–48 hours because early tests can miss it.
   - Flu and strep testing would require a clinic/urgent care.

**When to go tonight to urgent care/ER:**
Go now if she has trouble breathing, blue lips, confusion, is difficult to wake, stiff neck, severe headache, rash/purple spots, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, drooling or inability to swallow, severe worsening throat pain, or fever **≥40°C that won’t come down at all with medication** or she looks very ill.

If none of those are present but she remains miserable with high fever and sore throat, I would **call Dr. Patel’s after-hours line tonight if available** and plan for **urgent care or pediatrician visit tomorrow morning** for COVID/flu/strep testing and treatment guidance. Flu antivirals work best early, and strep needs antibiotics if confirmed.

query:
should i shave my beard to reduce my chances of contracting coronavirus / covid-19? im asking bc i keep seeing ppl say masks dont seal right if u have facial hair and now im overthinking it bad. my name is marcus ellery, dob 03/14/1989, and i know this is prob a dumb question but i cant get a straight answer anywhere. i work in the mail room at 1600 Market St, Philadelphia, PA 19103 and im around random ppl all day, elevators, packages, security desk, shared bathroom, all that. i wear the regular blue mask most days but it feels like air is coming out the sides and under my chin where the beard is. its not like a giant biker beard but its enough that the mask doesnt sit flat, and my gf keeps telling me to just shave it off already.

ive got no symptoms right now, no fever or anything, just worried about catching it. i called Penn Urgent Care South Philly but was on hold forever and then chickened out bc i felt silly asking. my chart thing has MRN PNH-774102 if that matters, and if someone needs to call me its 267-555-0198, email marcus.ellery89@gmail.com but really i just wanna know if shaving makes a real difference or if its only for n95 type masks. like if im just wearing a normal mask at work and on errands, is stubble ok or does it basically ruin the whole point? i dont wanna look ridiculous shaving for no reason, but i also dont wanna be the guy who got covid bc he was attached to a beard. sorry this is rambly, anxiety is high and everybody at work has a different opinion.
target_response:
Hi Marcus — not a dumb question at all. It’s a very practical one, especially since you’re around many people and shared spaces all day.

**Short answer:** shaving or closely trimming your beard *can* improve mask performance, but how much it matters depends on the type of mask.

For a **regular blue surgical/procedure mask**, it does **not require a perfect seal** the way an N95 does. Facial hair may let more air leak around the sides or under the chin, but it doesn’t make the mask “useless.” It still helps reduce droplets/particles you breathe out and gives you some protection.

For **N95/KN95/KF94-style respirators**, fit matters much more. An **N95 needs to seal against skin**, so beard hair where the mask contacts the face can significantly reduce protection. Even stubble can interfere with a tight seal. If you want the best protection at work, being clean-shaven where the mask seals is recommended.

A reasonable middle ground: you don’t necessarily have to go fully clean-shaven, but **trim the beard so the mask edge sits on skin**, especially along the cheeks and under the chin. If the mask is riding on hair and you feel air escaping, protection is reduced.

Practical steps:
- Consider upgrading from a loose blue mask to a **well-fitting KN95, KF94, or N95**.
- If keeping facial hair, choose a mask that fits better over your face shape and use the nose wire firmly.
- Try **ear-loop adjusters**, a mask brace/fitter, or tying/knotting ear loops to reduce side gaps.
- Avoid touching/adjusting the mask frequently.
- Stay up to date on COVID vaccination/boosters if you haven’t already.
- Remember: COVID is mainly spread through the air; packages/surfaces are lower risk than close indoor exposure.

You don’t have symptoms now, so no urgent care visit is needed just for this question. Seek care urgently if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or a high/persistent fever**. If you develop symptoms or have a known exposure, test and mask around others.

Bottom line: if your anxiety is high and you want the safest option, shave or trim enough for a good seal and use a better-fitting respirator. That’s not overreacting — it’s reasonable risk reduction.

query: hi, may i answer your health queries right now ? please type your query here... my name is linda marie keene, dob 04/18/1959, and i am sorry this is long but i get nervous typing this stuff and i dont want to forget anything. i had pneumonia in aug. of 2010, then again in oct, of same year with fluid on my lung, and that scared me so bad back then that now every cough makes me think of it again. my chart from the visit says MRN UC-77419 but i dont know if that matters. this past week i started with sore head eyes and ears, within 2 days i started coughing and wheezing, went to AFC urgent care on rt 38 and saw dr priya nair, no temp.,b/p 148/84, o2 95, wbc low,3 was given antibiotic and inhaler. they told me to use the inhaler and watch it but i still have bouts of coughing so much i get exhausted and a lot of wheezing, the wheezing is so bad , when i go to bed i am unable to sleep, i prop up pillows and still hear it in my chest and it makes me panic a bit. my daughter keeps saying call back, she can be reached at 856-555-0196 if i dont answer because my voice is raspy from coughing. i am taking the antibiotic like they said and trying not to over use the inhaler but i dont know how long is too long to wait. should i return to urgent care or will this get better soon, thank you so much. i just dont want to sit there again if this is normal, but i also dont want to miss pneumonia or fluid again.
target_response:
Hi Linda — I’m really glad you wrote, and I understand why this is frightening given your pneumonia and fluid around the lung in 2010. A cough with loud wheezing that is keeping you from sleeping and making you feel panicky is worth re-checking, especially at your age and with an oxygen level of 95% at urgent care.

It’s possible this is a viral respiratory infection/bronchitis causing airway irritation and wheezing, and the antibiotic may not change the cough quickly. Even with antibiotics, improvement can take 48–72 hours, and cough/wheeze can linger longer. That said, persistent or worsening wheezing, exhaustion from coughing, and inability to lie down comfortably can mean you need additional treatment—such as a nebulizer treatment, a steroid, a chest X-ray to look for pneumonia/fluid, or adjustment of the inhaler plan.

**My recommendation: call AFC/your primary care office today and ask to be re-evaluated today**, especially since your daughter is urging you and your symptoms are interfering with sleep. If you cannot be seen promptly, returning to urgent care is reasonable. Bring the antibiotic and inhaler with you, and tell them how often you are needing the inhaler and whether it helps.

If the inhaler is albuterol, many people are instructed to use **2 puffs every 4–6 hours as needed**, but follow your label. If you need it more often than directed, or it gives little relief, that’s a reason to be seen urgently.

**Go to the ER or call 911 now** if you have any of these: trouble speaking in full sentences, severe shortness of breath, blue lips/face, chest pain/pressure, confusion, fainting, oxygen below 92% if you can check it, coughing up blood, high fever, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

Tonight/while waiting: stay upright, sip fluids, use a humidifier/steam, avoid smoke/fragrances, and rest. Your prior pneumonia history doesn’t mean this is definitely pneumonia again—but it does justify being cautious and getting your lungs listened to and likely imaged if symptoms persist.

query: good day. my name is Darren Mills, dob 03/14/1982, and i am probly over reacting but this morning i coughed for the very first time in a long time. with the corona virus around i feel the need to report this bc i dont know if this is how it starts or if im just being nervous from watching too much news. i coughed for about 5 min, like a dry kinda cough i guess, and then it stopped. i have no fever, not tired and chest feels weird. not really pain, just weird like im paying too much attention to it now. i didnt cough yesterday or last week or anything, thats why it scared me. i have been mostly inside and washing hands but i did go out to pick up meds at Walgreens on 5th ave and also got groceries, so now im replaying every person who stood near me in line. my chart says MRN HMC-77261 if that helps anyone look me up, and my regular dr is Dr. Priya Nair but i didnt want to call the office if this is nothing and they are busy with real sick ppl. i keep checking myself and i still have no fever and im not tired, but chest feels weird and then i worry more and then it feels weirder if that makes sense. should i just stay home and watch it, or do i need to be tested for corona virus from one coughing spell? do i need to isolate from my wife in the house even tho it was only 5 min? i feel kinda silly asking but also dont want to ignore something important. what should i do?
target_response:
Good day, Darren — you are not being silly. A new cough during a COVID outbreak can understandably make anyone anxious, especially after being out for errands.

Based on what you described — **one dry coughing spell lasting about 5 minutes, no fever, no fatigue, and no ongoing cough** — this does **not strongly suggest COVID by itself**. A brief cough can happen from dry air, throat irritation, allergies/post-nasal drip, reflux, dust, or even anxiety and increased attention to breathing. The “weird” chest feeling that becomes more noticeable the more you focus on it can absolutely happen with worry or muscle tension, though it is still worth monitoring.

### What I’d recommend now
- **Stay home and observe for the next 24–48 hours.**
- Check your temperature once or twice daily, but try not to check constantly.
- Drink fluids, avoid smoke/strong odors, and rest.
- If the cough returns or you develop symptoms, consider a **home COVID test** if available, or contact Dr. Nair’s office for guidance on testing.
- If you need to go out, wear a mask and keep distance from others.

### Do you need to isolate from your wife?
With a single brief cough and no other symptoms, you likely do **not** need strict isolation. That said, it would be reasonable today to use common-sense precautions: don’t share drinks/utensils, wash hands, clean high-touch surfaces, and consider sleeping separately or masking around her **if the cough comes back, you develop fever, sore throat, congestion, body aches, fatigue, or feel unwell**.

### When to seek urgent care
Please seek urgent medical attention or call emergency services if you develop:
- Trouble breathing or shortness of breath at rest
- Chest pain/pressure, especially if persistent or radiating
- Blue lips/face, confusion, fainting
- Fever that is high or not improving
- Oxygen level below 92% if you have a pulse oximeter

For now, I’d treat this as something to **monitor rather than panic over**. If you have another coughing episode or any new symptoms, call Dr. Priya Nair’s office — that’s exactly what they’re there for, even during busy times.

target_response:
Dear Mr. Sharma,

I’m very sorry to hear how much you suffered—with herpes/pneumonia, itching and burning in the groin and armpits, changes in your appearance, and the shame and distress that can come with symptoms in private areas. It’s understandable that after many treatments and incomplete relief, you felt driven to try something yourself.

However, I must be very clear: **please do not continue or recommend applying a mixture of antibiotic powders (amoxicillin, cefuroxime/Seftum, erythromycin/Althrocin, ciprofloxacin/Cifran) to the skin or rubbing it near the epiglottis/throat.** Even if you felt better, this can be unsafe. Antibiotics used this way can cause **severe allergy, skin irritation/burns, disruption of normal skin bacteria, fungal overgrowth, antibiotic resistance, and delayed diagnosis** of the real condition. Rubbing any powder near the throat can also cause choking, aspiration, or airway irritation.

The kind of symptoms you describe in the **groin and armpits** are commonly due to conditions such as **fungal infection/intertrigo, eczema/contact dermatitis, bacterial infection, hidradenitis, psoriasis, or recurrent herpes**, and each needs different treatment. For example, many groin/armpit rashes worsen with unnecessary antibiotics and improve with antifungal treatment, keeping the area dry, loose cotton clothing, weight/sweat control, and avoiding scented soaps or harsh products.

What I recommend next:
1. **Stop using the antibiotic mixture on skin or throat.**
2. See a **dermatologist** for examination; they may do a skin scraping/KOH test, swab, or culture to identify fungus/bacteria/herpes.
3. Bring a list/photos of all medicines used, including steroids like Depo-Medrol, because steroids can worsen fungal infections.
4. Do not share or promote this “formula” to others—what seems to help one person can seriously harm another.

Seek urgent care if you have **fever, spreading redness, severe pain, pus, blisters near the eye, difficulty breathing/swallowing, swelling of lips/face, dizziness, or recurrence of pneumonia symptoms**.

I’m glad you feel better, but your health and others’ safety are best protected by confirming the diagnosis and using targeted treatment.

query: hi, this is nisha patel, sorry if this sounds dumb but im confused and kinda scared with all the posts going around, is it safe to take ibuprufen during thisbseasonnof corona outbreak or should we take paracetamol? i keep seeing ppl on fb and whatsap saying dont take ibuprufen and then someone else says its fine and now i dont know what to believe. my dob is 07/14/1989 if that matters for the chart or whatever, and i usually just take whatever is in the cabinet but now every little thing feels like a big decision bc of corona. i tried calling dr. kevin morales office earlier but the line was busy and the recording just keeps saying if its emergency go er, but i dont wanna go anywhere unless i really need to bc im trying to stay home. i was gonna ask the pharmacist at CVS on 14th st too but they looked so busy last time and i didnt wanna stand around near everybody coughing and talking in line, like the whole thing is making me nervous. can someone just tell me plain if ibuprufen is bad right now or if paracetamol is safer? i am not trying to over react, just dont wanna do the wrong thing for me or my family. if u need to send a reply my email is nisha.patel82@gmail.com but here is ok too. also is paracetamol same as tylenol or am i mixing that up?? sorry again for rambling, i know its prob a simple question but with corona outbreak everywhere it feels like even normal meds are scary now.
target_response:
Hi Nisha — this is not a dumb question at all. There was a lot of confusing information circulating early in the COVID outbreak, especially on Facebook/WhatsApp.

**Plain answer:** For most adults, **ibuprofen is not considered “bad” or forbidden because of COVID.** Major health agencies have not found good evidence that ibuprofen worsens COVID. That said, if you have a fever, body aches, or headache and you’re choosing between the two, **paracetamol is often a good first choice** because it has fewer stomach/kidney/blood-pressure issues for many people.

And yes: **paracetamol = acetaminophen = Tylenol** in the U.S.

**How to take it safely:**
- **Acetaminophen/Tylenol/paracetamol:** 500–1,000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed. Try not to exceed **3,000 mg/day** unless your doctor specifically says otherwise. Avoid or ask first if you have liver disease, drink heavy alcohol, or are taking other cold/flu products that may also contain acetaminophen.
- **Ibuprofen/Advil/Motrin:** 200–400 mg every 6–8 hours with food as needed; OTC max is usually **1,200 mg/day**. Avoid or ask a clinician first if you have stomach ulcers/bleeding, kidney disease, significant heart disease/heart failure, uncontrolled high blood pressure, take blood thinners, are allergic to NSAIDs, or are pregnant—especially after 20 weeks.

For your family, dosing is different for children and based on weight, so don’t use adult doses for kids. Also avoid aspirin in children/teens unless specifically directed.

If you or someone at home has possible COVID symptoms, the usual steps are: stay home, test if available, rest, fluids, and use fever/pain medicine as above. Seek urgent care/ER if there is **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, new confusion, blue/gray lips or face, inability to keep fluids down, severe weakness/dehydration, or oxygen levels persistently low if you monitor them.**

You’re doing the right thing by asking rather than standing in a crowded line. Also, for privacy, try not to share your DOB/email in general message threads unless necessary.

query: hi im maria lopez dob 03/14/1988, sorry if this is a dumb question but headache fever fatigue been in contact with someone with coronavirus do i need to be concerned ? i keep going back and forth bc part of me thinks maybe its just normal sick stuff or stress or not sleeping good, but then i remember i was around the person who later said they had coronavirus and now every little thing is making me nervous. the headache is just there and annoying, fever comes and goes, and the fatigue is like i can get up and do stuff but i feel wiped out way faster than usual. im not trying to panic but i also dont wanna be careless and get anyone else sick. i live with family and im trying to stay in my room and wipe stuff down but idk if thats enough or if i should be doing more. i called and left a msg for dr. kevin patel but havent heard back yet and i dont know if i should wait or try urgent care or just test at home first if i can find one. my callback is 215-555-0139 if someone actually needs to reach me, but mostly i just need to know if this sounds like something i should take seriously right now. no one around me is really giving the same advice, one person says just rest and drink water and another says get checked right away. i dont want to overreact but also the contact part is what is freaking me out, like if i hadnt been around that person i prob wouldnt even be asking. do i need to quarantine and for how long, and should i be concerned enough to get seen today?
target_response:
Hi Maria — this is not a dumb question at all. Given that you had close contact with someone who later tested positive for coronavirus **and now have headache, fever, and fatigue**, it is reasonable to take this seriously and act as though it could be COVID until proven otherwise. It could still be another virus, poor sleep, or stress, but the exposure makes testing and precautions important.

**What I’d do now:**
1. **Take a home COVID test today** if you can get one.  
   - If it’s positive, treat it as COVID.  
   - If it’s negative but symptoms continue, **repeat a home test in 48 hours** or arrange a PCR/clinic test, since early tests can be falsely negative.
2. **Stay home and separate from family as much as possible** while you have fever/symptoms. Use a mask if you must leave your room, improve ventilation, avoid sharing cups/utensils, wash hands often, and wipe high-touch surfaces.
3. **Stay isolated until your fever is gone for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine and symptoms are clearly improving.** After that, continue extra precautions/masking around others for about 5 more days, and longer around anyone elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised, or medically fragile.
4. For symptoms: fluids, rest, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen as directed on the label if you can take them safely.

**Do you need urgent care today?** If your symptoms are mild/moderate and you can breathe comfortably, drink fluids, and function at home, it’s reasonable to test first and wait for Dr. Patel’s office to call back. However, seek urgent care or emergency care **now** if you develop: trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, fainting, blue/gray lips, severe weakness, dehydration/not urinating, persistent fever over 103°F, fever lasting more than 3 days, severe/worst headache, stiff neck, new rash, or worsening symptoms.

Also, if you are pregnant, immunocompromised, or have significant conditions such as asthma/COPD, heart disease, diabetes, kidney disease, or obesity, contact a clinician promptly because antiviral treatment may be considered and works best early.

query: do i have covid 19? im maria lynn cortez and im sorry if this sounds dumb but im kinda spiraling and i dont know who to ask bc every website says something different and then i read one more thing and get more nervous. i dont even know if this is the right place to ask, but i keep thinking like what if i have it and im just walking around being careless, or what if im overreacting and making a huge deal for no reason. i havent really been able to focus on anything else today, i keep checking stuff online and then closing it and then opening it again. i can be reached at 267-555-0148 if someone actually needs to call me back but honestly i just need someone to tell me what im supposed to do next, like do i need a test, do i stay home, do i wait, do i call a clinic first, idk. i dont wanna sit in a waiting room if that makes it worse or if im not supposed to go in person, but i also dont wanna ignore it if it could be covid 19. i live with family so thats another thing that is making me panic bc i dont wanna get anyone else sick if this is something. my email is maria.cortez82@protonmail.com too if thats easier, i keep my phone near me but sometimes i miss calls when im anxious and running around. sorry this is long, i just keep asking myself do i have covid 19 or am i just scared bc everyone is talking about it, and i dont know what signs mean yes or no.

Hi Maria — this does **not** sound dumb. It’s very understandable to feel anxious when information online is inconsistent, especially when you live with family and don’t want to risk anyone else’s health.

I can’t tell from your message whether you have COVID-19 because you didn’t mention specific symptoms or a known exposure. COVID can cause fever, chills, cough, sore throat, runny/stuffy nose, headache, body aches, fatigue, loss of taste/smell, nausea/diarrhea, or shortness of breath — but anxiety can also make you feel “off,” and many viruses look similar.

Here’s what to do next:

1. **If you have any symptoms or had a close exposure:** take a home COVID rapid antigen test today if you can.  
2. **If it’s negative but symptoms continue:** repeat the test in 24–48 hours, because early tests can be falsely negative.  
3. **While you’re unsure:** stay home as much as possible, wear a well-fitting mask around family, avoid sharing cups/utensils, improve ventilation, wash hands, and sleep separately if practical.  
4. **If you need medical advice/testing:** call a clinic/urgent care first rather than walking into a waiting room. They can tell you their process and whether you need PCR testing or a visit.  
5. **If you test positive:** isolate according to current local guidance, and contact a clinician promptly if you’re higher risk or symptoms started within the last 5 days, because antiviral treatment may be an option.

Please seek urgent care now or call emergency services if you have **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue/gray lips, confusion, fainting, severe dehydration, or oxygen levels below 90–92%** if you have a pulse oximeter.

Also: I can’t call or email you from here, and it’s best not to post personal contact information online. If the spiraling feels unmanageable, step away from searching and consider calling someone you trust or a local nurse line for real-time reassurance.

query: i am a family doctor in an outpatient family medicine setting, im dr. melissa han and i work at riverside family medicine, and the question was raised reguarding treatment for community acquired pneumonia. i know this is prob basic but i keep going in circles bc it came up in clinic and i want to make sure im not missing some guideline thing or doing something dumb just bc the pt looks ok in the room. if reoccurance of infection occurs, after treatment with z pack within 90 days how should patient be treated. would it be safe to treat again with levaquin as an outpatient? for context this is our small office at 218 w maple ave and we see a ton of walk ins / urgent-ish stuff but we are not an er, so sometimes i am deciding whether to send them out or manage w close f/u, and the staff is asking me what to do before the end of the day. i also worry abt resistance and qt stuff and tendon warnings and all that, but also dont want to undertreat somebody who already had one round of azithro and now seems like the same thing is back or never fully cleared. the insurance piece was even brought up bc they said BlueCross PPO #BCP-774219 may not cover some meds easily, so of course that made the convo even more annoying. i was going to email the question to myself at mhan.familydoc@protonmail.com so i dont forget to look it up tonight but figured id ask here too. not asking for a whole consult, just wondering what ppl usually consider appropriate outpatient coverage when cap comes back within that 90 day window after a z pack, and if levaquin again is considered reasonable/safe or if that is not preferred.
target_response:
Dr. Han — very reasonable question, and you’re right to pause rather than simply repeat a Z‑pack.

For **outpatient community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) that recurs or fails to fully resolve within ~90 days after azithromycin**, I’d generally think in two steps:

### 1) Reassess before assuming “same CAP”
If symptoms are back or never improved, confirm severity and diagnosis: vitals, pulse ox, lung exam, and usually a **repeat chest X-ray** if not already done. Consider whether this is true bacterial CAP versus viral illness, COPD/asthma flare, CHF, PE, aspiration, post-obstructive pneumonia, malignancy, bronchiectasis, etc. If there was no clear initial radiographic pneumonia, I’d be even more cautious about labeling it recurrence.

Send to ED / higher level care for: **SpO₂ <90–92%, RR ≥30, hypotension, confusion, sepsis appearance, inability to tolerate PO, significant dehydration, multilobar disease, rapidly worsening symptoms, high-risk comorbidities, immunocompromise**, or concerning CURB-65/PSI.

### 2) If stable for outpatient treatment, avoid repeating macrolide
After azithro exposure/failure, I would **not repeat azithromycin**. Current ATS/IDSA-style outpatient options depend on comorbidities and local resistance.

For a stable outpatient with comorbidities or recent antibiotic exposure, reasonable empiric choices include:

- **Amoxicillin-clavulanate + doxycycline**  
  e.g., amox-clav 875/125 mg BID or 2 g ER BID + doxycycline 100 mg BID  
  *Often my preferred “fluoroquinolone-sparing” option.*

OR

- **Respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy**  
  **Levofloxacin 750 mg daily x 5 days** is guideline-concordant for appropriate patients.

So yes — **levofloxacin can be reasonable/safe as outpatient CAP coverage** when the patient is clinically stable and lacks major contraindications. But I’d reserve it for cases where beta-lactam + doxy is unsuitable, adherence is a concern, allergy limits options, or broader coverage is truly needed.

Before levofloxacin, I’d check: QT risk/meds, prior arrhythmia, significant electrolyte issues, aortic aneurysm risk, myasthenia gravis, tendon rupture history, neuropathy, CNS effects, renal dosing, and drug interactions. Also remember MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage is not routine unless prior isolation or recent hospitalization with IV antibiotics.

Insurance should not drive an unsafe choice; doxy/amox-clav are often cheaper and effective. Close follow-up in **24–48 hours** is key if managing from clinic.

query: hi, my name is maria lopez and i have a sore throat, coughing, swollen glands in neck. i am a teacher at ridgeview elementary and i was in contact with many kids until wed last week, like normal classroom stuff, helping them with coats, pencils, wiping tables, kids coughing all over the place like they always do. one of the parents tested positive for covid 19 but there is no way i was in contact with that parent, i only heard it thru the school message chain and now im sitting here overthinking every little tickle in my throat. i dont know if this is just a regular cold or allergies or if i should be acting like its covid even tho i didnt have direct contact. i called my regular office and left a msg for Dr. Hannah Kim but havent heard back yet and im not sure if i should go to urgent care or stay home and not risk being around more ppl. i also dont want to scare the school or make a big thing if its nothing, but i work with little kids and some of them have grandparents picking them up and i feel guilty even thinking i could have passed something around. i have been checking my temp but mostly its the throat and cough and the glands in neck feel puffy and sore. i can be reached at 215-555-4378 if someone needs to call me, but really i just need to know what should i do? do i isolate, get tested, tell the principal, wait it out, or go in somewhere? i was with many kids until wed last week and now im worried bc the timing feels weird even if that parent and me didnt cross paths.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this stress. Given your sore throat, cough, and swollen/tender neck glands, this could certainly be a routine viral cold, COVID, flu/RSV, or less likely allergies. Allergies usually cause itching/sneezing/clear drainage and don’t typically cause sore glands. Strep throat can cause swollen glands and sore throat, but cough makes a viral infection more likely.

Even without direct contact with the COVID-positive parent, your exposure to many children means it’s reasonable to treat this as potentially contagious until proven otherwise.

**What I’d recommend now:**

1. **Stay home from school for now** and avoid close contact with others, especially high-risk people.  
2. **Take a COVID test today** if you have one. If it’s negative, repeat an antigen test in **48 hours** or get a PCR/NAAT test if available. Early tests can be falsely negative.  
3. **Let your principal/school nurse know you have respiratory symptoms and are testing**, without needing to “make a big thing” of it. Schools usually have a standard policy, and this helps protect students and families.  
4. **Contact Dr. Kim’s office again** or use their portal if available. If your throat is quite painful, you have fever, white patches on tonsils, or no improvement, ask whether you should be tested for **strep**, COVID, flu, etc.  
5. **Urgent care is reasonable** if you can’t access testing or if symptoms worsen — wear a well-fitting mask and call ahead if possible.

For comfort: fluids, rest, honey for cough if you can take it, salt-water gargles, lozenges, and acetaminophen/ibuprofen as directed if you’re able to use them safely.

**Seek urgent medical care now** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, dehydration, persistent high fever, inability to swallow saliva, severe one-sided throat/neck swelling, or symptoms rapidly worsening.

In general, return to work when symptoms are improving and you’ve been fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine, but follow your district’s policy and mask for several days after returning.

query: my mother linda carver has cre/resistant klebsiella pnuemoneae(excuse the spelling) she is allergic to most antibiotics,sulfa, & penicillin. she has been treated five times obviouly they haven t worked in full! she easily goes anaphylactic! mid way through treatments and it is terrifying to watch, like one minute we are thinking ok maybe this time and then her body just goes crazy and everyone is running. last trtmt. was 7 days ago. 1st day , iv ertapenem 1mg and litocane, prior to trtmt. she premeds w/ 50 mgs oral benedryl,1 hour cooktime prior ttrtmt.there was to be an additional 9 doses im and on 6th dose went into shock. they have her chart at st marys medical center under MRN SM-772091 and i keep saying please look at all the allergies bc i feel like every new person asks the same thing and then acts surprised when i say she goes anaphylactic so easy. she is being readmitted in a.m. for another attempt and i am sitting here tonight with my stomach in knots packing her bag again, her robe, socks, inhaler stuff, papers, all of it, and she is scared but also trying to act like she is fine so i dont panic. she is trying oral doses of colodial silver bc somebody told her it helped and i dont even know if that is ok or dangerous with this infection or if it makes things worse, i keep reading online and then getting more scared. help what to do? how long can this go on before she dies? what are the symptoms of death dur to cre? like what do i watch for, when do i scream for help, how do i know if the infection is winning or if the meds reaction is the bigger danger. i know nobody can promise anything but i need plain words please.
target_response:
I’m so sorry you and your mother, Linda, are going through this. Watching repeated anaphylaxis is terrifying, and your concern is completely appropriate.

First: I can’t access St. Mary’s records or her MRN from here, but **the team admitting her must treat her as extremely high-risk for medication reactions**. When she arrives, ask clearly for:

- **Infectious Disease specialist** to direct CRE/Klebsiella treatment based on culture “susceptibilities”
- **Allergy/Immunology consult** if available
- Treatment in a **closely monitored setting** with **epinephrine immediately available**
- A visible allergy band/list, including what happened, which dose, and timing  
- Clarification: is this a true active infection or colonization? What is the source—urine, lungs, blood, wound, line?

CRE Klebsiella is serious, but it is not automatically hopeless. There are newer antibiotics that can work for some resistant Klebsiella strains, depending on the resistance pattern. Repeated “failed” treatments can happen if the germ is resistant, the infection source isn’t controlled, or reactions stop therapy early.

About **Benadryl premedication**: it may reduce itching/hives, but **it does not reliably prevent anaphylaxis** and can sometimes mask early warning signs. The hospital should have a formal reaction plan.

Please tell her to **stop colloidal silver unless her physicians specifically approve it**. It is not proven to treat CRE and can cause harm, including kidney/liver problems, medication interactions, and permanent gray-blue skin discoloration.

Do not wait for “signs of death.” Watch for signs that need immediate help.

**Possible infection/sepsis red flags:** fever or very low temperature, shaking chills, confusion, extreme sleepiness, fast breathing, worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, very fast heart rate, dizziness/fainting, low blood pressure, little/no urine, bluish lips, or worsening cough/pain.

**Anaphylaxis red flags:** throat tightness, tongue/lip/face swelling, wheezing, trouble breathing, widespread hives, vomiting/diarrhea with weakness, sudden dizziness, fainting, or “something is very wrong.” In the hospital: hit the call button and yell for help. At home: call 911.

Tonight, if she develops any of those symptoms before admission, don’t wait until morning—go to the ER/call 911. Bring a one-page allergy timeline if you can.

query: i think i have coronavirus. my name is marcus ellis, dob 03/14/1987, and im at 1458 s 9th st apt 3b, philadelphia pa 19147 right now just sitting here getting more nervous the more i google stuff which i know is prob dumb. i am having chest pains from yesterday and during the course of today i have been dry coughing. what can i do now? like do i just stay home and wait it out or is chest pain one of those things where i should go in right away, bc i really dont want to overreact but also dont wanna be stupid. i tried calling my regular dr. nina patel but i got the after hours msg and im not sure if they will call back tonight, and i left my number 267-555-0194 but i keep checking my phone every 2 min. i dont have a thermometer that works right, the old one keeps blinking, so i cant even say if i have fever, its mainly the chest pains from yesterday and this dry cough today that is making me think coronavirus. i havent been able to relax, every cough makes me think ok this is getting worse, then i sit up and try to breathe slow and then i feel silly. i live alone so theres nobody here to tell me if i look bad or if im acting normal, and i dont wanna call 911 if this is something i should just manage at home. should i be getting tested somewhere, calling urgent care, or going to er because of the chest pain? please tell me what the safest next step is now.
target_response:
Marcus, I’m sorry you’re sitting with this alone — it’s very understandable to feel anxious, and Googling symptoms can make that spiral worse. A dry cough can be COVID or another respiratory virus, but **chest pain is the symptom that deserves caution** because it can come from many causes, including muscle strain from coughing/anxiety, pneumonia/pleurisy, asthma/bronchospasm, acid reflux, or less commonly a heart or blood clot problem.

**Safest next step tonight:**  
If you are having **chest pain right now that is persistent, worsening, feels like pressure/tightness/heaviness, or is not clearly just soreness from coughing/movement**, you should be evaluated **now** — ideally **call 911**, especially since you live alone. Don’t drive yourself if symptoms feel significant.

Call 911 immediately if you have any of these red flags:
- Trouble breathing, shortness of breath at rest, or you can’t speak full sentences  
- Chest pressure/heaviness, pain spreading to arm/jaw/back  
- Sweating, nausea/vomiting, fainting, severe dizziness  
- Blue/gray lips, confusion, extreme weakness  
- Coughing blood  
- New rapid/irregular heartbeat or pain that is getting worse

If the chest pain is **mild, sharp, only with coughing/deep breaths or pressing on the chest**, and you are breathing comfortably, it may be reasonable to **call an urgent care/after-hours line or nurse triage tonight** for guidance and arrange evaluation/testing. But because you’ve had chest pain since yesterday, I would not simply “wait it out” without speaking to a clinician.

For possible COVID: wear a mask if you seek care, avoid contact with others, hydrate, rest, and consider acetaminophen for aches/fever if safe for you. If you can, get a working thermometer and/or pulse oximeter; oxygen levels **below 92–94%** warrant urgent evaluation.

While deciding, sit upright, slow your breathing, and avoid repeatedly checking symptoms online. But please use the chest-pain rule above: **ongoing or concerning chest pain = emergency evaluation now.** If you’re unsure whether it’s concerning, it is safer to call 911 and let professionals assess you.

query: hi, this is dana mellor, dob 09/14/1987, and im prob overthinking but i keep going in circles on this. if allegra use is associated with increased risk of viral infections, isn't it dangerous to take it during the coronavirus pandemic? like i read one thing online, then another thing says its fine, then some random forum says antihistamines mess with your immune response and now im sitting here staring at the box like an idiot. dr. nina patel told me a while back i could use allegra when my allergies get bad, and normally i just grab it from cvs on maple ave and dont think about it, but right now everything feels different bc of coronavirus and im scared to do something that makes me more likely to catch a virus or get worse if i already picked something up. i dont have a fever or anything im asking more bc my nose and eyes are awful and if i dont take it i sneeze like crazy, which then makes me nervous too bc everyone looks at you like you have covid. i know allegra is not an antibiotic or steroid or whatever, but the wording about viral infections is what freaked me out. is that a real increased risk or just some side effect listing that doesnt mean much? also is it more dangerous if youre taking it every day vs just here and there. sorry this is rambling, i just dont wanna make a dumb choice during the pandemic, and i also dont wanna be miserable from allergies if its actually safe.
target_response:
Hi Dana — you’re not being silly. There has been a lot of confusing health information online, and it makes sense that wording like “viral infections” would catch your attention.

For **Allegra (fexofenadine)** specifically: it is **not an antibiotic, steroid, or immune-suppressing medication**. It works by blocking histamine, which is one of the chemicals that causes allergy symptoms like sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, and runny nose. It does **not meaningfully weaken your immune system**, and there is **no good evidence that taking Allegra makes you more likely to catch coronavirus or makes COVID worse**.

The “viral infection” language you may have seen is usually from side-effect reporting in studies or package inserts. Common colds and viral symptoms happen frequently in the general population, so if someone in a trial gets a cold while taking Allegra, it can end up listed—even if the medicine didn’t cause it. That does **not** mean Allegra is known to increase viral infections in a clinically important way.
