query: i am experiencing headache (severity: moderate) (time frame: gradual) (occurred: 1 - 3 hours) (side: front) (worsened by: psychological stress, being tired, menstruation, skipping meals) (unaffected by: bending forward, changes in sleep pattern, sneeze or like when i bend to pick stuff up it doesnt really change, its just this front pressure/ache and im trying not to panic. my name is melissa grant, dob 08/14/1991, and i think my chart thing says MG-774203 if that helps. i called earlier from 267-555-0198 but i was at work and missed the call back bc my phone was on silent, so email might be easier too, its mel.grant91@mailbox.com. i see dr priya nair usually but i know she may be booked and im not trying to be dramatic, i just dont know when a headache is normal vs when its something bad. it started gradual today, like not a thunderclap or anything, maybe 1-3 hours ago, and its in the front. i am on/near my period and i skipped breakfast bc i was rushing, then had coffee and now im tired and stressed so i know those are probably not helping. i did not notice it getting worse with bending forward, sleep changes, sneezing or that kind of thing, so thats why im confused. i havent taken anything yet except water and i was thinking of getting something at cvs on 19th and market after work but wanted to ask first bc i hate mixing meds when im already anxious. should i try ibuprofen or tylenol and food and wait, or should i be seen today? also if someone calls please leave a voicemail bc i keep missing calls and then i spiral lol.
target_response:
Hi Melissa — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this, and it’s completely understandable to feel anxious when a headache comes on.

From what you describe — **gradual onset**, **moderate frontal pressure/ache**, occurring while **near/on your period**, after **skipping breakfast**, with **coffee, stress, and fatigue** — this sounds most consistent with a common headache trigger pattern, such as a **tension-type headache** or **menstrual/hormonal migraine-type headache**. The fact that it was **not sudden/thunderclap** and is **not worsened by bending, sneezing, or position changes** is reassuring.

For now, if you are otherwise feeling okay, it is reasonable to try home treatment first:

- Eat something with protein/carbs since skipping meals can trigger headaches  
- Drink water or an electrolyte drink  
- Rest in a quiet/dim place if possible  
- Consider one medication option:
  - **Acetaminophen/Tylenol 650–1,000 mg** once, following package limits — avoid if significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use  
  - OR **Ibuprofen 400 mg with food** — avoid if pregnant, on blood thinners, history of stomach ulcers/bleeding, significant kidney disease, or advised not to take NSAIDs  
- Avoid taking multiple headache meds at once unless you know they are safe for you. Also avoid using pain relievers more than 2–3 days per week regularly, as that can worsen headaches over time.

You do **not necessarily need to be seen today** if the headache improves with food, fluids, rest, and medication and no concerning symptoms develop.

Please seek urgent care or ER evaluation **now** if you develop any red flags: sudden “worst headache of life,” new weakness/numbness, facial droop, trouble speaking, confusion, fainting, seizure, fever/stiff neck, vision loss, severe eye pain, headache after head injury, persistent vomiting, pregnancy/postpartum headache, or a headache that is rapidly worsening or very different from your usual.

If it persists beyond 24–48 hours, keeps recurring around your period, or doesn’t respond to usual measures, scheduling with Dr. Nair or a colleague would be appropriate. Also, for voicemail/email preferences, please update your clinic contact settings directly through the portal or front desk for privacy and reliability.

query: my friend linda marie koch suffers from diagnosed bronchiectias and gets pneumonia constantly. she is 72, dob 09/03/1953, and honestly i am just trying to help her because she is so tired of this whole loop and i feel like nobody really gives us a straight answer. each time they give her antibotics for a week and then the xray will show the pneumonia gone, however, it contantly returns. like she starts to feel a tiny bit better and then a few wks later same thing again, coughing and feeling awful and back for another xray and more meds. she has been to mayo, iowa city had many test, lung test, sputum stuff i think, scans, all of it, and then still it just keeps coming back. it's beginning to take a toll on her. she used to go out and do errands and see friends and now she worries every sniffle is going to turn into pneumonia again, and i worry too bc she downplays everything until she is really worn out. i would say she had pneumonia about 6 times last year and so far 2 or 3 this year. i dont know if its normal with bronchiectias for the xray to clear then come right back or if we should be asking about a different germ, immune thing, inhaled antibotics, daily prevention, pulmonary rehab, or if there is some specialist we are missing. she said i could put her cell here if someone needed follow up, 319-555-0174, but mostly i just want suggestions on what questions to ask at the next appt. should she be on something longer than a week when this happens? should cultures be done every time before antibotics? any suggestions would help because we are both kinda at a loss and she is getting discouraged.
target_response:
I’m sorry Linda is going through this. Recurrent “pneumonia” in someone with bronchiectasis is unfortunately common, but having 6 episodes in a year is a sign that her prevention plan may need to be revisited—not just repeated 7-day antibiotic courses.

A key point: in bronchiectasis, flare-ups can look like pneumonia on symptoms/X-ray, but sometimes they are “exacerbations” from chronically infected/damaged airways rather than a brand-new infection each time. Treatment often needs to be tailored to the bacteria she grows and may require longer courses—commonly 10–14 days, especially if she has Pseudomonas or more severe disease. A sputum culture before antibiotics is very helpful whenever feasible, ideally including testing for routine bacteria and, periodically, nontuberculous mycobacteria/NTM.

Questions to ask her pulmonologist at the next visit:

1. “Do you consider these true recurrent pneumonias or bronchiectasis exacerbations?”  
2. “What organisms has she grown on sputum cultures? Has she ever grown Pseudomonas, MRSA, or NTM?”  
3. “Should cultures be obtained before each antibiotic course, and should treatment be 10–14 days rather than 7?”  
4. “Does she need a formal daily airway-clearance plan?” This may include nebulized saline, bronchodilator if helpful, flutter valve/Acapella, chest vest, and respiratory therapy instruction.  
5. “Given the frequency, is she a candidate for prevention therapy?” Options can include long-term azithromycin in selected patients, inhaled antibiotics if chronic Pseudomonas is present, and aggressive airway clearance.  
6. “Have causes of recurrent infection been rechecked?” Examples: immune deficiency/immunoglobulin levels, aspiration/GERD or swallowing issues, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, sinus disease, COPD/asthma overlap, and NTM.  
7. “Would pulmonary rehab help her strength, stamina, and confidence?”

She should also stay current on influenza, COVID, RSV, and pneumococcal vaccines.

Seek urgent care if she has worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, oxygen levels persistently below her usual/under 90%, coughing blood more than streaks, high fever, dehydration, or rapid decline.

Also, for privacy, avoid posting her phone number online. But your instinct is right: she deserves a clearer bronchiectasis management plan, not just repeated rescue antibiotics.

query: do you think this corona virus stuff will ever go away? i'm tired of being a prisoner in my own home cause of everything getting cancelled, im not working and i cant volunteer cause of a bad knee and cant lift heavy stuff. i keep sitting here at 1208 s 9th st, philadelphia pa staring out the window like im 90 yrs old or something and its making me nuts. my name is maria delgado and i swear i used to be busy all the time, even if it was just running errands, going to the store, helping with little stuff, but now every plan is cancelled and every phone call is someone saying dont come, stay home, wait it out. like ok but wait how long?? so when will this be over? i called my primary dr. kevin o'malley and they just say the same thing, stay away from people, wash hands, be careful, but nobody can say if this is weeks or months or forever and my brain goes in circles. i cant even go help at the volunteer place cause my knee is bad and they mostly need people lifting boxes and moving stuff, and i cant do that without paying for it for 3 days. im not working either so its just quiet all day and news news news and more scary news. my daughter keeps texting me to not panic but then she tells me not to leave, so which is it lol. if someone needs to call me back its 215-555-4198 but really im just asking cause im tired and bored and anxious and i feel trapped in my own house over this corona virus stuff.
target_response:
Maria, I’m really sorry you’re feeling so trapped. What you’re describing—boredom, anxiety, frustration, your mind “going in circles”—is a very common and very real reaction to prolonged isolation and uncertainty. It does **not** mean you’re weak or “panicking”; it means you’ve lost a lot of your normal structure and connection all at once.

Will coronavirus “go away”? Probably not completely in the sense of disappearing forever, but it also won’t always feel like this. Over time, viruses become something we manage with vaccines, treatments, testing, and common-sense precautions. Restrictions and risk tend to come in waves depending on local spread, but “cancel everything forever” is not the long-term goal. I wish I could give you an exact date, but no honest clinician can promise weeks vs months. What we *can* do is help you get through this in smaller, manageable pieces.

A few practical steps for right now:

- **Limit news** to 1–2 short check-ins per day from reliable sources. Continuous news will feed anxiety.
- **Make a daily schedule**, even simple: wake time, meals, phone call, light activity, one chore, one enjoyable thing.
- **Get outside safely if permitted**—even sitting on the stoop, a short walk, or fresh air can help. Avoid crowds and follow current local guidance.
- **Stay useful without lifting**: ask volunteer groups if they need phone calls, paperwork, organizing donations, texting seniors, or remote help.
- For your knee, try **gentle range-of-motion exercises** or chair exercises, and ask Dr. O’Malley’s office whether physical therapy or pain-management advice is appropriate.

Please also tell your daughter and/or Dr. O’Malley plainly: “I’m not just bored—I’m feeling anxious and trapped.” They may be able to connect you with counseling, medication options if needed, or community supports.

Seek urgent help if you develop chest pain, severe shortness of breath, confusion, blue lips, fainting, or if you feel you might hurt yourself. For emotional crisis, call/text **988** in the U.S., or call 911 if you’re in immediate danger.

Also, for your privacy, try not to share your full address or phone number in public messages.

query: 3yrold having rash, my son mason ellis, dob 09/08/2022, specially under armpits and his thighs too. he can’t sleep at night due to that and im up with him almost every hour bc he keeps scratching and crying and then saying it burns when i try to look. i’ve tried lotion to hydrate, just the same baby lotion we always use and i put some after bath but it still looks red and kinda patchy, not like one big cut or anything. he had minor eczema when born but not too serious, it cleared up back then and we havent had big problems in a long time so now im confused if this is eczema again or heat rash or allergy to soap or something. we did switch laundry stuff last wk bc i grabbed whatever was cheapest, so idk if that matters. what can i do? don’t want to take him to hospital due to virus! im really trying not to panic but with him not sleeping and the rash being under armpits and thighs where its sweaty i keep thinking it might get worse. we live at 6237 w glendale ave apt 4 and it’s been hot in here, ac is weird, so maybe that made it flare. i called dr. priya nambiar office but they said no openings til next week and i dont wanna sit in urgent care with all the sick ppl. if there is something safe otc i can get from walgreens on 83rd ave tonight please tell me, like cream or bath stuff, or if i should stop lotion, loose clothes, no soap? he is eating ok and playing some in day but nights are awful and im scared to make it worse.
target_response:
I’m sorry Mason is so uncomfortable — itching/burning in the armpits and thigh folds can absolutely keep kids up. From what you describe, the most likely causes are **eczema flare/contact irritation** from the new laundry detergent, **heat/sweat rash**, or **intertrigo** in skin folds. If it’s very bright red/moist with little “satellite” bumps, yeast can also be involved.

What you can do tonight:

1. **Stop the new detergent** and rewash his pajamas, sheets, towels, and clothes in a **fragrance-free/dye-free detergent**. Avoid fabric softener/dryer sheets.
2. **Keep him cool and dry:** loose cotton pajamas, cool room/fan if safe, change sweaty clothes promptly.
3. **Bath:** lukewarm water only or a gentle fragrance-free cleanser; keep it short. You can try a **colloidal oatmeal bath**. Pat dry — don’t rub.
4. **Moisturize differently:** baby lotions can sting and contain fragrance. Use a thick, bland ointment like **Vaseline/Aquaphor/CeraVe Healing Ointment** on dry/patchy areas.
5. For itchy red eczema-like patches: **OTC hydrocortisone 1%** can be used in a *very thin layer* 1–2 times daily for **3–5 days**. Use cautiously in armpits/thigh folds; avoid genitals, open skin, or if it looks infected.
6. If the folds look **beefy red/moist with small red dots around the edges**, use **clotrimazole 1% cream** twice daily instead, and keep the area dry. Don’t use steroid alone on a yeast-looking rash.

For itching at night, you can consider **children’s cetirizine** per the package dosing for his age/weight; avoid sedating medicines unless his doctor advises.

Please seek urgent care tonight if he has **fever, rapidly spreading redness, swelling, pus, blisters, honey-colored crusting, severe pain, trouble walking, facial/lip swelling, breathing issues, lethargy, or decreased drinking/urination**.

Since he’s eating and playing some, this likely doesn’t require the hospital right now, but I’d call Dr. Nambiar’s office again and ask for a nurse call-back or to send photos. If not improving in 48–72 hours, he should be seen.

query: my 22 yo son jason miller travelled by plane between baltimore (bwi) & rhode island last wk and i am trying not to panic but honestly i keep going in circles about it. he was on southwest flight WN 4827, just a normal packed-ish plane from what he says, not like empty but not shoulder to shoulder either, and realistically, what are the risks of covid 19? he has no symptoms and there have not been any reported cases on his flight, at least nothing the airline emailed and nothing i can find online, but i keep thinking what if nobody knows yet or somebody was sick and didnt say anything. i am quarantining him as best i can at home, like hes in the back bedroom, using a seperate bathroom when he remembers, im wiping handles and the fridge and yelling at him to stop wandering into the kitchen lol but also not lol bc im nervous. i called dr. lena ortiz office and they basically said watch for fever/cough/sob and dont rush in unless symptoms start, but i still feel weird just waiting. he feels totally fine, eating normal, no cough, no fever, not tired, just annoyed at me. i even made him use the thermometer i bought at cvs on post rd because our old one was dead. should he stay totally away from me and my husband for 14 days or is that overkill since no symptoms and no reported cases? should i be doing masks inside the house or just hand washing and distance? i know im probably overreacting but he just got off a plane and everything on the news makes it sound like airports are the worst place to be.
target_response:
I understand why this is making you anxious—airports and planes have been talked about a lot, and “waiting to see” can feel very uncomfortable. The good news is that Jason being 22, feeling completely well, with no known positive case linked to the flight, is reassuring. The risk is **not zero**, but it is also not automatically high just because he was on a plane. Airplane ventilation/filtration helps, and the bigger risks are usually prolonged close exposure to someone infectious, crowded airport lines, or shared indoor spaces.

Dr. Ortiz’s office gave sound advice: monitor for symptoms rather than rushing him in while he feels fine. COVID symptoms can appear anywhere from a couple of days up to 14 days after exposure, most often around day 5 or so.

A practical middle-ground approach at home:

- Have Jason limit close contact with you and your husband for **14 days from the flight** if you want the most cautious plan, especially if either of you is older or has medical conditions.
- He does not need to be treated like he is “contaminated,” but he should stay mostly in his room, use a separate bathroom if available, and avoid hanging out in common areas.
- If he comes into the kitchen or shared spaces, have him wash hands first and consider having him wear a mask, especially if you are nearby.
- You and your husband can mask too if you are in the same room with him, but distance, ventilation, and hand hygiene are the biggest pieces.
- Wiping high-touch surfaces is reasonable, but try not to exhaust yourself—COVID is mainly spread through respiratory droplets/aerosols rather than fridge handles.
- Check temperature once or twice daily, but don’t overcheck if it is fueling panic.

If testing is available/appropriate where you are, a test around **3–5 days after travel** can add reassurance, but a negative test does not fully replace watching for symptoms through the incubation period.

Seek medical advice promptly if he develops fever, cough, sore throat, loss of taste/smell, significant fatigue, or body aches. Urgent care/ER is warranted for trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips, confusion, dehydration, or worsening shortness of breath.

You’re not wrong to be cautious—you’re being protective. But based on what you described, a calm, sensible precaution plan is enough.

query: hi, my name is marsha bennett and i use athraway chronic for rheumatoid arthritis, will this complicate things relating to the corona virus? im sorry if this is a dumb question but i keep seeing all this stuff on tv and online and then i get myself worked up, especially bc i know the rheumatoid arthritis meds can be weird with your immune system or whatever, and i dont really understand if that means im more likely to catch it or if it just means if i got it then it would hit me harder. i dont want to stop anything on my own because dr. leila khan told me before not to mess around with my arthritis stuff without asking, but then im also scared to keep taking it if everyone is saying corona virus is worse for people with immune problems. i got the athraway chronic filled last at cvs on maple ave and the pharmacist was nice but they were super busy and i didnt wanna stand there coughing questions at them or make a whole scene, so now im just sitting here overthinking it. i dont have anything dramatic going on right now, i just want to know if taking athraway chronic for rheumatoid arthritis makes corona virus more dangerous or makes symptoms harder to notice or anything like that. should i be calling the rheum office first or primary care or just waiting unless i get sick? also should i be avoiding stores and family visits more than regular people? i feel kinda silly but also kinda not, bc nobody explains this stuff in normal words and all the news makes it sound like anyone on these meds is just in trouble. pls tell me what i should do, like keep taking it, call someone, stay home, idk.
target_response:
Hi Marsha — this is **not** a dumb question at all. It’s very reasonable to feel anxious when you hear “immune system” and “coronavirus” in the same sentence.

In general, rheumatoid arthritis itself and some RA medicines can affect the immune system. That **does not necessarily mean you are more likely to catch COVID**, but depending on the exact medicine and your overall health, it can sometimes mean a **higher risk of getting more seriously ill** if you do catch it. The risk varies a lot by medication — for example, long-term steroids like prednisone tend to raise risk more than some other RA treatments. Since “Athraway Chronic” may have specific ingredients or immune effects, the safest next step is to confirm with your rheumatology team.

Most importantly: **do not stop your RA medication on your own.** Stopping suddenly can cause a flare, and a bad flare may lead to needing steroids or urgent care, which could actually complicate things more. Dr. Leila Khan’s advice not to “mess around” with it without asking was exactly right.

What I’d recommend:

1. **Keep taking Athraway Chronic as prescribed for now**, unless your rheumatologist tells you otherwise.  
2. **Call or message Dr. Khan’s rheumatology office** and ask: “Does Athraway Chronic increase my COVID risk, and should I change anything if I’m exposed or test positive?”  
3. Make sure you’re **up to date on COVID vaccines/boosters**, flu vaccine, and any other vaccines your doctor recommends.  
4. Take extra-practical precautions: avoid crowded indoor spaces when possible, wear a well-fitting mask in busy stores/clinics, wash hands, and consider outdoor or well-ventilated visits with family.

These meds usually don’t make COVID impossible to notice, but some immune-modifying medicines can make fever or inflammation less obvious. So pay attention to new cough, sore throat, fever/chills, fatigue, body aches, congestion, diarrhea, or loss of taste/smell.

Seek urgent care now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, dehydration, or oxygen levels below your usual/under 92% if you monitor it**.

So: you’re not “just in trouble” because you take RA medication — but you should be cautious and loop in rheumatology before making changes.

query: my 19 year old son Tyler James Rios was diagnosed with pneumonia 5 months ago and was given antibiotics but with minimal results. unfortunately he still suffers from the same symptoms but since he is in the military he refuses to go back to the doctor for fear of reprisal. his cough is still deep and his energy is low. i keep trying to tell him this isnt normal and he says hes fine, just tired, but i hear him cough when he thinks nobody is paying attention and it sounds like its way down in his chest, not just a little cold cough. he was seen the first time at Fort Liberty clinic and they gave him the antibiotics and basically told him to rest and hydrate, but being military he didnt really rest like a normal person would, he went right back to doing what they tell him to do. i dont know if the meds didnt work all the way or if it was something else from the start, but after 5 months i feel like something should be better by now. he says he cant keep complaining bc it will follow him or make him look weak and hes scared it will mess with his job, and i dont know how to convince him without making it worse for him. i am his mother so maybe im over worrying but his energy is still low, he gets worn out easy, and he is only 19 so it scares me. are there any long term repercussions for his lungs if he continues without treatment. like could this scar his lungs or turn into something chronic if he just keeps pushing thru it. if someone could call me or tell me what to say to him my number is 704-555-3819, i just need to know how serious this could get.
target_response:
I can understand why you’re worried. A deep cough and low energy **5 months after a pneumonia diagnosis is not something I’d dismiss**, especially in a healthy 19-year-old who has had to keep pushing physically without real rest.

I can’t call you from here, and because Tyler is an adult, his clinicians generally can’t discuss his care with you unless he gives permission. But your concern is medically valid: he should be re-evaluated.

There are several possibilities. Sometimes a cough can linger after pneumonia for weeks, but **5 months is longer than expected**. It could mean the pneumonia never fully cleared, the initial diagnosis was incomplete, or something else is going on—such as asthma/reactive airway disease after infection, bronchitis, pertussis, vaping/smoking-related irritation, allergies/reflux, COVID-related lung irritation, TB or another infection, or rarely a complication like lung scarring or bronchiectasis. Many of these are treatable, but they need evaluation.

What I’d recommend he do is return to the Fort Liberty clinic or another military medical clinic and say clearly:  
**“I was treated for pneumonia 5 months ago, but I still have a deep cough and fatigue. I need to be reassessed.”**  
He should ask about a repeat chest X-ray, oxygen level check, lung exam, possibly spirometry/pulmonary function testing, and further testing if symptoms or imaging warrant it.

You might tell him this: seeking care is not weakness—it is readiness. Ignoring a persistent respiratory problem could actually jeopardize his performance and career more than getting it checked. Military medicine sees this constantly; getting evaluated early may prevent a bigger issue later. He does not need to “complain”—he needs to report persistent symptoms that affect endurance.

Yes, untreated or ongoing lung inflammation/infection can sometimes lead to long-term problems such as scarring, reduced exercise tolerance, or chronic airway issues, though that is not inevitable. The point is to catch anything persistent before it becomes harder to treat.

He should seek urgent care immediately if he has shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, coughing blood, fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, fainting, bluish lips, or rapidly worsening fatigue.

Your instincts as his mother are appropriate here: this deserves a medical follow-up.

query:
hi this is nisha patel dob 04/18/1992, i know this may be a small thing but im feeling really off and i wanted to ask before i start panicking. body aching all of a sudden, head heavy and paining, sneezing, somewhat fever since yesterday. like it hit me quick, i was ok in the morning then by evening my whole body felt sore like i did some huge workout but i didnt. head is heavy and paining, not sharp exactly but annoying and making me wanna just lie down, and i keep sneezing on and off. fever is not very high i think, somewhat fever since yesterday, i dont have a proper thermometer battery working so i just feel hot then chills a little, then hot again. i havent taken anything except water and one regular pain tablet from home because i dont wanna mix stuff wrong.

im supposed to go in to work tomorrow and i dont know if i should stay away or if this is just cold/allergy. i called brightcare clinic earlier but got stuck on hold and then had to hang up, so im trying here first. if someone calls me back my number is 267-555-0148 but texting is easier because my head feels too heavy to talk much. no big cough really right now, mostly sneezing and aches and this feverish feeling since yesterday. i also slept badly and now im overthinking if it is flu or covid or just weather change. should i test today or wait, and what can i take for the body ache and head paining? also when should i worry enough to go urgent care, like if fever goes up or if breathing gets bad? sorry im rambling, just feeling suddenly sick and anxious.
target_response:
Hi Nisha — I’m sorry you’re feeling so miserable. A sudden onset of body aches, heavy headache, sneezing, chills/feverish feeling, and fatigue can definitely happen with a viral infection such as COVID, influenza, or another “cold” virus. Allergies usually cause sneezing/itchy eyes/runny nose but **do not typically cause body aches and chills/fever**, so what you’re describing sounds more infectious than allergy/weather change.

**What to do today**
- **Take a COVID test today** if you have one. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours** (early tests can be falsely negative).
- If you can access a clinic/pharmacy test, consider **flu testing**, especially since symptoms started suddenly and within the last 48 hours—antiviral medication works best early for flu in certain situations.
- Since you feel feverish and achy, I’d recommend **not going to work tomorrow**, especially until you’re clearly improving and fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medicine. This also helps protect coworkers.

**What you can take**
Because you already took a “regular pain tablet” but aren’t sure what it was, avoid stacking medicines until you confirm the ingredient. Common safe options:
- **Acetaminophen (Tylenol)** 650–1,000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed; max **3,000 mg/day** (avoid if significant liver disease or heavy alcohol use).
- **Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)** 400 mg every 6–8 hours with food; avoid if pregnant, kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, on blood thinners, or uncontrolled high blood pressure.
- Drink fluids, rest, use saline spray/steam, and consider honey/tea if throat irritation develops.

**Please seek urgent care now or today** if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, fainting, stiff neck, severe/worst headache, persistent vomiting, dehydration, blue lips, or a fever **≥103°F**, or fever lasting more than 3 days. Also get evaluated if symptoms rapidly worsen or you’re immunocompromised.

If you can, replace the thermometer battery—it will help guide decisions. You’re not rambling; feeling suddenly sick can be scary, but most viral illnesses improve with supportive care over a few days.

query:
i am recovering from pnuemonia and i keep trying not to be dramatic but im starting to get nervous about this one spot. my name is melissa hanley, dob 03/08/1979, and i was just seen thru mychart under MRN pgh-77291 but i dont wanna keep bugging them if this is just normal after coughing so much. i was thinking that that i tore a muscle coughing but now i am beginning to wonder. on the right side of my chest, under my breast it hurts when i cough. it hurts when i press on it, but when i pinchthe muscles with my fingers i have no tenderness, which is the part making me feel weird bc if it was just a pulled muscle wouldnt that hurt too? it hurts as i go to touch my toes, like that bending forward motion catches under the rib area and then i stop bc it freaks me out. could i be having a problem with an organ under my ribs ? i have no pain on my left side.

i finished the meds they gave me and ive been using the inhaler like they said, and i picked the cough stuff up at cvs on murray ave, but the cough is still enough that i keep bracing myself. dr. lena ortiz said the pnuemonia should take awhile to feel totally better but i didnt ask about this sharp under breast/rib pain specifically. i dont have crushing chest pain or pain going down my arm or anything like that, its just this right side thing, worse when coughing or pressing or bending. im worried maybe gallbladder or liver or something dumb im googling at 2am. do i need to get checked again or is this just from coughing hard for days and days?
target_response:
Melissa, you’re not being dramatic — new sharp pain while recovering from pneumonia is worth asking about, especially when coughing is still strong.

What you describe — **right-sided pain under the breast/rib area that worsens with coughing, pressing on the spot, and bending forward** — most commonly fits **chest wall/rib irritation from coughing**, such as an intercostal muscle strain, costochondral/rib joint irritation, or even a bruised rib. The fact that “pinching the muscle” doesn’t hurt doesn’t rule that out; the painful tissue may be **deeper between the ribs, along the rib cartilage, or the lining around the lung**, rather than the superficial muscle you can pinch.

Pneumonia can also cause **pleuritic pain** — irritation of the lung lining — which can feel sharp and be worse with coughing or deep breaths. That can linger as infection resolves, but it’s still something your care team may want to know about.

Gallbladder/liver pain is **less likely** based on your description because your pain is clearly triggered by coughing/pressing/movement. Gallbladder pain is more often deeper right upper abdominal pain, sometimes after fatty meals, with nausea/vomiting, and may radiate to the right shoulder/back.

Because you’re recovering from pneumonia and this is a focal sharp pain that’s making you stop movement, I’d recommend you **message or call Dr. Ortiz’s office** and specifically mention: “right-sided under-breast/rib pain, worse with cough/pressing/bending, ongoing cough after finishing meds.” You are not bugging them — this is exactly the kind of follow-up they expect. They may decide you need an exam, repeat chest X-ray, or just supportive care.

For now, if safe for you: use **acetaminophen or ibuprofen/naproxen** as directed, try heat or ice, brace the area with a pillow when coughing, hydrate, and continue the inhaler/cough medicine as prescribed.

Please seek urgent care/ER now if you develop **worsening shortness of breath, chest pressure, pain with dizziness/fainting, blue lips, coughing blood, fever returning, oxygen low if you monitor it, rapid heartbeat, severe worsening pain, new leg swelling, or significant right upper belly pain with vomiting or yellowing skin/eyes**.

query: hpv virus has affected my sex life so much and i honestly feel embarassed even typing this but i dont know what else to do. my name is maria santos, dob 03/14/1996, and ive been dealing with this for a while now and its making me feel like im broken or something. i bleed a lot during sex and on my period, like not just spotting sometimes, actual bleeding that makes me panic and then i cant relax at all. i did a cervical cauterisation with dr nisha patel and i was hoping that would finally make it better but my period is still the same and heavy and scary and i’m scared to have sex due to bleeding as i was once hospitalised for it at st marys hospital. that time really scared me because i thought maybe something had torn or gone very wrong and now every time anything starts i get anxious and tell my partner to stop even if i want to be close. i keep thinking about hpv and whether it means my cervix is just always going to bleed or if the cauterisation didnt work or if i need another one. i also dont know if its normal for the period to still be like this after that or if i should push harder to be seen again. i feel stupid calling again because they already checked me and did the procedure, but also this is messing with my relationship and my head so bad. i avoid sex now, i check for blood constantly, and when my period comes i just dread it. any tips on what i can do? like should i ask for more tests, another smear, ultrasound, hormones, anything? i just want to know what is normal and what is not because im tired of being scared of my own body.
target_response:
Dear Maria, I’m really sorry you’re going through this. Please don’t feel embarrassed—bleeding with sex and very heavy periods are distressing, and it makes complete sense that a prior hospitalization would leave you anxious and “on alert.” You are not broken.

HPV itself usually does **not** mean your cervix will “always bleed.” It can be associated with cervical cell changes, but bleeding during sex can also come from many other causes: cervical ectropion/inflammation, infection, polyps, hormonal changes, vaginal dryness/trauma, or less commonly significant cervical abnormalities. Cervical cauterisation can help bleeding from a fragile area of the cervix, but it **does not usually treat heavy periods**, so persistent heavy menstrual bleeding needs its own assessment.

Given you’re still having “actual bleeding” with sex and heavy/scary periods—especially with a past hospital visit—I would absolutely recommend booking a follow-up with your GP/gynecologist. You are not “bothering” them; this is exactly the kind of symptom that deserves reassessment. Reasonable things to ask about include:

- Speculum/pelvic exam to look at the cervix and check healing after cauterisation  
- Review of your cervical screening/HPV results and whether colposcopy is needed  
- Swabs for infections such as chlamydia/gonorrhea and cervicitis  
- Pregnancy test if relevant  
- Blood tests: full blood count and ferritin/iron stores; possibly thyroid tests  
- Pelvic ultrasound to look for fibroids, polyps, ovarian/uterine causes  
- Options to reduce bleeding: tranexamic acid during periods, NSAIDs if safe for you, hormonal contraception, or a hormonal IUD depending on your preferences/medical history

Until you’re reviewed, it’s okay to pause penetrative sex or use gentler approaches, plenty of lubricant, and agree with your partner that you can stop immediately without guilt. The anxiety response is understandable; if it’s taking over, support from a therapist—especially someone familiar with sexual health/medical trauma—can help.

Please seek urgent care now if you soak a pad every hour, feel dizzy/faint, have severe pelvic pain, fever, foul discharge, large clots, or bleeding after sex that doesn’t settle. Otherwise, please call and ask for a prompt gynecology review—you deserve answers and relief.

query: i'm michael riley, dob 04/18/1989, and i know this prob sounds dumb but i'm really finding difficult to maintain precisely 6 ft in grocery stores and it is making me nuts. i went to giant food store on 1001 s broad st in philadelphia pa today just to grab milk, bread, bananas, normal stuff, and i was trying so hard to stay away from ppl but the aisles are tight and ppl keep coming around corners like they dont see u. as i was leaving, someone entering the store that was (possibly) 3 ft away was coughing lightly and i took a shower when i got home. like i literally came in, put the bags down, washed hands, wiped the counter, then showered and changed clothes, and now im sitting here replaying it over and over. i'm a hypochondriac. possible covid-19? i dont have a fever right now and im not coughing, but i keep checking my throat and breathing and it makes me more anxious. i know it was only like a second or two and they werent coughing hard, more like a little cough, but they were coming in while i was going out and i dont know if 3 ft for that quick still counts as exposure or not. i also feel stupid asking but i live with someone who gets worried too, so now im worried i should stay in another room or wear a mask inside or something. if someone needs to call me my number is 267-555-0146 or email m.riley89@gmail.com, but mostly i just need to know if this is a real risk or my brain doing the usual panic thing.
pii_units: giant food store on 1001 s broad st
target_response:
Hi Michael — this does **not** sound dumb. What you’re describing is a very common anxiety loop, especially when you’re trying hard to follow rules and then something happens that feels outside your control.

Based on what you described — a **brief 1–2 second pass** near someone who was **lightly coughing**, possibly around **3 feet away**, while you were leaving the store — this would generally be considered a **low-risk exposure**, not the kind of “close contact” that typically raises major concern. COVID risk is highest with **longer indoor exposure**, especially being near someone for several minutes, poor ventilation, close conversation, or repeated contact. A quick pass-by can’t be called zero risk, but it is **very unlikely** to cause infection.

Showering, changing clothes, wiping the counter, and washing your hands were more than enough. COVID primarily spreads through the air we breathe, not usually from clothing or grocery bags. You do **not** need to isolate from your housemate or wear a mask indoors solely because of this event if you feel well. If it would reassure you and your housemate, you could keep a little extra distance today, but medically this doesn’t sound necessary.

What I’d recommend:

- Stop repeatedly checking your throat/breathing if you can — that often increases anxiety and makes normal sensations feel abnormal.  
- Monitor for symptoms over the next few days: fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, body aches, loss of taste/smell, fatigue.  
- If symptoms develop, take a COVID test and wear a mask around your housemate until you know more.  
- If you want extra reassurance despite no symptoms, testing around **day 3–5** after the encounter is reasonable, but not required for such a low-risk situation.

Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, bluish lips/face, fainting, or persistent high fever**.

For future grocery trips: go at less busy times, wear a well-fitting mask if it helps you feel safer, keep moving rather than trying to “perfectly” maintain 6 feet, and wash hands afterward. You did the right things. This sounds much more like your anxiety sounding the alarm than a meaningful COVID exposure.

query: what symptoms would make you think i do not have covid-19. i know that sounds backwards but im trying to figure out what would point more to just a regular cold or stomach bug or allergies or whatever and not covid. my name is maria lopez and i dont wanna be dramatic but im kind of spiraling bc i have a bad cold, like runny nose, stuffy head, sneezing and just feel gross, but got diarrhea twice, so does diarrhea mean it is not covid? i keep reading different stuff and one page says stomach stuff can happen and another makes it sound like if its diarrhea then maybe its something i ate, so now im confused. i called dr. nikhil patel office earlier but they were busy and said a nurse might call back, and i feel dumb calling again just to ask the same question. i dont have a high fever that i can tell, just feel hot then normal, and the cold part is honestly the main thing, but the diarrhea twice made me wonder if that rules covid out or if it makes it more likely. i also dont wanna go sit anywhere and expose ppl if it could be covid, but also dont wanna ignore it if there are certain signs i should watch for. i was gonna pick up cold meds at cvs on broad st but then thought maybe i should ask first if i should be testing or just staying home and drinking fluids. like if someone has a bad cold and only two diarrhea episodes, is that still a covid thing? what symptoms would make you say no that doesnt sound like covid-19, or is there really no way to tell without a test? sorry im rambling, im just anxious and keep checking my symptoms every 10 minutes.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re not being dramatic, and this is a very reasonable question. The frustrating truth is that **COVID-19 can look exactly like a “bad cold,” allergies, flu, or a stomach bug**, especially early on. So there really isn’t a symptom pattern that reliably says “this is definitely not COVID” without testing.

**Diarrhea does *not* rule out COVID.** Some people with COVID get nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Having diarrhea twice also does *not* make COVID more likely by itself — it could be from a virus, something you ate, stress/anxiety, or drainage/swallowed mucus irritating your stomach. In your case, the main symptoms — **runny nose, stuffy head, sneezing, feeling gross, maybe low-grade temperature changes** — could fit a common cold, allergies, or COVID.

Clues that lean more toward **allergies** would be: itchy/watery eyes, lots of sneezing, clear runny nose, no fever, and symptoms that come and go with exposure or improve with antihistamines. A **stomach bug/food-related issue** is more likely if diarrhea becomes the main symptom, especially with cramping, vomiting, or others who ate the same food getting sick. A **regular cold** often causes congestion, sore throat, sneezing, and mild fatigue — but again, COVID can do the same.

What I’d recommend: **take a home COVID test today if you have one or can get one**, especially before being around others. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**, because early tests can miss it. Until you know, it’s best to **stay home as much as possible**, hydrate, rest, and use supportive care. If you need CVS on Broad St, consider delivery/curbside if available; otherwise wear a well-fitting mask, go quickly, and avoid close contact.

Call Dr. Patel’s office again if you’re worried — that’s what they’re there for. Seek urgent care now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe dehydration, persistent high fever, blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, or diarrhea that is frequent/worsening**.

For now: fluids, bland foods, saline spray, acetaminophen/ibuprofen if safe for you, and testing is the clearest next step.

query:
hi, this is kevin mccarthy, dob 03/14/1979, and i’m prob overthinking this but i wanted to ask bc the news is making me crazy and i can’t get a straight answer from anyone. i take irbesartan (angiotensin receptor blocker) for uncomplicated hypertension. it increases cell expression of angiotensin receptors (ar’s). covid enters cells via ar’s. should i change my blood pressure meds? thanks. my chart has me as MRN HUP-774219 if that matters, and dr melissa tran is the one who originally put me on it after a couple higher bp readings, nothing fancy or complicated, just the same pill every day and it’s been working ok. i pick it up at cvs on 19th and chestnut and they said don’t stop anything unless a dr says so, which i get, but then i read like 4 articles and now i’m stuck thinking maybe the med is somehow making more “doors” for covid to get in or whatever, and that sounds bad even tho i know the internet is full of junk.

not asking for a whole med change over a msg, i just want to know if people on irbesartan are supposed to switch to something else right now or if stopping/changing it would be worse bc my bp could go up. i don’t have covid symptoms right now, no fever or cough or anything, this is more like a prevention worry bc i have family around and i’m trying not to be dumb. if there’s a safer bp med for this situation i can call in, but if the answer is stay on it and ignore the receptor stuff, please just say that bc i’m spiraling a bit.
target_response:
Hi Kevin — you’re not overthinking; this was a very common and understandable concern early in COVID, and the “receptor” language online can be confusing.

The short answer: **stay on your irbesartan unless Dr. Tran or another clinician specifically tells you to stop or change it.** Current evidence and major cardiology/hypertension groups recommend that people taking ARBs like irbesartan **continue them**. Stopping suddenly can lead to higher blood pressure and creates a real, known risk, while the COVID concern has not been shown to translate into worse outcomes.

A key clarification: COVID enters cells mainly through **ACE2**, not the usual angiotensin II receptors that ARBs block. Irbesartan blocks the **AT1 receptor**. There were theoretical concerns that ACE inhibitors/ARBs might affect ACE2 levels, but human studies have not shown that people on these medications are more likely to get COVID or have worse COVID because of them. In some studies, continuing these medicines has been neutral or possibly beneficial in certain patients — but the practical recommendation is simply: **don’t switch just for COVID prevention.**

Since your blood pressure has been controlled and this is uncomplicated hypertension, changing to another medication “just in case” would likely add risk without a proven benefit. CVS gave you good advice: don’t stop it without a clinician’s direction.

What to do now:
- **Keep taking irbesartan as prescribed.**
- Continue standard prevention: vaccination/boosters if due, good ventilation, staying home when sick, testing if symptoms/exposure, and masking in high-risk settings if concerned.
- If you do develop COVID, you should generally still continue it, **unless** you are significantly dehydrated, unable to keep fluids down, fainting/lightheaded, or told you have kidney problems or very low blood pressure.

Seek urgent care if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, oxygen levels persistently <92% if you monitor, or a very high BP with symptoms** such as severe headache, neurologic symptoms, or chest pain.

Bottom line: **no need to switch off irbesartan for COVID prevention. Staying on it is the safer, evidence-based choice.**

query: if you feel not quite well 3 or 4 evenings in a row,no fever,minor dry cough,little stuffy,maybe scratchy throat very minor,just crummy feeling in general and evenings only (feel fine during the day) could that be a mild case of coronavirus? thanks. i know this prob sounds dumb but im linda maroney and i keep going back and forth on whether to call somebody or just stop worrying. during the day i swear i feel basically normal, like i can make breakfast, answer emails, fold laundry, whatever, and then around dinner time i just start feeling blah and kinda off, not enough to say im really sick but enough that i notice it and then i get in my head. i checked temp a few times and no fever, just that minor dry cough here and there, little stuffy, maybe scratchy throat very minor like i said, and then by bedtime im convinced im getting something and by morning im fine again. i left a msg for dr elena rios thru the portal at main line family med but idk when they answer and i dont want to clog up the phone if its nothing. my cell is 215-555-7390 but honestly im trying not to be dramatic, just trying to know if this is how mild coronavirus can show up. im also worried bc i dont want to go get tested if theyre scarce and i dont actually need it, but also dont want to be that person who ignores it and then exposes ppl. i havent been running around or anything, just normal home stuff, but every little throat tickle makes me spiral. should i isolate just because evenings feel crummy even with no fever? or watch it a few more days?
target_response:
Hi Linda — this does **not** sound dumb at all. What you’re describing *could* be a very mild COVID infection, because COVID can present with just a dry cough, scratchy throat, stuffiness, fatigue/“blah” feeling, and no fever. That said, it could also be a regular cold, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry indoor air, reflux, or even the normal end-of-day dip when you’re tired and then start monitoring every sensation.

The evening-only pattern is a little reassuring, especially since you feel basically normal during the day and can do your usual activities. But because COVID can be mild, it’s reasonable to take a cautious middle path without panicking.

Here’s what I’d suggest:

1. **Take a home COVID test now** if you have one or can get one.  
   - If it’s negative but symptoms continue, **repeat in 24–48 hours**, since early tests can miss it.

2. **While you have symptoms, limit close contact with others**, especially anyone older, immunocompromised, pregnant, or with chronic medical issues. If you need to be around people, wear a good mask.

3. **You don’t need to fully “isolate like you’re very sick” unless you test positive or symptoms worsen**, but I would avoid social visits, crowded indoor places, and close unmasked contact until you’re clearly improving.

4. For symptoms: fluids, rest, saline spray/rinse, honey or lozenges for throat/cough, and consider an antihistamine if allergies/post-nasal drip seem likely.

It’s also perfectly appropriate that you messaged Dr. Rios. You’re not clogging anything — this is exactly what portals are for. If you’re over 65, immunocompromised, pregnant, or have significant heart/lung disease, diabetes, or kidney disease, I’d call rather than wait, because treatment decisions for COVID/flu are time-sensitive.

Seek urgent care now if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain/pressure, confusion, blue lips, severe weakness, dehydration, oxygen levels below 92% if you check them, or a persistent/high fever**.

Bottom line: yes, mild COVID is possible, but this also sounds very consistent with a minor respiratory irritation or cold. Test, be cautious around others, and monitor rather than spiral.

query: good afternoon, my mom linda marie fletcher is 68 and went to the hospital and i am just sitting here sick to my stomach. she is at st marys medical center and the nurse said her left long is completely covered with namonia spelling sorry and is going into the right. she is full code and so off the oxygen is 23 pulse is 18 so they put her on a vintalator. i know im probly not saying all this right but im trying to write down what they told me on the phone and i cant stop crying. dr. anil patel came in and talked real fast and said they are doing what they can but i dont know if that means there is hope or if they just dont want to say it plain. she has had a stroke, heart atack in the previous she has smoked for the longest i can remember, like since i was a kid, and i know that does not help her lungs at all. she always said she would quit and then wouldnt, and now i keep thinking did we do enough, did i fuss at her enough, should i have made her go in sooner. my sister karen fletcher is the poa and is keeping her on it, and i dont want to fight with her but i also dont want mom suffering just because we cant let go. she is full code so they said they had to do everything, but is it fare to her or what??? what are her chance of pulling out of this? can someone be on a vintalator with namonia that bad and still come back, even with old stroke and heart atack and smoking all them years? please let me know. worried daughter
target_response:
Dear worried daughter,

I’m so sorry you’re going through this. Hearing that your mom is on a ventilator is terrifying, and it’s very understandable that you feel sick and overwhelmed. Please don’t blame yourself. Smoking, prior stroke, prior heart attack, and severe pneumonia are major medical issues, and family members usually cannot “make” someone quit or know exactly when an infection is becoming life-threatening.

What you’re describing sounds like **very severe pneumonia**, possibly involving most of the left lung and spreading to the right, causing **respiratory failure**—meaning her lungs could not get enough oxygen into her body, so they placed her on a ventilator to support her breathing while antibiotics and other treatments work. Yes, people can be on a ventilator for severe pneumonia and still recover, even older patients. But at 68 with a history of stroke, heart attack, and long-term smoking, her risk is higher, and recovery may be harder and slower. No one can honestly give exact “chances” without knowing her oxygen levels, blood pressure, kidney function, infection severity, chest imaging, and how she responds over the next 24–72 hours.

“Doing everything we can” does **not automatically mean there is no hope**. It usually means she is critically ill and they are using intensive treatments—ventilator, antibiotics, fluids/medications, cultures, possibly sedation, and close monitoring.

A few practical steps right now:

1. **Ask for a family meeting** with Dr. Patel or the ICU doctor, nurse, and your sister Karen as POA.  
2. Ask plainly: “Is she improving, worsening, or stable?” “What are the biggest threats right now?” “What would recovery look like?”  
3. Ask whether she is comfortable/sedated and whether she appears to be suffering.  
4. Talk with Karen about what your mom would have wanted—not what anyone else wants. Full code means CPR/shocks if her heart stops, but families can revisit goals of care as the situation changes.

If you get a call that her blood pressure is dropping, oxygen levels are worsening despite the ventilator, kidneys are failing, or they mention “septic shock,” those are signs she is very critically ill and you should go in or request an urgent update.

There is still room for hope, but also reason to prepare. Please ask for clear answers—you deserve them.

query:
i’m so scared of the coronavirus with my diabetes, that i’m wondering if my wife should not work in her gp practice? it’s putting us both at major risk isn’t it? sorry i know i sound panicky but i can’t switch my brain off at all. my name is mark ellison and my wife is sarah ellison, and she keeps saying she has to go in because they are short staffed at st anne’s gp practice and people need seeing, but all i can think is she’s around coughing people all day, touching doors, notes, phones, all of it, then coming home to me. i’ve had diabetes for years and even though i try to keep it controlled i know i’m not perfect with it, and every news thing says diabetics are worse off if they get this virus so i’m sat here thinking are we being stupid letting her keep going in.

for ref my dob 03/18/1979, if that matters for risk stuff. i tried ringing the surgery number 0161 555 0418 but obv she works there so i felt daft asking them and i don’t want to make drama for her at work. i also put my email as mark.ellison79@mail.com on the online form but i’m not sure anyone checks it quick. she says she washes hands, changes top when she gets in, leaves shoes by the door, but is that enough?? should she sleep in the spare room, should i stay away from her, should she ask to do phone triage only, or should she just not be working there at all for now? i’m scared i’ll get it from her before i even know she’s been exposed, and then what. i know everyone is scared but with my diabetes it feels like a massive risk for both of us and i don’t know if i’m overreacting or being sensible.
target_response:
Hi Mark — I’m really sorry you’re feeling this frightened. You’re not “being daft”; having diabetes does make COVID feel more threatening, especially when Sarah works in a GP setting. It’s understandable your brain is going to worst-case scenarios.

Diabetes can increase the risk of becoming more unwell with COVID, particularly if glucose control is poor or there are complications, but it does **not** mean infection is inevitable or that severe illness is certain. The key is reducing risk in sensible layers rather than trying to remove every risk completely.

Sarah should **not have to choose between unsafe work and not working**. Her practice should be doing proper risk assessments, triaging respiratory symptoms, using appropriate PPE, ventilation, cleaning, and vaccination/booster policies. It would be reasonable for her to ask her practice manager/GP lead or occupational health whether she can do more **telephone triage/admin** if available, especially because she lives with someone at increased risk. That’s a practical request, not “drama.”

At home, what she’s doing is helpful. I’d add/confirm:

- Wash hands as soon as she arrives home  
- Leave work shoes/bag away from living areas  
- Change clothes promptly; shower if she’s had close contact with symptomatic patients  
- Wipe phone/ID badge/keys regularly  
- Keep rooms ventilated when possible  
- Don’t share towels; clean high-touch surfaces  
- If she develops symptoms or has a known significant exposure, she should test and you should avoid close contact, consider masks indoors, and sleep separately if practical

Sleeping separately every night when she is well is usually not necessary, but it’s reasonable temporarily if your anxiety is overwhelming or if there has been a specific exposure.

For you: make sure your diabetes plan is tight—check glucose more regularly if unwell, keep hydrated, have sick-day rules/ketone advice if relevant, and ensure vaccines are up to date. If you get COVID symptoms, contact your GP/NHS 111 promptly because some people at higher risk may be eligible for early treatment depending on current criteria.

Seek urgent help if you develop severe breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, blue lips, dehydration, persistent high sugars, vomiting, or ketones.

And Mark, the panic itself deserves support too—please speak to your GP about the anxiety rather than carrying it alone.

query: hi, this is maria kline, dob 04/18/1987, and sorry if this is a dumb question but im getting myself all worked up reading stuff online and i cant really tell what actually applies to me. is mild intermittent asthma an elevated risk group for covid 19 complications? i keep seeing asthma listed some places but then other places make it sound like only bad asthma or uncontrolled asthma and mine is usually pretty mild, like i dont use anything every day unless im having a rough patch, mostly just when it's occasionally necessary. if so, should i continue to use my inhalers when it's occasionally necessary or will the steroids in them put me at more risk? thank you. i asked Dr. Nisha Patel once before at Penn Family Care about inhaled steroids and she made it sound like it stays more in the lungs but now with covid 19 im second guessing everything bc ppl keep saying steroids lower your immune system and im like ok but also not breathing well seems bad too?? i have an albuterol rescue one and a steroid inhaler that i honestly only use when im told or when things flare, and i dont want to do the wrong thing either way. i was gonna call the office but the phone tree is a mess and i dont wanna clog it up if this is basic, plus im trying not to go in anywhere unless i have to. my inhalers are usually filled at CVS on aramingo ave and i can see the labels but im still confused on what is safe. also should i be acting like im high risk for work/grocery stuff or just normal careful like everyone else? im not having some big asthma attack right now, just nervous and trying to plan, especially bc if i get a cough i wont know if its asthma, allergies, or covid.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. The online information about asthma and COVID risk has been confusing, and your instinct is right: **not breathing well is also a risk**, so avoiding needed asthma medicine can backfire.

For someone with **mild intermittent asthma that is usually well controlled**, the risk of severe COVID complications is generally **not thought to be as high** as it is for people with **moderate-to-severe asthma, frequent exacerbations, recent ER visits/hospitalizations, or regular oral steroid use**. That said, any respiratory infection can trigger asthma symptoms, so it’s still worth being careful.

**Please continue to use your inhalers as prescribed.**  
- **Albuterol rescue inhaler**: safe to use when you have wheeze, chest tightness, shortness of breath, or asthma-type cough.  
- **Inhaled steroid inhaler**: also safe when it’s part of your asthma plan or during flares as directed. Dr. Patel’s prior explanation was right: inhaled steroids mostly act locally in the airways, with much less whole-body immune suppression than oral steroids like prednisone. Stopping them when you need them can increase inflammation and make an asthma flare more likely.

For work/groceries: I’d suggest being **careful, but not panicked**. Use the same strong precautions everyone should: stay up to date on COVID/flu vaccines, mask in crowded indoor settings or when local rates are high, avoid close contact with sick people, wash hands, and keep inhalers available. If your job involves high exposure risk, it’s reasonable to discuss accommodations if your asthma becomes more active.

If you develop cough, congestion, or chest symptoms, it may be asthma, allergies, or COVID—often hard to tell. A **COVID test** is appropriate with new cough, fever, sore throat, body aches, loss of taste/smell, or known exposure. Use your asthma action plan if you have one.

Seek urgent care/ER if you have **severe shortness of breath, trouble speaking full sentences, bluish lips, chest pain, confusion, oxygen saturation persistently <92% if you monitor, or albuterol not helping or needed repeatedly more often than every 4 hours**.

Also, check that your CVS inhalers aren’t expired and that you have enough supply.

query: hi sorry im prob overthinking but my 21 month old has a fever 38.7 and dry cough? his name is milo bennett, dob 09/12/2024, and hes usually running around like crazy so seeing him just sit on me and whine is making me nervous. i took the temp like 3 times bc i didnt believe it, same ear thermometer we always use, and it keeps being around 38.7. the cough is dry, like little short coughs, not the gross chesty kind, but still its enough that i keep staring at him every time he does it. i called the nurse line earlier and left my number 267-555-0198 but nobody called back yet and now its getting late and im stuck doing the whole should i wait or should i go in thing. his normal ped is dr lena morris but their office said no open appts till tomorrow afternoon unless it gets worse, and i hate not knowing what “worse” means when hes this small. i dont want to drag him to urgent care if its just a virus but also dont want to be the parent who waits too long. i have childrens tylenol here from walgreens on 5th ave but im not sure if i should give it now or wait, and the bottle dosing chart always stresses me out with toddlers. hes drinking a bit but not as much as normal, wants to be held, then cries when i put him down. no clue if this is just daycare germs again or something i need checked tonight. fever 38.7 and dry cough in a 21 month old, is that an er thing or call ped in morning thing?
target_response:
I understand why you’re worried — Milo feeling clingy/whiny and not acting like his usual busy self can be really unsettling. A temperature of **38.7°C (about 101.7°F)** with a **dry cough** in a 21-month-old is most often a viral illness, especially with daycare exposure. By itself, that fever is **not automatically an ER situation** if his breathing is comfortable and he’s still drinking some.

**What I’d do tonight:**  
- If he seems uncomfortable, it is reasonable to give **children’s Tylenol/acetaminophen now** rather than waiting. Dose is based on weight: **10–15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours as needed**, max **5 doses in 24 hours**. Most children’s liquid is **160 mg per 5 mL**, but check your bottle. If you tell me his weight and the concentration, I can help calculate the dose.  
- Encourage small frequent sips of fluids; popsicles or diluted juice can help.  
- Use a cool-mist humidifier or sit in a steamy bathroom briefly if the cough is irritating. Since he’s over 1 year, a small amount of honey can help cough. Avoid OTC cough/cold meds.

**Go to urgent care/ER tonight if any of these happen:**  
- Fast or labored breathing, ribs pulling in, grunting, wheezing/stridor, blue lips, or pauses in breathing  
- He is very hard to wake, limp, inconsolable, or “not with it” even after fever medicine  
- Signs of dehydration: no wet diaper/urination for **8+ hours**, very dry mouth, no tears, persistent vomiting  
- Fever **≥40°C/104°F**, seizure, stiff neck, purple/non-blanching rash  
- You feel something is seriously wrong or his condition is rapidly worsening

If none of those are present, it’s reasonable to **treat the fever, watch him closely overnight, and call Dr. Morris’s office in the morning**. He should also be seen if fever lasts more than 3 days, cough worsens, breathing changes, or he’s drinking poorly tomorrow. Trust your instincts — but from what you’ve described, this sounds like something that can likely be monitored at home tonight unless red flags appear.

query: are children with a respiratory pathology at greater risk for covid-19 (since there are so little cases of children getting the virus, but still their natural history could be an issue)? i keep seeing ppl say kids are fine or barely get it but then i also keep thinking that maybe the kids they are talking about are the totally healthy ones, not kids like my son miles bennett who already has breathing stuff and coughs so easy with colds. his dob is 07/14/2014 if that matters for age group, and he has had that wheezy/reactive airway thing since he was little, not like he is sick right this second but every winter we end up watching him close and using the inhaler plan. i asked dr. lena ortiz about it before but now everything is changing so fast and i dont want to be the annoying mom calling every day. im just confused bc the numbers in children seem low but also maybe they arent testing kids much?? and if their natural history could be different or delayed then how would i even know early enough. we have blue cross ppo #BXC-4482019 but im trying not to drag him into a waiting room unless its really needed bc that seems like the worst place to take a kid with breathing problems right now. should i be keeping him home from playdates and school stuff even if nobody around us is confirmed? and does a respiratory pathology mean greater risk for catching it, or greater risk once they have it, or both? sorry this is rambling, im just trying to understand what the real risk is for kids like him and not panic over every cough.
target_response:
I understand why you’re worried — you’re not being “annoying.” For a child like Miles, who has a history of wheezing/reactive airway symptoms with colds, it’s very reasonable to ask how COVID risk applies to *him*, not just to healthy children in general.

In general, children have had lower rates of severe COVID than adults, but the numbers can be misleading because children may have mild/asymptomatic infection and may be tested less often. For kids with asthma/reactive airway disease, the main concern is usually **not that they are more likely to catch COVID**, but that **any respiratory virus — including COVID — could trigger wheezing or breathing difficulty once they’re infected**. The risk is higher if asthma is moderate/severe, poorly controlled, or if there have been prior hospitalizations/ICU stays. If Miles usually does well between colds and follows an inhaler plan, that is reassuring, but he still deserves extra caution.

Practical steps:
- Keep his asthma/reactive airway action plan current; it’s appropriate to message/call Dr. Ortiz for an updated plan.
- Make sure you have unexpired rescue inhaler/spacer and any controller meds if prescribed.
- Avoid unnecessary waiting rooms; ask about telehealth or calling from the car before entering.
- If COVID is circulating locally, limit higher-risk exposures like indoor playdates, crowded events, and close contact with sick people. Outdoor, well-ventilated, small-group activities are lower risk. Follow school/public health guidance, but keep him home and test if he develops symptoms.
- Keep vaccines up to date, including flu and COVID if eligible/recommended for him.

For early warning, focus less on “any cough” and more on breathing: faster breathing, chest pulling/retractions, wheezing not improving with inhaler, trouble speaking/playing, bluish lips, unusual sleepiness/confusion, dehydration, persistent high fever, or oxygen saturation <92–94% if you have a pulse oximeter. Those warrant urgent care/emergency evaluation. Also seek care if symptoms worsen after initially improving.

Bottom line: Miles is not necessarily more likely to *catch* COVID because of reactive airways, but he may be more vulnerable to a flare if he gets it. A cautious, prepared approach — without panic — is exactly right.

query: hi my name is.brent malloy dob 07/18/1989 and i was diagnosed with pneumonia friday at MercyOne urgent care by dr. nina patel, since then i haven t been able to sleep at all due to vomiting and i m dizzy and the weirdest thang is if i touch a certain part of my neck or back i instantly feel sick like it hits me right away and i have to sit down or i feel like im gonna puke again. i know that sounds weird but its freaking me out bc i thought pneumonia was mostly chest cough stuff and now my stomach and head are all messed up too. they gave me meds and i picked them up at CVS on 42nd st but honestly i dont know if its the pills making me vomit or the pneumonia or maybe i got dehydrated from not keeping anything down. i tried crackers, water, gatorade, laying on my side, sitting up with pillows, nothing really makes sleep happen. i keep waking up hot and gross and then dizzy when i stand up. i dont wanna be dramatic or waste anybodys time but i also dont wanna ignore something if its bad, cause the neck/back touch thing is so strange. my callback is 515-555-0176 if someone needs to reach me but if you can give me advice that would be great, like do i wait it out, call the urgent care back, stop meds, or go er if the vomiting keeps up. i havent really slept since friday and im starting to feel kinda shaky and scared honestly.
target_response:
Hi Brent — I’m sorry you’re going through this. You’re not being dramatic. Pneumonia can cause fever, sweats, weakness, dizziness, poor sleep, and sometimes nausea/vomiting, but vomiting enough that you can’t sleep or keep fluids/meds down is a reason to be rechecked.

A few possibilities: the infection itself can make you feel very ill; some pneumonia antibiotics can upset the stomach; and dehydration from repeated vomiting can cause dizziness when standing, shakiness, headache, and feeling “hot/gross.” The neck/back touch causing instant nausea is unusual. It could be a tender muscle/nerve trigger from coughing or tension, but if it is associated with a stiff neck, severe headache, fever, confusion, rash, or sensitivity to light, that needs urgent evaluation.

Given you’ve had ongoing vomiting, dizziness on standing, and minimal sleep since Friday, I’d recommend you **call MercyOne urgent care/prescribing clinician now for same-day advice**—and if you can’t keep fluids down or you feel like you may pass out, **go to the ER today**. You may need an exam, oxygen check, labs, IV fluids, nausea medicine, and possibly a change in antibiotic. Bring the medication bottles from CVS so they can see exactly what you’re taking. **Don’t stop the antibiotic on your own unless you’re having signs of allergy** like facial/lip swelling, hives, wheezing, or trouble breathing—but if you’re vomiting doses back up, that’s another reason to be seen.

While arranging care: take tiny sips every 1–2 minutes of water or oral rehydration solution/Pedialyte/Gatorade diluted half-and-half. Try bland foods only if fluids stay down. Sit up after meds; if one is doxycycline, it can cause nausea/esophagus irritation if taken lying down.

Go to the ER/call 911 now for trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen <92% if you have a pulse ox, severe headache/stiff neck, vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, no urination for 8–10 hours, or persistent high fever/worsening symptoms.

query:
hi, i'm maya benton and i'm currently have a semi emerging wisdom tooth that is sharp and growing against my inner cheek causing slight discomfort. its like not horrible pain where im crying or anything but its sooo annoying bc every time i talk or chew it kinda scrapes the inside of my cheek and now i keep thinking about it nonstop. i was supposed to talk to dr. kevin malone about getting the wisdom tooth removed but now bc of the covid lockdown i can't have the surgery to remove the tooth now and idk how long this is all gonna last. i keep trying not to mess with it with my tongue but then i do it anyway and it makes it feel more sore. there is no big swelling that i can see and i dont think i have fever or anything, just the sharp tooth edge / gum area and cheek irritation, and im worried its gonna cut me more or get infected if it keeps rubbing there.

what can i do to ease the discomfort at home since i can't have the surgery to remove the tooth now due to covid lockdown? like is warm salt water enough, or should i use wax like for braces, or some kind of numbing gel? i have ibuprofen here but i dont wanna overdo it and i dont know if putting anything over the tooth is a bad idea. i usually use walgreens on river ave if i need to grab something but im trying not to go out unless i really need to. also im eating soft stuff but even then it catches my cheek. sorry this is long im just stuck at home and kinda anxious bc dental stuff freaks me out and i dont want it to turn into an emergency when everything is closed.
target_response:
Hi Maya — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this. A partially emerging wisdom tooth can be extremely irritating when a sharp edge rubs the inner cheek, and it’s understandable that it’s making you anxious, especially with the lockdown delaying removal. The good news is that what you describe — mild discomfort, no visible swelling, no fever — sounds more like mechanical irritation than an emergency infection right now.

Here are safe things you can do at home:

1. **Warm salt-water rinses**  
   Rinse gently 3–5 times daily, especially after eating: ½ teaspoon salt in a cup of warm water. This helps keep the area clean and may reduce soreness. Don’t swish aggressively.

2. **Dental/orthodontic wax**  
   Yes, you can use wax like the kind used for braces. Dry the tooth area as best you can with clean gauze or tissue, then press a small piece of wax over the sharp edge. Replace it when it falls off, and remove it before eating if it’s loose. This is a reasonable temporary barrier.

3. **Pain relief**  
   If you can take ibuprofen safely, you can use it with food. A common adult dose is **400 mg every 6–8 hours as needed**, staying under the maximum listed on the bottle. Avoid ibuprofen if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding, are on blood thinners, or have been told not to take NSAIDs. Acetaminophen is another option if needed, following package limits.

4. **Numbing gel**  
   An over-the-counter oral gel can help briefly, but use it sparingly and exactly as directed. Avoid overusing it, and don’t eat while your cheek is numb since you could bite it without noticing.

Also keep brushing/flossing gently around the area, avoid poking it with your tongue if you can, and stick with soft foods on the opposite side.

Please call Dr. Kevin Malone’s office, even during lockdown — many dental/oral surgery offices are triaging by phone and can advise if you qualify for urgent care.

Seek urgent dental/medical help if you develop facial/jaw swelling, fever, worsening severe pain, pus/bad taste, trouble opening your mouth, swallowing, or breathing.
