query: on and off fever, sore body? hi this is maria lopez and im prob overthinking but its been bugging me bc it keeps coming and going, like ill feel kinda ok and then later im hot again and achy all over and i cant tell if its just one of those random bugs or if i should actually be seen. my dob is 08/14/1989 if that matters for the chart or whatever. i dont wanna make a whole big thing if its nothing, but also i keep getting nervous bc i have stuff i need to do and i cant really just lay around and guess. i was gonna call dr. nina patel but the office line had me on hold and i got annoyed and hung up, so now im just asking here first. not trying to be dramatic, i just hate when fever is on and off bc it makes me think its better then it pops back up and im like ok what now. body feels sore too, like that heavy gross sore feeling, and i keep checking myself and second guessing every little thing. i can go to riverbend family clinic if i have to, but i dont know if this is urgent or if i should wait it out, drink fluids, rest, all that normal stuff. also i have to figure out a ride if i go in so im trying not to waste a trip. should i be worried with on and off fever and sore body or is this more like monitor at home unless it gets worse? sorry this is rambly i just want to know what the smart next step is before i start calling around again.
target_response:
Hi Maria — you’re not being dramatic. Fever that comes and goes can feel unsettling, especially when you briefly feel better and then the achiness and heat return.

The most common cause of **on-and-off fever with whole-body soreness** is a viral illness, such as a cold virus, flu, or COVID. With these, fever can fluctuate during the day and often rises again in the evening. Body aches are also very typical. If you’re otherwise breathing comfortably, able to drink fluids, and not having severe or unusual symptoms, it’s reasonable to **monitor at home for a short period**.

Smart next steps:

1. **Actually check your temperature** with a thermometer if you can. “Feeling hot” can be misleading, but a measured temp helps guide decisions.
2. **Hydrate and rest** as much as possible. Aim for frequent fluids; urine should not be very dark.
3. Consider a **COVID test**, and flu testing may be useful if symptoms started within the last 48 hours, since treatment can help some people.
4. For fever/aches:  
   - **Acetaminophen/Tylenol** as directed on the bottle, avoid exceeding daily limits.  
   - **Ibuprofen/Advil** can also help if you can take it safely — avoid if you have stomach ulcers/bleeding risk, significant kidney disease, are on blood thinners, or are pregnant unless a clinician says it’s okay.

I’d recommend calling **Dr. Nina Patel’s office or Riverbend Family Clinic** for advice/appointment if: the fever has lasted **more than 3 days**, is **103°F or higher**, keeps returning after seeming gone for a day, or you develop symptoms like worsening cough, ear/sinus pain, painful urination, significant sore throat with swollen glands, rash, or abdominal pain.

Please seek **urgent care/ER now** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, fainting, severe headache with stiff neck, blue lips, dehydration/not keeping fluids down, severe weakness, or a fever over **104°F**.

If you tell me how many days this has been happening, your highest measured temperature, and any other symptoms — cough, sore throat, nausea, urinary symptoms, rash — I can help you decide how soon you should be seen.

query: hi this is jenna cole, dob 08/14/1991, i have a sore throat and i’m tired. i haven’t slept well in the last two days. just haven’t been able to rest tossing and turning thinking - stress. like i lay down and then my brain starts going and i keep checking if my throat feels worse and then i get more nervous. should i assume sore throats is associated with the coronavirus . sore throat started today. it wasnt there yesterday really, maybe a little dry but today its like sore when i swallow and im tired from not sleeping so i cant tell if its sick tired or just no sleep tired. i dont have a good thermometer near me right now so i havent really checked temp, just keep touching my forehead like that helps lol. i tried tea and water and it helps for a few mins then i start thinking about it again. i was gonna maybe call upmc urgent care on centre ave if it keeps going but i dont wanna be dramatic or go sit somewhere if its just stress and bad sleep. my cell is 412-555-0198 if someone needs to call me back, but honestly i just need to know if a sore throat starting today by itself is enough to assume covid/coronavirus or if i should wait and see. i also have been working from home so i’m not sure where id even get anything, but then again groceries and elevators and all that makes me anxious. i’m trying not to google but of course i did and now every site says something diff. do i isolate right away from my roommate or just watch it tonight and try to sleep?
pii_units: upmc urgent care on centre ave
target_response:
Hi Jenna — I’m sorry you’re feeling caught in that anxious loop. A sore throat that started today **by itself** is *not enough to assume COVID*, but COVID can sometimes start with just a sore throat, so it’s reasonable to take simple precautions while you see how it evolves.

**What to do tonight/next 24 hours:**
- If you have access to a rapid COVID test, take one now or tomorrow morning. If it’s negative but symptoms continue, repeat in **24–48 hours**.
- Until you know more, try to **mask around your roommate**, avoid sharing cups/utensils, improve ventilation, and wash hands. You don’t necessarily need full “panic isolation,” but sleeping separately/keeping some distance is sensible.
- Hydrate, warm tea/honey, throat lozenges, salt-water gargles, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely.
- If possible, get a thermometer tomorrow—touching your forehead is very unreliable.

This could be a mild viral sore throat, dryness/post-nasal drip, reflux, irritation, or early COVID/flu. The fatigue may very well be from two nights of poor sleep, though illness can also contribute.

**Consider urgent care or a call for testing/strep evaluation** if you develop: fever, worsening throat pain, swollen/tender neck glands, white patches on tonsils, no cough with significant sore throat, known strep exposure, or symptoms lasting more than 3–5 days. Strep is more likely with fever, tonsil exudates, and tender front-neck nodes.

**Go urgently / ER** if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, inability to swallow saliva, severe one-sided throat swelling, muffled “hot potato” voice, dehydration, or high/persistent fever.

For sleep tonight: try to set a “check-in limit” for symptoms—e.g., decide you’ll reassess in the morning unless a red flag appears. Put the phone away, use a calming audio/breathing exercise, and remind yourself: “I have a plan; I don’t need to solve it at 2 a.m.”

If symptoms worsen tomorrow or you test positive, contact your clinic/UPMC for next steps. Otherwise, watching it overnight with precautions is reasonable.

query: should i be tested for covid 19 have been in contact with family member claire dubois who arrived from france on 13 march on air france af178. not showing any symptoms to date been 7days since visit ? im just asking bc im getting myself worked up and dont know if im meant to do something now or just sit tight. she came over to my place at 10 waverly ave and we had tea and she sat in the living room and used the same bathroom and all that normal family stuff, not like we were trying to be careless, this was before i really understood how serious ppl were saying this is. i dont have cough, no fever, no sore throat, no chills, nothing weird really, maybe just anxious so every little thing feels like a symptom even tho it isnt. i keep checking temp and its normal. i have been staying home mostly since then and washing hands like crazy, wiping door handles, remote, counters etc but still worried bc she had just traveled and now everyone is saying travel from europe is a thing. should i be tested even if i feel fine or is that wasting a test? also do i need to tell ppl i saw after that or only if i get symptoms? i can be reached at 781-555-0942 if someone needs to call but honestly i just need to know what the right step is, dont want to panic and dont want to be stupid either. its been 7days since visit and no symptoms to date, so does that mean im ok or still wait longer?
target_response:
I understand why you’re feeling anxious — you had a close indoor visit with a family member who had just traveled from France, and it’s hard not to second-guess everything afterward. The fact that you have **no fever, cough, sore throat, chills, or other symptoms 7 days later is reassuring**, but it does **not completely rule out COVID**, because symptoms can appear anywhere from about **2–14 days after exposure**.

Because you had normal close household-type contact — tea together, shared living room/bathroom — it is reasonable to treat this as a possible exposure, especially if Claire later develops symptoms or tests positive.

**What to do now:**
1. **Continue staying home and avoiding close contact with others** through at least 10 days after the visit; many public health recommendations have used 14 days as the safest monitoring window after exposure.
2. **Monitor for symptoms**: fever, cough, sore throat, shortness of breath, loss of taste/smell, body aches, diarrhea, unusual fatigue.
3. **Testing:** Since you are 7 days out, a COVID test now would be reasonable if testing is available in your area, even without symptoms. If testing is limited, priority may be given to people with symptoms, healthcare workers, or high-risk individuals. A negative test is reassuring but not perfect, so you should still finish the monitoring period.
4. **Ask Claire to update you** if she develops any symptoms or gets tested. If she remains well, your risk is lower.

Regarding people you saw afterward: if you were not symptomatic and have not tested positive, you may not be required to notify everyone. However, it is considerate to let anyone you had close contact with know there was a possible exposure, especially if they are older, pregnant, immunocompromised, or have lung/heart disease, so they can monitor themselves.

Seek urgent care or call emergency services if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, blue lips/face, severe weakness, or persistent high fever**.

Also, try not to keep checking your temperature constantly — anxiety can make normal sensations feel alarming. You’re doing the right things: staying home, hygiene, cleaning surfaces, and asking for guidance rather than panicking.

query: my daughter emily grace martin has been sick with the flu for over a week and now has strep throat and pneumonia. she s saw her doctor dr. karen wu, ran some tests and her white blood cell count keeps dropping. they just sent her to the er at northside children's er and i am sitting here in the parking lot typing this bc i feel like im gonna lose it, sorry. her dob is 04/18/2012 and they had me repeat it like 4 times at check in along with mrn nc-773920, which just made me more nervous for some reason. she was already miserable all week with the flu, then the throat pain got worse and they said strep, then the cough and chest stuff and they said pneumonia, and now this wbc thing is dropping and i keep googling which i know is dumb but i cant stop. she had meds called in to cvs on maple ave before they sent us over but now idk if they are changing everything or admitting her or what. what would cause her wbc to drop? i know there s probably a few reasons, but given this recent background of her health?? is it from the flu itself or the pneumonia or her body just being worn down, or is that a sign of something way worse like her immune system not fighting? she has been so tired and pale and just not herself and every time the nurse says they are waiting on more labs i get more scared. i am trying not to be dramatic but this is my kid and it feels like one thing after another in just a week. thank you. very worried mom
target_response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. You are not being dramatic—seeing your child worsen after a week of flu, then strep, then pneumonia, and hearing “white count dropping” would scare any parent. You did the right thing by getting her to the children’s ER.

A falling white blood cell count can happen for several reasons, and in Emily’s situation one of the most common is the flu itself. Influenza and other viruses can temporarily suppress the bone marrow, so the body makes fewer white cells for a short time. Sometimes the count also drops because white cells are being used up fighting infection. Certain medications can rarely contribute, but the timing and the specific type of white cell affected matter. More serious causes exist, but a low WBC during a significant viral illness does **not automatically mean** her immune system is “failing” or that this is something like cancer.

What the ER team will likely focus on is the **differential**, especially the neutrophil count/ANC, not just the total WBC. They may repeat labs, check oxygen levels, hydration status, chest imaging, blood cultures, inflammatory markers, and decide whether she needs IV antibiotics, fluids, oxygen, or admission for monitoring. Flu can also make kids more vulnerable to bacterial pneumonia, so escalation of care is very appropriate.

Since she was prescribed medications before being sent over, I would not start or change anything from CVS until the ER team tells you the plan. Let them know exactly what was prescribed, what she has already taken, allergies, fever pattern, and whether she is drinking/peeing normally.

Red flags that warrant immediate attention—and you’re already in the right place—include trouble breathing, lips/face turning blue, severe sleepiness or confusion, inability to keep fluids down, no urination for 8–10 hours, stiff neck, worsening chest pain, persistent high fever, or a new purple/petechial rash.

One small practical note: for privacy, avoid sharing her DOB/MRN online in the future. For now, take a breath, go one question at a time, and ask the ER team: “What is her ANC, oxygen level, and are you worried enough to admit her?”

query:
hi, my name is maria lopez and im kinda worried bc this came on so fast and i dont usually get stuff like this. preauricular lymph node on left side very tender, like right in front of my ear and it hurts even if i just barely touch it. scalp on left side of head tender and hurts to touch too, like if i move my hair or lay on that side it just feels sore and weird. superficial parotid lymph node area on left side swollen and tender also, and now im noticing pain behind both ears. no injury at all, i didnt hit my head, no fall, no weird bite that i know of, nothing like that. it came on suddenly and has been 1 day. temp 100.1° so not super high but enough that its making me nervous.

im trying not to be dramatic but the tenderness is making me think something is infected or swollen glands or idk. i called dr. nisha patel but the office said they may not call back til later and im sitting here googling which i know is dumb but im doing it anyway. i keep checking it and then it hurts more, so im probably making myself more anxious. i can swallow and talk, its just all the left side scalp/ear area feels angry and sore, plus that behind both ears pain is freaking me out.

if this is something i should go to urgent care for today i will, i just dont want to sit there for hours if theyre only gonna say take tylenol and wait. i use CVS on maple ave if anything needs to be called in, but mostly i want to know if tender preauricular/parotid lymph node stuff with scalp pain and low temp after only 1 day is urgent or can wait.
target_response:
Hi Maria — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this; sudden tenderness in that preauricular/parotid area plus scalp soreness can feel really alarming, especially when it’s new for you.

Tender lymph nodes in front of the ear and around the parotid region often mean your immune system is reacting to something nearby: scalp irritation/infection, an ear canal or middle-ear issue, dental/jaw inflammation, eye infection, or a viral illness. The scalp tenderness you describe can also happen early with **shingles** before a rash appears, especially if it’s one-sided and very sensitive to light touch. A low-grade temp of 100.1°F supports inflammation/infection but isn’t, by itself, dangerous.

Because this came on quickly, is quite tender, and involves the ear/scalp/parotid region, I would recommend **same-day evaluation at urgent care if Dr. Patel’s office can’t see or call you back today**—not necessarily because it sounds like an emergency, but because an exam can look for an ear infection, skin/scalp infection, parotid gland issue, dental source, or early shingles. Some of these are “watch and wait,” but others may need antivirals or antibiotics, and timing matters.

While waiting:  
- Try not to keep pressing/checking the nodes—it can make them more painful and irritated.  
- Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can take them safely.  
- Warm compresses to the tender area may help.  
- Hydrate and rest.  
- Check your scalp/ear area gently for rash, blisters, redness, swelling, or drainage.

Go to the ER rather than urgent care if you develop **fever >101.5–102°F**, rapidly worsening swelling/redness, severe headache, stiff neck, confusion, vomiting, facial weakness, new hearing loss, ear sticking out/swelling behind the ear, eye pain/redness/vision changes, trouble swallowing/breathing, or a blistering rash near the eye or ear.

If it’s stable and Dr. Patel can assess you today, that’s ideal. If not, urgent care today is reasonable given the location and sudden onset. They may still recommend supportive care, but they can also catch the treatable causes early.

query: hi, i’m jake morrison, dob 03/14/1998, and i got diagnosed with pneumonia yesterday at st vincent hospital and have been sent home with antibiotics (amoxicillin and doxycycline) from CVS on grant ave. i was just wondering what the risk factors would be if i were to have at least one cigarette? like i know it wouldn’t be good for the healing process(will probably slow it down) and i’m not trying to be stupid about it, i’m just asking honestly bc i’ve been kinda pacing around and stressing and the cravings are making me annoyed on top of feeling like crap. but is there any serious risk with smoking (ciggerattes or maybe even weed mixed with tobacco?) like could it actually make the pneumonia suddenly way worse or is it more like it irritates the lungs and makes the cough drag out longer? my doctor at the hospital that diagnosed me, dr. nina patel, knew i smoked and also knew i smoked at least one or two bongs and he said nothing when i was leaving about trying not to smoke etc etc? so now i’m confused if that means it’s not a huge deal or if he just forgot to say it bc the discharge was rushed and they were busy. i’m not saying i’m gonna sit here chain smoking, i mean literally like one cigarette maybe half of one, or a tiny bit of weed mixed with tobacco just to sleep, but i also don’t wanna end up back in hospital bc i was dumb. i’ve taken the antibiotics like they told me so far and i’m trying to drink water and rest but i keep overthinking every cough. just wanna know if there’s any serious danger from one or two or if it’s mainly just a bad idea for healing.
target_response:
Hi Jake — it’s a very reasonable question, and I’m glad you’re thinking it through rather than just guessing.

With pneumonia, the safest advice is **don’t smoke anything while you’re recovering** — cigarettes, tobacco mixed with weed, bongs, vaping, etc. Smoke irritates the inflamed airways, increases mucus, slows the tiny “clean-up” hairs in the lungs, and can make coughing, wheezing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath worse. It can also make it harder for your lungs to clear the infection.

That said, to answer your question directly: **one cigarette or a small amount is unlikely to suddenly make pneumonia dramatically worse or automatically send you back to the hospital.** The bigger concern is that it can trigger a coughing fit, bronchospasm/wheezing, lower your oxygen a bit if your lungs are already struggling, and prolong recovery. Weed mixed with tobacco or using a bong is not safer — it can be harsher/deeper inhalation and can irritate the lungs significantly.

So: **serious immediate danger from one or two is probably low if you’re otherwise breathing okay**, but it’s still a bad idea while your lungs are infected. Dr. Patel may simply not have had time to emphasize it during discharge; it doesn’t mean smoking is “fine.”

For cravings, consider **nicotine replacement** instead: nicotine gum, lozenges, or a patch are much safer for your lungs right now. CVS should have them, and they don’t interfere with amoxicillin or doxycycline. If you’re trying to sleep, avoid inhaling cannabis; if you use any THC product, be cautious because it can make you feel more short of breath/anxious or overly sedated.

Keep taking the antibiotics exactly as prescribed, hydrate, rest, and expect the cough to linger for days to weeks even as the infection improves.

Please seek urgent care or go back to the ER if you develop: worsening shortness of breath, blue lips, chest pain that’s severe or worsening, confusion, fainting, coughing blood, fever that persists/worsens after 48–72 hours of antibiotics, oxygen saturation under 92% if you’re checking it, or you can’t keep fluids/meds down.

Bottom line: avoid smoking if at all possible; use nicotine replacement to get through the cravings.

query: hi i have hypothyroidism. i’m maria lynn cruz, dob 08/14/1987, and i’ve been kinda spiraling tonight reading too much stuff online so sorry if this is a dumb question or if im repeating myself. i’ve been in chronic meds for over 5 years now, same thyroid pill every morning and i try not to miss it, but sometimes my levels still get weird and then i start thinking everything is connected. my thyroid dr is dr. kelsey tran and i left a msg but they usually take forever to call back, and i dont wanna be dramatic and run in if this is just anxiety from all the coronavirus stuff. am i at risk for coronavirus just because of hypothyroidism? like does that count as immune system problem or not really? i keep seeing ppl say underlying conditions and it makes me nervous bc i never know if mine counts or if its only heart/lung/diabetes type things. also should i boost my immune system? thanks, like should i be taking vitamin c or zinc or elderberry or whatever, or is that bad with thyroid meds? i get my refill at cvs on 9th ave and the pharmacist once told me not to take calcium/iron close to the thyroid pill so now im scared to add anything without asking. i dont have fever or anything right now, just worried bc i still have to go out for groceries and stuff and i dont want to make my thyroid worse or mess up my meds. i wash hands and all that but idk if i need extra precautions compared to everyone else. sorry again just trying to understand if hypothyroid makes me high risk or if im basically same as other people as long as meds are stable.
target_response:
Hi Maria — not a dumb question at all. It’s very understandable to feel anxious when “underlying conditions” gets mentioned broadly online.

In general, **hypothyroidism by itself does *not* make you immunocompromised** and does not usually put someone at higher risk for catching coronavirus or having severe COVID, **as long as it’s treated and reasonably stable**. Even if your hypothyroidism is due to Hashimoto’s, that is an autoimmune thyroid condition, but it is **not the same as being on immune-suppressing medication** or having a weakened immune system. Higher-risk conditions usually refer more to things like significant heart or lung disease, diabetes, kidney disease, cancer treatment/transplant medications, certain immune deficiencies, severe obesity, pregnancy, and older age.

The best thing for your thyroid is to **keep taking your thyroid pill consistently**: on an empty stomach with water, ideally 30–60 minutes before food/coffee. Your CVS pharmacist was right: **calcium, iron, magnesium, multivitamins, antacids, and some fiber supplements can block absorption**, so separate those from your thyroid medication by at least **4 hours**.

For “immune boosting”: there’s no supplement proven to reliably prevent COVID. **Vitamin C or zinc are generally okay in normal doses**, but zinc is often in mineral form, so keep it 4 hours away from your thyroid pill. Avoid megadoses. **Elderberry has limited evidence** and can cause GI upset; I wouldn’t start it without a specific reason, especially if you’re prone to medication sensitivity. Better immune support: sleep, hydration, balanced meals, staying up to date on COVID/flu vaccines, and managing stress.

For groceries, you likely need the **same precautions as everyone else**, not extra because of hypothyroidism: wash hands, avoid touching your face, consider a well-fitting mask in crowded indoor spaces, use ventilation, and use curbside/delivery if you’re especially anxious.

Call Dr. Tran’s office for routine guidance/labs if you’ve had symptoms of thyroid imbalance or missed doses. Seek urgent care if you develop **trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or persistent high fever**. For now, with no fever or symptoms, this sounds more like worry than an emergency.

query: i think i have covid-19. my name is Daniel Ruiz, dob 08/14/1989, and im sorry if this is rambling but im getting pretty nervous and dont really know what counts as go in now vs wait it out. i have difficulty breathing (shortness of breath), tightness in chest, sore throat and light fever. symptoms have been progressing and getting stronger over the last 4 days, like at first i thought it was just allergies or a cold but now the chest tight thing is making me feel kinda panicky and i keep checking if i can take a deep breath. i had diarrhea two days ago. not constant now but it happened and then the throat and breathing stuff just seemed to keep building after that. i havent been sleeping great bc i wake up thinking about my breathing and then i cant tell if its anxiety or actually worse, but it feels real, like my chest is tight even sitting still. my temp is just a light fever not crazy high but its there, and the sore throat is annoying and dry. i live alone so im trying to be careful and not go anywhere if this is covid, but i also dont want to wait too long if shortness of breath means something bad. if someone calls me back my number is 267-555-0198, and if i need meds i usually use CVS on 1424 Chestnut St, but idk if i should even go inside there. i dont have a home oxygen thing or anything, just a thermometer. should i get tested, go urgent care, or er? i feel like each day is stronger than the last and im scared of it turning into something worse overnight.
target_response:
Hi Daniel — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this, and it makes sense that the chest tightness and worsening shortness of breath are making you nervous. COVID-19 is definitely possible given the sore throat, fever, diarrhea, and progressive respiratory symptoms, but similar symptoms can also come from flu/RSV, pneumonia, asthma/bronchospasm, or other causes that need an exam.

Because you’re describing **shortness of breath and chest tightness even while sitting still, worsening over 4 days**, I would not simply “wait it out.” You should be **seen in person today** so someone can check your oxygen level, listen to your lungs, and decide whether you need testing, inhaler treatment, chest X-ray, or other care. If you feel able to speak in full sentences and walk around without significant worsening, an **urgent care that evaluates respiratory/COVID symptoms** is reasonable—call ahead and wear a well-fitting mask. If symptoms feel more severe, go to the **ER now**.

Please seek emergency care / call 911 right away if you have any of these:  
- Trouble breathing at rest, gasping, or unable to speak full sentences  
- Chest pain/pressure that is severe, persistent, or radiates to arm/jaw/back  
- Blue/gray lips or face, confusion, fainting, severe weakness  
- Fever that is very high or worsening, or dehydration  
- Symptoms suddenly worsening overnight

Testing is a good idea, but **don’t let testing delay care** given your breathing symptoms. If you have a home rapid test, take it now; if negative, repeat in 24–48 hours or get PCR/clinic testing. Since you’re within about 5 days of symptom onset, if you test positive and have risk factors, a clinician can decide whether antiviral treatment such as Paxlovid is appropriate.

For now: isolate, wear a mask if you need to go out for care, drink fluids, rest upright, and you can use acetaminophen for fever/sore throat as directed on the bottle. Avoid going inside the CVS if possible—use delivery, drive-through, or ask someone to pick up medications.

Anxiety can amplify the sensation of breathlessness, but progressive chest tightness should be checked medically. Please arrange evaluation today.

query: i am 33 years old, my name is not the issue here but i was seen by dr nisha patel and my chart says mrn mh-778204 at mercy hospital, and i am just getting really worried now because this has been dragging on and nobody is giving me a clear answer. i was diagnosed with pneumonia almost a month ago after a short period of low grade fever and dry cough. even after treatment with 4 courses of antibiotics dry cough has not resolved. i kept thinking ok maybe it will slowly go away but its still there and every time i cough i get anxious and start checking my breathing and then i notice all the pains more. i am having pain on the right lower side of the rib which is constant and experiencing back pain and pains all over my back and chest in a nonspecific pattern, like not one clean spot i can point to always, sometimes right side sometimes across the back and sometimes chest tight kind of pain but not sure if muscle or lung or what. a chest ct was performed and found subsegmental consolidatory changes in the anterior and lateral basal segments of right lower lobe with bronchial wall thickening, minimal ground glass opacification in the adjacent posterior and medial basal segments, few small mediastinal and axillary lymph nodes, plus few lymph nodes seen in the pretracheal region, few of them showing dense calcification. my last lab report from december 23rd is as follows total count-7200/cumm neutrophils-70%, lymphocytes-28%, eosinophils-2% esr-28 mm/hr. still i am having fatigue, dry cough and pains. i dont know if this is still pneumonia not clearing, inflammation, something like tb or fungal thing, or something more scary, and i keep reading online which is making it worse. what can it be?
target_response:
I’m sorry this has dragged on so long — it’s very understandable that persistent cough plus CT wording would make you worry, especially after multiple antibiotics.

Based on what you shared, a few points are reassuring: your white blood cell count is normal, the differential is not worrisome, and the ESR of 28 is only mildly elevated and can stay up after infection/inflammation. The CT findings — small areas of consolidation/ground-glass change with bronchial wall thickening in the right lower lobe — can fit with **resolving pneumonia or ongoing airway inflammation**. A dry cough can persist **6–8 weeks or longer** after pneumonia/viral bronchitis because the airways remain irritated, even when the infection is no longer active.

The rib/chest/back pains may be from **muscle strain, costochondritis, pleuritic irritation, or coughing-related inflammation**, especially when the pain moves around and is not one fixed spot. Anxiety can also make you monitor breathing more closely and amplify chest tightness sensations.

The lymph nodes: “few small” mediastinal/axillary nodes are often reactive. **Dense calcification** in pretracheal nodes often reflects an old healed granulomatous infection such as prior TB exposure or certain fungal infections, depending on where you live/travel — not automatically something dangerous. That said, given the persistence, it is reasonable to ask for a clear follow-up plan.

What I’d recommend next:
1. **Follow up with your doctor or a pulmonologist** and review the CT images, not just the report.  
2. Ask whether you need **repeat chest X-ray or CT in 6–8 weeks** to confirm resolution.  
3. Consider testing if appropriate: **TB blood test/skin test**, sputum studies if you produce phlegm, and fungal testing only if risk factors/geography fit.  
4. Discuss non-antibiotic causes of cough: post-infectious airway irritation, asthma/reactive airways, reflux, or postnasal drip. A trial of an inhaler or anti-inflammatory cough regimen may help.  
5. Avoid more antibiotics unless there is clear evidence of ongoing bacterial infection.

Seek urgent care now if you develop worsening shortness of breath, oxygen levels <92%, coughing blood, high persistent fever, severe/worsening chest pain, fainting, leg swelling, or unexplained weight loss/night sweats.

query: my friend melissa grant had pneumonia a few weeks ago and was given cipro and flagl antibiotics and i am just trying to understand what is happening bc none of it makes sense to me and she is scared but also acting like she doesnt want to bother anybody. she had to return to the hospital two more time because of spiking temperatures, like she would be ok-ish for a bit and then boom temp up again and chills and all that, and it was at st. agnes hospital both times after the first visit too. i wasnt in the room for every single thing so i may be saying it messy but they said fluid was tested from her lungs and came back negative, and dr. nikhil patel told her something about waiting it out and finishing the meds but then she keeps getting these temp spikes and i keep thinking, ok if the lung fluid was negative then what are we missing?? could she possibly have contacted mersa? i know maybe im spelling it wrong, mersa or mrsa, but like the bad infection ppl get in hospitals, is that something that could happen after pneumonia or after being in and out of hospital so much? she has been so tired and worried and im worried too bc she was around my family a little before she got so sick, not a ton but enough that now im thinking about it nonstop. im not asking for a diagnosis i guess, i just dont know if this sounds like something she should push them to test for specifically, or if negative fluid means no mersa, or if the antibiotics cipro and flagl would even touch that. sorry this is long im just nervous and trying to help her ask the right question.
target_response:
I’m sorry Melissa is going through this — recurrent fever spikes after pneumonia are scary, and it’s very reasonable for you to want clearer answers. You’re not “bothering” anyone by asking questions; repeated chills/fevers after treatment deserve follow-up.

A few key points:

**1) “Negative lung fluid” is reassuring, but it doesn’t always rule everything out.**  
If they drained/tested fluid around the lung, a negative culture can mean no bacteria grew there. But cultures can be negative if antibiotics were already started, if the infection is in the lung tissue rather than the fluid, or if the fever is coming from another source entirely.

**2) MRSA is possible, but not the only concern.**  
MRSA (“methicillin-resistant Staph aureus”) can cause pneumonia, especially after influenza/viral illness, in people recently hospitalized, or in severe/necrotizing pneumonia. However, recurrent fevers after pneumonia can also come from resistant bacteria, an undrained collection/empyema, lung abscess, blood infection, medication reaction, blood clot, viral infection, or even complications like C. diff diarrhea from antibiotics.

**3) Cipro + Flagyl may not reliably cover MRSA.**  
Flagyl covers anaerobic bacteria; Cipro covers certain gram-negative bacteria and some respiratory organisms, but it is **not standard reliable MRSA pneumonia coverage**. If her doctors suspect MRSA, they may use medicines like vancomycin or linezolid, depending on the situation.

**What she should ask her team directly:**  
- “Do you think MRSA is possible in my case?”  
- “Were blood cultures, sputum cultures, or a nasal MRSA PCR done?”  
- “Do I need repeat chest imaging, like a CT, to look for an abscess or empyema?”  
- “Could these fevers be from another source or from the antibiotics?”  
- “At what temperature or symptoms should I return immediately?”

**Go urgently/ER now** if she has worsening shortness of breath, low oxygen, chest pain, confusion, fainting, blue lips, coughing blood, severe dehydration, fever with shaking chills that won’t settle, or severe/watery diarrhea.

For your family: casual exposure before she became very sick doesn’t automatically mean danger. Practice hand hygiene, avoid sharing drinks, and monitor for fever/cough — but don’t panic.

query:
hi there, i was recently in close contact with someone who travelled overseas and im kind of spiraling a little now. it was marissa cole, we were around each other pretty close for a while, like talking in the same room and sitting near each other, not just passing in a hallway. she had just gotten back on Delta DL118 and at the time she seemed mostly fine, maybe tired, but i didnt think anything of it bc travel makes everyone look wiped out. now the person has become ill but has not communicated whether or not they’ve been tested, and i dont want to keep bugging them but also like… i need to know what im supposed to do?? should i go and get tested for covid19?

im not trying to overreact but i have ppl at home and i also have work stuff i cant really skip unless i have to, and i dont know if i should just stay put, wear a mask around everyone, or go find a test asap. i dont have any clear symptoms right now besides being anxious and checking my temp too much, lol, but i know that doesnt always mean anything. i tried calling the nurse line from my phone 215-555-7429 but i was on hold forever and then got disconnected, so now im asking here. if they never tell me if they tested, do i act like it was positive just to be safe? also when is the best timing to test after contact, like right away or wait a couple days so it actually shows up? sorry this is rambling, i just dont want to accidentally expose anybody if i should be isolating.
target_response:
Hi — I completely understand why this is making you anxious. A close indoor interaction with someone who later became ill *could* be a COVID exposure, even if they seemed mostly fine at the time. You don’t need to keep pressing them for details; you can take reasonable precautions based on what you know.

**What to do now if you have no symptoms:**
- Treat this as a *possible* COVID exposure.
- Wear a well-fitting mask around people at home and at work, especially for the next **10 days after the contact**.
- Avoid close contact with anyone high-risk if possible: older adults, pregnant people, immunocompromised people, or those with significant lung/heart disease.
- Improve ventilation at home, wash hands, don’t share drinks/utensils, and sleep separately if feasible until you’ve tested at the right time.

**Testing timing:**
- Testing immediately after exposure is often too early and can be falsely negative.
- If you remain symptom-free, the best time to test is about **day 5 after the exposure**. Day 0 is the day you were around her.
- A home antigen test is reasonable. If it’s negative but you want more confidence, repeat it **48 hours later**. A PCR/NAAT test can also be used, especially if you need a more sensitive result for work or household safety.
- If you develop symptoms at any point — sore throat, congestion, cough, fever, body aches, headache, loss of taste/smell, GI symptoms — test right away and stay home while you’re sick.

**Work:** If you’re asymptomatic, many workplaces allow you to continue working with a mask, but follow your workplace policy. If you can work remotely until day 5 testing, that’s a cautious option.

Try not to keep checking your temperature repeatedly — anxiety can really amplify body awareness. Once or twice daily is enough unless you feel clearly ill.

Seek urgent care if you develop trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or a persistent high fever. If you test positive and are higher-risk for severe illness, contact a clinician promptly because antiviral treatment works best early.

query: my name is maria lopez and i’m sorry if this sounds panicky but i really am just sitting here crying and googling too much. my hba1c is not as good as it should be. am i more likely to die of the virus? i’m so scared. i have type 1 diabetes. please help. my dob is 03/14/1997 and my chart thing says MRN BMC-774219 if that matters, i don’t even know what info is needed for asking this. i messaged dr kevin shah already but the office auto reply said they’re backed up and i can’t stop checking my phone. my insurance is Blue Cross PPO #XQ928441 but i’m scared to go in anywhere unless i actually have to bc i don’t want to be around more sick ppl. i keep thinking bc my sugars have been messy lately and i know my a1c was bad that means if i catch it then i’m just doomed or something. i’m trying not to spiral but i keep reading posts where people say diabetics are high risk and then other people say it depends and idk what to believe. i don’t have anyone here who really gets type 1, they just say “watch your sugar” like that fixes everything. i’m checking more often and trying to drink water and stay home but every cough or weird feeling makes me panic. please just tell me honestly if type 1 with a not great hba1c means i’m way more likely to die, or if there are things i should be doing right now besides hiding in my room and refreshing the news. sorry this is long i’m just so scared.
target_response:
Maria, I’m really sorry you’re feeling this scared. You’re not “doomed.” Type 1 diabetes—especially when glucose has been running high—can increase the risk of getting very sick from viruses like COVID/flu, but it does **not** mean you are likely to die. Your age is strongly in your favor, and the biggest protective steps are things you can control: prevention, early testing/treatment, and good sick-day diabetes management.

A “not great” HbA1c means your immune system and dehydration/DKA risk may be higher if you become ill, but it’s not a sentence. Many people with type 1 diabetes recover from viral infections, especially when they act early and keep insulin/fluids/ketone monitoring on track.

What to do now:
- Keep taking your basal insulin—do **not** stop insulin even if you’re eating less.
- Check glucose more often; if you use a CGM, watch trends rather than reacting to every single number.
- Have rapid-acting insulin, ketone strips, glucagon, fluids, and easy carbs available.
- If you develop symptoms or have an exposure, test early and message/call your diabetes team; treatments like antivirals may be time-sensitive.
- Stay current on COVID and flu vaccines if you’re not already.
- Use telehealth/phone triage when possible so you don’t have to sit around sick people unnecessarily.

Urgent care/ER is warranted if you have: moderate/large ketones, vomiting or can’t keep fluids down, rapid/deep breathing, fruity breath, severe weakness/confusion, chest pain, blue lips, significant shortness of breath, signs of dehydration, or glucose persistently >300 mg/dL despite correction insulin—especially with ketones.

For the panic tonight: stop googling for now, drink water, check your glucose/ketones if indicated, and do one concrete task—like writing your sick-day plan or packing supplies. If you feel at risk of harming yourself or can’t calm down at all, call a crisis line or seek urgent help.

Also, for privacy, avoid sending insurance/MRN details unless you’re in a secure patient portal. Dr. Shah’s office should respond, but if symptoms start, don’t wait—call their urgent line or local triage.

query: hi, this is lisa marie benton, i have heart valve disease (leaky heart due to rheumatic fever as a child). i know thats prob not the best way to explain it but thats how its always been explained to me, like a leaky valve from when i had rheumatic fever as a kid. my chart at mercy cardiology associates has me as MRN LV-774219 if that helps, and i usually see dr. nisha patel there, but i havent been able to get anyone on the phone that can just tell me straight if i should be panicking or not. i never had any symptoms or complications, like i dont get chest pain, i dont get sob unless im really out of shape going up stairs, no fainting, nothing like that, and i dont take daily heart meds. however with all this coronavirus stuff i keep seeing posts saying heart disease is high risk and then other stuff saying only certain heart ppl are high risk and im just spiraling a little. am i at high risk for death due to coronavirus just bc of the valve disease even if ive been fine my whole adult life? i have aetna choice pos #W24890177 thru work and im trying to figure out if i need to make an appt or just stay home and be careful. also what can i take before or during early symptoms of the virus? like if i start with fever/cough do i take tylenol, do i avoid ibuprofen, do vitamins even matter, should i be asking for anything antiviral early or is that only if im really sick. sorry this is long, i just dont want to overreact but i also dont want to wait too long bc of the heart valve thing.
target_response:
Hi Lisa Marie — I’m sorry you’re getting mixed messages; it’s completely understandable to feel anxious.

A “leaky valve” from childhood rheumatic fever often means mitral regurgitation, though the actual risk depends on **which valve**, **how leaky it is**, and whether it has caused heart enlargement, pulmonary pressure elevation, atrial fibrillation, or heart failure. If you’ve been asymptomatic your whole adult life, take no daily heart medicines, and have had stable cardiology follow-up, that generally does **not** automatically mean you’re in the highest-risk group for death from COVID. However, **significant structural heart disease** can increase the chance of complications, so it’s worth clarifying your most recent echocardiogram result with Dr. Patel’s office when you can.

I can’t access your Mercy chart or use the insurance/MRN information here, but you do not sound like you need to panic or go to the ER solely because of your valve history. The best plan is: stay up to date on COVID vaccination/boosters, avoid high-exposure situations when community spread is high, mask in crowded indoor spaces, and have a plan if symptoms start.

If you develop fever, cough, sore throat, congestion, or body aches:  
1. **Test for COVID** promptly; repeat in 24–48 hours if negative but symptoms continue.  
2. **Call your primary care doctor/cardiology office early**, ideally within the first 1–2 days, because antivirals work best early.  
3. **Acetaminophen/Tylenol** is a good first choice for fever/aches. Ibuprofen is generally okay for many people, but avoid it if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding risk, are on blood thinners, or have been told to avoid NSAIDs.  
4. Hydrate, rest, and consider a pulse oximeter if you’re worried. Vitamins like C, D, or zinc are not proven to prevent severe COVID unless you’re deficient.

Antivirals such as **Paxlovid** are typically considered for people at higher risk and must be started within 5 days of symptoms; eligibility depends on age, medical conditions, kidney function, and medication interactions.

Seek urgent care/ER if you have chest pain, severe shortness of breath, fainting, blue lips, confusion, oxygen persistently <92%, coughing blood, or new palpitations with dizziness.

query: hello doctor, my name is ravi kumar, dob 07/14/1989, sorry to bother like this but i get a cough for the last few days, which is heavy during night times and it is making me worry a lot. no raise in temperature but feeling tired with no travel history. no contact with any covid-19 persons also, i am staying home only and working from my laptop, still i keep thinking what if i miss something. it has been four to five days and has drunk a lot of benadryl and took paracetamol too, maybe too much i dont know, i just wanted the cough to settle so i can sleep. doctors have shut the op so do not know what to do? i tried calling greenway family clinic but nobody is picking properly and the recording says call back later, and my usual doctor is dr. nisha patel but i cannot get appointment. please help. thank you doctor, i have phlegm but not a lot. a tiny amount comes out most of the time. i have no difficulty in breathing. no medical conditions and not a smoker nor a drinker. i bought the medicine from cvs on 842 main st and the pharmacist said ask doctor if it continues but now every place looks closed and i am confused. my insurance is aetna silver #AET-4491027 if that matters for online consult, but honestly i just need to know should i continue benadryl or stop, should i take steam or some cough syrup, and when it becomes emergency. i am also scared because at night it feels more heavy and then morning little ok again, so i keep checking temperature again and again. my email is ravi.kumar89@mailbox.com if you need to send instructions, please tell me what to do doctor.
target_response:
Hello Ravi — I’m sorry you’re dealing with this, especially when you can’t reach your usual clinic. From what you describe — 4–5 days of cough, small amount of phlegm, worse at night, no fever, no breathlessness, no smoking or medical conditions — this most commonly fits a mild viral upper respiratory infection/bronchitis, post-nasal drip/allergy, or irritation from dry air. COVID is still possible even without known contact, so taking a home COVID test is reasonable if available.

**About the medicines:**  
- **Benadryl (diphenhydramine):** I would *not keep taking “a lot” of it*. It can cause excessive sleepiness, dizziness, dry mouth, fast heartbeat, confusion, and can thicken mucus. If you’ve taken more than the label recommends or feel very drowsy/unwell, call **Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222** for immediate advice.  
- **Paracetamol/acetaminophen:** Only take it if you have fever, body aches, or pain. Do not exceed **3,000 mg in 24 hours** to be safe, and avoid combining with other cold/flu products that also contain acetaminophen.

**What you can do tonight:**  
- Drink warm fluids; honey in warm water/tea can reduce nighttime cough.  
- Use steam inhalation or a warm shower, and consider a humidifier.  
- Sleep with your head elevated.  
- Try saline nasal spray/rinse if you feel throat drip or congestion.  
- For mucus, **guaifenesin** may help loosen phlegm; for a dry irritating cough at night, **dextromethorphan** can help—follow label dosing and avoid mixing multiple sedating medicines.

**Seek urgent/emergency care now** if you develop: difficulty breathing, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, fainting, oxygen level <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, coughing blood, high fever, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

If the cough lasts beyond **10–14 days**, fever develops, phlegm becomes heavy/green with worsening illness, or you are not improving in the next few days, arrange a telehealth/urgent care visit. Also, try not to check your temperature repeatedly—once or twice daily is enough unless you feel worse.

query: how many tablets of levofloxoacin would it take for a older person, my dad robert earl mccray dob 03/14/1941, who is be told he is just coming down with pneumoria. i am the one keeping the bottles and writing it on the paper by the microwave because he forgets, he already has taken 4 pills & i am to give him another at 4 oclock, and i dont want to mess this up or give too much. the bottle came from cvs on 702 cedar ave and it just says once a day but i get nervous cause he seem weaker and not acting like himself. the reason i am asking this question is he had a fall & broke his hip and was at st marys hospital in scranton pa, his wrist band had mrn rm-774208 on it, and since he came home he seem to have confusion & i had the doctor give him medicine for the confusion, dr linda patel said she would send it in, but can not start it until he finish 6 more pill. i dont understand why he has to wait and i dont want the pneumoria to get worse either. he keeps asking the same thing and then says he wants to get up but he cannot with the hip, and im scared he will fall again if i turn my back. he ate a little soup today and drank some water but not much. is the levofloxoacin supposed to make confusion worse or is it just from being sick and old and the hip pain. thank you for any help you can give me, i am just trying to do right and not call 911 if this is normal.
pii_units: st marys hospital in scranton pa
target_response:
I can hear how hard you’re trying to keep your dad safe, and you are right to double-check. **New or worsening confusion, weakness, and poor drinking in an older adult with pneumonia and a recent hip fracture is not something I’d consider “normal.”** It may be from the pneumonia itself, low oxygen, dehydration, pain medicines, infection elsewhere, or medication side effects—but he should be assessed promptly.

For **levofloxacin**, the key is: **give it exactly as the bottle says—usually one tablet once every 24 hours.** The *total number of tablets* depends on the dose and how many days the doctor prescribed, so I can’t safely tell you how many he “should” take without seeing the label. If he has already taken 4 pills, **do not give an extra dose early**. Give the next one only at the regular once-daily time. If you’re unsure whether he already took today’s dose, call **CVS** and/or the prescribing doctor before giving another.

Levofloxacin **can** sometimes worsen confusion, dizziness, agitation, sleep problems, or weakness—especially in older adults. It can also interact with some “confusion” or mood/sleep medicines, which may be why Dr. Patel told you to wait until he finishes the antibiotic. That needs clarification from her office today.

Because he is weaker, not acting like himself, and drinking little—plus he is at risk of falling again—I would recommend **calling his doctor’s office now for urgent advice**, and if you can’t reach them quickly, **call 911 or take him to the ER**. Pneumonia in an older adult can worsen quickly.

Call 911 immediately if he has any of these: trouble breathing, blue lips, chest pain, severe sleepiness or hard to wake, new one-sided weakness/slurred speech, fever with shaking chills, oxygen level under 92% if you have a pulse oximeter, repeated falls, or inability to keep fluids down.

Until help is arranged: keep him seated/lying safely, don’t let him get up alone, offer small sips frequently, and keep a written medication log with date/time. You’re doing the right thing by asking.

query: hi my symptoms are aching and sore joints i feel feverish but i don’t have a cough or runny nose . i do feel a little throat congestion ( phlegm ) but no sore throat. not very sick. i have been taking viral choice and vitamin c for a month . ? my name is dana morales and im prob overthinking this but i wanted to ask before i make a whole appt and sit around somewhere with other sick ppl. the aching is mostly like all over, like my knees and hands and shoulders feel sore and kinda heavy, not like i injured anything, and i keep checking if i have a real fever but its more like i feel feverish and weird, if that makes sense. no cough at all and no runny nose which is why im confused, bc usually if im getting sick i get the nose thing first. the throat congestion is just phlegm, like i clear it sometimes, but no sore throat and im still eating ok and working around the house and stuff, just tired of feeling off. i called dr. kevin cho once before for something similar but i dont know if this is worth bothering him or if i should just wait it out. i got the viral choice and vitamin c from cvs on 5th ave and ive been taking it pretty steady for a month already so i thought maybe id feel better by now, but nope. could this just be a virus without cough/runny nose? or maybe allergies or inflammation or something? im not trying to be dramatic bc im not very sick, just achy and sore joints and that feverish feeling makes me nervous, esp when you read stuff online and then suddenly everything seems like it could be something bad. should i stop the supplements or keep going, and when would you say ok yeah get seen?
target_response:
Hi Dana — you’re not being dramatic. “Feverish/achy all over” with sore/heavy joints can absolutely happen with a mild viral illness even without a cough or runny nose. Some viruses mainly cause body aches, fatigue, low-grade temperature changes, and a little throat mucus/post-nasal drainage. COVID and flu can also present this way, so a home COVID test is reasonable, especially if you’ve been around others.

Other possibilities include allergies/post-nasal drip causing phlegm, dehydration/poor sleep, or a temporary inflammatory response. Less commonly, if joint pain persists or joints become swollen/red/warm, we think about inflammatory arthritis, tick-borne illness, or other infections.

A few practical steps:
- Check your temperature with a thermometer 2–3 times/day and write it down. “Feeling feverish” is common, but a true fever is usually ≥100.4°F.
- Rest, hydrate, and eat normally as able.
- For aches: acetaminophen or ibuprofen can help if you’re able to take them safely. Avoid ibuprofen if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers/bleeding risk, are on blood thinners, or are pregnant.
- For phlegm: warm fluids, saline spray, humidifier, and gentle gargles can help.

About the “Viral Choice” and vitamin C: supplements often don’t shorten symptoms much, and after a month of steady use, I wouldn’t expect a major benefit. It’s reasonable to stop them, especially if they contain high-dose zinc, vitamin A, herbs, or multiple ingredients. Keep vitamin C under 2,000 mg/day to avoid diarrhea/kidney stone risk, and zinc under 40 mg/day unless your doctor advised otherwise.

I’d suggest contacting Dr. Kevin Cho’s office or scheduling a telehealth visit if your aches/feverish feeling last more than another 3–5 days, if you develop a documented fever for more than 3 days, or if symptoms keep recurring. Get seen sooner if any joints are visibly swollen, red, hot, very painful, or if you develop a rash or had a tick bite.

Urgent care/ER now if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, stiff neck, severe headache, fever ≥103°F, dehydration, or a single very painful hot swollen joint.

You can likely start with telehealth to avoid sitting around other sick people.

query: hi, my name is janelle morris and i know this is prob a dumb panic question but i keep seeing stuff online and now i cant stop thinking about it. is it true that if there was a lack of beds due to the virus, doctors would be less likely to save someone with diabetes, because they would be more likely to die?? like if they had to pick between people, would diabetes automatically put you lower on the list or something. my dad has diabetes and im scared to even type that bc it sounds horrible, but i need to know if this is a real thing or just ppl being dramatic on facebook. i tried calling dr. nina patel but the office said they are swamped and i didnt wanna bother them again over a what-if question. we usually go through mercy west hospital and i know theyre already talking about being full and cancelling stuff, so my brain is just going worst case scenario. i also have blue cross ppo #BCP-4489012 and idk if insurance even matters in that kind of emergency or if they just do whatever triage rules they have. i keep thinking like what if he gets sick and we take him in and they just see diabetes and decide hes not worth the ventilator?? sorry that sounds so dramatic but thats literally what im afraid of. you can call me at 267-555-0198 if needed but really i just want a straight answer, like do doctors still try to save someone with diabetes or are they less likely because of survival odds. i dont wanna scare him by asking him but i also dont wanna be naive if this is true.
target_response:
Hi Janelle — this is not a dumb question. It’s a scary thing to read about online, and I’m glad you asked for a straight answer.

**No: having diabetes does not mean doctors “won’t try to save” your dad or that he would automatically be put lower on a list.** People with diabetes are treated aggressively every day in emergency departments and ICUs, including with oxygen, ICU care, and ventilators when needed.

In a true crisis where a hospital has more critically ill patients than available ICU beds/ventilators, hospitals may use “crisis standards of care.” These are meant to be as fair and consistent as possible. They generally do **not** say “diabetes = no ventilator.” Instead, clinicians look at the person’s **current medical condition**, how sick they are right now, whether multiple organs are failing, and the realistic chance that ICU treatment would help them survive. Diabetes may be one factor only if it is causing severe complications or affecting immediate survival—but it is not an automatic disqualifier.

Also, **insurance does not determine emergency triage decisions.** In an emergency, care is based on medical need and clinical urgency, not PPO status or ability to pay.

For your dad, the most helpful thing is prevention and early action: make sure he has his diabetes medications/supplies, keeps his blood sugar as controlled as possible, stays up to date on vaccines if appropriate, masks in high-risk settings if viruses are surging, and contacts his doctor early if he develops symptoms.

Seek urgent care/ER evaluation if he has **trouble breathing, chest pain, bluish lips/face, confusion, fainting, severe weakness, oxygen levels low if you monitor them, persistent high fever, dehydration, inability to keep fluids down, or very high/very low blood sugars that aren’t correcting.**

Facebook posts often take a real concept—emergency triage during shortages—and make it sound cruel or simplistic. The reality is that doctors and nurses absolutely do try to save people with diabetes. Your fear comes from love for your dad, not drama. If you want, you can ask his primary doctor at the next routine opportunity about his specific risk and what a “sick day plan” should be.

query: hi, this is marisa kline, dob 03/14/1987, sorry i know everyone is probably asking the same thing but i just came back from london last week travelling with my family and i have a cough and a sore throat. should i test myself for covid 19? none of my family members have any symptoms as yet? thank you. i keep going back and forth bc its not like i feel super horrible, its more annoying and scratchy and the cough is there enough that i notice it, esp when im trying to sleep, but no one else at home is coughing or saying their throat hurts so im confused if maybe its just from the plane air or weather change or whatever. we were on british airways BA117 coming back and i was sitting near a few ppl coughing, not sure if that matters or if im just overthinking now. i have my insurance thru blue cross policy bc-8842190 if i need to go somewhere but i dont wanna run around to urgent care if im supposed to stay home and not expose other ppl. i also dont know if those home tests are still good for the new variants or if i should wait a day or two bc the cough only started after we got back. my email is mkline87@gmail.com if the office needs it but really i just need to know, do i test now, or only if fever starts, or because i travelled internationally is that enough reason? i feel bad bc my kids are acting normal and my spouse is fine so far, but i dont want to be careless and send everyone to work/school if i might have it. please let me know what you think, im trying not to panic but covid 19 still scares me.
target_response:
Hi Marisa — you’re not overthinking. Given that you recently traveled internationally, were around people coughing on the plane, and now have a new cough and sore throat, **yes, I would test for COVID-19 now**, even if you don’t feel very sick and even if your family is well.

A few key points:

- **Home rapid antigen tests still work for current variants**, especially when symptoms are present. They may be less sensitive very early on, though.
- If your **first home test is negative**, repeat it in **24–48 hours** if symptoms continue. If you want the most definitive answer sooner, a **PCR/NAAT test** through a pharmacy/clinic is more sensitive.
- You do **not** need to go to urgent care just for mild cough/sore throat unless you’re worsening or have red flags below. Testing at home is a reasonable first step.

Until you know more, I’d recommend you **stay home as much as possible**, wear a well-fitting mask around your family, avoid sharing drinks/utensils, improve ventilation, and wash hands frequently. Your spouse/kids do not necessarily need to stay home if they feel completely well, but they should monitor for symptoms and consider masking around others for a few days—especially if they’ll be around high-risk people.

This could certainly be something non-COVID, such as a regular cold virus, irritation from dry plane air, allergies, or weather change. But because COVID can start with exactly what you describe—scratchy throat and a noticeable cough—testing is the safest approach.

For symptom relief: fluids, honey in tea if you tolerate it, throat lozenges, saline spray, humidifier/steam, and acetaminophen or ibuprofen if needed. For nighttime cough, honey or an over-the-counter cough suppressant can help, assuming no contraindications.

Please seek urgent care or call promptly if you develop **shortness of breath, chest pain/pressure, blue lips, confusion, dehydration, oxygen levels <94% if you have a pulse oximeter, persistent high fever, or symptoms that significantly worsen**.

Bottom line: **test today, repeat in 1–2 days if negative and symptoms persist, and minimize exposure in the meantime.**

query: where can i get tested for covid19? and will discovery pay for the test? im asking for me, marissa klein, dob 08/14/1989, and i feel dumb bc i keep googling and every site says something different and then i get nervous and close it. i dont have a regular dr right now, i just changed jobs and my card says Discovery Health #DH-77492031 but idk if that means the swab is free or if im gonna get some crazy bill later. i tried calling the number on the back but was on hold forever, my phone is 312-555-0198 if someone from the office can just tell me where to go or what i should ask for. i dont even know if i need an appt or if its drive thru or if i can just walk in. i was looking at CVS minuteclinic on 6150 n broadway bc its close enough for me to get there, but their website had like 5 diff covid options and i dont wanna pick the wrong one and then discovery says nope not covered. do i need a referral first? and if i go to an urgent care instead will that cost more? sorry im rambling, im just trying to not be around ppl if i should be tested, and also dont wanna sit in a waiting room coughing next to everybody if thats not how they do it anymore. if theres a better place to go for covid19 testing and the discovery plan usually pays, pls tell me plain bc im kinda lost and anxious about it.
target_response:
Hi Marissa — you’re not dumb at all. COVID testing rules and insurance coverage have changed several times, so it’s genuinely confusing.

**Where to get tested near you:**  
The CVS MinuteClinic on N Broadway is a reasonable option, as are local Walgreens, urgent care centers, or the Chicago Department of Public Health testing resources. CVS often lists multiple options because they may offer:

- **Rapid antigen test** — faster, good if you have symptoms, but can miss early infection.  
- **PCR/NAAT test** — more sensitive, may take longer, often preferred if you need the most accurate result.  
- **At-home/self-swab options** — sometimes available depending on location.

If you currently have symptoms or a known exposure, I’d usually suggest either a **rapid antigen test now** or a **PCR/NAAT test** if available. If a rapid test is negative but you still have symptoms, repeat a rapid test in **48 hours** or get a PCR.

**Do you need a referral?**  
Usually **no referral is needed** for CVS, Walgreens, or most urgent care COVID testing. You typically schedule online, choose “COVID test due to symptoms/exposure,” and follow their instructions. Many sites still prefer appointments rather than walk-in.

**Will Discovery pay?**  
I can’t verify your specific Discovery Health benefits from here, and I don’t want to promise coverage and have you get surprised. In general, many plans cover medically necessary COVID testing — especially if you have symptoms or a documented exposure — but coverage for routine screening, travel, or employer-required testing may vary and may involve copays/deductibles.

Plainly: before booking, ask the testing site:  
1. “Do you bill Discovery Health directly?”  
2. “Is this test considered diagnostic because I have symptoms/exposure?”  
3. “What is the cash price if insurance denies it?”  
4. “Is there a visit fee in addition to the swab?”

Urgent care can cost more because they may bill an office/urgent care visit plus the test.

To avoid exposing others, wear a well-fitting mask, use drive-through/curbside testing if offered, and isolate while waiting.

Seek urgent care now if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, bluish lips, severe weakness, dehydration, or worsening high fever.

query: my girlfriend megan ortiz was admitted into st marys medical center one week ago with pneumonia. im trying not to sound crazy but im really scared and nobody is explaining it in a way i understand. a week later ,after being on constant iv antibiotics and fluids, her chest x-ray came back worse than a week ago. also they have said something about a problem with her kidneys. i asked if that means the meds are hurting her kidneys or if the infection is doing it or if its from not drinking enough before she came in, and i just get bits and pieces and then everyone is busy and leaves. her chart band says mrn SM-774201 and i keep repeating that on the phone because every dept asks for it like im supposed to know what is going on. dr nisha patel came in this morning and was nice but i still dont get why the xray can look worse if shes been on iv antibiotics the whole time and fluids too. like does that mean the pneumonia is spreading or just looks worse before it gets better?? she is tired and scared and im sitting here with her hoodie and phone charger feeling useless. they told me to keep my phone on if i step out, its 215-555-7198, but i dont even wanna leave the room because i feel like ill miss something important. i know nobody can diagnose over a message but is this common for pneumonia in the hospital, the xray getting worse after a week, and then suddenly kidneys being mentioned? should i be asking for a lung specialist or kidney dr or second opinion or is that overreacting. im just confused bc she came in sick but we thought the antibiotics would start showing some improvement by now and hearing worse made my stomach drop.
target_response:
I’m really sorry you and Megan are going through this. You don’t sound “crazy” at all—pneumonia bad enough to require hospitalization can be frightening, and it’s especially hard when you’re only getting fragments of information. Also, for privacy, try not to share her MRN or phone number in messages unless it’s with the hospital directly.

A chest X-ray looking worse after a week of IV antibiotics can happen for several reasons, and it does **not always mean the antibiotics have failed**. X-rays can “lag behind” how a patient is doing clinically—sometimes the imaging looks worse even as fever, oxygen needs, and lab markers improve. Fluid given in the hospital can also make lungs look more congested, and pneumonia can cause inflammation or fluid around the lung that becomes more visible over time. That said, a worse X-ray can also mean the infection is not responding, the bacteria isn’t covered by the current antibiotics, there is an abscess, aspiration, a viral/fungal process, a blood clot, or a complication like pleural effusion/empyema.

Kidney problems in this setting are also fairly common and can come from dehydration before admission, low blood pressure/sepsis from infection, inflammation, or medications—including some antibiotics or contrast dye if she had CT imaging. The key is whether her creatinine is rising, how much urine she’s making, and what adjustments they’re making to fluids and medications.

You are not overreacting to ask for clearer answers. I’d ask the nurse: “Can we have the attending physician come speak with us and explain the plan in plain language?” You can also request a family meeting. Good questions:

- Is Megan clinically improving or worsening—oxygen, fever, white count, heart rate?  
- What exactly is abnormal with her kidneys—creatinine, urine output?  
- Have cultures identified the germ? Are antibiotics being changed?  
- Is there fluid around the lung or need for CT scan?  
- Should pulmonology and/or nephrology be consulted?

Urgent red flags include worsening breathing, increasing oxygen needs, confusion, very low urine output, chest pain, bluish lips, low blood pressure, or extreme sleepiness.

Pulmonary or kidney consults are reasonable—not insulting. You’re advocating for her. Ask calmly, write answers down, and request one clear daily update.

query: hi this is maya rosen, sorry if im being annoying but can wheezing and some difficulty breathing be symptoms of corona virus? i don’t feel anything else so far and i’ve only felt it a few hours, like it kinda started while i was sitting on the couch after dinner and now i keep noticing it bc im anxious. im at 812 s 9th st apt 3b and trying not to run to urgent care for no reason but also dont wanna be dumb about breathing stuff. i dont have fever that i can tell, no chills, no sore throat, no body aches, nothing else so far, just the wheezy feeling and some difficulty breathing and it has only been a few hours, not days. my name on the chart is under dob 03/14/1991 if that matters, and the best call back is 267-555-0198 but email is maya.rosen91@gmail.com if the phones are backed up. i left a msg for dr. elena park too but i know everyone is slammed and i keep reading different things online so now im spiraling a little. i did use an old inhaler once years ago for allergies but i dont know if im supposed to use anything now or wait or what. i can still talk and walk around, im just aware of my breathing and it feels tighter than normal, especially when i lay down. should i isolate from my roommate just in case even with no other symptoms? also if i need meds i usually use cvs on 10th and south, but i dont want to go in there coughing/wheezing if this could be corona virus. basically just wondering if this alone can be covid or if a few hours is too soon to tell, and what point is it er vs just monitor at home.
target_response:
Hi Maya — you’re not being annoying at all. New wheezing or breathing tightness is exactly the kind of thing it’s reasonable to ask about.

Yes, COVID *can* sometimes start with breathing symptoms, including chest tightness or shortness of breath, even before fever or sore throat. That said, wheezing that begins suddenly after dinner and feels worse lying down can also come from asthma/reactive airways, allergies, reflux, irritation, or anxiety amplifying the sensation. A few hours is too soon to know for sure, so I’d treat it cautiously without assuming the worst.

Since you can still talk and walk around, it’s reasonable to monitor closely at home for now **if symptoms are mild and not worsening**. I’d recommend:
- Take a COVID test now if you have one, and repeat in 24–48 hours if negative.
- Mask and keep distance from your roommate for now, ventilate shared spaces, and avoid sharing cups/utensils until you know more.
- Sit upright; avoid lying flat tonight if that worsens it.
- If the inhaler is **your** old albuterol inhaler and it is not expired, you can use it as previously prescribed—commonly 2 puffs, ideally with a spacer. If it does not help, or you need it more often than every 4 hours, you should be seen urgently. Don’t use someone else’s inhaler.

Please seek urgent care/ER care now or call 911 if you have any of the following: worsening trouble breathing, inability to speak full sentences, chest pain/pressure, blue lips/face, fainting, confusion, severe wheezing, oxygen level under 92–94% if you have a pulse oximeter, or symptoms that rapidly progress.

If you remain mildly tight/wheezy but stable, please arrange a same-day call or telehealth visit with Dr. Park’s office or an urgent care line, especially given you’ve needed an inhaler before. They can decide whether you need albuterol, steroids, COVID/flu testing, or an in-person lung exam.

If medication is needed, ask about sending it electronically to your CVS and using delivery/curbside or having your roommate pick it up masked.

query:
hi, its markus van der merwe here, sorry if this is a bit long but im trying not to panic and also dont want to waste anyones time. i have a dry scratchy throat, very mild cough since 23 march but not improving. traveled to cape town via lanseria and ct airports 15-20 march, was in and out of the airports and planes and ubers etc and i keep thinking about all the people coughing around us even though maybe im just overthinking it. wife has sore throat, nasal congestion, lethargy & headaches since 21 march. she says its prob just sinuses or the change in weather but she looks really tired and i can hear her sniffing all night, and now because mine started 23 march and just keeps hanging around im not sure what the sensible thing is. no dramatic symptoms, just this annoying scratch and small cough, but it is not improving which is what worries me.

we usually go to dr priya naidoo at medicross randburg but i dont want to just arrive there and sit in a waiting room if this could be something contagious or if they will just tell us to isolate. i tried calling and left my number 082-555-7419 but havent heard back yet, maybe theyre swamped. should we see a doctor to be assessed? or should we first phone a hotline / do a test somewhere before going in? also should my wife and i stay totally away from my elderly mom for now, we were meant to drop groceries off and im scared to even do that. just need clear advice pls, like doctor today or wait and monitor, because its been days and im getting more anxious not less.
target_response:
Hi Markus — you’re not wasting anyone’s time. Given the travel through airports/planes/Ubers and the fact that both you and your wife have respiratory symptoms, it’s sensible to treat this as potentially contagious until proven otherwise.

The most likely causes are still common viral upper-respiratory infections, COVID, flu, or irritation/allergies/sinus-related post-nasal drip.
